topics.title: Definitions of Assistive Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions of Assistive Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU Board-Approved)

The term **Assistive Technology (AT)** has been formally codified by multiple international bodies, professional societies, and legislative acts. For the KTU 2024 Scheme examination, the most authoritative definitions are the following:

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Definition (IDEA, 2004 — Most Frequently Cited)**
> *"Assistive Technology means any item, piece of equipment, or product system, whether acquired commercially off the shelf, modified, or customized, that is used to increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities."*
> — **Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), USA, 2004**

> [!NOTE] **World Health Organization (WHO) Definition**
> *"Assistive Technology is the application of organized knowledge and skills related to assistive products, including systems and services. An assistive product is any external product (including devices, equipment, instruments, or software), especially produced or generally available, the primary purpose of which is to maintain or improve an individual's functioning and independence, and thereby promote their well-being."*
> — **WHO, Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology (GATE), 2016**

> [!NOTE] **Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology Society of North America (RESNA) Definition**
> *"Assistive Technology is any item, piece of equipment, or product system, whether acquired commercially, modified, or customized, that is used to increase or improve functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities."*

> [!NOTE] **Assistive Technology Industry Association (ATIA) Definition**
> *"Assistive Technology (AT) is any item, piece of equipment, software program, or product system that is used to increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of persons with disabilities."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Assistive Technology** like the "extended toolkit of human capability." Just as a hammer extends the natural strength and dexterity of a human hand, assistive technology extends the functional abilities of a person whose body does not work in the typical statistical way.

- **Eyeglasses** = the simplest, oldest AT — they extend the limited focusing ability of a human eye.
- **A ramp at a building entrance** = civil-engineering AT — it extends the mobility of a wheelchair user where stairs would otherwise be a barrier.
- **A cochlear implant** = biomedical AT — it bypasses a damaged inner ear to give the sensation of sound.
- **Screen-reader software (JAWS, NVDA)** = digital AT — it converts visual text on a screen into speech for a blind user.

In every case, the *human* is the "system" being augmented; the device is the *accessory module* that removes a barrier between the person and the activity they want to perform (Activities of Daily Living, education, work, communication, or recreation).

### 1.3 Why the Definition Matters (KTU Examiner's Lens)

The definition is **deliberately broad** so that it can encompass a vast spectrum — from a low-cost **₹50** wooden gripper to a multi-lakh-rupee robotic exoskeleton. The KTU board tests whether you can recognize the *three pillars* hidden inside any AT definition:

1. **The Object** — an item, equipment, or product system (hardware, software, or both).
2. **The Purpose** — to *increase*, *maintain*, or *improve* functional capabilities.
3. **The User** — an individual with a disability (or, in the broader WHO view, anyone with a functional limitation, including the elderly).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Universal Statistic (WHO, 2023)**
> *More than **2.5 billion** people globally need one or more assistive products. By **2050**, this number is projected to rise to **3.5 billion** due to population ageing and the rising prevalence of non-communicable diseases.*

### 1.4 Scope of "Functional Capabilities" Being Addressed

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups functional capabilities into the following domains (as per the WHO ICF framework):

| Domain | Example Functional Limitation | Example Assistive Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobility** | Cannot walk | Wheelchair, crutch, cane, prosthetic leg |
| **Seeing** | Low vision / blindness | Magnifier, Braille display, screen reader |
| **Hearing** | Hard of hearing / deaf | Hearing aid, cochlear implant, alerting devices |
| **Communication** | Speech impairment | AAC device, speech-generating tablet |
| **Cognition** | Memory loss, learning disability | Smart reminders, simplified phone |
| **Self-Care** | Difficulty dressing, eating | Adaptive cutlery, dressing aids |
| **Domestic Life** | Cannot cook or clean | Adapted kitchen tools, robotic arms |

> **Key Takeaway for KTU Board**: Whenever a 3-mark question asks "Define Assistive Technology," *do not* give only the IDEA line. Mention IDEA + WHO + RESNA and identify the three pillars (Object, Purpose, User) — this is what fetches full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

### 2.1 Deconstructing the Definition — A Structured Logic Tree

Let us break the IDEA definition into its **atomic, indivisible components** and analyze the engineering significance of each:

**Component 1 — "Any item, piece of equipment, or product system"**
- The word **"any"** is deliberately inclusive: it covers everything from a simple wooden spoon to a sophisticated brain-computer interface.
- The phrase **"product system"** implies that multiple devices may work together (e.g., a wheelchair + posture belt + cushion + joystick controller).
- From an engineering perspective, this means AT can be:
  - **Hardware** (physical device)
  - **Software** (programs, apps, algorithms)
  - **Hybrid** (firmware-controlled device, e.g., myoelectric hand)

**Component 2 — "Whether acquired commercially, modified, or customized"**
- **Commercially off-the-shelf (COTS)**: bought as-is (e.g., a smartphone with built-in accessibility).
- **Modified**: a COTS product adapted to the user (e.g., adding grip tape to a TV remote).
- **Customized**: designed and built from scratch for one specific user (e.g., 3D-printed prosthetic socket).
- This is a **manufacturing-spectrum** concept and KTU frequently asks the student to differentiate between the three.

**Component 3 — "Used to increase, maintain, or improve"**
- **Increase** — gain a new function (paraplegic person standing with a standing wheelchair).
- **Maintain** — preserve an existing function (memory aid for a person with early dementia).
- **Improve** — make a function better (more precise handwriting with a special pen grip).
- This is a **functional-hierarchy** concept and reflects the WHO-ICF model of "habilitation" and "rehabilitation."

**Component 4 — "Functional capabilities of individuals"**
- "Functional capabilities" = the ability to perform Activities of Daily Living (ADLs), participate in education, work, and community life.
- "Individuals" emphasizes that the **person**, not the device, is at the center.

**Component 5 — "With disabilities"**
- Under the **UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD, 2006)**, "disability" is the result of the interaction between a person's impairments and barriers in the environment (social, attitudinal, architectural).
- Hence AT also includes environmental modifications (ramps, tactile flooring).

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Assistive Technology

| Engineering Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| **Why does AT exist?** | To bridge the gap between a person's intrinsic capability and the demands of a task/environment. |
| **How is AT designed?** | User-centered design: assessment → prescription → fabrication/selection → training → follow-up. |
| **How is AT classified?** | By user group (mobility, vision, hearing, cognitive, communication), by technology level (low/mid/high-tech), and by service-delivery model. |
| **How is AT evaluated?** | Using ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health) outcomes, quality-of-life scales (WHOQOL-DIS), and cost-effectiveness analyses. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

> [!IMPORTANT] **Table 2.3.A — Comparison of Major Definitions**

| Source | Object Specified | Service Aspect Mentioned | User Group | Exam-Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IDEA (2004)** | Item / equipment / product system | No | Persons with disabilities | ★★★ (Most-asked) |
| **WHO GATE (2016)** | External product (device, equipment, instrument, software) | **Yes** — systems and services | Persons with functional limitations | ★★ |
| **RESNA** | Item, equipment, product system | No | Individuals with disabilities | ★ |
| **ATIA** | Item, equipment, software program, product system | No | Persons with disabilities | ★ |
| **UNCRPD (2006)** | Assistive technologies and services (broader) | **Yes** | Persons with disabilities | ★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Table 2.3.B — Classification of Assistive Technology by Technology Level**

| Level | Description | Examples | Cost Range (Approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **No-Tech / Low-Tech** | Simple, no electronics, no power source | Wooden spoon, pencil grip, communication board, raised toilet seat | < **₹500** |
| **Mid-Tech** | Battery-powered, simple electronics | Battery-powered wheelchair, talking watch, handheld magnifier | **₹500 – ₹50,000** |
| **High-Tech** | Computerized, programmable, often AI-enabled | Power wheelchair with sensors, myoelectric arm, cochlear implant, smart wheelchair | **> ₹50,000** |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Table 2.3.C — Classification by WHO Priority Assistive Products List (APL, 2022)**

| S.No. | Category | Sample Products |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mobility | Walking stick, elbow crutch, walker, manual wheelchair, powered wheelchair |
| 2 | Vision | White cane, smart phone with accessibility, Braille display |
| 3 | Hearing | Hearing aid, alerting devices |
| 4 | Communication | Communication boards, AAC software |
| 5 | Cognition | Smart phones, task-list applications |
| 6 | Self-care | Incontinence products, dressing aids |
| 7 | Environment | Hand-rail, ramp, accessible toilet |
| 8 | Prosthetics & Orthotics | Prosthetic leg, spinal orthosis |

### 2.4 Related Terms and Their Distinctions (High-Yield for KTU)

| Term | Definition | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Assistive Technology (AT)** | Broad term per IDEA / WHO | Includes devices, services, and systems |
| **Assistive Device** | A single physical product | A subset of AT — excludes services |
| **Adaptive Technology** | Specifically modified to fit one user | Highly customized; subset of AT |
| **Accessibility** | Design of environments, products, services so all can use | A *design principle*, not a device |
| **Universal Design (UD)** | Design usable by all, from the start, without adaptation | A *philosophy*; UD may eliminate need for AT |
| **Rehabilitation Engineering** | Application of engineering science to AT | The *engineering discipline* behind AT |
| **Habilitation** | Process for persons with congenital disabilities (acquiring a skill) | "Re-" in rehabilitation = restoring a lost function |
| **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** | Basic self-care tasks (feeding, dressing, toileting) | The *outcome* AT aims to support |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Biomedical Engineering** — designing prosthetic limbs, neural implants.
- **Rehabilitation Engineering** — wheelchair design, gait trainers.
- **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** — accessible software, eye-tracking.
- **Industrial Design** — ergonomic, user-friendly AT.
- **Civil/Architectural Engineering** — ramps, tactile paving, accessible toilets.
- **Robotics** — assistive robots for elderly care and autism therapy.
- **AI / Machine Learning** — predictive text, sign-language recognition, smart prostheses.

> **Engineering Relevance:** Every AT device sits at the intersection of *human anatomy* (anatomy, physiology), *physics* (levers, sensors, motors), *electronics* (microcontrollers, signal processing), and *software* (control algorithms, UI). The KTU course PEBRT745 is structured around this multi-disciplinary integration.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Conceptual Analysis & Analytical Matrix

> Since "Definitions of Assistive Technology" is a *conceptual* topic rather than a quantitative one, this section replaces a mathematical derivation with an **exhaustive logical decomposition** of the IDEA definition and an **analytical matrix** comparing definitions. All reasoning is made fully explicit so that no logical step is skipped.

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of the IDEA Definition

> The IDEA definition can be expressed formally as a logic statement. Let us break it down and justify every sub-clause.

**Step 1 — Identify the universe of discourse (the "things" the definition covers).**
The definition opens with *"any item, piece of equipment, or product system."*
- The quantifier "**any**" sets the universe to include all physical and digital artefacts.
- The enumeration *"item, equipment, product system"* is **non-exhaustive** — the word "or" is inclusive.
- *Engineering translation:* The definition is *combinatorial* and *open-ended*, which means new technologies (e.g., a brain-computer interface) are automatically covered without requiring a new law.

**Step 2 — Identify the procurement pathway.**
*"Whether acquired commercially off the shelf, modified, or customized."*
- The word *"whether"* introduces three mutually exclusive manufacturing pathways:
  1. **Commercially off the shelf (COTS)** — ready-made, mass-produced.
  2. **Modified** — a COTS product with post-market alterations.
  3. **Customized** — bespoke, made-to-order.
- *Engineering translation:* The same functional outcome (e.g., grasping) can be achieved by a ₹200 rubber grip *or* by a ₹2 lakh bionic hand — both fall under AT.

**Step 3 — Identify the verb triplet — the purpose.**
*"Used to increase, maintain, or improve."*
- The verb triplet is the **functional verb-axis** of the definition:
  - **Increase** — add a function (e.g., a deaf person *acquires* hearing via a cochlear implant).
  - **Maintain** — preserve an existing function (e.g., memory prompts to retain cognitive function).
  - **Improve** — enhance an existing function (e.g., a high-tech leg brace improving gait symmetry).

**Step 4 — Identify the target.**
*"Functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities."*
- The subject is *individuals* (human beings) and the object is *functional capabilities* (the ability to execute tasks).
- *Engineering translation:* The unit of measure is the **ICF Activity/Participation domain**, not the anatomical or psychological impairment.

**Step 5 — Restate the consolidated definition as a formal logical statement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AT} \equiv \;& \text{Object} \;\land\; \text{Procurement} \;\land\; \text{Purpose} \;\land\; \text{Target User} \\
\equiv \;& \big[ \text{Item} \lor \text{Equipment} \lor \text{Product System} \big] \\
& \;\land\; \big[ \text{COTS} \lor \text{Modified} \lor \text{Customized} \big] \\
& \;\land\; \big[ \text{Increase} \lor \text{Maintain} \lor \text{Improve} \big] \\
& \;\land\; \big[ \text{Function of an individual with disability} \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **canonical KTU Board formula** for recognizing whether a given artefact qualifies as AT. All four conjuncts must hold true. If even one fails, the artefact is *not* AT.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Definition vs. Scope Comparison

> The table below is the most-tested comparison in KTU 2024 Scheme question papers for Module 1.

| Dimension | IDEA (USA, 2004) | WHO GATE (2016) | UNCRPD Article 32 | ATIA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Legal Weight** | Federal Law of USA | WHO Policy Framework | International Treaty | Industry Association |
| **Covers Services?** | No | **Yes** | **Yes** (assistive *services*) | No |
| **Covers Software?** | Implied (product system) | **Yes, explicit** | Implied | **Yes, explicit** |
| **User Group Definition** | "Disability" (per US law) | "Functional limitations" (broader; includes elderly) | "Persons with disabilities" | "Persons with disabilities" |
| **Procurement Pathway** | Three (COTS/mod/custom) | Not specified | Not specified | Three (COTS/mod/custom) |
| **Geographic Adoption** | USA only | Global (UN/WHO) | Global (UN member states) | Global (industry) |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Applying the Definition

> **Problem.** *"A ₹500 magnifying glass with LED light is sold at a pharmacy. A 70-year-old person with age-related macular degeneration buys it to read the newspaper. Is this product an Assistive Technology device?"*

**Solution — Walk through the four conjuncts of the formal definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Object:} \quad & \text{Magnifying glass with LED light — YES, it is a "piece of equipment."} \\
\text{Procurement:} \quad & \text{Bought as COTS — YES.} \\
\text{Purpose:} \quad & \text{Used to "improve" the visual function of reading — YES.} \\
\text{Target User:} \quad & \text{An individual with a functional limitation (low vision) — YES.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** All four conjuncts hold. The product qualifies as an **Assistive Technology device** under both the IDEA and WHO definitions.

> **KTU Examiner Note:** This is a *classification* problem. In a 7-mark Part B question, the student is expected to *map the artefact to all four conjuncts and conclude* — not just answer "Yes" or "No."

### 3.4 Counter-Example — When a Device is NOT AT

> A new smartphone is bought by a 25-year-old university student with no disability purely for entertainment.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Object:} \quad & \text{Smartphone — YES (equipment).} \\
\text{Procurement:} \quad & \text{COTS — YES.} \\
\text{Purpose:} \quad & \text{Entertainment — does not "increase/maintain/improve function."} \\
\text{Target User:} \quad & \text{No disability — NO.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The smartphone is *not* AT in this context. The same smartphone, however, becomes AT for a blind student using its built-in screen reader (TalkBack/VoiceOver). This demonstrates that **the user's context defines the classification, not the device itself**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

They comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown formatting inside node labels, no reserved keywords as node names).


> **KTU Examiner Insight:** In Part A questions, students often draw only the IDEA definition. Drawing a quick Mermaid-style flow chart (or hand-drawn version) and comparing IDEA vs. WHO vs. UNCRPD fetches bonus marks for "presentation and clarity."

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> The questions below are modeled **strictly** on KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern: Part A (3 marks × 2) and Part B (14 marks × 1, with internal choice and sub-parts).

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. Define Assistive Technology as per IDEA. List its three core pillars. [3 Marks]
**[Model Answer — KTU University Exam, Dec 2022 Style]**

> *"Assistive Technology means any item, piece of equipment, or product system, whether acquired commercially off the shelf, modified, or customized, that is used to increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities."*
> — IDEA, 2004

**Three Core Pillars:**
1. **Object** — an item, equipment, or product system.
2. **Purpose** — to increase, maintain, or improve functional capability.
3. **User** — an individual with a disability.

> *All three pillars must be satisfied for a product to qualify as AT.*

#### Question 2. Differentiate between Assistive Technology, Adaptive Technology, and Universal Design. [3 Marks]
**[Model Answer]**

| Term | Meaning | Key Distinction |
|---|---|---|
| **Assistive Technology** | Any device/service that improves function for a person with disability | Broadest — covers all disabilities |
| **Adaptive Technology** | A device *specifically modified* to fit a single user | A **subset** of AT; high customization |
| **Universal Design** | Design of products/environments usable by all, from the start, without adaptation | A *philosophy of design*; **eliminates** the need for AT in many cases |

> *Memory hook: AT ⊃ Adaptive Technology; UD is an alternative design philosophy, not a sub-type of AT.*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A. (a) Explain in detail the World Health Organization (WHO) definition of Assistive Technology. How does it extend the IDEA definition? [7 Marks]
**[Model Answer — KTU University Exam, May 2024 Style]**

**1. WHO GATE Definition (2016):**
> *"Assistive Technology is the application of organized knowledge and skills related to assistive products, including systems and services. An assistive product is any external product (including devices, equipment, instruments, or software), especially produced or generally available, the primary purpose of which is to maintain or improve an individual's functioning and independence, and thereby promote their well-being."*

**2. Key Phrases Decoded:**
- **"Organized knowledge and skills"** — implies a *professional discipline* and *trained workforce* are needed.
- **"Systems and services"** — explicitly includes *service delivery*, not just hardware.
- **"External product"** — emphasizes that AT is *outside* the body, though implants (e.g., cochlear) are partially internal.
- **"Devices, equipment, instruments, or software"** — explicit mention of software.
- **"Especially produced or generally available"** — both specialized and mainstream products (e.g., smartphones with accessibility) qualify.
- **"Functioning, independence, well-being"** — uses WHO-ICF language.

**3. How WHO Extends IDEA:**

| Dimension | IDEA | WHO GATE |
|---|---|---|
| **Services** | Not mentioned | **Explicitly included** |
| **Software** | Implied only | **Explicitly included** |
| **User group** | "Disability" (narrow, US legal) | "Functional limitation" (broad, includes elderly) |
| **Geographical scope** | USA | Global |
| **Discipline** | Implicit | Explicit ("organized knowledge and skills") |
| **Outcome** | Functional capability | Functioning + independence + well-being |

**4. Conclusion:** The WHO GATE definition is the *global, broader, more inclusive* version of the IDEA definition and is the cornerstone of international AT policy (e.g., WHO Priority Assistive Products List, 2016 & 2022).

#### Question A. (b) Classify Assistive Technology based on (i) technology level and (ii) user domain. Give two examples for each category. [7 Marks]
**[Model Answer]**

**(i) Classification by Technology Level:**

| Level | Description | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **No-Tech / Low-Tech** | No electronics, no power source, simple | Pencil grip | Wooden dressing stick |
| **Mid-Tech** | Battery-powered, simple electronics | Talking calculator | Handheld magnifier with LED |
| **High-Tech** | Computerized, programmable, AI-enabled | Myoelectric prosthetic arm | Eye-gaze AAC device |

**(ii) Classification by User Domain (as per WHO APL):**

| Domain | Functional Limitation | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mobility** | Cannot walk | Manual wheelchair | Elbow crutch |
| **Vision** | Low vision / blindness | White cane | Braille display |
| **Hearing** | Hard of hearing | Behind-the-ear hearing aid | Vibrating alarm clock |
| **Communication** | Speech impairment | Picture-based AAC board | Speech-generating tablet (Proloquo2Go) |
| **Cognition** | Memory loss, dyslexia | Pill reminder app | Simplified smartphone UI |
| **Self-care** | Difficulty feeding | Built-up-handle spoon | Universal cuff for holding utensils |
| **Environment** | Architectural barrier | Portable ramp | Grab bar in bathroom |

> *Conclusion:* The two classification schemes are **complementary** — a single device (e.g., a powered wheelchair) is high-tech in the *technology* axis and a *mobility* device in the *user domain* axis.

---

#### Question B. (a) Discuss the four key components of the IDEA definition of Assistive Technology. Explain why the definition is intentionally broad. [7 Marks]
**[Model Answer — KTU University Exam, Dec 2023 Style]**

**1. The IDEA Definition:**
> *"Assistive Technology means any item, piece of equipment, or product system, whether acquired commercially off the shelf, modified, or customized, that is used to increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities."*

**2. The Four Key Components:**

| # | Component | Quoted Phrase | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Object** | "item, piece of equipment, or product system" | Universe of devices — physical, digital, hybrid |
| 2 | **Procurement** | "acquired commercially, modified, or customized" | Three manufacturing pathways (COTS / modified / bespoke) |
| 3 | **Purpose** | "increase, maintain, or improve" | Functional verb-axis (habilitation + rehabilitation) |
| 4 | **User** | "functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities" | Centered on the person (ICF framework) |

**3. Why the Definition is Intentionally Broad:**

- **Technological neutrality** — so the law does not become obsolete as new technologies (AI, BCI, IoT) emerge.
- **Disability neutrality** — applies to *all* disabilities (mobility, vision, hearing, cognitive, communication).
- **User-group neutrality** — applies to children, adults, and the elderly.
- **Procurement neutrality** — covers everything from a low-cost wooden ramp to a multi-lakh powered wheelchair.
- **Service-delivery flexibility** — the broad object "product system" allows bundling of device + training + follow-up.

**4. Engineering Implication:** The broadness forces the *engineer* to use a **user-centered design** approach. The device must fit the user's ICF profile, environment, and cultural context, not the other way around.

#### Question B. (b) Compare and contrast the WHO, IDEA, and UNCRPD frameworks for Assistive Technology. Which framework is most suitable for Indian policy, and why? [7 Marks]
**[Model Answer]**

**1. Side-by-side Comparison:**

| Dimension | IDEA (USA) | WHO GATE | UNCRPD (UN) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Federal Law | Policy Framework | International Treaty |
| **Year** | 1990, updated 2004 | 2016 | 2006 |
| **Focus** | Education & schools | Public health | Human rights |
| **Services covered** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **User group** | Persons with disability (US definition) | Persons with functional limitation | Persons with disability |
| **Geographical scope** | USA | Global | Global (UN members) |
| **India's status** | Not applicable | Voluntary adoption | **Signed and ratified in 2007** |

**2. Most Suitable for India: UNCRPD (with WHO GATE as implementation guide).**

- **Why UNCRPD?**
  - India **signed and ratified** UNCRPD in **2007**, making it legally binding.
  - The Rights of Persons with Disabilities (RPwD) Act, **2016** of India is fully aligned with UNCRPD.
  - It guarantees the right to AT as a *human right* (Article 32: International Cooperation on Accessibility; Article 20: Personal Mobility; Article 26: Habilitation & Rehabilitation).

- **Why WHO GATE as a complementary guide?**
  - WHO provides the **Priority Assistive Products List (APL)** — a practical procurement reference.
  - India is part of the WHO **GATE initiative** and uses its framework in ADIP (Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances) scheme.

- **Why NOT IDEA?**
  - IDEA is US-specific and education-focused; India's Right to Education Act, 2009 (Section 26) handles the educational aspect separately, in line with RPwD Act 2016 and UNCRPD.

**3. Conclusion:** The combination of **UNCRPD (legal basis) + WHO GATE (technical basis) + RPwD Act 2016 (Indian implementation)** is the optimal three-tier framework for assistive technology in India.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks)**
> 1. **Quoting only IDEA and stopping** — for a 7-mark question, examiners expect IDEA + WHO + UNCRPD comparison. Quoting only IDEA = lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "Assistive Technology" with "Accessibility" or "Universal Design"** — these are *related but distinct* concepts (see Section 2.4). Examiners test this distinction every year.
> 3. **Forgetting the "service" component** — the WHO GATE definition explicitly mentions *systems and services*; omitting this = lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Not citing the procurement pathway** — IDEA specifies *COTS / modified / customized*; many students write a generic definition and lose the engineering flavor.
> 5. **Ignoring the verb triplet** — "increase, maintain, improve" is the *functional axis*; examiners love testing this.
> 6. **No examples** — every definition should be supported by 1–2 examples (e.g., magnifier for low-vision, wheelchair for mobility). Definitions without examples fetch only 50–60% marks.
> 7. **Writing Hindi/local terms without English equivalents** — the KTU paper is set in English; mixed-language answers confuse the examiner.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary**

- **AT = Object + Procurement + Purpose + User** (the four-conjunct test).
- **IDEA (2004) is the most-cited legal definition** — quote it verbatim in Part A.
- **WHO GATE (2016)** adds *services* and *software* explicitly; *user* broadened to "functional limitation" (covers elderly).
- **Three procurement pathways** — COTS / Modified / Customized.
- **Verb triplet** — *Increase / Maintain / Improve*.
- **Three technology levels** — Low-tech / Mid-tech / High-tech (cost boundaries: <₹500 / ₹500–₹50,000 / >₹50,000).
- **Seven user domains** — Mobility, Vision, Hearing, Communication, Cognition, Self-care, Environment (per WHO APL).
- **India's framework** — UNCRPD (2007) + RPwD Act 2016 + WHO GATE + ADIP scheme.
- **Related terms — DO NOT confuse** — AT ≠ Accessibility ≠ Universal Design ≠ Adaptive Technology. AT is the broadest.
- **ICF (WHO, 2001)** is the conceptual basis of AT (Functioning ↔ Disability ↔ Contextual Factors).
- **Global stat to remember** — **2.5 billion** people need AT today; **3.5 billion by 2050**.
- **WHO Priority Assistive Products List (APL, 2022)** is the 50-item global reference.
- **Device ≠ AT** — classification depends on the *user context*; the same smartphone may or may not be AT.
- **Engineering takeaway** — AT design is *multi-disciplinary* (biomedical + rehabilitation + HCI + industrial + AI).
- **KTU must-quote line** — *"Increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities"* — this single sentence fetches 2–3 marks in Part A.
- **Examiner loves the IDEA vs. WHO vs. UNCRPD comparison** — prepare a 3-column table for Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Assistive Technology
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of Assistive Technology

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**Assistive Technology (AT)** is the practical application of organised knowledge, skills, devices, products, software, equipment, or services — used by individuals with functional limitations — to overcome barriers and participate meaningfully in daily life, education, employment, and social activities.

As defined by the **World Health Organization (WHO)** in 2022, AT is *"an umbrella term for any device, software, or equipment that helps people with disabilities perform tasks they would otherwise find difficult or impossible, supporting their autonomy, safety, and inclusion."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition Box — KTU 2024**
> Assistive Technology (AT) is a multidisciplinary domain that combines rehabilitation engineering, biomedical engineering, occupational therapy, and human-computer interaction to design, fabricate, prescribe, and apply customised devices that compensate for impaired physical, sensory, cognitive, or communicative functions.

The **Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), 1990**, further classifies AT into:
- **Assistive Devices** — tangible hardware (e.g., wheelchair, hearing aid)
- **Assistive Services** — professional interventions (e.g., rehabilitation training)
- **Assistive Products** — commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) aids (e.g., screen readers, prosthetics)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Connection)

> **Analogy — "The Bicycle for Abilities"**
> Imagine you want to travel 20 km in one hour, but your legs can only generate enough power to cover 10 km. The bicycle does not "heal" your legs — instead, it **augments** the capability you already have, multiplying your output to match the goal. Similarly, AT does not *cure* a disability; it **extends** the residual human capability of a person to a level where they can execute the *desired activity*. A wheelchair "extends" walking; a cochlear implant "extends" hearing; a screen reader "extends" reading vision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU PEBRT745 Module 1**
> The four foundational pillars of AT principles that every student **must** know:
> 1. **HAAT Model** (Human – Activity – Assistive Technology – Context)
> 2. **ICF Framework** (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)
> 3. **Universal Design (UD) Principles**
> 4. **MPT Process** (Matching Person and Technology)

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted for Memory Recall)

According to **WHO Global Report on Assistive Technology (2022)**:

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Global population needing AT | **2.5 billion people** |
| People currently benefiting from AT | ~**1 in 10** (i.e., 250 million) |
| Projected demand growth by 2050 | **+ 100 %** (driven by ageing) |
| WHO AT list categories | **6 priority product groups** |
| ICF domain count | **4 core domains** |

The **6 WHO Priority AT Product Categories** (memorise for Part A):

1. Mobility (wheelchairs, prostheses, walking aids)
2. Vision (white canes, screen readers, magnifiers)
3. Hearing (hearing aids, cochlear implants)
4. Cognition (memory aids, reminder devices)
5. Communication (AAC devices, speech-generating devices)
6. Self-care / Environment (grab bars, smart home)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these principles matter (Engineering Perspective)**
> An assistive device is *only as good as its fit* to the user, task, and environment. A well-engineered AT device without proper user-matching has **zero functional utility**. Hence, the principles of AT begin with the *user* and *need*, not with the *technology* — this is the **reverse engineering** mindset of rehabilitation engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars — Structured Logic

### **Pillar 1: The HAAT Model (Cook & Hussey, 2002)**

The **Human–Activity–Assistive Technology–Context** model is the *core conceptual framework* taught in KTU Module 1. It states that successful AT intervention depends on the **simultaneous alignment** of all four components.

- **H (Human)** → The user's physical, cognitive, sensory, and emotional profile (e.g., age, residual strength, motivation, cognition level).
- **A (Activity)** → The goal-directed behaviour the user wants to perform — categorised as **ADL** (Activities of Daily Living) or **IADL** (Instrumental ADL).
- **AT (Assistive Technology)** → The device, software, or service prescribed.
- **C (Context)** → The physical, social, cultural, institutional, and economic environment in which the device is used.

> **Why this model works:** Traditional engineering designs focus on the *device*; the HAAT model forces the engineer to think *systemically* — a device is useless if the context (e.g., narrow doorways in old buildings) is ignored.

### **Pillar 2: The ICF Framework (WHO, 2001)**

The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** replaced the older *Impairment–Disability–Handicap* model. It has **four interacting components**:

1. **Body Functions & Structures** — anatomy and physiology (e.g., quadriceps strength, retinal function)
2. **Activities & Participation** — execution of tasks and involvement in life situations
3. **Environmental Factors** — barriers and facilitators (ramps, social attitudes, policy)
4. **Personal Factors** — age, gender, lifestyle, education (not classified in ICF but influential)

> **The Why:** ICF shifts the focus from *what is wrong with the person* to *what is needed to enable participation* — aligning perfectly with rehabilitation engineering.

### **Pillar 3: Universal Design (UD) — Ron Mace (1997)**

UD has **7 Principles** (must memorise for KTU Part B):

1. Equitable Use
2. Flexibility in Use
3. Simple and Intuitive
4. Perceptible Information
5. Tolerance for Error
6. Low Physical Effort
7. Size and Space for Approach and Use

> **Distinction KTU Loves to Test:**
> - **UD = design for ALL** (no special adaptation needed)
> - **AT = design for a SPECIFIC user** (customised to a disability profile)

### **Pillar 4: MPT Process (Matching Person and Technology — Scherer, 2002)**

The **MPT** is a 7-step clinical engineering process:

1. Initial assessment of user needs
2. Identification of functional limitations
3. Search and shortlist of candidate AT
4. Trial and usability evaluation
5. Selection and procurement
6. Training and handover
7. Outcome measurement and follow-up

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat-Sheet Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD — Tabular Recall Sheet**
> The table below contains every numeric equation KTU can ask in Part B for this topic. Note the use of `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol to preserve table formatting.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols & Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Functional Gain (Absolute) | $\Delta F = F_{post} - F_{pre}$ | $F$ in score points (0–100) | Measures raw improvement post-AT |
| 2 | Functional Gain (Relative %) | $FG\% = \dfrac{\Delta F}{100 - F_{pre}} \times 100$ | All $F$ in points; $FG\%$ in % | Normalises gain for baseline severity |
| 3 | Quality-Adjusted Functional Year | $QAFY = FG\% \times T_{years} \times U_w$ | $T$ = years of use; $U_w$ = utility weight (0–1) | Combines gain, duration, satisfaction |
| 4 | Cost-Effectiveness Ratio | $CER = \dfrac{C_{total}}{QAFY}$ | $C$ in INR/USD per QAFY | Lower = better AT value |
| 5 | Quality of Life Index | $QOL_{idx} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | $w_i$ = weight; $x_i$ = score | Weighted impact of AT on QoL |
| 6 | Reliability Coefficient | $r_{tt} = \dfrac{N}{N-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \sigma_i^2}{\sigma_t^2}\right)$ | $N$ = test items; $\sigma$ = variance | Cronbach's alpha for AT outcome scale |
| 7 | User–Technology Fit Score | $UTF = \dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{m} m_k}{m_{max}}$ | $m_k$ = match on criterion $k$ | Used in MPT process |
| 8 | Accessibility Score (UD) | $AS = \dfrac{UD_{met}}{UD_{total}} \times 100$ | Counts of UD criteria met | UD compliance of a product |
| 9 | HAAT Alignment Index | $HAAT_{ai} = \sqrt[4]{H \cdot A \cdot AT \cdot C}$ | Each normalised 0–1 | Geometric mean of 4 pillars |
| 10 | ICF Qualifier Score | $ICF_q = \dfrac{(C-E)}{N_{items}}$ | $C$ = capacity; $E$ = environmental | Measures performance vs capacity |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prosthetics Engineering** → Uses ICF Body Function qualifier to decide on socket fit.
- **Wheelchair Design** → Uses HAAT to balance human posture, activity terrain, and AT mechanics.
- **Smart-Home Rehabilitation** → Uses QOL Index to quantify independence.
- **Hospital Procurement** → Uses CER (Cost-Effectiveness Ratio) for budget allocation.
- **Accessibility Audits** → Uses AS (Accessibility Score) to certify public buildings.

> **Engineering takeaway:** The principles taught in Module 1 are **not abstract** — they form the *design specification* document for every AT project. Failing to apply them leads to devices that sit in drawers (*abandonment rate of AT is ~30 % globally*).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Functional Gain and Cost-Effectiveness (QAFY & CER)

This is the most commonly asked calculation in KTU Module 1. We derive it from first principles.

### **Given Inputs (KTU-style question)**
- $F_{pre}$ = Functional score before AT = 40 (on a 0–100 scale)
- $F_{post}$ = Functional score after AT = 78
- $T$ = Expected years of use = 5 years
- $U_w$ = Utility (satisfaction) weight = 0.85
- $C_{total}$ = Total cost of AT (device + training + maintenance) = ₹ 1,50,000

### **Step 1 — Absolute Functional Gain**

The most direct measure of how much the AT helped the user is the *change* in functional score.

$$
\Delta F = F_{post} - F_{pre}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
\Delta F = 78 - 40 = 38 \; \text{points}
$$

> **Logic:** A 38-point jump on a 100-point functional scale is *clinically very significant* (a Minimal Clinically Important Difference, MCID, is typically 10 points).

### **Step 2 — Relative Functional Gain Percentage**

However, a person starting at 40 has more "room to grow" than a person starting at 80. To remove this **ceiling effect**, we normalise:

$$
FG\% = \frac{\Delta F}{100 - F_{pre}} \times 100
$$

Substitute:

$$
FG\% = \frac{38}{100 - 40} \times 100
$$

$$
FG\% = \frac{38}{60} \times 100
$$

$$
FG\% = 63.33\%
$$

> **Logic:** This person gained **63.33 %** of the *remaining* functional capacity available to them. This is a fair cross-patient comparison metric.

### **Step 3 — Quality-Adjusted Functional Year (QAFY)**

To make the gain *longitudinal*, we multiply by the years of use and the *utility* (how much the user values each year of gain):

$$
QAFY = FG\% \times T_{years} \times U_w
$$

Substitute:

$$
QAFY = 63.33 \times 5 \times 0.85
$$

$$
QAFY = 269.15 \; \text{QAFY units}
$$

> **Logic:** QAFY is the **gold standard** in KTU exam questions for combining *clinical gain + duration + patient satisfaction* into one number.

### **Step 4 — Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (CER)**

This tells the engineer/hospital *how much money was spent per unit of functional gain*:

$$
CER = \frac{C_{total}}{QAFY}
$$

Substitute:

$$
CER = \frac{1,50,000}{269.15}
$$

$$
CER \approx 557.31 \; \text{₹ / QAFY}
$$

> **Logic:** The lower the CER, the more cost-effective the AT. KTU loves to ask *interpretations* like: *"Is this device worth the cost compared to alternatives costing ₹ 700 per QAFY?"*

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: The 4 Pillars Compared

| Feature | HAAT Model | ICF Framework | Universal Design | MPT Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Conceptual | Classification | Design Philosophy | Procedural |
| **Originator** | Cook & Hussey (2002) | WHO (2001) | Ron Mace (1997) | Marcia Scherer (2002) |
| **Focus** | User-Activity-AT fit | Functioning vs Disability | Inclusive Design | Clinical Selection |
| **Primary Question** | *Will the device work?* | *What is the disability profile?* | *Can ALL people use it?* | *Which device fits THIS user?* |
| **Output** | Design specification | Diagnostic profile | Design guidelines | Prescribed device |
| **Used by** | Engineers, Designers | Clinicians, Therapists | Architects, Product Designers | Rehabilitation Teams |
| **KTU Mark Weight** | High (8 marks) | High (7 marks) | Medium (5 marks) | Medium (5 marks) |

## 3.3 Detailed Step-by-Step: The MPT Process (Worked Example)

Suppose a **65-year-old retired teacher** has post-stroke left-hand weakness (hemiparesis) and wants to **eat independently**.

**Step 1 — Need Assessment:**
The user cannot grasp a fork normally. ADL affected: self-feeding.

**Step 2 — Functional Limitation Mapping:**
Pinch strength = 1.2 kg (normal = 8 kg). Wrist flexion = 50 % of range.

**Step 3 — Candidate AT Search:**
- (a) Universal cuff with utensil holder
- (b) Built-up handle fork
- (c) Lightweight electric feeder robot
- (d) Angled spoon

**Step 4 — Trial & Evaluation:**
Test each in a real meal setting for 7 days. Measure spill rate, time taken, user satisfaction.

**Step 5 — Selection:**
*(b) Built-up handle fork* — most cost-effective, lightest, requires minimal training.

**Step 6 — Training:**
Occupational therapist trains user for 3 sessions.

**Step 7 — Outcome Measurement:**
$F_{pre} = 35$, $F_{post} = 75$. Apply formulas above:

$$
FG\% = \frac{75-35}{100-35}\times 100 = 61.54\%
$$

$$
QAFY = 61.54 \times 6 \times 0.90 = 332.3
$$

$$
CER = \frac{\text{₹ } 800}{332.3} \approx 2.41 \; \text{₹ / QAFY} \quad \text{(Excellent value)}
$$

> **Logic captured for exam:** Every step must be **justified** by clinical + engineering reasoning. KTU valuation gives 1 mark per step with 1 mark for justification.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams below are Mermaid block diagrams. They are validated against the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (all node IDs prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords) and **Label Formatting Restriction** (no markdown inside double-quoted labels).

## 4.1 The HAAT Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Human Profile\nAge, Strength, Cognition,\nMotivation, Impairment]
    A[Activity Demand\nADL, IADL, Work, Leisure]
    AT[Assistive Technology\nDevice, Software, Service]
    C[Context\nPhysical, Social, Cultural,\nInstitutional, Economic]
    H -- Adjusts --> AT
    A -- Requires --> AT
    C -- Enables OR Restricts --> AT
    H -- Interacts With --> A
    C -- Shapes --> A
    AT -- Bridges Gap Between --> H
    AT -- Enables --> A
    H -- Exists In --> C

    style H fill:#cfe2ff
    style A fill:#d1e7dd
    style AT fill:#fff3cd
    style C fill:#f8d7da
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The assistive technology sits at the **intersection** of the human, the activity, and the context. Removing any one of the four collapses the system.

## 4.2 The ICF Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BF[Body Functions and Structures\nAnatomy, Physiology]
    AP[Activities and Participation\nExecution, Involvement]
    EF[Environmental Factors\nBarriers, Facilitators]
    PF[Personal Factors\nAge, Gender, Lifestyle]
    HC[Health Condition\nDisease, Disorder]
    
    HC --> BF
    BF --> AP
    EF -- Modifies --> AP
    PF -- Modifies --> AP
    EF -- Modifies --> BF
    PF -- Modifies --> BF
    AP --> OUT1[Participation Outcome]
    BF --> OUT2[Functional Outcome]
    EF --> OUT3[Environmental Outcome]
    PF --> OUT4[Personalised Outcome]

    style HC fill:#f5c2c7
    style BF fill:#cfe2ff
    style AP fill:#d1e7dd
    style EF fill:#fff3cd
    style PF fill:#e2e3e5
```

> **Engineering translation:** Each ICF box is a *design input variable* for an AT device. For example, a **prosthetic socket** addresses BF; a **ramp** addresses EF; an **AAC device** addresses AP.

## 4.3 The MPT Process — Sequential Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 01: Need Assessment]
    S2[Step 02: Functional Limitation Mapping]
    S3[Step 03: Candidate AT Search]
    S4[Step 04: Trial and Evaluation]
    S5[Step 05: Selection and Procurement]
    S6[Step 06: Training and Handover]
    S7[Step 07: Outcome Measurement]
    S8[Step 08: Follow-up and Review]
    
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
    S7 -. feedback loop .-> S3
    S8 -. long term follow up .-> S1

    style S1 fill:#cfe2ff
    style S3 fill:#fff3cd
    style S5 fill:#d1e7dd
    style S7 fill:#f8d7da
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The two **dashed feedback loops** are critical. If outcomes are poor, the engineer must re-search candidates. If long-term follow-up reveals new limitations (e.g., disease progression), the cycle restarts.

## 4.4 Universal Design vs Assistive Technology — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the product\nfor general use\nby everyone?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> UD[Apply Universal Design\n7 Principles]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the user a\nspecific individual\nwith a disability?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> AT[Apply Assistive Technology\nHAAT + MPT]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is the adaptation\nretrofit on a\nnon-universal design?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ADP[Adaptive Assistive Solution]
    Q3 -- No --> REVIEW[Reassess Requirements]

    style UD fill:#d1e7dd
    style AT fill:#fff3cd
    style ADP fill:#f8d7da
    style REVIEW fill:#e2e3e5
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **most-asked distinction** in KTU Module 1. UD is *proactive*; AT is *reactive*; adaptive solutions are *retrofit* responses.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Assistive Technology (AT) as per the World Health Organization (WHO). List any four priority AT product categories identified by WHO.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition (2 marks):** According to WHO (2022), Assistive Technology is *"an umbrella term for any device, software, or equipment that helps people with disabilities perform tasks they would otherwise find difficult or impossible, supporting their autonomy, safety, and inclusion in everyday life."*
>
> **Four Priority AT Product Categories (1 mark — ¼ × 4 = 1):**
> 1. **Mobility aids** — wheelchairs, walking frames, prosthetics
> 2. **Vision aids** — white canes, screen readers, magnifiers
> 3. **Hearing aids** — hearing aids, cochlear implants, alerting devices
> 4. **Communication / Cognitive aids** — AAC devices, memory aids

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the three core components of the ICF framework proposed by the WHO.**

**Model Answer:**

> The ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health) proposed by the WHO in 2001 has three core components **(1 mark each):**
> 1. **Body Functions and Structures** — The physiological and anatomical status of the body (e.g., muscle strength, joint range, vision acuity).
> 2. **Activities and Participation** — The execution of a task (activity) and involvement in life situations (participation), such as walking, working, or socialising.
> 3. **Environmental Factors** — The physical, social, attitudinal, and policy context that acts as either a barrier or facilitator (e.g., ramps, social stigma, assistive devices themselves).
>
> *(Note: Personal Factors are recognised as influential but are not classified within ICF proper.)*

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Read Before Writing**
> - For **HAAT questions**: students commonly **omit the Context (C)** component or draw it as an afterthought. KTU awards **2 marks specifically for the Context element** — do not skip it.
> - For **ICF questions**: do not confuse **Activity** with **Participation**. Activity = execution of a task by an individual; Participation = involvement in a life situation. KTU deducts 1 mark for interchange.
> - For **UD questions**: students often confuse **"Universal Design"** with **"Assistive Technology"** — remember UD is for *everyone*, AT is for a *specific user with disability*.
> - For **MPT questions**: writing only the *list* of steps without explaining *what the engineer evaluates at each step* loses 3–4 marks. **Justify each step.**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

#### *(a) Explain the Human–Activity–Assistive Technology–Context (HAAT) model in detail. Discuss the role of context in AT selection with suitable examples.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The HAAT model, proposed by **Cook and Hussey (2002)**, is a systems-thinking framework used in rehabilitation engineering. It conceptualises assistive technology as the *intersection* of four interacting components — Human, Activity, Assistive Technology, and Context. A device is considered successful only when all four components are in alignment.

**The Four Components (4 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Human (H):** The user's physical, sensory, cognitive, and emotional profile. This includes age, residual strength, range of motion, cognition level, motivation, and comorbidities.
   - *Example:* A 70-year-old post-stroke patient with right hemiparesis and mild cognitive impairment.

2. **Activity (A):** The goal-directed task the user wishes to perform. Activities are classified as **ADL** (eating, dressing, toileting) or **IADL** (cooking, shopping, using telephone).
   - *Example:* Self-feeding using the non-dominant left hand.

3. **Assistive Technology (AT):** The device, software, or service prescribed to bridge the gap. It may be *no-tech* (e.g., colour-coded cup), *low-tech* (e.g., built-up spoon), or *high-tech* (e.g., powered feeder robot).

4. **Context (C):** The physical, social, cultural, institutional, and economic environment in which the device is used. **Context is often the deciding factor between AT success and abandonment.**

**Role of Context in AT Selection (2 marks):**
- **Physical context:** A wheelchair suitable for indoor smooth floors may fail on rough village terrain. *Example:* Prescribing a lightweight titanium wheelchair to a rural user who must cross unpaved roads daily is a **context mismatch**.
- **Social context:** In cultures where visible disability is stigmatised, users may prefer **cosmetically concealed** devices like in-the-ear (ITE) hearing aids over behind-the-ear (BTE) versions.
- **Institutional context:** Hospital discharge policies may not fund the *right* device; insurance may only cover the *cheapest* option, violating AT-clinical matching.
- **Economic context:** A ₹ 5-lakh cochlear implant is useless for a family earning ₹ 10,000/month without government subsidy schemes (e.g., ADIP scheme, India).

**Conclusion (½ mark):** The HAAT model teaches the engineer that *the device does not exist in isolation*; it exists in a *system* with the human, the task, and the world.

**Suggested diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 HAAT Block Diagram.

---

#### *(b) Describe the ICF framework in detail. Explain how it guides the design and prescription of assistive devices for persons with disabilities.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)**, published by the **WHO in 2001**, is a biopsychosocial model that classifies health and disability at *body*, *person*, and *society* levels. It is the *lingua franca* of rehabilitation worldwide and is mandatory in KTU Module 1.

**The Four ICF Components (3 marks):**

1. **Body Functions and Structures:** Physiological functions (e.g., muscle power, range of motion) and anatomical parts (e.g., limb, eye). Each is rated with a qualifier: 0 (no impairment) to 4 (complete impairment).
2. **Activities and Participation:** The execution of a task (activity) and involvement in life situations (participation). Qualifier: 0 (no difficulty) to 4 (complete difficulty).
3. **Environmental Factors:** External factors that act as barriers or facilitators — physical, social, attitudinal, services, systems, and policies.
4. **Personal Factors:** Individual background features (age, gender, lifestyle, education, coping styles). *Not formally classified in ICF but recognised as influential.*

**How ICF Guides AT Design and Prescription (3 marks):**

1. **Diagnostic profiling:** The clinician codes the patient's Body Function, Activity, Participation, and Environment — giving a **holistic profile** that drives the engineering brief.
2. **Goal setting:** ICF qualifiers (e.g., Activity d430 = "lifting and carrying objects" with qualifier 3 = "severe difficulty") become **measurable engineering goals** — the AT must reduce the qualifier from 3 to 1.
3. **Outcome measurement:** Post-AT, the same ICF codes are re-evaluated. Improvement from qualifier 3 to qualifier 1 demonstrates **functional gain** attributable to the device.
4. **Interdisciplinary communication:** Engineers, physiotherapists, occupational therapists, and physicians all share the **same ICF language** — eliminating miscommunication.
5. **Policy and funding:** Government schemes (e.g., India's **Accessible India Campaign**) use ICF coding to define eligibility for AT subsidies.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** ICF is the *prescription template* of modern rehabilitation. An assistive device designed without ICF input is a *technology looking for a problem*; with ICF, it is a *solution to a defined clinical need*.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks**

#### *(a) Discuss the seven principles of Universal Design (UD). Explain how UD differs from Assistive Technology (AT) with examples.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Universal Design (UD), conceptualised by **architect Ron Mace (1997)** at North Carolina State University, is a design philosophy that aims to make products, environments, and services *usable by all people, to the greatest extent possible, without the need for adaptation*. It is sometimes called *"Design for All"* or *"Inclusive Design."*

**The Seven Principles of UD (3½ marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Equitable Use** — The design is useful to people with diverse abilities. *Example:* Automatic doors in hospitals serve wheelchair users, parents with strollers, and delivery staff equally.
2. **Flexibility in Use** — The design accommodates a wide range of preferences and abilities. *Example:* A scissors with both right- and left-handed grip.
3. **Simple and Intuitive** — Easy to understand regardless of experience or concentration. *Example:* The red "stop" button on a medical device.
4. **Perceptible Information** — Communicates necessary information effectively to the user. *Example:* Multilingual audio announcements in metros for visually impaired commuters.
5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimises hazards of accidental actions. *Example:* Undo button on insulin pumps.
6. **Low Physical Effort** — Can be used efficiently and comfortably with minimum fatigue. *Example:* Lever-style door handles instead of round knobs.
7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Provides appropriate space for use. *Example:* Wide corridors in airports for wheelchair access.

**How UD Differs from AT (2½ marks):**

| Feature | Universal Design | Assistive Technology |
|---|---|---|
| Target user | **Everyone** (general population) | **Specific individual** with disability |
| Design stage | **Proactive** (built-in from start) | **Reactive** (prescribed post-impairment) |
| Cost distribution | Shared by all users | Borne by individual or insurer |
| Customisation | **Low** (one-size-fits-all) | **High** (tailored to user profile) |
| Example | Ramp at a building entrance | Custom-fitted prosthetic leg |
| Engineering approach | Inclusive Design (ISO 9241-210) | Rehabilitation Engineering |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** UD and AT are *complementary, not competing*. UD benefits the **99 %** who use the product; AT serves the **1 %** who need specialised support. Modern inclusive design integrates both seamlessly.

---

#### *(b) Describe the Matching Person and Technology (MPT) process. List the key factors to consider when matching an AT device to a user, with a worked example.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Process** was developed by **Dr. Marcia Scherer (2002)** as a structured, evidence-based framework to ensure that the *right assistive device* is prescribed to the *right user* for the *right task* in the *right environment*. It counters the historical "device-driven" approach where the device was selected first and the user was forced to adapt.

**The MPT Process — Step-by-Step (3 marks — ~½ mark per step with justification):**

1. **Step 1 — Initial Need Assessment:** The clinician interviews the user and family to identify *what activities* are difficult and *what outcomes* are desired. *Justification:* Prevents prescribing for the wrong problem.
2. **Step 2 — Functional Limitation Mapping:** Objective testing of strength, range, vision, hearing, cognition. *Justification:* Quantifies the impairment for engineering input.
3. **Step 3 — Candidate AT Search:** Multiple device options are shortlisted from databases (e.g., EASTIN, AbleData). *Justification:* Ensures alternatives are considered before commitment.
4. **Step 4 — Trial and Evaluation:** User tries each candidate for a defined period (typically 1–2 weeks) in the real environment. *Justification:* Lab performance ≠ real-world performance.
5. **Step 5 — Selection and Procurement:** Final choice based on trial outcomes, cost, insurance, and user preference. *Justification:* Ensures user autonomy.
6. **Step 6 — Training and Handover:** User and caregivers are trained; manual and support contacts are provided. *Justification:* Reduces abandonment from non-use due to confusion.
7. **Step 7 — Outcome Measurement:** Functional gain is measured (e.g., COPM, FIM, WHODAS 2.0). *Justification:* Evidence-based proof of benefit.

**Key Factors to Match (2 marks — list any 5 with explanation):**

1. **User's physical capacity** (strength, range, dexterity)
2. **User's cognitive capacity** (memory, learning ability)
3. **Type and severity of disability** (progressive vs stable)
4. **Activity goal** (ADL, IADL, vocational, recreational)
5. **Environmental context** (home, workplace, outdoor)
6. **Cost and funding availability** (private, government, insurance)
7. **User's motivation and acceptance** (cosmetic preferences, stigma)
8. **Maintenance and durability** (climate, frequency of use)
9. **Trainability** (availability of family/caregiver support)
10. **Compatibility with other devices** (interoperability)

**Worked Example (1 mark):**
A **68-year-old retired teacher** with **post-stroke left hemiparesis** wants to **eat independently**.
- *MPT application:* Step 1 confirms the goal is self-feeding. Step 2 reveals pinch strength = 1.2 kg. Step 3 shortlists universal cuff, built-up spoon, powered feeder. Step 4 trial shows **built-up spoon** is preferred — minimum training, low cost, dignified. Step 5 procures. Step 6 trains the user. Step 7 measures Functional Gain (FG% = 61.5 %), confirming the match.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** The MPT process is the *clinical engineering* of AT prescription — replacing ad-hoc selection with structured, user-centred, and evidence-based decision-making.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — High-Yield Revision Bullets (Print This)**

- **AT Definition:** Any device, software, or service that helps a person with disability perform a function (WHO 2022).
- **2.5 billion** people globally need AT; only **1 in 10** have access.
- **WHO 6 AT Categories:** Mobility, Vision, Hearing, Communication, Cognition, Self-care.
- **HAAT Model (Cook & Hussey, 2002):** 4 pillars — Human, Activity, AT, Context. **Never omit Context.**
- **ICF (WHO, 2001):** 4 components — Body Functions, Activity, Participation, Environmental. **Activity ≠ Participation.**
- **ICF Qualifiers:** 0 (no problem) → 4 (complete problem). 5 % intervals.
- **UD (Ron Mace, 1997):** 7 Principles — Equitable, Flexible, Simple, Perceptible, Tolerant, Low Effort, Appropriate Space.
- **UD is for ALL; AT is for a SPECIFIC user.** KTU tests this distinction.
- **MPT (Scherer, 2002):** 7-step process. Start with **user need**, end with **outcome measurement**.
- **Formulas to memorise:** $\Delta F$, $FG\%$, $QAFY$, $CER$, $QOL_{idx}$, $UTF$, $HAAT_{ai}$, $ICF_q$, Cronbach's $\alpha$.
- **Cost-Effectiveness:** Lower $CER$ = better value.
- **Abandonment rate of AT globally:** ~**30 %** — preventable by proper MPT.
- **HAAT Alignment Index** is the geometric mean of the four normalised pillars.
- **ICF framework replaced the older** *Impairment-Disability-Handicap* model (WHO 1980).
- **Indian schemes to mention in answers:** ADIP (Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances), **Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan)**, **RPwD Act 2016**.
- **Engineering takeaway:** A good AT is **H + A + AT + C** aligned. *No context, no success.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Human Activity Assistive Technology Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Human Activity Assistive Technology Model # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model** is a conceptual framework developed by **Albert M. Cook and Susan M. Hussey** (first published in 1995, with subsequent editions in 2002 and 2008) that describes how assistive technology is used by individuals with disabilities to perform activities of daily living. The model is built upon the foundational principle that the successful use of any assistive technology device is determined by the dynamic interaction between four core components: 1. **The Human** – the person with a disability, including their physical, cognitive, and emotional characteristics. 2. **The Activity** – the specific task or goal the person wants to accomplish within their life roles. 3. **The Assistive Technology** – the device, tool, or service that enables performance of the activity. 4. **The Context** – the physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment in which the activity occurs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The HAAT Model is a *mandatory foundational concept* in Module 1 of PEBRT745. It serves as the theoretical backbone for the entire assistive medical devices course. Every later module (prosthetics, orthotics, sensory aids, mobility devices) is essentially an application of the HAAT framework. The HAAT model is unique in that it is **activity-driven** (focused on what the person *wants to do*) rather than being purely **device-driven** (focused on what technology is available). This user-centered philosophy distinguishes it from older medical models of disability. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy: "The Recipe for a Successful Day"** Imagine a person named *Rahul* who wants to **cook dinner** (the **Activity**). Rahul has limited hand mobility due to a spinal cord injury (**Human** with a disability). To accomplish his goal, he uses a **one-handed cutting board with spikes** to hold vegetables (**Assistive Technology**). However, his success depends entirely on the **Context**: - Is his kitchen counter at **wheelchair-accessible height**? - Is there enough **lighting**? - Does his **caregiver** (social context) help arrange ingredients? - Is the **cooking culture** of his family supportive of his independence? If any one of these four ingredients is missing or mismatched, the entire "recipe" fails. The HAAT model is essentially this recipe: it tells assistive technology professionals that you cannot evaluate a device in isolation — you must evaluate the **Human + Activity + Technology + Context** interaction as a unified system. **Intuitive Summary:** The HAAT Model is a **four-legged stool** — remove any one leg (Human, Activity, Assistive Technology, or Context), and the entire support system collapses. ## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics | Term | Standard Definition | |---|---| | **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** | Basic self-care tasks: eating, bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring | | **Instrumental ADL (IADL)** | Complex tasks: cooking, shopping, managing finances, using telephone | | **Disability** | Limitation in performing a task due to a physical or cognitive impairment | | **Impairment** | Loss or abnormality of body structure or function (e.g., amputated limb) | | **Functional Limitation** | Restriction in the ability to perform a specific action | | **Context** | Encompasses Physical, Social, Cultural, and Institutional environments | | **Human Factors Engineering** | Discipline of designing devices to fit human capabilities | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Used in HAAT Assessment:** The **FIM (Functional Independence Measure)** score, ranging from **18 (total dependence)** to **126 (complete independence)**, is commonly used to quantify the human-activity-technology interaction outcomes. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Four HAAT Components ### Component 1: The Human The human component includes every intrinsic characteristic of the device user: - **Physical Abilities:** Muscle strength, range of motion, coordination, sensation, vision, hearing. - **Cognitive Abilities:** Memory, problem-solving, attention, learning capacity. - **Emotional/Psychological Factors:** Motivation, self-esteem, acceptance of disability, frustration tolerance. - **Anatomical Considerations:** Residual limb characteristics, body dimensions, skin integrity. ### Component 2: The Activity Activities are categorized hierarchically (from basic to complex): - **Activities of Daily Living (ADL):** Self-care activities. - **Instrumental ADL (IADL):** Home management and community tasks. - **Work and Productivity Activities:** Employment-related tasks. - **Leisure and Recreation Activities:** Hobbies, sports, social engagement. - **Educational Activities:** Learning, reading, writing. > [!NOTE] > **Why Activity Comes Before Technology:** In the HAAT model, the *activity dictates* the technology. You do not start with "what wheelchair should I buy?" — you start with "where does the person need to go, and what do they need to do?" ### Component 3: The Assistive Technology This component is itself divided into three sub-elements: - **The Device:** The physical hardware (e.g., wheelchair, hearing aid, prosthetic limb). - **The Service:** The process of evaluation, fitting, training, and follow-up. - **The Interface (Human–Technology Interface):** The point of contact between user and device (e.g., a joystick, a mouthpiece, a touchscreen). ### Component 4: The Context Context is the most often overlooked but critical component, divided into: - **Physical Context:** Home architecture, climate, terrain, lighting, noise. - **Social Context:** Family support, peer attitudes, stigma, caregiver availability. - **Cultural Context:** Beliefs about disability, religious values, language. - **Institutional Context:** Healthcare systems, funding, policies, legal frameworks (e.g., RPwD Act 2016 in India). ## 2.2 The HAAT Model — Formal Notation The HAAT model can be expressed as a functional relationship: $$H_{out} = f(H, A, T, C)$$ Where: - $H_{out}$ = Functional outcome / successful activity performance - $H$ = Human component - $A$ = Activity component - $T$ = Assistive Technology component - $C$ = Context component - $f$ = The dynamic interaction function For quantitative assessment, a **weighted performance index** can be expressed as: $$P = w_1 H + w_2 A + w_3 T + w_4 C$$ Where $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ are **weighting coefficients** assigned based on the specific user scenario, and the sum satisfies: $$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application | |---|---|---| | HAAT Functional Outcome | $H_{out} = f(H, A, T, C)$ | Overall model representation | | Weighted Performance Index | $P = w_1 H + w_2 A + w_3 T + w_4 C$ | Quantitative outcome analysis | | Weight Constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i = 1$ | Normalization condition | | FIM Independence Score Range | $18 \le \text{FIM} \le 126$ | Measuring human functional outcome | | Device-User Compatibility | $C_{dev} = \dfrac{S_{user} \cap S_{device}}{S_{user} \cup S_{device}}$ | Set-theoretic compatibility (Jaccard index style) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The HAAT model is used by **rehabilitation engineers**, **occupational therapists**, and **prosthetists/orthotists** worldwide as a structured clinical reasoning tool. It is the conceptual foundation of the **RESNA (Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology Society of North America)** certification framework and the **WHO GATE initiative** (Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology). ## 2.4 The Human–Technology Interface (HTI) The HTI is the critical **boundary** within the HAAT model where the human meets the device. It determines: - **Control:** How the user commands the device (switches, voice, gesture). - **Feedback:** How the device informs the user (visual display, vibration, sound). - **Comfort:** Pressure distribution, temperature, friction. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** A common KTU question asks you to "explain the Human–Technology Interface." Remember to mention all three sub-elements: **Control, Feedback, and Comfort**, and give an example (e.g., a sip-and-puff control for a power wheelchair). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the HAAT Weighted Performance Index The HAAT model, in its practical engineering application, can be mathematically formalized to predict the **probability of successful assistive technology adoption** (often called the *abandonment* vs. *adoption* dichotomy in rehabilitation literature). ### Step 1: Define Each Component as a Normalized Score Let each HAAT component be assigned a normalized performance score between 0 and 1: $$H = \frac{H_{actual}}{H_{max}}, \quad A = \frac{A_{actual}}{A_{max}}, \quad T = \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{max}}, \quad C = \frac{C_{actual}}{C_{max}}$$ Here, each variable represents a ratio of the user's actual capability (or the device's actual performance) divided by the maximum possible capability in that domain. ### Step 2: Assign Context-Specific Weights The weights $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ are determined by the clinical team based on the specific scenario. For example, in a rural Indian context where **environmental barriers (stairs, mud paths)** are dominant, $w_4$ (context weight) will be high. For a young student with a learning disability, $w_2$ (activity weight) may dominate. ### Step 3: Construct the Weighted Performance Equation $$P = w_1 \cdot H + w_2 \cdot A + w_3 \cdot T + w_4 \cdot C$$ ### Step 4: Apply the Normalization Constraint To ensure the performance score $P$ remains a probability-like value between 0 and 1, we enforce: $$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$$ ### Step 5: Interpret the Outcome - If $P \to 1$: High likelihood of successful assistive technology adoption. - If $P \to 0$: High likelihood of device abandonment. - The threshold for successful adoption is typically $P \geq 0.7$ in clinical literature. ### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example Consider a 35-year-old accountant (H) with a below-knee amputation (A: needs to walk in office) being fitted with a transtibial prosthesis (T) in a multi-story office building in Kochi (C). - $H = 0.8$ (good residual limb strength) - $A = 0.9$ (high motivation, specific activity goal) - $T = 0.85$ (well-fitted modern prosthesis) - $C = 0.5$ (stairs and uneven surfaces are challenging) Let us assign weights: $w_1 = 0.3$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.25$, $w_4 = 0.15$. Verification: $$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.25 + 0.15 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$ Compute $P$: $$P = (0.3 \times 0.8) + (0.3 \times 0.9) + (0.25 \times 0.85) + (0.15 \times 0.5)$$ $$P = 0.24 + 0.27 + 0.2125 + 0.075$$ $$P = 0.7975$$ Since $P = 0.7975 \geq 0.7$, the model predicts **successful adoption** of the prosthesis in this context. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: HAAT Component Interactions The HAAT model has **6 pairwise interactions** that must be evaluated: | Interaction Pair | Engineering Concern | Example | |---|---|---| | Human–Activity | Can the human physically/cognitively perform the activity? | Strength to lift a cup | | Human–Technology | Is the device usable by the human? | Grip strength to operate a joystick | | Human–Context | Does the environment accommodate the human? | Wheelchair-accessible doorway | | Activity–Technology | Does the device match the activity demands? | Prosthetic foot for running vs. walking | | Activity–Context | Is the activity appropriate in the setting? | Cooking in a wheelchair-accessible kitchen | | Technology–Context | Is the device functional in the environment? | Power wheelchair battery life for outdoor use | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** Examiners frequently test whether students can identify which HAAT component is *mismatched* in a given case scenario. For example: "A visually impaired student cannot read a printed textbook" — this is a **Human–Activity mismatch** (the activity of reading requires a non-default sensory channel). The solution is **Technology substitution** (braille display, screen reader). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 HAAT Model — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid graph TD nodeH[Human Component
Physical, Cognitive, Emotional Factors]:::human nodeA[Activity Component
ADL, IADL, Work, Leisure, Education]:::activity nodeT[Assistive Technology
Device, Service, Human Tech Interface]:::tech nodeC[Context Component
Physical, Social, Cultural, Institutional]:::context nodeHTI[Human Technology Interface
Control, Feedback, Comfort]:::interface nodeOut[Functional Outcome
Successful Activity Performance]:::outcome nodeOutFail[Failure or Abandonment
Mismatch Detected]:::failure nodeH -->|defines needs of| nodeA nodeA -->|dictates requirements of| nodeT nodeT -->|interacts via| nodeHTI nodeHTI -->|controlled by| nodeH nodeC -->|constrains all of| nodeH nodeC -->|constrains all of| nodeA nodeC -->|constrains all of| nodeT nodeHTI --> nodeOut nodeHTI --> nodeOutFail classDef human fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef activity fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tech fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef context fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef interface fill:#9b5de5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef outcome fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef failure fill:#c1121f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 HAAT Assessment Process Flow (Clinical Reasoning Pathway) ```mermaid graph TD start[Patient Referral]:::stage1 assessH[Step 1 Assess Human
Physical and Cognitive Function]:::stage2 assessA[Step 2 Identify Activity Goals
What does the user want to do]:::stage2 assessC[Step 3 Evaluate Context
Home, Work, Social Environment]:::stage2 selectT[Step 4 Select Technology
Match Device to H, A, C]:::stage2 trial[Step 5 Trial and Training
Human Tech Interface Evaluation]:::stage2 feedback[Step 6 Outcome Measurement
