FIM Score and User Satisfaction]:::stage2 adopt{Adoption Successful}:::decision followup[Follow Up and Modification]:::stage1 restart[Reassess and Iterate]:::stage1 start --> assessH assessH --> assessA assessA --> assessC assessC --> selectT selectT --> trial trial --> feedback feedback --> adopt adopt -->|Yes| followup adopt -->|No| restart restart --> assessH classDef stage1 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef stage2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchy of Activity Domains in HAAT ```mermaid graph TD root[All Human Activities]:::root adl[ADL
Eating, Bathing, Dressing]:::level1 iadl[IADL
Cooking, Shopping, Telephoning]:::level1 work[Work and Education
Reading, Writing, Computing]:::level1 leisure[Leisure and Recreation
Sports, Hobbies, Socializing]:::level1 root --> adl root --> iadl root --> work root --> leisure adl --> subADL1[Mobility and Transfers] adl --> subADL2[Self-Care Tasks] adl --> subADL3[Toileting and Continence] iadl --> subIADL1[Home Management] iadl --> subIADL2[Community Mobility] iadl --> subIADL3[Financial Management] work --> subWork1[Classroom Participation] work --> subWork2[Workplace Ergonomics] work --> subWork3[Communication Tasks] leisure --> subLeisure1[Adapted Sports] leisure --> subLeisure2[Arts and Crafts] leisure --> subLeisure3[Social Communication] classDef root fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef level1 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef subADL1 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef subADL2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef subADL3 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef subIADL1 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef subIADL2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef subIADL3 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef subWork1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef subWork2 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef subWork3 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef subLeisure1 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,color:#fff classDef subLeisure2 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,color:#fff classDef subLeisure3 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model and list its four components.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model** is a conceptual framework that describes the dynamic interaction between a person with a disability, the activities they wish to perform, the assistive technology used, and the context in which the activity occurs. The four components are: 1. **The Human** – the person with physical, cognitive, and emotional characteristics. 2. **The Activity** – the goal-directed task the person wants to accomplish. 3. **The Assistive Technology** – the device, service, and human–technology interface. 4. **The Context** – the physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment. (Each component carries 0.5 marks; framework definition carries 1 mark = 3 marks total) ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain the Human–Technology Interface (HTI) with an example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Human–Technology Interface (HTI)** is the boundary where the user interacts with the assistive device. It has three elements: 1. **Control:** How the user commands the device (e.g., sip-and-puff switch for a power wheelchair). 2. **Feedback:** How the device communicates status to the user (e.g., vibration alert on a hearing aid). 3. **Comfort:** Physical tolerance of the interface (e.g., pressure distribution in a prosthetic socket). (Example = 1 mark; three elements = 2 marks = 3 marks total) ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question] **(a)** Explain in detail the four components of the Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of the **Context** component in the HAAT model. Why is context often considered the most overlooked but critical factor in assistive technology prescription? Illustrate with two real-world examples. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **The four components of the HAAT Model are:** **1. The Human (2 Marks):** The human component refers to the person who uses the assistive technology. It includes: - **Physical abilities:** muscle strength, range of motion, sensation. - **Cognitive abilities:** memory, learning, judgment. - **Emotional factors:** motivation, self-esteem, acceptance of disability. - *Example:* A 25-year-old man with a C6 spinal cord injury has preserved shoulder and elbow function but no hand function — this determines which assistive devices are suitable. **2. The Activity (2 Marks):** The activity is the specific task the person wants to perform. Activities range from basic ADL (eating, dressing) to complex IADL (cooking, working) to leisure. - *Example:* The activity of "writing an exam" requires different technology than "driving a car." **3. The Assistive Technology (2 Marks):** This includes the **device** (e.g., wheelchair), the **service** (assessment, training, follow-up), and the **Human–Technology Interface** (control mechanism). - *Example:* A power wheelchair is the device; the joystick is the HTI; the service includes fitting and training. **4. The Context (1 Mark):** The environment in which the activity occurs — physical, social, cultural, institutional. #### Model Solution for (b): **The Role of Context in the HAAT Model (7 Marks):** Context refers to the **environment surrounding the human, activity, and technology**. It is divided into four sub-types: - **Physical Context:** Architecture, terrain, climate, lighting, noise. - **Social Context:** Family support, peer attitudes, caregiver availability. - **Cultural Context:** Beliefs about disability, religious practices, language. - **Institutional Context:** Healthcare policies, insurance, government schemes (e.g., ADIP scheme in India). **Why Context is Overlooked (3 Marks):** 1. Most engineers and clinicians focus on the *device* (technology) and the *user* (human) but forget that the same device can succeed in one setting and fail in another. 2. Context is **invisible during clinic-based assessments** — a wheelchair tested on smooth hospital floors may not work in a rural home with mud floors and narrow doorways. 3. Cultural stigma (e.g., visible assistive devices causing social embarrassment) can lead to abandonment even when the technology is technically perfect. **Real-World Examples (2 Marks):** *Example 1:* A **power wheelchair** prescribed in a Tier-1 city apartment (smooth floors, lifts) works perfectly. The same wheelchair in a **rural Kerala village** with monsoon mud paths and no ramped entrances becomes unusable — here, the *physical context* dominates. *Example 2:* A **myoelectric prosthetic hand** fitted for a young Indian college student may be rejected not because of technical failure, but because of *cultural context* — the family perceives a robotic-looking device as socially conspicuous, leading to abandonment. A **cosmetic (passive) hand** with similar function may be preferred. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Describe the Human–Technology Interface (HTI) in detail. Explain how control, feedback, and comfort determine the success of an assistive device. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 40-year-old school teacher with progressive multiple sclerosis wants to continue teaching. Apply the HAAT model to recommend an integrated assistive technology solution. Justify each component of your recommendation. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **The Human–Technology Interface (HTI) (7 Marks):** The HTI is the **physical and informational boundary** where the human and the assistive device meet. Its three sub-elements are: **1. Control (3 Marks):** - *Definition:* The mechanism by which the user sends commands to the device. - *Examples:* Joystick (power wheelchair), sip-and-puff switch (high-level SCI users), voice recognition (smart home), head-tracking, eye-gaze systems. - *Engineering considerations:* The control must be matched to the user's **reliable motor capacity**. If the user has tremors, a dead-man switch with a heavy joystick is unsuitable; a sip-and-puff may be better. - *Why it matters:* If control is too physically demanding, the user will abandon the device regardless of its sophistication. **2. Feedback (2 Marks):** - *Definition:* The information the device sends back to the user about its status. - *Examples:* Battery LED indicators, auditory beeps, vibration alerts, screen displays. - *Why it matters:* Without feedback, the user cannot tell if their command was received or if the device is functioning correctly. **3. Comfort (2 Marks):** - *Definition:* The physical tolerance of the interface over extended use. - *Examples:* Cushion pressure distribution, socket fit in prosthetics, skin integrity under orthotics. - *Why it matters:* Pressure sores, skin breakdown, and fatigue cause abandonment in 30% of mobility aid users. #### Model Solution for (b): **HAAT Application to a Teacher with MS (7 Marks):** **Human Component (1.5 Marks):** The teacher has progressive MS with: - Hand weakness and tremor (limits typing, writing). - Possible visual fatigue (screen use). - Preserved cognition, speech, and teaching knowledge. - High motivation to continue professional identity. **Activity Component (1.5 Marks):** The goal is **teaching** — which includes: - Delivering lectures (standing/moving in class). - Writing on the board. - Marking student papers. - Using a computer for presentations. **Assistive Technology Recommendation (2.5 Marks):** - **Powered sit-to-stand wheelchair** for classroom mobility and lecture delivery without fatigue. - **Speech-to-text software** (e.g., Dragon NaturallySpeaking) for marking and writing. - **Interactive smartboard** to replace handwriting on the board. - **Ergonomic adaptive seating** with pressure relief cushion for prolonged sitting. - **Voice amplification system** to compensate for weak voice projection. **Context Component (1.5 Marks):** - The **physical context** of the school — verify ramps, lift access, accessible washroom. - The **social context** — sensitize colleagues and students to avoid stigma. - The **institutional context** — apply for ADIP scheme funding and disability leave concessions. - The **cultural context** — preserve the teacher's dignity and authority in the classroom. **Justification (Justified HTI reasoning):** Each recommended device uses a control input matched to the user's *remaining* capacity (voice, head motion) — perfectly demonstrating the **HAAT principle of matching Human capability with Technology interface**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write only about the *device* (e.g., "wheelchair") and forget the **service** and **HTI** sub-components of the technology element. Always mention all three. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Treating "Context" as merely the physical environment. Remember: **Context = Physical + Social + Cultural + Institutional**. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Skipping the **Human–Technology Interface** in long answers. The HTI is a **3-mark sub-question in itself** if asked separately. Always draw a block diagram showing H, A, T, C interactions. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Not giving an *Indian context example* (e.g., ADIP scheme, RPwD Act 2016, Kerala State initiatives). Examiners reward regionally relevant examples. > - **Mark Loss Rule:** A diagram of the HAAT model is **mandatory** for any 7-mark question. Omitting the diagram = loss of 1–2 marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — HAAT Model (Module 1)** - ✅ **HAAT Model = Human + Activity + Assistive Technology + Context** (Cook & Hussey, 1995). - ✅ The model is **activity-driven**, not device-driven. - ✅ **Assistive Technology = Device + Service + Human–Technology Interface (HTI)**. - ✅ **HTI = Control + Feedback + Comfort**. - ✅ **Context = Physical + Social + Cultural + Institutional**. - ✅ Activities are hierarchical: **ADL → IADL → Work → Leisure → Education**. - ✅ Quantitative outcome measure: **FIM Score (18–126)**. - ✅ Weighted performance formula: $P = w_1 H + w_2 A + w_3 T + w_4 C$, with $\sum w_i = 1$. - ✅ Adoption threshold: $P \geq 0.7$ predicts successful device adoption. - ✅ The model has **6 pairwise interactions** that must be evaluated for a complete assessment. - ✅ Real-world application frameworks: **RESNA certification, WHO GATE initiative, India's ADIP scheme, RPwD Act 2016**. - ✅ The model is **iterative** — failed trials must trigger reassessment, not device prescription. - ✅ Remember to **draw the HAAT block diagram** in every long-answer question — examiners explicitly mark for it. - ✅ Common device examples to remember: **Wheelchair, Hearing aid, Prosthetic limb, Screen reader, Smart cane, Sip-and-puff control, Adaptive cutlery**.

topics.title: Foundational Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Foundational Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Assistive Medical Device?

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assistive Medical Device (AMD):** Any external instrument, apparatus, implant, software, material, or other article — including accessories and software intended by the manufacturer to be used specifically for diagnostic, therapeutic, monitoring, or compensatory purposes in the management of physical, sensory, cognitive, or communication impairments — whose primary intent is to **maintain, improve, or substitute** an individual's functional capacity and enable participation in daily life activities.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, an assistive medical device is differentiated from a *general* medical device by its **functional-restoration intent** rather than its diagnostic/curative intent.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Think of an assistive medical device as a **"functional prosthetic for capability."** Just as a pair of glasses is a *prosthesis for the eye* (it does not cure myopia, but it restores the lost visual function to near-normal levels), a wheelchair is a *prosthesis for ambulation*, and a cochlear implant is a *prosthesis for auditory transduction*. The device does not heal the underlying impairment — it **bridges the gap** between the user's residual biological capacity and the functional demands of their environment.

### Related Standardized Terminology (WHO ICF Framework)

| Term | Standard Meaning (WHO ICF) |
|------|---------------------------|
| **Impairment** | A loss or abnormality of body structure or physiological function (e.g., loss of a limb, low vision). |
| **Activity Limitation** | Difficulties an individual may have in executing tasks or actions (e.g., walking, reading). |
| **Participation Restriction** | Problems an individual may experience in involvement in life situations (e.g., employment, education). |
| **Disability** | An umbrella term for impairments, activity limitations, and participation restrictions. |
| **Handicap** | An outdated term — replaced by *participation restriction* in modern WHO nomenclature. |
| **Assistive Technology (AT)** | Any product, device, equipment, or service used to increase, maintain, or improve functional capabilities. |

### High-Yield Statistical Anchor (KTU 2024 Context)
> [!NOTE]
> According to the WHO/UNICEF **Global Report on Assistive Technology (2022)**, approximately **2.5 billion** people globally need at least one assistive product, yet only **10%** have access. This **access gap of ~90%** is the central engineering problem that the AMD discipline seeks to solve.

### The "Three Core Functions" of an AMD (Universal Triad)
Every assistive device, regardless of complexity, performs one or more of these three functional roles:

1. **Compensate** — Replace a missing or non-functional biological capability (e.g., prosthetic limb, eyeglasses).
2. **Augment** — Enhance an existing but deficient capability (e.g., hearing aid, screen reader for low vision).
3. **Adapt** — Modify the environment or task so the user's residual capability becomes sufficient (e.g., grab bars, ramp access, voice-controlled smart-home hub).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of Assistive Medical Devices

The KTU 2024 syllabus organizes assistive medical devices along **five orthogonal axes**. Mastering these axes is the single highest-yield topic in Module 1 for both descriptive and design-oriented exam questions.

### Axis A — By Functional Domain (ISO 9999:2022)
ISO 9999 is the international standard classification of assistive products. The major divisions are:

| ISO 9999 Class | Functional Domain | Example Devices |
|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **04** | Assistive products for **personal medical treatment** | Insulin pumps, nebulizers |
| **05** | Assistive products for **training in skills** | Braille training devices |
| **06** | Orthoses and **prostheses** | Artificial limbs, knee braces |
| **09** | Assistive products for **personal care and safety** | Bath boards, grab rails |
| **12** | Assistive products for **personal mobility** | Wheelchairs, walkers, crutches |
| **15** | Assistive products for **domestic activities** | Adapted kitchen utensils |
| **22** | Assistive products for **communication and information** | Screen readers, AAC devices |
| **24** | Assistive products for **manipulation** | Adapted handles, robotic arms |
| **27** | Assistive products for **environmental improvement** | Air purifiers, lighting controls |

### Axis B — By User Population
- **Paediatric** — designed for children (growth-adjusted sizing, e.g., paediatric prosthetics).
- **Geriatric** — designed for elderly users (low-effort interfaces, e.g., jar openers).
- **Vocational** — workplace-specific (e.g., sit-stand desks, vibration-dampening gloves).
- **Acute / Temporary** — short-term recovery (e.g., post-surgical knee immobilizer).
- **Chronic / Permanent** — lifelong use (e.g., cochlear implant, myoelectric hand).

### Axis C — By Regulatory Risk Class (FDA & MDR-EU)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **risk-based classification** of a device determines its regulatory pathway, design controls, and clinical evidence requirements. This is *guaranteed* to appear as a 3-mark short question in KTU ESE.

| Risk Class | Risk Level | Example AMD | Regulatory Pathway |
|------------|------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Class I** | Low risk | Walking stick, reacher | Self-declaration / 510(k) exempt |
| **Class II** | Moderate risk | Powered wheelchair, hearing aid | 510(k) premarket notification |
| **Class III** | High risk | Implantable cochlear, ventricular assist | Premarket Approval (PMA) |

### Axis D — By Technology Tier (WHO Essential List)
The WHO publishes the **Priority Assistive Products List (APL)** with 50 essential items, categorized by technology readiness:

1. **Tier 1 — Basic / Low-tech:** Crutches, white canes, magnifiers, simple prosthetics.
2. **Tier 2 — Mid-tech:** Powered wheelchairs, digital hearing aids, AAC tablets.
3. **Tier 3 — High-tech:** Brain-computer interfaces, myoelectric prosthetic hands, smart exoskeletons.

### Axis E — By Body-System Target
- **Sensory** — vision, hearing, tactile
- **Musculoskeletal** — orthopedic, mobility
- **Neurological** — neuroprosthetics, FES
- **Cardiopulmonary** — ventilators, oxygen concentrators
- **Cognitive / Communicative** — memory aids, AAC

---

## 2.2 The Universal Design Principles (Foundational Design Heuristic)

The **Seven Principles of Universal Design** (Ron Mace, NC State University, 1997) form the philosophical backbone of AMD design. Every KTU design question expects you to reference these.

| # | Principle | AMD Application Example |
|---|-----------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Equitable Use | Ramps usable by all users regardless of mobility status |
| 2 | Flexibility in Use | Adjustable-height wheelchair for child or adult |
| 3 | Simple and Intuitive | Single-button fall-alert pendant for elderly |
| 4 | Perceptible Information | Tactile + audio + visual cues in a fire alarm |
| 5 | Tolerance for Error | Pill dispenser that locks if wrong compartment opened |
| 6 | Low Physical Effort | Lever-style door handle instead of knob |
| 7 | Size and Space | Wide doorways accommodating wheelchairs |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Foundational Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Although Module 1 is largely conceptual, several quantitative metrics appear in KTU ESE numerical/short-answer questions. Memorize this table.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula or Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Accessibility Ratio (AR)** | $AR = \dfrac{N_{\text{accessible}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of users who can effectively use the device. Higher is better. |
| 2 | **Device Affordability Index (DAI)** | $DAI = \dfrac{\text{Minimum monthly wage of target population}}{\text{Unit price of device}}$ | Must be $\leq 1$ for affordability. |
| 3 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{\text{total operation}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Hours; reliability metric. |
| 4 | **Usability Score (SUS range)** | $SUS \in [0, 100]$ from 10-item Likert questionnaire | Score $> 68$ = above-average usability. |
| 5 | **Effective Functional Gain (EFG)** | $EFG = C_{\text{with device}} - C_{\text{without device}}$ | $C$ = functional capacity score (e.g., Barthel Index). |
| 6 | **Risk Classification Score (FDA)** | $R = P \times S$ where $P$ = probability, $S$ = severity | Categorical → Class I/II/III. |
| 7 | **Battery Endurance (BE)** | $BE = \dfrac{E_{\text{stored}} \text{ (Wh)}}{P_{\text{avg load}} \text{ (W)}}$ | Hours; critical for powered wheelchairs. |
| 8 | **Coverage Gap (WHO metric)** | $Gap = 1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{access}}}{N_{\text{need}}}$ | Current global value $\approx 0.90$. |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Foundational concepts are **not abstract philosophy** — they directly determine:
- **Design Control** documents required for FDA/CE submission.
- **Reimbursement codes** (e.g., HCPCS L-codes in the US, NHS tariff codes in the UK).
- **Procurement decisions** by ministries of health for national AT programs.
- **Clinical appropriateness** during patient-device matching by rehabilitation professionals.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "design an AMD for X," your first paragraph must include a *foundational classification* (which ISO 9999 class, which risk class, which user population) **before** you begin the technical design. This is the #1 differentiator between a 12-mark and a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Risk Classification of an AMD (FDA Framework)

**Problem:** A new **myoelectric upper-limb prosthetic** with embedded force sensors and machine-learning-based grip-pattern selection is to be classified. Determine its FDA risk class and justify the regulatory pathway required.

### Step 1 — Identify the device's intended use and failure consequences
The device is **bodily-attached** (worn for ~16 hours/day) and an **implantable component** may interface with the residual limb via osseointegration in some designs. Failure modes include:
- Erroneous grip force → object damage or user injury.
- Battery thermal runaway → burn injury.
- Sensor misinterpretation → drop of held object.

### Step 2 — Map to FDA risk dimensions
The FDA uses the product code and the predicate-device comparison. For myoelectric limbs, the relevant product code is **IQZ** (Powered external limb orthosis / prosthesis).

### Step 3 — Apply the risk formula
$$
R = P \times S
$$

where $P$ = probability of failure, $S$ = severity of harm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{software bug in ML grip}} &\approx 0.01 \text{ per use-hour (low–medium)} \\
S_{\text{drop of hot beverage}} &\approx 3 \text{ (moderate injury on FDA scale 1–4)} \\
R_{\text{software}} &= 0.01 \times 3 = 0.03 \text{ (moderate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{battery thermal event}} &\approx 10^{-6} \text{ (rare with Li-ion BMS)} \\
S_{\text{thermal burn}} &\approx 4 \text{ (severe injury)} \\
R_{\text{battery}} &= 10^{-6} \times 4 = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ (low absolute, but catastrophic potential)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion
Because the device is **life-sustaining or life-supporting in some use-cases** (e.g., preventing fall in a user who lacks natural grasp) **and** has a non-trivial probability of serious injury from software failure, the FDA designates it as:

$$
\boxed{\text{Class II (with possible Class III subset for implantable variants)} \Rightarrow \text{510(k) or PMA pathway required}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Coverage Gap Analysis for Hearing Aids in Kerala

**Problem:** As per the 2011 Census (with subsequent updates), Kerala has a **hearing-impaired population of approximately 4.4 lakh** (440,000 individuals). The state currently distributes **~6,000 government-funded digital hearing aids per year**. Compute the coverage gap and interpret it for a KTU Module-1 context question.

### Step 1 — Define the Coverage Gap metric
$$
\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{N_{\text{access}}}{N_{\text{need}}}
$$

$$
\text{Gap} = 1 - \text{Coverage}
$$

### Step 2 — Plug in the numbers
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{need}} &= 440{,}000 \\
N_{\text{access (annual dist.)}} &= 6{,}000 \\
\text{Coverage (annual)} &= \dfrac{6{,}000}{440{,}000} = 0.01364 = 1.36\% \\
\text{Gap (annual)} &= 1 - 0.01364 = 0.9864 = 98.64\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Adjust for device lifetime
A hearing aid's useful life is approximately **5 years**, so the *steady-state* need replacement pool is:

$$
N_{\text{need, steady}} = \dfrac{440{,}000}{5} = 88{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Coverage}_{\text{steady}} &= \dfrac{6{,}000}{88{,}000} = 0.0682 = 6.82\% \\
\text{Gap}_{\text{steady}} &= 93.18\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering interpretation
$$
\boxed{\text{Gap}_{\text{steady}} \approx 93\% \Rightarrow \text{Massive unmet need; justifies low-cost, locally-manufactured AMD design projects.}}
$$

This is the type of foundational numerical literacy that earns full marks in KTU 14-mark design questions.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Mapping an AMD to the WHO ICF Framework

**Problem:** A 62-year-old post-stroke patient has right-sided hemiparesis. The rehabilitation team prescribes a **four-wheeled walker with forearm troughs**. Map this prescription to the WHO ICF framework.

### Step 1 — Identify the impairment
$$
\text{Impairment} = \text{Right hemiparesis (loss of motor function, ICF code b730)}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the activity limitation
$$
\text{Activity limitation} = \text{Difficulty walking (ICF code d450)}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the participation restriction
$$
\text{Participation restriction} = \text{Unable to shop independently (ICF code d920)}
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the environmental factor (the AMD)
$$
\text{Environmental facilitator} = \text{Wheeled walker with forearm troughs (ICF code e1201)}
$$

### Step 5 — Synthesize the intervention logic
$$
\text{Impairment (b730)} \xrightarrow{\text{+e1201 (walker)}} \text{Reduced activity limitation (d450)} \xrightarrow{\text{enables}} \text{Restored participation (d920)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot-Score:** A complete ICF mapping of a device in your answer demonstrates biomedical-engineering maturity and routinely upgrades a Part-B answer from 10 to 14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — WHO ICF Framework with AMD Intervention

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **WHO International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** and shows where an AMD plugs into the model.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Health Condition / Disease"] --> nodeB["Body Functions and Structures (Impairment)"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Activity Limitations"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Participation Restrictions"]
    nodeE["Contextual Factors: Environmental and Personal"] --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeE --> nodeD
    nodeF["AMD Intervention Point: Compensate, Augment, Adapt"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeF -.-> nodeC
    nodeF -.-> nodeD
    nodeG["Outcome: Functional Capacity Restored and Quality of Life Improved"] --> nodeC
    nodeG --> nodeD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** show the natural causal chain of disability.
- The **dashed arrows from the AMD block** show the three intervention points at which assistive technology can interrupt the disability cascade.
- The **Contextual Factors** block represents the personal and environmental moderators (family support, architecture, stigma, climate, GDP per capita).

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification — ISO 9999 Major Divisions

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["ISO 9999: Classification of Assistive Products"]
    rootA --> n04["Class 04: Personal Medical Treatment"]
    rootA --> n05["Class 05: Training in Skills"]
    rootA --> n06["Class 06: Orthoses and Prostheses"]
    rootA --> n09["Class 09: Personal Care and Safety"]
    rootA --> n12["Class 12: Personal Mobility"]
    rootA --> n15["Class 15: Domestic Activities"]
    rootA --> n22["Class 22: Communication and Information"]
    rootA --> n24["Class 24: Manipulation"]
    rootA --> n27["Class 27: Environmental Improvement"]
    n12 --> n12a["Example: Manual Wheelchair"]
    n12 --> n12b["Example: Powered Wheelchair"]
    n12 --> n12c["Example: Walking Frame"]
    n22 --> n22a["Example: Screen Reader"]
    n22 --> n22b["Example: AAC Device"]
    n22 --> n22c["Example: Braille Display"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Regulatory Risk Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["Step 1: Identify Intended Use and Patient Contact Duration"] --> stepB["Step 2: Apply FDA Risk Matrix R = P times S"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 3: Determine Risk Class I, II, or III"]
    stepC --> stepDlow["If Class I: General Controls Only"]
    stepC --> stepDmid["If Class II: 510k Premarket Notification and Special Controls"]
    stepC --> stepDhigh["If Class III: Premarket Approval PMA with Clinical Trials"]
    stepDlow --> stepE["Step 4: ISO 13485 Design Controls"]
    stepDmid --> stepE
    stepDhigh --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF["Step 5: Post Market Surveillance and MDR Reporting"]
```

---

## 4.4 Stakeholder Map — AMD Ecosystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    user["End User (Patient or Caregiver)"]
    clinician["Clinician (Physiotherapist, OT, Prosthetist)"]
    designer["Biomedical Design Engineer"]
    manufacturer["Manufacturer and Quality Team"]
    regulator["Regulatory Body (FDA, CDSCO, CE)"]
    payer["Payer (Government, Insurance, User)"]
    researcher["Academic Researcher"]
    user <--> clinician
    clinician --> designer
    designer --> manufacturer
    manufacturer --> regulator
    regulator --> manufacturer
    payer --> user
    payer --> manufacturer
    researcher --> designer
    researcher --> clinician
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pedagogical Note:** The triangular relationship **User ↔ Clinician ↔ Designer** is the *minimum viable team* for any AMD project. If any one of these three is missing from your exam case study answer, you have described an incomplete product development cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Assistive Medical Device and distinguish it from a general medical device.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Assistive Medical Device (AMD)** is an external instrument, apparatus, software, or implant whose **primary intent** is to **maintain, improve, or substitute** an individual's functional capacity so as to enable participation in activities of daily life.

**Key distinctions from a general medical device:**

| Aspect | General Medical Device | Assistive Medical Device |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Primary intent | Diagnose, cure, treat, prevent disease | Restore or augment human function |
| Outcome measure | Clinical cure, biomarker change | Functional capacity, quality of life, participation |
| User role | Often passive recipient | Active operator of the device |
| Duration of use | Episodic (during treatment) | Often lifelong or chronic |
| Examples | MRI scanner, infusion pump, scalpel | Wheelchair, hearing aid, screen reader, prosthetic limb |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for distinction table.)*

---

### Question 2: State the **Seven Principles of Universal Design** with one AMD-relevant example for each.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Principle | AMD Example |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Equitable Use | Ramp usable by wheelchair users and parents with strollers |
| 2 | Flexibility in Use | Adjustable-height walking frame for variable user height |
| 3 | Simple and Intuitive Use | Single-button SOS wristband for elderly |
| 4 | Perceptible Information | Multi-modal fire alarm (audio + visual + tactile) |
| 5 | Tolerance for Error | Insulin pen that locks if dialed outside prescribed dose |
| 6 | Low Physical Effort | Lever door handle instead of round knob |
| 7 | Size and Space for Approach and Use | Wide bathroom door for wheelchair turning radius |

*(Award 0.5 mark per principle with a correct example.)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each, with internal choice (OR option). Each part (a) carries 7 marks and part (b) carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Classify the following six devices according to (i) ISO 9999 functional class, (ii) FDA risk class, and (iii) WHO technology tier. Justify each classification.  *(7 marks)*

1. Manual walking stick
2. Digital hearing aid
3. Myoelectric prosthetic hand
4. Powered wheelchair with tilt-in-space
5. Screen reader software (NVDA)
6. Implantable cardiac pacemaker (used as an assistive support in chronic CHF)

**(b)** A state government plans to roll out a "Universal Hearing Aid Program" for 5 lakh (500,000) hearing-impaired citizens. If the average device cost is ₹8,000 and the annual state health budget for AT is ₹40 crore, compute: (i) the maximum devices that can be procured, (ii) the coverage gap if devices must be replaced every 5 years, and (iii) recommend two engineering interventions to close the gap.  *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part A(a)

| Device | ISO 9999 Class | FDA Risk Class | WHO Tier | Justification |
|--------|----------------|----------------|----------|---------------|
| Manual walking stick | 12 (Personal Mobility) | I | 1 (Low-tech) | Non-powered, mechanical, no electronic failure modes. |
| Digital hearing aid | 22 (Communication) or 04 (Medical) | II | 2 (Mid-tech) | Electronic, contacts skin, but non-invasive; requires 510(k). |
| Myoelectric prosthetic hand | 06 (Prostheses) | II (or III for implantable myoelectric) | 3 (High-tech) | Multi-sensor, ML-based, sustained skin/muscle contact. |
| Powered wheelchair with tilt | 12 (Personal Mobility) | II | 2–3 | Battery-powered, motors, software controller. |
| Screen reader (NVDA) | 22 (Communication and Information) | I (software-only) | 2 (Mid-tech) | Non-contact software, no patient harm in failure. |
| Implantable pacemaker (CHF support) | 04 (Personal Medical Treatment) | III | 3 | Implanted, life-sustaining, requires PMA. |

*(Award 1 mark per correctly justified row, 1 mark for overall consistency.)*

#### Model Solution to Part A(b)

**(i) Maximum devices procurable:**
$$
N_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{40 \text{ crore}}{8{,}000} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{7}}{8 \times 10^{3}} = 5 \times 10^{4} = 50{,}000 \text{ devices}
$$

**(ii) Coverage and gap:**
$$
N_{\text{need, annual replacement}} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{5} = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Coverage} &= \dfrac{50{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 0.50 = 50\% \\
\text{Gap} &= 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Engineering interventions to close the gap:**
1. **Local low-cost manufacturing** — set up a Kerala-based hearing-aid assembly line using open-source DSP platforms to reduce unit cost from ₹8,000 to ~₹2,000, increasing procurement 4×.
2. **Open-fit BTE designs with rechargeable batteries** — extends device life from 5 to 7 years, reducing annual replacement pool to ~71,000 units and closing the gap further.
3. **Telerehabilitation + self-fitting AI apps** — reduces clinical overhead cost per device, freeing budget for more units.

*(Award 2 marks for part (i), 2 marks for part (ii), 3 marks for part (iii) with justification.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **WHO ICF framework** and identify the three intervention points at which an AMD can act. Give one specific AMD example for each intervention point.  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A rehabilitation hospital wants to procure 200 powered wheelchairs for adult patients with spinal cord injury (SCI). The wheelchairs must satisfy: range ≥ 25 km/day, max speed 6 km/h, payload 120 kg, and must pass ISO 7176 (wheelchair standards). Draft a **technical specification sheet** including: drive type, battery, motor, controller, braking, and safety standards. Justify each choice.  *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part B(a)

**WHO ICF Framework Block Diagram:**

```
Health Condition → Body Functions/Structures (Impairment)
        ↓
  Activity Limitations
        ↓
  Participation Restrictions
        ↑↓
Contextual Factors (Environmental and Personal)
        ↑
[AMD Intervention Points]
   1. Compensate the impairment
   2. Augment the activity
   3. Adapt the environment
```

**Three AMD intervention points with examples:**

1. **Compensate Impairment (Body level):**
   - Example: Myoelectric prosthetic hand for transradial amputee → substitutes lost grasp function.
   - ICF code addressed: b730 (muscle power functions).

2. **Augment Activity (Activity level):**
   - Example: Powered wheelchair for SCI patient → enables independent indoor/outdoor ambulation.
   - ICF code addressed: d460 (moving around in different locations).

3. **Adapt Environment (Environmental level):**
   - Example: Smart-home voice assistant + motorized curtains → allows bed-bound user to control environment.
   - ICF code addressed: e150 (design, construction, and building products of buildings for public use).

*(Award 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for intervention-point identification, 2 marks for ICF codes + examples.)*

#### Model Solution to Part B(b)

**Technical Specification Sheet — Powered Wheelchair for SCI Adults**

| S.No | Parameter | Specification | Justification |
|------|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Drive type | Rear-wheel drive (RWD) with two 24 V DC geared motors | Better obstacle climbing and stability for SCI users. |
| 2 | Motor rating | 2 × 350 W continuous, 700 W peak | Sufficient for 120 kg payload on 6° ramp. |
| 3 | Battery | 2 × 12 V, 60 Ah sealed lead-acid (or LiFePO₄ upgrade) | Stores $E = 2 \times 12 \times 60 = 1440$ Wh. |
| 4 | Range check | $BE = E / P_{avg} = 1440 / (2 \times 350 \times 0.5) = 1440 / 350 = 4.11$ h | At 6 km/h → $4.11 \times 6 = 24.7$ km/day — meets the 25 km target marginally. Recommend 75 Ah upgrade. |
| 5 | Controller | Programmable 70 A brushless DC controller with regenerative braking | Allows individualized acceleration profiles for SCI users (reduces spasm risk). |
| 6 | Braking system | Electromagnetic + mechanical park brake, dual redundant | ISO 7176-3 braking safety compliance. |
| 7 | Safety standards | ISO 7176-1, -3, -5, -8, -14, -15 (full suite) | Mandatory for clinical procurement. |
| 8 | Max speed limit | Programmatically capped at 6 km/h | Patient-safety constraint. |
| 9 | Payload | 150 kg rated, 120 kg nominal | 25% safety margin. |
| 10 | Joystick | Adjustable-force proportional joystick | Accommodates varying hand-control capability. |

**Battery upgrade check (LiFePO₄):**
$$
E_{\text{new}} = 24 \text{ V} \times 75 \text{ Ah} = 1800 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BE &= 1800 / 350 = 5.14 \text{ h} \\
\text{Range} &= 5.14 \times 6 = 30.86 \text{ km/day} \; (\text{exceeds 25 km target by 23\%})
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Award 1 mark per correct row × 7 rows; 3 marks for justification and battery math.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "disability" with "handicap."** Handicap is an obsolete WHO term; KTU 2024 scheme uses "participation restriction." Writing the old term costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Do NOT write only the FDA class without justification.** Always state the **predicate device** or the **product code** when classifying a device.
> 3. **Do NOT skip the ICF framework** in any 14-mark design answer — it is the universal scoring anchor.
> 4. **Do NOT mix up "orthosis" (external support to existing limb) and "prosthesis" (replacement of missing limb).** This is a single-line error that costs 2 marks in any orthotics/prosthetics question.
> 5. **Always include units and a numerical check** in battery / range / coverage problems — partial-credit survival depends on this.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Foundational Concepts: High-Density Summary for Last-Hour Revision**

- [x] **Definition of AMD** — primary intent is to *restore, maintain, or augment function*, not to cure.
- [x] **WHO ICF triad** — Impairment → Activity Limitation → Participation Restriction.
- [x] **Three AMD functions** — Compensate, Augment, Adapt.
- [x] **ISO 9999** has **12+ major classes**; memorize the 9 most clinically relevant (04, 05, 06, 09, 12, 15, 22, 24, 27).
- [x] **FDA risk classes** — Class I (low), Class II (moderate, 510(k)), Class III (high, PMA).
- [x] **WHO Essential APL** has 50 priority products; current global coverage gap ≈ **90%**.
- [x] **Seven Universal Design Principles** — *equitable, flexible, simple, perceptible, error-tolerant, low-effort, spacious*.
- [x] **Key metrics to remember:**
  - $AR = N_{acc}/N_{tot}$
  - $DAI = W_{min}/P_{unit}$
  - $MTBF = T_{op}/N_{fail}$
  - $EFG = C_{with} - C_{without}$
  - $BE = E_{stored}/P_{load}$
  - $R = P \times S$
  - $\text{Coverage Gap} = 1 - N_{access}/N_{need}$
- [x] **Stakeholder minimum viable team** = User + Clinician + Designer.
- [x] **Always map any AMD to the ICF framework** in design answers.
- [x] **Always justify the FDA risk class** with patient-contact duration and harm severity.
- [x] **Mandatory standards:** ISO 13485 (QMS), ISO 14971 (risk), IEC 60601 (electrical safety for active AMDs), ISO 7176 (wheelchairs).
- [x] **Avoid the term "handicap"** — use "participation restriction" per WHO 2001 onwards.
- [x] **Orthosis ≠ Prosthesis** — external support vs. replacement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of HAAT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application of HAAT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of the HAAT Model

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model** is an outcome-based, human-centered conceptual framework developed by **Roger Smith (1987)** at the **TRACE Research Center, University of Wisconsin-Madison**. It is formally adopted by assistive technology (AT) engineers, rehabilitation scientists, and clinical practitioners to describe, analyze, and prescribe assistive devices by mapping the dynamic interaction among four inseparable components:

$$
\text{HAAT} = f(\text{Human},\ \text{Activity},\ \text{Assistive Technology},\ \text{Context})
$$

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBRT745 – Assistive Medical Devices)**, the HAAT model is the foundational paradigm under **Module 1 (Fundamentals of Assistive Technology)** used to evaluate *why* a particular assistive medical device is chosen, *how* it integrates with the user, and *what contextual constraints* (hospital, home, school, workplace) govern its performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The HAAT model is **NOT a hardware classification** — it is an **analytical design and assessment framework** that mandates the engineer to *simultaneously* optimize the device, the human, the task, and the environment.

---

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of HAAT — A Structured Definition

| Pillar | Definition (KTU 2024 terminology) | Engineering Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Human (H)** | The end-user with a functional limitation (motor, sensory, cognitive, or anatomical). | Biomechanics, anthropometry, residual function, user acceptance. |
| **Activity (A)** | The specific purposeful task the user intends to perform (ADL, IADL, education, employment, leisure). | Task analysis, ergonomic fit, goal-oriented prescription. |
| **Assistive Technology (AT)** | Any item, device, equipment, software, or service used to increase, maintain, or improve functional capabilities. | Hardware design, software algorithms, biomedical instrumentation. |
| **Context (C)** | The physical, social, cultural, institutional, and temporal environment in which the AT is used. | Environmental barriers, social acceptance, policy, climatic conditions. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of HAAT as a "Four-Legged Table":**
> Imagine a four-legged table where the tabletop is the user's **independent goal** (e.g., a paralysed patient wants to drink water on their own).
> - **Leg 1 — Human:** The patient's body strength, arm length, grip power, vision.
> - **Leg 2 — Activity:** The specific act of *lifting a glass and sipping*.
> - **Leg 3 — Assistive Technology:** The robotic arm / adapted cup / straw holder.
> - **Leg 4 — Context:** The bedside table height, hospital lighting, caregiver presence.
>
> If **any one leg is too short** (e.g., the cup is too heavy, the room is dark, the patient cannot reach), the table **collapses** — meaning the assistive device *fails* in real use, even if it is the most expensive device in the world.
>
> **Therefore, HAAT insists: a successful assistive device is one where all four legs are of balanced, matched length.**

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Engineering Interpretation

For a B.Tech biomedical/biomedical-instrumentation student, the HAAT model can be mapped directly onto the **engineering design loop (SDLC)** as follows:

1. **Identify the Human's functional deficit** → Patient assessment (analogous to *requirements gathering*).
2. **Specify the Activity** → Functional goal statement (analogous to *use-case specification*).
3. **Design the Assistive Technology** → Hardware/software solution (analogous to *system architecture*).
4. **Validate in Context** → Field trial in hospital, home, outdoor (analogous to *user acceptance testing*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** The success of an HAAT-aligned device is measured not by its technical specifications alone (e.g., torque, sampling rate), but by the **functional outcome** — i.e., the **Quality of Life (QoL)** improvement quantified using standardized metrics such as the **WHO ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** framework or the **FIM (Functional Independence Measure)** score.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of the HAAT Framework

The HAAT model operates on a **closed-loop, transactional principle** — meaning the four components are in **continuous, bidirectional interaction**, not in a linear chain. The model can be broken down into the following structured engineering logic steps:

### Step 1 — Human Assessment (The Input Vector)
- The user's **residual functional capacity** is measured along multiple axes:
  - **Motor function** (manual muscle testing on the **0–5 MRC scale**).
  - **Sensory function** (visual acuity measured in **logMAR**, audiometric thresholds in **dB HL**).
  - **Cognitive function** (MMSE score, range **0–30**).
- Output of this step: a **functional profile vector** $H = [h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_n]$ representing the user's abilities and limitations.

### Step 2 — Activity Analysis (The Goal Function)
- The target activity is decomposed into **sub-tasks** using **Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA)**.
- Each sub-task is evaluated for: required range of motion, force, precision, duration, and sequencing.
- The output is a **task specification matrix** $A = [a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m]$ of required human capabilities.

### Step 3 — Assistive Technology Design (The Bridging Function)
- The AT device must **subtractively complement** the gap between $H$ (what the human *can* do) and $A$ (what the activity *requires*).
- The engineering requirement is: 
$$AT_{required} = A \setminus H$$
  where "$\setminus$" denotes the *set difference* — i.e., the capabilities that must be **externally supplied** by the device.

### Step 4 — Contextual Integration (The Constraint Envelope)
- The context imposes **boundary conditions** (e.g., electrical safety per **IEC 60601-1**, sterility requirements, IP rating for dust/water ingress, ambient temperature, social stigma, funding constraints).
- Context is treated as a **feasibility constraint set** $C$ such that the final AT design must satisfy $AT_{final} \subseteq C$.

### Step 5 — Outcome Evaluation (The Validation Loop)
- The combined system $(H + AT)$ is tested in the actual $C$ to perform $A$.
- Outcome is measured by **change in independence**: 
$$\Delta_{independence} = FIM_{post} - FIM_{pre}$$
  where **FIM** is the Functional Independence Measure (range 18–126).

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | The "Why" | The "How" |
|---|---|---|
| Human Assessment | A device designed without knowing the user's residual function will either over-engineer (waste) or under-engineer (fail). | Standardized clinical scales (MRC, MMSE, FIM) + biomechanical measurement. |
| Activity Analysis | Different activities demand different physical/cognitive loads. A wheelchair is a mobility AT, not a communication AT. | Hierarchical Task Analysis, time-motion studies, ergonomic workload indices. |
| AT Design | The device is the *bridge* — it must perfectly match the human-activity gap without imposing new burdens. | Modular design, user-centered design (UCD), iterative prototyping, ISO 9999 classification. |
| Context | A voice-activated wheelchair may work in a quiet home but fail in a noisy hospital ward. | Environmental audit, IP rating selection, cultural/social acceptability studies. |
| Outcome Evaluation | Without outcome measurement, the device's real-world value is unproven — KTU examiners test this. | Pre-post intervention studies, FIM scoring, ICF-based outcome reporting. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following high-yield formulas and definitions are **frequently asked in KTU ESE** for Module 1. Memorize the variables and the units.

| # | Concept / Formula | Symbolic Form | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HAAT Functional Definition | $\text{HAAT} = f(H, A, AT, C)$ | The four-pillar interaction model. |
| 2 | Required AT (capability gap) | $AT_{req} = A \setminus H$ | Set difference between required and available capability. Dimensionless capability vector. |
| 3 | Functional Independence Change | $\Delta_{FIM} = FIM_{post} - FIM_{pre}$ | Range of FIM: **18 to 126**; higher = more independent. |
| 4 | ICF Disability Model | $D = f(B, A, C, P)$ | WHO ICF: Body function, Activity, Context, Participation. |
| 5 | Device Acceptance Rate | $\eta_{acc} = \dfrac{N_{accepted}}{N_{prescribed}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of prescribed devices actually used. Industry target: **> 65%**. |
| 6 | Device Abandonment Rate | $\eta_{abd} = \dfrac{N_{discarded}}{N_{prescribed}} \times 100\%$ | Literature reports **~30%** abandonment; HAAT aims to reduce this. |
| 7 | Standard Safety Standard | **IEC 60601-1** | Medical electrical equipment safety. Leakage current $\le 0.1\ mA$ (Class I, BF). |
| 8 | AT Classification | **ISO 9999** | International classification of assistive products (06 = orthoses, 12 = mobility, 22 = communication). |
| 9 | User-Centered Design Loop | $D_{n+1} = D_n + \alpha (H_{feedback})$ | Iterative design refinement, $\alpha$ = learning rate. |
| 10 | Anthropometric Match | $\vert L_{device} - L_{user} \vert \le \epsilon$ | Device dimension must match user within tolerance $\epsilon$ (typically **$\pm 25\ mm$**). |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of HAAT

The HAAT model is used in:

- **Clinical rehabilitation engineering** — prescription of powered wheelchairs, prosthetics, and orthoses.
- **Smart prosthetics** — designing a myoelectric hand by matching residual EMG signal strength ($H$) with grasp tasks ($A$) using pattern-recognition algorithms ($AT$) in the user's home/workshop ($C$).
- **Hospital bed design** — matching patient mobility ($H$) with ADL like repositioning ($A$) using electric actuators ($AT$) in the ICU environment ($C$).
- **Tele-rehabilitation** — designing wearable sensors ($AT$) to monitor activity ($A$) of elderly users ($H$) in their homes ($C$).
- **Regulatory documentation** — IEC 60601-1 and FDA submissions use HAAT-style risk analysis to demonstrate that the device-human-context interaction has been validated.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Capability Gap Equation

We define the **activity demand vector** $A$ and the **human capability vector** $H$ as two row vectors of the same dimension $n$, where each component represents a specific physical/cognitive attribute (e.g., grip force, reach, vision, memory, etc.).

$$
A = [a_1,\ a_2,\ a_3,\ \ldots,\ a_n]
$$
$$
H = [h_1,\ h_2,\ h_3,\ \ldots,\ h_n]
$$

The **capability gap** for the $i^{th}$ attribute is defined as the difference between what the activity demands and what the human can supply:

$$
\Delta_i = a_i - h_i
$$

If $\Delta_i \le 0$, the human **already possesses** the required capability for that attribute (no AT needed for it). If $\Delta_i > 0$, the human has a **deficit**, and the AT must supply the missing $\Delta_i$ units of capability.

Therefore, the **required assistive technology capability vector** is defined as:

$$
AT_{req} = [\max(0, a_1 - h_1),\ \max(0, a_2 - h_2),\ \ldots,\ \max(0, a_n - h_n)]
$$

Using the **ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit)** notation from engineering (commonly used in neural networks and signal processing), this is elegantly written as:

$$
AT_{req} = \text{ReLU}(A - H)
$$

where $\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)$. The non-zero entries of $AT_{req}$ are the **engineering design specifications** for the assistive device.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Functional Independence Measure (FIM) Outcome

The FIM is an **18-item ordinal scale** rated from **1 (Total Assistance) to 7 (Complete Independence)** across six domains:

- Self-care (6 items)
- Sphincter control (2 items)
- Transfers (3 items)
- Locomotion (2 items)
- Communication (2 items)
- Social cognition (3 items)

The **total FIM score** is:

$$
FIM_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{18} s_i
$$

where $s_i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$.

The minimum possible score is:
$$
FIM_{min} = 18 \times 1 = 18 \quad \text{(total dependence)}
$$

The maximum possible score is:
$$
FIM_{max} = 18 \times 7 = 126 \quad \text{(complete independence)}
$$

Hence, the **range** of FIM is:

$$
FIM_{range} = FIM_{max} - FIM_{min} = 126 - 18 = 108
$$

The **change in independence** after AT intervention is:

$$
\Delta_{FIM} = FIM_{post} - FIM_{pre}
$$

A **clinically meaningful improvement** is conventionally defined as:

$$
\Delta_{FIM} \ge 22 \quad \text{(minimal clinically important difference, MCID)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU examination numerical problem may ask: *"A patient scored 58 on FIM before and 96 after receiving a powered wheelchair. Compute the percentage improvement."* The derivation below demonstrates the model answer.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $FIM_{pre} = 58$, $FIM_{post} = 96$.

Step 1 — Compute the absolute change:
$$
\Delta_{FIM} = 96 - 58 = 38
$$

Step 2 — Compare with the MCID of 22:
$$
\Delta_{FIM} = 38 \ge 22 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Clinically significant improvement.}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the percentage improvement relative to the achievable range above the baseline:
$$
\% \text{ improvement} = \dfrac{\Delta_{FIM}}{FIM_{max} - FIM_{pre}} \times 100\%
$$
$$
\% \text{ improvement} = \dfrac{38}{126 - 58} \times 100\% = \dfrac{38}{68} \times 100\% \approx 55.88\%
$$

Thus, the powered wheelchair intervention produced approximately **55.88% functional recovery** of the patient's remaining deficit, which is a **highly successful** HAAT-aligned outcome.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Device Acceptance vs. Abandonment

Let $N_p$ = number of devices prescribed, $N_a$ = number accepted (used regularly), $N_d$ = number discarded (abandoned). The acceptance rate is:

$$
\eta_{acc} = \dfrac{N_a}{N_p} \times 100\%
$$

The abandonment rate is:

$$
\eta_{abd} = \dfrac{N_d}{N_p} \times 100\%
$$

In an ideal HAAT-compliant prescription, the following conservation relation must hold:

$$
N_a + N_d = N_p
$$

Dividing throughout by $N_p$:

$$
\eta_{acc} + \eta_{abd} = 100\%
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The HAAT model predicts that devices prescribed without proper contextual analysis (Context) or user training have **higher abandonment** ($\eta_{abd} \uparrow$). Philips & Zhao (1993) reported an average AT abandonment rate of **~30%** in the absence of HAAT-aligned prescription, dropping to **<10%** with proper user-centered (HAAT) prescription.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Application of HAAT to Common Assistive Medical Devices

| Device Category | Human (H) | Activity (A) | Assistive Tech (AT) | Context (C) | HAAT Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Powered Wheelchair** | Paraplegic with intact cognition. | Indoor/outdoor mobility, ADL navigation. | Joystick controller, BLDC motors, lead-acid/Li-ion battery, microcontroller. | Hospital corridors, home doorways (≥ 800 mm), public ramps. | Device motor torque must match user's body mass (H) for the slope gradient (C) of the intended route (A). |
| **Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand** | Transradial amputee with residual EMG signal ≥ 100 µV. | Grasping, lifting, pinching objects. | EMG electrodes, classification algorithm (LDA/SVM), servo motors, battery. | Workplace, home, weather exposure (IP 54). | EMG pattern classifier must be trained on the specific user's residual signal (H) to recognize his/her grasp intentions (A) in daily settings (C). |
| **Cochlear Implant** | Severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss. | Speech perception, environmental sound awareness. | External microphone, sound processor, transmitter coil, implanted electrode array in cochlea. | Classrooms, noisy restaurants, telephone use. | Electrode mapping (frequency-to-place) is tuned per user audiogram (H) for speech in quiet and noise (A) in their typical environment (C). |
| **Insulin Pump** | Type-1 diabetic with stable glycemic patterns. | Continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion, meal-bolus control. | Micro-pump, CGM sensor, control algorithm (PID/fuzzy logic). | Office, school, during exercise. | Basal rate profile is personalized to the user's metabolism (H) for activity (A) of daily glucose control in their routine (C). |
| **Smart Hospital Bed** | Post-surgical patient with limited mobility. | Repositioning, transfer, pressure relief. | Electric actuators, pressure-sensor mattress, side-rail controls. | ICU, ward, with strict IEC 60601-1 electrical safety. | Actuator speed/force tuned to patient's weight (H) for safe turning (A) in sterile ICU environment (C). |
| **Augmentative & Alternative Communication (AAC) Device** | Non-verbal cerebral palsy / ALS patient. | Face-to-face and digital communication. | Tablet with AAC app, eye-tracker, switch input. | Classroom, home, public spaces; sunlight glare. | Eye-tracker calibration (H) is matched to letter-selection speed (A) under variable ambient lighting (C). |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HAAT Block Diagram (Mermaid — Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following block diagram illustrates the **four-pillar interaction** of the HAAT model and the engineering design loop that connects them.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Human User - H] --> B{Human Function Profile}
    B --> C[Activity Demand Analysis - A]
    C --> D[Capability Gap Computation]
    A --> D
    D --> E[Assistive Technology Design - AT]
    E --> F[Contextual Constraint Check - C]
    F --> G[Prototype Fabrication]
    G --> H[User Acceptance Test - UAT]
    H --> B
    H --> I{Outcome Validated?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Final AT Prescription]
    I -- No --> E
    J --> K[Quality of Life Improvement - Delta FIM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading of the Diagram:**
> - Boxes **A, C, E, F** represent the four HAAT pillars (Human, Activity, AT, Context).
> - The **feedback loop** from User Acceptance Test (UAT) back to the Human Function Profile represents the **iterative, user-centered design** that is central to HAAT.
> - The decision diamond **I** enforces the **outcome-based** nature of HAAT — the design is *not* complete until functional independence is improved.

---

## 4.2 HAAT Capability-Gap Engineering Flow

The following diagram zooms into the **engineering design core** of HAAT — the translation of a clinical deficit into a hardware specification.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Patient Assessment - MRC MMSE FIM] --> P2[Activity Decomposition - HTA]
    P2 --> P3[Demand Vector A]
    P1 --> P4[Capability Vector H]
    P3 --> P5[AT Required equals ReLU of A minus H]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Hardware Specification Document]
    P6 --> P7[Prototype Build]
    P7 --> P8[Contextual Trial - Hospital Home Outdoor]
    P8 --> P9[Outcome Score - FIM post minus FIM pre]
    P9 --> P10{Meets MCID greater or equal 22?}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[HAAT Success - Device Released]
    P10 -- No --> P1
```

---

## 4.3 HAAT Application Matrix — A Visual Mapping of Pillars to Engineering Sub-Disciplines

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HUMAN_PILLAR
        H1[Biomechanics]
        H2[Anthropometry]
        H3[Neurology]
        H4[Cognitive Science]
    end
    subgraph ACTIVITY_PILLAR
        A1[Activities of Daily Living - ADL]
        A2[Instrumental ADL - IADL]
        A3[Work and Education Tasks]
        A4[Leisure and Social Participation]
    end
    subgraph AT_PILLAR
        AT1[Mechanical Design]
        AT2[Embedded Electronics]
        AT3[Software and AI]
        AT4[Biomaterials]
    end
    subgraph CONTEXT_PILLAR
        C1[Physical Environment]
        C2[Social and Cultural Norms]
        C3[Regulatory Standards - IEC 60601-1 ISO 9999]
        C4[Economic Constraints]
    end
    HUMAN_PILLAR --> CENTRAL[HAAT Integration Engine - Outcome Validation]
    ACTIVITY_PILLAR --> CENTRAL
    AT_PILLAR --> CENTRAL
    CONTEXT_PILLAR --> CENTRAL
    CENTRAL --> OUT[Functional Independence Score - FIM - ICF]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When a question asks *"Explain the application of HAAT"*, drawing a labeled block diagram with the **four pillars and the feedback loop** earns full marks (typically 4–5 of 14 marks). A purely textual answer without a diagram loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 4.4 Decision Logic for AT Selection Using HAAT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Patient with Functional Limitation] --> S1[Step 1: Profile Human H]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Specify Activity A]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute Capability Gap]
    S3 --> S4{Gap is Zero?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[No AT Needed - Rehabilitation Sufficient]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Step 4: Survey Context C]
    S6 --> S7[Step 5: Match AT to Gap and Context]
    S7 --> S8[Step 6: Pilot Trial]
    S8 --> S9[Step 7: Measure FIM Pre and Post]
    S9 --> S10{Delta FIM greater or equal 22?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Successful HAAT Application - Prescribe AT]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Re-evaluate and Iterate - Return to Step 1]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define the HAAT model and list its four components.**
> *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) model** is a human-centered, outcome-based framework introduced by **Roger Smith (1987)** for the design, prescription, and evaluation of assistive medical devices. It describes the assistive technology as an integrated system in which the **Human (H)**, the **Activity (A)**, the **Assistive Technology (AT)**, and the **Context (C)** interact continuously. The functional definition is given as:

$$
\text{HAAT} = f(H, A, AT, C)
$$

The four components are:
1. **Human** – the user with a functional limitation.
2. **Activity** – the specific task the user wishes to perform.
3. **Assistive Technology** – the device, software, or service that bridges the capability gap.
4. **Context** – the physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment of use.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. Explain the role of "Context" in the HAAT model with two examples.**
> *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Context** in the HAAT model represents the *external environment* in which the human, activity, and assistive technology interact. It includes the **physical environment** (temperature, space, lighting, noise), **social environment** (family support, stigma), **cultural environment** (acceptance of disability aids), and **institutional environment** (regulatory standards, hospital policies, funding). Context is critical because the *same* AT device may succeed in one environment and fail in another.

**Example 1:** A voice-controlled smart-home device may work flawlessly in a quiet bedroom (favourable physical context) but fail in a noisy hospital ward (unfavourable physical context) due to ambient acoustic interference.

**Example 2:** A high-cost powered wheelchair prescribed for a low-income rural user may be abandoned (social/cultural/economic context) due to the absence of repair facilities and the social stigma associated with disability aids in that community.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Question Pattern]**

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**Q(A). (a)** Explain the HAAT model in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss how the four components interact in the design of a **myoelectric prosthetic hand** for a transradial amputee. *(7 marks)*

**Q(A). (b)** A stroke patient scored **42 on FIM** before intervention and **78 after 6 weeks** of using an AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) device.
  - (i) Compute the change in FIM.  
  - (ii) Determine whether the intervention is clinically significant (use MCID = 22).  
  - (iii) Calculate the percentage functional recovery.  
  *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q(A)(a)

**Step 1 — Define HAAT:**
The HAAT model is a human-centered framework that views the user, the desired activity, the assistive technology, and the context as an integrated, dynamic system.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram:**
(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Step 3 — Application to Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand:**

| HAAT Pillar | Application to Myoelectric Hand |
|---|---|
| **Human (H)** | The transradial amputee's residual forearm muscle activity is measured via surface EMG. The signal amplitude (typically **50–500 µV**) and the number of distinct muscle contraction patterns determine the device's input resolution. Cognitive ability to operate the device is also assessed. |
| **Activity (A)** | The user wants to perform grasp-and-release tasks such as holding a cup, opening a door, or typing. The activity is decomposed using Hierarchical Task Analysis into grip types: *power grip, precision pinch, lateral pinch, hook grip*. |
| **Assistive Technology (AT)** | Surface EMG electrodes → signal conditioning (amplification, filtering, rectification) → feature extraction → classifier (LDA, SVM, or neural network) → microcontroller → motor drivers → servo motors in the hand → grip actuation. The engineering requirement $AT_{req} = \text{ReLU}(A - H)$ gives the grip force, speed, and degrees of freedom the hand must deliver. |
| **Context (C)** | Home, workplace, outdoor weather; IP rating of at least **IP 54** (dust + splash resistance); battery life ≥ 1 day; cosmesis and weight (< 400 g preferred); compliance with **IEC 60601-1** for medical safety. |

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The success of the myoelectric hand depends not just on its electronics, but on the **balanced matching** of all four HAAT pillars. A state-of-the-art 6-DOF hand with AI-based grasp prediction will fail if the user's residual EMG is too weak (H-mismatch) or if the hand is too heavy for daily use (C-mismatch).

#### Model Solution — Q(A)(b)

**Given:** $FIM_{pre} = 42$, $FIM_{post} = 78$, $MCID = 22$, $FIM_{max} = 126$.

**(i) Change in FIM:**
$$
\Delta_{FIM} = FIM_{post} - FIM_{pre} = 78 - 42 = 36
$$

**(ii) Clinical significance:**
$$
\Delta_{FIM} = 36 \ge 22 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Clinically significant improvement.}
$$

**(iii) Percentage functional recovery (relative to remaining deficit):**
$$
\% \text{ recovery} = \dfrac{\Delta_{FIM}}{FIM_{max} - FIM_{pre}} \times 100\%
$$
$$
\% \text{ recovery} = \dfrac{36}{126 - 42} \times 100\% = \dfrac{36}{84} \times 100\% \approx 42.86\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The AAC device delivered a clinically meaningful 36-point FIM gain, representing a **42.86% recovery** of the patient's lost functional capacity. By the HAAT framework, this is a **successful assistive technology intervention**.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks)

**Q(B). (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **HAAT model**. Compare the HAAT model with the **WHO ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** model, highlighting at least **four points of similarity and difference**. *(7 marks)*

**Q(B). (b)** A rehabilitation clinic prescribes **150 assistive devices** in one year. Follow-up reveals that **45 devices** were abandoned by users within 3 months. Compute the **acceptance rate** and **abandonment rate**, and discuss **three HAAT-aligned strategies** to reduce abandonment. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q(B)(a)

**Step 1 — HAAT Block Diagram:**
(Refer to Section 4.1.)

**Step 2 — Comparison Table: HAAT vs. WHO ICF**

| Aspect | HAAT Model | WHO ICF Model |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Roger Smith, 1987 (engineering). | World Health Organization, 2001 (clinical). |
| Primary Focus | **Assistive Technology** as the central bridging element. | **Functioning and Disability** as a multi-dimensional health outcome. |
| Components | Human, Activity, AT, Context. | Body Functions & Structures, Activities & Participation, Contextual Factors (Environmental + Personal). |
| Engineering Emphasis | Strong — mandates device design and validation. | Moderate — provides a classification and outcome language, not a design tool. |
| Outcome Metric | FIM, functional independence. | ICF qualifiers (0–4 scale) on body function, activity, participation. |
| Treatment of Environment | "Context" — single integrated component. | "Contextual Factors" — split into Environmental and Personal. |
| Role of AT | AT is a first-class component of the model. | AT appears *within* Environmental Factors as a facilitator or barrier. |
| User-Centered? | Yes — iterative feedback loop. | Yes — emphasizes participation, but is descriptive rather than prescriptive. |

**Conclusion:** HAAT is **engineering-action-oriented**, while ICF is **classification-and-outcome-oriented**. They are **complementary** — modern rehabilitation practice uses ICF for clinical assessment and HAAT for device design.

#### Model Solution — Q(B)(b)

**Given:** $N_p = 150$ prescribed devices, $N_d = 45$ abandoned.

**Step 1 — Compute acceptance and abandonment rates:**
$$
\eta_{abd} = \dfrac{N_d}{N_p} \times 100\% = \dfrac{45}{150} \times 100\% = 30\%
$$
$$
\eta_{acc} = 100\% - \eta_{abd} = 70\%
$$

**Step 2 — Three HAAT-aligned strategies to reduce abandonment:**

1. **Improved User (Human) Profiling:** Conduct a detailed pre-prescription assessment of the user's physical, cognitive, and motivational profile using standardized scales. Mismatched user-device pairing accounts for a major fraction of abandonment.

2. **Contextual Adaptation:** Survey the user's home/workplace *before* prescription. Devices must be dimensionally, electrically, and environmentally compatible (e.g., wheelchair width vs. doorway width, IP rating vs. outdoor exposure, mains voltage compatibility).

3. **User Training and Follow-up Loop:** Establish an iterative follow-up schedule (1 week, 1 month, 3 months) to capture the feedback loop in HAAT. Many abandonments are due to simple operational difficulties that can be resolved by a brief training session or minor adjustment.

**Conclusion:** The 30% abandonment rate matches the published literature baseline. Applying the three HAAT strategies above can plausibly reduce abandonment to **below 10%**, in line with best-practice rehabilitation engineering studies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse HAAT with the simple "user–device fit" model.** HAAT is a *four-pillar* system; omitting the **Context** pillar loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT define HAAT as a hardware classification.** It is a *design and assessment framework*, not a taxonomy like ISO 9999.
> 3. **In numerical problems on FIM:** Always state the range (**18–126**) and the MCID threshold (**22**) before computing. Skipping these statements loses 1 mark each.
> 4. **In block diagrams:** Always draw **four boxes (H, A, AT, C)** with **bidirectional arrows** and a **feedback loop**. A unidirectional linear chain is *not* HAAT and will be penalized.
> 5. **Do NOT cite wrong attribution:** HAAT was developed by **Roger Smith (1987)**, *not* by WHO. ICF is by WHO (2001). Mixing up the attributions is a frequent mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HAAT (Module 1)**

- ⭐ HAAT stands for **Human Activity Assistive Technology**, developed by **Roger Smith, 1987**, at the **TRACE Research Center, University of Wisconsin-Madison**.
- ⭐ The model has **four pillars**: **Human (H), Activity (A), Assistive Technology (AT), Context (C)** — all four are *equally important*; none can be omitted.
- ⭐ HAAT is a **design and assessment framework**, **not a device classification** (classification is done by **ISO 9999**).
- ⭐ The capability gap equation is $AT_{req} = \text{ReLU}(A - H)$, i.e., the device must supply the **positive difference** between activity demand and human capability.
- ⭐ **Context** includes physical, social, cultural, institutional, and economic environments.
- ⭐ The **Functional Independence Measure (FIM)** scores range from **18 (total dependence) to 126 (complete independence)**.
- ⭐ The **Minimum Clinically Important Difference (MCID)** for FIM is **22 points**; interventions with $\Delta FIM \ge 22$ are considered clinically meaningful.
- ⭐ The **WHO ICF model** (2001) is the *clinical counterpart* of HAAT and complements it.
- ⭐ AT device **abandonment rate** in the literature is **~30%** without HAAT-compliant prescription; it drops to **<10%** with proper user-centered (HAAT) prescription.
- ⭐ Standard **safety** reference for medical electrical AT: **IEC 60601-1** (leakage current ≤ 0.1 mA, Class I, Type BF).
- ⭐ Standard **classification** reference for AT products: **ISO 9999** (06 = orthoses, 12 = mobility, 22 = communication).
- ⭐ In the **examination**, always (i) draw a labeled block diagram with four pillars and a feedback loop, (ii) state the FIM range and MCID before computing, and (iii) explicitly link *each* pillar of HAAT to the *specific* assistive device being discussed.
- ⭐ Remember the conservation relation: $\eta_{acc} + \eta_{abd} = 100\%$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Universal Design Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies: Hard and Soft Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Universal Design Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies: Hard and Soft Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Universal Design (UD) — Formal KTU Definition

**Universal Design** is a framework for the design of products, environments, services, and interfaces that is usable by all people, to the greatest extent possible, without the need for adaptation or specialized design. In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices**, Universal Design extends beyond disability-centric design to embrace human variability across the entire lifespan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Universal Design (Ronald L. Mace, 1997):**
> Universal Design is *not* a trend but a **principle-driven approach** where the design itself is inherently accessible, rather than retrofitting accessibility as an afterthought.

### 1.2 Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies

The **Functional Framework** is a structured classification system that categorizes assistive technologies based on the **human functional needs** they address, rather than on the user's specific diagnosis or impairment. It links the *activity limitation* (e.g., walking, reading, hearing) to the *assistive solution* required.

The framework is built upon the **WHO International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** model, which defines disability as a function of:

$$
D = f(\text{Health Condition},\text{Body Functions},\text{Activities},\text{Participation},\text{Environmental Factors})
$$

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Staircase vs. Ramp" Thought Experiment

Imagine a building with a grand staircase at its entrance. A **person using a wheelchair** cannot use the staircase. A *reactive* (specialized) design would add a small, hidden ramp at the back door — this is **Assistive Technology in isolation**.
