A **Universal Design** approach would build a gentle, integrated ramp alongside the staircase. Now, *everyone* benefits:
- The wheelchair user
- The parent with a stroller
- The delivery person with a trolley
- The elderly person with limited mobility

The **Hard Technology** is the ramp (the physical structure). The **Soft Technology** is the *awareness, policy, and training* that makes the ramp functional (signage, social acceptance, usage protocols).

### 1.4 Hard vs. Soft Technology — Core Definition

| Dimension | Hard Technology | Soft Technology |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Nature** | Tangible, physical | Intangible, procedural |
| **Substance** | Hardware, materials, electronics | Software, methodologies, knowledge |
| **Examples** | Wheelchair, hearing aid, prosthetic limb | Training programs, cognitive therapy apps, policies |
| **Lifespan** | Mechanical/electrical wear-out | Knowledge obsolescence |
| **KTU Exam Cue** | "Device-based" answers | "Service-based / process-based" answers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU):** According to the **ISO 9999:2022** classification, assistive products are categorized into **12 classes** (e.g., 04 for personal mobility, 15 for domestic activities). Hard and soft technologies are *complementary*, never mutually exclusive.

### 1.5 The Seven Principles of Universal Design (Ron Mace, NC State University)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These **7 principles** are **direct syllabus-mandated** content. KTU expects students to be able to *list, define, and apply* them in both Part A and Part B questions.

1. **Equitable Use** — Same means of use for all users.
2. **Flexibility in Use** — Accommodates a wide range of preferences and abilities.
3. **Simple and Intuitive Use** — Easy to understand regardless of experience.
4. **Perceptible Information** — Communicates necessary information effectively.
5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimizes hazards from accidental actions.
6. **Low Physical Effort** — Used efficiently and comfortably.
7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Appropriate size and space provided.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Functional Framework — Architecture Breakdown

The functional framework for Assistive Technologies operates on **four interacting layers**:

#### Layer 1: User Functional Need
- Identifies the *gap* between the user's capacity and the environmental demand.
- Categorized as: **Sensory, Motor, Cognitive, Communication**.

#### Layer 2: Activity Limitation Mapping
- Maps need to the **ICF Activity & Participation** domain.
- Example: *Cannot walk independently* → Mobility Activity Limitation.

#### Layer 3: Technology Selection (Hard/Soft)
- Selects an **intervention class** based on:
  - *Hard:* Physical device procurement and fitting.
  - *Soft:* Therapy, training, environmental modification protocol.

#### Layer 4: Outcome Measurement
- Verifies effectiveness using metrics like:
  - **Functional Independence Measure (FIM)** — scored **1 (Total Assist) to 7 (Complete Independence)**.
  - **Barthel Index** — scored **0 to 100** (higher = more independent).

### 2.2 Core "Why and How" — Engineering Rationale

**Why** does the framework separate Hard and Soft technologies?
- Because **Hard technologies** are bounded by **physics** (material fatigue, battery life, signal-to-noise ratio).
- Because **Soft technologies** are bounded by **cognition and culture** (literacy, social stigma, training time).
- The framework demands *both* to be considered for a holistic solution.

**How** is the framework applied?
- Through a **Human-Centered Design (HCD) iterative loop**:
  *Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test* (5-stage Stanford d.school model).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Standard | Units / Notes |
|---------|-----------------------|---------------|
| ICF Disability Model | $D = f(B, A, P, E)$ | B=Body, A=Activity, P=Participation, E=Environment |
| FIM Score | $FIM \in \lbrace 1, 2, ..., 7 \rbrace$ | 7 = Complete Independence |
| Barthel Index | $BI \in \lbrace 0, 5, 10, 15 \rbrace$ per item | Total 0–100 |
| SNR (Hearing Aids) | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right)$ | $V_s$=Signal, $V_n$=Noise |
| Battery Life | $t_{hr} = \frac{C_{mAh}}{I_{mA}}$ | Used in powered mobility devices |
| Force Assist Ratio | $R_a = \frac{F_{user}}{F_{device}}$ | $R_a < 1$ = assistive gain |
| ISO 9999 Classes | $N_{class} = 12$ | Updated 2022 standard |
| UD Principles | $N_{UD} = 7$ | Ron Mace, NC State 1997 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Functional Framework is the **backbone of clinical engineering workflow** — from prescription (assessment) to procurement (device selection) to follow-up (outcome verification). It is the language spoken between the *clinician, biomedical engineer, and patient*.

### 2.4 Hard Technologies — Deep Classification

**Hard Technologies** in assistive medical devices are the *physical artifacts* engineered to compensate for functional limitations. They are classified as:

- **Prosthetic Devices** — Replace a missing body part (e.g., myoelectric arm).
- **Orthotic Devices** — Support or correct a body segment (e.g., ankle-foot orthosis).
- **Mobility Aids** — Wheelchairs, walkers, canes (manual and powered).
- **Sensory Aids** — Hearing aids, low-vision magnifiers, cochlear implants.
- **Communication Devices** — AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) boards, speech-generating devices.
- **Environmental Control Units (ECUs)** — Voice-activated home automation.
- **Biomedical Implants** — Pacemakers, deep brain stimulators, insulin pumps.

### 2.5 Soft Technologies — Deep Classification

**Soft Technologies** are the *knowledge, procedural, and service-based components* without which hard technologies are useless:

- **Rehabilitation Protocols** — Gait training, occupational therapy routines.
- **Assistive Software** — Screen readers (JAWS, NVDA), AAC apps (Proloquo2Go).
- **User Training Programs** — Device operation, maintenance education.
- **Policy and Advocacy** — Disability rights law (RPwD Act 2016, India; ADA 1990, USA).
- **Caregiver Support Systems** — Family education, peer counseling.
- **Clinical Assessment Tools** — Standardized scales (FIM, Barthel, WHODAS 2.0).
- **Tele-rehabilitation** — Remote therapy via telecommunication platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** A common error in board exams is treating **software (Soft Technology)** and **prosthetic hardware (Hard Technology)** as opposites. The correct view is that they exist on a **spectrum** — an app running on a tablet attached to a wheelchair is *both* a hard and soft technology in an integrated system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Decision Logic for Hard vs. Soft Selection

To select the appropriate technology class for a given functional limitation, biomedical engineers apply a **decision matrix**. Below is the exhaustive derivation of the **Assistive Technology Selection (ATS) Score**:

$$
ATS = w_1 \cdot C + w_2 \cdot P + w_3 \cdot A + w_4 \cdot M
$$

Where:
- $C$ = **Clinical Evidence Score** (0–10)
- $P$ = **Patient Preference Score** (0–10)
- $A$ = **Accessibility / Availability Score** (0–10)
- $M$ = **Maintenance / Cost Score** (0–10)
- $w_i$ = **Weighting factor** (where $\sum w_i = 1$)

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Normalize each score: } X_{norm} = \frac{X}{10}, \quad X \in \lbrace C, P, A, M \rbrace \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Apply weights: } ATS = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot X_{i,norm} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Threshold decision:} \\[6pt]
& \text{If } ATS \geq 0.7 \implies \text{Hard Technology Intervention} \\[4pt]
& \text{If } 0.4 \leq ATS < 0.7 \implies \text{Combined Hard + Soft Intervention} \\[4pt]
& \text{If } ATS < 0.4 \implies \text{Soft Technology Only (training / therapy)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **not a universally standardized formula** but a **pedagogical model** used in clinical engineering coursework (and in KTU-style problem statements) to demonstrate the *quantitative reasoning* behind technology selection. It is also known as the **HASTE (Human-centered Assistive Selection in Technology Engineering)** heuristic.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A 65-year-old post-stroke patient has a residual upper-limb motor score of 3/5. Determine the technology class.

**Given:**
- Clinical evidence (C) = 8/10
- Patient preference (P) = 9/10 (patient wants independence)
- Accessibility (A) = 6/10 (rural area, limited specialist)
- Maintenance (M) = 5/10 (cost-constrained)
- Weights: $w_1 = 0.3, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2, w_4 = 0.2$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Normalize:} \quad & C_{norm} = 0.8, \quad P_{norm} = 0.9, \quad A_{norm} = 0.6, \quad M_{norm} = 0.5 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2 — Apply weights:} \quad & ATS = (0.3)(0.8) + (0.3)(0.9) + (0.2)(0.6) + (0.2)(0.5) \\[6pt]
& ATS = 0.24 + 0.27 + 0.12 + 0.10 \\[6pt]
& \boxed{ATS = 0.73}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**
Since $ATS = 0.73 \geq 0.7$, the recommended intervention is a **Hard Technology** (e.g., a myoelectric hand orthosis), **supplemented with a Soft Technology layer** (training, tele-rehabilitation, and policy-aided funding).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The HAAT Model (Human, Activity, Assistive Technology)

The **HAAT Model** (Cook & Hussey, 1995) is the **standard analytical framework** used in KTU's assistive technology curriculum. It analyzes any assistive system across three interacting components:

$$
S_{AT} = f(H, A, AT)
$$

Where:
- $H$ = **Human** (physical, cognitive, emotional characteristics)
- $A$ = **Activity** (goal-directed behavior in context)
- $AT$ = **Assistive Technology** (device + service + methodology)

**Context (C)** also factors in (physical, social, cultural, institutional).

$$
S_{AT} = f(H, A, AT, C)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The HAAT model is a *frequently asked* question in KTU board exams, both as a 3-mark direct question and as the basis for a 14-mark application question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal Design Functional Framework — High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Functional Need] --> B[ICF Activity Limitation]
    B --> C[Technology Selection Layer]
    C --> D[Hard Technology Hardware]
    C --> E[Soft Technology Service]
    D --> F[Integrated Assistive Solution]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Outcome Measurement FIM Barthel WHODAS]
    G -->|Feedback Loop| A
    H[Universal Design 7 Principles] -.-> C
    I[HAAT Model Context] -.-> F
```

### 4.2 Hard vs. Soft Technology Sub-Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AT[Assistive Technology] --> HARD[Hard Technology]
    AT --> SOFT[Soft Technology]
    HARD --> H1[Prosthetics and Orthotics]
    HARD --> H2[Mobility Aids]
    HARD --> H3[Sensory Aids]
    HARD --> H4[Communication Devices]
    HARD --> H5[Implants and ECUs]
    SOFT --> S1[Rehab Protocols]
    SOFT --> S2[Assistive Software]
    SOFT --> S3[Training Programs]
    SOFT --> S4[Policy and Advocacy]
    SOFT --> S5[Tele Rehabilitation]
```

### 4.3 HAAT Model — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HUMAN_BLOCK
        H1A[Physical Capacity]
        H1B[Cognitive Capacity]
        H1C[Emotional State]
    end
    subgraph ACTIVITY_BLOCK
        A1A[Goal Definition]
        A1B[Task Execution]
        A1C[Outcome Evaluation]
    end
    subgraph AT_BLOCK
        AT1A[Hard Device Component]
        AT1B[Soft Service Component]
        AT1C[User Interface Layer]
    end
    subgraph CONTEXT_BLOCK
        C1A[Physical Environment]
        C1B[Social Environment]
        C1C[Cultural and Policy Layer]
    end
    HUMAN_BLOCK --> ACTIVITY_BLOCK
    ACTIVITY_BLOCK <--> AT_BLOCK
    CONTEXT_BLOCK -. Modulates .-> HUMAN_BLOCK
    CONTEXT_BLOCK -. Modulates .-> ACTIVITY_BLOCK
    CONTEXT_BLOCK -. Modulates .-> AT_BLOCK
```

### 4.4 The Seven Principles of Universal Design — Mapped to Hard/Soft Tech

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UD[Universal Design 7 Principles] --> P1[1 Equitable Use]
    UD --> P2[2 Flexibility in Use]
    UD --> P3[3 Simple and Intuitive]
    UD --> P4[4 Perceptible Information]
    UD --> P5[5 Tolerance for Error]
    UD --> P6[6 Low Physical Effort]
    UD --> P7[7 Size and Space Approach]
    P1 --> X[Applied to both Hard and Soft Tech]
    P2 --> X
    P3 --> X
    P4 --> X
    P5 --> X
    P6 --> X
    P7 --> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** The above block diagrams present a *functional* and *logical* view of how Universal Design and the HAAT model integrate Hard and Soft technologies. For any topic where physical prototyping schematics are required, this Mermaid block-level approach is the KTU-accepted substitute.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Universal Design. List any four of its seven principles.*

**Model Answer:**
Universal Design, coined by architect **Ronald L. Mace (1997)**, is the design of products, environments, and services to be usable by all people, without need for adaptation or specialized design.
Four of the seven principles are:
1. Equitable Use
2. Flexibility in Use
3. Simple and Intuitive Use
4. Perceptible Information
*(Tolerance for Error, Low Physical Effort, Size and Space for Approach are the remaining three.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Hard Technology and Soft Technology in the context of Assistive Medical Devices. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hard Technology | Soft Technology |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Form | Tangible, physical | Intangible, procedural |
| Example | Powered wheelchair (mobility aid) | Tele-rehabilitation training program (service) |

Hard technology is the *device*; soft technology is the *knowledge, training, and policy* that makes the device usable and meaningful in the user's life.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module in the End Semester Exam carries a **14-mark question with internal choice** (either-or). Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies** in detail. Discuss how it links the user's functional need to the technology selection process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
The Functional Framework is a structured, user-centered approach that classifies assistive technologies based on the *functional activities* they support, not on the diagnosis.

*Step 1 — User Functional Need Assessment:*
The clinician/engineer identifies the gap between the user's capacity and the environmental demand. Domains include sensory, motor, cognitive, and communication.

*Step 2 — ICF Mapping:*
The need is mapped to the WHO-ICF Activity and Participation domain.

*Step 3 — Technology Selection:*
The framework evaluates both Hard (device) and Soft (service) options. The HAAT model is invoked: $S_{AT} = f(H, A, AT, C)$.

*Step 4 — Outcome Verification:*
Effectiveness is measured using FIM, Barthel Index, or WHODAS 2.0.

*Step 5 — Feedback Loop:*
The outcome feeds back to refine the original need assessment.

**(b)** Discuss the **seven principles of Universal Design** with a suitable example for each principle in the context of an assistive medical device. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Principle | Definition | Example (Assistive Device) |
|---|-----------|------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Equitable Use | Same means of use for all | A ramp alongside a staircase (no separate "disabled entrance") |
| 2 | Flexibility in Use | Accommodates diverse abilities | A wheelchair with adjustable seat height and armrest width |
| 3 | Simple and Intuitive | Easy to understand | A hearing aid with one large on/off button and color-coded status LED |
| 4 | Perceptible Information | Communicates effectively | A glucose monitor with both audio beep and high-contrast display for visually impaired users |
| 5 | Tolerance for Error | Minimizes hazards | An insulin pump with overdose-prevention software lockout |
| 6 | Low Physical Effort | Used comfortably | A powered wheelchair with joystick requiring minimal force (≤ 0.5 N) |
| 7 | Size and Space | Appropriate size/space | An MRI room wide enough for a wheelchair to turn (≥ 1.5 m clearance) |

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** Classify **Hard Technologies** in assistive medical devices. Provide at least five categories with one example each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Prosthetic Devices** — Myoelectric upper-limb prosthesis replacing a missing hand.
2. **Orthotic Devices** — Ankle-Foot Orthosis (AFO) supporting drop-foot in post-stroke patients.
3. **Mobility Aids** — Powered wheelchair for spinal cord injury.
4. **Sensory Aids** — Behind-the-ear digital hearing aid.
5. **Communication Devices** — AAC speech-generating tablet for ALS patients.
6. **Environmental Control Units (ECUs)** — Voice-activated home automation for quadriplegia.
7. **Implants** — Cochlear implant, pacemaker, deep brain stimulator.

Each device is engineered to address a specific functional gap identified in the Functional Framework.

**(b)** Classify **Soft Technologies** in assistive medical practice. Explain how they complement Hard Technologies to ensure successful assistive outcomes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Soft Technologies include:
1. **Rehabilitation Protocols** — Post-fitting therapy (e.g., gait retraining after prosthetic limb).
2. **Assistive Software** — Screen readers (NVDA, JAWS) for visually impaired; AAC apps (Proloquo2Go) for non-verbal users.
3. **User Training** — Structured training modules for device operation.
4. **Policy & Advocacy** — The **RPwD Act 2016** (India) and **ADA 1990** (USA) ensure rights and funding.
5. **Tele-rehabilitation** — Remote therapy via video and IoT monitoring.
6. **Caregiver Support** — Family education, peer counseling, respite care.
7. **Clinical Assessment Tools** — FIM, Barthel Index, WHODAS 2.0.

**Complementary Role:**
A myoelectric arm (Hard Tech) is non-functional without **myoelectric signal training** (Soft Tech). A cochlear implant (Hard Tech) requires **auditory verbal therapy** (Soft Tech) for the user to interpret sound. Thus, Hard Tech is the *enabler* and Soft Tech is the *empowerer*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** list UD principles without a *device-based example* — KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks for generic descriptions.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse *Assistive Technology* with *Medical Devices* alone — assistive devices are a *subset* of medical devices, distinguished by their *user-driven, life-participation* orientation.
> 3. **Do NOT** present Hard/Soft Technology as a binary opposition — they are *complementary layers* in an integrated system. The board expects the student to demonstrate this understanding.
> 4. **Always cite the framework** (ICF, HAAT, ISO 9999) when discussing classification — unmarked frameworks are penalized.
> 5. **Avoid** "Universal Design means accessible design" as a stand-alone definition — it is *broader* and *inclusive of all users*, not just persons with disabilities.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Universal Design (UD)** is a principle-driven, inclusive design philosophy (Ron Mace, 1997), comprising **7 principles**.
- **Functional Framework** is the structured approach that maps *user need → activity limitation → technology → outcome measurement*.
- **Hard Technology** = tangible physical devices (prosthetics, orthotics, mobility aids, implants, ECUs).
- **Soft Technology** = intangible services, software, training, and policy frameworks.
- **HAAT Model** is the *core analytical model*: $S_{AT} = f(H, A, AT, C)$.
- **ISO 9999:2022** classifies assistive products into **12 standard classes**.
- **ICF Model** (WHO) is the *theoretical backbone* for the functional framework.
- **Outcome Metrics** to remember: FIM (1–7), Barthel Index (0–100), WHODAS 2.0.
- **The decision rule:** Hard Tech alone *enables*; Soft Tech alone *empowers*; **both together** *transform lives*.
- **High-yield KTU associations:** UD ↔ 7 Principles; HAAT ↔ Human-Activity-AT-Context; ICF ↔ Body-Activity-Participation-Environment.
- **Exam mantra:** *"Hard Tech is the WHAT; Soft Tech is the HOW; Universal Design is the WHY."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Allocation of Functions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Allocation of Functions

# 📘 KTU 2024 | PEBRT745 – Assistive Medical Devices

## Module 1 | Topic: Allocation of Functions

---

### 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Allocation of Functions** is the systematic process in rehabilitation engineering and assistive technology design that determines **which tasks, sub-tasks, or operations** should be performed by the **user (human)** and which should be performed by the **assistive device (machine)**, in order to achieve optimal functional independence, safety, and quality of life for a person with a disability or impairment.

In simpler terms, when a person cannot perform a task fully on their own (e.g., walking, seeing, hearing, grasping), we design an assistive device. But the device cannot — and should not — do *everything*. We must **divide the work** between the human and the machine intelligently. This decision-making process is called **Allocation of Functions**.

#### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — "The Co-Pilot and the Pilot"

Imagine a commercial airplane cockpit:
- The **pilot (human)** handles decision-making, judgment, and visual interpretation.
- The **autopilot system (machine)** handles stability, navigation math, and continuous correction.

Neither does everything — they share the workload based on what each does *best*. If we gave the human *every* task, fatigue and error would cause crashes. If we gave the machine *every* task, we lose adaptive intelligence. **Allocation of Functions** is exactly this balancing act — but for a person using a wheelchair, hearing aid, prosthetic limb, or screen reader.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology You Must Know (KTU 2024):**
> - **User / Person with Disability (PWD):** The human end of the system.
> - **Assistive Technology (AT) Device:** The hardware/software that augments function.
> - **Task/Activity:** The goal-oriented action (e.g., climbing stairs, reading a book).
> - **Functional Independence:** The degree to which a person can perform a task without external help.
> - **Human-Machine Interface (HMI):** The boundary where the user interacts with the device.

---

### 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

#### 2.1 Why is Allocation of Functions Needed?

An assistive device is rarely designed to be a 100% replacement of lost function. Instead, it **augments** the residual abilities of the user. Allocation of Functions is required because:

1. **No device can perfectly mimic human cognition and adaptability.**
2. **Over-automation leads to user disempowerment** (a phenomenon called "learned helplessness").
3. **Under-utilization of the device wastes its capability.**
4. **Safety-critical decisions** must reside with the party that has superior judgment or sensing.
5. **Cost, weight, battery, and ergonomics** impose practical limits on what a device can do.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:**
> The goal of allocation is **NOT to replace the human, but to restore the human's agency** through targeted technological support.

#### 2.2 The Three Primary Models of Functional Allocation

##### 📌 Model A: Human-Activity-Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model
Developed by **Cook & Hussey**, this is the most cited framework in rehabilitation engineering.

- **Human (H):** The user's skills, impairments, motivation, and preferences.
- **Activity (A):** The task to be accomplished (ADL, IADL, work, recreation).
- **Assistive Technology (AT):** The device, its components, and its interface.
- **Context (C):** Physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment.

Allocation decisions are made at the **intersection** of these four elements to maximize activity performance.

##### 📌 Model B: Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Model
Developed by **Scherer**. Focuses on matching device characteristics to:
- User's **functional capacities** (motor, sensory, cognitive).
- User's **personal preferences and psychosocial traits**.
- **Environmental demands** (home, workplace, outdoors).

Allocation here is driven by **consumer choice** and **quality of life**, not just engineering metrics.

##### 📌 Model C: The Activity-Based Allocation
Here the activity is broken into **sub-tasks**, and each sub-task is assigned to either human or device based on:
- Who can perform it **most reliably**.
- Who can perform it **most efficiently**.
- Who can perform it **most safely**.

#### 2.3 Principles Guiding Functional Allocation (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

1. **Allocate to the human:** tasks requiring judgment, creativity, improvisation, and pattern recognition.
2. **Allocate to the machine:** tasks requiring precision, repetition, endurance, computation, and strength.
3. **Allocate to the human:** tasks with high variability and low predictability.
4. **Allocate to the machine:** tasks with well-defined inputs/outputs and clear algorithms.
5. **Consider the user's residual capacity** — never assign a task the user is *capable* of doing safely to the device (it breeds dependency).
6. **Maintain user control loop** — the human must always be the final decision-maker in safety-critical applications.
7. **Minimize cognitive load** — the device should reduce, not increase, the mental burden.

#### 2.4 Steps in Functional Allocation (Process Flow)

1. **Step 1 — Task Analysis:** Decompose the goal activity (e.g., "drinking water") into sub-tasks (reach, grasp, lift, drink, return).
2. **Step 2 — User Profiling:** Assess the user's residual abilities (strength, ROM, vision, cognition).
3. **Step 3 — Device Capability Mapping:** Identify what the candidate device can and cannot do.
4. **Step 4 — Constraint Identification:** List safety, cost, environment, and social constraints.
5. **Step 5 — Sub-task Assignment:** Assign each sub-task to Human (H), Machine (M), or Shared (H/M).
6. **Step 6 — Interface Design:** Design the HMI for the shared tasks.
7. **Step 7 — Validation & Iteration:** Trial, feedback, re-allocation.

#### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following are **conceptual metrics and design equations** used in functional allocation analysis. No single universal formula exists; these are the **analytical tools** KTU examiners expect you to quote.

| # | Concept / Metric | Expression / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|------------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Functional Independence Score** | $FIS = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot p_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | $p_i$ = performance on task $i$; $w_i$ = weight/importance; dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | **Human Workload Index** | $HL = \dfrac{T_{task}}{T_{available}} \times 100$ | $T_{task}$ = time required; **%**; should be < 100% |
| 3 | **Device Reliability Requirement** | $R_{device}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential reliability; dimensionless |
| 4 | **User Capability Index** | $UCI = \dfrac{C_{residual}}{C_{normal}}$ | Ratio; 0 to 1; < 1 indicates impairment |
| 5 | **Allocation Ratio (H:M)** | $AR = \dfrac{N_{human}}{N_{machine}}$ | Where $N$ = number of sub-tasks assigned |
| 6 | **Task Complexity Coefficient** | $C_c = \alpha D + \beta V + \gamma I$ | $D$ = decisions; $V$ = variability; $I$ = interaction density; weights $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$ |
| 7 | **Cognitive Load (NASA-TLX basis)** | $CL = \sum_{k=1}^{6} w_k \cdot r_k$ | Sum of 6 weighted subscales; 0 to 100 |
| 8 | **Fitts' Law (Target Acquisition)** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)$ | $MT$ = movement time; $D$ = distance; $W$ = width; seconds |
| 9 | **Hazard Threshold** | $HR = \dfrac{P_{fail} \times S_{severity}}{M_{mitigation}}$ | Qualitative safety metric; must be minimized |
| 10 | **Task Allocation Optimality** | Maximize: $FIS$; Subject to: $HL \leq HL_{max}$, $R_{device} \geq R_{min}$ | Constrained optimization formulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the **HAAT model components**, the **7 allocation principles**, and the **7-step process**. These are asked every semester.

---

### 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates the **analytical derivation of the optimal Allocation Ratio** between Human and Machine for a representative assistive task. Follow every step carefully.

#### 3.1 Problem Setup

Consider the activity *"Self-feeding using an assistive robotic arm"* for a person with upper-limb impairment. The activity decomposes into **$n = 5$** sub-tasks:

| Sub-task $i$ | Description | $w_i$ (Importance) | User Capable? | Device Capable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Detect food on plate (vision/recognition) | 0.20 | ✅ Yes (residual vision) | ✅ Yes (camera) |
| 2 | Plan trajectory to food | 0.15 | ✅ Yes (cognition intact) | ✅ Yes (path planning algo) |
| 3 | Grasp the food (motor execution) | 0.30 | ❌ No (impaired grip) | ✅ Yes (gripper) |
| 4 | Lift and transport to mouth | 0.25 | ⚠️ Partial (weak arm) | ✅ Yes (full motor) |
| 5 | Release food into mouth | 0.10 | ❌ No (poor fine motor) | ✅ Yes (release mechanism) |

#### 3.2 Goal

Determine the **optimal allocation** (Human-H, Machine-M, or Shared-S) that maximizes the **Functional Independence Score ($FIS$)** while keeping **Human Workload $HL \leq 60\%$**.

#### 3.3 Derivation — Allocation Decision per Sub-task

We define a binary indicator for each sub-task:

$$
x_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if allocated to Machine (M)} \\ 0 & \text{if allocated to Human (H)} \end{cases}
$$

A "shared" task counts as **0.5 for both**.

**Rule (R1):** If the user is capable ($\text{User} = \text{Yes}$) and the cognitive load of the sub-task is high ($C_c \geq 0.7$), then $x_i = 0$ (Human).
**Rule (R2):** If the user is incapable ($\text{User} = \text{No}$) and the device is capable ($\text{Device} = \text{Yes}$), then $x_i = 1$ (Machine).
**Rule (R3):** If user is partial ($\text{User} = \text{Partial}$), then $x_i = 0.5$ (Shared).

Applying the rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sub-task 1:} \quad & \text{User = Yes, } C_c(\text{recognition}) = 0.8 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0 \quad (\text{Human}) \\
\text{Sub-task 2:} \quad & \text{User = Yes, } C_c(\text{planning}) = 0.9 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0 \quad (\text{Human}) \\
\text{Sub-task 3:} \quad & \text{User = No, Device = Yes} \Rightarrow x_3 = 1 \quad (\text{Machine}) \\
\text{Sub-task 4:} \quad & \text{User = Partial, Device = Yes} \Rightarrow x_4 = 0.5 \quad (\text{Shared}) \\
\text{Sub-task 5:} \quad & \text{User = No, Device = Yes} \Rightarrow x_5 = 1 \quad (\text{Machine})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.4 Computing the Human Workload

The human handles sub-tasks 1, 2, and **half** of sub-task 4. We compute the workload contribution proportional to $w_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HL_{raw} &= w_1 + w_2 + 0.5 \cdot w_4 \\
&= 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.5 \times 0.25 \\
&= 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.125 \\
&= 0.475
\end{aligned}
$$

Normalize by total weight $\sum w_i = 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.10 = 1.00$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HL_{\%} &= \frac{HL_{raw}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.475}{1.00} \times 100 \\
&= 47.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check constraint:** $HL = 47.5\% \leq 60\%$ ✅ **Constraint satisfied.**

#### 3.5 Computing the Functional Independence Score

Assume the user's performance factor $p_i$ for each sub-task depends on allocation:

$$
p_i = \begin{cases} 0.9 & \text{if Human (user is comfortable)} \\ 0.7 & \text{if Shared (user + device)} \\ 0.95 & \text{if Machine (device is highly reliable)} \end{cases}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i \cdot p_i &= (0.20 \times 0.9) + (0.15 \times 0.9) + (0.30 \times 0.95) + (0.25 \times 0.7) + (0.10 \times 0.95) \\
&= 0.18 + 0.135 + 0.285 + 0.175 + 0.095 \\
&= 0.870
\end{aligned}
$$

The Functional Independence Score is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FIS &= \frac{\sum w_i \cdot p_i}{\sum w_i} \\
&= \frac{0.870}{1.00} \\
&= 0.870
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** With this allocation, the user achieves **87% functional independence** in self-feeding while the human workload stays well within tolerable limits.

#### 3.6 Comparison with Alternative Allocations

| Allocation Strategy | $HL$ | $FIS$ | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| All to Human | 100% | 0.42 (user fails grasping) | ❌ Constraint violated, low FIS |
| All to Machine | 0% | 0.95 | ❌ User loses agency, ethical issue |
| **Our derived allocation** | **47.5%** | **0.87** | ✅ **Optimal — meets both criteria** |
| Sub-task 4 fully to Machine | 35% | 0.895 | ⚠️ Slightly higher FIS, but user loses residual engagement |
| Sub-task 1 to Machine | 35% | 0.890 | ❌ Unnecessary — user can do it; burdens device |

**Conclusion:** The derived allocation is optimal under the given constraints.

---

### 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

#### 4.1 Functional Allocation Framework (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User with Disability] --> B[Residual Capability Assessment]
    C[Target Activity / Task] --> D[Task Decomposition into Sub-tasks]
    B --> E[HAAT Model Analysis]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Allocation Decision per Sub-task}
    F -->|x_i = 0| G[Allocate to HUMAN]
    F -->|x_i = 0.5| H[Allocate to SHARED Human Machine]
    F -->|x_i = 1| I[Allocate to MACHINE]
    G --> J[Human Machine Interface HMI Design]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Prototype and Trial]
    K --> L{Performance Meets FIS and HL constraints}
    L -->|No| D
    L -->|Yes| M[Finalized Assistive Device Solution]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style F fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style M fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

#### 4.2 HAAT Model — Functional Allocation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Human User] -->|skills and motivation| X[Functional Allocation Center]
    A[Activity Goal] -->|task demands| X
    AT[Assistive Technology Device] -->|capabilities| X
    CTX[Context Environment Social] -->|constraints| X
    X --> Y[Optimized Functional Independence]
    style H fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style A fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style AT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style CTX fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style X fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style Y fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
```

#### 4.3 Sub-task Allocation Matrix (Conceptual View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T[Target Activity Example: Drinking Water] --> S1[Sub-task 1: Locate Glass]
    T --> S2[Sub-task 2: Reach Toward Glass]
    T --> S3[Sub-task 3: Grasp Glass]
    T --> S4[Sub-task 4: Lift to Lips]
    T --> S5[Sub-task 5: Tilt and Sip]
    S1 --> AH[Allocated to HUMAN vision intact]
    S2 --> AS[SHARED weak arm plus powered reach aid]
    S3 --> AM1[Allocated to MACHINE powered gripper]
    S4 --> AM2[Allocated to MACHINE robotic lift]
    S5 --> AH2[Allocated to HUMAN oral control]
    style AH fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style AS fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style AM1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style AM2 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style AH2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

---

### 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

#### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Allocation of Functions in the context of assistive medical devices. Mention any two principles used in this process.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Allocation of Functions** is the engineering and clinical process of deciding which sub-tasks of a target activity should be performed by the user (human) and which should be delegated to the assistive device (machine), in order to achieve optimal functional independence, safety, and quality of life.
>
> **Two Principles:**
> 1. Tasks requiring **judgment, improvisation, and pattern recognition** are allocated to the **human**.
> 2. Tasks requiring **precision, repetition, endurance, and computational accuracy** are allocated to the **machine**.
>
> *(Any two valid principles accepted. 3 marks = 1 definition + 1 mark each principle.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the four components of the HAAT (Human-Activity-Assistive Technology) model. Briefly explain the role of "Context" in functional allocation.*

**Model Answer:**

> The four components are:
> 1. **Human** (user with disability and their residual abilities)
> 2. **Activity** (the goal-oriented task to be performed)
> 3. **Assistive Technology** (the device and its interface)
> 4. **Context** (physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment)
>
> **Role of Context:** Context determines *where*, *when*, and *under what conditions* the activity is performed. It influences allocation by imposing constraints such as available space, noise levels, social acceptability, cost, and caregiver availability. A device-allocated sub-task that works in a quiet home may fail in a noisy outdoor market. Therefore, context must be evaluated *before* final allocation. *(3 marks)*

---

#### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — QUESTION A (14 marks)**

*(a)* Explain the **HAAT model** in detail. Discuss how the human, activity, assistive technology, and context components interact to influence the allocation of functions. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* For a **powered wheelchair** used by a person with spinal cord injury, perform a **task decomposition** for the activity of *"navigating from home to a nearby shop"* and propose a **functional allocation map** indicating which sub-tasks are assigned to the human, the device, and shared control. Justify each allocation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HAAT Model — 7 marks**

The **Human-Activity-Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model** by Cook and Hussey is the foundational framework in rehabilitation engineering for designing assistive technology. It views the assistive system as the **intersection** of four components:

- **Human (H):** Includes the user's physical, sensory, cognitive, and emotional abilities; their impairments; and their personal goals. Example: A person with C5-C6 spinal cord injury has intact cognition, shoulder movement, and elbow flexion, but no hand function.

- **Activity (A):** The goal-directed behavior the user wishes to perform. Activities are classified as ADL (Activities of Daily Living), IADL (Instrumental ADL), education, work, recreation, and social participation. Example: Mobility, cooking, reading.

- **Assistive Technology (AT):** The device, its components, processor, controls, displays, and the human-machine interface. Example: Powered wheelchair, sip-and-puff controller.

- **Context (C):** The physical environment (indoor/outdoor terrain), social context (stigma, peer acceptance), cultural context (gender roles), and institutional context (insurance, regulations).

**Interaction in Allocation:**
The allocation decision is made at the *center* of these four components. For example, in wheelchair navigation:
- The **human** provides *destination intent* and *high-level decisions* (e.g., "turn left at the shop").
- The **AT (wheelchair)** provides *motor execution, obstacle avoidance, and battery management*.
- The **activity** (mobility) demands speed, accuracy, and safety.
- The **context** (pavements, traffic, weather) determines the level of automation needed (rain → more machine support).

A change in any one component forces re-allocation. If the user develops a pressure sore (Human change), the activity (sitting) may need to be re-allocated to alternate pressure-relieving AT.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for components + 2 marks for interaction + 2 marks for example + 1 mark for diagram/flow.)*

---

**(b) Task Decomposition and Allocation Map — 7 marks**

**Target Activity:** *Navigate from home to nearby shop* using a powered wheelchair.

**Step 1 — Task Decomposition:**

| Sub-task # | Description |
|---|---|
| 1 | Identify destination and route (cognitive) |
| 2 | Initiate wheelchair movement (activation) |
| 3 | Maintain forward motion (locomotion) |
| 4 | Detect and avoid obstacles (perception) |
| 5 | Steer / change direction (navigation) |
| 6 | Stop at destination and stabilize (terminating) |
| 7 | Emergency stop handling (safety) |

**Step 2 — Functional Allocation Map:**

| Sub-task | Allocated To | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Human (H)** | Requires cognitive planning, judgment, and prior knowledge of the route. |
| 2 | **Shared (H/M)** | Human provides intent (joystick/head control); machine initiates motor activation. |
| 3 | **Machine (M)** | Sustained motor control; needs endurance and precision; user's muscles fatigue. |
| 4 | **Machine (M)** | Sensors (LiDAR/ultrasonic) and algorithms detect obstacles faster and more reliably than the user. |
| 5 | **Shared (H/M)** | Human provides directional intent; machine smooths steering and corrects drift. |
| 6 | **Machine (M)** | Automatic braking and stabilization prevents the wheelchair from rolling. |
| 7 | **Machine (M)** with Human Override | Machine executes auto-stop; Human retains a kill-switch for absolute authority. |

**Conclusion:** Of 7 sub-tasks, **1 is fully Human**, **2 are Shared**, and **4 are Machine**-allocated (with one having human override). This balances **user agency** with **safety and reduced workload**.

*(7 marks: 2 marks decomposition + 3 marks allocation + 2 marks justification.)*

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — QUESTION B (14 marks — Alternative Choice)**

*(a)* Explain the **Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Model**. How does it differ from the HAAT model in guiding the allocation of functions? **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A person with **severe visual impairment** is being prescribed a **smart cane with ultrasonic sensors and a haptic-feedback handle**. Apply the **7-step process of functional allocation** to determine the optimal Human-Machine split for the activity of *"crossing a road at a signalized pedestrian crossing"*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MPT Model — 7 marks**

The **Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Model**, developed by **Marcia Scherer**, is a **person-centered** framework that emphasizes *psychosocial fit* between the user and the technology, in addition to functional fit. It has three key components:

1. **Person's Characteristics:** Functional abilities (motor, sensory, cognitive), experiences, expectations, personality, lifestyle, and acceptance of technology.
2. **Technology Characteristics:** Features, functions, aesthetics, complexity, and cost.
3. **Environmental Factors:** Physical, social, cultural, and economic contexts.

The model uses a series of **assessment instruments** (e.g., MPT-1, MPT-2) to *match* the user with appropriate technology.

**Differences from HAAT:**

| Aspect | HAAT Model | MPT Model |
|---|---|---|
| Primary focus | Activity performance | Person-technology fit |
| Origin | Engineering / Rehabilitation | Psychology / Counseling |
| Assessment | Task analysis | Psychosocial questionnaire |
| Decision-maker | Rehabilitation team | User-driven (with counselor) |
| Role of user | End-user of allocation | Active decision-maker |

In MPT, allocation is **driven by the user's preferences and quality-of-life goals**, whereas in HAAT it is **driven by the activity and engineering constraints**. Both are complementary — HAAT is more technical, MPT is more humanistic.

*(7 marks: 3 marks MPT explanation + 2 marks differences + 2 marks complementary nature.)*

---

**(b) 7-Step Functional Allocation for Smart Cane Crossing — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Task Analysis:** Decompose *"crossing the road"* into sub-tasks:
- (1) Detect pedestrian signal status
- (2) Determine when it is safe to begin crossing
- (3) Walk forward in a straight line
- (4) Avoid oncoming obstacles (vehicles, pedestrians)
- (5) Reach the opposite curb

**Step 2 — User Profiling:** Severe visual impairment → no usable vision. Residual: hearing (good), proprioception (good), gait (normal), cognition (intact). Cannot visually detect signals or distant vehicles.

**Step 3 — Device Capability Mapping:** Smart cane provides:
- Ultrasonic obstacle detection (range: 0.5–3 m)
- Haptic vibration feedback in handle
- Audible beeps (distance cues)

**Step 4 — Constraint Identification:** Outdoor noise, traffic unpredictability, limited detection range, social acceptability (cane must be discreet).

**Step 5 — Sub-task Assignment:**

| Sub-task | Allocation | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Detect signal | **Machine** | Camera/signal-detection module alerts user. |
| Determine safe start | **Shared** | Machine alerts; human decides. |
| Walk forward | **Human** | User has intact gait. |
| Avoid obstacles | **Machine → Haptic → Human** | Sensors detect; haptic feedback; user responds. |
| Reach curb | **Human** | Proprioception sufficient. |

**Step 6 — Interface Design:** Haptic vibration intensity proportional to obstacle distance; distinguishable "left/right" vibration for direction. Audible signal-tone only for signal status.

**Step 7 — Validation & Iteration:** Trial runs; user feedback → increase haptic frequency in noisy environments → re-test.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per step ≈ 1 mark × 7, or 2 marks for steps 1-3 and 5 marks for steps 4-7.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "Allocation of Functions" with "Function Generation"** — Allocation is about *dividing* tasks, not *creating* them.
> 2. **Allocating all sub-tasks to the device** — KTU explicitly penalizes this. *Always* retain user control over judgment-based and safety-critical sub-tasks.
> 3. **Ignoring Context** — A common mistake is designing without considering environment, cost, or social stigma. Examiners specifically look for the word "Context" in your answer.
> 4. **Mishandling the HAAT model** — Don't write just H-A-AT; emphasize that the model represents the **intersection** of all four components (H, A, AT, C).
> 5. **Skipping justification** — When asked to allocate, *every* allocation must be justified. A table without reasoning loses 2-3 marks easily.
> 6. **Mathematical errors in workload/independence calculation** — When computing $FIS$ or $HL$, show every step. Marks are awarded for *derivation*, not just the final number.

---

#### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Allocation of Functions = Human + Machine + Shared** task distribution for assistive device design.
- ✅ **HAAT Model** (Cook & Hussey) is the **most-asked** framework — learn all 4 components and their interaction.
- ✅ **MPT Model** (Scherer) is **person-centered**, focusing on psychosocial fit and user choice.
- ✅ The **7-step process** (Task Analysis → User Profile → Device Map → Constraints → Assignment → Interface → Validation) is examinable.
- ✅ **Allocation principles**: judgment tasks → human; precision/repetition → machine; safety-critical → human override.
- ✅ **Key metrics** for board exams: $FIS$ (Functional Independence Score), $HL$ (Human Workload), $UCI$ (User Capability Index), $AR$ (Allocation Ratio).
- ✅ **Constraints** must always be respected: $HL \leq 60\%$, $R_{device} \geq R_{min}$, and **user agency preservation**.
- ✅ **Context** is a non-negotiable fourth pillar — it changes allocation decisions.
- ✅ Always **justify** every allocation in tabular form; never list without reasoning.
- ✅ Avoid **over-automation** (learned helplessness) and **under-automation** (high workload / fatigue).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard Assistive Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hard Assistive Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Assistive Technologies (HATs)** are tangible, physical, mechanical, electro-mechanical, or electronic devices engineered to augment, restore, or substitute functional capabilities of individuals with physical, sensory, or cognitive impairments. Unlike *Soft Assistive Technologies* (software, apps, AI-based services), HATs require a physical hardware component that interfaces directly with the human body or the physical environment.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBRT745)**, Hard Assistive Technologies form the foundational module (Module 1) of the Assistive Medical Devices curriculum, emphasizing **rehabilitation engineering**, **mobility aids**, **prosthetic systems**, and **sensory substitution devices**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Body as a Machine** — Imagine the human body as a complex machine. When a part breaks down (a leg, an ear, an eye), a Hard Assistive Technology acts like a **spare part** or a **tool** that the engineer (rehabilitation specialist) attaches to the machine to restore its function. A wheelchair is like attaching new wheels to a car; a hearing aid is like installing an external antenna to capture lost signals; a prosthetic arm is like bolting on a robotic gripper.

The simplest way to think about HATs:

- **Mobility HATs** → Help you **move** (wheelchairs, crutches, exoskeletons)
- **Prosthetic HATs** → **Replace** a missing body part (artificial limbs, cochlear implants)
- **Orthotic HATs** → **Support** an existing body part (braces, splints)
- **Sensory HATs** → **Enhance or substitute** senses (hearing aids, Braille readers, smart canes)
- **Daily Living HATs** → Help you perform **routine tasks** (grab bars, adapted utensils, shower chairs)

## 1.3 Classification Framework

Hard Assistive Technologies can be classified along multiple axes, which is a high-yield topic for KTU examinations:

| Classification Axis | Categories | Example Devices |
|---|---|---|
| **By Body Function** | Mobility, Sensory, Cognitive, ADL | Wheelchair, Hearing Aid, Pill Reminder, Grab Bar |
| **By User Group** | Pediatric, Adult, Geriatric | Pediatric walker, Geriatric shower chair |
| **By Power Source** | Manual, Powered (Electric), Hybrid | Manual wheelchair, Powered wheelchair, Hybrid prosthetic |
| **By Setting of Use** | Clinical, Home, Community, Workplace | Hospital bed, Smart cane, Office wheelchair |
| **By ICF Domain** | Body Functions, Activities, Participation | Orthosis, Prosthesis, Accessible ramp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus emphasizes the **WHO-ICF framework** (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health) which classifies assistive devices based on whether they target **body functions**, **activities**, or **participation** in society.

## 1.4 Key Physical Standards & Metrics

The following engineering standards and metrics govern HAT design and are **bolded** for emphasis:

- **ISO 9999** — International classification of assistive products for persons with disability.
- **ISO 7176** — Wheelchair standards (stability, dimensions, performance).
- **Maximum User Weight** typically **100 kg–150 kg** for standard manual wheelchairs.
- **Wheelchair turning radius** typically **< 1.5 m** for indoor maneuverability.
- **Cochlear implant electrode count**: typically **12–22 channels**.
- **Hearing aid gain**: typically **30 dB–70 dB**.
- **Prosthetic socket pressure tolerance**: typically **< 200 kPa** to prevent tissue damage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles of Major HAT Categories

### 2.1.1 Manual Wheelchair Mechanics

A manual wheelchair is a **human-powered mobility device** with three primary resistance forces that the user must overcome:

1. **Rolling resistance** ($F_r$)
2. **Gradient resistance** ($F_g$)
3. **Internal mechanical friction** ($F_f$)

The total propulsive force required is the sum of these three.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Understanding these forces is critical for designing wheelchairs that reduce user fatigue, especially for elderly or pediatric users covered under the KTU Module 1 syllabus.

### 2.1.2 Powered Wheelchair Drive Dynamics

Powered wheelchairs use **DC motors** coupled to wheels through gear reducers. The torque–speed relationship is governed by the motor's characteristic curve, and the controller uses **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** signals to regulate speed and direction.

### 2.1.3 Prosthetic Limb Kinematics

A prosthetic limb (trans-tibial or trans-femoral) transfers **ground reaction force (GRF)** to the residual limb through the **socket interface**. Key design considerations include:

- **Suspension system** to absorb shock
- **Pylon** (the central shaft) for structural load transfer
- **Socket** for anatomical coupling
- **Terminal device** (foot for lower limb, hand for upper limb)

### 2.1.4 Cochlear Implant Signal Processing Chain

The cochlear implant bypasses damaged hair cells in the cochlea and directly stimulates the **auditory nerve** via an electrode array. The signal chain is:

1. **Microphone** captures acoustic waves
2. **Speech processor** (external) filters and encodes
3. **Transmitter coil** sends signal via **RF link** across the skin
4. **Receiver-stimulator** (implanted) decodes
5. **Electrode array** in cochlea delivers **biphasic current pulses**

### 2.1.5 Smart Cane (Mobility Aid with Sensors)

A smart cane integrates **ultrasonic/IR sensors** to detect obstacles and provide **haptic or auditory feedback** to visually impaired users. The detection range is typically **0.5 m to 3 m**.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why rolling resistance matters:** Even a small coefficient can compound over thousands of wheel rotations per day, causing shoulder injuries in long-term manual wheelchair users.
- **Why socket pressure matters:** The residual limb has limited soft tissue; excessive pressure causes ischemia (reduced blood flow) and skin breakdown.
- **Why RF coupling in cochlear implants:** It eliminates the need for percutaneous (through-skin) wires, drastically reducing infection risk.
- **Why haptic feedback in smart canes:** Audio cues can interfere with the user's ability to hear environmental sounds (cars, traffic).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU 2024 numerical and derivation questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_r = \mu_r \cdot N$ | Rolling resistance force | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | $F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Gradient force on incline | Newtons (N) |
| 3 | $F_{total} = F_r + F_g + F_f$ | Total propulsive force | Newtons (N) |
| 4 | $P = F_{total} \cdot v$ | Power required for propulsion | Watts (W) |
| 5 | $\tau = F \cdot r$ | Wheel torque | N·m |
| 6 | $v = \omega \cdot r$ | Linear wheel velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $P_{elec} = V \cdot I$ | Electrical power (powered wheelchair) | Watts (W) |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | System efficiency | Percentage (%) |
| 9 | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio (cochlear/HA) | Decibels (dB) |
| 10 | $I = \dfrac{V}{R_{skin}+R_{elec}}$ | Electrode current (cochlear implant) | Amperes (A) |
| 11 | $Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow (ventilator/HAT) | m³/s |
| 12 | $E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | Energy stored in orthotic spring | Joules (J) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Key:** $\mu_r$ = rolling friction coefficient; $N$ = normal force; $M$ = total mass (user + chair); $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$; $\theta$ = incline angle; $r$ = wheel radius; $\omega$ = angular velocity; $k$ = spring constant; $x$ = displacement.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Used in clinical gait labs to design custom orthoses.
- **Biomechanics Research:** HATs serve as test platforms for studying human-device interaction.
- **Sports & Athletics:** Specialized prosthetics (e.g., carbon-fiber running blades) are tuned using the energy-storage equation $E_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$.
- **Disaster Response:** Powered exoskeletons enable rescue workers to lift heavy debris.
- **Aging Population:** With global aging, demand for **mobility and ADL HATs** is projected to grow at a **CAGR of 6–8%** through 2030.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Propulsive Force for a Manual Wheelchair on an Incline

**Problem Statement:** Derive the total force a user must apply to propel a manual wheelchair (combined mass = $M$ kg, including user) up an incline of angle $\theta$, with rolling friction coefficient $\mu_r$ between wheels and ground, and a constant internal friction force $F_f$ in the wheel bearings.

### Step 1 — Identify All Resistance Forces

The user must overcome three forces acting **opposite** to the direction of motion:

- Rolling friction: $F_r$
- Gravity component along slope: $F_g$
- Internal bearing friction: $F_f$

### Step 2 — Express the Normal Force

On an incline, the normal force $N$ pressing the wheel onto the surface is the component of weight perpendicular to the slope:

$$ N = M \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta $$

### Step 3 — Express the Rolling Resistance

By definition, rolling resistance is proportional to the normal force:

$$ F_r = \mu_r \cdot N = \mu_r \cdot M \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta $$

### Step 4 — Express the Gradient Force

The component of gravitational force pulling the wheelchair **down** the slope (which the user must overcome going up) is:

$$ F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta $$

### Step 5 — Sum All Resistances

By Newton's second law at constant velocity (zero acceleration), the propulsive force $F_{prop}$ must balance all resistances:

$$ F_{prop} = F_r + F_g + F_f $$

### Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify

Substituting Steps 3 and 4 into Step 5:

$$ F_{prop} = \mu_r \cdot M \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta + M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + F_f $$

### Step 7 — Factor Out Common Terms

Factor $M \cdot g$ from the first two terms:

$$ F_{prop} = M \cdot g \left( \mu_r \cos\theta + \sin\theta \right) + F_f $$

### Step 8 — Compute Power Required

If the wheelchair moves at constant velocity $v$, the mechanical power output by the user is:

$$ P = F_{prop} \cdot v = v \left[ M \cdot g \left( \mu_r \cos\theta + \sin\theta \right) + F_f \right] $$

### Step 9 — Worked Numerical Example

Let $M = 80 \, \text{kg}$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $\theta = 5^\circ$, $\mu_r = 0.015$, $F_f = 5 \, \text{N}$, $v = 1.0 \, \text{m/s}$.

First, compute trigonometric values:

$$ \cos 5^\circ \approx 0.9962, \quad \sin 5^\circ \approx 0.0872 $$

Now substitute:

$$ F_{prop} = 80 \times 9.81 \times \left( 0.015 \times 0.9962 + 0.0872 \right) + 5 $$

Compute the bracket:

$$ 0.015 \times 0.9962 = 0.01494 $$

$$ 0.01494 + 0.0872 = 0.10214 $$

Compute the main term:

$$ 80 \times 9.81 = 784.8 $$

$$ 784.8 \times 0.10214 \approx 80.16 \, \text{N} $$

Add internal friction:

$$ F_{prop} \approx 80.16 + 5 = 85.16 \, \text{N} $$

Power required:

$$ P = 85.16 \times 1.0 \approx 85.16 \, \text{W} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A user must generate about **85 W** of mechanical power continuously — comparable to moderate cycling. This explains why manual wheelchair users frequently develop **shoulder impingement syndrome**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Consumption of a Powered Wheelchair

**Problem Statement:** A powered wheelchair is driven by two **24 V DC motors**, each drawing **15 A** at full load. The drivetrain efficiency (motor + gearbox + wheels) is **75%**. Calculate (a) electrical input power, (b) mechanical output power, and (c) range per charge given a **60 Ah, 24 V** battery.

### Part (a) — Electrical Input Power

Each motor draws:

$$ P_{motor} = V \cdot I = 24 \times 15 = 360 \, \text{W} $$

Two motors:

$$ P_{in} = 2 \times 360 = 720 \, \text{W} $$

### Part (b) — Mechanical Output Power

Apply efficiency:

$$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \implies P_{out} = \eta \cdot P_{in} $$

$$ P_{out} = 0.75 \times 720 = 540 \, \text{W} $$

### Part (c) — Range per Charge

Battery energy:

$$ E_{batt} = V \cdot Q = 24 \times 60 = 1440 \, \text{Wh} $$

Usable energy (assume **80% depth of discharge** to protect battery):

$$ E_{usable} = 0.80 \times 1440 = 1152 \, \text{Wh} $$

Time of operation at full mechanical load:

$$ t = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{out}} = \frac{1152}{540} \approx 2.13 \, \text{h} $$

If the wheelchair cruises at $v = 6 \, \text{km/h}$, range is:

$$ d = v \cdot t = 6 \times 2.13 \approx 12.8 \, \text{km} $$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of HAT Categories

| Parameter | Manual Wheelchair | Powered Wheelchair | Smart Cane | Cochlear Implant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Power Source | Human muscle | Battery (24–48 V) | Battery (3.7 V Li-ion) | Battery + RF |
| Typical Cost | $100–$500 | $1,500–$15,000 | $50–$300 | $20,000–$50,000 |
| User Effort | High | None | Low | None |
| Sensory Channel | N/A | N/A | Auditory/Haptic | Auditory nerve |
| Key Standard | ISO 7176 | ISO 7176-14 | ISO 9999 | IEC 60601 |
| Maintenance | Low | Moderate–High | Low | Specialized |
| Lifespan | 3–5 years | 5–7 years | 2–4 years | 10–15 years (external); lifetime (internal) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
