| 4 | $1\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \approx 98.0665\ \text{Pa}$ | Unit conversion for respiratory pressure | Pascals (Pa) |
| 5 | $EMG_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}$ | Root-mean-square amplitude of surface EMG signal (myoelectric control) | µV (typically 50–300 µV) |
| 6 | $f_{EEG\_mu} = 8 - 13\ \text{Hz}$ | Mu rhythm frequency band used in motor-imagery BCI | Hertz (Hz) |
| 7 | $f_{EEG\_beta} = 13 - 30\ \text{Hz}$ | Beta rhythm — event-related desynchronization (ERD) | Hertz (Hz) |
| 8 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio for EMG/EOG/EEG channel quality | decibels (dB) |
| 9 | $\eta_{site} = \dfrac{T_{use}}{T_{use} + T_{fatigue}}$ | Site efficiency: usable operational time vs. total elapsed time | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 10 | $DOF_{control} = f(S_{range}, M_{strength})$ | Degrees of freedom available from a site = function of range and strength | count |

> **Note on Symbol Hygiene:** Above, $\vert x \vert$ has been avoided inside markdown table cells by using $\vert \cdot \vert$ only where structurally safe; all delimiters are explicit $\vert$ inside math mode.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Powered Mobility:** Chin joysticks (Cervical), Head arrays (Cephalic), Sip-and-puff (Thoracic) are industry standards from manufacturers like Permobil, Quantum Rehab, and Pride Mobility.
- **Prosthetics:** Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR) at upper-limb residual sites allows intuitive control of multi-articulated prosthetic hands (e.g., DEKA LUKE Arm, Ottobock bebionic).
- **Communication:** Eye-gaze AAC devices (Tobii Dynavox) for ALS/locked-in syndrome users.
- **BCI Research:** EEG-based control at the cortical level for users with no remaining peripheral control.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sip-and-Puff Pressure-to-Command Mapping

The **sip-and-puff** interface is a pneumotachometric control system. The derivation of its pressure thresholds and command mapping is a KTU-favorite numerical.

**Step 1 — Define the control pressure variable.**

Let $P(t)$ be the time-varying oral pressure measured by a differential pressure transducer connected to a mouth tube.

**Step 2 — Define the discrete command states.**

$$
P(t) \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Command 1 (Sip)} & \text{if } P(t) < P_{sip,min} \\ \text{Command 2 (Hard Sip)} & \text{if } P(t) \ll P_{sip,min} \\ \text{Command 3 (Puff)} & \text{if } P(t) > P_{puff,min} \\ \text{Command 4 (Hard Puff)} & \text{if } P(t) \gg P_{puff,min} \\ \text{Idle} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply KTU standard pressure thresholds (clinical defaults).**

$$
P_{sip,min} = -2\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \quad ; \quad P_{sip,hard} = -10\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
P_{puff,min} = +5\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \quad ; \quad P_{puff,hard} = +15\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert pressure to SI units (Pascals).**

$$
P_{sip,min} = -2\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \times 98.0665\ \dfrac{\text{Pa}}{\text{cmH}_2\text{O}} = -196.13\ \text{Pa}
$$

$$
P_{sip,hard} = -10\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \times 98.0665\ \dfrac{\text{Pa}}{\text{cmH}_2\text{O}} = -980.665\ \text{Pa}
$$

$$
P_{puff,min} = +5\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \times 98.0665\ \dfrac{\text{Pa}}{\text{cmH}_2\text{O}} = +490.33\ \text{Pa}
$$

$$
P_{puff,hard} = +15\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \times 98.0665\ \dfrac{\text{Pa}}{\text{cmH}_2\text{O}} = +1471.00\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 5 — Hysteresis band to prevent false triggering.**

A dead-band of $\pm 0.5\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}$ is added to each threshold to prevent chatter at the boundary.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sip,enter} &= -2.0\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \\
P_{sip,exit} &= -1.5\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \\
P_{puff,enter} &= +5.0\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \\
P_{puff,exit} &= +4.5\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Debounce time constant.**

A typical latching debounce of $T_{db} = 50\ \text{ms}$ filters transient artifacts from coughing or swallowing.

### 3.2 Derivation: EMG Site Selection — Signal Quality Index

The **Root-Mean-Square (RMS)** amplitude of a surface EMG (sEMG) signal recorded at a candidate anatomic site is the primary figure-of-merit for myoelectric control.

**Step 1 — Acquire sEMG samples** $x_i$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ (typical $N = 256$ samples per epoch; sampling rate $f_s = 1\ \text{kHz}$).

**Step 2 — Compute the squared values.**

$$
x_1^2,\ x_2^2,\ \ldots,\ x_N^2
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the squared samples.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by sample count $N$.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2
$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root to obtain RMS.**

$$
EMG_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the KTU 2024 acceptance threshold.**

A site is clinically viable for myoelectric control if:

$$
EMG_{RMS} \geq 3 \times EMG_{noise,RMS}
$$

That is, the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** must be at least **9.54 dB**.

$$
SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(3) \approx 9.54\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Example (worked).**

Suppose a forearm flexor site yields 200 samples with mean-square value of $1600\ \mu V^2$ and resting noise RMS of $5\ \mu V$.

$$
EMG_{signal,RMS} = \sqrt{1600} = 40\ \mu V
$$

$$
EMG_{noise,RMS} = 5\ \mu V
$$

$$
SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{40}{5}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(8) \approx 18.06\ \text{dB}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Site accepted (SNR > 9.54 dB threshold).}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Site Selection by Injury Level

| SCI / Disability Level | Preserved Function | Recommended Anatomic Control Site(s) | Typical AT Device |
|------------------------|--------------------|--------------------------------------|--------------------|
| C1–C2 (High tetraplegia) | Head, neck, face | Head array, Sip-and-puff, EEG/BCI | PWC + ECU |
| C3–C4 | Neck, shoulders, diaphragm | Chin joystick, Sip-and-puff, Voice | PWC, AAC |
| C5 | Shoulder, elbow flexion, wrist (weak) | Shoulder joystick, Wrist switch, Head array | PWC, ADL aids |
| C6–C7 | Wrist, finger, triceps | Wrist-driven devices, Myoelectric, Hand switches | PWC, robotic arms |
| T1–T6 | Full UE, limited trunk | Hand joystick, Myoelectric, Sip-and-puff | Manual/powered WC |
| T7–L1 | Full UE, partial trunk | Hand joystick, Trunk tilt | Powered WC |
| L2–S5 | Full UE, hip/knee | Hand, Foot switches | Manual WC |
| ALS (advanced) | Eye motion, minimal facial | EOG, Eye tracker, EEG/BCI | AAC, ECU |
| Cerebral Palsy (spastic) | Variable; spasticity interferes | Head array (with damping), Switch-scanning, Sip-and-puff | Custom seating + switch |
| Amputation (transhumeral) | Residual limb muscles | TMR myoelectric sites, Pattern recognition | Multi-DOF prosthetic |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Mermaid Diagram: Anatomic Control Sites Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Anatomic Sites for Control of AT] --> B[Cluster 1: Cephalic Sites]
    A --> C[Cluster 2: Cervical Sites]
    A --> D[Cluster 3: Upper Limb Residual]
    A --> E[Cluster 4: Thoracic Respiratory]
    A --> F[Cluster 5: Trunk Lower Limb]

    B --> B1[Head array tilt switches]
    B --> B2[EOG eye tracking]
    B --> B3[EEG BCI mu beta]
    B --> B4[Facial EMG frontalis masseter]

    C --> C1[Chin joystick proportional]
    C --> C2[Neck flexion extension switch]
    C --> C3[Tongue Drive System intraoral]

    D --> D1[Myoelectric EMG biceps triceps]
    D --> D2[Wrist flexion extension]
    D --> D3[Targeted Muscle Reinnervation]
    D --> D4[Finger hand mechanical switch]

    E --> E1[Sip and Puff pneumatic SNP]
    E --> E2[Diaphragm intercostal EMG]
    E --> E3[Voice speech recognition]

    F --> F1[Shoulder shrug switch]
    F --> F2[Trunk tilt postural sway]
    F --> F3[Foot toe switch]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Human-to-Machine Command Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Intent Cognitive] --> B[Anatomic Control Site Physiological Source]
    B --> C[Transducer Conversion Stage]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning Amplify Filter]
    D --> E[Feature Extraction RMS Frequency]
    E --> F[Pattern Classification Algorithm]
    F --> G[Command Mapping Module]
    G --> H[Assistive Technology Actuator]
    H --> I[Task Execution Mobility Communication ADL]

    B -.Example Sites.-> Bx[Head Chin Tongue Sip-Puff EMG EOG EEG]
    C -.Example Transducers.-> Cx[Joystick Pressure Sensor Electrode Array Camera Microphone]
```

### 4.3 Decision Algorithm Flowchart — Selecting the Right Anatomic Site

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Patient Evaluation] --> S1{UE Function Intact?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Use Hand Wrist or Myoelectric Control]
    S1 -- No --> S3{Neck Shoulder Preserved?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Chin Joystick or Head Array]
    S3 -- No --> S5{Diaphragm Vocal Cords OK?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Sip and Puff or Voice Control]
    S5 -- No --> S7{Eye Motion Preserved?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[EOG or Eye Tracker AAC]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Cortical EEG BCI Last Resort]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define "Anatomic Site for Control of AT." List any four cephalic (head-based) anatomic sites used to control assistive technology devices.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** An anatomic site for control of AT is a specific region of the human body with preserved physiological function (motor, sensory, or cognitive) that is used as an **input signal source** to operate an external assistive device, forming the biological half of the human–machine interface.

> **Four Cephalic Sites (0.375 × 4 = 1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Head tilt / Head array** — for proportional directional wheelchair control.
> 2. **Forehead / Frontalis EMG** — surface EMG for switch activation.
> 3. **Eye gaze / EOG (Electro-oculography)** — measures corneo-retinal potential of **0.4–1.0 mV**.
> 4. **EEG / Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)** — uses mu (8–13 Hz) or beta (13–30 Hz) rhythms.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Explain the working principle of a "sip-and-puff" control system. State the typical pressure thresholds in cmH₂O.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Principle (2 marks):** A sip-and-puff system uses a **pneumotachometric pressure transducer** connected to a mouth tube. The user generates **negative pressure (sip)** by inhaling, and **positive pressure (puff)** by exhaling, into the tube. These pressure differentials are converted by the transducer into proportional electrical signals, which are then mapped to discrete commands (e.g., sip = move left, puff = move right). Advanced systems use a **four-state protocol** (sip, hard sip, puff, hard puff) for multi-function control.

> **Pressure Thresholds (1 mark):**
> - Sip: $P \approx -2\ \text{to}\ -10\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}$
> - Puff: $P \approx +5\ \text{to}\ +15\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "anatomic site" with "input device"** — a chin *joystick* is a device; the *chin* is the anatomic site. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Always state units (cmH₂O, mV, Hz, µV)** — KTU strict marking scheme penalizes unit omission.
> 3. **For SCI-level questions, always quote the specific vertebral level** (e.g., "C3–C4 tetraplegia") rather than generic "paralysis."
> 4. **Draw or describe a block diagram** in long answers — a labeled functional block diagram fetches **+2 marks** as a 'presentation bonus' per KTU 2024 rubrics.
> 5. **In numerical problems, show all intermediate steps** — partial marking is generous only when transitions are explicit.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a **labeled block diagram**, explain the **HAAT (Human-Activity-Assistive Technology) model** for selecting an anatomic control site. List the **five major anatomic site clusters** used in AT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **C3–C4 complete spinal cord injury** patient is referred for a powered wheelchair prescription. Justify the selection of an appropriate **anatomic control site**, describing **two viable options** in detail with their operating principles. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The HAAT model has four interlinked components:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Human Residual Anatomy Function] --> AT[Assistive Technology Device]
    A[Activity Task Goal] --> AT
    AT --> C[Context Environment]
    H --> A
    H --> C
    A --> C
    H --> AT
    C --> A
```

**Explanation of each component (2.5 marks):**

1. **Human (H):** The user's preserved **anatomy and physiology** (anatomic control site) — e.g., intact chin movement, diaphragm, eye muscles, residual EMG.
2. **Activity (A):** The **task goal** the user wants to accomplish — mobility, communication, manipulation, ADL.
3. **Assistive Technology (AT):** The **device** that executes the task — wheelchair, AAC, robotic arm, ECU.
4. **Context (C):** The **physical, social, and institutional environment** — home, workplace, weather, caregiver support.

The selected anatomic site must satisfy all four: e.g., a chin joystick works for indoor mobility (C), with a high-level SCI user (H), for driving a wheelchair (AT), to accomplish community ambulation (A).

**Five Anatomic Site Clusters (1.5 marks — 0.3 each):**
1. **Cephalic / Cranial sites** — head, face, eyes, brain
2. **Cervical / Neck sites** — chin, tongue, neck flexion
3. **Upper limb residual sites** — shoulder, wrist, hand, myoelectric
4. **Thoracic / Respiratory sites** — diaphragm, sip-and-puff, voice
5. **Trunk / Lower limb sites** — shoulder shrug, trunk tilt, foot switch

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Justification (2 marks):** At the **C3–C4 complete SCI level**, the patient retains voluntary control of the **head, neck, face, and diaphragm**, but has lost all upper-limb, lower-limb, and trunk motor function. Therefore, the control site must be chosen from the **cephalic, cervical, or thoracic clusters**.

**Option 1: Chin-Operated Joystick (2.5 marks)**

- A miniature proportional joystick is mounted on a **swing-away boom** at chin level.
- The patient's chin pushes the joystick in 4 directions (forward, back, left, right).
- The joystick's **Hall-effect sensors** convert mechanical deflection into proportional voltage signals.
- A microcontroller (e.g., **PGDT R-Net** or **Q-Logic 3**) maps deflection magnitude to motor speed.
- **Advantages:** Intuitive, proportional control, requires minimal training.
- **Limitations:** Requires sustained neck posture; fatiguing over long durations.

**Option 2: Sip-and-Puff Pneumatic Control (2.5 marks)**

- A **sterilizable mouth tube** is held near the lips, connected to a differential pressure transducer.
- A **soft sip** ($P < -2\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}$) yields Command 1; a **hard sip** ($P < -10\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}$) yields Command 2; analogous for puff.
- A **four-state digital protocol** provides discrete control signals to the wheelchair's motor controller.
- **Advantages:** Works for users with very limited head/neck control; non-contact, hygienic.
- **Limitations:** Discrete (not proportional); requires good pulmonary function; **latching debounce** of 50 ms needed to filter cough artifacts.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The chin joystick is preferred for users with good head/neck control and need for proportional driving; sip-and-puff is the gold-standard fallback when even cervical control is lost.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** What is **myoelectric control**? Derive the expression for **RMS amplitude** of a surface EMG signal and explain its role in selecting an anatomic site. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **EOG (Electro-oculography)**, **EEG (Electroencephalography)**, and **EMG (Electromyography)** as anatomic control modalities, citing at least **three parameters** for comparison. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Definition of Myoelectric Control (1 mark):** Myoelectric control is a method of operating an assistive device (typically a prosthetic limb or powered orthosis) by detecting and processing the **voluntary electrical activity (EMG)** generated by residual skeletal muscles at a preserved anatomic site, and using these signals to drive actuators.

**Derivation of RMS (4 marks — step-by-step):**

**Step 1:** Acquire a windowed segment of the sEMG signal containing $N$ discrete samples $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$.

**Step 2:** Square each sample:

$$
x_1^2,\ x_2^2,\ \ldots,\ x_N^2
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the squared values:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the arithmetic mean of squared samples:

$$
\overline{x^2} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2
$$

**Step 5:** Take the non-negative square root:

$$
EMG_{RMS} = \sqrt{\overline{x^2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}
$$

**Role in Site Selection (2 marks):**

- The RMS amplitude is a **time-domain feature** representing the **muscle activation level**.
- A site is **clinically viable** if $EMG_{RMS} \geq 3 \times EMG_{noise,RMS}$ (i.e., $\text{SNR} \geq 9.54\ \text{dB}$).
- High RMS indicates **strong, discriminable voluntary signal** suitable for proportional control.
- Low RMS sites are rejected as they yield poor controller performance and high false-trigger rates.
- RMS is the **standard feature** in pattern-recognition controllers (e.g., Linear Discriminant Analysis, SVM) for multi-DOF prosthetic control.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Three-Parameter Comparison Table (5 marks — populate 1.5 marks per row):**

| Parameter | EOG (Electro-oculography) | EEG (Electroencephalography) | EMG (Electromyography) |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Signal Source** | Corneo-retinal standing potential of the eye | Cortical neuronal postsynaptic potentials | Muscle fiber action potentials (motor units) |
| **Typical Amplitude** | **0.4–1.0 mV** (high) | **10–100 µV** (very low) | **50–300 µV** (medium) |
| **Frequency Range** | DC to ≈ 30 Hz (slow drift) | **0.5–50 Hz** (mu: 8–13 Hz; beta: 13–30 Hz) | **20–500 Hz** (main power 50–150 Hz) |
| **Best Suited Patient** | ALS / Locked-in syndrome with preserved eye motion | Total locked-in / late-stage ALS with no motor function | Amputees, SCI with residual limb muscles |
| **Limitation** | Eye fatigue, glasses reflection | Susceptible to EMG/EOG artifacts; long training | Electrode shift, sweat, crosstalk |
| **Control Bandwidth** | Moderate (saccade-based) | Low (≈ 5–25 bits/min) | High (proportional, fast) |

**Discussion (2 marks):** EOG and EMG are peripheral, muscle-/organ-based signals with relatively high SNR and ease of use, whereas EEG is a central nervous system signal offering control when *no peripheral site* is viable, but at the cost of lower bandwidth and longer training. The clinical choice follows the **HAAT model**: start with EMG (most natural) → fall back to EOG/head control → last-resort EEG/BCI.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Anatomic Sites for Control of AT**

- ✅ **Definition:** Anatomic sites = preserved body regions serving as biological input sources for AT.
- ✅ **Five Clusters:** Cephalic • Cervical • Upper-limb residual • Thoracic/Respiratory • Trunk/Lower-limb.
- ✅ **Selection Model:** **HAAT** (Human–Activity–Assistive Technology–Context), originally by Cook & Hussey.
- ✅ **High Tetraplegia (C1–C2):** Head array, Sip-and-puff, BCI.
- ✅ **Mid-cervical (C3–C4):** Chin joystick, Sip-and-puff, Voice.
- ✅ **C5–C6:** Shoulder joystick, Wrist switch.
- ✅ **C7 and below:** Hand control, Myoelectric, Wrist-driven.
- ✅ **Amputees:** TMR, Pattern recognition EMG.
- ✅ **ALS advanced:** EOG, Eye tracker, EEG/BCI.
- ✅ **Sip-and-Puff Thresholds:** $-2$ to $-10\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}$ (sip); $+5$ to $+15\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}$ (puff); $1\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \approx 98.07\ \text{Pa}$.
- ✅ **EOG:** Corneo-retinal potential $= \mathbf{0.4\text{–}1.0\ mV}$.
- ✅ **EEG bands:** Mu **8–13 Hz**; Beta **13–30 Hz**.
- ✅ **EMG:** RMS formula; SNR threshold $\geq \mathbf{9.54\ dB}$ for viability.
- ✅ **Safety**: Always include a **kill-switch** (typically a head-array or sip-and-puff hard command) for powered mobility.
- ✅ **TMR** = Targeted Muscle Reinnervation — re-routes nerves to spare muscles after amputation.
- ✅ **TDS** = Tongue Drive System — intraoral magnetic tracer-based control for high-level SCI.
- ✅ **Debounce time** for sip-and-puff: typically $\mathbf{50\ ms}$ latching.
- ✅ **Site Efficiency** $\eta_{site} = T_{use} / (T_{use} + T_{fatigue})$ — closer to 1.0 is better.
- ✅ **Always state units** in the exam; **always draw a labeled block diagram** in long answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of the Human/Technology Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Elements of the Human/Technology Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices (AMD)**, the **Human/Technology Interface (HTI)** — also called the **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** or **Human-Computer Interface (HCI)** in clinical engineering literature — is formally defined as the **bidirectional boundary system** that mediates the exchange of energy, information, and control signals between a human user (with a disability, impairment, or functional limitation) and a technological device designed to restore, augment, or substitute a lost physiological function.

According to the **KTU 2024 PEBRT745 syllabus (Module 2)**, the elements of the Human/Technology Interface comprise five principal functional blocks:

1. **Input / Sensing Elements** — capture bioelectric or mechanical intent from the user.
2. **Signal Conditioning & Processing Elements** — clean, amplify, and translate raw signals.
3. **Control / Decision Elements** — interpret intent and command the device.
4. **Output / Actuation Elements** — execute the commanded action on the body or environment.
5. **Feedback Elements** — close the loop by returning sensory information to the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** "The Human/Technology Interface refers to the integrated set of hardware, software, and physiological coupling mechanisms that enable a person with a disability to communicate intent to, and receive feedback from, an assistive technology device."

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Scope:** A *good* HTI must satisfy the **Three Pillars of Universal Design** as defined by the Centre for Universal Design (NCSU) — *Equitable Use, Flexibility in Use, and Simple and Intuitive Use*. Devices that fail any pillar are classified as **non-compliant interfaces** under KTU Module 2 evaluation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are sitting in a **modern car** that is fully drivable without your hands. Your **thoughts** ("turn left") are picked up by a tiny sensor on your scalp (an **EEG electrode**). The sensor is the **front desk receptionist** of a hotel — it receives your message and passes it upstairs. Upstairs, the **reception manager** (the **microcontroller / signal processor**) translates your fuzzy brainwave into a precise command: "rotate steering 15° left at 2°/sec." The **steering motor** (the **actuator**) is the bellboy who actually moves the wheel. The **dashboard lights and steering vibration** (the **feedback system**) are the bellboy tapping you on the shoulder to say, *"Done, sir."*

Without the receptionist, the bellboy, and the tap on the shoulder — i.e., without the **interface chain** — the most powerful car in the world is useless to you. This is exactly the role the Human/Technology Interface plays in assistive medical devices: it is the **complete chain of communication** between the human nervous system and the artificial device.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in HTI Design

The following quantitative parameters are universally used to grade an interface in KTU examinations:

- **Bit Rate (Throughput):** **30–60 bits/min** (normal) and as low as **< 5 bits/min** for severely disabled users using switch-scanning.
- **Information Transfer Rate (ITR):** measured in **bits/trial** — benchmark for BCI (Brain-Computer Interface) performance.
- **System Lag / Latency:** must be **< 200 ms** for real-time prosthetic control; **< 100 ms** for FES gait systems.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** **> 5,000 hours** for clinical-grade assistive devices.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** **> 20 dB** for EMG and EEG front-ends.
- **Acquisition / Detection Accuracy:** **≥ 95 %** for any clinically deployed interface.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Favourite Line:** *"Distinguish between the Input Interface and the Output Interface of an assistive device."* Always answer in terms of **direction of information flow** — Input = *Human → Device*, Output = *Device → Human*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Blocks — Structural Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Block 1 — Input / Sensing Elements

The *front-end* of every assistive device. These convert a **physiological signal** into an **electrical signal**.

| Sub-class | Signal Captured | Typical Sensor | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bioelectric (Active)** | Action potentials | Surface / Indwelling **EMG**, **EEG**, **ECoG**, **EOG** | Prosthetic arms, BCI communicators, wheelchair navigation |
| **Bioelectric (Invasive)** | Cortical neuron firing | Microelectrode arrays (Utah Array) | High-performance BCI research |
| **Biomechanical (Passive)** | Pressure, displacement, force | **FSR**, **strain gauge**, **gyroscope**, **accelerometer**, **encoder** | Sip-and-puff wheelchairs, joysticks, tilt sensors |
| **Biochemical** | Glucose, lactate, pH | Enzymatic electrodes | Closed-loop insulin pumps |
| **Residual Physiologic** | Breath pressure, voice, eye gaze | Pneumatic transducer, microphone, **IR-LED camera** | AAC devices, environmental control units |

**Operational Logic:**
1. Sensor transduces physical/chemical energy → analog voltage $V(t)$.
2. Anti-alias low-pass filter (cutoff $f_c \le f_s / 2.5$).
3. Amplification by **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** with CMRR **> 90 dB**.
4. Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) at sampling rate $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$.

### 2.1.2 Block 2 — Signal Conditioning & Processing

Raw signals are noisy, drifting, and contaminated with **mains interference (50/60 Hz)**. Conditioning includes:

- **Amplification** (gain $G$).
- **Filtering** — bandpass, notch (50 Hz), high-pass to remove DC electrode offset.
- **Rectification & Smoothing (RMS envelope)** — converts oscillating EMG to a **muscle activation envelope**.
- **Feature Extraction** — in time domain (MAV, RMS, ZC, waveform length) or frequency domain (MDF, MNF).
- **Classification** — Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), SVM, or Neural Networks (ANN/CNN/LSTM).

### 2.1.3 Block 3 — Control / Decision Elements

This is the **brain of the device**, typically a **microcontroller (ARM Cortex-M, Arduino, ESP32)** or **DSP (TMS320)**. It executes the control law:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int e(t) \, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

This is the classical **PID control law**, where $u(t)$ is the actuator command, $e(t)$ is the tracking error, and $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are tuned gains.

### 2.1.4 Block 4 — Output / Actuation Elements

Executes the physical work. Categories:

- **Mechanical Actuators:** DC motors, servo motors, pneumatic muscles, shape-memory alloys (SMA).
- **Electrical Stimulators:** Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) units delivering biphasic current pulses (**20–50 mA**, **20–50 Hz**, **pulse width 200–400 µs**).
- **Display / Communication Outputs:** LCD, Braille display, synthetic speech (TTS), haptic vibration motors.

### 2.1.5 Block 5 — Feedback Elements

Closes the loop so the user *knows* the device obeyed. Without feedback, prosthetic limbs feel like **"dead wood"** — this is the core motivation behind **sensory substitution** and **bidirectional neural interfaces**.

- **Visual:** LED status, GUI on tablet.
- **Auditory:** Tone pitch proportional to grip force.
- **Tactile/Haptic:** Vibrotactile motors, electrotactile skin stimulation.
- **Neural / Peripheral:** Intraneural stimulation (TENS, intraneural electrodes, cortical microstimulation).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **> 20 dB** (clinical) |
| 2 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | **> 90 dB** (INA) |
| 3 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot e$ | PID control law | $K_p, K_i, K_d \in \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ |
| 4 | $V_{\text{out}} = G \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$ | INA output | $G = 1 + \dfrac{49.4\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_G}$ |
| 5 | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} x_n^2}$ | EMG envelope | in $\mu$V or mV |
| 6 | $\text{ITR} = \dfrac{60}{T} \left[ \log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2 \dfrac{1-P}{N-1} \right]$ | BCI Information Transfer Rate | bits/min |
| 7 | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist criterion | Hz |
| 8 | $E_{pulse} = I_{stim} \cdot t_{pulse}$ | Stimulation charge per phase | $\mu$C |
| 9 | $\tau_{latency} = t_{actuator} - t_{intent}$ | Closed-loop latency | **< 200 ms** |
| 10 | $P_{accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} \times 100\%$ | Classification accuracy | **≥ 95 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid Markdown Breaks:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert G \vert$ in tables, use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert G \vert$). Never use the raw pipe symbol $\vert$ as it breaks the markdown table grid.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The HTI is the *determining factor* of whether an assistive device will be **adopted or abandoned**. Clinical literature (Philips 1993, Riemer-Reiss 2000) consistently reports that **~30 % of all prescribed assistive devices are abandoned within one year**, and the **single largest cause of abandonment is interface failure** — discomfort, unintuitive control, latency, and poor feedback. Hence HTI design is the most cost-effective leverage point in the entire AMD engineering chain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective SNR of an EMG Front-End

The raw surface EMG (sEMG) signal is the algebraic sum of motor unit action potentials. The acquired voltage is:

$$x(t) = s(t) + n_{thermal}(t) + n_{mains}(t) + n_{motion}(t)$$

where:
- $s(t)$ = genuine muscle signal (typically **20 µV – 5 mV**, dominant bandwidth **20–500 Hz**).
- $n_{thermal}$ = Johnson-Nyquist noise of electrode–skin interface.
- $n_{mains}$ = 50 Hz power-line interference (often **> 10× the signal**).
- $n_{motion}$ = motion artefact (< 20 Hz).

**Step 1 — Quantify thermal noise:**

The Johnson-Nyquist RMS noise voltage of the electrode–electrolyte resistance $R_e$ is:

$$V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R_e \, \Delta f}$$

where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is the **Boltzmann constant**, $T$ is absolute temperature (K), and $\Delta f$ is measurement bandwidth (Hz).

**Step 2 — Compute the differential amplification:**

The instrumentation amplifier rejects $n_{mains}$ common to both electrodes with CMRR. The effective mains noise at output becomes:

$$V_{mains}^{out} = \dfrac{V_{mains}^{in}}{\text{CMRR}_{\text{ratio}}} = \dfrac{V_{mains}^{in}}{10^{\text{CMRR}_{dB}/20}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute final SNR:**

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{V_{s,rms}}{\sqrt{V_{n,thermal}^{2} + \left(V_{mains}^{in}/10^{\text{CMRR}/20}\right)^{2}}} \right)$$

For a typical system: $V_{s,rms} \approx 1$ mV, $V_{mains}^{in} \approx 1$ mV, CMRR = 100 dB, $R_e = 10$ kΩ, $\Delta f = 500$ Hz, $T = 310$ K.

Plug in:

$$V_{n,thermal} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 310 \cdot 10^4 \cdot 500} \approx 9.25 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V} = 0.925 \; \mu\text{V}$$

$$V_{mains}^{out} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{100/20}} = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{10^5} = 10 \; \text{nV}$$

Since $V_{mains}^{out} \ll V_{n,thermal}$, the design is **dominated by thermal noise** and SNR ≈ 60.6 dB, which exceeds the clinical threshold of **20 dB** — *design is acceptable*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nyquist Sampling for an EEG-Based BCI

EEG band of interest (mu rhythm, motor imagery): **8–30 Hz**.

**Step 1 — Identify maximum frequency:**

$$f_{max} = 30 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Nyquist criterion:**

$$f_s \ge 2 f_{max} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_s \ge 60 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 2.5× oversampling rule of thumb (used in DSP textbooks):**

$$f_s \ge 2.5 \times f_{max} = 75 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Choose a standard, noise-friendly rate:**

$$f_s = 256 \text{ Hz} \quad \text{(commercial standard, e.g., OpenBCI, Emotiv)}$$

This is **3.4× the Nyquist minimum**, leaving comfortable margin for anti-alias filter roll-off and avoiding aliasing of mains harmonics.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — ITR (Information Transfer Rate) of a BCI Speller

Wolpaw's ITR equation for an $N$-class system:

$$\text{ITR} = \dfrac{60}{T} \left[ \log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2 \!\left( \dfrac{1-P}{N-1} \right) \right]$$

where:
- $T$ = average time per selection (seconds).
- $N$ = number of possible choices.
- $P$ = classifier accuracy (0 to 1).

**Worked Example:** $N = 32$ (P300 speller rows × columns), $P = 0.90$, $T = 4$ s.

Inside the bracket:

$$\log_2 32 = 5$$

$$0.90 \log_2 0.90 = 0.90 \times (-0.1520) = -0.1368$$

$$0.10 \log_2 \!\left( \dfrac{0.10}{31}\right) = 0.10 \times \log_2(0.003226) = 0.10 \times (-8.276) = -0.8276$$

Sum = $5 - 0.1368 - 0.8276 = 4.0356$ bits/trial.

$$\text{ITR} = \dfrac{60}{4} \times 4.0356 = 15 \times 4.0356 = 60.5 \text{ bits/min}$$

This matches the clinically reported value of **~60 bits/min** for P300 spellers — *a KTU examiner's favourite numerical answer*.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — PID Controller Tuning (Ziegler–Nichols Step 1)

Given a first-order plant with dead time:

$$G_p(s) = \dfrac{K \, e^{-L s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

The Ziegler–Nichols open-loop tuning rules are:

| Controller | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | $\dfrac{\tau}{K L}$ | $\infty$ | 0 |
| PI | $0.9 \dfrac{\tau}{K L}$ | $\dfrac{L}{0.3}$ | 0 |
| **PID** | $1.2 \dfrac{\tau}{K L}$ | $2 L$ | $0.5 L$ |

Then $K_i = K_p / T_i$ and $K_d = K_p T_d$.

**Worked Example:** $K = 2$, $L = 0.5$ s, $\tau = 3$ s.

For PID:

$$K_p = 1.2 \times \dfrac{3}{2 \times 0.5} = 1.2 \times 3 = 3.6$$

$$T_i = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ s} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K_i = \dfrac{3.6}{1.0} = 3.6$$

$$T_d = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \text{ s} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K_d = 3.6 \times 0.25 = 0.9$$

Final control law:

$$u(t) = 3.6 \, e(t) + 3.6 \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + 0.9 \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Interface Choice vs Disability

| User Condition | Recommended Input | Recommended Output | Typical Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| High-level quadriplegia (C1–C4) | Sip-and-puff, EEG-BCI | Synthetic speech, environmental control | Power wheelchair + ECU |
| Mid-level quadriplegia (C5–C6) | Sip-and-puff, head-array switch | Robotic arm, FES hand | JACO arm, Bioness H200 |
| Lower-limb amputation | EMG (residual muscle) | Powered knee/ankle, socket sensors | Genium, Rheo Knee |
| Visual impairment | Standard keyboard | Braille display, screen reader (TTS) | Refreshable Braille, JAWS |
| Hearing impairment | Standard mic | Bone-anchored hearing aid, vibrating pager | BAHA, alerting devices |
| Speech impairment | Eye-gaze, switch scanning | TTS, AAC symbols | Tobii Dynavox, Grid 3 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Human/Technology Interface Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER Brain Intent] --> B[BIOELECTRIC Sensor EMG EEG EOG]
    A2[USER Residual Motion] --> C[BIOMECHANICAL Sensor FSR Tilt Switch]
    B --> D[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Feature Extraction Time Frequency Domain]
    E --> F[Classifier LDA SVM ANN]
    F --> G[Microcontroller Decision Logic PID]
    G --> H[Actuator Motor FES Pneumatic]
    H --> I[External Effect Grip Step Stimulated Muscle]
    I --> J[Feedback Transducer Force Position Vision]
    J --> K[Feedback Display LED Buzzer Haptic Tactor]
    K --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The forward path flows left to right (Intent → Action) and the dashed/closing feedback loop flows right to left (Result → User). Any single broken link in this chain causes the device to become non-functional for the user.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Specific Example: Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Residual Limb Muscle Activation] --> A2[Surface EMG Electrodes Pair]
    A2 --> A3[INA Instrumentation Amplifier CMRR 100 dB]
    A3 --> A4[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
    A4 --> A5[Notch Filter 50 Hz Rejection]
    A5 --> A6[RMS Envelope Detection]
    A6 --> A7[Threshold Comparator On Off Control]
    A7 --> A8[Microcontroller STM32]
    A8 --> A9[H Bridge Motor Driver]
    A9 --> A10[DC Servo Motor in Prosthetic Hand]
    A10 --> A11[Finger Closure Around Object]
    A11 --> A12[Force Sensitive Resistor on Fingertip]
    A12 --> A13[Vibrotactile Motor on Residual Limb Skin]
    A13 --> A1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sensory Substitution Architecture (Tactile Vision Substitution)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Camera Module Mounted on Spectacles] --> B2[Image Processing Edge Detection]
    B2 --> B3[Electrotactile Array Driver 32 Channel]
    B3 --> B4[Electrode Array on Tongue or Abdomen]
    B4 --> B5[Somatosensory Cortex Perception]
    B5 --> B6[User Learns to Interpret Patterns as Vision]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define the term "Human/Technology Interface" in the context of assistive medical devices. List the five functional blocks that constitute this interface.**

**Model Answer:**

The Human/Technology Interface (HTI) is the bidirectional boundary system that enables a person with a disability to exchange control signals and feedback with an assistive device. The five functional blocks are:
1. Input / Sensing elements (e.g., EMG, EEG, FSR).
2. Signal conditioning & processing elements.
3. Control / decision elements (microcontroller, PID).
4. Output / actuation elements (motors, FES, displays).
5. Feedback elements (visual, auditory, haptic, neural). **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**What is CMRR of an instrumentation amplifier, and why is it important in EMG front-end design?**

**Model Answer:**

CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) is the ability of a differential amplifier to reject signals common to both inputs while amplifying the difference. Expressed in dB:

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}$$

In EMG, the 50 Hz mains interference appears equally on both surface electrodes. A high CMRR (> 90 dB) ensures this interference is suppressed, allowing the tiny (microvolt-level) muscle signal to be recovered cleanly. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **five functional blocks of a Human/Technology Interface** in an assistive medical device, giving **two examples** for each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Nyquist sampling criterion** and explain why a sampling rate of **256 Hz** is preferred for an EEG-based BCI speller. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Block 1 — Input / Sensing:** captures user intent. Examples: Surface EMG electrodes (bioelectric); Force Sensitive Resistor (biomechanical).
**Block 2 — Signal Conditioning:** filters, amplifies, rectifies. Examples: INA 128 amplifier; 50 Hz notch filter.
**Block 3 — Control / Decision:** interprets intent. Examples: PID controller; LDA classifier.
**Block 4 — Output / Actuation:** executes action. Examples: DC servo motor; FES stimulator.
**Block 5 — Feedback:** closes the loop. Examples: Vibrotactile motor; Refreshable Braille display.

Use a labelled diagram (see Section 4.1). Award 1 mark per block + 0.5 per example × 2 = 1 mark. Total = 6 + 1 for diagram = 7 marks.

#### Model Solution (b):

A band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be reconstructed losslessly if sampled at:

$$f_s \ge 2 f_{max} \quad \text{(Nyquist–Shannon theorem)}$$

For EEG motor imagery, the relevant mu/beta rhythm extends to **30 Hz**, giving $f_s \ge 60$ Hz. Applying the **2.5× oversampling rule** yields $f_s \ge 75$ Hz. The **256 Hz** standard is chosen because:
1. It is **3.4× the Nyquist minimum**, allowing gentle anti-alias filter roll-off.
2. It captures harmonics of mains noise for **adaptive cancellation** (e.g., 256/50 ≈ 5.12 samples per 50 Hz cycle).
3. It is the **industry standard** (OpenBCI, Emotiv, g.tec), ensuring compatibility with BCI2000 and other toolchains.

Award 2 marks for derivation, 3 marks for numerical application, 2 marks for justification. Total = 7 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a myoelectric prosthetic hand**, with a functional block diagram. Show the role of **surface EMG electrodes, INA, RMS envelope detector, threshold comparator, and DC motor**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a P300 BCI speller with $N = 26$ (alphabet), classification accuracy $P = 0.85$, and average trial duration $T = 5$ s, calculate the **Information Transfer Rate (ITR)** in bits/min using the Wolpaw equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

In a myoelectric hand, residual muscles (e.g., forearm flexors/extensors) generate action potentials when the user attempts to close/open the hand. Surface electrodes pick up this EMG. The INA amplifies the differential signal with high CMRR to reject mains noise. The bandpass filter restricts the band to 20–500 Hz. The **RMS envelope detector** computes the moving root-mean-square value, providing a smooth estimate of muscle activation. The threshold comparator decides "close" or "open" based on whether the RMS exceeds a programmed threshold. The microcontroller drives an H-bridge motor driver, which powers a DC servo motor to actuate the prosthetic fingers. Force feedback from an FSR closes the loop by driving a vibrotactile motor on the residual limb, giving the user "grip awareness."

Use a labelled diagram (see Section 4.2). Award 1 mark per component (5 × 1 = 5) + 2 marks for description of feedback. Total = 7 marks.

#### Model Solution (b):

Wolpaw's ITR formula:

$$\text{ITR} = \dfrac{60}{T} \left[ \log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2 \!\left( \dfrac{1-P}{N-1} \right) \right]$$

Substituting $N = 26$, $P = 0.85$, $T = 5$ s:

$$\log_2 26 = 4.7004$$

$$0.85 \log_2 0.85 = 0.85 \times (-0.2345) = -0.1993$$

$$0.15 \log_2 \!\left( \dfrac{0.15}{25}\right) = 0.15 \times \log_2(0.006) = 0.15 \times (-7.380) = -1.107$$

Sum = $4.7004 - 0.1993 - 1.107 = 3.394$ bits/trial.

$$\text{ITR} = \dfrac{60}{5} \times 3.394 = 12 \times 3.394 \approx 40.7 \text{ bits/min}$$

**Final Answer: ≈ 40.7 bits/min** (typical P300 speller range: 20–60 bits/min). Total = 7 marks.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 2 HTI):**
> 1. **Confusing "input" and "output" interfaces** — A student's most common error. Remember: Input = Human → Device (intent). Output = Device → Human (feedback/result). Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify units** in numerical problems (bits/min, mV, Hz, dB). A correct number with no unit gets **half marks** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark questions. A neat, labelled block diagram is worth **1–2 marks** by itself; omitting it is a guaranteed deduction.
> 4. **Mixing up Nyquist (2×) with the 2.5× oversampling rule** of thumb. Examiners expect both — state Nyquist, then justify the 2.5× multiplier.
> 5. **Writing "EMG measures brain signals"** — this is a fatal conceptual error. EMG = *muscle*. EEG = *brain*. EOG = *eye*. Examiners deduct 1 mark and circle the mistake.
> 6. **Not mentioning CMRR in INA-based designs** — clinical EMG interfaces live or die by CMRR. Always state "INA with CMRR > 90 dB."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** HTI = bidirectional boundary between user and assistive device.
- **Five Blocks:** Input → Conditioning → Control → Actuation → Feedback.
- **Key Sensors:** sEMG, EEG, EOG, FSR, encoder, gyroscope, sip-and-puff, eye-gaze camera.
- **Key Actuation:** DC motor, FES (20–50 mA, 20–50 Hz), pneumatic muscle, TTS, Braille.
- **INA Equation:** $V_{out} = G \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$ with $G = 1 + 49.4\text{k}/R_G$.
- **CMRR:** must exceed **90 dB** for clinical EMG.
- **Nyquist Rule:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ (use **2.5×** in practice → **256 Hz** for EEG).
- **PID Control:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot e$.
- **Ziegler–Nichols PID:** $K_p = 1.2 \tau / (K L)$, $T_i = 2 L$, $T_d = 0.5 L$.
- **ITR (Wolpaw):** $\text{ITR} = \dfrac{60}{T} \left[ \log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P) \log_2 \dfrac{1-P}{N-1} \right]$ bits/min.
- **Latency budget:** < 200 ms total (prosthetic), < 100 ms (FES gait).
- **Threshold accuracy:** ≥ 95 % for clinically deployed interfaces.
- **Nyquist safeguard:** always leave a 2.5×–5× oversampling margin.
- **Universal design pillars:** Equitable Use, Flexibility, Simple & Intuitive.
- **Adoption reality:** ~30 % of prescribed devices are abandoned — *the interface is the cause, not the hardware*.
- **Direction of flow:** "Input = human-to-device", "Output = device-to-human" — **memorize this line** for viva.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speech
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Speech

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Speech" in the Context of Assistive Medical Devices?

In the domain of **Assistive Medical Devices (PEBRT745)**, *Speech* is treated as a multi-disciplinary engineering subject that deals with the **production, acquisition, digital processing, synthesis, recognition, and artificial reproduction of the human voice** for individuals who have lost — or are at risk of losing — their natural ability to communicate verbally.

Formally, as per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus:

> **Speech (Engineering Definition):** A time-varying, quasi-stationary acoustic pressure waveform generated by the vibration of the vocal folds (voiced sounds) or by turbulent airflow through constrictions in the vocal tract (unvoiced/fricative sounds), characterized in the frequency domain by a fundamental frequency (F0) and several resonant peaks known as *formants* (F1, F2, F3…).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** This module emphasizes the engineering of systems that **restore, augment, or substitute** human speech — i.e., **Speech Synthesizers**, **Speech Recognizers**, and **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) devices**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Speech = Plumbing + Music" Intuition

Think of human speech as a combination of two simple things:

- 🎺 **A musical instrument (the Glottis / Vocal Folds):** The vocal folds vibrate like the reed of a saxophone, producing a buzzing tone (the *excitation signal*).
- 🚿 **A shaped pipe (the Vocal Tract):** The throat, tongue, and mouth act like a flexible pipe that filters and shapes that buzz, turning it into recognisable vowels and consonants (the *transfer function*).

So when an engineer says *"speech is the convolution of a source and a filter"*, they mean:
$$\text{Speech}(t) = \text{Excitation}(t) * \text{Vocal Tract Impulse Response}(t)$$

When this system breaks down (e.g., laryngectomy, ALS, cerebral palsy, stroke), **assistive speech devices** step in to recreate the music, the pipe, or both — using electronics and software.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants (Must Memorize)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speech Frequency Range | **300 Hz – 3400 Hz** (telephony) / **20 Hz – 8 kHz** (wideband) | Hz |
| Fundamental Frequency (F0) – Adult Male | **85 – 180 Hz** | Hz |
| Fundamental Frequency (F0) – Adult Female | **165 – 255 Hz** | Hz |
| Speaking Rate | **120 – 200 words/minute** | wpm |
| Nyquist Sampling Frequency (8 kHz bandwidth) | **16 kHz** | Hz |
| Standard Quantization (PCM telephony) | **8 bits / 64 kbps** | bits |
| Phoneme Inventory (English) | **~44 phonemes** | – |
| Formant Bandwidth (typical) | **50 – 150 Hz** | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter for KTU exams:** Whenever a numerical problem on speech sampling, bit-rate calculation, or bandwidth appears, examiners expect you to use the telephony-standard 4 kHz bandwidth and 8 kHz sampling rate unless explicitly stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Speech in Assistive Technology

The topic "Speech" under Module 2 is best understood by splitting it into **three engineering pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Speech Production (The Human Side)
- **Respiratory system** provides airflow (the *power supply*).
- **Larynx (vocal folds)** acts as the *excitation generator* (voiced/unvoiced source).
- **Articulators (tongue, lips, jaw, velum, teeth)** shape the vocal tract filter.
- **Phonemes** are the smallest contrastive units (Vowels, Fricatives, Plosives, Nasals, etc.).

### Pillar 2 — Speech Synthesis (Text-to-Speech / TTS)
A TTS system converts **written text → spoken audio**. Architecture:

1. **Text Analysis (Front-End):** Tokenization, normalization, POS tagging, letter-to-sound (LTS) rules.
2. **Linguistic Processing:** Grapheme-to-phoneme conversion, stress assignment, intonation (prosody) generation.
3. **Waveform Generation (Back-End):**
   - *Concatenative Synthesis* (unit selection, diphone synthesis)
   - *Formant Synthesis* (rule-based, Klatt synthesizer)
   - *Articulatory Synthesis*
   - *Statistical Parametric Synthesis* (HMM-based, DNN-based)
   - *Neural TTS* (Tacotron 2, WaveNet, FastSpeech)

### Pillar 3 — Speech Recognition (Automatic Speech Recognition / ASR)
ASR converts **spoken audio → text or intent**. Pipeline:

1. **Acoustic Front-End:** Pre-emphasis → Framing → Windowing (Hamming) → FFT → Mel filterbank → MFCC features.
2. **Acoustic Model (AM):** Maps audio features → phonemes (HMM-GMM or DNN/CNN/RNN-Transformer).
3. **Language Model (LM):** Predicts word sequences (n-gram or neural LMs).
4. **Decoder:** Viterbi/Beam search to find the most probable word sequence.

## 2.2 Why These Three Pillars Matter Clinically

| Pillar | Clinical Application | Assistive Device Example |
|---|---|---|
| Synthesis (TTS) | Laryngectomy, severe dysarthria | Speech-generating devices (SGDs) — e.g., **Tobii Dynavox**, **PRC Accent** |
| Recognition (ASR) | Motor-impaired users, hands-free control | Voice-controlled wheelchairs, smart-home hubs |
| Production understanding | Designing intelligible prostheses | **Tracheoesophageal puncture (TEP) valves** like Provox, Blom-Singer |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note:** The vertical pipe symbol `\|` is replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. Always cite the correct **unit** in numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing | Hz |
| 2 | Speech bit-rate (PCM) | $R_b = f_s \times n$ | Bits per second for digital storage | bps |
| 3 | Speech signal power (dB) | $P_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)$ | Decibel scale for intensity ratio | dB |
| 4 | Pre-emphasis Filter | $H(z) = 1 - \alpha z^{-1}, \quad \alpha \approx 0.97$ | Boosts high frequencies before analysis | – |
| 5 | MFCC (log Mel spectrum) | $X_{mel} = \log\!\left(\sum_{k} \vert X(k)\vert^2 H_m(k)\right)$ | Cepstral coefficients for ASR front-end | – |
| 6 | Cepstrum (real) | $c(n) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\log \vert X(\omega)\vert\}$ | Separates source & filter in speech | – |
| 7 | LPC Predictor Polynomial | $A(z) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}$ | Models vocal tract as an all-pole filter | – |
| 8 | Formant Frequency (tube approx.) | $F_n = \dfrac{(2n-1) c}{4 L}$ | Closed-open tube; $c$ = speed of sound, $L$ = vocal tract length | Hz |
| 9 | Pitch (Autocorrelation) | $F_0 = \dfrac{1}{\tau^*}$ where $R(\tau^*) = \max R(\tau)$ | Detects glottal period | Hz |
| 10 | Shannon–Hartley (bandwidth) | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Channel capacity for speech codec | bps |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hearing Aids & Cochlear Implants:** Use **formant tracking** to enhance speech intelligibility in noise.
- **Smartphones (Siri, Google Assistant):** Live ASR with on-device neural networks.
- **Screen Readers (JAWS, NVDA):** Use **TTS engines** to vocalize screen content for the visually impaired.
- **AAC Devices:** Combine **symbol-to-speech mapping** with predictive language modeling for non-verbal users.
- **Telemedicine:** Voice biometrics authenticate patients via speaker-verification algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sampling Rate for Speech Signal

**Problem:** A speech signal is bandlimited to **4 kHz** (standard telephony bandwidth). Determine the minimum sampling frequency according to the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem, and calculate the bit rate for 8-bit uniform PCM encoding.

**Given:**
- Maximum frequency of speech, $f_{max} = 4\,\text{kHz} = 4000\,\text{Hz}$
- Number of quantization bits, $n = 8\,\text{bits}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist Criterion.**
By the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem, to reconstruct a bandlimited signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest frequency component:
$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$
Substituting the value:
$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 4000\,\text{Hz} = 8000\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Select the Standard Sampling Rate.**
The ITU-T (formerly CCITT) standard **G.711** for PCM speech encoding mandates:
$$
f_s = 8\,\text{kHz} = 8000\,\text{samples/second}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Bit Rate.**
For a uniform PCM system, the bit rate is:
$$
R_b = f_s \times n
$$
Substituting:
$$
R_b = 8000 \times 8 = 64000\,\text{bps} = 64\,\text{kbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification & Practical Note.**
This is precisely the **64 kbps PCM channel** used in the digital PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network). The bandwidth utilization is therefore $64\,\text{kbps}$.

> ✅ **Final Answer:** $f_s = 8\,\text{kHz}$, $R_b = 64\,\text{kbps}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Formant Frequency of a Uniform Vocal Tract Model

**Problem:** Approximate the first three formant frequencies of the vowel /a/ (as in "father") assuming the vocal tract behaves as a uniform closed-open tube of length $L = 17\,\text{cm}$. Use the speed of sound in warm air as $c = 353\,\text{m/s}$.

**Step 1 — Recall the Boundary Conditions.**
A vocal tract closed at the glottis end and open at the lips end behaves acoustically as a **closed-open (quarter-wave) tube**. The natural resonant (formant) frequencies of such a tube are:
$$
F_n = \frac{(2n - 1)\,c}{4\,L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Units.**
$$
L = 17\,\text{cm} = 0.17\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute F1 (n = 1).**
$$
F_1 = \frac{(2 \times 1 - 1) \times 353}{4 \times 0.17} = \frac{1 \times 353}{0.68}
$$
$$
F_1 = 519.1\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute F2 (n = 2).**
$$
F_2 = \frac{(2 \times 2 - 1) \times 353}{4 \times 0.17} = \frac{3 \times 353}{0.68} = \frac{1059}{0.68}
$$
$$
F_2 = 1557.4\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute F3 (n = 3).**
$$
F_3 = \frac{(2 \times 3 - 1) \times 353}{4 \times 0.17} = \frac{5 \times 353}{0.68} = \frac{1765}{0.68}
$$
$$
F_3 = 2595.6\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-Check with Empirical Data.**
Real formant frequencies for /a/ in adult male speech: $F_1 \approx 730\,\text{Hz}$, $F_2 \approx 1090\,\text{Hz}$, $F_3 \approx 2440\,\text{Hz}$.

> **Interpretation:** The uniform-tube model underestimates $F_1$ and overestimates $F_2$ because the real vocal tract is **non-uniform** (constricted at specific points). The model nonetheless correctly predicts that all formants lie below **4 kHz**, justifying the telephony bandwidth.

> ✅ **Final Answer:** $F_1 \approx 519\,\text{Hz}$, $F_2 \approx 1557\,\text{Hz}$, $F_3 \approx 2596\,\text{Hz}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pitch Detection via Short-Time Autocorrelation

**Problem:** A segment of voiced speech $x(n)$ is windowed and the short-time autocorrelation is computed. Peaks are observed at lags $\tau = 36, 72, 108, \dots$ samples. If the sampling frequency is $f_s = 16\,\text{kHz}$, determine the fundamental frequency (pitch) $F_0$ of the speaker.

**Step 1 — Identify the First Non-Zero Peak.**
The autocorrelation $R(\tau)$ of a periodic voiced signal peaks at lags that are integer multiples of the pitch period $T_0$:
$$
R(\tau^\star) = \max_{\tau > 0} R(\tau)
$$
Given the first peak at $\tau^\star = 36$ samples, the pitch period in samples is $N_0 = 36$.

**Step 2 — Convert Pitch Period to Seconds.**
$$
T_0 = \frac{N_0}{f_s} = \frac{36}{16000} = 2.25 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 2.25\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Fundamental Frequency.**
$$
F_0 = \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{2.25 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 444.4\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result.**
Since $F_0 \approx 444\,\text{Hz}$, this corresponds to a **child's voice or a soprano** (typical adult male: 85–180 Hz, adult female: 165–255 Hz). This kind of calculation is critical in **voice prostheses** and **gender/age classification** modules in modern AAC devices.

> ✅ **Final Answer:** $F_0 \approx 444\,\text{Hz}$ (child/soprano range).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Speech Synthesis Techniques

| Method | Underlying Principle | Output Quality | Computational Cost | Use in Assistive Devices |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Formant (Klatt) Synthesis** | Rule-based source-filter model | Robotic, intelligible | Very Low | Low-cost AAC devices, embedded systems |
| **Diphone Concatenation** | Joins pre-recorded diphone units | Natural, mildly choppy | Moderate | Mid-range SGDs, screen readers |
| **Unit Selection (Corpus-based)** | Picks best unit from large database | Very natural | High | Modern TTS, Google TTS, navigation systems |
| **HMM-based Parametric** | Statistical parametric model | Smooth, slightly muffled | Moderate | Festival, older assistive TTS |
| **Neural TTS (Tacotron 2 + WaveNet)** | End-to-end deep learning | Highly natural | Very High | Cloud-based AAC, smartphone assistants |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Speech-Generating Device (SGD)

This diagram maps the **end-to-end functional flow** of a typical Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) Speech-Generating Device — the most clinically important assistive technology under Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input Interface] --> B{Input Mode Selection}
    B -->|Touch| C1[Touchscreen Keyboard]
    B -->|Switch| C2[Single Switch Scanner]
    B -->|Gaze| C3[Eye-Tracking Sensor]
    B -->|Head Movement| C4[Head-Pointer Sensor]
    C1 --> D[Symbol and Text Encoder]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Language Model and Prediction Engine]
    E --> F[Phonetic and Prosodic Processor]
    F --> G[Speech Synthesis Engine]
    G --> G1{Backend Selection}
    G1 -->|Formant| H1[Klatt Synthesizer]
    G1 -->|Concatenative| H2[Unit Selection Synthesizer]
    G1 -->|Neural| H3[Deep Learning TTS Model]
    H1 --> I[Audio Output Amplifier]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Speaker or Headphone Output]
    D --> K[Context Aware Word Prediction]
    K --> E
    G --> L[Onboard Battery and Power Management Unit]
    L --> A
```

### Reading the Diagram (for the Examiner)

1. **User Input Layer (A → C1–C4):** The patient uses whichever motor channel is still functional. This is the **accessibility layer** of the device.
2. **Encoding & Prediction (D, E, K):** The device converts the input into a linguistic message and uses a **language model** to predict the next word, dramatically increasing typing speed.
3. **Prosody & Synthesis (F → G):** The prosody module adds intonation, stress, and rhythm — critical for intelligibility and naturalness.
4. **Backend Selection (G1):** Modern high-end devices (e.g., Tobii Dynavox) deploy **neural TTS** for natural-sounding voice; legacy low-cost devices still use **formant synthesis**.
5. **Output (I → J):** Audio is delivered via speaker or headphone to the listener.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) Pipeline for Assistive Control

This second diagram shows how a voice-controlled wheelchair or smart-home hub processes the user's voice command.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microphone Input] --> B[Analog Front End and Anti Aliasing Filter]
    B --> C[ADC Sampled at 16 kHz]
    C --> D[Pre Emphasis Filter alpha 0.97]
    D --> E[Framing 25 ms with 10 ms Hop]
    E --> F[Hamming Window]
    F --> G[FFT Magnitude Spectrum]
    G --> H[Mel Filterbank 40 filters]
    H --> I[Log and DCT to MFCC]
    I --> J[Acoustic Model DNN or Transformer]
    J --> K[Decoder Viterbi or Beam Search]
    K --> L[Language Model n gram or Neural]
    L --> M[Recognized Text or Intent]
    M --> N{Decision Module}
    N -->|MOVE| O1[Wheelchair Motor Controller]
    N -->|CALL| O2[Smart Home Hub]
    N -->|EMERGENCY| O3[Caregiver Alert System]
```

### Key Engineering Takeaway
The MFCC feature extraction block (steps D → I) is identical to what you would study in a digital signal processing course. KTU examiners frequently test students on **why Mel scale is used** (it approximates the human ear's frequency resolution) and **why DCT is applied after the log-Mel spectrum** (to decorrelate features and compact energy into lower coefficients).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a *formant* in speech signals. Why are the first three formants (F1, F2, F3) sufficient for vowel identification in most speech synthesis systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **formant** is a resonant peak in the frequency spectrum of a voiced speech sound, produced by the natural resonances of the vocal tract acting as an acoustic filter. **(1 mark)**

The position of the formants in the frequency domain distinguishes one vowel from another. **F1** primarily correlates with vowel height (tongue height), **F2** correlates with vowel frontness/backness, and **F3** contributes to lip rounding and speaker identity. **(1 mark)**

For most practical speech synthesis and recognition systems, vowels can be uniquely identified using only F1, F2, and F3 because the energy of speech above 3.5 kHz contributes minimally to vowel intelligibility. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is the difference between **voiced** and **unvoiced** speech sounds? Give one clinical example where the distinction is critical for designing an assistive device.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Voiced sounds** (e.g., /a/, /b/, /d/) are produced when the vocal folds vibrate, generating a quasi-periodic excitation with a measurable fundamental frequency $F_0$. **(1 mark)**
- **Unvoiced sounds** (e.g., /s/, /f/, /sh/) are produced by turbulent airflow through a constriction in the vocal tract, with no vocal fold vibration — the excitation is noise-like. **(1 mark)**
- **Clinical Example:** In designing a **voice prosthesis (e.g., electrolarynx)** for post-laryngectomy patients, the device must produce a buzz-like source mimicking voiced excitation. However, fricatives like /s/ are formed by shaping airflow through the mouth and do not require the electrolarynx source — only voicing-related sounds do. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Text-to-Speech (TTS) system** used in modern Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) devices. Compare at least three synthesis techniques. *(Module 2 — Speech)*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: TTS Architecture

**Model Solution:**

A TTS system is functionally divided into two major blocks: the **Front-End (Text Analysis)** and the **Back-End (Speech Generation)**.

**Front-End performs the following steps:**
1. **Text Normalization:** Expands abbreviations, numbers, and symbols. *Example: "Dr. ≈ Doctor"*
2. **Tokenization:** Splits the input into words and sentences.
3. **Linguistic Analysis:** Performs Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging and assigns prosodic structure.
4. **Letter-to-Phoneme Conversion:** Uses linguistic rules or ML models to map graphemes to phonemes.
5. **Prosody Generation:** Determines intonation contour, stress, and rhythm (F0 trajectory, duration, energy).

**Back-End (Synthesis) options:**
- **Formant Synthesis (Klatt):** Models the vocal tract as a set of parallel/formant resonators. Highly parametric, low bit-rate, robotic-sounding. Used in low-cost devices.
- **Concatenative Synthesis:** Concatenates pre-recorded speech units (diphone, demisyllable, or full corpus). Very natural, but requires large storage and has concatenation artifacts.
- **Neural TTS (Tacotron 2 / WaveNet):** End-to-end deep learning that maps text directly to mel-spectrograms or raw waveforms. State-of-the-art naturalness, but computationally expensive (cloud or powerful edge AI required).

*(7 marks — 2 marks for block diagram description, 3 marks for front-end steps, 2 marks for back-end explanation)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Formant | Concatenative | Neural |
|---|---|---|---|
| Naturalness | Low | High | Very High |
| Intelligibility | High | High | High |
| Footprint | Tiny (kB) | Large (MB–GB) | Very Large (GB) |
| Real-time on edge | Yes | Yes | Limited (needs GPU/NPU) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Best for | Low-cost AAC | Mid-range SGD | Premium / cloud AAC |

**Conclusion:** For **low-resource assistive devices** (e.g., rural-area AAC for children with cerebral palsy), formant synthesis is preferred. For **high-end AAC devices** (e.g., Tobii Dynavox with eye-tracking), neural TTS offers the best user experience but requires either a strong processor or cloud connectivity.

*(7 marks — 4 marks for table comparison, 2 marks for application mapping, 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]** Explain the **MFCC feature extraction pipeline** for an Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) system used in a voice-controlled wheelchair. Compute the bit rate of a PCM system with $f_s = 16\,\text{kHz}$ and $n = 16\,\text{bits}$.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: MFCC Pipeline

**Model Solution:**

The MFCC (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficient) extraction is the standard acoustic front-end for ASR. The steps are:

1. **Pre-emphasis:** $y(n) = x(n) - \alpha x(n-1)$, with $\alpha = 0.97$. Boosts high-frequency energy that is attenuated during speech production.
2. **Framing:** The signal is split into 25 ms frames with a 10 ms hop, making the signal approximately stationary.
3. **Windowing:** Each frame is multiplied by a Hamming window $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(2\pi n / N)$ to reduce spectral leakage.
4. **FFT:** A 512-point FFT is applied to each frame, producing the magnitude spectrum $\vert X(k) \vert$.
5. **Mel Filterbank:** A bank of ~40 triangular filters spaced according to the Mel scale is applied:
   $$f_{mel} = 2595 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$$
6. **Log Compression:** The Mel-spectrum energy is log-compressed, mimicking human loudness perception.
7. **DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform):** Yields 12–13 cepstral coefficients (MFCCs), plus delta and delta-delta for dynamics.

These MFCCs are then fed into an Acoustic Model (HMM or DNN) for phoneme recognition.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per step, with 1 mark for the Mel-scale equation)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Bit Rate Calculation

**Given:** $f_s = 16\,\text{kHz}$, $n = 16\,\text{bits}$

**Solution:**
$$
R_b = f_s \times n = 16000 \times 16 = 256{,}000\,\text{bps} = 256\,\text{kbps}
$$

**Application Note:** A voice-controlled wheelchair transmits commands at this bit rate over a short-range wireless link (Bluetooth/Wi-Fi). 256 kbps is sufficient for full-bandwidth (50 Hz – 8 kHz) speech, ensuring that the caregiver or remote support center receives high-quality voice in case of emergency override.

*(7 marks — 4 marks for formula & substitution, 2 marks for final answer with unit, 1 mark for clinical application)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Speech Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $F_0$ (pitch) with formants.** $F_0$ is the *fundamental frequency* from vocal-fold vibration; formants are *vocal-tract resonances*. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** (cm → m, ms → s) in derivation problems. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 3. **Writing "Hz" for bit rate** in PCM problems. Bit rate is in **bps / kbps**, not Hz. This is a guaranteed 0.5-mark penalty.
> 4. **Omitting the Mel-scale equation** in MFCC derivations. Examiners expect $f_{mel} = 2595 \log_{10}(1 + f/700)$.
> 5. **Stating "speech is a periodic signal"** — this is false. Speech is *quasi-stationary*; only the voiced component is periodic over short intervals.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ✅ Speech = **Source (glottis) + Filter (vocal tract)**; model as convolution.
- ✅ Standard telephony bandwidth = **4 kHz**; Nyquist sampling = **8 kHz**; PCM bit rate = **64 kbps**.
- ✅ Adult male $F_0$ = **85–180 Hz**; Adult female $F_0$ = **165–255 Hz**; Child $F_0$ = **> 250 Hz**.
- ✅ Formants: **F1 (height), F2 (frontness), F3 (rounding)**; only first three needed for vowel identity.
- ✅ Three pillars: **Production, Synthesis (TTS), Recognition (ASR)**.
- ✅ TTS back-ends ranked by naturalness: **Formant < Concatenative < Neural**.
- ✅ ASR pipeline: **Pre-emphasis → Framing → Windowing → FFT → Mel → Log → DCT (MFCCs)**.
- ✅ MFCCs approximate **human auditory perception** using the Mel scale.
- ✅ Assistive devices: **AAC/SGDs** (e.g., Tobii Dynavox) for synthesis; **voice-controlled wheelchairs** for ASR; **electrolarynx / TEP valves** for production rehabilitation.
- ✅ Pitch detected by **autocorrelation**; $F_0 = 1/\tau^\star$.
- ✅ Speech is **quasi-stationary**, not purely periodic or random.
- ✅ Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX when writing absolute values inside markdown tables (e.g., $\vert X(k)\vert$).
- ✅ LPC models the vocal tract as an **all-pole filter**: $A(z) = 1 - \sum a_k z^{-k}$.
- ✅ Engineering trade-off: **naturalness vs. footprint vs. real-time edge deployment**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contexts Affecting HTI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Contexts Affecting HTI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Human-Technology Interaction (HTI)** is the dynamic, bidirectional relationship between a human user and a technological artifact (system, device, or tool) in which the user operates, controls, and receives feedback from the technology to accomplish a specific goal. In the domain of **Assistive Medical Devices (AMD)**, HTI is profoundly shaped by *context* — the multilayered set of physical, social, organizational, cognitive, and cultural surroundings within which the interaction unfolds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context-Aware Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> According to the *Contextual Factors Framework* (adopted widely in Human Factors and Ergonomics literature for medical devices), the **Context Affecting HTI** refers to the complete set of internal and external conditions — physical, social, organizational, task-related, user-related, and environmental — that dynamically influence the way a user perceives, operates, trusts, and benefits from an assistive medical technology.

The classical **SEIPS 2.0 model** (Systems Engineering Initiative for Patient Safety, Holden et al., 2013) and the **Contextual Design Model** (Beyer & Holtzblatt) identify the following primary contextual layers affecting HTI:

1. **Physical Environment Context**
2. **Social & Cultural Context**
3. **Organizational/Institutional Context**
4. **Task & Activity Context**
5. **User / Personal Context**
6. **Technological Context**
7. **Temporal Context**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Blind Man with a Smart Cane**
> Imagine an elderly person using a *smart walking cane with obstacle detection*. The cane itself is the technology, but the HTI is wildly different if:
> - He uses it in a **quiet, well-lit home corridor** → high trust, smooth interaction.
> - He uses it in a **noisy, crowded marketplace** → false alerts, low trust, clumsy use.
> - He is in a **hospital under post-surgery fatigue** → slower reactions, anxiety, errors.
> - His **family discourages its use** due to stigma of "looking disabled" → he abandons it.
>
> **The technology never changed — only the context did. Yet the interaction completely transformed.**

This analogy is the *heart* of why KTU emphasizes "Contexts Affecting HTI." A perfectly engineered device can **fail in the real world** if designers ignore context. A device that *looks* inferior on paper can **excel** if it harmonizes with the user's context.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Standard Constants in Context Engineering

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Relevance to HTI |
|---|---|---|
| Ambient Illumination | **300 – 750 lux** (indoor clinical) | Affects display legibility |
| Acoustic Noise Threshold | **≤ 45 dB** (WHO hospital standard) | Affects auditory alerts |
| Skin Contact Temperature | **≤ 41 °C** (ISO 80601 medical device limit) | Thermal safety context |
| Cognitive Load Limit | **Miller's Law: 7 ± 2** working memory chunks | UI design context |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (auditory cue) | **≥ 15 dB above ambient** | Alarm salience context |
| Response Latency Tolerance | **≤ 250 ms** (motor control feedback) | Real-time HTI loop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> Module 2 explicitly stresses that *context is not a "background" variable but a co-equal design parameter.* This distinguishes modern KTU curriculum (NEP 2020 aligned) from older "device-centric" approaches.
