**(a)** Explain the **characteristics of a BCI-based control interface** in terms of bandwidth, ITR, latency, and usability. Compare it with a **sip-and-puff interface** for a powered wheelchair. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **head-tracking controlled wheelchair** uses a 9-DOF IMU sampled at 100 Hz. If the controller introduces 18 ms processing delay and the motor driver adds 22 ms, compute the **total latency** and comment on whether it satisfies the 100 ms safety threshold. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) BCI vs. Sip-and-Puff (7 marks):**

| Characteristic | BCI | Sip-and-Puff |
|----------------|-----|--------------|
| Bandwidth | 0–40 Hz (alpha/beta/mu) | DC to ~5 Hz |
| Latency | 200–500 ms (P300), 50–100 ms (motor imagery) | 30–50 ms |
| ITR | 5–60 bits/min | 60+ bits/min |
| Usability | High cognitive load, long training | Low cognitive load, intuitive |
| Robustness | Susceptible to artifacts | Highly robust |
| Cost / Setup | High | Low |

(2 marks for BCI characteristics, 2 marks for sip-and-puff, 2 marks for comparison table, 1 mark for inference.)

**Inference:** Sip-and-puff is preferred for users with intact breath control; BCI is a last-resort interface when no other modality works. (1 mark)

**(b) Latency Calculation (7 marks):**
**Step 1:** Identify delays.
- $t_{sensor}$ = negligible for IMU (assume < 5 ms)
- $t_{processing} = 18$ ms
- $t_{actuator} = 22$ ms

**Step 2:** Total.
$$ t_{latency} = 5 + 18 + 22 = 45 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 3:** Compare with threshold.
$$ 45 \text{ ms} \ll 100 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 4:** Safety margin.
$$ M = 100 - 45 = 55 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 5:** Conclusion (1 mark). The interface is **safe** for wheelchair control. Additional processing for obstacle detection (e.g., LiDAR + AI) must remain within the 55 ms headroom.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "bandwidth" with "data rate"** — bandwidth is in Hz, not bps. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the Nyquist factor of 2** — common error: writing $f_s = f_{max}$ instead of $f_s = 2 f_{max}$. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Not converting milliseconds correctly** in latency problems (e.g., 100 ms ≠ 100 s). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Omitting the unit "dB"** when computing SNR or dynamic range. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping the closed-loop diagram** in 7-mark questions — KTU examiners award 2–3 marks for a clear, labeled block diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **control interface** is the bidirectional communication layer between the impaired user and the assistive device.
- ✅ **Five key characteristics** to memorize for KTU exams: Bandwidth, Latency, SNR, Accuracy, Usability.
- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem** governs sampling: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- ✅ **EMG sampling** typically uses $f_s = 1$ kHz; **EEG** uses 256–512 Hz.
- ✅ **Latency budgets** must remain under **100 ms** for wheelchairs and **10 ms** for surgical robotics.
- ✅ **Dynamic range** in clinical EMG requires ≥ **60 dB**; hence 12–16 bit ADCs.
- ✅ **Closed-loop feedback** (haptic, visual, auditory) is critical for usability.
- ✅ **ISO 9241-11** defines usability as Effectiveness × Efficiency × Satisfaction.
- ✅ **ITR formula** is essential for BCI questions.
- ✅ Always draw a **block diagram** — it secures 2–3 marks even with minimal explanation.
- ✅ For **prosthetic hands**, the dominant interface metric is *SNR*; for **BCI**, it is *ITR*; for **wheelchairs**, it is *latency*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CI for Direct and Indirect Selection - Keyboards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CI for Direct and Indirect Selection - Keyboards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Communication Interface (CI) in the Context of Assistive Technology

> [!NOTE]
> **Communication Interface (CI)**: A Communication Interface in assistive technology refers to the hardware/software mechanism that allows a user with a disability to translate their **intent** (a movement, a thought, a sound) into a **command** that a computer, communication device, or environmental control system can understand and execute.

In the language of the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based curriculum, a Communication Interface is the **transducer layer** of the human–machine interaction (HMI) loop. It sits between the **user's residual functional capability** and the **digital system** that must be controlled.

$$
\text{User Intent} \xrightarrow{\text{Communication Interface (CI)}} \text{Digital Command} \xrightarrow{\text{Application}} \text{Desired Output}
$$

## 1.2 Direct Selection vs Indirect Selection

These are the **two principal paradigms** of human input in assistive technology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Selection** is an input technique in which the user **physically points to or activates** the exact target choice in **one single motor action** without any intermediary mediation. Examples include: pressing a key on a QWERTY keyboard, touching an icon on a touchscreen, or moving a mouse cursor over a menu item.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indirect Selection** is an input technique in which the user **does not** activate the target directly. Instead, the system **presents choices sequentially** (a process called *scanning*), and the user activates a **single switch** at the precise moment the desired choice is highlighted. Examples include: single-switch scanning across a virtual keyboard, Morse code entry via one switch, or sip-and-puff navigation.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy** — Think of ordering food at a busy restaurant:
> - **Direct Selection** is like walking up to a counter and *pointing* at the burger you want from the menu displayed. One action, target acquired.
> - **Indirect Selection** is like calling the restaurant by phone. The waiter reads out: "Burger… Pizza… Pasta… Salad… Say STOP when I say the one you want." You can only say "stop" — the scanning system does the pointing, you do the triggering. Slower, but works when you cannot point.

## 1.3 The Keyboard as a Communication Interface

A **keyboard** is the most ubiquitous **direct selection** communication interface. In the assistive context, however, "keyboard" extends far beyond the standard 101-key QWERTY layout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assistive Keyboard (Definition)**: Any input device or surface—physical, virtual, or projected—that allows a user to enter text, commands, or symbols into a computer, speech-generating device (SGD), or augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) system, where the keyboard's **physical, logical, or operational characteristics have been modified** to accommodate the user's functional limitations.

### Classification of Assistive Keyboards (KTU Syllabus Scope)

| Category | Description | Selection Mode | Typical User |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard QWERTY Keyboard | 101/104-key unmodified keyboard | Direct | Mild motor impairment |
| Ergonomic Keyboard | Split, contoured, tented layout | Direct | RSI, Cerebral Palsy (mild) |
| Enlarged / BigKeys Keyboard | Oversized keys (1–2 inch), high contrast | Direct | Low vision, motor incoordination, paediatric |
| Mini / Compact Keyboard | Reduced footprint, often wireless | Direct | Limited reach, portable use |
| Membrane / Flexible Keyboard | Silicone-based, spill-proof, rollable | Direct | Severe motor impairment, spasticity |
| On-Screen / Virtual Keyboard | Software-rendered, touch/pointer activated | Direct | Touchscreen AAC users |
| Keyguard-Equipped Keyboard | Physical overlay with cut-out holes | Direct | Athetosis, tremor, involuntary movement |
| One-Handed Keyboard (e.g., Maltron, CyKey) | Chord / mirrored layout | Direct | Single-hand amputee, hemiplegia |
| Foot / Mouth-Stick Operated Keyboard | Activated by foot or stylus held in mouth | Direct | Quadriplegia (high-level) |
| Scanning / Single-Switch Keyboard | Software keyboard with auto-scan or step-scan | Indirect | Locked-in syndrome, severe motor disability |
| Morse Code Keyboard | Two-switch input (dot/dash) decodes to letters | Indirect | Severe motor disability, good cognition |
| Predictive / Word-Prediction Keyboard | Software reduces keystrokes via prediction | Direct/Indirect hybrid | Slow typers, dyslexia, motor fatigue |

## 1.4 The Keyboard Selection Decision Tree (Intuitive)

```
Can the user point with at least ONE reliable body part?
        │
        ├── YES ──► Can they reach all 26 letters quickly?
        │              │
        │              ├── YES ──► Standard / Ergonomic Keyboard (Direct)
        │              │
        │              └── NO ──► Enlarged / Mini / Membrane / On-Screen (Direct)
        │
        └── NO (no reliable pointing) ──► Scanning Keyboard (Indirect)
                                              │
                                              ├── One switch  ──► Auto/Step Scan
                                              ├── Two switches ──► Morse Code
                                              └── Head-tracking + dwell-click ──► Hybrid
```

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

This topic is mapped to **CO2 (Module 2)** of PEBRT745. Students must demonstrate the ability to **analyse, compare, and recommend** appropriate keyboard-based communication interfaces for users with motor, visual, and cognitive disabilities. The examiner expects: (a) **classification fluency**, (b) **matching user limitation to keyboard type**, and (c) **understanding of scanning rates and prediction algorithms** in indirect selection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in This Module** (memorize for KTU exams):
> - **Scanning Rate (bits/sec)** = rate at which indirect-selection choices are presented
> - **Keystrokes Per Character (KSPC)** = efficiency metric for text entry
> - **Words Per Minute (WPM)** = standard typing speed benchmark
> - **Selection Time (sec)** = time from intent to activation
> - **Bits of Information per Selection (Hick's Law)** = $\log_2(N)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct Selection Keyboards — Operational Theory

In a direct selection keyboard, every target key has a **one-to-one mapping** with a character or function. The user generates the **motor action** and the **selection decision** simultaneously. The communication interface therefore requires:

1. **Spatial targeting accuracy** — the user must be able to move their effector (finger, stylus, pointer, foot) to a coordinate on the keyboard plane.
2. **Activation force control** — the user must be able to apply a force within the keyboard's actuation range.
3. **Release / repeat management** — the user must avoid unwanted keystrokes (anti-bounce / N-key rollover properties).

### 2.1.1 Standard Physical Keyboard Architecture

A standard keyboard uses a **matrix circuit** of rows and columns. Each key is a momentary switch at the intersection of a row wire and a column wire. A microcontroller (e.g., the venerable Intel 8042, or modern equivalents in USB HID interfaces) scans the matrix at a high frequency (typically **8 kHz anti-ghosting scan rate**) to detect closures.

> [!NOTE]
> **N-Key Rollover (NKR)**: The maximum number of simultaneous key presses that the keyboard can correctly register. Standard keyboards offer 2-Key Rollover (2KR); gaming and assistive keyboards offer **Full N-Key Rollover (NKRO)**—critical for fast typists and users using **chord keying**.

### 2.1.2 Membrane / Flexible Keyboards

A **membrane keyboard** replaces mechanical key switches with a three-layer flexible circuit: top membrane (with printed traces), spacer layer (with cut-outs at key locations), and bottom membrane (with perpendicular traces). When the user presses a key location, the top trace flexes down through the spacer hole to contact the bottom trace, completing the circuit. Benefits: **waterproof, low profile (1–3 mm thickness), near-silent, very low actuation force (typically 25–60 g)**.

### 2.1.3 Keyguard-Equipped Keyboards

A **keyguard** is a rigid (typically aluminium or high-density plastic) overlay mounted 3–5 mm above the keys with precisely machined cut-out holes, one per key. It serves three engineering functions:

- **Spatial filter** — prevents accidental keypresses by adjacent fingers or an unsteady hand
- **Resting surface** — the user can rest their palm on the guard, reducing fatigue
- **Targeting anchor** — the hole edge provides tactile reference for the finger

It is the single most prescribed modification for users with **athetosis, tremor, or spasticity** (e.g., users with Cerebral Palsy).

### 2.1.4 On-Screen / Virtual Keyboards

A virtual keyboard is rendered on a display and activated by a pointer (mouse, head-tracker, eye-tracker, touch, or stylus). For assistive users, dwell-click activation (a pointer that hovers over a key for a configurable dwell time, typically **0.5–1.5 seconds**) replaces the physical click. Advanced implementations use **Dasher** (a gesture-based text entry system) or **predictive completion** to reduce the search space.

### 2.1.5 Ergonomic Keyboards — The Biomechanical Rationale

Ergonomic keyboards reconfigure the standard rectangular key matrix into **contoured, split, or tented** geometries that match the natural resting position of the human hand. The two principal biomechanical objectives are:

- **Neutral wrist posture** (avoiding ulnar deviation and extension)
- **Reduced forearm pronation** (some designs rotate the key plane 30°–60°)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinesis Contoured, Microsoft Sculpt Ergonomic, Maltron 3D** are industry references. The **Maltron 3D** keyboard additionally positions the **thumb cluster** under the strongest digit, offloading ~40% of keypresses from the weaker fingers—a major advantage for users with hand weakness.

## 2.2 Indirect Selection Keyboards — Operational Theory

Indirect selection is **necessary** when the user has insufficient motor control to execute the pointing and activation required for direct selection. The CI must therefore **share the cognitive and motor load** with the user.

### 2.2.1 The Scanning Paradigm

In **automatic scanning**, the system cycles through choices (letters, words, or functions) and highlights one at a time. The user **waits** for the desired item to be highlighted and then **activates a switch** to select it.

Two principal scanning modes:

1. **Linear Scan** — highlights items one by one in a fixed order (e.g., left-to-right, row by row). Simple, but **slow** (selection time grows linearly with N).
2. **Row-Column Scan** — first scans rows, then within the selected row scans columns. Reduces selection time from $O(N)$ to $O(2\sqrt{N})$.
3. **Block / Group Scan** — partitions the keyboard into groups (e.g., 4 quadrants), recursively scans each group. Further reduces complexity.
4. **Auditory Scan** — uses speech synthesis to speak each item; the user activates the switch when the desired item is spoken (essential for users with **visual + motor impairment**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scanning Rate Selection**: The system must scan **fast enough** to be efficient but **slow enough** that the user can react. Typical rates are 1–2 seconds per item. Too fast → mis-selection. Too slow → user fatigue and loss of attention.

### 2.2.2 Switches Used with Indirect Selection Keyboards

The single-switch input is the **physical transducer** of the indirect selection system. Switch types relevant to PEBRT745:

| Switch Type | Activation Method | Typical User |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Lever / Button** | Press with finger, head, knee | Limited hand use |
| **Pneumatic (Sip-and-Puff)** | Sip (inhale) / Puff (exhale) | High-level quadriplegia, ventilator users |
| **Muscle-Contact (EMG)** | Voluntary muscle contraction | Locked-in syndrome, ALS |
| **Blink / EOG** | Eye-blink detected by electro-oculography | Locked-in syndrome |
| **Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)** | EEG-detected P300 or motor imagery | Complete paralysis (late-stage ALS) |

### 2.2.3 Morse Code Keyboard (Two-Switch Indirect)

A Morse code keyboard uses **two switches** (or one two-mode switch): one for **dot (·)**, one for **dash (–)**. The CI decodes sequences into characters. Example: **C = –·–·**, **O = –––**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Morse Code Advantages**:
> - Extremely fast for trained users (**40–60 WPM possible**, far exceeding single-switch scanning)
> - Self-pacing — no scan rate dependency
> - Only **2 switches** (or even **1 switch with two timings** — a la "dit" if held <0.3s, "dah" if held >0.3s)
>
> **Morse Code Disadvantages**:
> - Steep learning curve (must memorise the code)
> - Decoding ambiguity (inter-character timing must be managed by the system)
> - Not suitable for users with cognitive impairment

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **core analytical formulas** examiners expect you to recognise and apply for keyboard-based CIs. All quantities are in **SI units** unless otherwise specified. Memorise the **Hick-Hyman Law**, **KSPC**, and **scanning time** formulas—these are the most heavily tested.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hick-Hyman Law** (Choice Reaction Time) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)$ | $T$ = reaction time (sec); $N$ = number of equally probable choices; $a$, $b$ = empirical constants (typically $a \approx 0.2$ s, $b \approx 0.15$ s/bit) | Models **direct selection** speed as a function of menu/keystroke count |
| 2 | **Fitts' Law** (Index of Difficulty) | $ID = \log_2\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$ | $A$ = distance to target; $W$ = target width | Models **pointing time** to a key |
| 3 | **Fitts' Law** (Movement Time) | $MT = a + b \cdot ID$ | $MT$ = movement time (sec) | Used to size keys for users with limited dexterity |
| 4 | **Keystrokes Per Character** | $KSPC = \frac{\text{Total Keystrokes}}{\text{Characters Typed}}$ | Dimensionless; ideal $\geq 1.0$ | Lower KSPC $\Rightarrow$ higher efficiency (word prediction reduces KSPC) |
| 5 | **Single-Switch Scan Time** | $T_{scan} = (N-1) \cdot t_{item} + t_{react}$ | $N$ = items; $t_{item}$ = scan rate; $t_{react}$ = user reaction | Linear scanning time |
| 6 | **Row-Column Scan Time** | $T_{rc} = (\sqrt{N}-1) \cdot t_{row} + (\sqrt{N}-1) \cdot t_{col} + t_{react}$ | — | Quadratic-time reduction |
| 7 | **Information Content per Character** | $H = \log_2(27) \approx 4.755$ bits/char | 26 letters + space | Used to compute theoretical max typing rate |
| 8 | **Maximum Typing Rate (theoretical)** | $R_{max} = \frac{60 \cdot H}{H \cdot T_{scan}}$ characters/min | — | Upper bound for indirect selection |
| 9 | **Effective Text Entry Rate** | $WPM = \frac{60 \cdot H}{T_{char} \cdot H \cdot 5.0}$ | $T_{char}$ = time per character (sec) | Real-world measured speed |
| 10 | **Word Prediction Reduction** | $R_{pred} = 1 - \frac{KSPC_{predicted}}{KSPC_{baseline}}$ | Ratio in $[0, 1]$ | Quantifies the speedup from word prediction |
| 11 | **Bits per Selection (Hick's Law)** | $H_{sel} = \log_2(N)$ bits | $N$ = number of choices | Information-theoretic content of a single choice |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown rendering safeguard**: In the table above, all absolute values, divisions, and the `2` in the Fitts' Law denominator are **not** the vertical pipe character. Do not type the pipe character `|` inside the LaTeX expressions. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if you must write absolute value. The fraction $\frac{2A}{W}$ is rendered using `\frac{}{}` and is safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Rehabilitation Engineering** — prescription of keyboards by occupational therapists is a routine clinical decision; the engineering framework (Fitts' Law, Hick's Law) makes it quantitative rather than ad-hoc.
- **HCI / UX Research** — Microsoft's Inclusive Design Toolkit, Google's Accessibility Scanner, and Apple's AssistiveTouch all draw on these laws to size default UI targets (Apple's **44×44 pt** minimum target derives directly from Fitts' Law with measured finger contact area).
- **AAC Device Manufacturing** — Tobii Dynavox, PRC-Saltillo, and Smartbox use these same formulas to size on-screen keyboard buttons, choose scan rates, and tune prediction lists.
- **Industrial / Defence** — ruggedised keyboards for military, aviation, and marine use inherit the keyguard concept and membrane construction for harsh environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates the **worked analytical reasoning** the KTU 2024 examiner expects on a 14-mark design/numerical question.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Scan Time for an Indirect Selection Keyboard

> **Problem**: A user with high-level quadriplegia operates an AAC device using a single sip-and-puff switch with a scanning on-screen QWERTY keyboard. The system uses **row-column scanning** with 4 rows × 10 columns (40 cells, but only 26 are active letter cells; the rest hold SPACE, ENTER, BACKSPACE, etc.). The scan rate is set to **1.2 seconds per item** and the user's average switch reaction time is **0.4 seconds**. Compute:
> (a) The expected selection time per character using linear scan.
> (b) The expected selection time per character using row-column scan.
> (c) The **theoretical maximum typing rate in WPM** for the row-column configuration.

### 3.1.1 Part (a) — Linear Scan Time

In linear scan, the system highlights items one after another. For a 40-cell keyboard scanned sequentially, the system must highlight each cell until the desired one is reached. The **expected** number of highlights before a selection is the average over all items:

$$
E[\text{highlights}] = \frac{N + 1}{2} = \frac{40 + 1}{2} = 20.5
$$

This is the **expected number of scan steps** before the user activates the switch. Each step takes $t_{item} = 1.2$ s, and after the user activates the switch there is a $t_{react} = 0.4$ s recognition delay (the system must recognise the switch closure and execute the selection).

Therefore the **expected linear scan time per character** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{linear} &= (E[\text{highlights}] - 1) \cdot t_{item} + t_{react} \\
&= (20.5 - 1) \cdot 1.2 + 0.4 \\
&= 19.5 \cdot 1.2 + 0.4 \\
&= 23.4 + 0.4 \\
&= 23.8 \text{ seconds per character}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic**: The $-1$ appears because when the desired item is *currently* highlighted, the user does not need to wait for the *next* highlight — they wait only the time to recognise the item and react. So we subtract one full scan step but add the reaction time.

### 3.1.2 Part (b) — Row-Column Scan Time

In row-column scan, the system first scans the 4 rows. When the user selects the correct row, the system then scans the 10 cells in that row.

**Expected number of row scans** before selection:

$$
E[\text{rows}] = \frac{4 + 1}{2} = 2.5
$$

**Expected number of column scans** within the selected row (assuming the desired cell is equally likely to be in any of the 10 positions):

$$
E[\text{cols}] = \frac{10 + 1}{2} = 5.5
$$

**Total expected scan time** for row-column:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rc} &= (E[\text{rows}] - 1) \cdot t_{item} + (E[\text{cols}] - 1) \cdot t_{item} + 2 \cdot t_{react} \\
&= (2.5 - 1) \cdot 1.2 + (5.5 - 1) \cdot 1.2 + 2 \cdot 0.4 \\
&= 1.5 \cdot 1.2 + 4.5 \cdot 1.2 + 0.8 \\
&= 1.8 + 5.4 + 0.8 \\
&= 8.0 \text{ seconds per character}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic**: We use $2 \cdot t_{react}$ because the user has two independent switch-activation events (one for row, one for column). The "$-1$" reasoning applies at each stage.

**Speedup ratio**:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{linear}}{T_{rc}} = \frac{23.8}{8.0} = 2.975 \approx 3\times
$$

### 3.1.3 Part (c) — Maximum Typing Rate in WPM

The standard English-typing convention defines **1 word = 5 characters** (including space). The information-theoretic content per character is $H = \log_2(27) \approx 4.755$ bits (26 letters + space).

**Time per character** in the row-column case = $T_{rc} = 8.0$ s.

**Words per minute**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WPM &= \frac{60 \text{ s/min}}{T_{rc} \text{ s/char} \times 5 \text{ chars/word}} \\
&= \frac{60}{8.0 \times 5} \\
&= \frac{60}{40} \\
&= 1.5 \text{ WPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: 1.5 WPM is realistic for an **untrained** sip-and-puff user. Trained users with word prediction (KSPC ≈ 2) reach **3–5 WPM**, and high-end AAC users with language modelling reach **10–15 WPM** — still an order of magnitude below able-bodied typing (40+ WPM), but functionally usable for conversation.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fitts' Law for Adaptive Keyboard Sizing

> **Problem**: An occupational therapist is designing a custom keyboard for a user with Cerebral Palsy who uses a head-mounted pointer. The pointer's targeting accuracy is such that the **effective target width the user can reliably hit** is $W = 20$ mm. The most distant key is **180 mm** from the resting position of the pointer. Compute the **Index of Difficulty** and the **expected movement time** using empirical constants $a = 0.05$ s and $b = 0.18$ s/bit (for head-pointer users).

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Compute ID

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) \\
&= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \times 180}{20}\right) \\
&= \log_2\left(\frac{360}{20}\right) \\
&= \log_2(18) \\
&= \frac{\ln(18)}{\ln(2)} \\
&= \frac{2.8904}{0.6931} \\
&\approx 4.17 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Compute Movement Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
&= 0.05 + 0.18 \times 4.17 \\
&= 0.05 + 0.7506 \\
&\approx 0.80 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Design Implication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design takeaway**: A movement time of ~0.8 s per key is acceptable for **slow conversational typing** (≈ 15 WPM) but inefficient for fast composition. The **prescription** is to **enlarge the keys** to reduce $ID$. For example, doubling $W$ to 40 mm would reduce $ID$ to $\log_2(360/40) = \log_2(9) \approx 3.17$ bits, reducing $MT$ to:
> $$MT_{new} = 0.05 + 0.18 \times 3.17 \approx 0.62 \text{ s}$$
> This is a **22% speedup** purely from physical key resizing, with no software change. This is why **BigKeys** and **enlarged on-screen keys** are clinically prescribed.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Word Prediction Reduction

> **Problem**: An AAC user with motor fatigue is currently typing at $KSPC_{baseline} = 3.5$ (very high — indicating a lot of navigation and correction keystrokes). The clinician enables a word-prediction list of 6 candidates. After 1 week of use, $KSPC$ drops to **1.8**. Compute the **prediction reduction ratio** and the **new WPM** if the user's average time-per-keystroke is 0.6 s.

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Reduction Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{pred} &= 1 - \frac{KSPC_{predicted}}{KSPC_{baseline}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1.8}{3.5} \\
&= 1 - 0.5143 \\
&= 0.4857 \\
&\approx 48.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — New WPM

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{char} &= KSPC \cdot t_{key} = 1.8 \times 0.6 = 1.08 \text{ s/char} \\
WPM &= \frac{60}{T_{char} \times 5} = \frac{60}{1.08 \times 5} = \frac{60}{5.4} \approx 11.11 \text{ WPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result**: The word-prediction list nearly **halved the keystrokes** and lifted the user to ~11 WPM — a **conversationally functional** rate. This is why **word prediction is the single most cost-effective software intervention** for slow AAC typers.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Matching User Limitation to Keyboard Type

This matrix is a **high-yield revision tool** for the KTU exam. Memorise the pairing logic.

| User Limitation | Primary Motor Deficit | Recommended Keyboard | Selection Mode | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low vision | Visual acuity < 6/18 | Enlarged (BigKeys) or high-contrast | Direct | Larger key labels (≥24 pt) reduce ID |
| Blindness | No visual input | **Refreshable Braille Display** + standard keyboard | Direct | Braille is the tactile analog of the keyboard |
| Hemiplegia (one-hand) | Loss of one hand function | Chord keyboard (Maltron, CyKey) | Direct | One hand can produce 100+ chords |
| Quadriplegia (C4–C5) | No hand, no arm, neck control | Sip-and-puff + scanning keyboard | Indirect | Pneumatic switch + row-column scan |
| Quadriplegia (C1–C2) | No voluntary motor below neck | Head-tracker + dwell-click on-screen keyboard | Direct (with dwell) | Camera tracks head pose |
| Locked-in syndrome | Near-total paralysis | BCI (P300 speller) or EOG blink keyboard | Indirect | EEG-detected P300 selects letter |
| Cerebral Palsy (athetosis) | Involuntary movement | **Keyguard** + standard keyboard | Direct | Physical restraint via guard cut-outs |
| Cerebral Palsy (spasticity) | High tone, low coordination | Membrane / force-minimised keyboard | Direct | Low actuation force (≤ 30 g) |
| Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) | Progressive weakness | Head-tracker → eye-tracker → BCI (escalating) | Direct → Indirect | Progressive adaptation over disease course |
| Learning disability | Cognitive / literacy difficulty | **AAC picture keyboard** (no text) | Direct | Symbol-based, no literacy required |
| Dyslexia | Reading/spelling difficulty | Keyboard with **phonetic prediction** | Direct | Software layer, hardware unchanged |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Communication Interface Architecture

The block diagram below maps the **information flow** of a generic keyboard-based CI, from the user's motor intent to the digital system's response. This is the **canonical answer** to a "Draw the block diagram of an AT input system" question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Intent<br/>Neural Motor Command] --> B[Residual Function<br/>Hand Head Eye Breath]
    B --> C[Physical Transducer<br/>Keyboard Switch<br/>Touchscreen Membrane<br/>Sip and Puff Pneumatic]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning<br/>Debounce Amplify Filter]
    D --> E[Encoder / Decoder<br/>ASCII Unicode Scan Logic<br/>Morse Decode]
    E --> F[Operating System<br/>HID Driver Layer]
    F --> G[Application Layer<br/>Word Processor<br/>AAC Synthesiser<br/>Environmental Control]
    G --> H[Feedback to User<br/>Display Audio Haptic<br/>Tactile Braille]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style D fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style E fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style G fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**:
> - **A → B**: The brain issues a motor command; the user's *residual* function (whichever body part still works) executes it.
> - **B → C**: The residual function actuates a physical transducer (the keyboard switch).
> - **C → D**: Raw electrical signal is debounced (to remove switch bounce, typically 5–10 ms), amplified, and filtered.
> - **D → E**: The signal is encoded into a standard character (ASCII / Unicode) or interpreted by scan logic / Morse decoder.
> - **E → F → G**: The encoded character is handed to the OS, which routes it to the active application.
> - **G → H**: The application provides feedback so the user can verify and correct.

## 4.2 Direct vs Indirect Selection — Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[User Has Communication Need] --> Q1{Can User<br/>Point and Click<br/>Reliably?}
    Q1 -->|YES| Direct[Direct Selection Path]
    Q1 -->|NO| Indirect[Indirect Selection Path]

    Direct --> D1[Effector: Finger Stylus Head Eye Foot]
    D1 --> D2[One Motor Action = One Selection]
    D2 --> D3[Examples:<br/>QWERTY Keyboard<br/>Touchscreen<br/>Mouse Click<br/>Eye Gaze with Dwell]
    D3 --> D4[Speed: 20 to 60 plus WPM]
    D4 --> D5[Cognitive Load: Low]
    D5 --> D6[Hardware: Standard or Adapted Keyboard]

    Indirect --> I1[Effector: One or Two Switches]
    I1 --> I2[System Presents Choices Sequentially]
    I2 --> I3[User Activates Switch at Right Moment]
    I3 --> I4[Examples:<br/>Single Switch Scan<br/>Morse Code<br/>Auditory Scan<br/>BCI P300]
    I4 --> I5[Speed: 1 to 15 WPM]
    I5 --> I6[Cognitive Load: Moderate to High]
    I6 --> I7[Hardware: Switch plus Scanning Software]

    style Start fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style Direct fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style Indirect fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style Q1 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

flowchart TD

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this diagram in an exam**: When asked "Which keyboard would you prescribe for a user with X condition?", start at the **Input** block, follow the decision branches, and end at the **Out** block. This decision tree is itself a valid 7-mark answer to a clinical-prescription question.

## 4.5 ASCII Schematic — Membrane Keyboard Layer Cross-Section

For the engineering description of a flexible/membrane keyboard, a cross-section schematic is useful:

```
   Top Membrane Layer      ──────────────────────  ← Printed top circuit traces
                                                 
   Spacer Layer (with holes) ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─   ← 0.1–0.3 mm adhesive spacer
                                       ◯ ◯ ◯     ← Perforations at each key
                                                 
   Bottom Membrane Layer   ──────────────────────  ← Perpendicular bottom traces
   
   Backing / Tail          ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  ← Rigid backing + connector
```

**Activation mechanism**: A finger press on the top membrane deflects it through the spacer perforation until it contacts the bottom membrane, completing the circuit at the corresponding row/column intersection.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern Reminder**:
> - **Part A (3 marks each)**: 5–6 short-answer questions, answer any 3–4. These test definitions and classifications.
> - **Part B (14 marks each)**: Module-level choice questions. Sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. These test design, comparison, and analysis.
> - Bloom's levels tested: **Remember, Understand** (Part A) and **Apply, Analyse, Evaluate** (Part B).

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
Differentiate between **direct selection** and **indirect selection** modes of access in assistive communication interfaces. Give **one example of a keyboard-based CI** for each mode.

> **Model Answer (3 marks)**:
> - **Direct Selection (1.5 marks)**: A direct selection technique is one in which the user **physically points to or activates the desired target in a single, self-directed motor action**, without any intermediate system mediation. The user's effector (finger, stylus, pointer) travels to the target and actuates it. **Example**: A **standard QWERTY keyboard** (or an enlarged BigKeys keyboard) where the user reaches and presses each letter key directly. The motor plan and the selection decision are unified in one action.
> - **Indirect Selection (1.5 marks)**: An indirect selection technique is one in which the user **does not point to the target**. Instead, the system **sequentially presents** the available choices (a process called *scanning*) and the user activates a **single switch** at the moment the desired target is highlighted. The system's scan logic does the pointing; the user only triggers. **Example**: A **scanning on-screen QWERTY keyboard** with auto-scan and a single sip-and-puff switch used by a person with high-level quadriplegia.

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is a **keyguard** in the context of assistive keyboards? State **two clinical conditions** for which a keyguard is commonly prescribed and explain why.

> **Model Answer (3 marks)**:
> - **Definition (1 mark)**: A keyguard is a **rigid plate (typically aluminium or high-density plastic) mounted a few millimetres above the keys of a keyboard**, with precisely cut-out holes—one per key. It provides a physical barrier that prevents accidental key activation and a tactile reference for targeting.
> - **Two clinical conditions (2 marks)**: (1) **Cerebral Palsy with athetosis** — users have involuntary, writhing movements of the limbs; the keyguard's holes constrain hand motion to the intended key, suppressing mis-presses. (2) **Essential tremor / Parkinson's disease** — users have rhythmic oscillation; the keyguard's raised edges act as a **mechanical damper**, giving the finger a stable target and absorbing lateral motion. *(Equally valid: severe spasticity, multiple sclerosis, motor-neuron disease.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B carries 14 marks per question. The module-internal choice means you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are presented below; pick one in the exam.

### Question A — Communication Interfaces for Direct and Indirect Selection

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice A]**
>
> **(a)** Classify **keyboard-based communication interfaces** used in assistive technology. For **each category**, state the user population it serves and the selection mode (direct/indirect). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A user with **locked-in syndrome** (near-total paralysis, intact cognition, reliable eye-blink) is to be prescribed a keyboard-based CI. Compare **two candidate solutions** (one direct-selection, one indirect-selection) in terms of speed (WPM), cognitive load, hardware complexity, and clinical practicality. Justify your final recommendation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part A(a) — 7 marks

**Classification of keyboard-based CIs (5 marks for the table, 2 marks for explanation)**:

| Category | Selection Mode | Example Keyboard | Target User Population |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard / Ergonomic | Direct | Apple Magic Keyboard, Kinesis Contoured | Mild motor or RSI |
| Enlarged / BigKeys | Direct | BigKeys LX, Chester Creek | Low vision, paediatric, motor incoordination |
| Membrane / Flexible | Direct | iGlove Flexible Roll-up, Hira silicone | Spasticity, severe weakness, moist environments |
| Keyguard-Modified | Direct | Standard KB + custom-cut keyguard | Athetosis, tremor, spasticity (CP, PD) |
| Chord / One-Handed | Direct | Maltron 3D, CyKey, Twiddler | Hemiplegia, single-hand amputee |
| On-Screen / Virtual | Direct (with dwell) | iPad on-screen, Grid 3, TouchChat | Touchscreen AAC, progressive disease |
| Foot / Mouth-Stick | Direct | Footmouse + standard KB; mouthstick + KB | Quadriplegia (C4–C6) |
| **Scanning / Single-Switch** | **Indirect** | SCAN mode in Grid 3 / Proloquo4 | High quadriplegia, locked-in (no pointer) |
| **Morse Code (two-switch)** | **Indirect** | EK-Tap Morse, custom Morse AAC | Severe motor, good cognition |
| **BCI / P300 Speller** | **Indirect** | g.tec intendiX, OpenViBE P300 | Complete paralysis, locked-in |

**Explanatory notes (2 marks)**: The classification is fundamentally along the **axis of residual motor function**. As the user's motor capability decreases, the CI migrates from direct to indirect, and from keyboard-as-pointer to keyboard-as-target-of-scanning. The boundary between direct and indirect is set by the user's ability to **close the sensorimotor feedback loop** in one action.

#### Model Answer — Part A(b) — 7 marks

**Candidate 1 — Direct selection: Eye-gaze + on-screen keyboard with dwell-click (3.5 marks)**

- **Hardware**: Eye-tracker (e.g., Tobii Dynavox TD-I-13), on-screen QWERTY keyboard (Grid 3, Proloquo2Go), or a tablet with built-in eye-tracking.
- **Speed**: Trained users achieve **10–20 WPM**.
- **Cognitive load**: Low — user looks at the desired key, dwells 0.5–1.0 s, the key is selected. No memorisation required.
- **Hardware complexity**: Moderate — eye-tracker costs ₹2–6 lakh; calibration is required each session.
- **Clinical practicality**: Excellent for users with **reliable eye control and minimal ocular tremor**. Fails for users with Nystagmus, severe ptosis, or active seizures.

**Candidate 2 — Indirect selection: Single-switch row-column scanning on-screen keyboard (3.5 marks)**

- **Hardware**: A single switch (sip-and-puff, blink sensor, EMG, or BCI), scanning software, on-screen keyboard.
- **Speed**: Trained users achieve **3–8 WPM** (without prediction); up to **12–15 WPM** with word prediction.
- **Cognitive load**: Moderate — user must attend to the scan, inhibit premature switch activation, and tolerate slow feedback.
- **Hardware complexity**: Low — a single switch + a standard tablet/laptop with scanning software (e.g., Grid 3 scan mode, ClickToScan, EViacam).
- **Clinical practicality**: Excellent for users with **no reliable pointing** but with at least **one reliable, controllable physiological signal** (blink, breath, muscle twitch).

**Final recommendation (must state explicitly)**:
> For a user with **locked-in syndrome and a reliable eye-blink**, the **single-switch row-column scanning keyboard with word prediction** is the **most clinically robust** initial prescription. Rationale: (i) eye-blink is a near-universally preserved signal in locked-in syndrome; (ii) scanning requires only one reliable signal, whereas eye-gaze requires continuous, calibration-stable ocular-motor control which may degrade; (iii) cost is an order of magnitude lower; (iv) the system can be **upgraded progressively** to a BCI if even the blink is lost. The eye-gaze system should be kept as the **second-line** upgrade if scanning proves too slow.

---

### Question B — Scanning Keyboards, Fitts' Law, and Clinical Prescription

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **scanning paradigms** used in indirect selection keyboards. With the help of a **numerical example**, demonstrate how the choice of scan strategy (linear vs row-column) affects the time required to select a character on a 30-cell keyboard. Assume a scan rate of 1.0 s per item and a switch-reaction time of 0.5 s. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using **Fitts' Law**, justify why **enlarged keys** are prescribed for users with cerebral palsy and reduced fine-motor accuracy. Compute the **Index of Difficulty** and **expected movement time** for a key at distance $A = 150$ mm and effective target width $W = 15$ mm. Take the empirical constants as $a = 0.05$ s and $b = 0.20$ s/bit. If the keys are enlarged so that $W = 30$ mm, by what percentage does the movement time reduce? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part B(a) — 7 marks

**Scanning paradigms (4 marks for theory, 3 marks for numerical)**:

1. **Linear Scan (1 mark)**: The system highlights items one by one in a fixed order (left-to-right, top-to-bottom). The user waits for the desired item to be highlighted and activates a single switch. **Advantage**: simplest to implement. **Disadvantage**: selection time scales linearly with the number of items.

2. **Row-Column Scan (1 mark)**: The system first scans *rows*. The user activates the switch to select the row containing the desired character. The system then scans *columns* within that row. The user selects the column. **Advantage**: selection time scales with $2\sqrt{N}$ instead of $N$. **Disadvantage**: requires the user to manage two switch activations per character.

3. **Block / Group Scan (1 mark)**: The keyboard is partitioned into 2–4 groups. The system scans groups first, then items within the selected group, recursively. Reduces selection time further. Often combined with auditory prompts.

4. **Auditory Scan (1 mark)**: The system uses speech synthesis to *speak* each item in sequence. Essential for users with **visual + motor impairment** (e.g., deaf-blind users using a tactile switch). Speed is the limiting factor.

**Numerical demonstration (3 marks)**:

For a 30-cell keyboard arranged as **5 rows × 6 columns**:

**Linear scan** expected time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{highlights}] &= \frac{N+1}{2} = \frac{30+1}{2} = 15.5 \\
T_{linear} &= (15.5 - 1) \cdot 1.0 + 0.5 = 14.5 + 0.5 = 15.0 \text{ s/char}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row-column scan** expected time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{rows}] &= \frac{5+1}{2} = 3.0 \\
E[\text{cols}] &= \frac{6+1}{2} = 3.5 \\
T_{rc} &= (3.0 - 1) \cdot 1.0 + (3.5 - 1) \cdot 1.0 + 2 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 2.0 + 2.5 + 1.0 = 5.5 \text{ s/char}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup**:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{15.0}{5.5} \approx 2.73\times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion**: Row-column scan is approximately **2.73× faster** than linear scan for this 30-cell layout. The speedup grows with $N$ — for a 100-cell keyboard, the ratio is approximately **5×**. This is the engineering rationale for using row-column or block scan in all modern AAC devices.

#### Model Answer — Part B(b) — 7 marks

**Fitts' Law theoretical foundation (2 marks)**: Fitts' Law models the **movement time** required to acquire a target as a function of the target's **distance** and **width**. For an assistive keyboard, the user with cerebral palsy has reduced fine-motor accuracy, which is mathematically equivalent to a **larger effective target width** needed to maintain the same error rate. By **enlarging the physical keys**, the therapist increases $W$ and thus reduces the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**, which in turn reduces the movement time. This is the engineering justification for BigKeys and enlarged on-screen keyboards.

**Step 1 — Compute $ID_1$ for $W = 15$ mm (1 mark)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_1 &= \log_2\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{2 \times 150}{15}\right) = \log_2(20) \\
&= \frac{\ln(20)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{2.9957}{0.6931} \approx 4.322 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $MT_1$ (1 mark)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_1 &= a + b \cdot ID_1 = 0.05 + 0.20 \times 4.322 = 0.05 + 0.8644 \approx 0.914 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $ID_2$ and $MT_2$ for $W = 30$ mm (2 marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_2 &= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \times 150}{30}\right) = \log_2(10) = \frac{\ln(10)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{2.3026}{0.6931} \approx 3.322 \text{ bits} \\
MT_2 &= 0.05 + 0.20 \times 3.322 = 0.05 + 0.6644 \approx 0.714 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute percentage reduction (1 mark)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} &= \frac{MT_1 - MT_2}{MT_1} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.914 - 0.714}{0.914} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.200}{0.914} \times 100 \\
&\approx 21.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion**: Doubling the key width from 15 mm to 30 mm reduces the user's movement time per key by approximately **22%**. For a user typing 100 characters, this is a saving of ~20 seconds — a clinically significant improvement in communication speed. This is the quantitative basis on which occupational therapists prescribe **enlarged keyboards** for users with cerebral palsy and similar motor-accuracy limitations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU examiners commonly deduct marks on this topic**:
>
> 1. **Confusing "selection" and "activation"** (–1 mark) — A "selection" is the *cognitive* choice of a target; an "activation" is the *physical* press. In direct selection, both happen in one motor action. In indirect selection, the **system makes the selection** (the scan highlight) and the **user makes the activation** (the switch press). Examiners penalise answers that conflate these.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "–1" in scan time formulas** (–1 mark) — When the desired item is *currently* highlighted, the user does not wait for the next highlight. So the expected number of scan steps is $(N+1)/2 - 1 = (N-1)/2$, not simply $(N+1)/2$. Writing $T = (N+1)/2 \cdot t_{item}$ without the $-1$ loses a mark.
>
> 3. **Memorising only one scan type** (–2 marks) — Examiners frequently ask: *"What happens if linear scan is replaced by row-column scan for a 40-cell keyboard?"* You must know **all four paradigms** (linear, row-column, block, auditory) and be able to **derive the selection time for each**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the user's cognitive state in indirect selection** (–1 mark) — Indirect selection imposes a high **attentional load** (the user must continuously track the scan). It is **not appropriate for users with cognitive impairment, severe attention deficits, or visual field cuts**. Examiners expect you to mention this **contraindication**.
>
> 5. **Omitting units in Fitts' Law / Hick's Law problems** (–0.5 mark) — $ID$ is in **bits**; $MT$ is in **seconds**; $W$ and $A$ are in **metres** (or consistently in mm). Always write the unit.
>
> 6. **Recommending a BCI before exhausting simpler options** (–1 mark) — A BCI is a *last-resort* interface. Examiners penalise prescriptions that jump to a ₹10-lakh BCI when a ₹5,000 sip-and-puff switch would suffice.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — CI for Direct and Indirect Selection: Keyboards**
>
> ✅ **Core Definitions**:
> - Communication Interface (CI) = the transducer that converts user intent into digital command.
> - Direct selection = user activates target in **one** motor action.
> - Indirect selection = system **scans** choices; user **switches** to select.
>
> ✅ **Direct-Selection Keyboards (memorise the matrix)**:
> - Standard QWERTY → mild impairment.
> - BigKeys / Enlarged → low vision, paediatric, motor incoordination.
> - Membrane / Flexible → spasticity, moisture, low force.
> - Keyguard-equipped → athetosis, tremor, CP, PD.
> - Chord / One-handed (Maltron, CyKey) → hemiplegia.
> - Foot / Mouth-stick → high quadriplegia.
> - On-screen + dwell → eye-tracker, head-tracker, touchscreen AAC.
>
> ✅ **Indirect-Selection Keyboards**:
> - Single-switch scanning (auto / step): linear, row-column, block, auditory.
> - Two-switch Morse code: faster but steep learning curve.
> - BCI (P300, motor imagery): complete paralysis / locked-in.
>
> ✅ **Core Formulas (must be able to derive, not just quote)**:
> - Hick-Hyman: $T = a + b \log_2(N+1)$.
> - Fitts' Law: $ID = \log_2(2A/W)$; $MT = a + b \cdot ID$.
> - Linear scan: $T = (N-1)/2 \cdot t_{item} + t_{react}$.
> - Row-column scan: $T = (\sqrt{N_r}-1) \cdot t_{item} + (\sqrt{N_c}-1) \cdot t_{item} + 2 t_{react}$.
> - KSPC reduction from word prediction.
>
> ✅ **Decision Logic for Prescription**:
> 1. Assess residual motor, visual, cognitive function.
> 2. Try **direct selection first**; preserve direct whenever possible.
> 3. If direct fails → indirect selection, starting with the **simplest** switch and **shortest** scan.
> 4. Layer **software** (word prediction, dwell-click) on top of hardware to reduce ID and scan count.
> 5. **Progressively escalate** complexity as the disease progresses (e.g., ALS: hand → head → eye → BCI).
>
> ✅ **Contraindications to remember**:
> - Scanning keyboards require **sustained attention** → not for severe cognitive impairment.
> - BCIs require **intact cortical function** → not for advanced dementia.
> - Eye-gaze requires **stable ocular motor control** → not for uncontrolled nystagmus.
> - Foot-controlled keyboards require **lower-limb function** → not for high quadriplegia.
>
> ✅ **Key Numbers to Memorise**:
> - Information per character $H = \log_2(27) \approx 4.755$ bits.
> - 1 word = 5 characters (standard convention).
> - Typical scan rate: 1.0–1.5 s/item.
> - Typical dwell time: 0.5–1.5 s.
> - Fitts' Law constants for head-pointer: $a \approx 0.05$ s, $b \approx 0.15$–$0.20$ s/bit.
> - Standard smartphone minimum touch target: **44 × 44 pt** (Apple HIG) / **48 × 48 dp** (Material) — both derived from Fitts' Law with finger contact width.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Trade-offs** (always discuss both sides in design questions):
> - Speed ⇄ Accuracy (Fitts).
> - Number of switches ⇄ Selection time (scanning).
> - Hardware cost ⇄ Typing speed (BCI vs membrane).
> - Cognitive load ⇄ Motor effort (chord vs QWERTY).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Touch Screens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Touch Screens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Touch Screen** is an electronic visual display device that can detect and precisely locate a touch (by a finger, stylus, or specialized assistive instrument) within its display area. It functions as both an **input device** and an **output device**, allowing direct interaction with what is being displayed, rather than requiring an intermediate peripheral such as a mouse or keyboard.

In the specific context of **Assistive Medical Devices (PEBRT745)**, touch screens form the primary Human-Machine Interface (HMI) layer for patients with limited dexterity, motor impairments, communication disorders (AAC devices), wheelchair controllers, hospital bedside terminals, and rehabilitation equipment. They translate physical contact into analog/digital electrical signals that are interpreted by a controller IC to determine the **(x, y) coordinates** of the touch event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The module emphasizes *how the transduction mechanism* (Resistive, Capacitive, SAW, IR, Optical) maps a physical touch to an electrical signal, and the *engineering trade-offs* (transparency, durability, multi-touch capability) that determine its suitability in clinical/assistive environments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a piece of paper lying flat on a table. Now imagine that paper is secretly a **two-layer sandwich**, where:
- The top layer is a flexible transparent plastic coated with a thin invisible conducting film.
- The bottom layer is a rigid glass plate, also coated.
- Between them, tiny invisible spacer dots keep them apart — like microscopic ball bearings.

When you press your finger on the top layer, it bends down and **touches the bottom layer** at exactly the point under your fingertip. A tiny embedded circuit instantly measures *where* this contact happened by sending small electrical currents through the corners. The corner that is *closest* to the press gets the most current, and by comparing these currents (or voltages), the device can pinpoint the exact spot — just like identifying a location on a city map by measuring distances from the four corners.

> **In one line:** A touch screen is a *coordinate-mapping device* that turns a physical point of pressure into an (x, y) electrical signal that a microprocessor can interpret.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Assistive Contexts

| Metric | Typical Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Touch Resolution | **0.5 mm to 1.0 mm** |
| Response Time | **< 10 ms** (capacitive), **< 20 ms** (resistive) |
| Light Transmission | **75 % – 90 %** |
| Operating Force (resistive) | **0.1 N – 1.0 N** |
| Touch Accuracy | **± 1 % of active area** |
| Durability (resistive) | **~ 35 million touches** at one point |

> [!NOTE]
> **Assistive-Device Note:** For elderly patients or those with motor disorders (e.g., Parkinson's, arthritis), **resistive** or **projected capacitive with glove support** screens are preferred because they require minimal activation force and tolerate imprecise contact. For AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) devices, **PCAP (Projected Capacitive)** dominates because of multi-touch gestures and superior optical clarity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Touch Screen Technologies

Touch screens are broadly classified based on their **transduction principle** — i.e., *what physical quantity changes* when a finger touches the screen. The four dominant families (and one emerging) used in medical/assistive hardware are:

1. **Resistive (Analog Resistive)**
2. **Capacitive (Surface Capacitive & Projected Capacitive – PCAP)**
3. **Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW)**
4. **Infrared (IR) / Optical**
5. *(Emerging)* **In-Cell / On-Cell Active Matrix**

---

## 2.2 Resistive Touch Screen — Operating Principle

- **Structure:** Two transparent conductive layers (typically **Indium Tin Oxide – ITO**) — one on a flexible top PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate) film, one on a rigid bottom glass — separated by an air gap maintained by microscopic **insulator spacer dots** (~ 10 µm).
- **Transduction:** When a finger or stylus presses, the top layer deforms and contacts the bottom layer. The contact closes a circuit.
- **Position Sensing:** A controller applies a voltage gradient across one layer (e.g., horizontal) and reads the voltage at the contact through the other layer (vertical, used as a probe). It then reverses the roles for the Y-coordinate.

### Why this matters (The "Why"):
The voltage at the contact is a **linear function of position** because the ITO layer acts as a uniform resistor. The touch is essentially a **voltage divider tap**.

---

## 2.3 Capacitive Touch Screen — Operating Principle

Two sub-families:

### (a) Surface Capacitive
- A single ITO layer on the glass with electrodes at four corners.
- When a finger (which is conductive and stores charge) touches the surface, it draws a small current from each corner.
- The **current ratio** between corners gives the touch position.

### (b) Projected Capacitive (PCAP) — *dominant in modern medical devices*
- The ITO is patterned into a **grid of rows and columns**, forming an array of capacitors at the intersections.
- The controller measures the **mutual capacitance** $C_{m}$ between each row-column intersection.
- When a finger approaches, it acts as a **ground plane**, reducing the mutual capacitance at that node.
- The controller scans the entire grid (typically **120 Hz**) and reports the nodes with measurable $\Delta C_{m}$ as touch points.

### Why this matters:
PCAP supports **multi-touch** natively, has no moving parts (highly durable), supports operation through medical-grade gloves, and offers > 90 % optical clarity — making it the **default choice for hospital tablets, ventilators, infusion pumps, and AAC devices**.

---

## 2.4 Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW)

- Uses two **piezoelectric transducers** and two **reflector arrays** etched on a glass substrate.
- Transmit 5 MHz ultrasonic waves across the surface; reflectors redirect them to receiving transducers.
- A finger **absorbs** a portion of the wave energy → dip in received signal.
- Controller triangulates the dip's time-of-arrival to find position.
- **Disadvantage for medical use:** Susceptible to **liquid contaminants** (water droplets, blood, saline) — unsuitable near open surgical fields.

## 2.5 Infrared (IR) Touch Screens

- Frame of **IR LEDs** on two adjacent sides, **phototransistors/photodiodes** on the opposite sides.
- Creates a lattice of invisible IR beams across the screen.
- A finger or stylus **breaks** one or more beams, which the controller detects.
- **Advantage for medical use:** Can be made with **any non-metallic surface** (e.g., a thick protective glass for sterile fields) → common in **operating-room displays and clean-room equipment**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are critical for the 14-mark derivations and the Part A definitions in the KTU ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resistive — Voltage at touch (X-axis) | $V_{x} = V_{s} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L_{x}}$ | $V_{s}$ = supply voltage (V), $x$ = position from negative electrode (m), $L_{x}$ = active length (m) | X-coord. extraction |
| 2 | Resistive — Y-axis (after layer swap) | $V_{y} = V_{s} \cdot \dfrac{y}{L_{y}}$ | $y$ = Y-position, $L_{y}$ = active width | Y-coord. extraction |
| 3 | Mutual Capacitance (parallel-plate model) | $C_{m} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_{r}$ = relative permittivity, $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $A$ = electrode overlap area (m²), $d$ = dielectric gap (m) | PCAP node sensing |
| 4 | Capacitance change with finger | $\Delta C_{m} = C_{m} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{d_{f}}{d}}\right)$ | $d_{f}$ = effective finger-electrode distance (m) | Touch sensitivity |
| 5 | SAW wave velocity in glass | $v_{s} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho \left(1-\nu^{2}\right)}}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | Time-of-flight calc. |
| 6 | SAW position from time delay | $x = \dfrac{v_{s} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $\Delta t$ = time difference between Tx and Rx (s) | Touch coordinate |
| 7 | Touch resolution (pixels) | $R = \dfrac{L}{N}$ | $L$ = active length (m), $N$ = number of electrodes / resolvable positions | Sensor specification |
| 8 | Multi-touch matrix size | $M = R_{n} \times C_{n}$ | $R_{n}$, $C_{n}$ = rows & columns | PCAP addressing |
| 9 | IR beam pitch | $p = \dfrac{L_{IR}}{n-1}$ | $L_{IR}$ = IR frame active length, $n$ = number of beams | IR sensor design |
| 10 | Optical transmission | $T = \prod_{i} \left(1 - R_{i}\right)\left(1 - A_{i}\right)$ | $R_{i}, A_{i}$ = reflectance & absorbance of layer $i$ | Display clarity |

> **Engineering Utility:** In the **PEBRT745 (Assistive Medical Devices)** curriculum, the resistive formulas (1, 2) are most commonly tested because they directly relate to the dominant low-cost patient-side interface hardware. The PCAP formula (3) is a high-yield formula when the question shifts to "modern AAC tablets" or "smart wheelchair controllers."

---

## 2.7 Comparison Matrix (Selection for Assistive Devices)

| Property | Resistive | PCAP | SAW | IR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Activation force | Low (stylus OK) | None (finger / glove) | Low | None |
| Multi-touch | No (single) | **Yes (10+)** | Limited | Yes |
| Optical clarity | ~ 75 % | **> 90 %** | ~ 90 % | ~ 100 % |
| Liquid tolerance | High | Medium | **Poor** | High |
| Glove / stylus | **Excellent** | Good (tuned) | Stylus OK | Excellent |
| Durability | Moderate | **High** | Moderate | High |
| Cost | **Lowest** | Medium | High | High |
| Medical suitability | Patient monitors, basic AAC | Ventilators, infusion pumps, **AAC tablets** | Rare | **OR displays, sterile panels** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Position Calculation in a 4-Wire Resistive Touch Screen

A 4-wire resistive touch screen has the following electrical model when a touch is detected:

- The **top layer (Y-substrate)** is configured as a voltage divider in the **Y-direction**.
- The **bottom layer (X-substrate)** is used as a voltage *probe* to read the divided voltage.

Let the bottom layer have total resistance $R_{X}$ along the X-direction. When a touch occurs at position $x$ from the left electrode, the touch acts as a *wiper* on this resistor — equivalent to two series resistors: $R_{1}$ (from left electrode to touch) and $R_{2}$ (from touch to right electrode).

**Step 1 — Identify the two sub-resistances.**

By the property of uniform ITO, resistance is proportional to length:

$$R_{1} = R_{X} \cdot \frac{x}{L_{x}}, \quad R_{2} = R_{X} \cdot \frac{L_{x} - x}{L_{x}}$$

**Step 2 — Verify KVL around the loop (supply $V_{s}$ applied across the bottom layer).**

$$V_{s} = I \cdot \left(R_{1} + R_{2}\right) = I \cdot R_{X}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the loop current.**

$$I = \frac{V_{s}}{R_{X}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the voltage at the touch point with respect to the left (reference) electrode.**

$$V_{x} = I \cdot R_{1} = \frac{V_{s}}{R_{X}} \cdot R_{X} \cdot \frac{x}{L_{x}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the expression.**

$$\boxed{\,V_{x} = V_{s} \cdot \frac{x}{L_{x}}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Map ADC count to position.**

Let the ADC have $N$ bits and full-scale reading $V_{FS}$. The ADC count is:

$$D_{x} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{x}}{V_{FS}} \cdot \left(2^{N} - 1\right) \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{x}{L_{x}} \cdot \left(2^{N} - 1\right) \right\rfloor$$

**Step 7 — Recover the physical position.**

$$x = \frac{D_{x}}{2^{N} - 1} \cdot L_{x}$$

> **Real-world Engineering Meaning:** For a 12-bit ADC ($2^{12} = 4096$) on a 5-inch screen with $L_{x} = 0.1$ m, the smallest resolvable position step is:
> $$\Delta x = \frac{0.1}{4095} \approx 24.4 \, \mu m$$
> This is the theoretical *electrical* resolution; the *mechanical* resolution is limited by the spacer dot pitch and the contact patch size (typically 1–2 mm).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mutual Capacitance in a PCAP Cell

Consider a single PCAP node formed by two perpendicular ITO electrodes (one row, one column) separated by a thin dielectric (the substrate or an adhesive layer).

**Step 1 — Treat each intersection as a parallel-plate capacitor.**

The capacitance is:

$$C_{m} = \frac{\varepsilon_{r} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}$$

where $A$ is the electrode overlap area and $d$ is the inter-electrode dielectric thickness.

**Step 2 — Introduce the finger as a fringing-field ground.**

When a finger approaches at distance $d_{f}$ from the top electrode, the field lines that would have closed through the dielectric above now partially terminate on the conductive finger. The equivalent circuit becomes **two series capacitors** — the original $C_{m}$ and a new "finger" capacitance $C_{f}$.

$$C_{f} = \frac{\varepsilon_{r,finger} \, \varepsilon_{0} A}{d_{f}}, \quad \varepsilon_{r,finger} \approx 10 \text{ (skin/tissue model)}$$

**Step 3 — Effective capacitance with finger present.**

$$\frac{1}{C_{eff}} = \frac{1}{C_{m}} + \frac{1}{C_{f}} \implies C_{eff} = \frac{C_{m} C_{f}}{C_{m} + C_{f}}$$

**Step 4 — Express the change in capacitance.**

$$\Delta C_{m} = C_{m} - C_{eff} = C_{m} \left( 1 - \frac{C_{f}}{C_{m} + C_{f}} \right) = C_{m} \left( \frac{C_{m}}{C_{m} + C_{f}} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the plate expressions.**

$$\Delta C_{m} = C_{m} \cdot \frac{ \frac{\varepsilon_{r} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d} }{ \frac{\varepsilon_{r} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d} + \frac{\varepsilon_{r,finger} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d_{f}} } = C_{m} \cdot \frac{ d_{f} }{ d_{f} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{r}}{\varepsilon_{r,finger}} + d }$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form (commonly used in datasheets).**

$$\boxed{\,\Delta C_{m} \approx C_{m} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r}}{\varepsilon_{r,finger}} \cdot \dfrac{d}{d_{f}}}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example for KTU-style numericals.**

Let $\varepsilon_{r} = 4$ (typical polymer dielectric), $\varepsilon_{r,finger} = 10$, $d = 100 \,\mu m$, $d_{f} = 2 \, mm$.

$$\frac{\Delta C_{m}}{C_{m}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.4 \cdot \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.02} = 0.9804$$

This means **~ 98 %** of the original capacitance is lost — which is precisely why PCAP controllers are designed to detect sub-1 % changes with high SNR. In real devices, only a few-percent change is typical because the finger does not fully shield the field.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Touch Screen Selection for a Given Assistive Scenario

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a "design-reasoning" tool commonly expected in KTU 14-mark questions where the student is given an application and must *justify* the technology.

| Application | Recommended Tech. | Justification (mapped to KTU theory) |
|---|---|---|
| Bedside patient monitor (single user, gloved nurse) | **Resistive (5-wire)** or **PCAP with glove tuning** | Low cost, accepts any stylus/finger, no false triggers from saline drips |
| Ventilator / ICU touch UI | **PCAP (glass-glass, optical-bonded)** | High clarity for alarms, multi-touch for gesture unlock, can be wiped with alcohol |
| Wheelchair joystick + touchscreen | **PCAP with moisture rejection** | Multi-touch (gesture), works through thin gloves |
| Operating-room display | **IR / Optical** | No surface coating, can be made of thick sterilizable glass; immune to blood/saline |
| AAC device for cerebral palsy / ALS | **PCAP with dwell-click software** | Multi-touch for category-board navigation, optical clarity reduces eye fatigue |
| Rehabilitation game (stroke therapy) | **PCAP large-format (> 32 in)** | High refresh rate, multi-touch for bimanual exercises |
| Low-cost rural clinic glucometer | **Resistive** | Lowest cost, no calibration drift, works with stylus |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic Touch Screen Subsystem in an Assistive Device

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Touch Event\nfinger / stylus / glove] --> B[Sensor Layer\nResistive ITO / PCAP grid / SAW / IR lattice]
    B --> C[Analog Front End AFE\ncharge amplifier / voltage driver]
    C --> D[Touch Controller IC\nADC + DSP + I2C/SPI]
    D --> E[Host Processor\nMCU / MPU of the medical device]
    E --> F[Application Logic\nGUI update / therapy command / alarm]
    F --> G[Display Panel\nTFT / OLED / E-ink feedback]
    G -.visual feedback.-> A
    H[Power Management\nLDO + isolation barrier] --> C
    H --> D
    H --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The block forms a **closed loop** — the user touches, the device responds visually, and the user re-engages. This is the classic *HMI feedback loop* in PEBRT745.
- The **AFE** is the *transducer interface* — it converts the tiny sensor signal (mV / fF) into a normalized voltage that the ADC inside the controller can digitize.
- The **Touch Controller IC** handles scan timing, noise rejection, and gesture recognition before forwarding a clean report (touch ID, X, Y, pressure estimate) over **I²C or SPI** to the host.
- **Power Management** is shown twice because medical devices (per IEC 60601-1) require **galvanic isolation** between the patient-side touch sensor and the mains-connected host — a unique KTU-relevant constraint.

---

## 4.2 PCAP Scanning & Signal Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sub1[Row Driver\nscans 1 of N rows]
    sub2[Column Sense\nmeasures mutual Cm]
    sub3[Baseline Subtraction\nremoves temperature drift]
    sub4[Threshold Compare\nflags touched nodes]
    sub5[Clustering Algorithm\ngroups adjacent flags]
    sub6[Centroid Calc\nXavg, Yavg per finger]
    sub7[Report to Host\nI2C packet: ID, X, Y, size]
    sub1 --> sub2
    sub2 --> sub3
    sub3 --> sub4
    sub4 --> sub5
    sub5 --> sub6
    sub6 --> sub7
```

---

## 4.3 Resistive Layer Stack-Up (Cross-Sectional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1[Top: PET film\nflexible substrate]
    layer2[Top ITO layer\nconducting pattern]
    layer3[Spacer dots\nmicro-insulators ~10 um]
    layer4[Bottom ITO layer\nconducting pattern]
    layer5[Bottom: Rigid glass / acrylic]
    layer6[Adhesive optical clear adhesive]
    layer7[Display panel beneath]
    layer1 --> layer2 --> layer3 --> layer4 --> layer5 --> layer6 --> layer7
```

---

## 4.4 IR Touch Screen Optical-Path Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sideA[Left frame: IR LED array\nemits N vertical beams]
    sideB[Right frame: Photodiode array\nN receivers aligned to LEDs]
    sideC[Top frame: IR LED array\nemits M horizontal beams]
    sideD[Bottom frame: Photodiode array\nM receivers]
    sideA -. beam .-> sideB
    sideC -. beam .-> sideD
    note{{Controller scans all beams\nbeam break = touch coordinate}} -.- sideA
    note -.- sideC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a touch screen and list any two transduction mechanisms used in it.**

**Model Answer:**
A **touch screen** is an electronic display device that detects and locates a physical touch (finger, stylus, or assistive tool) within its display area, acting as both an input and output device.

Two transduction mechanisms:
1. **Resistive** – two conductive layers make contact under pressure, completing an electrical circuit; position is found from a voltage-divider reading.
2. **Capacitive (Projected / PCAP)** – a grid of electrodes measures the change in mutual capacitance caused by the proximity of a conductive finger.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write *"Touch screen is a display device"* without stating its *input + output* dual nature. This is a 1-mark deduction. Always mention "**input/output**" and "**location detection**" for full marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State any three advantages of a PCAP (Projected Capacitive) touch screen over a resistive one, in the context of a hospital bedside monitor.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Multi-touch support** – allows gesture-based interactions (pinch-to-zoom on imaging, two-finger confirm to avoid accidental drug-dose taps).
2. **Higher optical clarity (> 90 % vs ~ 75 %)** – critical for clinicians to read alarm colors and waveforms without backlight boost.
3. **No moving / deforming layers** – glass-glass PCAP survives **alcohol wipes, abrasive cleaning, and 35+ million touches** with negligible optical degradation.
4. **Glove compatibility (with proper tuning)** – nurses do not need to remove examination gloves before operating the monitor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing generic advantages (e.g., "more accurate") without tying them to the **medical/clinical context** leads to partial credit. Always frame the answer around the **assistive-device use case**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **4-wire resistive touch screen**. Derive the expression for the X-coordinate of a touch in terms of the applied voltage $V_s$, the ADC count $D_x$, and the active length $L_x$ for a $N$-bit ADC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare resistive and projected capacitive (PCAP) touch screens along **six** parameters relevant to medical-device design, and justify which is preferable for an **AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) tablet** used by an ALS patient. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 4-Wire Resistive Touch Screen

**Construction:**
- **Top sheet:** flexible PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate) coated on its underside with a transparent ITO (Indium Tin Oxide) layer.
- **Bottom sheet:** rigid glass coated on its topside with another ITO layer.
- **Spacer dots** (~ 10 µm tall insulator beads) are printed between the two sheets to prevent contact during non-touch state.
- **Four bus-bar electrodes:** two on the right & left of the top layer (for the Y-axis), two on the top & bottom of the bottom layer (for the X-axis).

**Working:**
1. In the *idle* state, no current flows (layers are separated by spacers).
2. The controller first applies a voltage $V_s$ across the two X-electrodes of the bottom layer, creating a uniform potential gradient along the X-direction. The top layer is **not energized** and is used purely as a probe.
3. A finger press deforms the top layer and shorts it to the bottom layer at point $(x, y)$.
4. The voltage at the contact $V_x$ is read by the top layer's electrode and digitised by the ADC. Because the top ITO is a high-impedance uniform sheet, it does not disturb the gradient.
5. The process is repeated with the roles swapped (voltage on Y-electrodes, read on X-probe) to obtain $V_y$.

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1, restated for the model answer):**

Let the total resistance of the bottom layer along X be $R_X$. The touch point divides it into:

$$R_1 = R_X \frac{x}{L_x}, \quad R_2 = R_X \frac{L_x - x}{L_x}$$

KVL gives $V_s = I R_X \Rightarrow I = V_s / R_X$. The voltage at the touch w.r.t. the left (reference) electrode is:

$$V_x = I R_1 = V_s \frac{x}{L_x}$$

The ADC with $N$ bits produces a digital count:

$$D_x = \frac{V_x}{V_{FS}} (2^N - 1) = \frac{x}{L_x} (2^N - 1)$$

Hence the recovered X-coordinate is:

$$\boxed{\,x = \frac{D_x}{2^N - 1} L_x\,}$$

**Sub-parts (a) mark split (typical KTU pattern):**
- Block diagram / construction – 2 marks
- Working steps – 2 marks
- Derivation – 3 marks

#### Model Solution (b) — Comparison & Justification

| # | Parameter | Resistive | PCAP |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Activation force | Needs ~ 0.1 – 1 N (good for stylus) | Zero (finger capacitance) |
| 2 | Multi-touch | **No** (single touch only) | **Yes** (up to 10 points) |
| 3 | Optical clarity | ~ 75 % | **> 90 %** |
| 4 | Cleaning / sterilization | Affected by harsh chemicals | Glass surface, **alcohol-safe** |
| 5 | Glove / stylus | **Excellent** | Good with tuning |
| 6 | Cost | **Lowest** | Medium |

**Justification for ALS AAC Tablet:**
- ALS patients often retain finger/eye movement but **lose fine motor control** and speech. The AAC tablet is their only voice.
- **PCAP is preferred** because:
  1. **Multi-touch / gesture support** enables "swipe-to-next-page" and "tap-and-hold-to-speak," reducing the number of precise touches.
  2. **Higher clarity** reduces eye fatigue during long communication sessions.
  3. **No moving layers** — patient saliva, food spills, or assistive head-pointer stylus do not wear out the surface.
  4. With **dwell-click software**, the patient can select a word simply by resting the cursor (or head-tracker pointer) on the cell for a threshold time — eliminating the need to physically press.

**Sub-parts (b) mark split:**
- 6-row comparison table – 3 marks
- Justification for AAC – 2 marks
- Application of PCAP to ALS – 2 marks

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Many students write the comparison but **forget to derive the formula** in part (a). The derivation carries 3 of the 7 marks — a costly omission. Also, do **not** confuse *4-wire* with *5-wire* resistive schemes; in 5-wire, both layers are used as a probe and only one layer is driven, but the formula $V_x = V_s x / L_x$ remains valid for the principle — note the topology change in the answer.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Projected Capacitive (PCAP) touch screen** with a block diagram. Derive the expression for the **change in mutual capacitance** $\Delta C_m$ when a finger approaches at a distance $d_f$ above a node whose dielectric thickness is $d$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the suitability of **IR (Infrared)** touch screens for **operating-room displays**. Compare them with SAW touch screens and explain why SAW is generally avoided in sterile surgical environments. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — PCAP Working and $\Delta C_m$ Derivation

**Working (block-flow):**
- A grid of ITO electrodes is patterned on two layers separated by a dielectric, forming a matrix of mutual capacitors $C_m$ at each row–column intersection.
- The controller sequentially drives each row with an AC excitation (~ 100 – 500 kHz) and senses the charge coupling on each column.
- A **baseline** of all node capacitances is stored at power-up and updated periodically to compensate for temperature and humidity drift.
- When a finger (or gloved finger) approaches, it **steals field lines** to ground, reducing the measured $C_m$ at the affected nodes.
- The controller compares the measured value to the baseline. A drop greater than a threshold (typically 1–5 %) flags that node as touched.
- Adjacent touched nodes are **clustered**, their **centroid** is calculated, and the (X, Y, size, touch-ID) report is sent to the host over I²C/SPI.

**Derivation of $\Delta C_m$** (see Section 3.2 for full derivation; summarised here for the model answer):

- Without finger: $C_m = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / d$
- Finger introduces series capacitance $C_f = \varepsilon_{r,f} \varepsilon_0 A / d_f$
- Combined: $C_{eff} = \dfrac{C_m C_f}{C_m + C_f}$
