```mermaid
    A[User Motor Intention] --> B[Sensor Transducer]
    B --> C[Amplifier Stage]
    C --> D[Bandpass Filter]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter]
    E --> F[Coordinate Mapping Algorithm]
    F --> G[Velocity Smoothing Filter]
    G --> H[Display Driver]
    H --> I[Screen Cursor Render]
    I --> J[Visual Feedback to User]
    J --> A
    K[Selection Switch Click Sip Puff Blink] --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The main loop flows clockwise from *User Intention* to *Screen Cursor Render* and back via *Visual Feedback* — this is the closed Fitts' Law loop. The lower branch (K) shows the *selection* signal, which is logically separate from the *position* signal but shares the same downstream mapping stage.

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Pointing Interfaces

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pointing Interfaces] --> B[Hand or Finger Operated]
    A --> C[Head Tracking]
    A --> D[Eye Tracking]
    A --> E[Brain Computer Interface]
    A --> F[Alternative Body Channel]

    B --> B1[Standard Mouse]
    B --> B2[Trackball]
    B --> B3[Joystick Isometric]
    B --> B4[Touchpad]
    B --> B5[Touchscreen]

    C --> C1[Camera Based Webcam Tracker]
    C --> C2[IR Reflective Dot Tracker]
    C --> C3[Ultrasonic Head Pointer]
    C --> C4[Gyroscope Headband]

    D --> D1[Video Oculography VOG]
    D --> D2[Electro Oculography EOG]
    D --> D3[Infrared Oculography IROG]

    E --> E1[EEG P300 Speller]
    E --> E2[SSVEP Cursor]
    E --> E3[ECoG Implant]

    F --> F1[Chin Joystick]
    F --> F2[Sip and Puff]
    F --> F3[Foot Pedal]
    F --> F4[Tongue Drive]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise the five root-level branches — *Hand, Head, Eye, Brain, Alternative*. The KTU 2024 question paper has been known to ask: *"Classify pointing interfaces used in assistive technology and give one example of each."* (7 marks)

---

## 4.3 Fitts' Law Pointing Task — Acquisition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Cursor Position] --> B[Approach Phase Open Loop]
    B --> C[Correction Phase Closed Loop]
    C --> D{Target Acquired}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Selection Click Event]
    E --> F[Reaction Time Recorded]
    F --> G[Movement Time Recorded]
    G --> H[Compute ID and Throughput]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters clinically:** The *open-loop* approach phase is ballistic (no visual correction) and reveals motor planning ability. The *closed-loop* correction phase reflects visual feedback integration. In Parkinson's disease, the open-loop phase is markedly slowed; in cerebellar ataxia, the closed-loop phase shows excessive corrections. KTU 14-mark questions sometimes ask for the clinical interpretation of these phase durations.

---

## 4.4 EOG Electrode Placement Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forehead Reference Fpz] --> B[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    C[Left Outer Canthus] --> B
    D[Right Outer Canthus] --> B
    E[Supraorbital Above Eye] --> B
    F[Infraorbital Below Eye] --> B

    B --> G[Horizontal Channel H]
    B --> H[Vertical Channel V]

    G --> I[High Pass 0.1 Hz Drift Removal]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Low Pass 30 Hz Noise Removal]
    J --> K[Coordinate Mapping]
    K --> L[Cursor Position on Screen]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Pointing Interfaces — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Pointing Interface. List any four major families of pointing interfaces used in assistive medical devices.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A pointing interface is an HCI subsystem that translates a user's voluntary motor or bioelectric signals into spatial cursor commands (x, y) on a display, enabling selection, manipulation, and navigation in a digital environment (1 mark).
Four major families are (1 mark for listing 4):
1. Hand/Finger-operated pointers (mouse, trackball, joystick, touchpad)
2. Head-tracking pointers (camera-based, IR reflective, ultrasonic)
3. Eye-tracking pointers (VOG, EOG)
4. Brain–Computer Interface pointers (EEG-based P300, SSVEP)
5. Alternative-body-channel pointers (chin joystick, sip-and-puff, tongue drive) — *(any 4)*

*(Final mark for clear, labelled listing and a relevant concluding line.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Fitts' Law and explain the significance of the Index of Difficulty (ID) in evaluating pointing devices.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fitts' Law (1 mark):
$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$
where MT is movement time, D is distance to target, W is target width, and $a, b$ are empirically determined constants.
ID = $\log_2(D/W + 1)$ is measured in **bits** and quantifies task difficulty (1 mark). It allows comparison across different device sizes, screen resolutions, and user populations by converting physical geometry into information-theoretic units. The throughput $TP = ID/MT$ (bits/s) provides a single performance number to rank pointing devices (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **working principle of a head-tracking pointing interface** used for motor-impaired users. Explain each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Index of Difficulty** from Fitts' Law and compute the **Throughput** for a user with $D = 320$ pixels, $W = 40$ pixels, and $MT = 0.9$ s. Comment on the device's clinical acceptability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 marks)

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
```
User Head Motion → Camera Sensor → Image Pre-processing
   → Feature Detection (nose tip / IR marker) → Coordinate Extraction
   → Affine Mapping → Smoothing Filter → Cursor Display
   → Visual Feedback → User
```

**Block-by-block explanation (4 marks, ~½ mark each):**
1. **Camera Sensor:** A CMOS/CCD camera (USB webcam or head-mounted) captures frames at 30–60 fps. For better contrast, an **IR camera with an IR LED illuminator** is often used, with a small reflective dot stuck on the user's forehead.
2. **Image Pre-processing:** Convert to grayscale, apply histogram equalisation or gamma correction, and reduce noise with a Gaussian filter (kernel size 5×5 typical).
3. **Feature Detection:** Algorithm locates the nose tip or the IR dot. Classical methods use **Haar-cascade classifiers** or **template matching**; modern systems use **CNN-based facial landmark detectors** (e.g., dlib's 68-point model).
4. **Coordinate Extraction:** The detected feature point $(u, v)$ in image coordinates is fed forward.
5. **Affine Mapping:** The image coordinates are converted to screen coordinates using the calibrated scale factors $s_x, s_y$ and offset $(u_0, v_0)$.
6. **Smoothing Filter:** A low-pass IIR filter with $\tau \approx 80$ ms removes high-frequency jitter caused by involuntary head tremor and sensor noise.
7. **Selection Trigger:** A separate switch (often a sip-and-puff or blink detector) is used to fire the click event.

> **Closing sentence (1 mark):** A head-tracking pointing interface enables hands-free computer use, dramatically improving autonomy for users with high-level spinal cord injuries (C3–C5) or advanced ALS.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**
- Define target geometry: $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width.
- Effective width using ISO 9241-9 convention: $W_e = 4.133 \sigma_x$.
- Number of distinguishable target positions along approach axis:

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W_e} + 1\right) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

- Fitts' Law in linear form: $MT = a + b \cdot ID$.
- Throughput: $TP = ID/MT$ (bits/s).

**Numerical computation (2 marks):**
$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{320}{40} + 1\right) = \log_2(9) = 3.170\ \text{bits}
$$
$$
TP = \frac{3.170}{0.9} = 3.522\ \text{bits/s}
$$

**Clinical comment (1 mark):** A standard able-bodied mouse user achieves **4–6 bits/s**. A head-tracker user at **3.52 bits/s** falls in the *mild-to-moderate impairment* range and is clinically acceptable for productive computer work, especially when paired with a **Dwell-click** feature.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of electro-oculography (EOG)**. Describe the electrode placement, signal characteristics, and derive the **mapping from EOG voltage to cursor coordinates**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the following pointing interfaces in a tabular form: **Standard Mouse, Head-Tracker, Eye-Tracker (EOG-based), and Sip-and-Puff**. Compare across the parameters of required motor ability, accuracy, speed, fatigue, and clinical indication. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):** The human eyeball acts as an electric dipole — the cornea is **electropositive** (~+0.4 to +1.0 mV) relative to the retina, which is **electronegative**. When the eye rotates, this dipole orientation changes, and surface electrodes placed around the orbit detect a voltage proportional to the eye-rotation angle. This is the **corneo-retinal standing potential (CRSP)**.

**Electrode placement (2 marks):** Standard 5-electrode bipolar montage:
- Two horizontal electrodes at the **outer canthi** (left and right temples) — measure horizontal eye motion.
- Two vertical electrodes, one **supraorbital** (above the eye) and one **infraorbital** (below the eye) — measure vertical eye motion.
- One **reference electrode** on the forehead (Fpz position per the 10–20 EEG system).
- Skin is cleaned with alcohol; conductive gel applied; impedance kept below **5 kΩ**.

**Signal characteristics (1 mark):** Amplitude 0.05–1.0 mV, frequency content 0.1–30 Hz, prone to baseline drift, blink artefacts (large spikes ~1 mV for ~200 ms), and EMG contamination from facial muscles.

**Derivation of voltage-to-cursor mapping (2 marks):**
$$
\theta_H = \frac{V_H}{k_H}, \quad \theta_V = \frac{V_V}{k_V}
$$
$$
x = \frac{R_x}{2} + \frac{V_H}{k_H \cdot \Theta_{H,max}} \cdot R_x, \quad
y = \frac{R_y}{2} - \frac{V_V}{k_V \cdot \Theta_{V,max}} \cdot R_y
$$
A high-pass filter (cut-off 0.1 Hz) removes drift; a low-pass filter (30 Hz) removes EMG noise.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) (7 marks)

**Comparison table (7 marks — 1 mark per cell filled correctly, 1 mark for overall structure):**

| Parameter | Standard Mouse | Head-Tracker (Camera) | EOG Eye-Tracker | Sip-and-Puff |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Required motor ability** | Full hand and arm control | Active neck/head control | Active eye muscle control | Oral/facial muscle control |
| **Accuracy (typical TP)** | 4–6 bits/s | 2–4 bits/s | 1.5–3 bits/s | 0.5–1.5 bits/s |
| **Speed (MT for 100 px)** | 0.3–0.6 s | 0.7–1.5 s | 0.9–1.8 s | 1.5–3.0 s |
| **Fatigue** | Low | Moderate–high (neck strain) | Low–moderate (dry eyes) | Low |
| **Clinical indication** | Mild impairment, arthritis | High SCI, ALS, CP | Locked-in syndrome, advanced ALS | High SCI, ventilator users |
| **Special hardware** | Standard USB mouse | Webcam + IR dot | EOG electrodes + amplifier | Pressure transducer + tube |
| **Cost (approx.)** | ₹300–1,500 | ₹3,000–25,000 | ₹15,000–80,000 | ₹2,000–10,000 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Writing "E" without units:** Fitts' Law ID must be in **bits**; MT in **seconds**; TP in **bits/s**. Examiners specifically look for these units.
> 2. **Confusing EOG with EEG:** EOG measures eye motion via the corneo-retinal dipole (DC-like, mV range). EEG measures cortical activity (AC, µV range). This is a frequently-tested 2-mark distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the +1 in Fitts' Law:** Omitting the +1 in $\log_2(D/W + 1)$ is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Skipping the calibration step:** Head-trackers and EOG systems *must* be calibrated for each user session. Examiners deduct marks if the answer jumps from sensor output straight to cursor position without a calibration paragraph.
> 5. **Not mentioning Dwell-Click:** Whenever you describe a pointing interface for severely motor-impaired users, mention that the **Click** is implemented via *Dwell* (cursor held still ~1 s) or *blink detection* (for EOG) — not via a physical button.
> 6. **Using | symbol in tables:** Many students try to use absolute-value bars like $|V_H|$ inside a markdown table. This **breaks the table**. Use $\vert V_H \vert$ or $\mid V_H \mid$ instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullet List — Read Twice Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- [x] **Definition:** Pointing interface = subsystem that maps user signal → (x, y) cursor coordinates.
- [x] **Three universal tasks:** Acquire, Track, Select — mnemonic **"ATS"**.
- [x] **Five families:** Hand, Head, Eye, Brain, Alternative-body — mnemonic **"HHEBA"**.
- [x] **Six stages of operation:** Intention → Sensor → Conditioning → Mapping → Rendering → Feedback.
- [x] **A-F-A in signal conditioning:** Amplify → Filter → ADC.
- [x] **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ — note the **+1** and the **base 2**.
- [x] **ID** is in **bits**; **MT** in **seconds**; **TP** in **bits/s**.
- [x] **Typical TP:** Standard mouse 4–6 bps; head-tracker 2–4 bps; EOG 1.5–3 bps; sip-and-puff 0.5–1.5 bps.
- [x] **EOG** uses corneo-retinal standing potential (~0.4–1.0 mV); **EEG** uses cortical activity (~10–100 µV).
- [x] **EOG electrode montage:** Outer canthi (H), supra/infra-orbital (V), forehead reference.
- [x] **Drift removal in EOG:** High-pass filter at **0.1 Hz**.
- [x] **Nyquist theorem:** Sample at least **2×** the highest signal frequency.
- [x] **SNR** is measured in **dB**; bioelectric amplifiers target SNR > 40 dB.
- [x] **Dwell-click** substitutes the physical button for users who cannot click — hold cursor still for ~1 s.
- [x] **Head-tracker block chain:** Camera → Image processing → Feature detection → Affine map → Smoothing → Display.
- [x] **Affine map:** $x = s_x(u - u_0) + x_0$, $y = s_y(v - v_0) + y_0$.
- [x] **Smoothing time-constant:** Typical $\tau \approx 80$ ms for head-trackers.
- [x] **Fitts' Law clinical use:** Higher $a$ → slower initiation (Parkinsonian); higher $b$ → slower per-bit execution (spasticity).
- [x] **WCAG 2.1 / Indian RPwD Act 2016** mandate accessible pointing interfaces in public ICT systems.
- [x] **Mandatory mention in any 14-mark answer:** Calibration, drift removal, and selection method (Dwell/blink/physical switch).
- [x] **Units matter — always state them.** No bare numbers in a KTU answer script.
- [x] **Never use `|` in markdown tables** — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value.

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — POINTING INTERFACES**
> *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme, PEBRT745 — Assistive Medical Devices, Outcome-Based Education (NEP 2020) framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switches** in the context of assistive medical devices are defined as *binary, electromechanical, pneumatic, optical, or bioelectric transducers* that translate a voluntary physical, physiological, or neurological action of a user (who has limited motor, speech, or cognitive capability) into a discrete on/off electrical signal that can be processed by an external assistive system such as a wheelchair controller, a communication aid, a robotic manipulator, or an environmental control unit (ECU).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Course PEBRT745 – Module 2), switches form the **fundamental input layer** of nearly every assistive technology interface. They are the lowest-level human-machine bridge, replacing conventional input devices (keyboards, joysticks, mice) that require fine motor control. The system performance, accessibility, and quality of life outcome for the end-user is heavily dependent on the **correct selection, mounting, and calibration of the switch**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are inside a dark room and there is exactly one light switch on the wall. You can either *press it (ON)* to turn the bulb on, or *release it (OFF)* to turn the bulb off. There is no in-between, no "halfway," no "dimming" – the light bulb is either fully lit or fully dark.
>
> An assistive switch behaves **identically**. The "bulb" in an assistive device could be a wheelchair motor, a voice synthesizer, or a television. The user's physical action (a blink, a sip of air, a muscle twitch, a finger tap) is the "finger pressing the wall switch." Because the action is binary, even a user with severe paralysis can produce a usable control signal.

### 1.3 Operational Importance in Assistive Technology

Switches are *not* just simple electrical contacts in this discipline. They must be carefully engineered to:
- Require the **minimum possible physical effort (low activation force)** to operate.
- Provide reliable **haptic / auditory / visual feedback** so the user knows the activation was registered.
- Be physically mountable on **wheelchairs, trays, headrests, or bed frames** at ergonomically reachable positions.
- Have a long operational lifetime (typically > **1,000,000 actuation cycles**) to withstand repeated daily use.

### 1.4 Classification of Switches (High-Level Overview)

Switches used in assistive medical devices are broadly classified into the following families:

| # | Family | Activation Medium | Typical User Profile |
|---|--------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Mechanical (Lever / Push / Button) | Physical force from a body part | Users with residual hand/arm movement |
| 2 | Pneumatic (Sip & Puff) | Air pressure via mouth | Quadriplegics, high-level SCI |
| 3 | Optical / Photoelectric | Beam interruption (finger, hand) | Users with limited hand mobility |
| 4 | Proximity / Capacitive | No contact – body part near sensor | Severe motor limitations |
| 5 | Bioelectric (EMG / EOG / EEG) | Muscle/eye/brain electrical signals | Locked-in, ALS, advanced MD |
| 6 | Mechanical-Adjustable (Graded) | Variable force / position | Users with tremor or weakness |
| 7 | Membrane / Touch | Capacitive touch or pressure pad | Users with gross motor ability |
| 8 | Tilt / Mercury / Accelerometer | Orientation of body part | Head-movement control users |

### 1.5 Key Performance Metrics for Assistive Switches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The selection of a switch in clinical practice depends on quantifiable performance metrics, summarized below:
>
> - **Activation Force (Fₐ):** The minimum force (measured in **grams** or **Newtons**) required to actuate the switch. For users with severe weakness, Fₐ should be ≤ **25 g** (≈ 0.245 N).
> - **Activation Travel (d):** The distance the switch surface must move before contact closure, measured in **mm** or **inches**.
> - **Feedback Modality:** Tactile, auditory, or visual cue confirming activation.
> - **Mounting Compatibility:** Standard mounting plates, Velcro, gooseneck, or custom brackets.
> - **Operating Environment:** Resistance to moisture, dust, saliva, and disinfectants.
> - **Connector Standard:** Typically 3.5 mm mono jack or **9-pin D-sub** for clinical devices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Switch as a Two-State System

Mathematically, every assistive switch is modeled as a **binary state machine**. The output $S(t)$ at time $t$ is:

$$S(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if switch is ACTIVE (pressed)} \\ 0, & \text{if switch is INACTIVE (released)} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A single switch provides only **1 bit of information per unit time**. To perform meaningful control (e.g., navigating a wheelchair through 4 directions), switches are combined into arrays called **switch interfaces** or **multi-switch encoders**.

### 2.2 Switch Operation Logic Step-by-Step

1. **Sensing Layer** – Detects a physical, pneumatic, optical, or bioelectric stimulus.
2. **Transduction Layer** – Converts the stimulus into an electrical quantity (resistance change, voltage, current, or capacitance change).
3. **Thresholding Layer** – Compares the electrical quantity against a reference value to produce a clean binary decision.
4. **Debounce Layer** – Removes mechanical or signal noise that could cause false triggers.
5. **Output Driver Layer** – Produces a standardized logic-level signal (typically **TTL: 0 V / 5 V** or **CMOS: 0 V / 3.3 V**).
6. **Feedback Layer** – Returns an indication to the user (click sound, LED flash, vibration).

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Switch Adaptation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard commercial switches (e.g., wall light switches) typically require Fₐ > **200 g** and a travel of several millimeters. A person with **spinal cord injury at C4-C5 level**, **cerebral palsy**, or **muscular dystrophy** cannot generate such force or precise motion. Assistive switches are therefore *engineered down* in activation requirements (sometimes to < **5 g**) and *engineered up* in terms of feedback and reliability.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles all analytical expressions relevant to switch design and selection. **No vertical pipes `|` are used inside the table cells** – mathematical bars are replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Equation / Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|--------|------|-------|
| 1 | Binary Output | $S(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ | $S$ | — | Discrete on/off state |
| 2 | Activation Force | $F_a$ | $F_a$ | **g or N** | Min. force to actuate; 1 g ≈ 9.81 mN |
| 3 | Activation Travel | $d$ | $d$ | **mm / in** | Distance before closure |
| 4 | Actuation Energy | $E_a = F_a \cdot d$ | $E_a$ | **mJ** | Energy user must supply |
| 5 | Switch Response Time | $t_r$ | $t_r$ | **ms** | Time to register a state change |
| 6 | Bounce Time | $t_b$ | $t_b$ | **ms** | Mechanical contact chatter |
| 7 | Scanning Rate | $f_s = 1 / T_s$ | $f_s$ | **Hz** | Speed at which interface cycles switches |
| 8 | Effective Throughput | $R = f_s \cdot \log_2 N$ | $R$ | **bits/s** | Info rate with $N$ switches |
| 9 | Force Conversion | $F(\text{N}) = m(\text{kg}) \cdot g$ | $F$ | **N** | $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ |
| 10 | Reliability | $MTBF = N_{cycles} / f_{op}$ | $MTBF$ | **hours** | Mean Time Between Failures |

### 2.5 Single-Switch Scanning (Detailed Logic)

When a user can operate **only one switch**, the system uses a technique called **scanning** to deliver multi-option control using a single input.

Let $N$ = number of options to choose from, $T_s$ = scan cycle time (seconds), $t_d$ = dwell time required to confirm selection (seconds).

$$T_s = N \cdot t_{slot}$$

$$P_{sel} = \frac{t_d}{t_{slot}}$$

> Here $P_{sel}$ is the *probability of a correct, voluntary selection* during the appropriate slot. The total **information transfer rate** in bits/second using a single switch with scanning is:

$$R_{scan} = \frac{\log_2 N}{T_s} = \frac{\log_2 N}{N \cdot t_{slot}}$$

**Engineering Utility:** This formula is critical when designing AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) devices and wheelchair controllers. A smaller $t_{slot}$ (faster scanning) improves throughput but increases user fatigue and accidental selections. Typical KTU-recommended values: $t_{slot} = 0.5$ to $1.5$ s.

### 2.6 Bioelectric Switch Signal Model

For EMG/EOG/EEG-based switches, the bioelectric potential $V_b(t)$ is amplified, filtered, and thresholded:

$$V_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \vert V_b(t) \vert \geq V_{th} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $V_b(t)$ = amplified bioelectric signal (typically **μV to mV** range).
- $V_{th}$ = user-adjustable threshold voltage.
- The **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** must be ≥ **10 dB** for reliable switch operation.

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}} \right)$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Single-Switch Throughput Maximization

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A speech-generating AAC device has $N = 26$ letters (English alphabet). A single switch with scanning is used. The dwell time required for selection is $t_d = 0.8$ s. Find the optimal scan slot time $t_{slot}$ that maximizes throughput, assuming $t_{slot} \geq t_d$ for safety.

**Step 1 – Write the throughput equation:**

The total scan cycle time for $N$ options is:

$$T_s = N \cdot t_{slot}$$

**Step 2 – Express information content per selection:**

Each letter corresponds to $\log_2 26$ bits of information:

$$I = \log_2 26 \approx 4.700 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 – Write the throughput (selections per second times bits per selection):**

$$R_{scan} = \frac{I}{T_s} = \frac{\log_2 N}{N \cdot t_{slot}}$$

**Step 4 – Identify the design variable and constraint:**

The variable is $t_{slot}$. The constraint is $t_{slot} \geq t_d = 0.8$ s.

**Step 5 – Minimize the scan cycle to maximize throughput:**

The expression $\frac{\log_2 N}{N \cdot t_{slot}}$ is maximized when $t_{slot}$ is minimized. Hence, the optimal value is the boundary value:

$$t_{slot}^{*} = t_d = 0.8 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 – Compute the maximum throughput:**

$$R_{scan}^{max} = \frac{\log_2 26}{26 \cdot 0.8}$$

$$R_{scan}^{max} = \frac{4.700}{20.8} \approx 0.226 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 7 – Convert to characters per minute for human readability:**

$$C_{min} = R_{scan}^{max} \cdot 60 = 0.226 \cdot 60 \approx 13.56 \text{ characters/min}$$

This explains *why* single-switch typing on AAC devices is slow – the user can realistically produce only about **5-15 characters per minute**, even under ideal clinical conditions.

### 3.2 Derivation: Activation Energy Budget for a Lever Switch

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A pediatric assistive lever switch has $F_a = 25$ g and an activation travel $d = 3$ mm. Calculate the activation energy the child must produce per switch press.

**Step 1 – Convert mass to force:**

$$F_a = 25 \text{ g} \times 9.81 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/g} = 0.24525 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 – Convert travel to meters:**

$$d = 3 \text{ mm} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 – Compute activation energy using work-energy principle:**

$$E_a = F_a \cdot d$$

$$E_a = 0.24525 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$E_a = 7.3575 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.73575 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 4 – Convert to more intuitive units:**

$$E_a \approx 0.736 \text{ mJ per actuation}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the energy of a small insect's wing flap. It is well within the capability of users with conditions like **muscular dystrophy** or **mild cerebral palsy**, illustrating why low-force switches are clinically essential.

### 3.3 Derivation: EMG Switch Threshold Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The surface EMG signal from the frontalis (forehead) muscle of a user with ALS has an RMS amplitude of $V_{signal} = 120 \text{ μV}$ when voluntarily contracted, and a noise floor of $V_{noise} = 12 \text{ μV}$. Design a threshold voltage $V_{th}$ that minimizes false triggers while remaining reachable.

**Step 1 – Compute the SNR:**

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{120}{12} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(10) = 20 \text{ dB}$$

This is an excellent SNR (≥ 10 dB), so a clean threshold can be chosen.

**Step 2 – Choose threshold between noise and signal peaks:**

A safe design rule is to set the threshold at **2× to 3× the noise RMS**, but well below the signal RMS:

$$V_{th} = 2.5 \cdot V_{noise} = 2.5 \cdot 12 = 30 \text{ μV}$$

**Step 3 – Verify the margin:**

$$M = \frac{V_{signal}}{V_{th}} = \frac{120}{30} = 4$$

A 4× margin is industry-standard for clinical reliability.

**Step 4 – Comment on safety:**

This design ensures that involuntary muscle twitches (which stay near the noise level) will *never* trigger the switch, but any intentional contraction will *always* trigger it.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Switch Selection for Clinical Conditions

| User Condition | Recommended Switch Type | Justification |
|----------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| High-level SCI (C2-C4) | Sip-and-puff pneumatic | Mouth retained function |
| Locked-in Syndrome (LIS) | EOG / EEG / p300 BCI switch | Only eye movement or brain signals available |
| Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) | EMG or proximity switch | Progressive weakness → non-contact switches preferred |
| Cerebral Palsy (CP) – spastic | Large-target lever with high guard | Accommodates uncontrolled motions |
| Muscular Dystrophy (MD) | Low-force membrane / touch switch | Minimal force, fatigue-resistant |
| Parkinson's Disease | Tilt / accelerometer switch | Utilizes tremor as input feature |
| Stroke Hemiplegia | One-handed mechanical on contralateral side | Re-trains motor pathways |
| Intellectual Disability | Large colorful tactile switch with strong feedback | Simplifies learning curve |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Block Diagram of an Assistive Switch System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeU[USER PHYSIOLOGICAL ACTION] --> nodeS1[SENSOR LAYER]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2[TRANSDUCTION AMPLIFIER]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[FILTER AND RECTIFIER]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[THRESHOLD COMPARATOR]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[DEBOUNCE AND LATCH]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[OUTPUT DRIVER TTL CMOS]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[HOST DEVICE ECU WHEELCHAIR AAC]
    nodeS6 --> nodeFB[FEEDBACK MODULE LED BUZZER VIBRATION]
    nodeFB -.-> nodeU
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree – Choosing the Right Switch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START PATIENT ASSESSMENT] --> nodeB[CAN USER PRODUCE RELIABLE MOTOR FORCE GREATER THAN 25 GRAMS]
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC[USE MECHANICAL LEVER OR PUSH BUTTON SWITCH]
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeD[CAN USER CONTROL MOUTH BREATHING SIP PUFF]
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE[USE PNEUMATIC SIP AND PUFF SWITCH]
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeF[CAN USER GENERATE GROSS BODY MOVEMENT HEAD FOOT]
    nodeF -- YES --> nodeG[USE TILT OR ACCELEROMETER SWITCH]
    nodeF -- NO --> nodeH[CAN USER PRODUCE MUSCLE CONTRACTION EMG]
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeI[USE BIOELECTRIC EMG SWITCH]
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeJ[CAN USER CONTROL EYE MOVEMENT EOG]
    nodeJ -- YES --> nodeK[USE EYE MOVEMENT EOG SWITCH]
    nodeJ -- NO --> nodeL[USE BRAIN COMPUTER INTERFACE P300 EEG SWITCH]
```

### 4.3 Single-Switch Scanning Timing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[TIME 0 SECONDS] --> nodeB[OPTION A HIGHLIGHTED]
    nodeB --> nodeC[TIME 1 SEC OPTION B HIGHLIGHTED]
    nodeC --> nodeD[TIME 2 SEC OPTION C HIGHLIGHTED]
    nodeD --> nodeE[TIME 3 SEC OPTION D HIGHLIGHTED]
    nodeE --> nodeF[USER PRESSES SWITCH ON OPTION C]
    nodeF --> nodeG[OPTION C EXECUTED FEEDBACK GIVEN]
    nodeG --> nodeH[SCAN RESTARTS]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Functional View of a Sip-and-Puff Switch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeM[MOUTH INTERFACE MOUTHPIECE] --> nodeT[TUBE PRESSURE LINE]
    nodeT --> nodeDP[DIAPHRAGM PRESSURE SENSOR]
    nodeDP --> nodeA1[AMPLIFIER STAGE]
    nodeA1 --> nodeTH[THRESHOLD DETECTOR POSITIVE FOR PUFF NEGATIVE FOR SIP]
    nodeTH --> nodeMIC[MCU DIGITAL LOGIC]
    nodeMIC --> nodeO[OUTPUT TWO CHANNELS SUCK SIGNAL AND BLOW SIGNAL]
```

### 4.5 EMG Switch Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeE1[SURFACE ELECTRODES ON MUSCLE] --> nodeE2[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER GAIN 1000]
    nodeE2 --> nodeE3[BANDPASS FILTER 20 HZ TO 500 HZ]
    nodeE3 --> nodeE4[RECTIFIER AND SMOOTHING ENVELOPE]
    nodeE4 --> nodeE5[LEVEL COMPARATOR WITH ADJUSTABLE THRESHOLD]
    nodeE5 --> nodeE6[TTL OUTPUT TO HOST DEVICE]
```

### 4.6 Force–Travel Characteristic Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX[TRAVEL DISTANCE d MM] --> nodeY[SWITCH FORCE F GRAMS]
    nodeY1[ORIGIN 0 0] --> nodeY2[LINEAR PRELOAD REGION SLOPE k]
    nodeY2 --> nodeY3[OPERATING POINT F_a d_a CLOSURE]
    nodeY3 --> nodeY4[POST TRAVEL BEYOND CLOSURE]
    nodeY4 --> nodeY5[OVERTRAVEL LIMIT]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an assistive switch. List any four desirable characteristics of a switch used in assistive technology.*

**Model Answer:**

An assistive switch is a binary input transducer that converts a voluntary physiological action of a user with disability into an electrical on/off signal for an external device.

Four desirable characteristics:
1. **Low activation force** (≤ 25 g preferred) to accommodate weak users.
2. **Positive feedback** (audible click, LED, or vibration) confirming activation.
3. **Mounting flexibility** (Velcro, gooseneck, clamp) for various body positions.
4. **High reliability** with operational life > 1 million actuations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the working principle of a sip-and-puff switch. Mention two clinical conditions where it is prescribed.*

**Model Answer:**

A sip-and-puff switch uses a mouthpiece connected to a pressure-sensitive diaphragm. The user *sips* (creates negative pressure → triggers SUCK channel) or *puffs* (creates positive pressure → triggers BLOW channel) to generate two independent binary control signals.

Clinical conditions:
1. High-level spinal cord injury (C2–C4) where hand function is absent.
2. Advanced amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) with limb paralysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Essay Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **signal chain of an EMG-based assistive switch**. Discuss the role of each stage in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **information throughput equation for a single-switch scanning interface** with $N$ options. Compute the throughput in characters per minute for $N = 26$ and slot time $t_{slot} = 1$ second. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EMG Switch Signal Chain – Block Diagram Explanation:**

1. **Surface Electrodes Stage:** Three Ag/AgCl electrodes are placed on the chosen muscle (e.g., frontalis, biceps). They pick up differential bioelectric signals in the range of **50 μV to 5 mV** along with significant 50 Hz mains interference.
2. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** A high-CMRR (> **100 dB**) instrumentation amplifier boosts the differential signal by a factor of **1000** while rejecting common-mode noise. The CMRR is critical because EMG signals are much smaller than the 50 Hz interference.
3. **Bandpass Filter (20 Hz – 500 Hz):** Removes low-frequency motion artifacts (caused by electrode cable movement) and high-frequency RF noise, isolating the useful EMG bandwidth.
4. **Rectifier and Envelope Detector:** Full-wave rectification followed by a low-pass smoothing filter extracts the *envelope* (RMS-like) of the muscle activity, which slowly rises and falls with voluntary contraction.
5. **Level Comparator with Adjustable Threshold:** Compares the envelope to a user-calibrated threshold voltage $V_{th}$. If the envelope exceeds $V_{th}$, output is HIGH (1); otherwise LOW (0).
6. **Output Driver (TTL/CMOS):** Produces a clean logic-level signal for the host device.
7. **Haptic/Visual Feedback Module:** A small buzzer or LED confirms the switch has been activated, allowing the user to learn to control the threshold.

**(b) Throughput Derivation:**

**Step 1 – Information per selection:**

$$I = \log_2 N = \log_2 26 \approx 4.700 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 – Total cycle time:**

$$T_s = N \cdot t_{slot} = 26 \cdot 1 = 26 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 – Throughput in bits/second:**

$$R = \frac{I}{T_s} = \frac{4.700}{26} \approx 0.181 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 4 – Convert to characters per minute:**

$$C_{min} = R \cdot 60 = 0.181 \cdot 60 \approx 10.85 \text{ characters/min}$$

**Conclusion:** A single-switch typing system for the English alphabet using a 1-second slot time yields only about **11 characters per minute**, which is significantly slower than the typical able-bodied typing speed of 200–400 characters/minute. This is a key motivator for using **multi-switch interfaces** when possible.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Classify the different **types of switches used in assistive medical devices**, giving one clinical example for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pediatric patient with cerebral palsy can press a switch with a force of only **15 g** over a travel of **2 mm**. Calculate the **activation energy per switch press** in millijoules. Comment on whether this is within the operational budget of low-force switches. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Assistive Switches:**

1. **Mechanical Switches** – Lever, push button, plate switch. *Example:* Plate switch mounted on a wheelchair tray for users with residual hand function.
2. **Pneumatic Switches** – Sip-and-puff. *Example:* Prescribed for high-level SCI (C2–C4) users to control a powered wheelchair.
3. **Optical / Photoelectric Switches** – Beam-interrupt type. *Example:* Used by users with limited reach who can wave a hand through a beam.
4. **Bioelectric Switches** – EMG, EOG, EEG. *Example:* EMG switch from frontalis muscle for ALS patients.
5. **Tilt / Accelerometer Switches** – Mercury tilt or MEMS accelerometer. *Example:* Head-tilt switch for users with neck mobility.
6. **Proximity / Capacitive Switches** – No-contact activation. *Example:* Used in sterile hospital environments where touch is undesirable.
7. **Membrane / Touch Switches** – Flat pressure-sensitive pads. *Example:* Low-force touch switch for pediatric cerebral palsy therapy.
8. **Mechanical-Adjustable / Variable-Force Switches** – User can tune the activation threshold. *Example:* Adaptive switch for users with fluctuating muscle strength.

**(b) Activation Energy Calculation:**

**Step 1 – Convert mass to force:**

$$F_a = 15 \text{ g} \times 9.81 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/g} = 0.14715 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 – Convert travel to meters:**

$$d = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 – Apply work equation:**

$$E_a = F_a \cdot d$$

$$E_a = 0.14715 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 2.943 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 – Convert to millijoules:**

$$E_a = 0.2943 \text{ mJ} \approx 0.29 \text{ mJ per actuation}$$

**Comment:** This value (0.29 mJ) is well below the typical activation energy budget of **1–2 mJ** for low-force clinical switches. Therefore, the patient can comfortably operate a standard low-force pediatric switch, and an even more sensitive switch could be selected if the user fatigues quickly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse switch activation force with operating force** – many datasheets quote the *operating* (post-pre-travel) force, not the initial *activation* force. Use the activation force $F_a$ for clinical selection.
> 2. **Do NOT forget the base of the logarithm** in throughput equations – KTU strictly expects base-2 logarithm ($\log_2$) when computing information content in bits.
> 3. **Do NOT ignore the debounce time** in step 4 of the signal chain – KTU examiners frequently deduct 1 mark if the debounce/latch stage is omitted in the block diagram.
> 4. **Sip-and-puff is a TWO-channel switch**, not a single switch. A common mistake is to call it a single switch; it actually provides TWO binary outputs (SUCK and BLOW).
> 5. **Mounting context is essential** – never recommend a switch without specifying how it is to be mounted (tray, headrest, bedrail, wheelchair arm).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Switch Definition:** Binary transducer converting voluntary physiological action into on/off electrical signal.
- **Core Performance Metrics:** Activation force $F_a$ (g/N), travel $d$ (mm), activation energy $E_a = F_a \cdot d$ (mJ), response time $t_r$ (ms), and reliability MTBF (hours).
- **Single-Switch Throughput:** $R = \log_2 N / (N \cdot t_{slot})$ bits per second; reaches **10–15 characters/min** for English alphabet.
- **Signal Chain Order:** Sensor → Amplifier → Filter → Rectifier → Comparator → Debounce → Output Driver → Feedback.
- **Major Switch Families:** Mechanical, Pneumatic (sip-and-puff), Optical, Bioelectric (EMG/EOG/EEG), Tilt/Accelerometer, Proximity, Membrane/Touch, Variable-force.
- **Key Clinical Mapping:** High-level SCI → sip-and-puff; LIS/ALS → EOG/EMG/BCI; Cerebral palsy → low-force mechanical/membrane; Parkinson's → tilt/accelerometer; Stroke hemiplegia → one-handed mechanical.
- **Feedback is mandatory** for all assistive switches – tactile, auditory, or visual confirmation is a clinical necessity, not a luxury.
- **EMG threshold rule:** $V_{th} \approx 2.5 \times V_{noise}$, with $\text{SNR} \geq 10$ dB for clinical reliability.
- **Bounce and debounce:** Mechanical switches exhibit $t_b$ of a few ms; software/hardware debounce is essential before the host device.
- **Standard connectors:** 3.5 mm mono jack for consumer devices; 9-pin D-sub for clinical/research devices.
- **Examination Tip:** Always state the *user condition* before recommending a switch – KTU examiners expect clinical reasoning, not just technical recall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switch Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switch Arrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Switch Arrays

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Switch Array** is a structured, organized collection of multiple discrete switching elements arranged in a geometric configuration (typically a row–column matrix) that enables an individual with severe motor impairment to access, control, and communicate with electronic assistive devices through deliberate activation of one or more switches.

In the context of **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)** and computer access technology, a switch array functions as the primary **Human–Machine Interface (HMI)** layer, translating the user's residual motor capability (which may be limited to a single muscle group) into a rich set of control commands via sequential scanning or encoding strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A switch array is the *physical layer* of any access method. The same switch hardware can drive letter selection, wheelchair navigation, or Environmental Control Unit (ECU) commands — only the *software mapping* changes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The TV Remote Made Smart

Imagine a television remote that originally had 50 separate buttons (one for each channel). For an elderly person with severe arthritis, pressing any of those 50 small buttons is impossible. A **switch array** solves this exactly the way modern TV remotes did:

| Old TV Remote | Switch Array Analogy |
|---|---|
| 50 small direct buttons | 1 (or 2) large accessible switch(es) |
| Direct selection | Indirect **scanning** (highlighting one option at a time) |
| User must hit correct button | User only hits one switch when the *correct option* is highlighted |
| Fast for able-bodied | Universally usable for any motor ability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** The switch array trades *speed of selection* for *universality of access*. A single switch + scanning array can mimic the function of hundreds of physical buttons, at the cost of time.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following industry-standard metrics govern switch array design and are explicitly expected in KTU answers:

- **Activation Force:** typically **0.2 N – 1.5 N** (for users with limited strength, e.g., muscular dystrophy)
- **Activation Travel / Throw:** typically **0.5 mm – 5 mm**
- **Scan Rate:** **0.5 Hz – 5 Hz** (typical clinical default is **1 Hz**)
- **Debounce Time:** **5 ms – 30 ms** (mechanical switch debouncing)
- **Standard Array Sizes:** 4×4 (16 cells), 5×5 (25 cells), 6×6 (36 cells), 8×8 (64 cells for full QWERTY-style access)

> [!TIP]
> **Why these numbers?** They are derived from clinical trials with Cerebral Palsy, ALS, and Spinal Cord Injury populations. Anything requiring more than **1.5 N** of force is unusable by approximately 40% of the target population.

## 1.4 Classification of Switch Arrays (KTU Expectation)

```
Switch Arrays
├── By Physical Layout
│   ├── 1-D Linear Array
│   ├── 2-D Matrix Array (row × column)
│   └── 3-D Volumetric Array
├── By Scanning Strategy
│   ├── Automatic / Linear Scan
│   ├── Row-Column Scan
│   ├── Directed Scan
│   └── Group / Block Scan
├── By Encoding Strategy
│   ├── Binary (Morse) Encoding
│   ├── Huffman Encoding
│   └── T-9 / Multi-tap Encoding
└── By User Output
    ├── Direct Selection Arrays
    └── Scanned Selection Arrays
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of a Switch Array

Every switch-array–based access system executes a deterministic six-step pipeline. KTU questions frequently test a student's ability to draw and explain this pipeline.

1. **Switch Activation (Input Layer):** A physical sensor (mechanical, pneumatic, optical, or bioelectric) detects a deliberate user action. The signal is a clean digital edge — `0 V → 3.3 V` (or `5 V`).
2. **Debounce Filtering:** Mechanical contacts produce multiple spurious edges for 5–30 ms. A debounce circuit or software filter (low-pass + Schmitt trigger) produces a *single clean pulse*.
3. **Scan Position Tracking:** A counter (row/column index) maintains the currently-highlighted cell of the array.
4. **Decision Logic:** When the switch is pressed at the *correct* scan position, the system commits the selection (e.g., a letter, a direction, a command).
5. **Output Mapping:** The committed selection is mapped to a downstream action — typing a character, moving a wheelchair, sending a CAN-bus command to an ECU.
6. **Feedback Generation:** Auditory (beep), visual (LED), or tactile (vibration) feedback confirms the selection and resets the scan.

> [!NOTE]
> The "Why" of Step 2: Without debouncing, a single intentional press could register as 3–5 selections — corrupting the user's intent. This is one of the most common KTU short-answer questions.

## 2.2 Scanning Algorithms — The Heart of the Switch Array

### 2.2.1 Linear (Sequential) Scanning

A single highlight moves through every cell in a fixed order: cell 1 → cell 2 → cell 3 → ... → cell N → cell 1 → ...

- **Time to reach the *k*-th cell:** $t_k = (k-1) \cdot t_{\text{step}}$
- **Mean access time (uniform distribution assumption):** $\bar{t} = \frac{N+1}{2} \cdot t_{\text{step}}$

### 2.2.2 Row-Column Scanning (The Standard)

The scan first highlights *rows* sequentially. When the user presses the switch on the *correct row*, the scan then moves *column-by-column* within that row until the user presses again. This halves the effective search space per decision.

- **Mean selections per item:** $\sqrt{N}$ (for an N-cell array) — a massive improvement over linear.
- **Number of switches required:** 1 (single-switch access) or 2 (one for row, one for column — *directed* row-column).

### 2.2.3 Block / Group Scanning

Items are clustered into groups. Scan highlights *groups* first, then *items within the chosen group*. This is mathematically identical to row-column but optimized for letter-frequency distributions (like Huffman coding).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Switch Arrays

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim. KTU board questions frequently combine 2–3 of these formulas in a single 7-mark sub-question.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = n \times m$ | Total array cells = rows × columns | cells | Array sizing |
| 2 | $\bar{t}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{N+1}{2} \cdot t_{\text{step}}$ | Mean access time, linear scan | s | Linear scan design |
| 3 | $\bar{t}_{\text{row-col}} \approx \sqrt{N} \cdot t_{\text{step}}$ | Mean access time, row-column scan | s | Row-column scan design |
| 4 | $R = \frac{\log_2 M}{T}$ | Effective information transfer rate | bits/s | AAC throughput |
| 5 | $M = 2^n$ | Number of unique items from *n* binary codes | items | Morse / binary encoding |
| 6 | $T = N \cdot t_{\text{step}} \cdot p$ | Total scan time, weighted by probability | s | Huffman weighted scan |
| 7 | $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Information entropy (Shannon) | bits/char | Optimal coding lower bound |
| 8 | $\eta = \frac{\bar{t}_{\text{linear}}}{\bar{t}_{\text{row-col}}} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2}$ | Speed-up factor of row-column over linear | dimensionless | Algorithm comparison |
| 9 | $F_{\text{act}} \le 1.5$ | Maximum activation force (clinical limit) | N | Switch selection |
| 10 | $f_{\text{scan}} = \frac{1}{t_{\text{step}}}$ | Scan frequency | Hz | Scan-rate design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation safeguard:** All absolute values (e.g., $\vert t \vert$) inside the table above have been written with `\vert` to prevent markdown-table syntax corruption. If a question uses $\vert x \vert$, render it as $\vert x \vert$ in your answer script.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Switch arrays are not abstract theory — they are the **physical access point** in:

- **Eye-tracked + single-switch fallback AAC devices** (e.g., PRC-Saltillo, Tobii Dynavox with switch scanning)
- **Powered-wheelchair controllers** for users without hand function (sip-and-puff switches on a 2×2 array → 4 directional commands)
- **Environmental Control Units (ECUs)** for smart-home access from a bed-bound patient
- **Computer access for Locked-In Syndrome (LIS) patients** — one switch + row-column scan = full text entry
- **Surgical robotics & rehabilitation exoskeletons** — safety-critical enable switches arranged in a deliberate array

The unifying engineering principle: **a switch array is a *bandwidth-translation* device** — it converts a low-bandwidth residual motor channel into a high-bandwidth control stream via *temporal multiplexing* (scanning) and/or *temporal encoding* (Morse, Huffman).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean Access Time for Linear Scanning

**Problem Setup:** Consider an $N$-cell linear switch array scanned sequentially at a fixed step interval $t_{\text{step}}$. The user is equally likely to want any cell, so the target cell index $k$ is uniform on $\{1, 2, \dots, N\}$. Compute the mean time to make a selection.

**Step 1 — Define the access time for the *k*-th cell:**

The highlight starts at cell 1 and increments by 1 every $t_{\text{step}}$ seconds. To reach cell $k$, exactly $(k - 1)$ steps must elapse.

$$
t_k = (k - 1) \cdot t_{\text{step}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the expected (mean) access time as a discrete expectation:**

$$
\bar{t} = \mathbb{E}[t_k] = \sum_{k=1}^{N} t_k \cdot P(k)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the uniform probability $P(k) = \frac{1}{N}$:**

$$
\bar{t} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} (k - 1) \cdot t_{\text{step}} \cdot \frac{1}{N} = \frac{t_{\text{step}}}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} (k - 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the arithmetic sum using $\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} j = \frac{(N-1)N}{2}$:**

$$
\bar{t} = \frac{t_{\text{step}}}{N} \cdot \frac{(N-1)N}{2} = \frac{(N-1) \, t_{\text{step}}}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — For large $N$ (the practical case), this is approximated as:**

$$
\bar{t} \approx \frac{N \, t_{\text{step}}}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{t}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{N \, t_{\text{step}}}{2}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Halving $N$ or halving $t_{\text{step}}$ both linearly halve the mean access time. The dominant engineering lever is **reducing $N$ through hierarchical (row-column) scanning** — which is exactly the next derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Access Time for Row-Column Scanning

**Problem Setup:** An $N = n \times m$ array is scanned row-by-row. The user first waits for the *correct row* to be highlighted, then waits for the *correct column* within that row. Compute the mean total access time assuming uniform cell probability.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean number of row steps to selection:**

There are $n$ rows. By the same logic as §3.1, the mean row step is $\frac{n}{2}$. Each row step takes $t_{\text{step}}$ seconds.

$$
\bar{t}_{\text{row}} = \frac{n \, t_{\text{step}}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean number of column steps within the chosen row:**

There are $m$ columns per row. Mean column step is $\frac{m}{2}$.

$$
\bar{t}_{\text{col}} = \frac{m \, t_{\text{step}}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the two phases (they are sequential, not parallel):**

$$
\bar{t}_{\text{row-col}} = \bar{t}_{\text{row}} + \bar{t}_{\text{col}} = \frac{n \, t_{\text{step}}}{2} + \frac{m \, t_{\text{step}}}{2} = \frac{(n + m) \, t_{\text{step}}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — For the optimal square array $n = m = \sqrt{N}$:**

$$
\bar{t}_{\text{row-col}} = \frac{(\sqrt{N} + \sqrt{N}) \, t_{\text{step}}}{2} = \sqrt{N} \, t_{\text{step}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{t}_{\text{row-col}} = \sqrt{N} \, t_{\text{step}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the speed-up factor over linear scan:**

$$
\eta = \frac{\bar{t}_{\text{linear}}}{\bar{t}_{\text{row-col}}} = \frac{N / 2}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2}
$$

**Numerical example for $N = 36$ (6×6 array):**

$$
\eta = \frac{\sqrt{36}}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3
$$

A row-column scan is **3× faster** than a linear scan for a 36-cell array. This is a frequent KTU numerical.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Information Transfer Rate of a Switch Array

**Problem Setup:** Compute the effective throughput (bits per second) of a switch-array–based communication system. This is the key AAC metric.

**Step 1 — Total number of equiprobable messages the array can produce:** $M = N$ cells.

**Step 2 — Bits per message, assuming all cells equally likely (worst case):**

$$
I_{\text{msg}} = \log_2 M = \log_2 N \quad \text{[bits/selection]}
$$

**Step 3 — Time per selection (mean, row-column scan):**

$$
T = \bar{t}_{\text{row-col}} = \sqrt{N} \, t_{\text{step}} \quad \text{[s/selection]}
$$

**Step 4 — Throughput:**

$$
R = \frac{I_{\text{msg}}}{T} = \frac{\log_2 N}{\sqrt{N} \, t_{\text{step}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical check for a 32-cell array (typical AAC page) at $t_{\text{step}} = 0.5$ s:**

$$
R = \frac{\log_2 32}{\sqrt{32} \times 0.5} = \frac{5}{5.657 \times 0.5} = \frac{5}{2.828} \approx 1.77 \text{ bits/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{\log_2 N}{\sqrt{N} \, t_{\text{step}}}}
$$

**Engineering insight:** To raise $R$, the most powerful lever is **reducing $t_{\text{step}}$** (faster scanning) — but only up to the user's cognitive reaction time (~200 ms). Beyond that, increasing $N$ *hurts* throughput (denominator grows faster than numerator). This is why AAC vocabularies are partitioned into *pages* of ~30–50 items, not one giant page of 1000.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Huffman-Weighted Scan Time for Letter Selection

**Problem Setup:** A row-column array is used to type English letters. Letter frequencies are non-uniform (E, T, A, O are common; Q, X, Z are rare). Find the *optimal* array layout that minimizes mean access time.

**Step 1 — Shannon entropy of the English letter distribution (per character):**

$$
H = -\sum_{i=1}^{26} p_i \log_2 p_i \approx 4.14 \text{ bits/char}
$$

**Step 2 — Expected access time, weighted by letter probability $p_i$, where each letter lives in a row-column position with mean access time $t_i$:**

$$
\bar{T}_{\text{weighted}} = \sum_{i=1}^{26} p_i \cdot t_i
$$

**Step 3 — For uniform row-column placement (all $t_i = \sqrt{N} \, t_{\text{step}}$):**

$$
\bar{T}_{\text{uniform}} = \sqrt{N} \, t_{\text{step}}
$$

**Step 4 — For an optimally arranged array (high-frequency letters in *low-access-time* positions — e.g., top-left corner, accessed in $\approx 1$ step):**

$$
\bar{T}_{\text{huffman}} = t_{\text{step}} \sum_{i=1}^{26} p_i \cdot \sqrt{r_i}
$$

where $r_i$ is the effective position rank of letter $i$ (1 = top-left, $N$ = bottom-right).

**Step 5 — Typical clinical gain:** $\bar{T}_{\text{huffman}} \approx 0.55 \cdot \bar{T}_{\text{uniform}}$ — i.e., a **~45% speed-up** by intelligent layout.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 scheme values this derivation highly — it integrates **probability, information theory, and ergonomics** in a single answer. A 7-mark question on Huffman array layout is a board-exam favourite.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Metric | Formula | Best-Case Value | Worst-Case Value | Engineering Lever |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Array capacity | $N = n \cdot m$ | Application-defined | Cognitive load bound | Partition into pages |
| Linear scan time | $\bar{t} = N t_{\text{step}} / 2$ | $\approx N t_{\text{step}} / 2$ | $N t_{\text{step}}$ | Switch to row-column |
| Row-column scan time | $\bar{t} = \sqrt{N} \, t_{\text{step}}$ | $\sqrt{N} t_{\text{step}}$ | $\sqrt{N} t_{\text{step}}$ | Make array square |
| Throughput | $R = \log_2 N / (\sqrt{N} t_{\text{step}})$ | Maximize | Minimize | Tune $t_{\text{step}}$ to user RT |
| Coding efficiency | $\eta = H_{\text{used}} / H_{\text{max}}$ | 1.0 | 0 | Use Huffman layout |
| Force requirement | $F \le 1.5$ N | 0.2 N | 1.5 N | Match switch to user |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Switch Array Access System

This is the canonical "block-level functional architecture" of a switch-array–based AAC / ECU / wheelchair-access system. It is a high-yield KTU diagram — draw it for any 14-mark design question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Intent - Residual Motor Capability] --> B[Switch Sensor - Mechanical / Pneumatic / Optical]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning - Schmitt Trigger + Debounce]
    C --> D[Microcontroller Scan Engine - Row Column Counter]
    D --> E{Selection Made}
    E -- No --> F[Continue Scan - Next Cell Highlighted]
    F --> D
    E -- Yes --> G[Output Mapper - Character / Direction / ECU Command]
    G --> H[Actuator Layer - Display / Motor / Relay]
    H --> I[Feedback - Audio Beep / LED / Vibration]
    I --> J[Reset Scan - Return to Start]
    J --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in your answer script:** Trace the signal from the *User Intent* node on the left, through *Signal Conditioning* (where debounce happens — a frequent sub-question), into the *Scan Engine* (the heart of the system), out through the *Output Mapper* to the actuator, and back via *Feedback*. The closed loop `D → F → D` represents the scanning cycle; the open loop `E → G → H → I → J → D` represents a committed selection.

## 4.2 Row-Column Scanning Sequence (Step-Wise Schematic)

The following diagram illustrates the *two-phase* decision process of row-column scanning for a 3×3 array (cells labelled 1–9). This is a *very* common KTU diagram to draw.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Row Scan - Highlight Row 1] --> Q1{Switch Pressed}
    Q1 -- No --> P2[Phase 1: Highlight Row 2]
    P2 --> Q2{Switch Pressed}
    Q2 -- No --> P3[Phase 1: Highlight Row 3]
    P3 --> Q3{Switch Pressed}
    Q3 -- No --> P1
    Q1 -- Yes --> C1[Phase 2: Column Scan within Row 1]
    C1 --> D1{Column Pressed}
    D1 -- Yes --> SEL1[Selected - Cell 1 or 2 or 3]
    D1 -- No --> SEL1
    Q2 -- Yes --> C2[Phase 2: Column Scan within Row 2]
    C2 --> SEL2[Selected - Cell 4 or 5 or 6]
    Q3 -- Yes --> C3[Phase 2: Column Scan within Row 3]
    C3 --> SEL3[Selected - Cell 7 or 8 or 9]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** When drawing this for KTU, explicitly label the *two phases* and the *branching decision diamonds*. A diagram without decision labels loses 1–2 marks.

## 4.3 Switch Array Hardware Topology

The following mermaid diagram shows the *physical* electrical topology of a switch array — rows driven by a driver, columns read by a scanner. This is the matrix-multiplexed hardware foundation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROWS [Row Drivers]
        R1[Row Driver 1]
        R2[Row Driver 2]
        R3[Row Driver 3]
        R4[Row Driver N]
    end
    subgraph MATRIX [Switch Matrix N x M]
        S11[Switch 1,1] --- S12[Switch 1,2] --- S13[Switch 1,M]
        S21[Switch 2,1] --- S22[Switch 2,2] --- S23[Switch 2,M]
        SN1[Switch N,1] --- SN2[Switch N,2] --- SN3[Switch N,M]
    end
    subgraph COLS [Column Scanners]
        C1[Column Scan 1]
        C2[Column Scan 2]
        C3[Column Scan M]
    end
    R1 --> S11
    R2 --> S21
    R3 --> S31
    R4 --> SN1
    S11 --> C1
    S12 --> C2
    S13 --> C3
    SN3 --> C3
```

**Working principle (textual explanation, since physical schematic is complex):**
The microcontroller activates **one row at a time** (sinks it low). It then **reads all column lines**. Any column that reads low indicates a *closed switch* at the intersection. This is a classic *row-column matrix scan* — it uses $N + M$ GPIO pins to read $N \times M$ switches, a $2 \times$ pin saving over direct wiring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner note:** This matrix-scan trick is the *exact* same principle used in a standard 4×4 keypad. KTU may ask: *"How would you extend a 4×4 keypad concept to a 16-switch assistive array?"* — Answer: by reusing the matrix-scan firmware, you get 16 switches with only 8 GPIO pins.

## 4.4 Encoding Strategy Flowchart — Morse vs. Huffman vs. T-9

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[User Activates Switch] --> Q1{Encoding Strategy}
    Q1 -- Two Switches --> M[Morse Encoding: Dot-Dash Timing]
    Q1 -- One Switch --> Q2{Layout Type}
    Q2 -- Frequency-Optimized --> H[Huffman / Frequency-Ordered Layout]
    Q2 -- Equal-Weight --> R[Row-Column Scan]
    Q2 -- Multi-Tap --> T9[T-9 / Multi-tap Encoding]
    M --> OUT1[Translates to ASCII Character]
    H --> OUT1
    R --> OUT1
    T9 --> OUT1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering trade-off:** Morse encoding is the *fastest* for expert users (~15–25 wpm) but the *steepest* learning curve. Row-column with frequency layout is the *most accessible* for a new user (~3–8 wpm). T-9 is a *middle ground* but requires language-model disambiguation. A good KTU answer mentions the trade-off explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a switch array used in assistive technology. List any two clinical requirements that govern its design.**

**Model Answer:**

A *switch array* is a structured collection of multiple discrete switching elements arranged in a row-column matrix that allows a user with severe motor impairment to access electronic assistive devices (AAC systems, wheelchairs, ECUs) through one or more switches combined with scanning or encoding strategies.

Two clinical requirements:

1. **Low activation force** — typically $\le 1.5$ N (and ideally $\le 0.5$ N), because users with conditions such as muscular dystrophy, ALS, or advanced cerebral palsy cannot exert higher forces reliably.
2. **Short activation travel** — typically 0.5 mm to 5 mm, because large displacements require joint range-of-motion that the user may not possess.

*(A third acceptable point: debounce time of 5–30 ms, or scan rate of 0.5–5 Hz matched to user reaction time.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the difference between linear scanning and row-column scanning in a switch array? State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Linear Scanning | Row-Column Scanning |
|---|---|---|
| Highlight order | Cell 1, 2, 3, ... N, 1, 2, ... | First all rows, then columns within chosen row |
| Mean access time | $\bar{t} = N \cdot t_{\text{step}} / 2$ | $\bar{t} = \sqrt{N} \cdot t_{\text{step}}$ (square array) |
| Switches required | 1 | 1 (automatic) or 2 (directed) |
| User cognitive load | Low | Moderate |
| Speed | Slow | Faster (by factor $\sqrt{N} / 2$) |

**Advantage of linear:** simplest firmware; ideal for users with severe cognitive impairment.

**Advantage of row-column:** scales as $\sqrt{N}$ rather than $N$, so for a 36-cell array it is ~3× faster.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Question A and Question B below are designed to be *independent alternatives* — a student answers one of the two.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a switch-array–based Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) system. Label every block and describe the role of the debounce and feedback stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 5×5 switch array scanned using the row-column strategy at a step time of 0.5 s, compute:
- (i) The total number of selectable items. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The mean access time per selection. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The information transfer rate in bits/second, assuming all cells are equiprobable. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The functional architecture of a switch-array–based AAC system consists of the following blocks (refer to §4.1 diagram for the visual):

1. **User / Residual Motor Source** — the patient's available voluntary motion (e.g., finger, toe, head, sip-and-puff, blink).
2. **Switch Sensor** — converts physical intent to an electrical edge (mechanical, pneumatic, optical, or EMG-based).
3. **Signal Conditioning (Debounce + Schmitt Trigger)** — mechanical switches produce multiple spurious edges over 5–30 ms. A Schmitt trigger with hysteresis + RC low-pass filter or a software debounce counter produces a *single clean digital pulse*, preventing false multi-selections.
4. **Microcontroller Scan Engine** — implements the row-column algorithm: drives one row low at a time, reads column lines, advances the highlight at fixed $t_{\text{step}}$ (e.g., 0.5 s).
5. **Decision Logic** — when the clean switch press coincides with the *correct* scan position, the system commits the selection.
6. **Output Mapper** — maps the committed cell to an action (ASCII character, wheelchair direction, ECU command, etc.).
7. **Actuator Layer** — the device that physically executes the action (LCD / speaker / motor driver / relay).
8. **Feedback Stage** — produces an audio beep, LED flash, or vibration confirming the selection, and resets the scan.
9. **Power Supply** — typically 3.3 V or 5 V regulated DC, often battery-backed.

**Role of Debounce:** Mechanical switches exhibit *contact bounce* — the contacts make/break multiple times in the first 5–30 ms. Without debouncing, a single intentional press could register as 3–5 selections, corrupting the user's intent. A debounce filter (hardware RC + Schmitt, or software counter with 20 ms window) ensures *one selection per intent*.

**Role of Feedback:** Without feedback, the user cannot confirm *whether* their press was registered or *which* cell was selected. Auditory + visual + tactile feedback is essential for users with sensory impairments. It also *resets* the scan, signalling the start of the next selection cycle.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Total selectable items:**

$$
N = n \times m = 5 \times 5 = 25 \text{ cells}
$$

**(ii) Mean access time (row-column scan, square array):**

$$
\bar{t}_{\text{row-col}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot t_{\text{step}} = \sqrt{25} \times 0.5 \text{ s} = 5 \times 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Information transfer rate:**

Bits per selection (equiprobable):

$$
I = \log_2 N = \log_2 25 \approx 4.644 \text{ bits/selection}
$$

Throughput:

$$
R = \frac{I}{\bar{t}} = \frac{4.644}{2.5} \approx 1.858 \text{ bits/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{R \approx 1.86 \text{ bits/s}}
$$

**Optional comparison:** A linear scan would take $\bar{t}_{\text{linear}} = 25 \times 0.5 / 2 = 6.25$ s — a **2.5× slowdown**. This numerical comparison is a *bonus mark* opportunity in KTU valuation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain three switch-encoding strategies (Morse, Huffman, and T-9/multi-tap) used with switch arrays. Compare their learning curves, typing speed, and physical-switch requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the layout of a 4×4 switch array for a powered-wheelchair controller with single-switch row-column access. Specify which cells map to which commands, justify the placement using the principle of minimization of mean access time, and compute the worst-case access time at a scan rate of 1 Hz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Morse (Two-Switch Binary) Encoding**

- **Operation:** Two switches represent *dot* (short press) and *dash* (long press). Combinations encode ASCII.
- **Speed:** Highest expert speed (15–25 wpm).
- **Learning curve:** Steepest — user must memorize codebook.
- **Switches:** 2.
- **Best for:** Highly motivated users with intact cognition (e.g., late-stage ALS).

**2. Huffman / Frequency-Ordered Row-Column Layout**

- **Operation:** Letters are placed in the array positions ordered by *English letter frequency* — E, T, A, O, I, N at top-left (reached in 1 scan step), Z, Q, X, J at bottom-right (reached in $\sqrt{N}$ steps).
- **Speed:** Moderate (8–15 wpm) but improves with practice.
- **Learning curve:** Lowest — user just selects the letter they want.
- **Switches:** 1 (or 2 for directed).
- **Best for:** Most clinical users; the *default* in modern AAC.

**3. T-9 / Multi-Tap Encoding**

- **Operation:** A single switch + timer. E.g., press once = A, twice quickly = B, three times = C, pause = commit. Cells group letters of the alphabet.
- **Speed:** Moderate-low (5–10 wpm) with a language model; lower without.
- **Learning curve:** Moderate — user must learn the group-to-letter mapping.
- **Switches:** 1.
- **Best for:** Users transitioning from phone-style text entry, or those with no cognitive impairment.

**Comparison Table (expected in KTU answer):**

| Strategy | Switches | Expert Speed | Learning Curve | Cognitive Load |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Morse | 2 | 15–25 wpm | Steep | High (memorize) |
| Huffman row-col | 1–2 | 8–15 wpm | Shallow | Low |
| T-9 / Multi-tap | 1 | 5–10 wpm | Moderate | Moderate |

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Cell-mapping for a 4×4 powered-wheelchair controller (single-switch row-column):**

The wheelchair needs 8 commands: Forward, Reverse, Left, Right, Speed-Up, Speed-Down, Stop (Emergency), and a *Menu* (lights, horn, etc.). A 4×4 array provides 16 cells — sufficient.

**Optimal layout (high-frequency commands in top-left, accessed in 1 step):**

| Cell \ Row | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Forward (FF) | Right (RT) | Stop (ST) | Speed-Down (SD) |
| **2** | Left (LT) | Reverse (RV) | Speed-Up (SU) | Menu (MN) |
| **3** | (Spare) | (Spare) | (Spare) | (Spare) |
| **4** | (Spare) | (Spare) | (Spare) | (Spare) |

**Justification by minimization of mean access time:**

- The **most-frequently used** commands (Forward, Right, Left, Stop) are placed in **row 1**, which is reached in a *single row-step* (≈ 0.5 s at 1 Hz scan).
- **Speed-control and Menu** are placed in **row 2**, accessed in 2 row-steps (≈ 1.0 s).
- **Spares** (future expansion, e.g., tilt, recline) are in **rows 3–4**, low-access-time cost.
- This minimizes the *expected* access time $\bar{T} = \sum p_i \cdot t_i$, which is the same Huffman principle as in §3.4.

**Worst-case access time** (selection of bottom-right cell at 1 Hz scan):

The worst case is row 4, column 4. Row-scan phase requires 4 row-steps to reach row 4; column-scan phase requires 4 column-steps to reach column 4.

$$
t_{\text{worst}} = (4 + 4) \cdot t_{\text{step}} = 8 \cdot 1.0 \text{ s} = 8.0 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{worst}} = 8 \text{ s}}
$$

**Safety-critical comment (KTU examiner values this):** The **Stop** command *must* be accessible in **1 step** (top-left) — a wheelchair that takes 8 seconds to stop is a hazard. This is why row 1, column 3 is allocated to STOP in the layout above.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes that cost marks in switch-array questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing scan *time* with scan *rate*.** A student writes "scan rate is 0.5 s" — it is scan *time per step* that is 0.5 s; the *rate* is 2 Hz. KTU deducts 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the √N factor** in row-column access time. Writing $\bar{t} = N \cdot t_{\text{step}}$ for row-column is *wrong* — that is the linear-scan formula. KTU deducts 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing array cells with characters.** A 25-cell array does *not* type 25 different letters at maximum efficiency — it is the *scan algorithm* that determines throughput, not the cell count alone.
> 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers. Marks are reserved for units (s, bits/s, N, Hz). A correct number without units gets 0 marks for the sub-part.
> 5. **Not drawing a labeled block diagram.** In any 7-mark design question, a block diagram is *expected* — without it, expect a 2-mark cap on the answer.
> 6. **Forgetting safety-critical placement.** A wheelchair-controller design that places STOP at the *worst-accessed* cell loses 2 marks immediately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- [x] **Switch array** = structured set of switches in a row-column matrix, used to translate residual motor ability into device-control commands.
- [x] **Clinical force limit:** $F_{\text{act}} \le 1.5$ N (often $\le 0.5$ N preferred).
- [x] **Clinical scan rate:** $f_{\text{scan}} = 0.5$–$5$ Hz (typical 1 Hz).
- [x] **Debounce time:** 5–30 ms — required to suppress mechanical-switch contact bounce.
- [x] **Linear scan mean access time:** $\bar{t}_{\text{linear}} = N \cdot t_{\text{step}} / 2$.
- [x] **Row-column scan mean access time (square array):** $\bar{t}_{\text{row-col}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot t_{\text{step}}$.
- [x] **Speed-up factor of row-column over linear:** $\eta = \sqrt{N} / 2$ — equals **3 for a 36-cell array**, **4 for a 64-cell array**.
- [x] **Information transfer rate:** $R = \log_2 N / (\sqrt{N} \cdot t_{\text{step}})$ bits/s.
- [x] **Three encoding strategies** (must know all three): Morse (fastest, 2 switches), Huffman row-col (most accessible, 1–2 switches), T-9 / multi-tap (middle ground, 1 switch).
- [x] **Huffman principle:** high-frequency letters → low-access-time cells (top-left). Improves mean access time by ~45% over uniform layout.
- [x] **Matrix-scan hardware trick:** $N + M$ GPIO pins read $N \times M$ switches — same as 4×4 keypad.
- [x] **Safety rule:** Stop / emergency commands in single-switch row-column designs must occupy **row 1, column 1** (1-step access).
- [x] **Block diagram must contain:** User → Switch → Debounce → Scan Engine → Decision → Output Mapper → Actuator → Feedback.
- [x] **Always include units** in numerical answers; **always label** diagrams; **always justify** safety-critical placements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Joysticks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete Joysticks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Discrete Joystick** (also called a *digital* or *switch-based* joystick) is a manually operated, multi-directional input device that generates a **finite set of distinct digital output states** corresponding to specific angular displacements of its shaft. Unlike its analog counterpart, which produces a continuously variable voltage proportional to deflection angle, a discrete joystick transitions between clearly defined, quantized logic levels (typically **0 V = LOW = '0'** and **+5 V or +3.3 V = HIGH = '1'**).

In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices**, discrete joysticks are the primary human-machine interface (HMI) for individuals with severe motor impairments, enabling control over powered wheelchairs, environmental control units (ECUs), and robotic manipulators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Conceptual Mapping):**
> A discrete joystick is an electromechanical positioning device in which the output is a set of **discontinuous binary states** representing discrete directional commands (e.g., Forward, Reverse, Left, Right, or combinations thereof), implemented through micro-switches, Hall-effect sensors with Schmitt triggers, or optical interrupter arrays.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the directional pad (D-pad) on a classic 1990s video game controller, or the indicator stalk in a car. When you push it, it does not glide smoothly. Instead, it "clicks" into a specific position, and a switch inside makes contact. There is no in-between position. That is the essence of a **discrete joystick**—it speaks the language of **YES/NO** rather than **"how much"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a residential staircase (discrete) versus a ramp (continuous/analog). You can stand only on specific steps (discrete states) on a staircase, whereas a ramp allows you to stop at any fractional point along its length. A discrete joystick behaves like the staircase; an analog (proportional) joystick behaves like the ramp.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following industry-standard parameters govern discrete joystick design and must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **Operating Voltage (V_CC):** typically **5 V DC** (TTL) or **3.3 V DC** (CMOS).
- **Logical HIGH (V_IH):** ≥ 2.0 V for TTL, ≥ 2.0 V for 5 V CMOS.
- **Logical LOW (V_IL):** ≤ 0.8 V for TTL, ≤ 1.5 V for 5 V CMOS.
- **Number of Discrete States (N):** For an n-direction joystick, **N = 2n** (including the neutral/center state).
- **Switch Bounce Time (t_b):** Mechanical micro-switches exhibit **5 ms to 20 ms** of contact bounce.
- **Standard Deflection Angle:** ±30° to ±45° from the vertical neutral position.
- **Actuation Force (F_act):** typically **1.5 N to 3.0 N** for assistive applications (low-force variants for users with muscular dystrophy or arthritis).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A standard 4-switch discrete joystick can resolve **9 unique states** (1 center + 4 cardinal + 4 diagonal directions), forming a 4-bit digital word at its output port.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The discrete joystick subsystem is composed of **five functional blocks**, working in a strict sequence:

1. **Mechanical Shaft & Gimbal Mount:** A central shaft is held in the neutral position by a spring-return mechanism (typically a conical or helical compression spring). The user deflects the shaft; the spring restores it to center when released.
2. **Directional Encoding Element:** For each principal direction (N, E, S, W), a dedicated actuator (cam, pusher, or magnet) is mechanically linked to the shaft.
3. **Switching Transducer Array:** Each actuator drives an independent electrical switch. The transducer may be:
   - **Electromechanical Micro-switch (Reed switch / Snap-action switch)**
   - **Hall-effect sensor + Schmitt trigger** (magnetic, non-contacting)
   - **Optical interrupter (IR LED + phototransistor pair)**
