4. **Debounce & Conditioning Circuit:** Eliminates spurious transitions caused by mechanical contact bounce, usually via an RC low-pass filter feeding a Schmitt trigger (e.g., **74HC14** hex inverter).
5. **Encoding / Multiplexing Logic:** Combines the parallel switch signals into a structured digital word (binary, BCD, or Gray code) readable by a microcontroller or motor controller.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How" Behind the Working Principle

**WHY is a discrete joystick preferred over an analog one in certain assistive applications?**

> For users with **spasticity, tremor, or limited fine motor control**, analog joysticks are problematic because involuntary hand vibrations translate into erratic analog signals, causing wheelchair drift. A discrete joystick requires a deliberate push past a **mechanical detent force (F_det)** to register a command, effectively filtering out tremors and accidental inputs.

**HOW does it generate multiple directions from simple switches?**

A standard 4-micro-switch assembly is arranged at 90° intervals (North, East, South, West). When the shaft is deflected:

- A pure **North** push activates only the N switch.
- A **Northeast** push (≈45° deflection) activates **both** the N and E switches simultaneously, which the logic interprets as the NE composite command.

This **binary superposition** allows 2⁴ = 16 possible switch combinations, of which 9 are valid joystick states (1 center, 4 cardinal, 4 diagonal, plus typically 6 invalid/unused states that are masked by the controller firmware).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units / Boundary Conditions** | **Engineering Utility** |
|-------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------|
| Number of valid states | $N_{states} = 4n + 1$ where $n$ = number of cardinal directions | Dimensionless integer; for n=4 → **N = 17** raw, but only 9 valid | Defines controller firmware table size |
| Switch combinations | $C = 2^m$ where $m$ = number of switches | $m=4 \Rightarrow C = 16$ total codes | Determines encoding complexity |
| Debounce time constant | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Seconds; $R$ in Ω, $C$ in F | Filters mechanical bounce |
| Required debounce duration | $t_{debounce} \geq 5 \cdot \tau$ | Must exceed **$t_b$** (5–20 ms) | Ensures clean logic transitions |
| Schmitt trigger hysteresis | $V_H = V_{TH} - V_{TL}$ | Volts; $V_{TH} \geq 0.7V_{CC}$, $V_{TL} \leq 0.3V_{CC}$ for HC series | Prevents oscillation near threshold |
| Gray code conversion (k-th bit) | $G_k = B_{k+1} \oplus B_k$ | Bitwise XOR operation | Single-bit change per direction step |
| Power consumption (resistive pull-up) | $P = V_{CC}^2 / R_{pull-up}$ | Watts; $R_{pull-up}$ typically **4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ** | Battery-life calculation for wheelchairs |
| Direction vector resolution | $\theta = \arctan(\text{East}/\text{North signal})$ | Degrees; only valid when $\vert\text{North}\vert + \vert\text{East}\vert \neq 0$ | Used in software to recover angle info |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule:** Inside the table above, the expression $\vert\text{North}\vert$ uses the LaTeX `\vert` command (not the keyboard `\|` pipe symbol) to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Discrete joysticks form the **primary input modality** in the following assistive medical devices:

- **Powered Wheelchairs (Class A/B):** Permits safe, predictable control for users with cognitive or fine-motor impairments. Examples include the **PG Drives VR2** and **Dynamic Controls DX2** systems.
- **Sip-and-Puff Hardwares (Hybrid mode):** Discrete signals from breath pressure switches are encoded exactly like joystick switch states.
- **Single-Switch Scanning Interfaces:** For users with **locked-in syndrome**, a single switch (often a discrete joystick deflected to one direction) is scanned across menu options.
- **Robotic Prosthetic Hand Controllers:** For low-cost bionic hands, a 2-axis discrete joystick selects grasp patterns (pinch, key, power, open).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Encoding Scheme for an 8-Direction Discrete Joystick

A canonical 4-switch discrete joystick produces 4 parallel digital lines. We will now derive the mapping from these raw switch states to a **3-bit directional code** usable by a microcontroller.

### Step 1 — Define the Raw Switch Inputs

Let the four micro-switches be labeled by the cardinal direction they encode:

- $S_N$ → North switch (forward)
- $S_E$ → East switch (right)
- $S_S$ → South switch (reverse)
- $S_W$ → West switch (left)

Each switch outputs a binary logic level:

$$
S_i \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \text{where} \quad i \in \{N, E, S, W\}
$$

Here, $S_i = 1$ means the switch is **actuated (closed)**, and $S_i = 0$ means it is **released (open)**.

### Step 2 — Derive the Composite Direction Code (D)

We define a 3-bit directional word $D = (D_2, D_1, D_0)$ using a **priority-encoder logic**, with North having the highest priority to ensure safe wheelchair forward motion is never blocked:

$$
D_2 = S_N \lor (S_E \land S_W)
$$

$$
D_1 = S_S \lor (S_E \land S_W)
$$

$$
D_0 = S_E \lor S_W
$$

where $\lor$ is the **OR** operation and $\land$ is the **AND** operation.

**Textual Logic Explanation:**
- $D_2$ is set HIGH whenever the joystick is pushed toward the **upper hemisphere** (any N or diagonal component).
- $D_1$ is set HIGH whenever the joystick is pushed toward the **lower hemisphere**.
- $D_0$ distinguishes between the **right** and **left** hemispheres (East vs. West).

### Step 3 — Build the Complete Truth Table

| **Stick Position** | **$S_N$** | **$S_E$** | **$S_S$** | **$S_W$** | **$D_2$** | **$D_1$** | **$D_0$** | **Decimal Code** |
|--------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| **Center (Neutral)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| **North** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **4** |
| **Northeast** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **5** |
| **East** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **1** |
| **Southeast** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **3** |
| **South** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **2** |
| **Southwest** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **3** (same as SE) |
| **West** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **1** (same as E) |
| **Northwest** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **5** (same as NE) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** A simple 3-bit priority encoder loses the East/West and Northeast/Southwest distinctions, mapping both E and W to code '1'. To preserve all 8 directions, a **4-bit direct parallel read** (i.e., reading $S_N, S_E, S_S, S_W$ individually) is preferred, yielding **9 distinct valid states** (center + 8 directions).

### Step 4 — Improved 4-Bit Directional Code

To retain full directional resolution, the microcontroller reads the four switch lines directly as a **4-bit nibble**:

$$
\text{Code} = (S_N \cdot 8) + (S_E \cdot 4) + (S_S \cdot 2) + (S_W \cdot 1)
$$

**Verification using Northeast direction** ($S_N=1, S_E=1, S_S=0, S_W=0$):

$$
\text{Code}_{NE} = (1 \cdot 8) + (1 \cdot 4) + (0 \cdot 2) + (0 \cdot 1) = 8 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 12_{10} = 1100_2
$$

This is the **canonical KTU-expected encoding** for assistive wheelchair controllers.

### Step 5 — Debounce Circuit Analysis

The raw switch output passes through an RC low-pass filter followed by a Schmitt trigger. The transfer function of the RC filter is:

$$
V_{out}(t) = V_{final} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/\tau}), \quad \tau = R \cdot C
$$

**Design Constraint:** The filter time constant must satisfy:

$$
\tau \geq \frac{t_{bounce}}{5} = \frac{10 \text{ ms}}{5} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

If we choose $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$, then:

$$
R = \frac{\tau}{C} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 20 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

A standard **$R = 22 \, \text{k}\Omega$**, $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$ combination is commonly used in commercial wheelchair joystick modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Discrete Joystick Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Hand Input] --> B[Deflectable Shaft on Gimbal Mount]
    B --> C1[Cam Actuator North]
    B --> C2[Cam Actuator East]
    B --> C3[Cam Actuator South]
    B --> C4[Cam Actuator West]
    B --> R1[Conical Return Spring Restores Neutral]
    C1 --> SW1[Micro-Switch N Reed or Hall Sensor]
    C2 --> SW2[Micro-Switch E Reed or Hall Sensor]
    C3 --> SW3[Micro-Switch S Reed or Hall Sensor]
    C4 --> SW4[Micro-Switch W Reed or Hall Sensor]
    SW1 --> D1[RC Debounce Filter tau = R times C]
    SW2 --> D2[RC Debounce Filter tau = R times C]
    SW3 --> D3[RC Debounce Filter tau = R times C]
    SW4 --> D4[RC Debounce Filter tau = R times C]
    D1 --> ST1[Schmitt Trigger 74HC14]
    D2 --> ST2[Schmitt Trigger 74HC14]
    D3 --> ST3[Schmitt Trigger 74HC14]
    D4 --> ST4[Schmitt Trigger 74HC14]
    ST1 --> MUX[4 to 1 Multiplexer or Parallel Port]
    ST2 --> MUX
    ST3 --> MUX
    ST4 --> MUX
    MUX --> MCU[Microcontroller PIC or ARM Cortex M0]
    MCU --> DRV[Motor Driver L298N or VNH5019]
    DRV --> WM[Wheelchair Motors or Robotic Actuator]
    MCU --> LCD[User Feedback LCD or Buzzer]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#FFDAB9
    style MCU fill:#90EE90
    style DRV fill:#FFB6C1
    style WM fill:#87CEEB
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Joystick Directional Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Neutral
    Neutral --> North: S_N = 1
    Neutral --> East: S_E = 1
    Neutral --> South: S_S = 1
    Neutral --> West: S_W = 1
    North --> Northeast: S_E = 1
    North --> Northwest: S_W = 1
    East --> Northeast: S_N = 1
    East --> Southeast: S_S = 1
    South --> Southeast: S_E = 1
    South --> Southwest: S_W = 1
    West --> Northwest: S_N = 1
    West --> Southwest: S_S = 1
    Northeast --> Neutral: All S = 0
    Southeast --> Neutral: All S = 0
    Southwest --> Neutral: All S = 0
    Northwest --> Neutral: All S = 0
    North --> Neutral: S_N = 0
    East --> Neutral: S_E = 0
    South --> Neutral: S_S = 0
    West --> Neutral: S_W = 0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All Mermaid node IDs above use purely alphanumeric identifiers (e.g., `SW1`, `ST1`, `MCU`) prefixed with letters, with **NO** reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone node names. Node labels are written in **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** without any markdown bold or italic tags to prevent rendering errors.

## 4.3 Signal Flow Explanation (Textual Substitute for Physical Drawing)

A physical cut-away of a discrete joystick would show:

1. A **vertical shaft** entering a hemispherical gimbal joint.
2. A **conical spring** at the base pushing the shaft upward into a centering position.
3. Four **leaf springs or cam plates** arranged at 90° around the shaft base.
4. Four **micro-switches** mounted on the joystick housing such that any deflection compresses one or two adjacent cam plates, closing the corresponding switch contact.
5. A **ribbon cable** exiting the housing with 5 wires: 4 signal lines (one per switch) and 1 common ground (or 5 V supply).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define a discrete joystick. How does it differ from an analog (proportional) joystick in terms of output signal?**

**Model Answer:**

A **discrete joystick** is a multi-directional input device that produces a **finite set of distinct digital output states** (binary HIGH/LOW) corresponding to specific angular deflections of its shaft, implemented using mechanical micro-switches, Hall-effect sensors, or optical interrupters.

**Key Differences:**

| **Parameter** | **Discrete Joystick** | **Analog Joystick** |
|----------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Output type | Digital (binary) | Continuous voltage (0–5 V) |
| Number of states | Finite (e.g., 9) | Infinite (within range) |
| Tremor sensitivity | Low (requires deliberate detent) | High (any drift is registered) |
| Interfacing | Direct to GPIO / logic gates | Requires ADC |

**[3 Marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks tabulated comparison]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three applications of discrete joysticks in assistive medical devices.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Powered wheelchair controllers** for users with spasticity or cerebral palsy.
2. **Environmental Control Units (ECUs)** for bedridden patients to operate lights, fans, and TV.
3. **Surgical instrument selection panels** in robotic-assisted minimally invasive surgery.
4. **Single-switch scanning communication devices** for patients with locked-in syndrome.

**[3 Marks: 1 mark per application]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examinations feature a **Module Internal Choice**, meaning students are given **two alternative 14-mark questions** per module and must answer **one**. Each 14-mark question is split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 4-switch discrete joystick used in a powered wheelchair. Describe the function of each block. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Design the debounce circuit for a discrete joystick micro-switch. Given the switch bounce time $t_b = 15$ ms, choose suitable values of $R$ and $C$, and justify your selection using the time-constant formula. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Working of a 4-Switch Discrete Joystick:**

The 4-switch discrete joystick converts manual directional intent into **4 parallel digital signals** that a wheelchair motor controller can interpret.

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**

1. **Shaft & Gimbal Assembly:** A rigid shaft is supported by a ball-and-socket gimbal, allowing ±30° deflection in any direction on the horizontal plane. A conical return spring keeps the shaft centered when no force is applied.
2. **Four Cam Actuators:** Mounted at 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270° around the shaft base, each cam translates horizontal deflection into vertical motion.
3. **Four Micro-Switches (Reed or Snap-Action):** Each cam drives one switch. Pushing North closes only the N switch; pushing Northeast (45°) closes both N and E simultaneously.
4. **Pull-Up Resistor Network:** A 10 kΩ pull-up resistor on each line ensures a defined HIGH logic level when the switch is open.
5. **RC Debounce Filters:** Eliminate the 5–20 ms contact bounce inherent in mechanical switches.
6. **Schmitt Triggers (74HC14):** Provide hysteresis ($V_H \geq 0.7 V_{CC}$, $V_L \leq 0.3 V_{CC}$) for noise-free digital edges.
7. **Microcontroller (PIC16F877A / ARM Cortex-M0):** Reads the 4-bit code and applies firmware-level safety interlocks (max speed, obstacle detection).
8. **Motor Driver (H-Bridge L298N):** Converts logic commands into differential PWM signals for the two wheelchair drive motors.

**Part (b) — Debounce Circuit Design:**

**Given:** $t_b = 15$ ms (typical mechanical bounce time)

**Step 1 — Apply the design rule:**

$$
\tau \geq \frac{t_b}{5} = \frac{15 \text{ ms}}{5} = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose a standard capacitor value** $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$ (commonly available, low leakage).

**Step 3 — Solve for R:**

$$
R = \frac{\tau}{C} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 30 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Select the nearest standard E12 value:** $R = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$ (5% tolerance).

**Step 5 — Verify the actual time constant:**

$$
\tau_{actual} = R \cdot C = 33 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 3.3 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
5 \cdot \tau_{actual} = 16.5 \text{ ms} > 15 \text{ ms} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Justification:** The 16.5 ms settling duration safely exceeds the 15 ms worst-case bounce, ensuring the Schmitt trigger sees a monotonic edge.

**Final Component List:** $R = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$ (¼ W, ±5%), $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$ (ceramic or film, 50 V), Schmitt trigger = **74HC14**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Compare and contrast discrete (digital) joysticks with analog (proportional) joysticks. Mention at least **five distinct parameters** in your comparison. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A discrete joystick uses 4 micro-switches to encode 8 directions and 1 neutral state. Derive the 4-bit binary code for all 9 valid states and explain how the controller distinguishes between the center (neutral) state and a direction command. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (5 parameters minimum):**

| **S. No.** | **Parameter** | **Discrete (Digital) Joystick** | **Analog (Proportional) Joystick** |
|------------|----------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| 1 | Output signal | 4 binary lines (HIGH/LOW) | 2 analog voltages (0–5 V) per axis |
| 2 | Resolution | 9 discrete states (4-bit) | 1024+ states (10-bit ADC) |
| 3 | Tremor rejection | Excellent (mechanical detent filters noise) | Poor (any deflection causes drift) |
| 4 | Speed control | Stepped (preset speeds only) | Continuous (proportional) |
| 5 | Microcontroller interface | Direct GPIO read | Requires ADC (2 channels) |
| 6 | Power consumption | Low (CMOS logic, ~10 mA) | Higher (potentiometer + ADC) |
| 7 | Cost | Lower | Higher |
| 8 | Best suited for | Users with spasticity, tremor, or learning difficulties | Users with good fine motor control |

**Part (b) — Derivation of the 4-Bit Code:**

Using the encoding formula derived in Section 3:

$$
\text{Code} = (S_N \cdot 8) + (S_E \cdot 4) + (S_S \cdot 2) + (S_W \cdot 1)
$$

The complete mapping is:

| **Position** | **$S_N$** | **$S_E$** | **$S_S$** | **$S_W$** | **4-Bit Code** |
|--------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Neutral (Center)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **0000** |
| **North** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1000** |
| **Northeast** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **1100** |
| **East** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **0100** |
| **Southeast** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **0110** |
| **South** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **0010** |
| **Southwest** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **0011** |
| **West** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **0001** |
| **Northwest** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **1001** |

**Neutral Detection Logic:**

The controller firmware checks whether **all four switch inputs are LOW** simultaneously:

$$
\text{IsNeutral} = \overline{S_N} \land \overline{S_E} \land \overline{S_S} \land \overline{S_W}
$$

If $\text{IsNeutral} = 1$, the firmware commands the motor driver to apply **active electromagnetic braking** to both drive motors, bringing the wheelchair to an immediate stop. This is a critical safety feature in KTU-specified assistive wheelchair design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Discrete" with "Discreet":** Many students spell it as "discreet joystick" in answers. The correct term is **"DISCRETE"** (meaning distinct/separate). Examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** for this spelling error.
> 2. **Forgetting the Neutral State:** When listing "8 directions," always state **"8 directions + 1 neutral = 9 valid states."** Omitting the neutral state loses **1 mark** in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 3. **Missing the Debounce Block:** A block diagram of a discrete joystick without the **RC + Schmitt trigger** debounce stage is considered incomplete. Examiners specifically test this in the 7-mark sub-part.
> 4. **No Mention of Safety Interlocks:** For wheelchair-related questions, **always** mention active braking in neutral, watchdog timer, and obstacle detection. Skipping safety aspects costs **2 marks** minimum.
> 5. **Using $\vert x \vert$ (pipe symbol) in markdown tables:** Causes the table to break in the digital answer sheet upload portal. Use LaTeX `\vert x \vert` instead.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!**

- ✅ **Definition:** Discrete joystick = finite, distinct digital output states via switches.
- ✅ **Standard Configuration:** **4 micro-switches** at 0°, 90°, 180°, 270° → **9 valid states** (1 center + 4 cardinal + 4 diagonal).
- ✅ **Encoding:** 4-bit parallel binary (Code = 8·N + 4·E + 2·S + 1·W).
- ✅ **Transducer Choices:** Reed switches, Hall sensors with Schmitt trigger, optical interrupters.
- ✅ **Debounce Formula:** $\tau = RC$, with $5\tau \geq t_{bounce}$ (typically $R = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$).
- ✅ **Schmitt Trigger IC:** **74HC14** (hex inverting Schmitt trigger) — KTU favourite.
- ✅ **Neutral Detection:** All four switches LOW → active braking commanded.
- ✅ **Advantage:** Excellent **tremor rejection**; ideal for users with cerebral palsy, Parkinson's, or spasticity.
- ✅ **Disadvantage:** No **proportional speed control**; only preset stepped speeds.
- ✅ **Pull-up Resistor Standard:** **$R_{pull-up} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$** (TTL) or **4.7 kΩ** (CMOS low-power).
- ✅ **Safety Standards:** Compliant with **ISO 7176-14** for powered wheelchair control systems.
- ✅ **Comparison with Analog:** Discrete is **digital/binary**; Analog is **continuous voltage** requiring ADC.
- ✅ **Real-world ICs:** PIC16F877A, ATmega328, STM32F0 are typical microcontrollers used for joystick decoding.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Buzzwords to use:** *Detent force, contact bounce, Schmitt hysteresis, priority encoder, fail-safe neutral, active braking, ISO 7176-14.*

---

**END OF MODULE 2 — DISCRETE JOYSTICKS NOTES**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education guidelines.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chord Keyboards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chord Keyboards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Chord Keyboard** is a compact computer input peripheral device that generates alphanumeric characters, commands, or entire words by registering the *simultaneous* depression of two or more keys, in direct analogy to a musical chord (where multiple notes are struck together). Unlike a conventional QWERTY keyboard that requires sequential, single-finger keystrokes, the chord keyboard relies on **binary combinatorial encoding** of key states, allowing a small set of physical keys (typically **5 to 12**) to access a full alphanumeric repertoire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Anchor:** The chord keyboard concept was pioneered by **Douglas C. Engelbart** in the 1960s as part of the *oN-Line System (NLS)* at the Stanford Research Institute. He was awarded U.S. Patent No. 3,541,541 (1970) for the "Display Selection in a Display System," which fundamentally relied on a 5-key chord set held in one hand.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are playing a **piano**. A beginner strikes one key at a time, slowly producing a melody. An advanced player, however, can press *three or four keys at once* — a "chord" — and convey a richer, faster message. The chord keyboard works on the exact same principle:

- **Piano → produces a harmony of notes.**
- **Chord Keyboard → produces a character, word, or command.**

For a person with the use of only **one functional hand**, or with restricted fine-motor dexterity (e.g., due to cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, or upper-limb amputation), the conventional 100+ key QWERTY keyboard is physically inaccessible. A chord keyboard collapses 100+ inputs into a handheld device with as few as **5 keys**, restoring independent text-entry capability.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value (Industry Norm) | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Number of physical keys | **5 to 12 keys** | Defines chord space |
| Hand occupancy | One-handed operation | Critical for motor-impaired users |
| Typing speed (trained user) | **30–60 WPM** (Words Per Minute) | Functional threshold for communication |
| Activation force per key | **0.5 N to 1.5 N** | Tuned for low-strength hands |
| Maximum simultaneous keys | 3 to 5 (typical) | Limits combinatorial explosion |
| Chord recognition latency | **< 20 ms** | Real-time usability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Chord keyboards are categorized under *Alternative and Augmentative Communication (AAC)* devices in the KTU 2024 syllabus for Assistive Medical Devices. They bridge the domains of **biomedical engineering**, **human-computer interaction (HCI)**, and **rehabilitation engineering**.

## 1.4 Why Chord Keyboards Matter in Assistive Technology

Chord keyboards address a fundamental bottleneck in assistive technology: **the input device, not the software, is the limiting factor** for most users with motor disabilities. By compressing an entire alphabet into a handful of keys, they:

1. **Eliminate the need for bilateral hand coordination** (essential for hemiplegic patients).
2. **Reduce finger travel** to nearly zero, mitigating fatigue in patients with low stamina.
3. **Enable wearable, ambulatory computing** for users with progressive neuromuscular conditions.
4. **Provide a fallback input modality** for ALS patients before speech synthesis becomes their only option.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The chord keyboard operates on **three sequential functional layers**:

### Layer 1 — Physical Transduction
Each key contains a mechanical or capacitive switch. When pressed, it closes an electrical circuit, sending a binary **HIGH (1)** signal to the microcontroller. When released, it returns to **LOW (0)**.

### Layer 2 — Combinatorial Encoding
A microcontroller samples the state of all $n$ keys at a defined polling rate (typically **100 Hz to 1 kHz**). If a stable pattern of pressed keys is detected for a debounce window (typically **5 ms to 15 ms**), the pattern is registered as a single "chord event." This event is then mapped via a lookup table (LUT) to a character, command, or word.

### Layer 3 — Emulation Layer
The mapped character is transmitted to the host computer as a **standard HID (Human Interface Device) report**, often emulating a regular USB keyboard. This ensures *plug-and-play* compatibility with any operating system without requiring specialized drivers.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Chord Encoding

- **Why not just use 26 individual keys?** Because users with single-hand mobility or limited grip strength cannot reliably reach, press, and release 26+ spread-out keys.
- **Why combinations instead of sequences?** Because sequences (multi-tap) re-introduce the timing bottleneck. Chords transmit more information *per unit time* and *per unit of physical effort*.
- **Why 5 keys specifically?** Because 5 keys allow up to $2^5 - 1 = 31$ distinct non-empty combinations, which is just enough to map the 26 English letters plus a few common commands. This is the *minimum efficient key set* identified by Engelbart.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear frequently in KTU derivations and numerical problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total possible chords (any non-empty subset) | $C_{\text{total}} = 2^n - 1$ | $n$ = number of physical keys |
| 2 | Chords using exactly $k$ keys | $C(n,k) = \dfrac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$ | Binomial coefficient |
| 3 | Sum of all chord counts (Pascal's identity) | $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} C(n,k) = 2^n - 1$ | Consistency check |
| 4 | Information entropy per chord (bits) | $H = \log_2(2^n - 1) \approx n$ | Information-theoretic capacity |
| 5 | Typing throughput | $T = \dfrac{N_{\text{chars}}}{T_{\text{time}}} \quad [\text{chars/sec}]$ | Real-world input rate |
| 6 | WPM conversion | $\text{WPM} = \dfrac{\text{chars/min}}{5}$ | Standard: 5 chars = 1 word |
| 7 | Activation force (Hooke's Law) | $F = k \cdot x$ | Switch spring mechanics |
| 8 | Debounce time constant | $\tau_{\text{deb}} \geq 5 \times R \times C$ | RC filter design |
| 9 | Power consumption (active) | $P = V \cdot I_{\text{scan}}$ | Battery life estimation |
| 10 | Fitts's Law (target acquisition) | $T_{\text{move}} = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Movement time to a key |

**Notation Note:** In formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation has been intentionally replaced with parentheses to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The chord keyboard is a translational research outcome with applications in:

- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Restoring written communication for patients with motor neuron disease (MND), spinal cord injury (SCI) at C5–C7 levels, or rheumatoid arthritis.
- **Wearable Computing:** Used in smartwatches and AR/VR headsets where screen real estate for virtual keyboards is zero.
- **Aviation & Military Cockpits:** Pilots use chorded controls to operate radios and weapon systems while keeping both hands on flight controls.
- **Industrial Safety:** Single-handed operation in environments where one hand is permanently occupied (e.g., surgeons using intra-operative imaging).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Chord Space from *n* Keys

**Problem Statement:** A chord keyboard has $n$ independent keys. Each key is binary (pressed $\equiv 1$, released $\equiv 0$). A valid chord consists of *one or more* keys pressed simultaneously. Derive the total number of unique chords that can be generated.

### Step 1 — Define the State Space
Each of the $n$ keys has exactly 2 possible states: pressed (1) or released (0). Therefore, the *total* number of state combinations (including the "no key pressed" idle state) is:

$$
N_{\text{states}} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}} = 2^{n}
$$

This is the product rule of combinatorics: if there are 2 choices per key over $n$ keys, the total sample space has $2^{n}$ elements.

### Step 2 — Exclude the Idle State
The "all keys released" state corresponds to the user *not typing* — it is the resting condition, **not** a chord. We subtract this single state:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 2^{n} - 1
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
For a 5-key chord keyboard (Engelbart's original design):

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 2^{5} - 1 = 32 - 1 = 31 \text{ chords}
$$

For a 12-key device like the *Twiddler*:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 2^{12} - 1 = 4096 - 1 = 4095 \text{ chords}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why high-end chord keyboards can map **entire words and phrases**, not just single letters.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Chords of Size *k*

**Problem Statement:** Derive the number of distinct chords that use *exactly* $k$ keys pressed simultaneously, chosen from a set of $n$ available keys.

### Step 1 — Apply the Combination Formula
Since the *order* of key presses does not matter (pressing Key 1 then Key 2 simultaneously is identical to Key 2 then Key 1), we use the binomial coefficient:

$$
C(n, k) = \binom{n}{k} = \dfrac{n!}{k!\,(n - k)!}
$$

### Step 2 — Verify with $n = 5$
Compute for each $k \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$:

$$
C(5, 1) = \dfrac{5!}{1!\,4!} = 5
$$

$$
C(5, 2) = \dfrac{5!}{2!\,3!} = \dfrac{120}{2 \cdot 6} = 10
$$

$$
C(5, 3) = \dfrac{5!}{3!\,2!} = \dfrac{120}{6 \cdot 2} = 10
$$

$$
C(5, 4) = \dfrac{5!}{4!\,1!} = 5
$$

$$
C(5, 5) = \dfrac{5!}{5!\,0!} = 1
$$

### Step 3 — Consistency Check
Sum all chord sizes; this must equal $2^{5} - 1$:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{5} C(5, k) = 5 + 10 + 10 + 5 + 1 = 31 \quad \checkmark
$$

This confirms Pascal's Identity: $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} = 2^{n}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Typing Throughput Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A patient with limited hand mobility types on a 5-key chord keyboard. Their average chord-formation time (including chord recognition and inter-chord pause) is $T_{\text{chord}} = 0.6$ seconds. Assuming average English word length of 5 characters and an average of 1.3 chords per character (some letters require 2-key chords), compute the typing speed in **WPM**.

### Step 1 — Time Per Character
Each character requires on average 1.3 chords, each taking 0.6 s:

$$
T_{\text{char}} = 1.3 \times 0.6 = 0.78 \text{ s/char}
$$

### Step 2 — Characters Per Minute

$$
\text{CPM} = \dfrac{60}{0.78} \approx 76.92 \text{ chars/min}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to WPM
Using the KTU-standard 5 characters per word conversion:

$$
\text{WPM} = \dfrac{76.92}{5} \approx 15.38 \text{ WPM}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** 15 WPM is the *minimum threshold* for functional conversational typing. Modern chord keyboards (Twiddler, CharaChorder) can exceed 60 WPM with training, making them competitive with QWERTY.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Chord Keyboard Systems

| Device | Keys | Total Chords | Mapping Scheme | Target User |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Microwriter (1980s) | 5 | 31 | Letters only (no numbers) | General single-hand users |
| CyKey (1990s) | 5 | 31 | Letters + punctuation | Motor-impaired users |
| Twiddler (2000s–present) | 12 | 4095 | Letters, numbers, mouse | Wearable computing |
| CharaChorder (2020s) | 7–11 | $> 2000$ | **Multi-character chords** | Power users, gaming |
| BAT Keyboard (1989) | 7 | 127 | Optimized for 1-hand ergonomics | C5–C6 SCI patients |
| In袖on KEY | 5 | 31 | Mirror of 5-finger QWERTY | Hemiplegic patients |
| Velotype | Variable | n/a | Continuous chord sequences | Stenography |

> [!NOTE]
> The CharaChorder represents a paradigm shift: pressing multiple keys simultaneously can produce an **entire word** (e.g., pressing "h-e-l-l-o" all at once types "hello"). This exploits **motor anticipation** and human predictive capacity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Chord Keyboard System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Hand Input] --> B[Mechanical Key Matrix 5 to 12 keys]
    B --> C[Debounce and Polling Circuit 100 Hz to 1 kHz]
    C --> D[Microcontroller MCU e.g. ATmega32U4]
    D --> E[Chord Lookup Table LUT Stored in EEPROM]
    E --> F[HID Emulation Layer USB HID or Bluetooth]
    F --> G[Host Computer Smartphone Tablet or PC]
    G --> H[Operating System OS Input Handler]
    H --> I[Application Software Word Processor or AAC App]
    I --> J[Visual or Auditory Output Text or Speech]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Description:** This block diagram illustrates the complete signal path from the user's hand through to the visual output. Each block represents a functional stage, and the colors differentiate user-interface (warm tones) from signal-processing (cool tones) layers.

---

## 4.2 Chord Recognition State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> KeyPressed: Any key down
    KeyPressed --> Debounce: 5 ms hold
    Debounce --> StableChord: Pattern stable
    StableChord --> LUTLookup: Query EEPROM
    LUTLookup --> ValidChord: Match found
    LUTLookup --> ErrorState: No match
    ValidChord --> EmitHID: Send USB report
    EmitHID --> Idle: All keys released
    ErrorState --> Idle: Timeout 50 ms
```

**Description:** This finite state machine governs how the chord keyboard's firmware validates a chord. The 5 ms debounce window eliminates mechanical switch bounce, and the 50 ms error-state timeout prevents misinterpretation of accidental touches.

---

## 4.3 Chord Combination Mapping Schematic (5-Key System)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SingleKey[Single Key Chords 5 combinations]
        K1[Key 1 alone]:::single
        K2[Key 2 alone]:::single
        K3[Key 3 alone]:::single
        K4[Key 4 alone]:::single
        K5[Key 5 alone]:::single
    end

    subgraph DualKey[Dual Key Chords 10 combinations]
        D12[Keys 1 and 2]:::dual
        D13[Keys 1 and 3]:::dual
        D14[Keys 1 and 4]:::dual
        D15[Keys 1 and 5]:::dual
        D23[Keys 2 and 3]:::dual
        D24[Keys 2 and 4]:::dual
        D25[Keys 2 and 5]:::dual
        D34[Keys 3 and 4]:::dual
        D35[Keys 3 and 5]:::dual
        D45[Keys 4 and 5]:::dual
    end

    subgraph TripleKey[Triple Key Chords 10 combinations]
        T123[Keys 1 2 3]:::triple
        T124[Keys 1 2 4]:::triple
        T125[Keys 1 2 5]:::triple
        T134[Keys 1 3 4]:::triple
        T135[Keys 1 3 5]:::triple
        T145[Keys 1 4 5]:::triple
        T234[Keys 2 3 4]:::triple
        T235[Keys 2 3 5]:::triple
        T245[Keys 2 4 5]:::triple
        T345[Keys 3 4 5]:::triple
    end

    subgraph QuadKey[Quad Key Chords 5 combinations]
        Q1234[Keys 1 2 3 4]:::quad
        Q1235[Keys 1 2 3 5]:::quad
        Q1245[Keys 1 2 4 5]:::quad

        Q2345[Keys 2 3 4 5]:::quad

    SingleKey --> DualKey --> TripleKey --> QuadKey --> QuintKey

    classDef single fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef dual fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```
