**Description:** This hierarchical diagram shows the **combinatorial structure** of a 5-key chord keyboard. The total 31 chords are partitioned by *size* (number of keys pressed): 5 + 10 + 10 + 5 + 1 = 31. This is the foundational mapping for systems like *Microwriter* and *CyKey*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a chord keyboard. How does it differ from a conventional QWERTY keyboard in terms of user interaction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A chord keyboard is a compact input device that generates characters by registering the *simultaneous* depression of two or more keys, analogous to a musical chord. **(1 mark)**

Unlike a QWERTY keyboard, which requires sequential single-finger keystrokes spread across 100+ keys, a chord keyboard typically uses only **5 to 12 keys** and relies on binary combinations. **(1 mark)**

The key interaction differences are:
1. **Sequential vs. Simultaneous:** QWERTY uses one key at a time; chord keyboards use multiple keys concurrently.
2. **Hand occupancy:** QWERTY needs both hands optimally; chord keyboards operate fully with one hand.
3. **Physical reach:** QWERTY requires large finger travel; chord keys remain in a fixed hand-hold position. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the formula for the total number of unique chords possible with *n* keys, and compute the value for $n = 7$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The total number of unique non-empty chord combinations from $n$ keys is given by:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 2^{n} - 1 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

For $n = 7$:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 2^{7} - 1 = 128 - 1 = 127 \text{ chords} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern requires you to answer **one** question from a Module choice. Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### 🔹 Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q.** (a) Explain in detail the **operational principle** of a chord keyboard, including its three functional layers: transduction, combinatorial encoding, and HID emulation. **(7 marks)**

(b) A patient recovering from a C6 spinal cord injury is being fitted with a 6-key chord keyboard for assistive text entry. The patient takes an average of **0.8 seconds per chord** and uses an average of **1.2 chords per character**. Calculate: **(i)** the typing throughput in characters per minute, **(ii)** the corresponding speed in WPM, and **(iii)** whether the patient can achieve **functional conversational typing** (threshold = 15 WPM). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The operational principle of a chord keyboard rests on three integrated functional layers:

**Layer 1 — Physical Transduction (2 marks):**
Each of the $n$ keys contains a mechanical dome switch or capacitive sensor. When pressed, the switch closes and outputs a digital HIGH (logic 1); when released, it returns to LOW (logic 0). The keys are typically arranged in a curved or linear cluster designed to fit the natural curvature of the hand, allowing actuation by the four fingers and thumb without repositioning.

**Layer 2 — Combinatorial Encoding (3 marks):**
A microcontroller (commonly ATmega32U4 or similar) polls the state of all keys at a frequency between **100 Hz and 1 kHz**. When a stable pattern of pressed keys is detected for at least **5 ms** (debounce duration), the firmware constructs an *n-bit binary word* representing the chord. This word is used as an index into a **Lookup Table (LUT)** stored in non-volatile memory (EEPROM/Flash), which maps the binary pattern to a Unicode character, ASCII code, or HID usage code. For instance, in a 5-key Microwriter layout, the binary pattern `10100` (Keys 1 and 3 pressed) might map to the letter "B."

**Layer 3 — HID Emulation (2 marks):**
The mapped character is packaged into a standard **USB HID (Human Interface Device) report** or Bluetooth HID packet, conforming to the universal keyboard class specification. The host operating system (Windows, Linux, Android, iOS) recognizes the device as a standard keyboard, requiring **no custom drivers**. This layer ensures *cross-platform compatibility* and allows the assistive device to interface with any application — word processors, AAC apps, or web browsers — as a plug-and-play peripheral.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Number of keys $n = 6$ (not directly needed for throughput, but defines chord space)
- Time per chord $T_{\text{chord}} = 0.8$ s
- Chords per character $C_{\text{chord}} = 1.2$

**(i) Typing throughput in characters per minute:** **(3 marks)**

Time per character:

$$
T_{\text{char}} = T_{\text{chord}} \times C_{\text{chord}} = 0.8 \times 1.2 = 0.96 \text{ s/char}
$$

Throughput:

$$
\text{CPM} = \dfrac{60 \text{ s/min}}{0.96 \text{ s/char}} = 62.5 \text{ chars/min}
$$

**(ii) Speed in WPM:** **(2 marks)**

$$
\text{WPM} = \dfrac{62.5}{5} = 12.5 \text{ WPM}
$$

**(iii) Functional comparison:** **(2 marks)**

The calculated speed is **12.5 WPM**, which is **below** the 15 WPM functional conversational threshold. **Conclusion:** At this rate, the patient cannot yet achieve functional conversational typing. However, with practice and the addition of common-word chords (e.g., mapping a single chord to "the," "and," "is"), the chord-per-character ratio can drop below 1.0, pushing throughput above the threshold.

---

### 🔹 Question B-B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q.** (a) Discuss the **clinical applications** of chord keyboards as assistive medical devices, citing at least **three specific patient populations** and explaining why conventional keyboards fail them. **(7 marks)**

(b) With the help of derivations, prove that the **total number of unique chords** possible from $n$ keys is $2^{n} - 1$, and verify the result for $n = 4$ by enumerating all possible chord combinations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Chord keyboards serve as essential assistive devices in the following clinical populations: **(1 mark per population, plus explanation)**

**1. Patients with Cerebral Palsy (CP):**
Many individuals with CP have *spastic quadriplegia*, with limited fine-motor control and often poor bilateral coordination. The wide key-spacing of a QWERTY keyboard demands precise, independent finger movement that is physically impossible for these patients. A chord keyboard consolidates the entire alphabet into a 5–7 key cluster that can be operated by gross hand movements alone. **(2 marks)**

**2. Patients with Upper-Limb Amputation:**
A unilateral hand amputee has access to only one fully functional hand. QWERTY keyboards are designed for bimanual use; typing with one hand at typical efficiency is extremely slow (less than 15 WPM even for trained users). A chord keyboard, by design, is **single-hand native**, restoring independence. **(2 marks)**

**3. Patients with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS):**
In early-to-mid stage ALS, hand strength diminishes progressively, and eventually the patient loses the ability to type at all. Chord keyboards are often the *bridge technology* between full hand mobility and complete reliance on eye-tracking or brain-computer interfaces. They require only **0.5 N to 1.5 N** of actuation force, well within the residual strength of early-ALS patients. **(2 marks)**

*(Optionally, a 4th population — Rheumatoid Arthritis or Muscular Dystrophy — may also be credited.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation:** **(5 marks)**

Consider a chord keyboard with $n$ independent binary keys. Each key has exactly two possible states:
- State 0: Released
- State 1: Pressed

By the **Multiplication Principle** of combinatorics, the total number of state combinations over $n$ keys is:

$$
N_{\text{states}} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{n} = 2^{n}
$$

This count includes the *idle state* — the state where no key is pressed. Since a chord is defined as a pattern involving **at least one** pressed key, we must subtract this single idle state:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 2^{n} - 1
$$

**Verification for $n = 4$:** **(2 marks)**

Total expected chords: $2^{4} - 1 = 16 - 1 = 15$ chords.

Enumeration by chord size:

| Chord Size $k$ | Combinations $C(4, k)$ | List |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C(4,1) = 4$ | (1), (2), (3), (4) |
| 2 | $C(4,2) = 6$ | (1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (2,3), (2,4), (3,4) |
| 3 | $C(4,3) = 4$ | (1,2,3), (1,2,4), (1,3,4), (2,3,4) |
| 4 | $C(4,4) = 1$ | (1,2,3,4) |
| **Total** | $4 + 6 + 4 + 1$ | **15 chords ✓** |

The enumeration confirms the derived formula.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in chord keyboard questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the idle state:** Many students incorrectly state the total chord count as $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$. Always exclude the "no key pressed" case. *(–1 to –2 marks)*
>
> 2. **Unit confusion in throughput problems:** Mixing up "characters per second" with "characters per minute," or computing WPM using a divisor of 4 or 6 instead of the KTU-standard **5 characters per word**. *(–1 mark)*
>
> 3. **Skipping clinical justification in Part (a):** Examiners expect *why* the conventional keyboard fails, not just *who* uses the chord keyboard. Always state the **specific physical limitation** that the chord keyboard overcomes (e.g., bilateral coordination, finger travel, actuation force). *(–2 to –3 marks)*
>
> 4. **Not defining acronyms:** "HID" and "LUT" should be spelled out at first use (Human Interface Device; Lookup Table). Examiners deduct for undefined jargon.
>
> 5. **Confusing "chord size" with "chord space":** Chord size = number of keys pressed in one chord (a single value $k$). Chord space = the *total* number of possible chords ($2^n - 1$). Do not interchange these terms.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — memorize before the exam:**

- ✅ A **chord keyboard** generates characters via *simultaneous* key presses, not sequential ones.
- ✅ Total chord combinations from $n$ keys: $\boxed{2^{n} - 1}$.
- ✅ Chords of size $k$ from $n$ keys: $C(n, k) = \dfrac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$.
- ✅ Pascal's identity: $\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} = 2^{n}$.
- ✅ Engelbart pioneered chord keyboards in the **1960s** (NLS system, U.S. Patent 3,541,541).
- ✅ The **5-key** layout is the minimum efficient set: $2^5 - 1 = 31$ chords, just enough for 26 letters + commands.
- ✅ **Activation force** of assistive keys: **0.5 N to 1.5 N** (low-force, suitable for weak or spastic hands).
- ✅ **Debounce time** of switch: typically **5 ms to 15 ms** to filter mechanical bounce.
- ✅ Typing speed benchmark for functional conversation: **≥ 15 WPM**.
- ✅ **HID emulation** ensures plug-and-play with all major operating systems — no special drivers.
- ✅ Clinical target populations: **Cerebral Palsy, ALS, SCI (C5–C7), upper-limb amputation, muscular dystrophy, rheumatoid arthritis**.
- ✅ WPM formula: $\text{WPM} = \dfrac{\text{chars/min}}{5}$.
- ✅ Chord keyboard's core engineering advantage: collapses a **100+ key** interface into a **5–12 key** hand-held device, restoring **one-handed, low-force, low-travel** text input.
- ✅ **CharaChorder (2020s)** introduced *multi-character chords* — pressing several keys at once types a whole word, exploiting motor prediction.
- ✅ Three functional layers: **Transduction → Combinatorial Encoding → HID Emulation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selecting a CI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selecting a CI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Cochlear Implant (CI) Selection** is the systematic, multidisciplinary clinical process of evaluating and identifying candidates who are most likely to derive significant audiological, communicative, and quality-of-life benefits from a surgically implanted electronic prosthetic device that directly stimulates the auditory nerve, bypassing the damaged hair cells of the cochlea.

In strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the selection process involves a **Candidacy Assessment Protocol** governed by audiological, medical, radiological, psychological, and rehabilitative criteria established by the FDA, cochlear implant manufacturer guidelines, and institutional review boards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Favourite):** A Cochlear Implant is a **bimodal** device consisting of an **external sound processor** and a **surgically implanted internal receiver-stimulator electrode array** that converts acoustic signals into electrical pulses delivered to the auditory nerve fibers.

## Key Terminology

- **CI** – Cochlear Implant
- **HL** – Hearing Loss (measured in decibels, dB HL)
- **PTA** – Pure Tone Average (average of thresholds at 500, 1000, 2000, and 4000 Hz)
- **SRS** – Speech Recognition Score (often measured as CNC words or HINT sentences)
- **SNHL** – Sensorineural Hearing Loss
- **ABI** – Auditory Brainstem Implant (alternative for NF2 patients)
- **EAS** – Electric Acoustic Stimulation (hybrid implant for residual low-frequency hearing)
- **CIED** – Cochlear Implant External Device

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Broken Piano & The USB Keyboard**
> Imagine a grand piano where the hammers (hair cells) are broken, but the strings (auditory nerve) and soundboard (brain) are perfectly intact. A hearing aid is like a louder hammer — it cannot work because the hammers are shattered. A **Cochlear Implant is like bypassing the broken hammers entirely and connecting a USB keyboard (the electrode array) directly to the soundboard**, sending digital signals that the brain learns to interpret as music. The "USB connection" (selection process) must be carefully chosen — the wrong cable (device) or wrong port (anatomy) will not produce harmony. Hence, the meticulous **selection of a CI** is analogous to choosing the right cable-port combination before installing a custom digital interface.

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Normal Hearing Threshold:** 0–20 dB HL
- **Severe Hearing Loss:** 71–90 dB HL
- **Profound Hearing Loss:** greater than **90 dB HL**
- **Standard Frequency Range for PTA:** **500 Hz, 1000 Hz, 2000 Hz, 4000 Hz**
- **Speech Frequency Range (Crucial for Speech Understanding):** **300 Hz to 3000 Hz**
- **FDA Adult Candidacy Threshold (Bilateral Severe-to-Profound):** PTA greater than or equal to **70 dB HL** with limited benefit from hearing aids (word score ≤ 50% on recorded tests)
- **FDA Pediatric Candidacy Threshold:** PTA greater than or equal to **90 dB HL** in both ears for children aged 12 months and older
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Multidisciplinary Candidacy Framework

A cochlear implant is never selected by a single clinician. The decision emerges from a **Minimum Four-Discipline Team**:

1. **Otologist / Otolaryngologist** – evaluates middle/inner ear anatomy, surgical feasibility
2. **Audiologist** – performs behavioural and objective audiological tests
3. **Speech-Language Pathologist (SLP)** – assesses communication mode and rehabilitation potential
4. **Radiologist** – interprets imaging of the cochlea, vestibular system, and auditory nerve

Two additional team members, the **psychologist/social worker** and **paediatrician** (for children), are recommended by the KTU 2024 syllabus for evaluating cognitive readiness and family support.

## 2.2 Two-Tiered Selection Process

### Tier 1 — Pre-Selection Screening (Non-Invasive)

- Comprehensive case history and onset of hearing loss
- Otoscopic examination and tympanometry
- Pure Tone Audiometry (PTA) and Speech Audiometry
- **Aided audiogram** (with optimally fitted hearing aids)
- Hearing aid trial compliance (minimum 3–6 months in adults)

### Tier 2 — Pre-Implant Evaluation (Diagnostic)

- **Promontory Stimulation Test** (in selected adults) — electrical stimulation of the cochlea to confirm nerve responsiveness
- **Auditory Brainstem Response (ABR)** — objective threshold estimation
- **Otoacoustic Emissions (OAE)** — confirms outer hair cell dysfunction
- **HRCT / MRI of Temporal Bone** — assesses cochlear patency, nerve integrity, and congenital malformations
- Balance and vestibular function tests
- Cognitive and psychological evaluation
- Vaccinations (pneumococcal and meningococcal — pre-surgery protocol)

## 2.3 The KTU "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Hearing Aid Trial Before Considering CI?**
> The KTU board examiner expects this clinical rationale: a hearing aid is non-invasive, cheaper, and preserves residual hearing. Selection of a CI must only proceed when the audiological benefit of an optimally fitted hearing aid plateaus — i.e., **Speech Recognition Score (SRS) ≤ 50%** for adults. This ensures that irreversible surgery is justified.

## 2.4 Adult vs Paediatric Candidacy Criteria

| Parameter | Adult Criteria | Paediatric Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| Age | 18 years and above (some centres 12+) | 12 months and above (FDA), 9 months (off-label) |
| Degree of HL | Severe-to-Profound bilateral SNHL (PTA ≥ 70 dB HL) | Profound bilateral SNHL (PTA ≥ 90 dB HL) |
| Speech Score | ≤ 50% on open-set sentence test with HA | Lack of age-appropriate auditory milestones |
| Hearing Aid Trial | 3–6 months with minimal benefit | 3–6 months of optimal amplification |
| Medical Fitness | Fit for general anaesthesia | Paediatric fitness, up-to-date immunisations |
| Family Commitment | Reasonable expectations, motivation | Strong family involvement mandatory |

## 2.5 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Threshold | Unit / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Pure Tone Average (PTA) | PTA = (HL500 + HL1000 + HL2000 + HL4000) / 4 | dB HL |
| Severe HL Threshold | 71 to 90 | dB HL |
| Profound HL Threshold | greater than 90 | dB HL |
| Adult CI Candidacy PTA | PTA ≥ 70 | dB HL |
| Paediatric CI Candidacy PTA | PTA ≥ 90 | dB HL |
| Maximum Speech Score for Adult Candidacy | SRS ≤ 50 | percent |
| Insertion Depth of Electrode Array | 18 to 30 | mm |
| Number of Active Channels (typical) | 12 to 24 | channels |
| Stimulation Rate (typical) | 900 to 5000 | pulses per second (pps) |
| FDA Approved Age Limit (Paediatric) | ≥ 12 | months |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Pure Tone Average (PTA)

A 45-year-old adult patient has the following audiometric thresholds in the better ear (in dB HL):

- 500 Hz → 80 dB HL
- 1000 Hz → 90 dB HL
- 2000 Hz → 95 dB HL
- 4000 Hz → 100 dB HL

**Determine if the patient meets the audiological criterion for adult CI candidacy.**

### Step 1: Identify the Formula

The KTU-mandated four-frequency Pure Tone Average formula is:

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{HL_{500} + HL_{1000} + HL_{2000} + HL_{4000}}{4}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Measured Values

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{80 + 90 + 95 + 100}{4}
$$

### Step 3: Perform the Numerator Summation

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{365}{4}
$$

### Step 4: Final Division

$$
\text{PTA} = 91.25 \text{ dB HL}
$$

### Step 5: Apply the Selection Criterion

$$
91.25 \text{ dB HL} \;\geq\; 70 \text{ dB HL (FDA Adult Candidacy Threshold)}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{ Audiological criterion is SATISFIED.}
$$

### Step 6: State the Conclusion

> The patient's PTA of **91.25 dB HL** falls within the **Profound Hearing Loss** category and meets the audiological threshold of **70 dB HL** required for adult cochlear implant candidacy under the KTU 2024 / FDA reference framework.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Verifying Hearing Aid Trial Outcome

A 55-year-old patient has used optimally fitted binaural hearing aids for 6 months. The aided speech recognition score (CNC monosyllabic words) is **38%** in the better ear, with a PTA of **85 dB HL**.

**Determine if this patient meets the audiological criterion for CI candidacy on the SRS parameter.**

### Step 1: Identify the Formula / Threshold

The adult candidacy speech recognition criterion is:

$$
\text{SRS}_{\text{patient}} \;\leq\; \text{SRS}_{\text{threshold}}
$$

$$
38\% \;\leq\; 50\%
$$

### Step 2: Evaluate the Inequality

$$
38 \text{ percent} \;\leq\; 50 \text{ percent} \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \text{TRUE}
$$

### Step 3: Combine with PTA Criterion

$$
\text{PTA} = 85 \text{ dB HL} \;\geq\; 70 \text{ dB HL} \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \text{TRUE}
$$

### Step 4: Final Selection Decision

> Both the audiological PTA threshold and the speech recognition criterion are satisfied. Combined with the 6-month unsuccessful hearing aid trial, the patient is **clinically eligible** for cochlear implant surgery, subject to medical, radiological, and psychological clearance.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Critical Factors in CI Selection

| Factor | Why It Matters | Acceptance Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| **Onset of Deafness** | Post-lingual patients adapt faster; pre-lingual require intense rehab | Post-lingual strongly preferred |
| **Duration of Deafness** | Long auditory deprivation degrades outcomes | Less than 10 years ideal for adults |
| **Cognitive Status** | Affects ability to learn new auditory input | IQ greater than 70 typical |
| **Ear Anatomy** | Cochlear malformations (e.g., Mondini) need special electrodes | Patent cochlea on imaging |
| **Auditory Nerve Integrity** | Stimulus must reach the brain | Normal nerve on MRI |
| **Family Support (Paediatric)** | Drives therapy consistency | Mandatory counselling |
| **Realistic Expectations** | Avoids post-op disappointment | Counselling session required |
| **Vaccination Status (Paediatric)** | Prevents post-op meningitis | Pneumococcal + Meningococcal given |
| **Economic Access** | Device + surgery + lifelong mapping | Insurance / institutional funding |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Categorising Hearing Loss Severity

A patient presents with a PTA of **75 dB HL**. **Classify the hearing loss and identify the next step in selection.**

### Step 1: Recall the KTU Standard Classification

$$
\text{Normal} = 0 \text{ to } 20 \text{ dB HL}
$$
$$
\text{Mild} = 21 \text{ to } 40 \text{ dB HL}
$$
$$
\text{Moderate} = 41 \text{ to } 55 \text{ dB HL}
$$
$$
\text{Moderately Severe} = 56 \text{ to } 70 \text{ dB HL}
$$
$$
\text{Severe} = 71 \text{ to } 90 \text{ dB HL}
$$
$$
\text{Profound} = \text{greater than } 90 \text{ dB HL}
$$

### Step 2: Place the Patient's PTA in the Range

$$
71 \text{ dB HL} \;\leq\; 75 \text{ dB HL} \;\leq\; 90 \text{ dB HL}
$$

### Step 3: Conclude the Category

$$
\therefore \text{ The patient has SEVERE sensorineural hearing loss.}
$$

### Step 4: Next Step in Selection

> Initiate **Trial with Optimally Fitted Binaural Hearing Aids** for 3 to 6 months. If Speech Recognition Score remains less than or equal to 50% with HA, proceed to **Tier-2 Pre-Implant Evaluation**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — CI Selection Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Presents with Hearing Loss] --> B[Comprehensive Case History]
    B --> C[Otoscopic Exam and Tympanometry]
    C --> D[Pure Tone Audiometry and Speech Audiometry]
    D --> E{PTA ≥ 70 dB HL Adult or 90 dB HL Paediatric?}
    E -->|No| F[Hearing Aid Fitting and Monitoring]
    E -->|Yes| G[Trial with Optimal Hearing Aids for 3 to 6 Months]
    G --> H{Aided Speech Score ≤ 50 percent?}
    H -->|No| I[Continue Hearing Aid Use and Reassess Annually]
    H -->|Yes| J[Promontory Stimulation Test in Selected Adults]
    J --> K[ABR and OAE Objective Tests]
    K --> L[HRCT and MRI of Temporal Bone]
    L --> M{Cochlea Patent and Nerve Intact?}
    M -->|No| N[Consider ABI or Auditory Midbrain Implant]
    M -->|Yes| O[Psychological and Cognitive Evaluation]
    O --> P[Vaccinations and Medical Fitness Clearance]
    P --> Q[Multidisciplinary Team Review]
    Q --> R{Approved for CI?}
    R -->|No| S[Document Reasons and Counsel Patient]
    R -->|Yes| T[Surgical Implantation of CI]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a CI System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microphone on Sound Processor] --> B[Sound Processor DSP Unit]
    B --> C[External Coil Transmitter]
    C --> D[RF Link Across Skin]
    D --> E[Internal Receiver Stimulator]
    E --> F[Decoding and Pulse Generation Circuit]
    F --> G[Electrode Array Inserted in Cochlea]
    G --> H[Auditory Nerve Fibers]
    H --> I[Brainstem and Auditory Cortex]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Adult vs Paediatric Decision Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Candidate Enters Selection Protocol] --> B{Age 18 or Above?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Apply Adult Criteria: PTA ≥ 70 dB HL and SRS ≤ 50 percent]
    B -->|No| D[Apply Paediatric Criteria: PTA ≥ 90 dB HL and Age ≥ 12 Months]
    C --> E[3 to 6 Month HA Trial]
    D --> F[3 to 6 Month HA Trial with Family Involvement]
    E --> G[Multidisciplinary Team Review]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Approved?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Schedule Surgery]
    H -->|No| J[Document and Counsel Family]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mapping the Selection Team Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1[Clinical Domain]
        Oto[Otologist]
        Aud[Audiologist]
    end
    subgraph T2[Rehabilitation Domain]
        SLP[Speech Language Pathologist]
        Aud1[Aural Rehabilitation Specialist]
    end
    subgraph T3[Support Domain]
        Psy[Psychologist]
        SW[Social Worker]
    end
    subgraph T4[Imaging and Diagnostics]
        Rad[Radiologist]
        Ana[Anaesthesiologist]
    end
    Oto <--> Aud
    Aud <--> SLP
    SLP <--> Aud1
    Oto <--> Psy
    Oto <--> Rad
    Oto <--> Ana
    Psy <--> SW
    SW <--> Aud1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List the four key disciplines that form the multidisciplinary cochlear implant candidacy team and state the role of the audiologist in the selection process.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution)

1. **Otologist / Otolaryngologist** – Assesses middle and inner ear anatomy, determines surgical feasibility, and performs the implantation. *(0.75 mark)*
2. **Audiologist** – Conducts the entire audiological test battery: PTA, speech audiometry, ABR, OAE, and aided scores. Confirms the audiological candidacy thresholds. *(0.75 mark)*
3. **Speech-Language Pathologist** – Evaluates communication mode (oral, manual, total) and rehabilitative potential. Designs post-implant therapy plan. *(0.75 mark)*
4. **Radiologist** – Interprets HRCT and MRI of the temporal bone to assess cochlear patency, nerve integrity, and rule out anomalies. *(0.75 mark)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model Question]**
**Define Pure Tone Average (PTA). State the FDA adult candidacy PTA threshold for CI selection.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution)

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Pure Tone Average is the arithmetic mean of hearing thresholds (in dB HL) measured at four standard frequencies: **500 Hz, 1000 Hz, 2000 Hz, and 4000 Hz**.

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{HL_{500} + HL_{1000} + HL_{2000} + HL_{4000}}{4}
$$

- **Adult Candidacy Threshold (1.5 marks):** According to FDA-approved criteria referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus, an adult with bilateral **severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss** having a PTA greater than or equal to **70 dB HL** in the better ear (with limited speech perception benefit from optimally fitted hearing aids) qualifies for CI candidacy.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain the step-by-step pre-operative evaluation protocol for selecting an adult candidate for cochlear implantation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 60-year-old post-lingually deafened patient has thresholds of 85, 90, 95, and 100 dB HL at 500, 1000, 2000, and 4000 Hz respectively in the better ear. Aided CNC word score is 32%. Discuss whether the patient qualifies as a CI candidate. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Step-by-Step Pre-Operative Evaluation (7 Marks)

The pre-operative evaluation for adult CI selection is a structured 5-stage protocol:

1. **Stage 1 — Case History and Otological Examination (1 mark):** Document type, onset, and duration of hearing loss; rule out active infection, middle ear pathology, or cholesteatoma. Confirm absence of retrocochlear lesions clinically.

2. **Stage 2 — Audiological Test Battery (2 marks):**
   - **Pure Tone Audiometry** to determine PTA
   - **Speech Audiometry** (SRS / word recognition) in quiet and noise, both unaided and aided
   - **ABR and OAE** for objective confirmation
   - **Aided audiogram** with the patient's own hearing aid set at maximum tolerable gain

3. **Stage 3 — Hearing Aid Trial (1 mark):** Mandate a minimum 3 to 6-month trial of optimally fitted binaural hearing aids, with documented poor speech perception outcomes (SRS ≤ 50%).

4. **Stage 4 — Radiological Evaluation (1.5 marks):** HRCT of the temporal bone to assess cochlear patency, mastoid pneumatisation, facial nerve course; MRI to confirm auditory nerve integrity and rule out vestibular schwannoma.

5. **Stage 5 — Counselling and Final Multidisciplinary Review (1.5 marks):** Psychological evaluation, realistic expectation counselling, vaccination update, anaesthetic fitness, and final team consensus before listing for surgery.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Verification (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute PTA (2 marks)**

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{85 + 90 + 95 + 100}{4}
$$

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{370}{4}
$$

$$
\text{PTA} = 92.5 \text{ dB HL}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with FDA Adult Threshold (1 mark)**

$$
92.5 \text{ dB HL} \;\geq\; 70 \text{ dB HL} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{ PTA criterion SATISFIED.}
$$

**Step 3: Compare SRS with Threshold (1 mark)**

$$
32\% \;\leq\; 50\% \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{ Speech criterion SATISFIED.}
$$

**Step 4: Combine Both Criteria (1 mark)**

> Both audiological criteria are met. The patient is a **clinically eligible** candidate, assuming medical, radiological, and psychological clearance.

**Step 5: Add the Post-Lingual Bonus (1 mark)**

> Since the patient is post-lingually deafened, the central auditory pathways have prior language exposure. This is a **favourable prognostic factor** that strongly predicts excellent post-implant speech outcomes.

**Step 6: Final Decision (1 mark)**

> The patient is recommended for CI surgery, subject to HRCT/MRI confirming cochlear patency, auditory nerve integrity, and a successful multidisciplinary team clearance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** Compare the adult and paediatric candidacy criteria for cochlear implant selection. List any five differentiating factors. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the role of pre-operative imaging (HRCT and MRI) in cochlear implant selection. What findings would contraindicate a cochlear implant? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Adult vs Paediatric Comparison (7 Marks)

| Differentiating Factor | Adult | Paediatric |
|---|---|---|
| **Age** | 18 years and above | 12 months and above (FDA) |
| **PTA Threshold** | ≥ 70 dB HL (Severe-Profound) | ≥ 90 dB HL (Profound) |
| **Speech Criterion** | Open-set SRS ≤ 50% with HA | Lack of age-appropriate auditory milestones |
| **HA Trial Duration** | 3 to 6 months | 3 to 6 months with strong parental involvement |
| **Family / Caregiver Role** | Patient self-motivation | Family is the primary rehabilitation agent |
| **Vaccinations** | Not mandatory pre-surgery | Pneumococcal and Meningococcal mandatory |
| **Outcome Expectations** | Rapid speech perception gains | Long-term language development trajectory |

*(Each correct contrasting pair = 1 mark × 5 = 5 marks. For 2 additional marks, state the rationale: e.g., post-lingual adults adapt faster, paediatric cases need earlier intervention to leverage neural plasticity.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Imaging and Contraindications (7 Marks)

**Role of HRCT (3 marks):**
- Evaluates bony anatomy of temporal bone
- Detects **cochlear malformations** (Mondini dysplasia, common cavity, IP-I, IP-II, IP-III)
- Identifies cochlear ossification, otosclerosis, and facial nerve dehiscence
- Confirms mastoid pneumatisation for surgical access

**Role of MRI (2 marks):**
- Assesses the **cochlear nerve integrity** (critical for stimulation)
- Rules out **vestibular schwannoma**, lipoma, or other retrocochlear lesions
- Evaluates inner ear fluid signal and detects labyrinthitis ossificans

**Contraindications for CI (2 marks):**
1. **Absent or hypoplastic cochlear nerve** (MRI finding) — no pathway for signal transmission; consider ABI instead
2. **Complete cochlear ossification** preventing electrode insertion
3. **Active middle ear infection** (must be cleared pre-surgery)
4. **Medical unfitness for general anaesthesia** (severe cardiac, pulmonary compromise)
5. **Lack of realistic expectations** or absence of family support (relative contraindication)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Selecting a CI" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Hearing Aid Trial** — A common KTU pitfall is recommending CI directly based on PTA alone. Always mention the **3 to 6 month HA trial** with documented poor speech scores.
> 2. **Confusing Adult vs Paediatric PTA Thresholds** — Adult threshold is **70 dB HL**; Paediatric threshold is **90 dB HL**. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Multidisciplinary Team** — Many answers focus only on the audiologist. The KTU board expects **Otologist + Audiologist + SLP + Radiologist** as the minimum four.
> 4. **Skipping Post-Lingual vs Pre-Lingual Discussion** — Always mention **onset of deafness** as a prognostic factor.
> 5. **Forgetting Vaccinations in Paediatric Cases** — Pneumococcal and Meningococcal vaccines are a board-favourite addition.
> 6. **Not Listing Contraindications** — Imaging contraindications (absent nerve, ossified cochlea) are often asked as a direct 2-mark sub-question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Selecting a CI"**

- A **Cochlear Implant** bypasses damaged hair cells by directly stimulating the auditory nerve.
- Selection is a **multidisciplinary process** — minimum 4 team members required.
- **Adult PTA threshold:** greater than or equal to **70 dB HL**.
- **Paediatric PTA threshold:** greater than or equal to **90 dB HL**.
- **Speech Recognition Score** must be less than or equal to **50%** for adult candidacy.
- **Hearing aid trial of 3 to 6 months** is mandatory before CI consideration.
- Standard PTA formula uses **4 frequencies: 500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz**.
- **HRCT** detects bony malformations; **MRI** confirms nerve integrity.
- **Contraindications:** absent cochlear nerve, complete ossification, active infection, anaesthetic unfitness.
- **Paediatric patients** must receive **pneumococcal and meningococcal** vaccinations pre-surgery.
- **Post-lingual** deafness has better outcomes than **pre-lingual** deafness.
- **Family support** is the single most important prognostic factor in paediatric cases.
- **ABI (Auditory Brainstem Implant)** is the alternative when the cochlear nerve is absent.
- **EAS (Electric Acoustic Stimulation)** hybrid implants are selected for patients with residual low-frequency hearing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Versus Integrated CI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple Versus Integrated CI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology

In the context of **Cochlear Implant (CI)** engineering, the classification of **"Multiple versus Integrated CI"** refers to the two fundamental hardware-architectural philosophies used in the design of the **External Sound Processor (ESP)** and the **Implanted Receiver-Stimulator (IRS)** of a cochlear implant system.

- **Multiple CI (Discrete / Multi-Component Architecture):** A design approach in which the cochlear implant system is constructed from **several discrete, functionally separated integrated circuits (ICs)** or processor modules. Each IC is dedicated to a specific signal-processing task (e.g., one chip for the analog front-end, another for the filterbank, another for the coding strategy, and another for the RF telemetry/stimulation driver). These chips are connected on a Printed Circuit Board (PCB) via inter-chip buses.

- **Integrated CI (System-on-Chip / SoC Architecture):** A design approach where the **entire signal-processing chain** — from microphone analog pre-amplification, through Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC), digital filterbank, envelope/pulsatile coding, stimulation current generation, RF telemetry decoder, and even the back-telemetry uplink — is consolidated onto a **single silicon die** (or a small set of dies in a Multi-Chip Module / MCM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The terms "Multiple" and "Integrated" in this module **do not refer to the number of electrodes** in the electrode array (which would be "single-channel" vs "multi-channel"). They refer to the **degree of hardware consolidation** of the processing electronics. The KTU examiner expects this distinction explicitly in 7-mark answers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **professional restaurant kitchen** preparing a complex meal for a deaf patient to "hear" again:

- **Multiple CI = The Old Brigade System:** Picture a kitchen with **10 different specialty chefs** working in separate rooms. One chef only chops, another only boils, another only grills, and a runner has to carry dishes between rooms. They are **individually excellent** (you can swap the grilling chef for a better one easily), but the kitchen is **huge, consumes enormous power (gas/electricity), and the food delivery is slower**. If one chef is sick, a substitute can be brought in easily. This is the **early Cochlear Nucleus 22, Med-El Combi 40+, and Advanced Bionics Clarion** generation.

- **Integrated CI = The Modern Master-Chef System:** Now picture **one master chef** who chops, boils, grills, and plates — all in **one compact, ultra-efficient kitchenette**. The kitchen is **tiny, runs on a small battery, and produces the meal in milliseconds**. Swapping out individual skills is harder, but the **reliability, power efficiency, and small form factor** are unmatched. This is the philosophy behind modern **Cochlear Ltd. (Kanso 2, Nucleus 7), MED-EL (Sonnet 2 / Rondo 3), and Advanced Bionics (Marvel CI)** sound processors.

> [!NOTE]
> The cochlear implant sits **behind the ear or fully under the skin**. Size and battery life are critical. This is *why* the industry has shifted dramatically from Multiple → Integrated architectures over the last 25 years.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics

The following **bold** metrics are universally accepted benchmarks in cochlear implant design and must be memorized for KTU exams:

| Parameter | Multiple CI (Legacy) | Integrated CI (Modern SoC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Typical Power Dissipation** | **150 – 400 mW** | **5 – 30 mW** |
| **Form Factor Volume** | **> 30 cm³** (body-worn) | **< 3 cm³** (single-unit BTE) |
| **Number of Active Silicon Dies** | **4 – 8 dies** | **1 die (SoC) or 2 (MCM)** |
| **Clock Frequency (DSP core)** | **1 – 10 MHz** | **10 – 80 MHz** (low-power MCU) |
| **Typical Process Node** | **0.5 µm – 1.0 µm CMOS** | **40 nm – 65 nm CMOS** |

## 1.4 Module Context

Within **Module 2 of PEBRT745 (Assistive Medical Devices)**, this topic sits at the intersection of *biomedical signal processing* and *VLSI/biomedical electronics*. It directly follows the study of **electrode arrays, coding strategies (CIS, ACE, HiRes, FSP), and stimulation paradigms**, and is the **conceptual bridge** to later modules on **fully-implantable CIs** and **neural recording implants** (like the Utah Array and BrainGate).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture Decomposition — The Signal Chain

A cochlear implant performs a fixed sequence of operations. How these operations are **partitioned across silicon** is what defines Multiple vs. Integrated CI.

The canonical CI signal chain is:

$$\text{Microphone} \rightarrow \text{Pre-Amp} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Filterbank} \rightarrow \text{Coding Strategy} \rightarrow \text{Stimulation Current Generator} \rightarrow \text{Electrode Array}$$

### 2.1.1 Multiple CI — The Partitioned Architecture

In a **Multiple CI** system, this chain is broken into **4 to 6 separate ICs**:

**Step 1 — Analog Front-End (AFE) IC**
- Handles the **microphone electret** signal, typically with a **20 Hz – 8 kHz** bandwidth.
- Performs pre-amplification with **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)**.
- Contains a **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ) ADC** to convert analog audio into a 16-bit or 24-bit digital stream.
- **Why separate?** The analog circuitry is **noisy**. Keeping it physically distant from digital switching circuits reduces **crosstalk** and power-supply noise injection.

**Step 2 — Digital Signal Processor (DSP) Chip**
- A general-purpose **DSP** (e.g., TMS320 series, or a custom ASIC like the **Spartan-class** early processors).
- Executes the **filterbank** (typically an FFT or a bank of bandpass filters, often **16–22 channels** for CIS/ACE).
- Implements the **coding strategy** (CIS, ACE, SAS, FSP) in software/firmware.
- **Why separate?** Allows the firmware to be updated or replaced without redesigning the analog or stimulation hardware — critical for clinical research and regulatory upgrades.

**Step 3 — Stimulation Current Driver IC**
- Generates the **biphasic, charge-balanced current pulses** that drive the electrodes.
- Typical parameters: **pulse amplitude 0 – 1.75 mA**, **pulse width 25 – 400 µs**, **compliance voltage up to ±8 V**.
- **Why separate?** This IC must handle **high voltages (HV)** for stimulation, which requires a different fabrication process (e.g., **DMOS** or **BCD process** at 0.35 µm or larger) incompatible with low-voltage digital logic.

**Step 4 — RF Telemetry / Transcutaneous Link IC**
- Encodes/decodes the **inductively-coupled RF link** (typically at **5 MHz** for backward telemetry, **49–50 MHz** for forward telemetry in modern systems; older systems used **2.5 MHz / 10 MHz**).
- Manages **impedance measurement**, **Neural Response Telemetry (NRT)**, and **Electrically Evoked Compound Action Potential (ECAP)** recording.

**Step 5 — Power Management IC**
- Regulates the **inductively-received power** from the external coil.
- Stores energy in **decoupling capacitors** and may include a **rechargeable Li-ion cell** in body-worn processors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why was Multiple CI the standard for ~25 years?** The technology simply did not exist to put a low-noise analog front-end, a 16-bit ADC, a 50 MHz DSP, and a high-voltage compliance stimulation driver on the **same silicon die**. The processes were fundamentally incompatible (HV CMOS vs. low-power CMOS, analog precision vs. digital density).

---

### 2.1.2 Integrated CI — The System-on-Chip (SoC) Revolution

An **Integrated CI** consolidates the entire chain into **one or two dies**, using a **mixed-signal SoC** built on a modern low-power CMOS process (typically **40 nm to 65 nm**).

**Step 1 — Single Mixed-Signal SoC**
- Contains: low-noise AFE + Σ-Δ ADC + low-power ARM Cortex-M-class core + filterbank accelerator + current stimulator + RF baseband + power management.
- The **stimulation driver** is often built in a **higher-voltage island** within the SoC (using thick-oxide I/O devices) to provide the ±8 V compliance required for safe neural stimulation, while the rest of the chip runs at **0.9 V – 1.2 V** core voltage.

**Step 2 — Why the Shift?**
- **Power efficiency:** A modern SoC can perform the same operation at **1/10th to 1/20th** of the power because there is **no inter-chip bus driving large PCB traces** (which capacitively loads the signal).
- **Form factor:** Enables **single-unit off-the-ear sound processors** like the **Cochlear Kanso 2** and **MED-EL Rondo 3**, which sit as a single disc on the scalp.
- **Reliability:** Fewer solder joints, fewer connectors, fewer failure modes. Critical for a **medical device expected to function for 20+ years inside a human body**.
- **Manufacturing cost:** One die to test and package vs. five.

**Step 3 — Trade-offs Introduced**
- **Reduced flexibility:** Once the filterbank is hard-wired (synthesized as a hardware accelerator), changing the coding strategy requires a **full silicon re-spin** (millions of dollars and 12+ months).
- **Analog performance compromise:** Putting the noisy digital switching on the same die as the sensitive microphone pre-amp requires careful **substrate isolation, deep n-well, and guard rings** to prevent digital switching noise from corrupting the analog signal (target SNR: **> 90 dB** for high-fidelity audio coding).
- **Mixed-voltage process complexity:** Integrating a 12 V-tolerant stimulation output with a 1.0 V core on the same die demands **stepped oxide**, **DTI (Deep Trench Isolation)**, and careful **electrostatic discharge (ESD)** clamping at every pad.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Relation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Charge per stimulation phase** | $Q = I_{\text{stim}} \times t_{\text{pw}}$ | $Q$ in **Coulombs (C)**, $I_{\text{stim}}$ in **Amps**, $t_{\text{pw}}$ in **seconds** |
| **Charge density (safety limit)** | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{elec}}}$ | $D$ in **µC/cm²** (Shannon limit: $\vert D \vert \le 30\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$ per phase) |
| **Power budget per channel** | $P_{\text{ch}} = V_{\text{cc}} \times I_{\text{stim}} \times f_{\text{stim}}$ | $P_{\text{ch}}$ in **mW**, $f_{\text{stim}}$ in **pulses/sec (pps)** |
| **Total SoC dynamic power** | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{L}} \cdot V_{\text{DD}}^2 \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$ | $\alpha$ switching activity, $C_{\text{L}}$ load cap, $V_{\text{DD}}$ supply, $f_{\text{clk}}$ clock |
| **Voltage scaling benefit** | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^2$ | If $V$ halves, power drops to **1/4** — the **#1 driver** of Integrated CI |
| **Energy per audio frame** | $E_{\text{frame}} = P_{\text{avg}} \times T_{\text{frame}}$ | $T_{\text{frame}}$ = **22.7 µs** for 44.1 kHz audio |
| **Effective number of channels** | $N_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{BW_{\text{audio}}}{BW_{\text{filter}}}$ | $BW_{\text{audio}}$ $\approx$ **8 kHz**; $BW_{\text{filter}}$ for 22 channels $\approx$ **360 Hz** |
| **Stimulation rate per channel** | $R_{\text{ch}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{active}}}$ | Typically **900 – 5000 pps** per channel |
| **RF forward telemetry carrier** | $f_{\text{RF, fwd}} \approx 49\ \text{MHz}$ (modern) | Older systems: **10 MHz** |
| **RF back-telemetry (NRT)** | $f_{\text{RF, back}} \approx 5\ \text{MHz}$ | Used for impedance / ECAP measurement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Shannon Safety Limit** ($D \le 30\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$) is the single most important equation for CI safety. If you violate it, you will generate **toxic electrochemical byproducts** (e.g., H₂, Cl₂, metal ions) at the electrode-tissue interface and cause **neural damage**. **Mention this limit in every CI safety answer.**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Multiple → Integrated transition is **not unique to cochlear implants**. It is the same architectural transition that occurred in:
- **Pacemakers** (multiple discrete ICs in the 1980s → single-chip SoC with HV output in modern devices like Medtronic Azure XT)
- **Insulin pumps** (separate microcontroller, AFE, and motor driver → Medtronic 780G integrated platform)
- **Cochlear implants** (the canonical case study for KTU Module 2)

For a KTU graduate, the engineering takeaway is: **when the technology node allows, always integrate — but only if the analog performance is not compromised.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Integrated CI Consumes Less Power — The Voltage-Scaling Argument

The most important quantitative argument for Integrated CI is derived from CMOS dynamic power dissipation. Let us derive it rigorously.

**Step 1: Define dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS gate.**

When a CMOS inverter switches at a frequency $f_{\text{clk}}$ with a load capacitance $C_{\text{L}}$ from a supply $V_{\text{DD}}$, each switching event charges the capacitor to $V_{\text{DD}}$, storing energy $\tfrac{1}{2} C_{\text{L}} V_{\text{DD}}^2$ in the capacitor and dissipating an equal $\tfrac{1}{2} C_{\text{L}} V_{\text{DD}}^2$ in the PMOS pull-up transistor. Therefore, **per transition**, the energy dissipated is:

$$E_{\text{transition}} = \frac{1}{2} C_{\text{L}} V_{\text{DD}}^2$$

**Step 2: Account for switching activity.**

Not every clock cycle produces a transition. Define the **switching activity factor** $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ (typically $\alpha \approx 0.1$ for real audio DSP). The average power per gate is therefore:

$$P_{\text{gate}} = \alpha \cdot f_{\text{clk}} \cdot C_{\text{L}} \cdot V_{\text{DD}}^2$$

**Step 3: Sum over N gates to get total dynamic power.**

For the entire SoC containing $N$ logic gates (each with effective load cap $C_{\text{L}}$):

$$P_{\text{dyn, total}} = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{L}} \cdot V_{\text{DD}}^2 \cdot f_{\text{clk}} \cdot N$$

**Step 4: Compare Multiple vs. Integrated CI numerically.**

Assume both systems perform the same DSP task, with the following representative parameters:

| Parameter | Multiple CI (Legacy) | Integrated CI (Modern SoC) |
|---|---|---|
| Supply voltage $V_{\text{DD}}$ | **3.3 V** | **1.0 V** |
| Clock frequency $f_{\text{clk}}$ | **5 MHz** | **40 MHz** |
| Effective gate count $N$ | $5 \times 10^5$ (across 4 dies) | $2 \times 10^6$ (single die, smaller geometry) |
| Load cap (per fan-out) | $C_{\text{L}} = 50\ \text{fF}$ (PCB-trace + bond-pad) | $C_{\text{L}} = 5\ \text{fF}$ (on-die local interconnect) |
| Activity factor $\alpha$ | 0.15 | 0.15 |

Computing the dynamic power for each:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{dyn, Multiple}} &= 0.15 \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times (3.3)^2 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times (5 \times 10^5) \\
&= 0.15 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 10.89 \times 2.5 \times 10^{12} \\
&= 0.15 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 27.225 \times 10^{12} \\
&\approx 0.15 \times 1.361 \times 10^{-1} \\
&\approx 2.04 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{W} \\
&\approx \mathbf{20.4\ mW}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{dyn, Integrated}} &= 0.15 \times (5 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.0)^2 \times (40 \times 10^6) \times (2 \times 10^6) \\
&= 0.15 \times 5 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.0 \times 8 \times 10^{13} \\
&= 0.15 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 6.0 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{W} \\
&= \mathbf{60\ mW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Wait — that seems wrong! Integrated should be lower power!**

This is a **classic KTU trap question** and a frequent student pitfall. The above result is **counterintuitive** but accurate *for raw dynamic power only*. The actual power savings of Integrated CI come from three additional factors:

**(a) The inter-chip bus driver power** that the Multiple CI must spend — these **PCB traces** have capacitances of 5–20 pF per net (vs. 5 fF on-die), and the I/O buffer transistors are huge (50 µm wide). This "**I/O tax**" typically adds **100 – 200 mW** in a Multiple CI and is **completely eliminated** in the Integrated CI.

**(b) The clock frequency reduction enabled by hardware acceleration:** Once the filterbank is a **dedicated hardware accelerator** (FFT engine) on the SoC, the system does not need a 40 MHz general-purpose CPU. The same task can run at 5 MHz, dropping the integrated power to:

$$P_{\text{integrated, accelerated}} = 0.15 \times 5 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.0 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times (2 \times 10^6) = 7.5\ \text{mW}$$

**(c) The voltage scaling of the actual peripheral blocks** (ADC, eFlash, etc.) that all benefit from the lower $V_{\text{DD}}$.

**Step 6: Net real-world power comparison.**

| System | Total Estimated Power |
|---|---|
| Multiple CI (legacy, with I/O tax) | **~ 200 – 400 mW** |
| Integrated CI (modern SoC) | **~ 5 – 30 mW** |

The **~10x – 20x** power reduction is the engineering *raison d'être* for the integrated architecture.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Multiple vs. Integrated CI

This matrix is a **direct KTU-answer-ready summary**. Memorize it for 14-mark questions.

| Design Attribute | **Multiple CI** (Discrete Architecture) | **Integrated CI** (SoC Architecture) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of ICs** | 4 – 8 separate dies | 1 die (SoC) or 2 (MCM) |
| **Power consumption** | **150 – 400 mW** | **5 – 30 mW** |
| **Volume / form factor** | Large, body-worn BTE | Compact, off-the-ear or fully implantable |
| **Flexibility** | High — coding strategy can be updated via firmware flash | Low — coding strategy hard-wired; updates require mask re-spin |
| **Time to clinical trial** | **6 – 12 months** (firmware only) | **18 – 36 months** (full silicon re-spin) |
| **Manufacturing cost (per unit)** | Lower (commodity ICs) at low volume | Higher NRE (~$2 – 5 M mask cost), lower per-unit at high volume |
| **Reliability / MTBF** | Lower (more solder joints, connectors) | Higher (single package, no inter-chip buses) |
| **Analog noise performance** | Excellent (analog and digital are physically separated) | Challenging (requires deep-n-well, DTI, guard rings) |
| **Stimulus compliance voltage** | Easy (separate HV process) | Requires thick-oxide I/O or on-die charge pump |
| **Suitable for fully implantable CI?** | No — too large & power-hungry | **Yes — the only viable path** |
| **Clinical example (legacy)** | Cochlear Nucleus 22, AB Clarion CII | — |
| **Clinical example (modern)** | — | **Cochlear Nucleus 7 / Kanso 2, MED-EL Sonnet 2, AB Marvel** |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Shannon Charge-Density Safety Limit

The Shannon limit governs **all** CI electrode designs, regardless of Multiple or Integrated architecture. Derivation:

**Step 1:** Charge per phase = $Q = I_{\text{stim}} \cdot t_{\text{pw}}$.

**Step 2:** Electrode surface area $A_{\text{elec}}$. For a typical banded electrode (e.g., MED-EL standard), $A \approx 0.2\ \text{mm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$.

**Step 3:** Charge density $D = Q / A_{\text{elec}}$.

**Step 4:** Shannon (1992) experimentally determined that **safe** neural stimulation (no electrochemical damage, no gas evolution, no metal-ion dissolution) requires:

$$D \le 30\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2 \text{ (per phase, cathodic first)}$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the maximum allowed $I \cdot t_{\text{pw}}$ product:

$$I_{\text{stim}} \cdot t_{\text{pw}} \le D_{\max} \cdot A_{\text{elec}} = (30 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C/cm}^2)(2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{stim}} \cdot t_{\text{pw}} &\le (30 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2 \times 10^{-2})\ \text{C} \\
&\le 6 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{C} \\
&\le \mathbf{600\ nC\ per\ phase}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Cross-check with clinical typical values. For $t_{\text{pw}} = 100\ \mu\text{s}$:

$$I_{\text{stim, max}} = \frac{600\ \text{nC}}{100\ \mu\text{s}} = 6\ \text{mA}$$

This is the **theoretical maximum**. In practice, CIs limit $I_{\text{stim}} \le 1.75\ \text{mA}$ to leave a wide safety margin — a fact worth stating in any KTU answer on CI safety.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multiple CI

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the discrete-component partitioning of a legacy Multiple CI system. Note that every node ID is alphanumeric and all labels are plain uppercase text (no markdown formatting inside quotes) per the protocol safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[MICROPHONE] --> B1[AFE IC<br/>PREAMP + AGC]
    B1 --> C1[SIGMA DELTA ADC<br/>16 to 24 BIT]
    C1 --> D1[DSP CHIP<br/>TMS320 CLASS]
    D1 --> E1[FILTERBANK<br/>16 to 22 CHANNELS]
    E1 --> F1[CODING STRATEGY<br/>CIS OR ACE OR FSP]
    F1 --> G1[STIMULATION DRIVER IC<br/>HV CMOS BCD PROCESS]
    G1 --> H1[ELECTRODE ARRAY<br/>IN COCHLEA]
    D1 -.-> I1[RF TELEMETRY IC<br/>5 MHZ BACK 49 MHZ FWD]
    I1 <--> J1[EXTERNAL PROCESSOR COIL]
    J1 <--> K1[IMPLANT COIL]
    B1 --> P1[POWER MANAGEMENT IC]
    C1 --> P1
    D1 --> P1
    G1 --> P1
    I1 --> P1
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Integrated CI (SoC)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the consolidated architecture. Notice that **all digital, analog, and HV stimulation functions are now within one SoC**, with only the microphone, RF coils, and electrode array remaining off-chip.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SOC[INTEGRATED CI SOC SINGLE DIE 40 to 65 NM CMOS]
        A2[LOW NOISE AFE<br/>MIC PREAMP AGC] --> B2[SIGMA DELTA ADC<br/>16 BIT]
        B2 --> C2[ARM CORTEX M0 PLUS CORE<br/>AT 10 to 40 MHZ]
        C2 --> D2[FFT FILTERBANK ACCELERATOR<br/>22 CHANNELS HARDWIRED]
        D2 --> E2[CODING STRATEGY BLOCK<br/>CIS ACE FSP]
        E2 --> F2[HV STIMULATION DRIVER<br/>THICK OXIDE ISLAND 12V]
        F2 --> G2[OUTPUT ESD CLAMPS<br/>AND CHARGE BALANCE]
        C2 -.-> H2[RF BASEBAND<br/>5 MHZ 49 MHZ]
        H2 --> I2[TELEMETRY DECODER<br/>ECAP NRT]
        A2 --> J2[ON DIE POWER MANAGEMENT<br/>LDO AND CHARGE PUMP]
        C2 --> J2
        F2 --> J2
    end
    A2 --> M1[OFF CHIP MICROPHONE]
    G2 --> M2[ELECTRODE ARRAY<br/>IN COCHLEA]
    H2 <--> M3[EXTERNAL COIL<br/>INDUCTIVE LINK]
    M3 <--> M4[EXTERNAL SOUND PROCESSOR<br/>BATTERY AND MIC]
```

## 4.3 Architectural Comparison — Functional Mapping

The following Mermaid diagram provides a **side-by-side mapping** of the same function performed by a discrete IC in Multiple CI vs. a block in Integrated CI.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEG[LEGACY MULTIPLE CI 4 to 8 DIES]
        L1[AFE IC] --- L2[ADC IC] --- L3[DSP IC] --- L4[STIM DRIVER IC] --- L5[RF TELEMETRY IC]
    end
    subgraph MOD[MODERN INTEGRATED CI 1 SOC]
        M1[AFE BLOCK] --- M2[ADC BLOCK] --- M3[CPU CORE] --- M4[STIM DRIVER HV ISLAND] --- M5[RF BASEBAND]
    end
    LEG -. FUNCTIONALLY EQUIVALENT .-> MOD
```

## 4.4 Design-Decision Flow — When to Choose Multiple vs. Integrated

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NEW CI DESIGN START] --> B{TARGET<br/>FULLY IMPLANTABLE?}
    B -- YES --> C[INTEGRATED SOC MANDATORY<br/>POWER LESS THAN 30 MW]
    B -- NO --> D{CLINICAL FLEXIBILITY<br/>REQUIRED?}
    D -- YES --> E[USE MULTIPLE CI<br/>WITH EXTERNAL DSP<br/>UPDATABLE FIRMWARE]
    D -- NO --> F{PRODUCTION VOLUME<br/>GREATER THAN 50 K PER YEAR?}
    F -- YES --> G[INTEGRATED SOC<br/>AMORTIZE 2 to 5 M USD NRE]
    F -- NO --> H[MULTIPLE CI<br/>LOWER UPFRONT COST<br/>SHORTER TIME TO MARKET]
    C --> I[END]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the flowchart for KTU answers:** All modern commercial CIs (post-2015) take the **YES → Integrated SoC** path because the patient demand for **invisible, all-day-wear, fully-implantable** sound processors is overwhelming. The research-grade and custom clinical fittings still use a Multiple-architecture sound processor for flexibility.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**"Differentiate between Multiple CI and Integrated CI architectures in cochlear implants. Give one commercial example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Multiple CI | Integrated CI |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Discrete ICs — separate AFE, ADC, DSP, HV stimulator, and RF ICs on a PCB | Single mixed-signal SoC containing all the above blocks on one die |
| Power | High (150–400 mW) | Low (5–30 mW) |
| Example | Cochlear Nucleus 22 (legacy), Advanced Bionics Clarion CII | Cochlear Nucleus 7 / Kanso 2, MED-EL Sonnet 2 |

*Allocation: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the power/form-factor difference, 1 mark for the example.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**"State the Shannon charge-density safety limit for cochlear implant electrode stimulation. What is its significance in integrated CI design?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Shannon limit states that the charge density at the electrode-tissue interface must satisfy:

$$D = \frac{I_{\text{stim}} \cdot t_{\text{pw}}}{A_{\text{elec}}} \le 30\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2 \text{ per phase}$$

**Significance in Integrated CI:** When the HV stimulator is integrated on the same die as low-voltage digital logic, the design must still guarantee this safety constraint. This drives the use of **thick-oxide I/O transistors**, **on-die charge pumps**, and **charge-balanced biphasic current waveforms** — all of which must be carefully designed to avoid violating the Shannon limit during any fault condition. (1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the value, 1 mark for the integrated-CI significance.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Multiple CI** system. List the major discrete ICs and the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe an **Integrated CI (SoC)** system. Highlight three key engineering challenges in integrating the HV stimulation driver with low-voltage digital logic on a single die. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A **Multiple CI** system is built from 4–6 discrete integrated circuits mounted on a small PCB inside the behind-the-ear (BTE) or body-worn sound processor case. The major ICs are:

1. **Analog Front-End (AFE) IC (1.5 marks):** Accepts the signal from the electret microphone, provides low-noise pre-amplification (gain 40–60 dB), and performs Automatic Gain Control (AGC) with attack/release times of ~5 ms / 50 ms to map the 100 dB dynamic range of human hearing into a 16-bit digital range.

2. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) IC (1 mark):** A Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ) ADC sampling at **44.1 kHz** (or higher oversampling rates such as **5.6 MHz** with internal decimation) producing 16- to 24-bit audio samples.

3. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP) IC (2 marks):** A programmable DSP (e.g., early implementations used the **Texas Instruments TMS320C5x** family) that executes in real-time: the FFT/filterbank (16–22 channels across 250 Hz – 8 kHz), envelope detection (full-wave rectification + low-pass filtering), amplitude mapping (logarithmic compression mapping the 100 dB input to ~20 dB of electric dynamic range), and the chosen coding strategy (CIS, ACE, SAS, or FSP).

4. **Stimulation Current Driver IC (1.5 marks):** Generates the **biphasic, charge-balanced current pulses** that are delivered to the electrode array via the RF-coupled transcutaneous link. This IC is built in a **BCD (Bipolar-CMOS-DMOS) process** to provide high compliance voltage (±8 V) and precise current sources (8-bit resolution, 0–1.75 mA).

5. **RF Telemetry IC (0.5 marks):** Handles the **inductive transcutaneous link** at 5 MHz (back) and 49 MHz (forward) for both data and power transmission.

6. **Power Management IC (0.5 marks):** Regulates the power received from the external coil and distributes it to the other ICs.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram** (refer to the Mermaid figure in Section 4.2).

**Three key engineering challenges in integrating the HV stimulation driver with low-voltage digital logic on a single die:**

**Challenge 1 — Mixed-Voltage Process Complexity (2.5 marks):** The HV stimulator requires transistors that tolerate ±12 V across their drain-source to deliver adequate compliance for high-impedance electrode contacts. The DSP core, however, runs at 0.9–1.2 V on **40 nm or 65 nm CMOS** for power efficiency. The solution is to use **thick-oxide I/O transistors** (typically 0.18 µm or 0.25 µm oxide, instead of the core 1.2 V thin-oxide) confined to a dedicated **HV island** on the die, isolated from the core logic by deep n-wells and guard rings. This adds **2–3 mask layers** and **2–5 million USD** in NRE cost.

**Challenge 2 — Substrate Noise Coupling (2.5 marks):** The digital DSP switching injects noise into the common p-substrate. This noise can corrupt the sensitive microphone preamp and the ADC's reference voltage, degrading SNR from a target of > 90 dB to as low as 60 dB. Mitigation techniques: **deep trench isolation (DTI)**, **dedicated analog ground planes**, **separate analog and digital supply pins**, **differential signaling** for the microphone, and **chopper-stabilized** analog front-ends.

**Challenge 3 — ESD and Latch-up Protection at the Electrode Interface (2 marks):** The electrode pads are exposed to the human body and can receive electrostatic discharge pulses of **±8 kV contact / ±15 kV air discharge** (per IEC 60601-1). The on-die ESD clamps must sink this energy without entering **CMOS latch-up** (a destructive thyristor-like short between V_DD and GND). The solution is to use **dual-diode ESD clamps with SCR (silicon-controlled-rectifier) structures**, and to add **software-controlled safe-mode** that disables stimulation upon detecting an ESD event.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare the power dissipation of a **Multiple CI** and an **Integrated CI** performing the same filterbank task. Use the CMOS dynamic-power equation and show that integrated architectures achieve an order-of-magnitude power reduction despite higher raw $CV^2f$ on the SoC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss why **fully-implantable cochlear implants** are technologically infeasible with Multiple CI architecture, and how Integrated CI has made them possible. Mention any two commercial or research-grade fully-implantable CI systems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**CMOS dynamic power equation:**

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{L}} \cdot V_{\text{DD}}^2 \cdot f_{\text{clk}} \cdot N$$

For a Multiple CI: $V_{\text{DD}} = 3.3\ \text{V}$, $f_{\text{clk}} = 5\ \text{MHz}$, $N = 5 \times 10^5$ gates, $C_{\text{L}} = 50\ \text{fF}$ (PCB-trace loaded) per gate.

Computing:
$$P_{\text{dyn, Multiple}} \approx 20.4\ \text{mW (per earlier derivation)}$$

**However**, this is *not* the total system power. The Multiple CI must also drive inter-chip bus pins through relatively large **I/O buffers** (50 µm wide transistors) with PCB trace capacitances of 5–20 pF. Each bus transition consumes:

$$E_{\text{IO}} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{\text{trace}} V_{\text{DD}}^2$$

For $C_{\text{trace}} = 10\ \text{pF}$, $V_{\text{DD}} = 3.3\ \text{V}$, at a bus rate of 10 MHz with 16 active bus lines:

$$P_{\text{IO tax}} = 10 \times 10^{-12} \times (3.3)^2 \times 10^7 \times 16 \approx 174\ \text{mW}$$

This **I/O tax** alone (174 mW) is **5–10x the entire SoC power** and accounts for the bulk of the Multiple CI's power dissipation. (**3 marks** for the equation + I/O tax argument)

For the **Integrated CI**, this I/O tax is **eliminated** because all inter-block communication is on-die with $C_{\text{L}} = 5\ \text{fF}$ and $V_{\text{DD}} = 1.0\ \text{V}$. Furthermore, the filterbank is implemented as a **hardware accelerator** (FFT engine) that runs at 5 MHz, not the full 40 MHz. The dynamic power is therefore:

$$P_{\text{dyn, Integrated}} \approx 7.5\ \text{mW (per earlier derivation)}$$

Total system power (including peripherals) is **< 30 mW**. (**2 marks** for the integrated SoC calculation)

**Net reduction: 200 mW → 30 mW = ~7–10x.** The reduction comes *not* from the $CV^2f$ term on the core logic, but from **eliminating the I/O tax** and **enabling hardware acceleration + voltage scaling**. (**2 marks** for the conclusion)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

A **fully-implantable CI** (FICl) has **no external sound processor**. The microphone, sound processor, power source (rechargeable battery), and stimulator are **all inside the body**, under the skin. The fundamental requirements are:

- **Power budget < 30 mW continuous** (so the implanted battery can last 8–16 hours per charge and have a 10-year lifetime).
- **Volume < 3 cm³** (to fit on the mastoid bone).
- **Hermeticity for 20+ years** (single-package electronics with no inter-chip connectors).
- **No external coil** (an implanted microphone, typically mounted on the incus or under the skin of the ear canal, picks up sound).

With a **Multiple CI** architecture, these requirements are **impossible** to meet because:
- The discrete ICs together consume > 150 mW — the implanted battery would drain in < 2 hours. (**2 marks**)
- The PCB with 4–8 ICs is > 30 cm³ — far too large to implant. (**1 mark**)
- The inter-chip connectors are the #1 failure mode in long-term implants (corrosion, fluid ingress). (**1 mark**)

**Integrated CI (SoC) enables FICl** by reducing power to < 30 mW, volume to < 1 cm³, and providing a single hermetic package. (**1 mark**)

**Two commercial / research FICl systems:**

1. **Cochlear Ltd. — Profile Plus / Nucleus Profile Plus II (research collaboration with the University of Melbourne):** Aimed at fully-implantable sound processors using advanced low-power SoC designs. (1 mark)

2. **MED-EL — Total Cochlear Implant (research project under the EU Horizon programme):** Investigating a fully-implantable CI with a subcutaneous microphone and integrated SoC sound processor. (1 mark)

*(Additional valid example: Envoy Medical's "Esteem" — a fully-implantable middle-ear transducer, often used as a comparative example in KTU.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Multiple" with "Multi-Channel":** The single biggest error students make is to interpret "Multiple CI" as a cochlear implant with multiple (e.g., 22) electrode channels. **It does not.** It refers to multiple *processing ICs*. A 22-channel CI can be either Multiple (discrete ICs) or Integrated (SoC). If you write this in your exam, expect a **2-mark deduction** immediately.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Shannon limit:** Any question on CI stimulation safety that omits the $D \le 30\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$ limit is marked down **1–2 marks**. Always quote the number and the unit.
> 
> 3. **Drawing a block diagram with arrows that don't make sense:** In 7-mark sub-parts, your block diagram carries **2–3 marks by itself**. Make sure every block has a **name, an arrow direction, and is connected**. The Mermaid diagrams in Section 4 are the model.
> 
> 4. **Stating power values without justification:** Saying "Integrated CI uses less power" without showing the $V_{\text{DD}}^2$ scaling argument or the elimination of the I/O tax is worth only **partial credit** (~1/3 marks for that sub-part).
> 
> 5. **Confusing NRT and ECAP:** Neural Response Telemetry (NRT) is the **Cochlear-brand name** for ECAP (Electrically Evoked Compound Action Potential) measurement. In KTU answers, use **ECAP** for generic discussion and **NRT** only when specifically referring to Cochlear devices.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **"Multiple" and "Integrated" refer to the number of processing ICs, NOT the number of electrode channels.**
- [x] **Multiple CI = 4–8 discrete ICs** on a PCB; uses **150–400 mW**.
- [x] **Integrated CI = single mixed-signal SoC** (40–65 nm CMOS); uses **5–30 mW**.
- [x] The power reduction comes **mainly from eliminating the inter-chip I/O tax**, *not* from raw $CV^2f$ scaling.
- [x] **Voltage scaling** ($V_{\text{DD}}$ from 3.3 V → 1.0 V) gives a **9x dynamic power reduction** by itself.
- [x] **Hardware acceleration** of the filterbank (FFT engine) on the SoC lets the system run at lower clock frequency (~5 MHz) — further reducing power.
- [x] **Shannon limit** for safe neural stimulation: $D \le 30\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$ per phase.
- [x] Typical CI stimulation parameters: $I_{\text{stim}} \le 1.75\ \text{mA}$, $t_{\text{pw}} = 25$–$400\ \mu\text{s}$, $f_{\text{stim}} = 900$–$5000$ pps per channel.
- [x] Modern commercial examples: **Cochlear Nucleus 7 / Kanso 2, MED-EL Sonnet 2 / Rondo 3, Advanced Bionics Marvel CI**.
- [x] Legacy multiple-architecture examples: **Cochlear Nucleus 22, Advanced Bionics Clarion CII**.
- [x] Fully-implantable CIs are **only feasible with Integrated SoC architecture**.
- [x] Three integration challenges: **mixed-voltage process (HV I/O), substrate noise coupling (DTI/guard rings), and ESD/latch-up protection at the electrode pads**.
- [x] Key formulas to memorize: $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C_{\text{L}} V_{\text{DD}}^2 f N$, $Q = I \cdot t_{\text{pw}}$, $D = Q / A$, $N_{\text{eff}} = BW_{\text{audio}} / BW_{\text{filter}}$.
- [x] RF telemetry: **49 MHz forward, 5 MHz back** (modern); older systems used **10 MHz / 2.5 MHz**.
- [x] ECAP and NRT are the **same measurement** (manufacturer-specific naming) — use ECAP in generic answers.
- [x] Always finish a 14-mark answer with a **forward-looking conclusion** mentioning the trend toward fully-implantable, neural-recording, and closed-loop adaptive CIs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of CI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cochlear Implant (CI) Evaluation** is the comprehensive, multi-stage clinical and technical assessment procedure used to determine candidacy for implantation, ensure proper intra-operative device function, and quantify post-operative auditory performance and patient outcomes. It encompasses **pre-operative, intra-operative, and post-operative assessment protocols** governed by international clinical standards (NICE, FDA, HEARRING network consensus).

The evaluation framework is built upon three pillars:

1. **Audiological/Diagnostic Evaluation** – measuring auditory thresholds and speech understanding capacity.
2. **Surgical/Intra-operative Evaluation** – confirming electrode placement, neural interface integrity, and device safety.
3. **Rehabilitative/Outcome Evaluation** – quantifying the functional benefit of the implant using standardized speech perception and quality-of-life instruments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Telephone Repair Shop":**
> Imagine a person who has lost the wiring in their home telephone (deafened auditory nerve). A cochlear implant is essentially installing a brand-new, fully functional telephone line (the electrode array) that bypasses the broken wires. But before installing, the technician must:
> 1. **Diagnose the fault** (audiological testing) — is the line really broken, or just weak?
> 2. **Test the new line during installation** (intra-operative neural response telemetry) — does the signal actually reach the other end?
> 3. **Tune the volume and clarity** after installation (mapping/programming) — find the right settings for comfortable, clear conversation.
> 4. **Check long-term customer satisfaction** (outcome measures) — is the person actually making phone calls comfortably?

This multi-stage approach is precisely what **CI Evaluation** entails.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **dB HL (Hearing Level)**: Reference scale (0 dB HL = normal adult hearing threshold).
- **dB SPL (Sound Pressure Level)**: Absolute physical sound pressure (20 µPa = 0 dB SPL).
- **CAP (Categories of Auditory Performance)**: 0–9 scale for hearing function.
- **SIR (Speech Intelligibility Rating)**: 1–5 scale for speech production.
- **CNC words**: Consonant-Nucleus-Consonant monosyllabic word test.
- **HINT**: Hearing in Noise Test (sentence recognition in noise).
- **BKB-SIN**: Bamford-Kowal-Bench Speech-in-Noise test.
- **Impedance (kΩ)**: Electrical resistance of electrode-tissue interface.
- **T-level (T)**: Threshold level — minimum current producing audible sensation.
- **C-level (M-level)**: Comfortable / Maximum level — upper limit of comfortable loudness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Candidates must remember that the **dB HL → dB SPL** conversion is frequency-specific and uses RETSPL (Reference Equivalent Threshold Sound Pressure Level) values: **dB SPL = dB HL + RETSPL(f)**.
