Rows and columns are **randomly flashed** in rapid succession (≈ 8–15 flashes per letter). Only the row *and* the column containing the target letter produce a P300 in the EEG. The system averages the responses to all flashes and uses **LDA classification** to identify the most-likely target row and column; the intersection is the selected letter. **Flowchart (1 mark):** Refer to the Mermaid sequence diagram in Section 4.4. Essential nodes: *User attends → Row/Column flashes → EEG captures P300 → Averaging → LDA classification → Letter output → Next cycle.* > **Examiner's tip:** Mention that **Farwell-Donchin (1988)** invented the original P300 speller, and modern versions use **8-flash sequences** with **Bayesian linear discriminant analysis** for higher ITR. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must memorize before every KTU exam:** - ✅ BCI = *direct* brain-to-device pathway; **no muscles involved.** - ✅ Five stages: **Acquire → Pre-process → Extract → Classify → Actuate**, plus a **feedback loop**. - ✅ Three acquisition modalities: **EEG (non-invasive, 10–100 µV), ECoG (semi-invasive, 50–500 µV), Intracortical (invasive, single-neuron spikes).** - ✅ Three paradigms: **P300 (≈ 300 ms positive peak), SSVEP (6–30 Hz flicker), MI (mu/beta ERD/ERS).** - ✅ **Mu rhythm = 8–13 Hz; Beta rhythm = 13–30 Hz; both at sensorimotor cortex (C3, Cz, C4).** - ✅ **SNR formula:** $\mathrm{SNR}_{\mathrm{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(A_s / A_n)$. - ✅ **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (so ≥ 100 Hz for 50 Hz EEG). - ✅ **ERD % formula:** $\mathrm{ERD}\% = \dfrac{P_{\text{event}} - P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{ref}}} \times 100$ (negative = desynchronization). - ✅ **Wolpaw ITR formula:** $B = \log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P) \log_2 \!\left(\dfrac{1-P}{N-1}\right)$ bits/trial; ITR = $(B/T) \times 60$ bits/min. - ✅ **Synchronous** = system-paced; **Asynchronous** = self-paced (the clinical goal). - ✅ **BCI illiteracy** = 15–30 % of users cannot achieve above-chance accuracy; addressed by hybrid BCIs. - ✅ **Hybrid BCI** = combines two paradigms (e.g., MI + SSVEP) to boost accuracy. - ✅ The **10–20 International System** standardises EEG electrode placement; key electrodes: **Fp1/Fp2 (frontal), C3/C4 (sensorimotor — central!), P3/P4 (parietal), O1/O2 (occipital).** - ✅ Always include the **feedback loop** in any BCI block diagram — it is non-negotiable in KTU valuation. - ✅ Always quote ITR in **bits/min** — never bits/sec. - ✅ **P300 is a *latency* (ms), NOT a frequency (Hz).** This is the single most common KTU error. > **Final Note:** The BCI is the *most interdisciplinary* topic in PEBRT745 — it merges signal processing, neuroanatomy, machine learning, and rehabilitation engineering. Master the ITR derivation and the P300/SSVEP/MI comparison table, and you will secure 12–14 marks on this topic in any KTU exam. ---

topics.title: Computer Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computer Access

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Access (in the context of Assistive Medical Devices)** refers to the comprehensive set of *hardware, software, and human-interface strategies* that enable individuals with physical, sensory, cognitive, or neurological impairments to independently operate, navigate, and interact with standard or specialized computer systems.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for assistive medical devices, Computer Access is positioned as a **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) sub-discipline** that bridges the gap between a user's residual functional abilities and the demands imposed by conventional computing interfaces (keyboard, mouse, monitor, touchscreen).

The field is formally evaluated under the **WHO ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** domains of *Activity* and *Participation*, with engineering metrics centered on:

- **Throughput** (characters per minute / bits per second)
- **Latency** (input-to-action delay, ideally **< 100 ms**)
- **Error Rate** (target **< 2%** for trained users)
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** for adaptive hardware

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Universal Remote for a Locked Door"**
>
> Imagine you are standing in front of a high-security door that has only two slots: one shaped like a piano key and another shaped like a mouse. You do not have fingers, or you cannot see the slots, or you cannot move your hand. The door *can* open, but only if you can communicate with it through an **intermediary** that translates **whatever you can do** (blink, sip air, twitch a cheek, speak) into the *piano key* or *mouse* signals it understands.
>
> **Computer Access technologies are that intermediary translator.** They do not change the computer; they translate the *user's available ability* into the *computer's expected input language*.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of User Impairment Profiles

| User Profile | Primary Limitation | Engineering Response |
|---|---|---|
| **Visually Impaired** | Cannot rely on visual monitor output | Screen readers (JAWS, NVDA), Braille displays |
| **Blind** | Zero visual channel | Refreshable Braille, audio-only OS shells |
| **Low Vision** | Partial sight, glare, blur | Screen magnifiers, high-contrast themes |
| **Motor Impaired (Upper Limb)** | Cannot use hands | Eye trackers, head pointers, sip-and-puff |
| **Quadriplegic / Locked-In** | Minimal voluntary movement | Single-switch scanning, EEG (BCI) |
| **Cognitive / Learning** | Memory, attention, language deficits | Simplified UIs, predictive text |
| **Hearing Impaired** | Audio feedback inaccessible | Visual alerts, captions, haptic feedback |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Highlight:** A *single* solution rarely fits all profiles. The professional mandate is to perform a **Functional Needs Assessment (FNA)** — measuring the user's *current* range of motion, vision, cognition, and stamina — before prescribing any device.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Layer Architecture of Computer Access

Computer Access solutions are conventionally analyzed as a **four-layer stack**, mirroring the ISO OSI conceptual model:

### Layer 1 — Physical / Mechanical Interface
The tangible hardware the user physically contacts. Examples: a jelly-switch, a sip-and-puff tube, a head-mounted camera.

### Layer 2 — Signal Translation Layer
Converts physical action into a standard electrical signal. Example: A force-sensitive resistor (FSR) converts pressure into a voltage $V_{out}$ readable by a microcontroller.

### Layer 3 — Logical / OS-Level Driver
Maps the translated signal to standard OS events (e.g., `KEY_LEFTCLICK`, `KEY_TAB`, ASCII codes). This is where the **Accessibility API** of the OS (Windows MSAA/UIA, macOS AX, Linux AT-SPI) is invoked.

### Layer 4 — Application-Layer Adaptation
Software that restructures the UI for accessibility (screen readers, on-screen keyboards, dwell-clicking logic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this layer model matters for KTU:** Examiner questions often ask *"Where would you intervene if the user can press a switch but cannot move the cursor smoothly?"* The correct answer is a combination of **Layer 2 (filtering/averaging)** and **Layer 3 (dwell-click logic)**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the equations a KTU 2024 student is expected to know for design and evaluation problems on Computer Access systems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Definitions | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Switch Debounce Time | $T_{debounce} \ge 5 \cdot \tau_{RC}$ | $T_{debounce}$ = debounce interval, $\tau_{RC} = R \cdot C$ | seconds (s) |
| 2 | Throughput (Hatscher Formula) | $W = \dfrac{1}{T_s + T_m}$ | $W$ = words/min, $T_s$ = selection time, $T_m$ = movement time | words/min |
| 3 | Fitts's Law (Pointer Targeting) | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W_t} + 1\right)$ | $MT$ = movement time, $D$ = distance, $W_t$ = target width, $a,b$ = empirical constants (typically $a=50\,$ms, $b=150\,$ms) | seconds |
| 4 | Effective Dwell Time | $T_{dwell} = \dfrac{1}{f_{sample}} \cdot N_{confirm}$ | $f_{sample}$ = sampling freq, $N_{confirm}$ = confirm frames | seconds |
| 5 | Error Rate | $ER = \dfrac{N_{err}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{err}$ = erroneous selections, $N_{total}$ = total attempts | percent (%) |
| 6 | Eye-Tracker Accuracy | $A_{eye} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2}$ | $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ = std. deviation of gaze in x,y | degrees ($^{\circ}$) |
| 7 | Scanning Cycle Time | $T_{cycle} = N_{opt} \cdot T_{highlight}$ | $N_{opt}$ = options in scan ring, $T_{highlight}$ = dwell per option | seconds |
| 8 | BCI Information Transfer Rate | $ITR = \dfrac{\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2\!\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right)}{T}$ | $N$ = choices, $P$ = accuracy, $T$ = trial duration | bits/min |
| 9 | Screen Magnification Factor | $M = \dfrac{\text{Display Pixels}}{\text{Virtual Pixels}}$ | $M \ge 1$ for magnification | dimensionless |
| 10 | Refreshable Braille Cell Rate | $f_{braille} \ge 50\,$Hz (typ.) | Pin actuation frequency | Hz |

## 2.3 Key Engineering Trade-offs

1. **Speed vs. Accuracy (Fitts's Law conflict):** Reducing $W_t$ (target width) increases $MT$ exponentially — critical when designing on-screen keyboards for tremor patients.
2. **Latency vs. False Acceptance:** Lowering dwell time $T_{dwell}$ improves speed but increases the false-click rate.
3. **Portability vs. Power:** Wearable eye-trackers trade battery life for ergonomic benefit.
4. **Cognitive Load vs. Vocabulary Size:** AAC systems with larger symbol sets reduce keystrokes but increase search time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas are applied in clinical rehabilitation engineering labs, where a rehabilitation engineer calibrates an AAC device for a cerebral palsy patient. Fitts's Law is used to size on-screen targets; ITR is used to compare BCI spellers before regulatory submission.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fitts's Law Applied to Adaptive Targeting

**Problem Context:** A user with cerebral palsy uses a head-tracking mouse. The on-screen "OK" button has a target width $W_t = 64$ pixels and is at distance $D = 256$ pixels from the current cursor position. Given empirical constants $a = 0.05\,$s and $b = 0.15\,$s, derive the movement time $MT$.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**

$$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W_t} + 1\right)$$

This is Paul Fitts's 1954 information-theoretic model of aimed movements, where the argument of $\log_2$ is the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** in bits.

**Step 2 — Compute the Index of Difficulty.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W_t} + 1\right) \\[6pt]
   &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{256}{64} + 1\right) \\[6pt]
   &= \log_2(4 + 1) \\[6pt]
   &= \log_2(5)
\end{aligned}
$$

> Here we divided pixel counts directly because the ID is dimensionless when $D$ and $W_t$ share units (pixels). This is the standard normalization in HCI textbooks (MacKenzie, 1992).

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2(5) \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{\ln 5}{\ln 2} \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{1.6094}{0.6931} \\[6pt]
   &\approx 2.3219\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Fitts's Law.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\[6pt]
   &= 0.05 + 0.15 \cdot 2.3219 \\[6pt]
   &= 0.05 + 0.3483 \\[6pt]
   &\approx 0.398\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
The head-tracker will require approximately **$398\,$ms** to move from the current cursor position to the "OK" button. If the user has a tremor with $\sigma \approx 6$ pixels, the *effective* target width becomes:

$$
W_{t,eff} = W_t + 2k\sigma
$$

where $k=1.96$ for 95% confidence. Plugging in:

$$
W_{t,eff} = 64 + 2(1.96)(6) = 64 + 23.52 \approx 87.5\ \text{pixels}
$$

> This is a clinical insight: **enlarging the target compensates for tremor**, but it also increases the ID slightly, demanding more cursor travel. The optimal design is found by minimizing total selection time.

**Step 6 — Recompute MT with the effective width.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_{eff} &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{256}{87.5} + 1\right) \\[6pt]
        &= \log_2(2.9257 + 1) \\[6pt]
        &= \log_2(3.9257) \\[6pt]
        &\approx 1.973\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_{eff} &= 0.05 + 0.15 \cdot 1.973 \\[6pt]
         &= 0.05 + 0.296 \\[6pt]
         &\approx 0.346\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Counter-intuitive result:** A *larger* target yields a *faster* movement because the speed gain from a smaller ID outweighs the cost of more travel. This is the **steering law** effect in reverse.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Information Transfer Rate (ITR) of a BCI Speller

**Problem Context:** A P300 BCI speller presents 36 characters. Average accuracy is $P = 0.85$ and the time per trial is $T = 2.5\,$s. Compute the ITR.

**Step 1 — State the equation.**

$$
ITR = \frac{\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P) \log_2\!\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right)}{T}
$$

where $N$ is the number of choices and the result is in **bits per trial**, then divided by $T$ for **bits/second**. Multiplied by 60, we get **bits/min** for clinical reporting.

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ITR &= \frac{1}{2.5}\Bigg[\log_2(36) + 0.85 \log_2(0.85) + 0.15 \log_2\!\left(\frac{0.15}{35}\right)\Bigg]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term.**

$$
\log_2(36) = \frac{\ln 36}{\ln 2} = \frac{3.5835}{0.6931} \approx 5.1699
$$

$$
0.85 \log_2(0.85) = 0.85 \times (-0.2345) \approx -0.1993
$$

$$
0.15 \log_2\!\left(\frac{0.15}{35}\right) = 0.15 \log_2(0.004286) = 0.15 \times (-7.8666) \approx -1.1800
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the bracket.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 5.1699 - 0.1993 - 1.1800 \\
                 &= 3.7906\ \text{bits/trial}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $T$ and convert to bits/min.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ITR &= \frac{3.7906}{2.5} = 1.516\ \text{bits/s} \\[6pt]
ITR_{min} &= 1.516 \times 60 \approx 90.98\ \text{bits/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Clinical interpretation.**
A healthy user types at $\sim 200$ characters/min = $\sim 1000$ bits/min. The BCI user is at **$\approx 91$ bits/min**, or roughly **9% of able-bodied throughput**. This quantifies the *participation gap* that rehabilitation engineering aims to close.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Access Methods

| Method | Required Residual Ability | Typical Throughput | Typical Latency | Best-Suited Pathology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Keyboard | Full hand function | $40\text{–}60$ WPM | $<10$ ms | None (baseline) |
| Membrane / Large-Key Keyboard | Gross hand motion | $15\text{–}25$ WPM | $<20$ ms | Arthritis, mild CP |
| Single-Switch Scanning | One reliable switch site | $2\text{–}5$ WPM | Depends on cycle | ALS (advanced), Locked-in |
| Sip-and-Puff | In/out breath control | $5\text{–}10$ WPM | $50\text{–}100$ ms | High-level SCI (C3–C4) |
| Head Pointer / Tracker | Neck rotation | $10\text{–}20$ WPM | $30\text{–}80$ ms | Cervical SCI, muscular dystrophy |
| Eye Tracker | Oculomotor control, gaze | $15\text{–}40$ WPM | $50\text{–}120$ ms | ALS, severe CP |
| Voice Recognition (ASR) | Clear speech | $40\text{–}80$ WPM | $200\text{–}500$ ms | RSI, low vision, amputees |
| BCI (EEG) | Attention / P300 | $1\text{–}5$ WPM (clinical) | $2\text{–}10$ s | Complete locked-in syndrome |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Computer Access System

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **end-to-end signal flow** from the user's residual ability to the application output. This is the *canonical* diagram KTU expects students to draw for "Explain the architecture of an assistive computer access system" type questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Residual Ability] --> B[Physical Sensor Layer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    C --> D[Microcontroller ADC Sampling]
    D --> E[OS Driver Accessibility API]
    E --> F[Application Layer Screen Reader OnScreen Keyboard]
    F --> G[Computer Application Output]
    G --> H[Feedback Haptic Audio Visual]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed-looking *loop back* from `H` to `A` represents the **closed-loop feedback** that makes the system learnable and error-correctable. Without feedback, no adaptive system can be operated reliably by a user with a disability.

---

## 4.2 Process Diagram: Single-Switch Scanning Logic

This diagram illustrates how a single-switch user selects letter **"H"** from a row-column scanning on-screen keyboard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Scan Cycle] --> B[Highlight Row 1 A B C]
    B --> C{Switch Pressed}
    C -- No --> D[Highlight Row 2 G H I]
    D --> C
    C -- Yes --> E[Activate Row 2]
    E --> F[Highlight Column 1 G]
    F --> G{Switch Pressed}
    G -- No --> H[Highlight Column 2 H]
    H --> G
    G -- Yes --> I[Output Letter H]
    I --> J[Refresh Scan Cycle]
```

> **Insight for KTU answer-writing:** A *full* row-column scan of a 36-key keyboard requires up to $6 + 6 = 12$ highlight steps, but **block scanning** (groups of 6) reduces the worst case to $6 + 6 = 12$ still — yet **double block scanning** can reduce to $3 + 3 + 6 = 12$. The optimal is *alphabetically re-ordered* layouts (e.g., **"ETAVOIN"** arrangement) which exploit letter frequency.

---

## 4.3 Architecture Diagram: Eye-Tracker Integration with OS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER_SIDE [User Side Hardware]
        eye1[IR Illuminator 850nm] --> eyeball[User Eye]
        eyeball --> cam1[Eye Camera CMOS 200Hz]
        cam1 --> proc1[On-board DSP Gaze Estimation]
    end
    proc1 --> usb1[USB HID Stream x y gaze data]
    subgraph OS_SIDE [Operating System]
        usb1 --> driver[OS Driver Windows Eye Control macOS]
        driver --> mouse[Virtual Mouse Cursor Emulation]
        driver --> dwell[Dwell Click Logic Layer]
        driver --> api[Accessibility API UI Automation]
    end
    api --> apps[Target Application Browser Office]
```

> **Why this matters for KTU:** The 850 nm IR illuminator is invisible to the human eye but produces a high-contrast **glint (corneal reflection)** that the CMOS camera uses as a stationary reference point. The pupil-glint vector $\vec{v} = \vec{p}_{pupil} - \vec{p}_{glint}$ is mapped to screen coordinates via a polynomial regression calibrated per user.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: BCI P300 Speller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EEG Electrodes 8 to 64 Channel] --> B[Amplifier Bandpass 0.1 to 30 Hz]
    B --> C[ADC Sampling 256 Hz]
    C --> D[Feature Extraction PCA xDAWN]
    D --> E[Classifier LDA SVM]
    E --> F[Decision P300 detected or not]
    F --> G[Row Column Flash Selection]
    G --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **P300** is a positive EEG deflection peaking $\approx 300\,$ms after a rare, task-relevant stimulus. The speller flashes rows/columns; the user attends to the desired character. The classifier's role is to detect which flash elicited the P300 — selecting the intersecting row and column.

---

## 4.5 Comparative Selection: Mermaid Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Computer Access Methods]
    root --> input[Input Methods]
    root --> output[Output Methods]
    input --> sw[Switches and Sensors]
    input --> ptr[Pointing Alternatives]
    input --> vce[Voice Input]
    input --> bci[Brain Computer Interface]
    sw --> sip[Sip and Puff]
    sw --> jelly[Jelly Bean Switch]
    ptr --> head[Head Tracking]
    ptr --> eye[Eye Tracking]
    ptr --> track[Trackball Adapted]
    vce --> asr[Automatic Speech Recognition]
    vce --> dvt[Dragon NaturallySpeaking]
    bci --> p300[P300 Speller]
    bci --> motor[Motor Imagery]
    output --> visual[Visual Adaptations]
    output --> audio[Audio Output]
    output --> tactile[Tactile Output]
    visual --> mag[Screen Magnifier]
    visual --> highC[High Contrast Themes]
    audio --> sr[Screen Reader JAWS NVDA]
    audio --> os[OS VoiceOver Narrator]
    tactile --> br[Refreshable Braille Display]
    tactile --> hap[Haptic Vibration Cues]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "Computer Access" in the context of assistive medical devices. List any four major categories of impairment that necessitate computer access technologies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Computer Access refers to the integrated use of adaptive hardware, software, and human-interface strategies that enable individuals with disabilities to operate, navigate, and benefit from standard computing systems, by translating their residual functional abilities into standard input/output events.

> **Four major categories (0.375 each, total 1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Visual impairment** (low vision, blindness)
> 2. **Motor impairment** (loss of hand/arm function, tremor, paralysis)
> 3. **Cognitive/learning impairment** (dyslexia, intellectual disability)
> 4. **Speech/hearing impairment** (aphonia, deafness requiring visual alerts)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Fitts's Law? State its mathematical form and explain the significance of the Index of Difficulty.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Fitts's Law (1954) is a predictive model of human motor performance that quantifies the time required to move rapidly to a target area, as a function of the distance to and size of the target.

> **Mathematical form (1 mark):**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W_t} + 1\right)
$$

> **Index of Difficulty (1 mark):** $ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W_t} + 1\right)$ is measured in **bits**, treating aimed movement as an *information transmission* problem. Larger distance and smaller target increase the ID, and thus the movement time. The clinical significance is that **enlarging targets** or **reducing distance** (e.g., via Dwell-click) directly reduces the time burden for motor-impaired users.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Read Before Answering!**
> Students most commonly **lose 3–4 marks** on Part-B questions for the following reasons:
> 1. **No diagram** — A block diagram is **mandatory** for any "explain the system" question; absence typically costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing *Input* with *Output* access methods** — Screen readers are *output*; switches are *input*. Mixing them up costs a full sub-part.
> 3. **Units missing** in Fitts's Law or ITR problems → **0.5 mark deduction per occurrence**.
> 4. **Forgetting the OS Accessibility API layer** when describing signal flow → 1-mark loss for "incomplete architecture".

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **four-layer architecture** of a Computer Access system, clearly identifying the role of each layer and giving **one engineering example** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A motor-impaired user employs a head-tracking pointer. The on-screen target is at a distance $D = 480$ pixels with a target width $W_t = 80$ pixels. The user's tremor standard deviation is $\sigma = 8$ pixels. Using Fitts's Law with $a = 50$ ms and $b = 150$ ms, compute:
- (i) the Index of Difficulty $ID$
- (ii) the effective movement time accounting for tremor
- (iii) the recommended new target width if the movement time is to be reduced by 20% **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 marks]**

> **Layer 1 — Physical / Mechanical Interface (1.5 marks)**
> The tangible hardware the user physically contacts. *Example:* a jelly-bean mechanical switch (3.2 cm diameter, 75 g actuation force) mounted on a wheelchair armrest.

> **Layer 2 — Signal Translation (1.5 marks)**
> Converts the physical action into a clean electrical signal. *Example:* a Force-Sensitive Resistor (FSR) whose resistance $R$ decreases as pressure $F$ increases, with output $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_{ref}}{R_{FSR} + R_{ref}}$.

> **Layer 3 — OS Driver / Accessibility API (2 marks)**
> Maps the cleaned signal to standard OS events such as `KEY_ENTER` or `MOUSE_LEFTDOWN`. *Example:* the Windows **UI Automation (UIA)** framework exposes accessible properties to assistive clients.

> **Layer 4 — Application Adaptation (2 marks)**
> Software that restructures the GUI for accessibility. *Example:* the **NVDA** screen reader announcing focused elements via the **SAPI 5** speech synthesizer.

*(+ 0 marks reserved for diagram — see block diagram in §4.1)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 marks]**

**Subpart (i) — Compute ID (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W_t} + 1\right) \\
   &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{480}{80} + 1\right) \\
   &= \log_2(6 + 1) \\
   &= \log_2(7) \\
   &\approx 2.807\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subpart (ii) — Effective movement time with tremor (3 marks):**

Effective target width at 95% confidence ($k=1.96$):

$$
W_{t,eff} = W_t + 2k\sigma = 80 + 2(1.96)(8) = 80 + 31.36 \approx 111.4\ \text{pixels}
$$

Recompute $ID$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_{eff} &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{480}{111.4} + 1\right) = \log_2(4.309 + 1) = \log_2(5.309) \approx 2.408\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Movement time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_{eff} &= a + b \cdot ID_{eff} \\
         &= 0.050 + 0.150 \cdot 2.408 \\
         &= 0.050 + 0.361 \\
         &\approx 0.411\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subpart (iii) — New target width for 20% time reduction (2 marks):**

Target movement time: $MT_{target} = 0.411 \cdot 0.80 = 0.329\,$s.

Solve for $ID_{new}$:

$$
0.329 = 0.050 + 0.150 \cdot ID_{new} \Rightarrow ID_{new} = \frac{0.279}{0.150} = 1.860\ \text{bits}
$$

Solve for $W_{t,new}$:

$$
1.860 = \log_2\!\left(\frac{480}{W_{t,new}} + 1\right) \Rightarrow 2^{1.860} = \frac{480}{W_{t,new}} + 1
$$

$$
2^{1.860} \approx 3.635 \Rightarrow W_{t,new} = \frac{480}{3.635 - 1} = \frac{480}{2.635} \approx 182.2\ \text{pixels}
$$

> **Answer:** The new target width should be enlarged to **$\approx 182$ pixels** (from 80) to achieve a 20% reduction in movement time.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **single-switch scanning system** with a block diagram. Discuss **row-column scanning** vs. **block scanning**, and explain how **alphabetical re-ordering** improves throughput. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Information Transfer Rate (ITR)** for a BCI system. A P300 speller has $N = 26$ choices, average accuracy $P = 0.90$, and trial time $T = 2.0$ s. Compute the ITR in bits/minute and comment on its clinical significance compared to able-bodied typing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 marks]**

> **Working of single-switch scanning (3 marks):** A single-switch user cannot move a cursor. Instead, the computer *highlights* options one at a time (or in groups) and the user *presses* their switch to select the currently highlighted option. Scanning proceeds in a deterministic cycle. The method is essential for users with severe motor impairment (advanced ALS, high cervical SCI).

> **Row-column vs. block scanning (2.5 marks):**
> - **Row-column:** Highlights one row at a time → user selects → highlights columns of that row → user selects. Worst-case selections for a 36-key keyboard = $6 + 6 = 12$ steps.
> - **Block:** Keyboard is divided into 6 blocks of 6. First, blocks are scanned → user picks a block → then keys within the block are scanned. Worst case = $6 + 6 = 12$, but *average* selection is lower, improving throughput.
> - **Double block (3-3-6):** Further reduces average steps to $\approx 9$.

> **Alphabetical re-ordering (1.5 marks):** Standard QWERTY is poorly suited to letter frequency. Re-ordering keys by English letter frequency (e.g., **"ETAOINSHRDLU..."**) places common letters in early-scanned positions, cutting **average** selection time by 30–40% (research by MacKenzie & Felzer, 2010).

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 marks]**

> **ITR definition (1 mark):** ITR quantifies the effective communication speed of a BCI in **bits per unit time**, accounting for both the number of choices and classification accuracy.

> **ITR equation (1 mark):**

$$
ITR = \frac{\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P) \log_2\!\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right)}{T}
$$

> **Numerical computation (4 marks):**

$$
\log_2(26) \approx 4.700
$$

$$
0.90 \log_2(0.90) = 0.90 \times (-0.1520) = -0.1368
$$

$$
0.10 \log_2\!\left(\frac{0.10}{25}\right) = 0.10 \log_2(0.004) = 0.10 \times (-7.966) = -0.7966
$$

Numerator:

$$
4.700 - 0.1368 - 0.7966 = 3.7666\ \text{bits/trial}
$$

Per second and per minute:

$$
ITR = \frac{3.7666}{2.0} = 1.883\ \text{bits/s} \Rightarrow 112.99\ \text{bits/min}
$$

> **Clinical significance (1 mark):** Able-bodied typists reach $\approx 200$ WPM, equivalent to $\sim 1000$ bits/min. The BCI user at $113$ bits/min achieves only **$\approx 11\%$ of able-bodied throughput**, but for a totally locked-in patient this represents **functional communication** — a transformative gain in *participation* under the WHO ICF model.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3, Computer Access**

- ✅ Computer Access = *Input Translation + Output Adaptation* that bridges residual ability to standard computing.
- ✅ **Four-layer architecture:** Physical → Signal Translation → OS Driver/Accessibility API → Application Adaptation.
- ✅ **Fitts's Law:** $MT = a + b \log_2(D/W_t + 1)$; enlarging targets reduces movement time for tremor patients.
- ✅ **Single-switch scanning** is the *last-resort* input method; alphabet re-ordering + block scanning maximize throughput.
- ✅ **Eye-tracking** uses IR glint + pupil vector mapping; typical accuracy $\sim 0.5^{\circ}$, latency $50\text{–}120\,$ms.
- ✅ **Sip-and-puff** is preferred for high cervical SCI; **head tracking** for moderate motor loss.
- ✅ **Screen readers** (JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver) are *output* aids; they query the **Accessibility API** (UIA/AT-SPI/AX).
- ✅ **BCI ITR equation** accounts for accuracy penalty; clinical P300 spellers achieve $\sim 1\text{–}2$ bits/s.
- ✅ **Refreshable Braille** cells actuate at $\geq 50\,$Hz; 8-dot Braille supports the full Unicode braille pattern.
- ✅ **FNA (Functional Needs Assessment)** is the *engineering prescription step* — measure before you prescribe.
- ✅ **OS-level hooks:** Windows = MSAA + UIA, macOS = NSAccessibility, Linux = AT-SPI 2.
- ✅ **Throughput benchmarks:** Standard typing 40–60 WPM, AAC best 15–25 WPM, BCI 1–5 WPM, single switch 2–5 WPM.
- ✅ Always include a **labeled block diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners allocate **1–2 marks** explicitly for the figure.
- ✅ State **units** in every numerical answer; the absence of "bits/min" or "seconds" is a common 0.5-mark penalty.
- ✅ Always cite the **WHO ICF domain** (Activity / Participation) when justifying a Computer Access intervention in design questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access to Phones and Tablets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Access to Phones and Tablets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mobile Device Accessibility** refers to the engineered set of hardware, software, firmware, and human–computer interaction (HCI) features embedded within smartphones and tablet computers that enable users with sensory, motor, cognitive, or physical impairments to perceive, operate, and benefit from the device's full functional capability on par with non-impaired users.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, *Access to Phones and Tablets* is a sub-domain of **Assistive Medical Devices (PEBRT745)** that focuses on **non-invasive, consumer-electronics-based accessibility solutions** as opposed to dedicated medical-grade assistive hardware (e.g., AAC dedicated speech devices, sip-and-puff ventilators, cochlear implants).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEBRT745, Module 3):**
> "Access to Phones and Tablets" covers: (i) Platform-level accessibility services in iOS and Android, (ii) Touch, gesture, voice, and switch-based input accommodations, (iii) Screen reader architecture, (iv) Communication (AAC) applications, and (v) Accessibility settings for low-vision, deaf, hard-of-hearing, and motor-impaired users.

### 1.2 The Three-Layer Accessibility Stack (Conceptual)

Accessibility in modern mobile devices is not a single feature — it is a **three-layer engineered stack**:

| Layer | Name | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | **Platform / OS Layer** | Built into the operating system kernel and UI framework | Android `AccessibilityService` API, iOS `UIAccessibility` |
| **L2** | **Built-in Assistive Apps** | Pre-installed user-facing accessibility tools | TalkBack, VoiceOver, Magnification, Live Captions |
| **L3** | **Third-Party Assistive Apps** | Downloadable solutions built on top of L1 APIs | Proloquo4Go, Be My Eyes, Wheelmap |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Digital Ramp"**
>
> Imagine a beautiful multi-storey building with stairs at the entrance. A wheelchair user cannot enter. So we install a **ramp** alongside the stairs. The building is still the same; nothing about the architecture changed. The **ramp** is a *parallel pathway* offering the **same destination** through a **different physical channel**.
>
> A **phone's accessibility features work identically**. The original touch-screen UI is the "stairs." Voice control, switch access, and screen readers are the "ramps." They do not change the app — they simply offer an **alternative neural/physical channel** to reach the same screen, button, and information.
>
> A **screen reader** is the equivalent of a personal guide reading every sign aloud. A **switch control** is the equivalent of a single push-button that scans through options, allowing someone who can move only one finger — or even one toe — to operate a full smartphone.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Compliance Anchors

- **WCAG 2.2** (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines, W3C Recommendation) — defines **A**, **AA**, **AAA** conformance levels for digital content.
- **EN 301 549** — European standard for ICT accessibility (mandated for public-sector procurement in the EU).
- **Section 508** (US Rehabilitation Act Amendment) — accessibility of federal ICT.
- **RP1 (Reasonable Proportionality)** — design principle: the *effort to make something accessible* should be **proportional to its benefit**.

The standard screen reader response time threshold for fluid interaction is **< 100 ms** (Nielsen Norman Group usability research, often cited in HCI literature).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Functional Pillars of Phone/Tablet Access

Modern mobile accessibility is best analyzed through six functional pillars. Each pillar is implemented as a **software abstraction** that intercepts or supplements the standard touch/visual/audio I/O loop.

### Pillar 1 — **Screen Reader (Visual → Auditory/Tactile Channel)**

A screen reader is an **always-on event listener** that converts the visual UI tree into a synthetic-speech or refreshable-braille stream.

**Operational Steps:**
1. The OS exposes a semantic tree of every visible UI element (called the *Accessibility Tree* in Android, *AX Tree* in iOS).
2. The screen reader service iterates the tree **in reading order** (left-to-right, top-to-bottom, language-aware).
3. For each node it announces: **role** (button, heading), **label** (displayed text), **state** (checked, disabled), and **value** (slider position).
4. The user performs a *gesture* (e.g., one-finger swipe right) → screen reader advances the **focus cursor** (often called the "VO cursor" on iOS, or the "TalkBack focus" on Android).
5. A *double-tap* on the focused element triggers its default action — emulating the click that a sighted user would do directly.

| Platform | Screen Reader | Vendor | Voice Engine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Android | **TalkBack** | Google | Google TTS |
| iOS / iPadOS | **VoiceOver** | Apple | Apple AVSpeechSynthesizer |
| HarmonyOS | **ScreenReader** | Huawei | Huawei TTS |
| Custom / Linux | **Orca** | GNOME | eSpeak / Festival |

### Pillar 2 — **Voice Control (Motor-free, hands-free input)**

Voice control converts spoken natural-language commands into UI actions. Both **Apple Siri Shortcuts + Voice Control** and **Android Voice Access** (by Google) follow the same conceptual pipeline:

1. **Wake-word detection** ("Hey Siri" / "Hey Google" — *always-on, on-device*).
2. **Speech-to-Text (STT)** using on-device neural networks (e.g., Apple's *Speech.framework*, Google's *Speech Services v2*).
3. **Intent classification** (NLP layer maps utterances like "Open WhatsApp" → `Intent.MAIN` + `category=LAUNCHER`).
4. **Action dispatch** — the system simulates a touch event at the coordinate or invokes the appropriate accessibility action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On-Device vs. Cloud Inference** — Modern voice control runs the ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition) model *locally* on the device's Neural Processing Unit (NPU) for **latency reduction** and **HIPAA/GDPR compliance** for medical users.

### Pillar 3 — **Switch Access (Single-switch or Two-switch scanning)**

For users with severe motor impairment (ALS, locked-in syndrome, high-level spinal cord injury), the entire UI can be navigated using **one or two physical switches**.

**Scanning Algorithm (the core math of switch access):**

Let $S$ be the set of selectable UI elements on the current screen, $n = \vert S \vert$, and $T_{\text{dwell}}$ the dwell time per item.

**Linear Scan (Row-Column):**
$$T_{\text{total}} = n \cdot T_{\text{dwell}} \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

**Optimized Group Scan (e.g., 3-level):**
$$T_{\text{group}} = n^{1/3} \cdot T_{\text{dwell}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{\text{total}} = 3 \cdot n^{1/3} \cdot T_{\text{dwell}}$$

**Why this matters:** For a 27-icon home screen, linear scan requires $27 \cdot 1.5\,\text{s} = 40.5\,\text{s}$ per icon, while a 3-level group scan requires $3 \cdot 3 \cdot 1.5 = 13.5\,\text{s}$ — a **3× speedup** for a user who may have only one muscle group under voluntary control.

### Pillar 4 — **Magnification & Display Modifications (Low Vision)**

Low-vision users can use a **full-screen magnifier** (3×–15×), **invert colors**, **enable bold text**, or apply **color filters** (for protanopia, deuteranopia, tritanopia). The WCAG-mandated **minimum text contrast ratio** is:

$$C = \frac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05} \quad \text{where } L_1 > L_2$$

For **WCAG AA**, normal text requires $C \geq 4.5{:}1$; large text (≥ 18 pt) requires $C \geq 3{:}1$. For **AAA**, the thresholds rise to **7:1** and **4.5:1** respectively.

### Pillar 5 — **Hearing Accommodations (Deaf / Hard-of-Hearing)**

- **Live Captions** (Android 12+ / iOS 16+) — on-device, real-time transcription of any audio.
- **Hearing Aid Compatibility (HAC)** — the device's T-coil / M/T rating must be **M3/T3 or higher** under the **ANSI C63.19-2019** American National Standard.
- **Visual / Haptic Alerts** — flash LED, vibrate patterns, and notification banners substitute for auditory alerts.
- **Mono Audio** — merges stereo channels for users with unilateral hearing loss.

### Pillar 6 — **Cognitive & Learning Accommodations**

- **Guided Access** (iOS) / **Screen Pinning** (Android) — locks the device to a single app.
- **Time-limit extensions** and simplified UI modes.
- **AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) apps** like **Proloquo4Go**, **TouchChat**, and **Avaz** transform the tablet into a dedicated speech-generating device (SGD).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Reference Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variable Definitions | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Switch Scan Time | $T_{\text{lin}} = n \cdot T_d$ | $n$ = items, $T_d$ = dwell time | seconds |
| Group Scan (level $k$) | $T_{\text{grp}} = k \cdot n^{1/k} \cdot T_d$ | $k$ = group levels | seconds |
| WCAG Contrast Ratio | $C = (L_1 + 0.05) / (L_2 + 0.05)$ | $L_1, L_2$ = relative luminances | dimensionless |
| WCAG AA Normal Text | $C \geq 4.5$ | — | — |
| WCAG AA Large Text | $C \geq 3.0$ | — | — |
| WCAG AAA Normal Text | $C \geq 7.0$ | — | — |
| ANSI C63.19 HAC Rating | M3 / T3 minimum | M = mic, T = telecoil | categorical |
| TalkBack Latency Budget | $t < 100$ | ms | milliseconds |
| Refreshable Braille Cell Density | $N_{\text{cell}}$ | 6 or 8 dot per cell | cells/line |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same accessibility abstractions are now driving the **Internet of Medical Things (IoMT)**:
- Hospital infusion pumps expose **Android `AccessibilityService` hooks** for blind nurses.
- Surgical tablets use **voice control** to maintain sterile-field integrity.
- AAC tablets are **prescribed as Class I medical devices** in many jurisdictions.
- **Tele-rehabilitation apps** use phone cameras and on-device ML to deliver home-based physiotherapy, exploiting the same low-latency on-device neural inference used by Voice Control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Group Scanning Outperforms Linear Scanning

We wish to prove that for $n$ items and a 2-level **row-column** switch scan, the average selection time is minimized at $k$ levels, where $k$ approaches a small integer.

**Given:**
- $n$ = total number of selectable items on screen.
- $T_d$ = dwell time per item (typically **1.0 s to 1.5 s** for clinical switch access).
- $k$ = number of grouping levels (1 = linear, 2 = row-col, 3 = ternary, etc.).

**Step 1 — Count items per group at level $k$:**
Assume items are perfectly balanced into $k$-ary groups. Each group contains $g = n^{1/k}$ items. The user must first select the correct group, then descend into the group.

**Step 2 — Total selection time at one level:**
The user scans $g$ items to identify the correct group, taking time:
$$T_{\text{level}} = g \cdot T_d = n^{1/k} \cdot T_d$$

**Step 3 — Total time across all $k$ levels:**
$$T_{\text{total}} = k \cdot n^{1/k} \cdot T_d$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical example, $n = 27$, $T_d = 1.5$ s:**

For $k = 1$ (linear):
$$T_{\text{total}} = 1 \cdot 27^{1/1} \cdot 1.5 = 40.5 \text{ s}$$

For $k = 2$ (row-column):
$$T_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot 27^{1/2} \cdot 1.5 = 2 \cdot 5.196 \cdot 1.5 = 15.588 \text{ s}$$

For $k = 3$ (ternary):
$$T_{\text{total}} = 3 \cdot 27^{1/3} \cdot 1.5 = 3 \cdot 3 \cdot 1.5 = 13.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Minimize $T_{\text{total}}$ by taking the derivative w.r.t. $k$:**
Set $\frac{d}{dk}\left(k \cdot n^{1/k}\right) = 0$. Using $\frac{d}{dk}(n^{1/k}) = -\frac{\ln n}{k^2} \cdot n^{1/k}$:

$$\frac{d}{dk}\left(k \cdot n^{1/k}\right) = n^{1/k} - \frac{\ln n}{k} \cdot n^{1/k} = 0$$

$$1 - \frac{\ln n}{k} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_{\text{opt}} = \ln n$$

For $n = 27$, $k_{\text{opt}} = \ln 27 \approx 3.296$. Since $k$ must be an integer, **$k = 3$ is optimal** — matching our numerical result above.

**Conclusion:** The ternary (3-level) group scan is mathematically optimal for a 27-item screen, reducing selection time by **3.0×** compared to linear scanning. This is why modern switch-access implementations (iOS Switch Control, Android Switch Access) default to a 3-level scan when item count exceeds 20.

## 3.2 Derivation: WCAG Contrast Ratio from Relative Luminance

The relative luminance $L$ of an sRGB color channel value $V \in [0, 1]$ is defined by the **piecewise sRGB transfer function**:

$$
L = \begin{cases}
\left(\dfrac{V + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} & \text{if } V > 0.03928 \\
\dfrac{V}{12.92} & \text{if } V \leq 0.03928
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $L$ for each sRGB channel independently** ($R$, $G$, $B$).

**Step 2 — Compute total relative luminance**:
$$L_{\text{total}} = 0.2126 \cdot R + 0.7152 \cdot G + 0.0722 \cdot B$$

**Step 3 — Compute contrast ratio**:
$$C = \frac{L_{\text{light}} + 0.05}{L_{\text{dark}} + 0.05}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** Black text ($V = 0$) on white background ($V = 1$).

For black, $L = 0$ (falling in the $\le 0.03928$ branch → $0/12.92 = 0$).

For white, $V = 1 > 0.03928$, so:
$$L_{\text{white}} = \left(\frac{1 + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} = (1)^{2.4} = 1.0$$

Therefore:
$$C = \frac{1.0 + 0.05}{0 + 0.05} = \frac{1.05}{0.05} = 21$$

**Black-on-white contrast is 21:1** — the maximum possible, satisfying even **WCAG AAA** (7:1) easily. This is why medical software often uses pure black-on-white for prescription labels and patient instructions.

**Step 5 — Verify AA threshold example:** Light gray #767676 on white.

For #767676, normalized $V = 0.4627$:
$$L = \left(\frac{0.4627 + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} = (0.4905)^{2.4} \approx 0.180$$
$$C = \frac{1.05}{0.18 + 0.05} = \frac{1.05}{0.23} \approx 4.57$$

This **just passes WCAG AA** for normal text (≥ 4.5). Any lighter gray fails the standard.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Phone vs. Tablet Accessibility Trade-offs

| Parameter | Smartphone (e.g., 6.1" display) | Tablet (e.g., 11" display) |
|---|---|---|
| Portability | Excellent (pocketable) | Moderate (carry bag) |
| AAC use case | Backup / quick communication | Primary dedicated SGD |
| Screen-reader hand ergonomics | One-handed, thumb gestures | Two-handed or stand-mounted |
| Battery for switch port | Limited | Longer (larger cell) |
| Magnification utility | Adequate for short text | Superior for long documents |
| Mounting for motor impairment | Crutch / wheelchair clip | Wheelchair tray / desk stand |
| Cost barrier (AAC app + device) | Lower | Higher |
| Real-time captioning utility | Phone-call captioning | Tele-rehab, group therapy |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mobile Accessibility Service Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userInput[User Input Channel]:::io
    sensory[User Sensory Channel]:::io
    userInput --> switch1[Physical Switch]
    userInput --> touch1[Touchscreen Capacitive Layer]
    userInput --> mic1[MEMS Microphone Array]
    userInput --> cam1[Front Camera with Face Tracking]

    oslayer[Operating System Layer]:::platform
    switch1 --> oslayer
    touch1 --> oslayer
    mic1 --> oslayer
    cam1 --> oslayer

    oslayer --> axtree[Accessibility Tree XML]:::core
    oslayer --> eventBus[Accessibility Event Bus]:::core

    axtree --> tts1[Text to Speech Engine]:::svc
    axtree --> brl1[Refreshable Braille Driver]:::svc
    eventBus --> sr1[Screen Reader Service]:::svc

    sr1 --> tts1
    sr1 --> brl1

    tts1 --> audioOut[Speaker or Bone Conduction Output]:::out
    brl1 --> brailleDisp[USB or Bluetooth Braille Display]:::out
    sensory --> audioOut
    sensory --> brailleDisp

    tts1 --> user[End User with Impairment]:::user
    brl1 --> user
    audioOut --> user
    brailleDisp --> user

    classDef io fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A05000,color:#000
    classDef platform fill:#9CC3E5,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#F4B183,stroke:#7F2704,color:#000
    classDef svc fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFE699,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Voice Control to App Action

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant MIC as Phone Microphone
    participant ASR as On-Device STT Engine
    participant NLU as Intent Classifier
    participant ACC as Accessibility Service API
    participant UI as Target Application
    participant SP as Phone Speaker

    U->>MIC: Speaks "Open WhatsApp"
    MIC->>ASR: Audio buffer (PCM 16 kHz)
    ASR->>ASR: Feature extraction (MFCC + neural encoder)
    ASR->>NLU: Token sequence "open whatsapp"
    NLU->>NLU: Maps to Intent MAIN category LAUNCHER
    NLU->>ACC: Resolve component com.whatsapp
    ACC->>UI: Launch WhatsApp MainActivity
    UI-->>SP: Plays launch sound and speaks result
    SP-->>U: Audio confirmation
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Switch Access Scanning Loop

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Scanning: User presses switch 1
    Scanning --> GroupSelection: Highlight current group
    GroupSelection --> ItemSelection: Switch 1 selects group
    ItemSelection --> Action: Switch 2 triggers
    Action --> Idle: Action executed
    Scanning --> Scanning: Auto-advance after T_dwell
    GroupSelection --> Scanning: Switch 2 cancels
    ItemSelection --> GroupSelection: Switch 2 goes back
    Action --> [*]: Exit or timeout
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Tree: iOS vs Android Built-in Accessibility

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root[Mobile Accessibility Stacks]:::root

    root --> ios[iOS and iPadOS]:::platform
    root --> android[Android Open Source]:::platform

    ios --> vo[VoiceOver]:::svc
    ios --> vc[Voice Control]:::svc
    ios --> zoom[Magnifier]:::svc
    ios --> hac[Hearing Aid MFi]:::svc
    ios --> ga[Guided Access]:::svc
    ios --> sc[Switch Control]:::svc

    android --> tb[TalkBack]:::svc
    android --> va[Voice Access]:::svc
    android --> lf[Live Caption]:::svc
    android --> sw[Switch Access]:::svc
    android --> sel[Select to Speak]:::svc
    android --> mgn[Magnification]:::svc

    classDef root fill:#264478,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef platform fill:#9CC3E5,stroke:#003366,color:#000

```
