## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define seating technology and list its three primary clinical objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Seating technology is the engineering discipline that designs specialized seating systems for individuals with physical disabilities. Its three primary clinical objectives (the **PPF framework**) are:

1. **Posture** — maintain neutral pelvic and spinal alignment to prevent deformities.
2. **Pressure** — redistribute interface pressure below the capillary closing pressure (32 mmHg) to prevent pressure ulcers.
3. **Function** — enable the user to perform daily activities such as self-propulsion, feeding, and transfers with optimal energy efficiency.

*(1 mark each for definition, identification of three pillars, brief description.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the capillary closing pressure, and why is it clinically critical in seating design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **capillary closing pressure (CCP)** is the minimum external pressure at which blood flow through skin capillaries ceases, leading to tissue ischemia. The clinically accepted value is approximately **32 mmHg (4.27 kPa)**.

It is critical because:
- Any sustained interface pressure **above 32 mmHg** over a bony prominence will occlude capillaries and cause tissue death.
- Specialized seating cushions are engineered to keep interface pressure **below this threshold**, especially over the **ischial tuberosities and sacrum**.
- For high-risk patients (SCI), the target is even lower — **below 20 mmHg**.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark value, 1 mark clinical significance.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option (a) or (b)**

> **Choose (a) OR (b)**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **biomechanics of human sitting**, the **etiology of pressure ulcers**, and the **engineering strategies** employed in modern wheelchair cushion design to address both posture and pressure. **(14 marks)**

#### OR

**(b)** Describe the **cushion technology taxonomy** (foam, gel, air, honeycomb, custom-molded) with emphasis on **mechanism of pressure relief, clinical indications, advantages, and limitations** of each. Compare air and foam cushions for a **spinal cord injury patient at high risk of pressure ulcers**. **(14 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 14 Marks

**Part (a-1) — Biomechanics of Human Sitting [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Normal Sitting Posture [2 marks]**
- In ideal upright sitting, the pelvis is in slight **anterior tilt (~10–15°)**, preserving the lumbar lordosis.
- The **ischial tuberosities (ITs)** are the primary weight-bearing points, supporting **~75% of body weight** in upright sitting.
- The **90-90-90 rule** describes the ideal joint angles: hips 90°, knees 90°, ankles 90°.

**Step 2: Deviations in Disabled Populations [3 marks]**
- **Sagittal plane:** Posterior pelvic tilt → loss of lumbar lordosis → thoracic kyphosis → cervical hyperextension → forward head posture.
- **Coronal plane:** Pelvic obliquity → scoliosis → windswept hip deformity.
- **Transverse plane:** Pelvic rotation → functional scoliosis → asymmetric loading.

**Step 3: Postural Consequences [2 marks]**
- Compromised respiration (reduced vital capacity due to kyphosis).
- Impaired swallowing and visual horizon.
- Increased energy cost of sitting.
- Predisposition to pressure ulcers, joint contractures, and pain.

---

**Part (a-2) — Etiology of Pressure Ulcers [3 marks]**

**Step 1:** Pressure ulcer = localized skin and underlying tissue damage due to prolonged pressure, shear, friction, and moisture.

**Step 2:** Pathophysiology — when external pressure exceeds **capillary closing pressure (32 mmHg)**, ischemia occurs. After ~2 hours of unrelieved pressure, tissue necrosis begins, leading to ulceration.

**Step 3:** Risk factors — immobility, incontinence, malnutrition, sensory loss (SCI), shear forces, advanced age, and prior ulcer history.

**Step 4:** Staging (NPUAP/EPUAP):
- **Stage I:** Non-blanchable erythema.
- **Stage II:** Partial-thickness skin loss.
- **Stage III:** Full-thickness skin loss.
- **Stage IV:** Full-thickness tissue loss with exposed bone/tendon.
- **Unstageable / Suspected Deep Tissue Injury (SDTI).**

---

**Part (a-3) — Engineering Strategies in Cushion Design [4 marks]**

| Strategy | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Immersion** | Maximize depth pelvis sinks into cushion | High-profile air cushions |
| **Envelopment** | Cushion conforms to body contours | Viscoelastic memory foam |
| **Offloading** | Shift load from ITs to femorals | Custom-molded seat with ischial relief channel |
| **Shear Reduction** | Multi-directional cell movement | Roho air cells, gel cushions |
| **Alternating Pressure** | Cyclic inflation/deflation of cells | Powered air cushions (e.g., AtmosAir) |
| **Postural Stabilization** | Contoured base + lateral pelvic supports | Custom-carved foam seats |

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 14 Marks

**Part (b-1) — Cushion Technology Taxonomy [7 marks]**

**(i) Foam Cushions [1.5 marks]**
- *Material:* Polyurethane, viscoelastic (memory) foam.
- *Mechanism:* Material compression redistributes load; immersion is moderate.
- *Advantages:* Lightweight, low cost, easy fabrication, good postural stability.
- *Limitations:* "Bottoming out" over time, heat retention, limited lifespan (~1–2 years), not suitable for high-risk patients.

**(ii) Gel Cushions [1.5 marks]**
- *Material:* Viscous polymer gel enclosed in a bladder.
- *Mechanism:* Fluid displacement under load; good immersion.
- *Advantages:* Excellent comfort, good pressure distribution, temperature-stable.
- *Limitations:* Heavy, slow recovery (poor dynamic response), gel migration.

**(iii) Air Cushions [1.5 marks]**
- *Material:* Multiple interconnected neoprene air cells (Roho, Vicair).
- *Mechanism:* Air-cell deformation; highest immersion and envelopment; lowest peak pressure.
- *Advantages:* **Gold standard for SCI high-risk patients**; adjustable; dynamic; allows immersion without bottoming-out.
- *Limitations:* Puncture risk; requires daily pressure checks; poor postural support alone; needs stable base.

**(iv) Honeycomb Cushions [1 mark]**
- *Material:* Thermoplastic elastomer in geometric cell pattern.
- *Mechanism:* Controlled cell collapse.
- *Advantages:* Lightweight, durable, washable, moderate immersion.
- *Limitations:* Cost; limited postural shaping.

**(v) Custom-Molded Cushions [1.5 marks]**
- *Material:* Carved foam, vacuum-formed thermoplastic, 3D-printed polymer.
- *Mechanism:* Total surface contact via personalized shape (sometimes called "captured seat").
- *Advantages:* Best postural control; accommodates fixed deformities; consistent pressure distribution.
- *Limitations:* Expensive; non-adjustable (growth issue in pediatrics); requires skilled fabrication; non-recyclable.

---

**Part (b-2) — Air vs Foam for High-Risk SCI Patient [7 marks]**

| Parameter | Air Cushion (Roho) | Foam Cushion |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak Interface Pressure** | **<20 mmHg** (excellent) | 50–80 mmHg (moderate) |
| **Immersion** | High (8–10 cm) | Low to moderate (3–5 cm) |
| **Shear Reduction** | Excellent (cell movement) | Poor (rigid base) |
| **Postural Support** | Requires contoured base | Built-in shaping possible |
| **Maintenance** | Daily pressure check, puncture risk | Low maintenance |
| **Lifespan** | 3–5 years | 1–2 years (foam fatigue) |
| **Cost** | Higher (~₹15,000–40,000) | Lower (~₹1,500–5,000) |
| **Best for SCI** | **Yes — gold standard** | Only for low-risk, mobile patients |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For a high-risk SCI patient with complete sensory loss, the **air cushion is biomechanically superior** because it provides the lowest peak interface pressure, the best shear management, and adjustable immersion — all critical for pressure ulcer prevention. Foam cushions may be used as a **base layer** under an air cushion (hybrid) to improve postural stability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing "cushion" instead of "seating system"** — loses 1 mark for not addressing postural + functional components.
> 2. **Forgetting units** (mmHg vs kPa) in numerical questions — ½ mark deduction.
> 3. **Not stating capillary closing pressure = 32 mmHg** explicitly — critical clinical anchor.
> 4. **Confusing foam "density" with "ILD"** — density is mass/volume; ILD is the load required for 25% indentation (different concept!).
> 5. **Writing only definitions, no engineering principle** — questions ask for "design" or "strategy," not just identification.
> 6. **Skipping the pelvic stabilization principle** — every seating answer MUST begin with pelvis-first logic.
> 7. **Not mentioning shear force** alongside pressure — shear is the silent cause of deep tissue injury.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Last-Minute Revision Bullets (HIGH DENSITY):**

- **Definition anchor:** Seating technology = PPF (Posture + Pressure + Function).
- **Capillary Closing Pressure = 32 mmHg (4.27 kPa).** SCI target = <20 mmHg.
- **Ischial Tuberosities bear ~75% of body weight** in upright sitting.
- **"Stabilize the pelvis first"** — universal seating principle.
- **90-90-90 rule:** hips, knees, ankles all at 90° (ideal, not always achievable).
- **Pressure ulcer Stage IV = exposed bone/tendon** (most severe).
- **Push-up relief:** ≥15 sec every 30 min (manual). **Tilt-in-space:** ≥30° for ≥1 min (powered).
- **Air cushion (Roho) = gold standard for SCI high-risk patients.**
- **Viscoelastic foam = best for comfort and low-risk patients.**
- **Custom-molded = best for fixed deformities (CP, severe scoliosis).**
- **Gel cushions = heavy, slow recovery, good for comfort not high-risk.**
- **Honeycomb = lightweight, active users, durable.**
- **Hooke's Law F = kx** governs foam elastic region.
- **Interface pressure P = F/A** — basic engineering formula.
- **Tilt-in-space math: P(θ) = P₀ · cos(θ).** At 45°, P drops to ~70%.
- **Foam ILD ≠ Foam density.** ILD is indentation load (lbs/50 in²), density is mass/volume (kg/m³).
- **Shear force** damages tissue even at pressures below 32 mmHg — must be addressed.
- **NPUAP staging:** I (erythema), II (partial thickness), III (full thickness), IV (bone/tendon exposed).
- **RESNA standards** govern wheelchair and seating performance testing.
- **Pediatric seating** must accommodate growth — modular designs preferred.
- **Tilt-in-space ≥ recline:** tilt preserves hip angle (better for posture); recline opens hip angle (better for hygiene/catheterization).
- **Pressure mapping** is the gold-standard verification tool post-prescription.
- **24-hour postural management:** seating is part of a continuum (lying → sitting → standing).
- **Pediatric populations:** Leckey, R82, Convaid, Snug Seat — modular growth seating.
- **Active users:** rigid carbon-frame wheelchairs with custom-contoured rigid foam backs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"A correct seating prescription saves lives, prevents deformities, and restores dignity — it is the foundation of all wheelchair mobility."* Commit this hierarchy to memory: **Pelvis → Spine → Head → Limbs → Function.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seating Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Seating Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Seating Assessment** is a systematic, multidisciplinary, and client-centered clinical evaluation process used in rehabilitation engineering to determine the optimal seating system, postural support, and wheelchair configuration for an individual with physical disabilities, postural deformities, or mobility limitations. It involves the comprehensive analysis of the user's anatomical, physiological, functional, and environmental needs to prescribe a custom-molded or modular seating system that maximizes comfort, prevents secondary complications (such as pressure injuries and skeletal deformities), and enhances functional independence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Statement:** *"Seating Assessment is the structured biomechanical and clinical evaluation of an individual's posture, pressure distribution, range of motion, and functional abilities in sitting, used to design or prescribe a therapeutic seating and wheeled mobility system."*

The assessment integrates principles of **biomechanics**, **kinesiology**, **pressure mapping**, and **ergonomics** to derive a seating prescription that aligns the pelvis, trunk, and lower extremities in a stable, symmetrical, and functional position.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a patient as a **marble balanced on a curved wooden bowl** (the pelvis). If the bowl is tilted even slightly, the marble rolls. Similarly, the human pelvis is the foundation of the seated posture — if the pelvis is not level, the spine, head, and limbs will all compensate, leading to deformities, pain, and loss of function.

A **Seating Assessment** is essentially the process of:
1. **Measuring the marble** (the patient's body dimensions, joint ranges, and muscle tone).
2. **Stabilizing the bowl** (correcting the pelvic tilt).
3. **Adding a custom cushion under the marble** (pressure redistribution).
4. **Building a frame around it** (trunk and head supports) so the marble never rolls again — even when the "table" (wheelchair) moves on bumpy roads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Parallel:** Think of a tailor measuring a client for a bespoke suit. A seating specialist measures the body for a bespoke "postural suit" — the wheelchair and cushion. Off-the-shelf solutions fail; only a precise fit (assessed through proper protocols) prevents long-term damage.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (Key Metrics in Bold)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Posture** | The alignment and orientation of body segments relative to the gravitational line. |
| **Pressure** (P) | Force per unit area, **P = F / A**, measured in **mmHg** or **kPa** at seating interfaces. |
| **Pressure Injury Threshold** | Sustained tissue pressure of **> 32 mmHg** (capillary closure pressure) causing ischemia. |
| **Pelvic Obliquity** | Asymmetry of the pelvis in the frontal plane (one iliac crest higher than the other). |
| **Pelvic Tilt** | Anterior (forward) or posterior (backward) rotation of the pelvis in the sagittal plane. |
| **Sagittal Balance** | Alignment of head, shoulders, trunk, and pelvis over the ischial base of support. |
| **Mat Assessment** | Supine evaluation of joint range, muscle tone, and skeletal alignment. |
| **Cushion Interface Pressure** | The pressure measured between the buttocks and the seating cushion. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 32 mmHg Rule:** Healthy capillary blood pressure is approximately **20–32 mmHg**. If the interface pressure between the buttocks and the cushion exceeds **32 mmHg** for prolonged periods, capillaries collapse → tissue ischemia → **pressure sore (decubitus ulcer)**. This single number is the most tested fact in KTU examinations on seating.

### 1.4 Why Seating Assessment Matters in KTU Context

The course **PEBRT745 – Assistive Medical Devices** treats seating assessment as the *bridge* between the **device** (wheelchair) and the **user** (patient). Without proper assessment, even the most expensive powered wheelchair becomes a tool of harm — accelerating scoliosis, hip dislocation, and pressure injuries. With proper assessment, seating becomes **therapeutic** rather than merely supportive.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Three-Phase Model of Seating Assessment

A complete seating assessment is conducted in **three sequential phases**, each with specific objectives and clinical tools. KTU examinations frequently test these phases in both Part A (definitions) and Part B (descriptive/case-study questions).

#### **PHASE I — Mat Assessment (Supine Evaluation)**

Conducted on a **plinth/mat table** in the supine (lying) position. Gravity is eliminated, allowing the clinician to identify:
- **Skeletal alignment** (true pelvic, hip, knee, ankle positions).
- **Joint contractures** (fixed limitations in ROM).
- **Muscle tone** (spasticity, flaccidity, rigidity).
- **Spinal deformities** (scoliosis, kyphosis, lordosis).
- **Leg length discrepancy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Pearl:** *Always examine the pelvis first.* In a wheelchair-bound patient, **a level pelvis is the keystone** — every other postural correction cascades from it.

#### **PHASE II — Sitting Assessment (Simulated/Supported Seating)**

The patient is brought to a supported sitting position (on the edge of the mat or in a simulator chair) to observe:
- **Postural control** (ability to maintain upright trunk against gravity).
- **Compensatory strategies** (e.g., leaning on one elbow, forward head tilt).
- **Functional reach** and **head/trunk control**.
- **Effects of gravity** on postural deformities identified in Phase I.

#### **PHASE III — Equipment Assessment (Wheelchair & Seating Interface)**

The patient is placed in the actual (or trial) wheelchair/seating system to evaluate:
- **Pressure distribution** (using a pressure mapping system or finger palpation).
- **Cushion suitability** (foam, gel, air, hybrid).
- **Backrest angle, seat depth, seat width, legrest length**.
- **Pelvic positioners, chest straps, headrests, lateral trunk supports (LTS)**.
- **Functional mobility** in the prescribed system.

### 2.2 The Biomechanics of Sitting — Why Pressure Mapping Works

When a person sits on a flat, hard surface:
- The entire **body weight** (minus the weight of the lower legs) is transmitted through the **pelvis** (specifically the **ischial tuberosities** and surrounding soft tissue).
- The **contact area** at the buttocks is small (roughly **~25 cm²** for a typical adult sitting on a hard surface).
- Therefore, the **interface pressure** is extremely high.

$$P_{\text{interface}} = \frac{F_{\text{body}}}{A_{\text{contact}}}$$

When a properly contoured cushion is used:
- The **effective contact area (A)** is increased (deeper immersion into the cushion).
- The **load is distributed** to areas with greater tissue tolerance (thighs, trochanters).
- The **peak pressure at the ischial tuberosities** is reduced below the capillary closure threshold (**32 mmHg**).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. Every formula below has been asked in KTU university examinations.**

| # | Concept | Equation / Metric | Units | Clinical Significance |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | Interface Pressure | $P = F / A$ | mmHg, kPa, N/cm² | Direct determinant of pressure injury risk |
| 2 | Capillary Closure Pressure | $P_{\text{capillary}} \approx 32$ | mmHg | Threshold above which ischemia begins |
| 3 | Body Weight on Ischial Tuberosities | $F \approx 0.75 \times W_{\text{body}}$ in upright sitting | N, kgf | ~75% of upper body weight is borne by the pelvis |
| 4 | Seat Depth Formula | $\text{Seat Depth} = \text{Thigh Length} - 2 \text{ to } 4\,\text{cm}$ | cm | Prevents pressure behind the knee (popliteal fossa) |
| 5 | Seat Width Formula | $\text{Seat Width} = \text{Hip Width} + 2 \text{ to } 4\,\text{cm}$ | cm | Allows clothing/winter wear; prevents hip abduction constraint |
| 6 | Seat-to-Backrest Angle (Standard) | $90°$ to $100°$ | degrees | For users with good trunk control |
| 7 | Seat-to-Backrest Angle (Tilt-in-Space) | $90°$ to $120°$ | degrees | For users with poor trunk control, pressure redistribution |
| 8 | Legrest Length Formula | $\text{Legrest Length} = \text{Lower Leg Length} - \text{Cushion Thickness}$ | cm | Ensures thighs are fully supported on the seat |
| 9 | Backrest Height | $\text{Below scapulae (active)} \text{ or } \text{Above shoulders (passive)}$ | cm | Based on trunk control |
| 10 | Pressure Gradient | $\Delta P = P_{\text{ischial}} - P_{\text{thigh}}$ | mmHg | Should be positive: thighs load > ischials in ideal sitting |

> [!WARNING]
> **Avoid the vertical pipe symbol `|` in your answer scripts.** When KTU asks for absolute value, write $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX, **not** $|x|$ in plain text, as it breaks the markdown table parser.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of seating assessment are applied in:
- **Custom-molded seating systems** (e.g., for cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy).
- **Powered wheelchair prescription** with tilt-in-space, recline, and elevating legrests.
- **Office ergonomics** (every office chair uses pressure redistribution theory).
- **Automotive seating design** (driver seats use pressure-mapping-driven contouring).
- **Aircraft and spacecraft seating** (NASA uses pressure mapping for astronauts).

### 2.5 Common Postural Deviations Identified in Assessment

| Deviation | Plane | Clinical Effect | Seating Solution |
|-----------|-------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Posterior Pelvic Tilt** | Sagittal | Sacral sitting, kyphosis | Anterior pelvic stabilizer, anti-thrust cushion |
| **Anterior Pelvic Tilt** | Sagittal | Lordosis, forward trunk | Pelvic belt, contoured backrest |
| **Pelvic Obliquity** | Frontal | Scoliosis, hip subluxation | Wedge, build-up under low side |
| **Pelvic Rotation** | Transverse | Asymmetrical posture, windsweeping | Pelvic positioning belt, anti-rotation cushion |
| **Windswept Hip Deformity** | Frontal | Both knees to one side | Asymmetrical cushion, abduction block |
| **Kyphosis** | Sagittal | Forward head, reduced lung capacity | Firm backrest, chest strap |
| **Scoliosis** | Frontal | Lateral trunk lean | Lateral trunk supports (LTS), three-point force system |
| **Lordosis** | Sagittal | Excessive lumbar curve | Lumbar roll, contoured backrest |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Interface Pressure at the Ischial Tuberosities

**Problem:** A 70 kg adult sits upright on a hard wooden chair. The contact area under the ischial tuberosities is approximately **25 cm²**. Calculate the interface pressure and determine whether it exceeds the capillary closure pressure.

**Step 1 — Identify the force on the seat.**
The portion of the body weight transmitted to the pelvis in upright sitting is approximately **75% of the body mass above the seat**.

$$F = 0.75 \times m \times g$$

$$F = 0.75 \times 70\,\text{kg} \times 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$$

$$F = 0.75 \times 686.7\,\text{N}$$

$$F = 515.025\,\text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Convert contact area to SI units.**

$$A = 25\,\text{cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2 = 0.0025\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute interface pressure in Pascals.**

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{515.025\,\text{N}}{0.0025\,\text{m}^2}$$

$$P = 206{,}010\,\text{Pa} = 206.01\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mmHg (1 mmHg = 133.322 Pa).**

$$P = \frac{206{,}010}{133.322} = 1545.4\,\text{mmHg}$$

**Step 5 — Clinical interpretation.**

$$P_{\text{interface}} = 1545.4\,\text{mmHg} \gg P_{\text{capillary}} = 32\,\text{mmHg}$$

The interface pressure on a hard chair is **~48 times** the capillary closure pressure. This is why **even a few minutes** of sitting on a hard surface can begin to cause tissue damage in a person with loss of sensation (e.g., spinal cord injury).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A contoured cushion increases the contact area (A) from ~25 cm² to ~250 cm² (with immersion into foam/gel). The same force of 515 N distributed over 0.025 m² yields only **~20.6 kPa ≈ 154 mmHg** — still high, but with proper immersion and load redistribution, peak pressure at the ischials can be brought below **32 mmHg**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cushion-Induced Pressure Reduction

**Problem:** A 60 kg paraplegic patient uses a custom contoured cushion. The cushion increases the effective contact area to **600 cm²** and redistributes the load such that only **40% of the upper body weight** is borne by the ischial tuberosities. The remaining 60% is transferred to the thighs and trochanters. Calculate the new interface pressure at the ischials.

**Step 1 — Force on the pelvis.**

$$F_{\text{pelvis}} = 0.40 \times m \times g = 0.40 \times 60 \times 9.81 = 235.44\,\text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Force on the ischial tuberosities (subset of pelvis contact).**
Assume the ischials carry 50% of the pelvic load (the rest goes to surrounding soft tissue):

$$F_{\text{ischial}} = 0.50 \times 235.44 = 117.72\,\text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Effective contact area at the ischial region (typical ~150 cm² on a contoured cushion).**

$$A_{\text{ischial}} = 150\,\text{cm}^2 = 0.015\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute interface pressure at the ischials.**

$$P_{\text{ischial}} = \frac{117.72}{0.015} = 7848\,\text{Pa} = 7.848\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mmHg.**

$$P_{\text{ischial}} = \frac{7848}{133.322} = 58.87\,\text{mmHg}$$

**Step 6 — Clinical assessment.**

$$P_{\text{ischial}} = 58.87\,\text{mmHg} < 100\,\text{mmHg} \text{ (acceptable for high-risk patients with weight shifts)}$$

$$P_{\text{ischial}} = 58.87\,\text{mmHg} > 32\,\text{mmHg} \text{ (still above capillary closure)}$$

This indicates that the patient **still needs**:
1. A **pressure-relieving cushion** (air, gel, or hybrid) instead of foam alone.
2. **Regular pressure relief maneuvers** (push-ups, tilt-in-space recline every 15–30 min).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Seat Dimension Prescription

**Problem:** A pediatric patient with cerebral palsy has the following measurements:
- Hip width = **32 cm**
- Thigh length (posterior buttock to popliteal fossa) = **38 cm**
- Lower leg length (popliteal fossa to heel) = **32 cm**
- Trunk height (seat surface to top of shoulder) = **42 cm**
- Cushion thickness = **5 cm**

Calculate: (a) Seat width, (b) Seat depth, (c) Legrest length, (d) Backrest height (assuming moderate trunk control).

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Seat Width:**

$$\text{Seat Width} = \text{Hip Width} + 2\,\text{to}\,4\,\text{cm}$$

$$\text{Seat Width} = 32 + 3 = 35\,\text{cm}$$

**Step (b) — Seat Depth:**

$$\text{Seat Depth} = \text{Thigh Length} - 2\,\text{to}\,4\,\text{cm}$$

$$\text{Seat Depth} = 38 - 3 = 35\,\text{cm}$$

**Step (c) — Legrest Length:**

$$\text{Legrest Length} = \text{Lower Leg Length} - \text{Cushion Thickness}$$

$$\text{Legrest Length} = 32 - 5 = 27\,\text{cm}$$

**Step (d) — Backrest Height (moderate trunk control → below scapulae):**

$$\text{Backrest Height} = \text{Trunk Height} - 5\,\text{to}\,10\,\text{cm}$$

$$\text{Backrest Height} = 42 - 8 = 34\,\text{cm}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pressure Mapping Interpretation

| Pressure Reading (mmHg) | Color Code (Typical Mapping Software) | Clinical Status | Action Required |
|--------------------------|---------------------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **< 32** | Blue / Cool | Safe (below capillary closure) | Continue current cushion |
| **32 – 60** | Green / Yellow | Caution (prolonged sitting may cause ischemia) | Monitor, encourage weight shifts |
| **60 – 100** | Orange | High risk of pressure injury | Replace cushion with pressure-relieving type |
| **> 100** | Red | Critical — imminent tissue damage | Immediate intervention, custom-molded seating |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examinations often present a case study with a **pressure map reading** and ask the student to *interpret* the result and *prescribe* an intervention. The matrix above is your exam-ready reference.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> **Define seating assessment. List any four objectives of seating assessment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Seating assessment is a systematic clinical and biomechanical evaluation of an individual's posture, pressure distribution, joint range of motion, muscle tone, and functional abilities in the seated position, conducted to prescribe a customized seating and wheeled mobility system.

**Objectives (1.5 Marks — any four):**
1. To determine the most appropriate seating system for the user.
2. To prevent secondary complications such as pressure injuries and skeletal deformities.
3. To maximize comfort during prolonged sitting.
4. To enhance functional independence in activities of daily living.
5. To promote proper postural alignment of the pelvis, trunk, and head.
6. To optimize pressure redistribution below the capillary closure threshold of **32 mmHg**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
> **What is the significance of the 32 mmHg capillary closure pressure in seating assessment?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The capillary closure pressure is the minimum pressure required to collapse the small blood vessels (capillaries) in soft tissue. In a healthy individual, this value is approximately **20–32 mmHg**.

**Significance in seating (2 Marks):**
1. **Threshold for tissue viability:** If the interface pressure between the buttocks and the cushion exceeds 32 mmHg for a sustained period, capillaries collapse, cutting off oxygen and nutrient supply to the tissues.
2. **Pressure injury risk marker:** Sustained interface pressure > 32 mmHg leads to **ischemia**, **tissue necrosis**, and ultimately a **decubitus ulcer (pressure sore)**.
3. **Design goal of seating systems:** All therapeutic cushions, backrests, and tilt-in-space mechanisms are designed to keep the peak interface pressure **below 32 mmHg** at weight-bearing prominences (especially the ischial tuberosities and sacrum).

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

> ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Model QP]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the three phases of a complete seating assessment. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 65-year-old male patient with a complete spinal cord injury at T6 level is referred for wheelchair seating assessment. Discuss the specific postural, pressure, and functional considerations you would evaluate. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Three Phases of Seating Assessment (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Seating assessment is a structured, client-centered process performed by a multidisciplinary team (physiotherapist, occupational therapist, rehabilitation engineer, physician). It is conducted in three phases: **mat assessment, sitting assessment, and equipment assessment.**

**Phase I — Mat Assessment (2 Marks):**
Conducted on a plinth/mat table with the patient supine.
- Evaluates **true skeletal alignment** (eliminating gravitational effects).
- Measures **joint range of motion** at hips, knees, and ankles.
- Identifies **fixed contractures** (e.g., hip flexion contracture, hamstring tightness).
- Assesses **muscle tone** (spasticity, flaccidity, rigidity).
- Detects **leg length discrepancy, scoliosis, kyphosis, and pelvic asymmetry**.
- Documents the patient's *innate* posture before any seating intervention.

**Phase II — Sitting Assessment (2 Marks):**
The patient is brought to a supported sitting position on the edge of the mat or in a simulator chair.
- Evaluates **postural control** against gravity.
- Observes **compensatory strategies** (e.g., propping on one arm, lateral trunk lean).
- Assesses **head and neck control, trunk stability, and functional reach**.
- Identifies the **need for external supports** (lateral trunk supports, headrest, pelvic belt).
- Measures the patient's ability to **weight-shift** (critical for pressure relief).

**Phase III — Equipment Assessment (2 Marks):**
The patient is placed in the trial or prescribed wheelchair/seating system.
- **Pressure mapping** is performed using a sensor mat to identify peak pressure zones.
- **Cushion trial** (foam, gel, air, hybrid) to determine optimal pressure redistribution.
- Verification of **seat dimensions** (width, depth, height), **backrest angle**, and **legrest length**.
- Assessment of **functional mobility** — ability to self-propel, transfer, and perform ADLs.
- Final **seating prescription** is generated with specifications for fabrication (if custom).

---

**(b) Seating Assessment Considerations for T6 Spinal Cord Injury Patient (7 Marks):**

**Clinical Background (1 Mark):**
A patient with T6 complete spinal cord injury has **loss of sensation and motor control below the nipple line**, including the trunk, pelvis, and lower extremities. This patient is at **very high risk** for pressure injuries, postural deformities, and functional decline. The seating assessment must be highly rigorous.

**Postural Considerations (2 Marks):**
1. **Pelvic position:** Loss of trunk control → patient tends to **sacral sit (posterior pelvic tilt)**, increasing ischial pressure.
2. **Spinal alignment:** Risk of **kyphosis** due to weak trunk extensors; risk of **scoliosis** from asymmetric muscle tone.
3. **Lower extremities:** Risk of **hip flexion contracture, hamstring tightness, and ankle plantarflexion contracture** if unsupported.
4. **Head and neck:** Generally preserved, but fatigue from poor trunk support may cause forward head posture.

**Pressure Considerations (2 Marks):**
1. **Loss of sensation** → patient cannot feel pressure or pain → high risk of undetected pressure injury.
2. **Use of a pressure mapping system is essential** to identify peak ischial and sacral pressures.
3. **Cushion prescription:** A **pressure-relieving cushion** (air or hybrid) is preferred over foam.
4. **Tilt-in-space and recline mechanisms** must be built into the wheelchair for periodic pressure relief (every 15–30 minutes).

**Functional Considerations (2 Marks):**
1. **Self-propulsion:** Need to assess upper limb strength, range of motion, and ability to push a manual wheelchair.
2. **Transfers:** Evaluate sliding board transfer capability from the prescribed seating system.
3. **ADL performance:** The seating system should support trunk stability to free the upper limbs for feeding, writing, and phone use.
4. **Cushion weight and portability** (especially for active users).
5. **Durability and maintenance** of the cushion (air cushions require daily pressure checks; gel may leak).

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
A T6 SCI patient requires a **rigid-frame lightweight manual wheelchair with a tilt-in-space option, a high-quality pressure-relieving air or hybrid cushion, a contoured backrest, lateral trunk supports, and a pelvic positioning belt.** Follow-up reassessment every 6 months is mandatory.

---

> ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice)**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted Model QP]**
>
> **(a)** Define the following terms with clinical significance: (i) Pelvic obliquity, (ii) Windswept deformity, (iii) Sacral sitting, (iv) Capillary closure pressure. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the various types of wheelchair cushions used in seating systems, comparing their pressure redistribution characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Definitions with Clinical Significance (7 Marks):**

**(i) Pelvic Obliquity (1.75 Marks):**
Pelvic obliquity is a postural deviation in the **frontal (coronal) plane** where the pelvis is not level — one iliac crest is higher than the other. **Clinical significance:** It causes compensatory **scoliosis**, **hip subluxation**, **asymmetrical weight distribution** leading to pressure injury on the high side, and **functional imbalance** during sitting and propulsion. It is corrected with a **wedge build-up under the lower side** of the cushion or an **asymmetrical cushion contour**.

**(ii) Windswept Deformity (1.75 Marks):**
Windsweeping is a postural deviation in which **both knees are displaced to one side of the body** while the pelvis remains rotated in the opposite direction. **Clinical significance:** It is commonly seen in **cerebral palsy and post-stroke patients** with asymmetric spasticity. It leads to **hip subluxation/dislocation**, **scoliosis**, and severe **asymmetrical pressure loading**. It is managed with an **abduction pommel/block**, **asymmetrical cushion**, and **pelvic positioning belt**.

**(iii) Sacral Sitting (1.75 Marks):**
Sacral sitting is a posture in which the **pelvis is tilted posteriorly**, the **sacrum bears weight** instead of the ischial tuberosities, and the **lumbar spine is flattened or kyphotic**. **Clinical significance:** It is the most common cause of **sacral pressure injuries** (since the sacrum has very little soft tissue padding) and is associated with **sliding out of the wheelchair** (forward sliding). It is corrected with an **anterior pelvic stabilizer, anti-thrust cushion, and proper seat depth**.

**(iv) Capillary Closure Pressure (1.75 Marks):**
Capillary closure pressure is the minimum external pressure required to occlude the capillary blood vessels in soft tissue, typically around **32 mmHg** in healthy adults. **Clinical significance:** It is the **biomechanical threshold** for pressure injury. All seating systems are designed to keep interface pressure **below this value** at weight-bearing prominences. It is the **primary metric** used in pressure mapping interpretation.

---

**(b) Types of Wheelchair Cushions — Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Cushion Type | Material / Mechanism | Pressure Redistribution | Best For | Limitations |
|--------------|----------------------|--------------------------|----------|-------------|
| **Foam** | Polyurethane or memory foam | Moderate; compresses over time; limited immersion | Low-risk users, short-duration sitting | Bottoming out; poor durability; heat retention |
| **Gel** | Silicone or polymer gel sacs | Good; conforms to body shape; high immersion | Users needing comfort and moderate pressure relief | Heavy; can leak; requires kneading; poor stability |
| **Air** | Inflated air cells (single or multiple chambers) | Excellent; highest immersion; customizable pressure | High-risk users (SCI, burns, elderly) | Requires daily pressure checks; puncture risk; postural instability if not properly inflated |
| **Hybrid** | Combination of foam + gel, or foam + air | Very good; combines immersion and stability | Most clinical settings; balance of comfort and pressure relief | More expensive; moderate weight |
| **Custom Contoured / Molded** | Foam carved or molded to patient's body shape | Excellent; anatomical load distribution; maximum contact area | Severe deformities, fixed contractures, cerebral palsy | Expensive; long fabrication time; not adjustable; must be remade as the child grows |
| **Alternating Pressure (Dynamic)** | Air cells that cyclically inflate/deflate via a pump | Excellent; active pressure relief | Hospital and home care for high-risk immobile patients | Heavy, requires power, noisy, expensive |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
No single cushion is ideal for all patients. The choice of cushion is determined by the **patient's risk level, postural needs, functional abilities, weight, continence status, and environment**. KTU expects students to **justify the cushion choice** based on the individual assessment findings, not merely list cushion types.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Writing definitions without clinical significance:** A bare definition gets 0.5 marks. Always link the term to its **clinical consequence** and **seating intervention**.
> 2. **Ignoring units and numbers:** KTU examiners reward students who write **"32 mmHg"** instead of just "capillary pressure." Always use the numeric value.
> 3. **Confusing seat depth with seat width formula:** Seat width = hip width + clearance; Seat depth = thigh length − clearance. The signs are opposite — students frequently reverse them.
> 4. **Listing cushion types without comparison:** KTU wants a **comparative table** highlighting pressure redistribution, advantages, and limitations. A bare list gets only 2–3 marks out of 7.
> 5. **Forgetting mat assessment:** In any seating question, mentioning the **mat assessment first** (supine evaluation) is mandatory. Skipping to equipment assessment loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning follow-up:** A complete seating prescription includes **follow-up and reassessment** (every 6 months for adults, every 3–4 months for growing children). Missing this loses 1 mark.

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following 25 high-yield points before any KTU examination on this topic.**

**Core Concepts (Must Know):**
1. ✅ Seating assessment = clinical + biomechanical evaluation of posture, pressure, ROM, and function in sitting.
2. ✅ Goal = prescribe a wheelchair and cushion that **prevent pressure injuries, correct/control posture, and enhance function**.
3. ✅ Three phases = **Mat assessment → Sitting assessment → Equipment assessment**.
4. ✅ Mat assessment is done **supine** to eliminate gravity and reveal the true skeletal alignment.
5. ✅ Sitting assessment is done in a **simulator chair** to test postural control against gravity.
6. ✅ Equipment assessment is done in the **trial/prescribed wheelchair** with **pressure mapping**.
7. ✅ **Capillary closure pressure = 32 mmHg** — the cardinal number in seating.
8. ✅ Interface pressure **P = F / A** — increase area to decrease pressure.
9. ✅ Contoured cushions increase **contact area** by **immersion**, reducing peak ischial pressure.
10. ✅ Pressure mapping colors = **< 32 (safe), 32–60 (caution), 60–100 (high risk), > 100 (critical)**.

**Key Measurements (Formula Sheet):**
11. ✅ **Seat Width** = Hip Width + 2 to 4 cm.
12. ✅ **Seat Depth** = Thigh Length − 2 to 4 cm.
13. ✅ **Legrest Length** = Lower Leg Length − Cushion Thickness.
14. ✅ **Backrest Height** = Trunk Height − 5 to 10 cm (active users) or full trunk + shoulders (passive).
15. ✅ **Seat-to-backrest angle** = 90°–100° (standard) or 90°–120° (tilt-in-space).

**Key Postural Deviations (Definition + Solution):**
16. ✅ **Pelvic obliquity** = frontal-plane pelvic tilt → **wedge under low side**.
17. ✅ **Pelvic rotation** = transverse-plane pelvic asymmetry → **anti-rotation cushion, pelvic belt**.
18. ✅ **Posterior pelvic tilt (sacral sitting)** → **anterior pelvic stabilizer, anti-thrust cushion**.
19. ✅ **Anterior pelvic tilt (lordosis)** → **pelvic belt, lumbar roll, contoured backrest**.
20. ✅ **Windsweeping** → **abduction pommel, asymmetrical cushion**.
21. ✅ **Kyphosis** → **firm backrest, chest strap, headrest support**.
22. ✅ **Scoliosis** → **lateral trunk supports, three-point force system**.

**Cushion Comparison (Exam Favorite):**
23. ✅ Foam = cheap, low risk only. Gel = comfort, moderate. Air = high-risk, requires maintenance. Hybrid = balanced. Custom = severe deformities. Alternating = hospital/immobile.
24. ✅ No cushion is "best" — choice is **patient-specific** based on the assessment findings.

**Examination Etiquette:**
25. ✅ Always end with **follow-up and reassessment** (6 months adults, 3–4 months children) and **caregiver training** for cushion maintenance and pressure relief.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Intervention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Intervention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principles of Intervention** in the context of assistive medical devices refers to the **systematic, evidence-based, and ethically grounded framework** of guidelines that govern the **assessment, prescription, design, application, and evaluation** of assistive technology (AT) for individuals with functional impairments, disabilities, or chronic health conditions. These principles are rooted in the **WHO International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** model and emphasize **user-centeredness, functional independence, interdisciplinary collaboration, and quality of life** enhancement.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBRT745)** syllabus, the *Principles of Intervention* form the philosophical and methodological foundation upon which the **Human–Activity–Assistive Technology (HAAT) model**, the **Matching Person and Technology (MPT) model**, and the **Prevention–Restoration–Maintenance–Compensation (PRMC) framework** are constructed.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tailor's Measurement" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a tailor who customizes a suit.** A good tailor does not pull a random size off the rack. Instead, the tailor:
> 1. **Measures** the customer (Assessment)
> 2. **Understands the occasion** — wedding, office, sports (Goal Setting)
> 3. **Selects appropriate fabric and style** (Device Selection)
> 4. **Sews and alters** (Implementation)
> 5. **Checks the fit and adjusts** (Follow-up)
> 6. **Ensures comfort, dignity, and affordability** (Ethical & User-Centered Care)
>
> Similarly, an assistive device intervention is **never a one-size-fits-all approach** — it must be **individually tailored** to the person's body, environment, goals, and lifestyle.

---

## 1.3 Why "Principles" Matter in Intervention

The principles serve **four cardinal purposes** in clinical rehabilitation engineering:

1. **Standardization** of care across diverse patient populations.
2. **Ethical safeguard** against unnecessary or harmful device prescription.
3. **Cost-effectiveness** by ensuring resources are optimally deployed.
4. **Outcome measurement** to validate the efficacy of intervention.

> [!TIP]
> The KTU examiner frequently tests whether students can **differentiate between the Medical Model and the Social/Biopsychosocial Model of intervention.** Remember: the *Medical Model* focuses on fixing the *person*, whereas the *Biopsychosocial Model* (ICF-based) focuses on **enabling the person** by modifying the environment, task, or assistive device.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Impairment** | Loss or abnormality of body structure/function (e.g., amputated limb). |
| **Activity Limitation** | Difficulty in executing a task (e.g., cannot walk). |
| **Participation Restriction** | Difficulty in involvement in life situations (e.g., cannot go to school). |
| **Assistive Technology (AT)** | Any device, equipment, or system that maintains or improves the functioning of a person with disability. |
| **Intervention** | A purposeful action designed to achieve a specific rehabilitation outcome. |
| **Functional Independence** | The ability to perform daily activities without external assistance. |
| **Universal Design** | Design philosophy making products usable by all people without adaptation. |
| **Least Restrictive Intervention** | Choosing the most natural, least invasive option first. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Biopsychosocial Foundation — ICF Model

The **WHO ICF Model (2001)** replaced the older medical/disability model. The Principles of Intervention in assistive medical devices are **fundamentally built upon this model**.

### The Four ICF Domains

- **Body Functions and Structures** — physiological/psychological functions and anatomical parts.
- **Activities and Participation** — execution of tasks and involvement in life situations.
- **Environmental Factors** — physical, social, attitudinal environment (where AT lives!).
- **Personal Factors** — age, motivation, lifestyle, education.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The assistive device itself is classified as an **Environmental Factor (a facilitator)** in the ICF model. This is why KTU examiners emphasize that AT intervention is essentially an **environmental modification strategy**, not just a medical fix.

---

## 2.2 The PRMC Framework — Prevention, Restoration, Maintenance, Compensation

The **PRMC model** is one of the highest-yield topics for KTU module examinations.

### 2.2.1 Prevention
- Goal: **Prevent the onset or progression** of disability.
- Example: Ergonomic keyboards to prevent repetitive strain injury (RSI).
- Principle applied: **Primary healthcare** approach.

### 2.2.2 Restoration
- Goal: **Restore lost function** to the highest possible level.
- Example: Myoelectric prosthetic hand for upper-limb amputee.
- Principle applied: **Rehabilitation medicine**.

### 2.2.3 Maintenance
- Goal: **Preserve existing function** and prevent further decline.
- Example: Powered wheelchair for progressive muscular dystrophy.
- Principle applied: **Long-term care**.

### 2.2.4 Compensation
- Goal: **Substitute for permanently lost function** through alternative means.
- Example: Screen reader for blind user; voice-controlled wheelchair.
- Principle applied: **Assistive technology and adaptive strategies**.

---

## 2.3 The HAAT Model (Cook & Hussey, 1995)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Human–Activity–Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model** is the **single most frequently tested model** in KTU 2024 Scheme assessments for this module.

The four interacting components are:

1. **Human** — the person (physical, cognitive, emotional attributes).
2. **Activity** — the goal-directed task the person wants to perform.
3. **Assistive Technology** — the device or service.
4. **Context** — physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment.

**The intervention is successful only when all four components are in dynamic balance.** If any one is misaligned, the AT will be **abandoned or underutilized** — a phenomenon called **device abandonment** (reported in 30% of cases globally per WHO).

---

## 2.4 The Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Model — Marcia Scherer

Scherer's MPT framework identifies **three matching dimensions**:

- **Person's needs and preferences**
- **Technology's features and usability**
- **Environmental support and expectations**

> **Matching Equation (Conceptual):**
> $$\text{Successful AT Use} = f(\text{Person}) \times f(\text{Technology}) \times f(\text{Environment})$$
> where $f(\cdot)$ denotes the suitability function of each factor. A zero in any factor collapses the entire product to zero — illustrating the **multiplicative (not additive) nature** of intervention success.

---

## 2.5 Levels of Intervention (Public Health Perspective)

| Level | Target | Goal | Example AT |
|-------|--------|------|------------|
| **Primary** | Healthy individuals | Prevent disability | Anti-glare screen, ergonomic chair |
| **Secondary** | At-risk individuals | Early detection & intervention | Fall detection sensors for elderly |
| **Tertiary** | Persons with disability | Restore function, prevent complications | Prosthetics, orthotics |
| **Quaternary** | Chronic/terminal conditions | Palliation, comfort | Pressure-relief mattresses, ventilators |

---

## 2.6 Core Principles of Ethical Intervention

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **deduct marks** if students do not explicitly map AT intervention to the **four bioethical principles of Beauchamp and Childress**.

1. **Autonomy** — Respect the user's right to choose or refuse a device.
2. **Beneficence** — Act in the best interest of the user.
3. **Non-maleficence** — "Do no harm" (e.g., avoid ill-fitting prosthetics causing ulcers).
4. **Justice** — Equitable distribution of AT resources (especially under WHO's GATE initiative).

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Module 3)

| Concept | Key Takeaway | KTU Weightage |
|---------|--------------|---------------|
| ICF Model | Biopsychosocial, 4 domains | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| HAAT Model | 4 components must align | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| PRMC Framework | 4 intervention strategies | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| MPT Model | Scherer's 3-dimensional match | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| Medical vs Social Model | Fixing person vs enabling person | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| Levels of Intervention | Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, Quaternary | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| Bioethics | Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| Universal Design | 7 principles (Ron Mace) | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| Device Abandonment | 30% rate, caused by poor matching | ⭐ Low-Medium |
| WHO GATE Initiative | Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology | ⭐ Low-Medium |

---

## 2.8 Engineering Utility of These Principles

In real-world biomedical engineering practice, these principles translate into:

- **Design specifications** that comply with accessibility standards (e.g., **ISO 9999** for AT classification, **WCAG 2.1** for digital accessibility).
- **Human-factors engineering** inputs such as anthropometric ranges, grip strength norms, and cognitive load limits.
- **Regulatory compliance** with **FDA**, **CE Mark**, and **BIS** standards for medical devices.
- **Outcome metrics** like the **Functional Independence Measure (FIM)** and the **WHO Disability Assessment Schedule (WHODAS 2.0)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of the Intervention Process

## 3.1 The Standardized 5-Stage Intervention Process (Expanded for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

### **Stage 1: Referral and Intake**

- **Triggering Event:** A physician, teacher, caregiver, or self-referral identifies a functional limitation.
- **Action:** Demographic and clinical data are collected; the case is logged in the **rehabilitation registry**.
- **KTU Expectation:** Students should mention **multidisciplinary referral pathways** and the role of the **rehabilitation council**.

### **Stage 2: Comprehensive Assessment**

This stage is the most heavily tested. It has **three sub-domains**:

- **Body-Level Assessment** — Range of motion, muscle strength (Manual Muscle Testing, MMT), sensation, cognition (MMSE/MoCA).
- **Activity-Level Assessment** — Functional Independence Measure (FIM), Barthel Index, Goal Attainment Scaling (GAS).
- **Participation-Level Assessment** — Community Integration Questionnaire (CIQ), WHO Disability Assessment Schedule (WHODAS 2.0).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The ICF assessment ladder moves from **Impairment → Activity Limitation → Participation Restriction → Contextual Factors**. Students who write answers in this order automatically score the highest CO mapping marks in KTU rubrics.

### **Stage 3: Goal Setting (SMART Goals)**

Goals must be:

- **S**pecific
- **M**easurable
- **A**chievable
- **R**elevant
- **T**ime-bound

Example: "Patient will independently transfer from bed to wheelchair using a sliding board within **2 weeks** of intervention."

### **Stage 4: Intervention Implementation (The PRMC Selection)**

Here, the practitioner selects the **appropriate PRMC strategy** and **HAAT-aligned device**. The decision matrix is:

$$
\text{Intervention Strategy} = \arg\max_{s \in \{P, R, M, C\}} \left[ \text{Effectiveness}(s) - \text{Cost}(s) - \text{Risk}(s) \right]
$$

where the chosen strategy $s$ maximizes the net benefit.

### **Stage 5: Evaluation and Follow-up**

- **Short-term:** Device fit, comfort, immediate functional gain.
- **Medium-term:** Goal attainment (GAS score), user satisfaction (QUEST 2.0 — Quebec User Evaluation of Satisfaction with Assistive Technology).
- **Long-term:** Community participation, device retention vs. abandonment.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Choosing the Right Wheelchair

**Case:** A 65-year-old male with **late-stage Parkinson's disease** cannot walk independently, has postural instability, and lives in a small rented apartment with his wife.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Decision:

**Step 1: Identify ICF Domains Affected**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Body Function} &: \text{Postural instability, bradykinesia, tremor} \\
\text{Activity Limitation} &: \text{Cannot ambulate independently} \\
\text{Participation Restriction} &: \text{Cannot visit market or temple} \\
\text{Environment} &: \text{Narrow doors, rented apartment}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Map to PRMC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prevention} &: \text{Fall prevention, pressure-sore prevention} \\
\text{Restoration} &: \text{Not feasible (progressive disease)} \\
\text{Maintenance} &: \text{Yes — preserve upper limb function} \\
\text{Compensation} &: \text{Yes — substitute ambulation with wheelchair}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply HAAT Alignment**

- **Human:** Cognitive intact, but tremor and rigidity → needs **easy-to-operate joystick**, not sip-and-puff.
- **Activity:** Indoor community visits, prayer at temple → needs **foldable, lightweight design**.
- **Technology:** A **powered wheelchair with tilt-in-space and foldable frame** is selected.
- **Context:** Narrow doors (~750 mm) → device width must be **< 650 mm**.

**Step 4: Verification**

$$
\text{Door width clearance} = 750 \text{ mm} - 100 \text{ mm buffer} = 650 \text{ mm}
$$

The selected chair (typically **600 mm wide**) satisfies the constraint.

---

## 3.3 The Matching Person and Technology (MPT) — Quantitative Illustration

The **MPT matching score** can be conceptualized as:

$$
\text{MPT Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot m_i
$$

where:

- $n$ = number of relevant variables (e.g., physical, sensory, cognitive, motivational, environmental)
- $w_i$ = weight (importance) of variable $i$
- $m_i$ = match score (0 to 1) of variable $i$
- $m_i = 1$ → perfect match, $m_i = 0$ → total mismatch

**Threshold for successful intervention:**

$$
\text{MPT Score} \geq 0.75
$$

If the score is below 0.75, the intervention is likely to **fail or be abandoned**.

**Example Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MPT Score} &= (0.3 \times 0.9) + (0.2 \times 0.8) + (0.2 \times 0.7) + (0.15 \times 0.85) + (0.15 \times 0.6) \\
&= 0.27 + 0.16 + 0.14 + 0.1275 + 0.09 \\
&= 0.7875
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.7875 \geq 0.75$, the intervention is **predicted to succeed**.

---

## 3.4 Failure Mode Analysis — Why Interventions Fail

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Mitigation Principle |
|--------------|------------|----------------------|
| Device abandonment | Poor user involvement in selection | Person-centered design |
| Underutilization | Inadequate training | Comprehensive training protocol |
| Pressure ulcers from prosthetic | Poor fitting | Periodic re-assessment |
| Cultural rejection | Imposed Western technology | Culturally sensitive design |
| Device breakdown | Lack of local maintenance | Sustainable service model |
| Stigma | Visible, conspicuous device | Aesthetically integrated design |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define the Principles of Intervention in the context of Assistive Medical Devices. Mention any three core principles.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Principles of Intervention are the **systematic, ethical, and evidence-based guidelines** that govern the selection, design, application, and evaluation of assistive medical devices for individuals with functional impairments.

**Three core principles are:**

1. **User-Centeredness** — The intervention must revolve around the unique needs, goals, and lifestyle of the individual user.
2. **Function-Based Approach** — The focus is on enabling activity and participation, not merely correcting the impairment (ICF model).
3. **Least Restrictive Intervention** — The simplest, most natural, and least invasive option should be tried first before progressing to more complex solutions.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three principles with brief explanation.)*

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between the Medical Model and the Biopsychosocial Model of intervention.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Medical Model | Biopsychosocial Model (ICF) |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------------|
| **Focus** | Pathology/Impairment in the person | Functioning in context |
| **Goal** | Cure or fix the person | Enable participation through environmental and personal support |
| **Role of AT** | Auxiliary support | Core environmental facilitator |
| **Outcome Metric** | Symptom reduction | Quality of life, participation, independence |
| **Decision-Making** | Physician-led | User-led, interdisciplinary team |

*(Award 1 mark each for the three correct contrasting points.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

> **"Intervention in assistive medical devices is not merely device prescription but a multi-stage, person-centered, and ethically driven process."**
> **(a)** Explain the **PRMC framework** of intervention with suitable examples for each category. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Describe the **HAAT model** and explain how mismatch in any one component leads to **device abandonment**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — PRMC Framework (7 Marks)**

The **PRMC framework** classifies intervention strategies into four mutually supportive categories:

**1. Prevention (1.5 Marks)**
- Goal: To **prevent the onset or progression** of disability.
- Principle: Primary healthcare philosophy.
- Example: Provision of **ergonomic split keyboards** to software professionals to prevent carpal tunnel syndrome; **anti-glare eyewear** for visually-intensive workers.

**2. Restoration (1.5 Marks)**
- Goal: To **restore lost function** to the highest possible level.
- Principle: Active rehabilitation.
- Example: A **myoelectric prosthetic hand** for a person with below-elbow amputation; **cochlear implant** for a child with congenital sensorineural hearing loss.

**3. Maintenance (2 Marks)**
- Goal: To **preserve existing function** and prevent secondary complications.
- Principle: Chronic disease management.
- Example: A **powered wheelchair with tilt-in-space** for a person with progressive muscular dystrophy to prevent scoliosis and pressure ulcers; **replacement of worn prosthetic sockets** every 12–18 months.

**4. Compensation (2 Marks)**
- Goal: To **substitute permanently lost function** through alternative means.
- Principle: Adaptive and assistive strategies.
- Example: **Screen readers (JAWS, NVDA)** for blind users; **sip-and-puff controlled wheelchairs** for high-level quadriplegics; **voice-controlled smart home systems** for persons with motor neuron disease.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — HAAT Model & Device Abandonment (7 Marks)**

The **Human–Activity–Assistive Technology (HAAT) model** (Cook & Hussey, 1995) views successful AT use as the product of four interacting components:

$$
\text{Successful AT Use} = f(H, A, T, C)
$$

where $H$ = Human, $A$ = Activity, $T$ = Technology, $C$ = Context. (1 Mark)

**The four components (3 Marks):**

1. **Human** — Physical, cognitive, sensory, and emotional attributes of the user.
2. **Activity** — The goal-directed task the user wants to perform (ADL, IADL, education, work, leisure).
3. **Assistive Technology** — The device, software, or service.
4. **Context** — Physical, social, cultural, institutional, and economic environment.

**Mismatch and Device Abandonment (3 Marks):**

Device abandonment is defined as **non-use of a prescribed device for 3 or more consecutive months**. According to WHO, **30% of all prescribed AT is abandoned** due to misalignment in the HAAT model.

**Mismatch Examples:**

- **Human–Technology Mismatch:** A visually-impaired elderly user is prescribed a **screen reader requiring 4 GB RAM**, but their device has only 2 GB → abandonment.
- **Activity–Technology Mismatch:** A **manual hand-cycle** prescribed for a rural farmer who needs to travel on dirt roads (intended for urban paved use) → abandonment.
- **Context–Technology Mismatch:** A **power wheelchair** prescribed for a household using narrow traditional doorways (~700 mm) → cannot navigate indoors → abandonment.
- **Human–Context Mismatch:** Family stigma, lack of caregiver training, or religious beliefs → AT is locked in a closet → abandonment.

**The intervention succeeds only when all four HAAT components are in dynamic equilibrium**, which is why **comprehensive assessment and follow-up** are non-negotiable components of the intervention process.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Alternative Choice]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **WHO ICF Model** and show how an assistive device is classified within it. Why has the ICF model replaced the older medical model? **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **four bioethical principles** that must guide every assistive device intervention. Give one example for each principle from the field of rehabilitation engineering. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — WHO ICF Model (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)**, published by WHO in 2001, is a **biopsychosocial framework** that classifies health and disability at the level of **body, person, and society**.

**Block Diagram of ICF (3 Marks):**

The ICF comprises two main parts, each with two components:

- **Part 1: Functioning and Disability**
  - (i) **Body Functions and Structures** (Impairments)
  - (ii) **Activities and Participation** (Limitations / Restrictions)
- **Part 2: Contextual Factors**
  - (i) **Environmental Factors** (Facilitators / Barriers — *where AT fits*)
  - (ii) **Personal Factors** (Motivation, lifestyle, age)

All four components interact dynamically. (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1 for visual representation.)

**Classification of AT within ICF (1.5 Marks):**
An **assistive device is classified as an Environmental Factor** that acts as a **Facilitator** in the ICF taxonomy (coded as `e115` for products and technology for personal use in daily living). This is a **paradigm shift** from the medical model, where the device was considered an "auxiliary medical aid."

**Why ICF Replaced the Medical Model (1.5 Marks):**

- The medical model **pathologized disability** as residing within the person; ICF **externalizes** it as a mismatch between person and environment.
- ICF integrates **participation and quality of life**, not just impairment reduction.
- ICF is **universally applicable** across cultures, ages, and conditions, making it the **standard WHO reference** for rehabilitation policy.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — Bioethical Principles (7 Marks)**

The four principles (Beauchamp and Childress, 1979) are:

**1. Autonomy (1.75 Marks)**
- Definition: Respecting the user's right to **self-determined decisions**.
- Example: A 22-year-old bilateral above-knee amputee **refuses** a wheelchair and insists on learning to use **bilateral running prostheses** for sports. The rehabilitation engineer must respect this choice, provide training, and **not impose** a wheelchair.

**2. Beneficence (1.75 Marks)**
- Definition: Acting in the **best interest** of the user.
- Example: Prescribing a **custom-molded seating system** for a child with cerebral palsy to prevent scoliosis, improve feeding posture, and enable school participation — clearly beneficial.

**3. Non-Maleficence (1.75 Marks)**
- Definition: **"Do no harm"** — avoiding injury, discomfort, or secondary complications.
- Example: An ill-fitting prosthetic socket causing **pressure ulcers** must be immediately remolded; continuing use would cause osteomyelitis and further harm.

**4. Justice (1.75 Marks)**
- Definition: **Equitable distribution** of limited AT resources.
- Example: Under WHO's **GATE initiative**, prioritizing low-cost, high-impact AT (e.g., wheelchairs, prosthetics, hearing aids) for **Low- and Middle-Income Countries (LMICs)** before funding advanced robotic exoskeletons that serve only a privileged few.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing HAAT with ICF:** HAAT is the *intervention model*; ICF is the *classification model*. Do not use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Treating AT as a "medical fix":** Always position the device as an *environmental facilitator* (ICF language) — this is worth 2–3 easy marks.
> 3. **Skipping examples:** Every principle MUST be backed by a clinical example. A definition without an example scores only half marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the assessment ladder:** Always assess in the order: *Impairment → Activity → Participation → Context*. Examiners explicitly look for this sequencing.
> 5. **Ignoring device abandonment:** Almost every KTU question on the HAAT model expects a discussion of abandonment causes.
> 6. **Missing the ICF diagram:** In 14-mark answers, an ICF or HAAT diagram is **mandatory** — without it, 1 to 2 marks are typically deducted under the "presentation" CO.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> Print this section and revise the night before the KTU exam.

- ✅ **Principles of Intervention** = systematic, ethical, evidence-based guidelines for AT prescription.
- ✅ The **Medical Model** fixes the *person*; the **Biopsychosocial (ICF) Model** modifies the *environment*.
- ✅ **ICF has 4 domains:** Body Functions/Structures, Activities/Participation, Environmental Factors, Personal Factors.
- ✅ **AT is classified as an Environmental Facilitator** in the ICF framework.
- ✅ **PRMC = Prevention, Restoration, Maintenance, Compensation** — the four intervention strategies.
- ✅ **HAAT Model** has 4 components: **H**uman, **A**ctivity, **A**ssistive **T**echnology, **C**ontext.
- ✅ HAAT must be in **dynamic balance**; mismatch leads to **device abandonment (~30% rate)**.
- ✅ **MPT Model (Scherer)** evaluates 3 matching dimensions: Person, Technology, Environment.
- ✅ **Levels of intervention:** Primary (prevent), Secondary (early), Tertiary (rehab), Quaternary (palliative).
- ✅ **4 Bioethical Principles:** Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-Maleficence, Justice.
- ✅ **Universal Design** (Ron Mace) = 7 principles for inclusive product design.
- ✅ **WHO GATE Initiative** = Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology (equitable access).
- ✅ **Standardized assessment tools:** FIM, Barthel Index, QUEST 2.0, WHODAS 2.0, GAS.
- ✅ **Successful AT use** is a **multiplicative**, not additive, function of Person, Technology, and Environment.
- ✅ **SMART Goals** are mandatory in KTU intervention frameworks.
- ✅ **Device abandonment** is the most clinically and economically significant failure mode.
- ✅ KTU expects **diagrams** (ICF, HAAT, PRMC) in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheeled Mobility: Supporting Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wheeled Mobility: Supporting Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **supporting structure** of a wheeled mobility device (wheelchair) is the **load-bearing chassis** that transfers all static and dynamic loads from the occupant, the seat, the backrest, and the armrests down to the wheels and ultimately to the ground. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the supporting structure encompasses the **side frames, cross-brace (or scissor linkage), axle plates, seat rails, push-handles, and footrest hangers**, all of which collectively form the structural skeleton of the wheelchair.

The supporting structure must comply with international performance standards, primarily:
- **ISO 7176-5** (Determination of overall dimensions, mass, and turning space)
- **ISO 7176-8** (Static, impact, and fatigue strengths)
- **RESNA WC-1 / WC-2** (American National Standards for wheelchairs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The supporting structure is NOT the cushion or the upholstery. It is the rigid metallic/composite framework that determines the wheelchair's **weight capacity (typically 113 kg for standard adult, 158 kg for heavy-duty)**, **stiffness**, **durability**, and **folding/portability characteristics**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the wheelchair supporting structure like the **chassis of a car** or the **skeleton of a human body**:
- Just as a car chassis holds the engine, seats, and wheels together and absorbs road shocks, the wheelchair frame holds the user, cushion, and wheels together and absorbs bumps from sidewalks and ramps.
- Just as a human skeleton determines posture and supports weight, the wheelchair frame determines the user's seated posture (seat angle, seat-to-back angle, leg position) and supports their entire body weight.

A poorly designed frame is like a **bicycle with a wobbly frame** — it flexes under load, causes discomfort, wastes energy during propulsion, and may eventually fail catastrophically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The "supporting structure" is the *static* framework, while the "wheels, casters, and propulsion system" belong to the *mobility* subsystem. They are mechanically coupled (via the axle plate) but functionally distinct.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard occupant mass for testing:** **113 kg (250 lb)** per ISO 7176-8
- **Standard drop-test height for frame fatigue:** **10 cm repeated 6,667 cycles**
- **Minimum frame fatigue life target:** **≈ 3 years of daily use**
- **Typical frame mass:** **8–15 kg (manual), 15–30 kg (powered)**
- **Young's Modulus of common frame materials:** Steel ≈ **200 GPa**, Aluminum 6061-T6 ≈ **69 GPa**, Titanium Grade 9 ≈ **105 GPa**, Carbon Fiber Composite ≈ **70–150 GPa (directional)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of the Supporting Structure

The supporting structure can be decomposed into **four primary sub-assemblies**, each with a distinct load-transfer function:

### A. Side Frames (Left & Right)
- Two mirror-image longitudinal members running parallel to the user's body.
- Carry the **primary bending moment** caused by the user's weight (acting vertically through the seat) and the **reaction forces** at the rear wheels and front casters.
- Constructed as **welded tubular structures** (steel/aluminum) or **monocoque molded composites** (high-end sports wheelchairs).

### B. Cross-Brace (Scissor Linkage)
- The horizontal X-shaped or single-bar linkage connecting the two side frames.
- Provides **lateral rigidity** (resists twisting) and enables **folding** in folding wheelchairs.
- Subjected primarily to **axial compression** and **torsional shear** when the chair is loaded asymmetrically (e.g., user leans to one side, or one wheel hits a curb).

### C. Axle Plate (Axle Bracket)
- The mounting interface between the side frame and the rear wheel quick-release axle.
- Permits **adjustment of the rear-wheel position** (fore-aft and vertical) to alter the **wheelbase**, **seat angle**, and **center-of-gravity alignment** — critical for maneuverability and tip-resistance.
- The **adjustability range** is typically **± 5 cm horizontally** and **± 3 cm vertically** around the neutral position.

### D. Seat Rails & Backrest Posts
- Linear members welded to the side frames that support the **seat sling (fabric or rigid panel)** and the **backrest**.
- The seat-to-back angle is typically **90°–105°** (standard chairs) or **70°–90°** (recliner/tilt-in-space chairs).

## 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How" Behind Load Transfer

When a user of mass **m** sits in the wheelchair, the gravitational force **F_g = m·g** acts downward through the approximate center of mass (CoM), located near the **user's navel** at roughly **0.45 × seat depth** from the backrest.

This force is transferred as follows:
1. **F_g** acts on the seat sling.
2. The seat sling is supported by the **seat rails** → transfers load to **side frames**.
3. The side frames channel the load to **two reaction points**: the **rear axle(s)** and the **front caster forks**.
4. The **cross-brace** resists any tendency of the two side frames to splay apart (lateral racking) when the user shifts weight side-to-side or negotiates uneven terrain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a properly designed frame, the side frames act as **simply-supported beams** between the rear axle and the front caster. The cross-brace functions as a **tension/compression tie** that converts the lateral splaying moment into pure axial forces.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Gravitational load on frame | $F_g = m \cdot g$ | $m$ in kg, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$, $F_g$ in N |
| 2 | Maximum bending moment on side frame | $M_{max} = \dfrac{F_g \cdot L}{4}$ (central load) or $M_{max} = F_g \cdot a$ (overhang) | $L$ = wheelbase, $a$ = lever arm, units N·m |
| 3 | Bending stress (flexure formula) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | $c$ = distance to outer fiber, $I$ = 2nd moment of area, $m^4$ |
| 4 | 2nd moment of area (hollow tube) | $I = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( r_o^4 - r_i^4 \right)$ | $r_o, r_i$ = outer/inner radii, $m^4$ |
| 5 | Section modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{r_o^4 - r_i^4}{r_o}$ | Units $m^3$ |
| 6 | Deflection of side frame (point load at center) | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{F \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | $E$ = Young's Modulus, units Pa |
| 7 | Euler buckling load for cross-brace | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{(K \cdot L)^2}$ | $K$ = effective length factor (0.5–1.0) |
| 8 | Factor of Safety | $FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Dimensionless; design target $\geq 3.0$ for wheelchairs |
| 9 | Von Mises equivalent stress (combined loading) | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | $\sigma$ = normal, $\tau$ = shear stress, Pa |
| 10 | Combined stress (bending + axial) | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{F}{A} \pm \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | $A$ = cross-sectional area |
| 11 | Tip-over (anti-tip) safety moment | $M_{tip} = W \cdot d_{CoG}$ | $W$ = total weight, $d_{CoG}$ = horizontal distance from rear axle |
| 12 | Fatigue life (Basquin's law) | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \cdot (2N_f)^b$ | $N_f$ = cycles to failure, $b$ ≈ -0.1 for steel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - For a **side frame treated as a simply-supported beam**: reactions at the rear axle and front caster sum to $F_g$ (static equilibrium).
> - For a **cross-brace treated as a pin-ended column**: $K = 1.0$ in Euler's buckling equation.
> - The **standard test load** is **113 kg** and the **cycle count** is **6,667 drops from 10 cm** (per ISO 7176-8).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The supporting structure is the **single most critical component** for wheelchair longevity and user safety. Failure modes directly traceable to inadequate frame design include:
- **Fatigue cracking at weld joints** (a common recall cause in budget wheelchairs).
- **Cross-brace buckling** under obese users (drives the heavy-duty category).
- **Excessive frame flex** that absorbs 15–20% of the user's propulsion energy (a key differentiator in sports wheelchair design).
