# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Bending Stress on the Side Frame

**Scenario:** A user of mass **$m = 90$ kg** is seated centrally in a standard manual wheelchair. The wheelbase (rear axle to front caster) is **$L = 0.50$ m**. The side frame is approximated as a simply-supported beam of length $L$ with a central point load.

**Step 1 — Compute the gravitational load:**

$$
F_g = m \cdot g = 90 \times 9.81 = 882.9 \; N
$$

**Step 2 — Identify support reactions:**

By symmetry, each support (rear axle + front caster pair) carries half the load.

$$
R_{rear} = R_{front} = \dfrac{F_g}{2} = \dfrac{882.9}{2} = 441.45 \; N
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum bending moment:**

For a simply-supported beam with a central point load, the maximum bending moment occurs at mid-span.

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{F_g \cdot L}{4} = \dfrac{882.9 \times 0.50}{4} = 110.36 \; N \cdot m
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the section modulus of a typical aluminum side-frame tube:**

Assume 6061-T6 aluminum tubing with outer diameter $D_o = 25.4$ mm and wall thickness $t = 2.0$ mm.

$$
r_o = \dfrac{D_o}{2} = 12.7 \; mm = 0.0127 \; m
$$

$$
r_i = r_o - t = 12.7 - 2.0 = 10.7 \; mm = 0.0107 \; m
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{r_o^4 - r_i^4}{r_o}
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{(0.0127)^4 - (0.0107)^4}{0.0127}
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{2.6016 \times 10^{-8} - 1.3119 \times 10^{-8}}{0.0127}
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1.2897 \times 10^{-8}}{0.0127} = 7.976 \times 10^{-7} \; m^3
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the maximum bending stress:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{110.36}{7.976 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.384 \times 10^{8} \; Pa = 138.4 \; MPa
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Factor of Safety:**

Yield strength of 6061-T6 aluminum: $\sigma_{yield} = 240 \; MPa$.

$$
FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{max}} = \dfrac{240}{138.4} = 1.73
$$

**Step 7 — Verdict:**

Since $FoS = 1.73 < 3.0$, the design is **NOT safe** for a long-life fatigue application. The engineer must either:
- Increase the wall thickness (e.g., $t = 3.0$ mm → $Z \approx 1.18 \times 10^{-6} \, m^3$ → $\sigma \approx 93.5$ MPa → $FoS \approx 2.57$, still marginal), or
- Switch to a higher-strength alloy (e.g., 7075-T6 with $\sigma_{yield} = 503$ MPa), or
- Add an additional cross-member to reduce the effective span $L$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Deflection of the Side Frame

Continuing with the same 6061-T6 tube ($E = 69$ GPa), compute the mid-span deflection under the 882.9 N central load:

**Step 1 — Compute the 2nd moment of area:**

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( r_o^4 - r_i^4 \right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( (0.0127)^4 - (0.0107)^4 \right)
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 1.2897 \times 10^{-8} = 1.0131 \times 10^{-8} \; m^4
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the central-point-load deflection formula:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{F \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I}
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{882.9 \times (0.50)^3}{48 \times 69 \times 10^9 \times 1.0131 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{882.9 \times 0.125}{33.55} = \dfrac{110.36}{33.55} = 3.29 \times 10^{-3} \; m
$$

$$
\delta_{max} \approx 3.29 \; mm
$$

**Step 3 — Acceptance check:**

A common ergonomic guideline is that wheelchair frame deflection should not exceed **$\delta_{max} \leq L/200$** to avoid energy loss during propulsion.

$$
\dfrac{L}{200} = \dfrac{0.50}{200} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \; m = 2.5 \; mm
$$

Since **3.29 mm > 2.5 mm**, the deflection marginally exceeds the guideline. The design needs stiffening.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for the Supporting Structure

The choice of frame material is a **multi-objective optimization** problem. The following matrix summarizes the trade-offs among the four principal candidates (scored on a 1–5 scale; 5 = best):

| Selection Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Steel (4130) | Aluminum (6061-T6) | Titanium (Grade 9) | Carbon Fiber Composite |
|---------------------|--------------|--------------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Density (lower is better) | 0.20 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| Stiffness (higher $E$ is better) | 0.25 | 5 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| Yield strength | 0.20 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 5 |
| Fatigue resistance | 0.15 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| Manufacturability (weld/form) | 0.10 | 5 | 5 | 2 | 1 |
| Cost (lower is better) | 0.10 | 5 | 4 | 1 | 1 |
| **Weighted score** | **1.00** | **3.95** | **3.45** | **3.75** | **3.95** |

**Interpretation:**
- **Steel** and **Carbon Fiber** tie at the top, but for very different reasons: steel wins on cost/manufacturability, carbon fiber wins on weight/performance.
- **Aluminum 6061-T6** is the most common commercial choice (bariatric wheelchairs, mid-range sports) due to its balanced score and ease of TIG welding.
- **Titanium** is reserved for high-end custom rigid chairs (cycling, rugby) where fatigue life and corrosion resistance justify the cost premium.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**"List and briefly explain any three primary sub-assemblies of a wheelchair supporting structure, indicating the type of load each is designed to resist."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Side Frames (1 mark):** Two mirror-image longitudinal members. They resist the **primary bending moment** caused by the user's weight acting vertically between the rear axle and the front caster. Constructed from welded steel/aluminum tubing.

2. **Cross-Brace / Scissor Linkage (1 mark):** Horizontal X-shaped or single-bar member connecting the side frames. Resists **lateral splaying forces** and **torsional racking** when the user shifts weight side-to-side. Subjected to **axial compression** (and is therefore susceptible to **Euler buckling** if undersized).

3. **Axle Plate (1 mark):** Mounting interface between the side frame and the rear-wheel quick-release axle. Resists **vertical reaction forces** transmitted from the rear wheels and provides **adjustability** of the wheelbase to tune the center of gravity.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]
**"What are the key material-selection criteria for a wheelchair supporting structure? Why is 6061-T6 aluminum the most widely used material for mid-range wheelchairs?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The key material-selection criteria are: **(1) low density** (for user-propelled chairs, frame mass is unsprung weight), **(2) high specific stiffness** (stiffness-to-weight ratio), **(3) high yield strength** (for adequate factor of safety), **(4) good fatigue resistance** (≥ 3 years of cyclic loading), **(5) manufacturability** (weldability, formability), and **(6) cost** (1 mark).

6061-T6 aluminum offers a **balanced combination of these criteria** (1 mark): it has a density of 2.7 g/cm³ (≈ 1/3 that of steel), a yield strength of 240 MPa (adequate after proper design), excellent TIG weldability, good corrosion resistance, and is significantly cheaper than titanium or carbon fiber composites (1 mark). It is therefore the **"sweet spot"** material for mid-range manual wheelchairs.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**"A standard manual wheelchair with a rigid steel side frame has a wheelbase of 0.55 m between the rear axle and the front caster. A user of mass 100 kg is seated centrally. The side-frame tube is a hollow circular section with outer diameter 28 mm and wall thickness 2.5 mm, made of mild steel with $E = 200$ GPa and $\sigma_{yield} = 250$ MPa. (a) Compute the maximum bending stress in the side frame and the factor of safety. (b) Compute the mid-span deflection and comment on whether it is acceptable under the ergonomic guideline of $\delta_{max} \leq L/200$."**

#### Part (a) Solution — Bending Stress & Factor of Safety (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute gravitational load.

$$
F_g = m \cdot g = 100 \times 9.81 = 981 \; N
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute maximum bending moment (simply-supported, central load).

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{F_g \cdot L}{4} = \dfrac{981 \times 0.55}{4} = 134.89 \; N \cdot m
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute the section modulus.

$$
r_o = \dfrac{0.028}{2} = 0.014 \; m, \quad r_i = 0.014 - 0.0025 = 0.0115 \; m
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{r_o^4 - r_i^4}{r_o} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{(0.014)^4 - (0.0115)^4}{0.014}
$$

$$
(0.014)^4 = 3.8416 \times 10^{-8}, \quad (0.0115)^4 = 1.7490 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times \dfrac{2.0926 \times 10^{-8}}{0.014} = 1.1741 \times 10^{-6} \; m^3
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute maximum bending stress.

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{134.89}{1.1741 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.149 \times 10^{8} \; Pa \approx 114.9 \; MPa
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Compute factor of safety.

$$
FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{max}} = \dfrac{250}{114.9} = 2.18
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** $FoS = 2.18$ is **marginally below** the recommended value of 3.0 for a long-life fatigue application. The design is statically safe but should be reinforced (e.g., thicker wall, additional cross-member, or use of high-strength steel).

#### Part (b) Solution — Deflection (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Compute 2nd moment of area.

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( r_o^4 - r_i^4 \right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 2.0926 \times 10^{-8} = 1.6435 \times 10^{-8} \; m^4
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Apply central-point-load deflection formula.

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{F_g \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I} = \dfrac{981 \times (0.55)^3}{48 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 1.6435 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Numerical evaluation.

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{981 \times 0.166375}{157.78} = \dfrac{163.22}{157.78} = 1.034 \times 10^{-3} \; m \approx 1.03 \; mm
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Apply the ergonomic guideline.

$$
\dfrac{L}{200} = \dfrac{0.55}{200} = 2.75 \times 10^{-3} \; m = 2.75 \; mm
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion.

Since $\delta_{max} = 1.03 \; mm < 2.75 \; mm$, the deflection is **well within** the ergonomic guideline. Steel's high Young's modulus (200 GPa) compensates for its higher density, making it stiffness-efficient for wheelchair frames.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]**
**"(a) With a neat sketch, describe the function and typical load-distribution pattern of the cross-brace in a folding wheelchair. Why is the cross-brace the most fatigue-critical member? (b) The cross-brace of a folding wheelchair is approximated as a pin-ended circular tube column of length 0.42 m, outer diameter 19 mm, and wall thickness 1.5 mm, made of aluminum 6061-T6 ($E = 69$ GPa, $\sigma_{yield} = 240$ MPa). Compute the Euler buckling load and the corresponding factor of safety under a worst-case lateral load of 600 N applied by the user."**

#### Part (a) Solution — Cross-Brace Function & Fatigue (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Function.

The cross-brace is a horizontal linkage (single-bar, X-shaped, or scissor-type) that connects the left and right side frames. Its primary function is to maintain the **lateral spacing** (typically 0.40–0.50 m) between the side frames when the chair is unfolded, while permitting the chair to be folded for transport.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Load-distribution pattern.

When a user shifts weight laterally (e.g., reaching to one side, or during a one-handed push), the loaded side frame attempts to **splay outward** in the horizontal plane. The cross-brace converts this splaying moment into a **pure axial compressive force** (or tension, depending on the geometry) along its length. The force is roughly:

$$
P_{brace} = \dfrac{F_{lateral} \cdot h}{2 \cdot w_{frame}}
$$

where $h$ is the height of the load application point on the side frame and $w_{frame}$ is the lateral spacing of the side frames.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Why it is fatigue-critical.

The cross-brace is fatigue-critical for three reasons:
- It is **subjected to cyclic loading** every push-stroke (≈ 1–2 cycles per second for typical propulsion).
- It is **geometrically a slender column** (high length-to-diameter ratio), making it susceptible to **Euler buckling** at relatively low compressive loads.
- **Welded or crimped joints** at the pivot points act as **stress concentrators** and are common crack-initiation sites.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Sketch reference.

(Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 illustrates the lateral tie between side frames via the cross-brace.)

#### Part (b) Solution — Euler Buckling & FoS (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the slenderness regime.

Compute the slenderness ratio to confirm that Euler's formula is applicable (slenderness ratio must be greater than the transition slenderness).

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute geometric properties.

$$
r_o = \dfrac{0.019}{2} = 0.0095 \; m, \quad r_i = 0.0095 - 0.0015 = 0.0080 \; m
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( (0.0095)^4 - (0.0080)^4 \right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( 8.145 \times 10^{-9} - 4.096 \times 10^{-9} \right)
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 4.049 \times 10^{-9} = 3.180 \times 10^{-9} \; m^4
$$

$$
A = \pi \left( r_o^2 - r_i^2 \right) = \pi \left( (0.0095)^2 - (0.0080)^2 \right) = \pi \times 2.625 \times 10^{-5} = 8.247 \times 10^{-5} \; m^2
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute the slenderness ratio.

Radius of gyration:

$$
k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.180 \times 10^{-9}}{8.247 \times 10^{-5}}} = 6.21 \times 10^{-3} \; m
$$

Slenderness ratio (pin-ended, $K = 1.0$):

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{K \cdot L}{k} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.42}{6.21 \times 10^{-3}} = 67.6
$$

This exceeds the typical transition slenderness for aluminum (≈ 50), confirming **Euler buckling** is the governing failure mode.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute the Euler buckling load.

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{(K \cdot L)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 69 \times 10^9 \times 3.180 \times 10^{-9}}{(0.42)^2}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 69 \times 3.180}{0.1764} \times 10^0 = \dfrac{2166.3}{0.1764} = 12{,}281 \; N \approx 12.28 \; kN
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Compute the factor of safety.

$$
FoS = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{P_{applied}} = \dfrac{12{,}281}{600} = 20.5
$$

**Conclusion:** $FoS = 20.5 \gg 3.0$, so the cross-brace has **ample safety margin** against Euler buckling. The governing failure mode for the cross-brace in practice is therefore **fatigue at the welded pivot joint**, not gross column buckling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units conversion:** $mm \rightarrow m$ errors are the #1 cause of wrong answers. Always convert tube dimensions to meters before substituting into Pa-based formulas.
> 2. **Using the wrong deflection formula:** The formula $\delta = FL^3/(48EI)$ applies **only** to a simply-supported beam with a **central** point load. For off-center loads, use $\delta = F a^2 b^2 / (3 E I L)$ where $a$ and $b$ are the distances from the supports.
> 3. **Confusing buckling for cross-brace with bending for side frame:** Cross-brace failure is governed by **Euler buckling** (axial); side-frame failure is governed by **bending stress** (flexure). Mixing these gives wrong answers and full mark deductions.
> 4. **Not stating the assumed support condition:** Always state "simply-supported," "fixed-fixed," or "pin-ended" with the corresponding $K$ value.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor-of-safety check:** KTU 2024 Scheme questions almost always require a final "Is the design safe?" conclusion with the $FoS$ value compared against a stated design target.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The supporting structure is the **load-bearing chassis** (side frames, cross-brace, axle plates, seat rails, backrest posts) of a wheelchair.
- **Primary functions:** (1) Carry the user's weight, (2) transfer loads to the wheels, (3) maintain geometric integrity of the seat system, (4) provide a mounting interface for accessories.
- **Standard test load:** **113 kg** per ISO 7176-8.
- **Most fatigue-critical member:** The **cross-brace**, due to cyclic loading and stress concentrations at pivot welds.
- **Governing failure modes:**
  - **Side frames:** Bending stress + fatigue at welds.
  - **Cross-brace:** Euler buckling + fatigue at pivot joints.
  - **Axle plate:** Shear + bearing stress at the quick-release bore.
- **Key formula — Bending stress:** $\sigma_{max} = M_{max} / Z$
- **Key formula — Deflection (central load, simply supported):** $\delta_{max} = F L^3 / (48 E I)$
- **Key formula — Euler buckling:** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / (K L)^2$
- **Key formula — Factor of Safety:** $FoS = \sigma_{yield} / \sigma_{working} \geq 3.0$
- **Material hierarchy (stiffness-to-weight):** Carbon Fiber ≈ Steel > Titanium > Aluminum. Cost hierarchy reverses.
- **6061-T6 aluminum** is the **most common** mid-range material; **4130 chromoly steel** is the most common budget/heavy-duty material; **Grade 9 titanium** and **carbon-fiber composites** dominate the high-end sports segment.
- **Ergonomic deflection limit:** $\delta_{max} \leq L / 200$ to minimize energy loss during propulsion.
- **Rigid vs. Folding:** Rigid frames are **lighter and stiffer** (better propulsion efficiency); folding frames are **more portable** (better for travel) at the cost of some structural compliance.
- **Adjustability range of axle plate:** Typically $\pm 5$ cm horizontal, $\pm 3$ cm vertical around neutral — used to tune tip-resistance and maneuverability.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frame Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frame Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Frame" in Assistive Medical Devices?

In the context of assistive medical devices (particularly wheelchairs, walkers, commode chairs, standing frames, and pediatric positioning devices), a **frame** refers to the **primary load-bearing structural skeleton** that supports the user, transfers loads to the ground (or wheels), provides mounting points for seating, wheels, brakes, armrests, footrests, and accessories, and defines the overall geometry, stability, and ergonomics of the device.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"A frame is the principal structural chassis of an assistive device, typically fabricated from metallic alloys, polymers, or composite materials, engineered to withstand static and dynamic physiological loads exerted by the user while maintaining dimensional stability, fatigue resistance, and biomechanical alignment."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Human Skeleton

Think of an assistive device frame exactly like a **human skeleton**:

- The **bones** = the **tubular/rectangular structural members** of the frame.
- The **joints** = the **welds, rivets, bolts, or pivot pins** connecting members.
- The **muscles** = the **fasteners and braces** that hold joints firm under load.
- The **skin's role** = the **paint, anodized coating, or padding** that protects the user and the structure.

Just as a human skeleton must be **lightweight (so we can move)**, **strong (to bear body weight)**, and **rigid (to hold shape)**, an assistive device frame must satisfy three simultaneous constraints:

| Engineering Property | Real-World Analogy | Design Target |
|---|---|---|
| **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio** | Light but strong bones | Easy self-propulsion |
| **Stiffness / Rigidity** | Bones that don't bend | Stable seating posture |
| **Fatigue Resistance** | Bones that don't fracture over years | 5–10 year service life |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Frame Selection Matters in Assistive Technology:**
> The frame is the **single most expensive component** (often 30–50% of device cost) and the **most safety-critical element**. Frame failure can result in user falls, pressure injuries, or fatality. Therefore, KTU examiners place **high weightage** on frame materials, geometry, and load analysis.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Frame Design

The following standard metrics and limits are universally used in rehabilitation engineering and are expected knowledge for KTU 2024 Scheme students:

- **ISO 7176 Series** (Wheelchair Standards — applies to most assistive devices)
- **Maximum User Mass (Standard):** $\mathbf{100 \text{ kg}}$ (light-duty), $\mathbf{150 \text{ kg}}$ (heavy-duty), $\mathbf{250+ \text{ kg}}$ (bariatric)
- **Safety Factor (Static Load):** $\mathbf{SF \geq 3}$ on ultimate tensile strength
- **Fatigue Test Cycle:** $\mathbf{2 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles}}$ minimum (simulating ~5 years of use)
- **Young's Modulus of Common Frame Materials:**
  - Aluminum 6061-T6: $E = 68.9 \text{ GPa}$
  - Steel AISI 1020: $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$
  - Titanium Ti-6Al-4V: $E = 113.8 \text{ GPa}$
  - Carbon Fiber Composite: $E = 70\text{–}150 \text{ GPa}$ (direction-dependent)
- **Density:**
  - Aluminum: $\rho = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$
  - Steel: $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
  - Titanium: $\rho = 4430 \text{ kg/m}^3$
  - Carbon Fiber: $\rho = 1600 \text{ kg/m}^3$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Frame Classification

A KTU examiner will expect students to **justify** why a particular frame type is chosen for a specific clinical use-case. Frames are classified along three engineering axes:

1. **Geometric Configuration** (Rigid vs. Folding vs. Articulated)
2. **Material Composition** (Metallic vs. Polymeric vs. Composite)
3. **Manufacturing Process** (Welded vs. Bolted vs. Cast vs. Hybrid)

Each axis directly influences the device's **weight, cost, adjustability, transportability, and durability**.

## 2.2 Classification of Frame Types — Primary Taxonomy

### A. By Geometric Configuration

| Frame Type | Structural Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Rigid (Fixed) Frame** | Single welded/bolted unit, no moving parts; load path is continuous and direct | Sports wheelchairs, bariatric chairs, active-user daily chairs |
| **Folding (Cross-Fold) Frame** | Uses an X-shaped or H-shaped cross-brace pivot mechanism | Standard manual wheelchairs for portability |
| **Modular (Adjustable) Frame** | Telescoping tubes, slotted brackets allowing width/depth/height adjustment | Pediatric, growing-user, multi-user clinical settings |
| **Articulated / Tilt-in-Space Frame** | Multiple pivot axes permit seat-back angle change without rearward wheelbase shift | Postural support, pressure relief for users with poor trunk control |
| **Standing Frame** | Rigid vertical/articulated chassis supporting a user in upright posture | Rehabilitation, bone-density preservation, circulatory health |
| **Walker / Rollator Frame** | Four-legged or three-wheeled tubular chassis with handgrips and brakes | Ambulatory assistive device for elderly / balance-impaired |

### B. By Material Composition

| Material | Yield Strength | Density | Corrosion Resistance | Cost | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel** | ~250 MPa | 7850 kg/m³ | Poor (needs paint) | Low | Budget folding wheelchairs |
| **Stainless Steel (304/316)** | ~215–290 MPa | 8000 kg/m³ | Excellent | High | Commode chairs, hospital-grade |
| **Aluminum 6061-T6** | 240–275 MPa | 2700 kg/m³ | Good (anodized) | Medium | Lightweight active chairs |
| **Aluminum 7000-series** | 500–600 MPa | 2810 kg/m³ | Good | Medium-High | High-performance sports |
| **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V** | 880 MPa | 4430 kg/m³ | Excellent | Very High | Custom-molded ultralight chairs |
| **Carbon Fiber Composite** | 500–1500 MPa | 1500–1600 kg/m³ | Excellent | Very High | Racing / elite-sport chairs |
| **Engineering Polymer (Nylon, PP, ABS)** | 40–80 MPa | 900–1150 kg/m³ | Excellent | Low | Pediatric / toy-grade |

### C. By Manufacturing Process

- **TIG/MIG Welded:** Strongest joints; permanent; non-adjustable.
- **Brazed:** Lower temperature; suitable for thin-wall aluminum tubes.
- **Bolted / Riveted:** Disassemblable; lower stiffness at joints.
- **Cast:** Complex 3D geometries (e.g., hub-mounted brackets).
- **Adhesive-Bonded / Co-cured:** Used for carbon-fiber composite frames.

## 2.3 Mechanical Loading on an Assistive Device Frame

A frame in service is subjected to the following loads:

1. **Static Vertical Load:** User's body weight (W) distributed over the seating surface.
2. **Dynamic Shock Load:** Curbs, drops, and uneven terrain impose 2–3× body weight.
3. **Propulsion Reaction Loads:** Hand-rim push forces (typically 30–80 N per stroke).
4. **Braking Loads:** Decelerating the user + chair system.
5. **Tip-Over Moment:** Lateral instability when one wheel lifts off the ground.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **must-know** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination). The "Marks Weight" column indicates the relative frequency in past papers.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Euler Critical Buckling Load** (slender frame tube) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | $E$: Young's modulus (Pa), $I$: Area moment of inertia (m⁴), $L_e$: effective length (m) | High |
| 2 | **Effective Length** (depends on end conditions) | $L_e = K \cdot L$ | $K$: end-condition factor (1.0 pinned, 0.5 fixed-fixed, 0.7 fixed-pinned, 2.0 fixed-free) | High |
| 3 | **Area Moment of Inertia (Hollow Tube)** | $I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(D^4 - d^4\right)$ | $D$: outer diameter (m), $d$: inner diameter (m) | High |
| 4 | **Bending Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | $M$: bending moment (N·m), $c$: distance to outer fiber (m) | High |
| 5 | **Static Factor of Safety** | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{applied}}$ | Both in same units (Pa) | High |
| 6 | **Tip-Over Safety Condition** (anti-tip wheel check) | $M_{resisting} \geq SF \cdot M_{tipping}$ | $M$: moment about the tip-axis (N·m), typically $SF \geq 1.5$ | Medium |
| 7 | **Specific Strength (Material Selection Index)** | $\phi = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | $\sigma_y$: yield strength, $\rho$: density | Medium |
| 8 | **Specific Stiffness** | $\phi_E = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | $E$: Young's modulus, $\rho$: density | Medium |
| 9 | **Sprung Mass Natural Frequency** (frame-rider system) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$: suspension stiffness (N/m), $m$: total mass (kg) | Low |
| 10 | **Fatigue Life (Basquin's Law)** | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ | $\sigma_a$: stress amplitude, $N_f$: cycles to failure, $b$: fatigue strength exponent | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"Why is aluminum preferred over steel for lightweight wheelchairs?"*, the answer is **NOT just "because it's lighter."** The correct justification is: *Aluminum has a higher specific strength $\sigma_y / \rho$ than mild steel* (aluminum $\approx 95 \text{ kNm/kg}$ vs. steel $\approx 32 \text{ kNm/kg}$), giving better strength-per-weight — the true figure of merit for manually-propelled devices.

## 2.5 Why This System / Equation Matters in Engineering

- **Euler buckling** governs the design of the **vertical upright members** (backrest canes) and **leg rest tubes**, which are long, slender, and axially compressed.
- **Specific strength and stiffness** are the **material selection indices** that drive the choice of carbon-fiber or titanium in elite-sport wheelchairs.
- **Tip-over moment analysis** is the **biomechanical safety check** that determines whether an anti-tip wheel is mandatory, especially for amputee users and pediatric chairs.
- **Fatigue analysis (Basquin's law)** is critical because assistive devices are used **daily for years**, and even sub-yield cyclic loading can initiate cracks at welded joints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Buckling Load of a Folding-Frame Cross-Brace

> **Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A folding wheelchair cross-brace is fabricated from an aluminum 6061-T6 tube. The tube has an outer diameter $D = 25 \text{ mm}$ and a wall thickness $t = 1.6 \text{ mm}$. The effective (pinned-pinned) length of the brace under compression is $L = 380 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the **critical Euler buckling load** and the **allowable compressive load** using a factor of safety of 3.

### Given Data

- Material: **Aluminum 6061-T6**, so $E = 68.9 \text{ GPa} = 68.9 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$.
- Outer diameter: $D = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$.
- Wall thickness: $t = 1.6 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow d = D - 2t = 25 - 2(1.6) = 21.8 \text{ mm} = 0.0218 \text{ m}$.
- Effective length: $L = 380 \text{ mm} = 0.380 \text{ m}$.
- End condition: pinned-pinned, so $K = 1.0$, giving $L_e = 0.380 \text{ m}$.
- Factor of safety: $SF = 3$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Inner Diameter

This step is necessary because the tube is hollow; we must know the inner dimension before computing the second moment of area.

$$
d = D - 2t = 25 - 2(1.6) = 21.8 \text{ mm} = 0.0218 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Area Moment of Inertia (I)

For a hollow circular cross-section, the second moment of area about the centroidal axis is:

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D^4 - d^4\right)
$$

Substituting the values (in meters):

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left((0.025)^4 - (0.0218)^4\right)
$$

Compute the fourth powers individually:

$$
(0.025)^4 = 3.90625 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
(0.0218)^4 = 2.2577 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4
$$

Subtract them:

$$
D^4 - d^4 = (3.90625 - 2.2577) \times 10^{-7} = 1.6486 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4
$$

Now multiply by $\pi / 64$:

$$
I = \frac{3.14159}{64} \times 1.6486 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
I = 0.04909 \times 1.6486 \times 10^{-7} = 8.093 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4
$$

So $I \approx 8.09 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Euler Buckling Formula

The critical load at which the column will buckle elastically is given by Euler's formula:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}
$$

Substitute:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \times (68.9 \times 10^9) \times (8.093 \times 10^{-9})}{(0.380)^2}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
\pi^2 \approx 9.8696
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 9.8696 \times 68.9 \times 10^9 \times 8.093 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 9.8696 \times 68.9 \times 8.093 = 5503.5 \text{ N·m}^2
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
(0.380)^2 = 0.1444 \text{ m}^2
$$

Divide:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{5503.5}{0.1444} = 38{,}112 \text{ N} \approx 38.1 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Factor of Safety

The allowable load is the critical load divided by the safety factor:

$$
P_{allow} = \frac{P_{cr}}{SF} = \frac{38{,}112}{3} = 12{,}704 \text{ N} \approx 12.7 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation and Engineering Insight

For a folding-wheelchair cross-brace carrying only a fraction of the user's weight (typically a peak of ~1.5 kN dynamic load for a 100 kg user), the safety margin is:

$$
\text{Margin} = \frac{12.7}{1.5} = 8.47 \times
$$

This large margin confirms why aluminum tubes are sufficient for wheelchair frames even with thin walls — buckling is rarely the limiting failure mode for properly designed members. The dominant failure modes in practice are **weld fatigue** and **joint loosening**, not Euler buckling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Selection — Specific Strength Comparison

This derivation shows students how to **mathematically justify** material choice between steel and aluminum for a manual wheelchair frame.

> **Problem Statement:**
> Compare mild steel and aluminum 6061-T6 for a wheelchair upright member. Compute the **specific strength** and determine the **mass ratio** for equal-strength designs.

### Given Data

| Property | Steel AISI 1020 | Aluminum 6061-T6 |
|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength $\sigma_y$ | 250 MPa | 240 MPa |
| Density $\rho$ | 7850 kg/m³ | 2700 kg/m³ |

### Step 1 — Compute Specific Strength

Specific strength is defined as yield strength divided by density.

For steel:

$$
\phi_{steel} = \frac{250 \times 10^6}{7850} = 31{,}847 \text{ N·m/kg} = 31.8 \text{ kNm/kg}
$$

For aluminum:

$$
\phi_{Al} = \frac{240 \times 10^6}{2700} = 88{,}888 \text{ N·m/kg} = 88.9 \text{ kNm/kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Strength-Per-Weight Ratio

$$
\frac{\phi_{Al}}{\phi_{steel}} = \frac{88.9}{31.8} = 2.79
$$

This means that, **per kilogram of frame mass, aluminum provides 2.79× the strength of steel**.

### Step 3 — Compute Mass Ratio for Equal Strength

For an equal-strength design carrying the same load $P$:

$$
m_{Al} = m_{steel} \times \frac{\phi_{steel}}{\phi_{Al}} = m_{steel} \times \frac{1}{2.79}
$$

So a steel frame weighing 10 kg could be replaced with an aluminum frame of only:

$$
m_{Al} = \frac{10}{2.79} = 3.58 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Insight

A **2.79× weight reduction** is profound for a self-propelled user: every kilogram of frame mass is a kilogram the user must repeatedly accelerate, decelerate, and lift. This is why **ISO 7176 performance standards** explicitly classify "ultralight wheelchairs" as those under **15 kg total mass**, achievable only through aluminum or titanium frames.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tip-Over Stability of a Standard Wheelchair

> **Problem Statement:**
> A standard rear-wheel-drive wheelchair has its combined center of gravity (user + chair) at a horizontal distance $d = 0.55 \text{ m}$ behind the rear axle and a height $h = 0.85 \text{ m}$ above the ground. The wheelbase (rear axle to front caster) is $L = 1.05 \text{ m}$. Determine whether anti-tip wheels are required. Assume user-chair mass $m = 95 \text{ kg}$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Tip-Axis

The chair will tip backward about the **rear axle** (specifically, about the contact line of the rear wheel) if the moment of gravity exceeds the restoring moment from the user's weight on the front casters.

### Step 2 — Compute the Tipping Moment

The tipping moment is generated by gravity acting at height $h$:

$$
M_{tip} = m \cdot g \cdot h = 95 \times 9.81 \times 0.85 = 792.1 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Restoring Moment

The restoring moment is provided by the user's weight acting at horizontal distance $(L - d)$ ahead of the rear axle:

$$
M_{resist} = m \cdot g \cdot (L - d) = 95 \times 9.81 \times (1.05 - 0.55)
$$

$$
M_{resist} = 95 \times 9.81 \times 0.50 = 465.975 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Static Stability Factor

$$
SSF = \frac{M_{resist}}{M_{tip}} = \frac{465.975}{792.1} = 0.588
$$

Since $SSF < 1$, the chair is **inherently unstable** in the rearward direction without intervention.

### Step 5 — Conclusion

Anti-tip wheels are **mandatory** for this configuration. They are typically positioned 50–75 mm behind the rear axle and effectively shift the tip-axis rearward, increasing $L$ to a new larger effective wheelbase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** is the standard metric used in ISO 7176-1. Rear-wheel-drive wheelchairs typically have an SSF between 0.8 and 1.5, but **a value below 1.0 is only acceptable if anti-tip wheels are fitted and the user is trained**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid Diagram)

This diagram presents the complete taxonomy of assistive device frame types in a clean, hierarchical Mermaid block (all identifiers are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords):

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Assistive Device Frames] --> B[By Geometry]
    A --> C[By Material]
    A --> D[By Manufacturing]
    A --> E[By Clinical Use]
    B --> B1[Rigid Fixed Frame]
    B --> B2[Folding Cross Brace]
    B --> B3[Modular Adjustable]
    B --> B4[Articulated Tilt]
    B --> B5[Standing Frame]
    B --> B6[Walker Rollator]
    C --> C1[Mild Steel]
    C --> C2[Stainless Steel]
    C --> C3[Aluminum 6061]
    C --> C4[Aluminum 7075]
    C --> C5[Titanium Alloy]
    C --> C6[Carbon Fiber]
    C --> C7[Engineering Polymer]
    D --> D1[TIG or MIG Welded]
    D --> D2[Brazed]
    D --> D3[Bolted or Riveted]
    D --> D4[Cast]
    D --> D5[Adhesive Bonded]
    E --> E1[Manual Wheelchair]
    E --> E2[Powered Wheelchair]
    E --> E3[Pediatric Chair]
    E --> E4[Bariatric Chair]
    E --> E5[Sports Racing Chair]
    E --> E6[Commode Shower Chair]
    E --> E7[Standing Aid]
    B1 --> F1[High Stiffness]
    B1 --> F2[Low Weight]
    B2 --> F3[Portable Foldable]
    B2 --> F4[X Brace Pivot]
    B3 --> F5[Width Depth Adjust]
    B4 --> F6[Tilt in Space]
    B4 --> F7[Recline]
    B5 --> F8[Upright Posture]
    B5 --> F9[Bone Density]
    B6 --> F10[Balance Support]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Load Path in a Folding Wheelchair

This diagram traces the complete **load path** from the user through every structural member of a folding wheelchair, showing how forces are transmitted to the ground. This is the KTU 2024 "functional flow" diagram style:

```mermaid
graph LR
    user1[User Body Mass] --> seatTube[Seat Rail Tube]
    seatTube --> crossBrace1[Cross Brace Member]
    crossBrace1 --> pivotPin1[Central Pivot Pin]
    pivotPin1 --> crossBrace2[Opposite Cross Brace]
    crossBrace2 --> sideFrame1[Side Frame Member]
    sideFrame1 --> rearAxle1[Rear Axle Bracket]
    rearAxle1 --> rearWheel1[Rear Wheel]
    rearWheel1 --> ground1[Ground Contact]
    user1 --> backrestTube1[Backrest Upright Tube]
    backrestTube1 --> sideFrame2[Side Frame Member]
    sideFrame2 --> rearAxle2[Rear Axle Bracket]
    rearAxle2 --> rearWheel2[Rear Wheel]
    rearWheel2 --> ground2[Ground Contact]
    user1 --> footrestTube1[Footrest Hanger Tube]
    footrestTube1 --> sideFrame3[Front Side Frame]
    sideFrame3 --> casterFork1[Front Caster Fork]
    casterFork1 --> casterWheel1[Front Caster]
    casterWheel1 --> ground3[Ground Contact]
    sideFrame3 --> antiTipBracket1[Anti Tip Bracket]
    antiTipBracket1 --> antiTipWheel1[Anti Tip Wheel]
    antiTipWheel1 -.-> ground3
```

## 4.3 Material Selection Logic Flow (Engineering Decision Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start Material Selection] --> q1{Frame Weight Target}
    q1 -->|Under 12 kg ultralight| q2{Budget Limit}
    q1 -->|12 to 18 kg standard| pathA[Aluminum 6061 T6]
    q1 -->|Over 18 kg heavy duty| pathB[Steel or Stainless]
    q2 -->|High budget| pathC[Titanium or Carbon Fiber]
    q2 -->|Medium budget| pathD[Aluminum 7075 T6]
    q2 -->|Low budget| pathE[Aluminum 6061 T6]
    start --> q3{Corrosion Exposure}
    q3 -->|Commode or shower| pathF[Stainless 316 or Aluminum Anodized]
    q3 -->|Dry indoor| pathA
    q3 -->|Outdoor variable| pathF
    start --> q4{Fatigue Cycles}
    q4 -->|Over 2 million| pathG[Butted Tubing Welds Ground Smooth]
    q4 -->|Under 2 million| pathA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *Define the term "frame" as used in assistive medical devices. List any **four** common materials used for fabricating wheelchair frames, with one specific advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **frame** in an assistive medical device is the **principal load-bearing structural chassis** that supports the user, transmits physiological and dynamic loads to the wheels/ground, and provides the geometric platform for all functional components (seating, wheels, brakes, armrests, footrests).

**Four common frame materials with their specific advantages:**

| Material | Specific Advantage |
|---|---|
| **Mild Steel** | Lowest cost; easy to weld; high absolute strength |
| **Aluminum 6061-T6** | High specific strength (88.9 kNm/kg); lightweight; corrosion-resistant when anodized |
| **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V** | Best strength-to-weight ratio among metals; biocompatible; excellent fatigue life |
| **Carbon Fiber Composite** | Highest specific stiffness when fiber-aligned; can be molded into complex aerodynamic shapes for sports chairs |

> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Award **full 3 marks** only if all four materials are listed with **distinct, correct advantages**. Generic statements like "lightweight" or "strong" without quantification or context lose marks.

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
> *With the help of a neat sketch, explain the difference between a **rigid frame** and a **folding (cross-brace) frame** in manual wheelchairs. State one clinical advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **rigid frame** is a single welded/bolted unit with no moving structural members. Loads travel through a continuous, uninterrupted path. Its clinical advantage is **maximum propulsion efficiency** — every Newton of push force is transmitted to the rear wheel with minimal energy loss, making it the preferred choice for **active, independent, long-duration users** (e.g., spinal cord injury patients).

A **folding frame** uses an **X-shaped or H-shaped cross-brace** with a central pivot pin that allows the side frames to collapse toward each other, reducing the chair's stowed volume. Its clinical advantage is **transportability** — the chair can be folded and placed in a car trunk, making it suitable for **elderly users**, **occasional users**, and those without dedicated accessible transport.

(Examiner expects a hand-sketch showing the X cross-brace and the rigid perimeter; even a labeled Mermaid-style block diagram is acceptable.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **classification of wheelchair frames** based on (i) geometric configuration, (ii) material, and (iii) manufacturing process. Give one example of a specific device for each category. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **folding wheelchair cross-brace** is fabricated from a steel tube of outer diameter $30 \text{ mm}$ and wall thickness $2 \text{ mm}$. The effective length of the brace under compression is $400 \text{ mm}$ (pinned-pinned ends). Given $E_{steel} = 200 \text{ GPa}$ and a factor of safety of 3, compute the **Euler critical buckling load** and the **allowable load**. Comment on whether this design is safe for a 100 kg user who may impose a peak dynamic load of 2.5× body weight. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The classification is presented in three structured subsections. Award marks as follows:

- **Geometric configuration (3 marks):** Rigid, Folding (Cross-brace), Modular/Adjustable, Articulated (Tilt-in-Space), Standing frame, Walker/Rollator. Each explained with one example (e.g., Rigid → Sports wheelchair; Folding → Standard manual; Modular → Pediatric growing chair; Articulated → Postural support chair; Standing → Rehabilitation standing frame; Walker → Elderly rollator).
- **Material (2 marks):** Mild steel, stainless steel, aluminum (6061/7075), titanium, carbon-fiber composite, engineering polymer. State one trade-off for each (e.g., steel = cheap/heavy; titanium = light/expensive; carbon = stiff/very expensive).
- **Manufacturing (2 marks):** Welded (TIG/MIG), brazed, bolted/riveted, cast, adhesive-bonded/co-cured. State one application per process.

### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**

- $D = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}$
- $t = 2 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow d = 30 - 2(2) = 26 \text{ mm} = 0.026 \text{ m}$
- $L_e = 400 \text{ mm} = 0.400 \text{ m}$ (pinned-pinned, $K = 1.0$)
- $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $SF = 3$

**Step 1 — Compute I:**

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left((0.030)^4 - (0.026)^4\right)
$$

$$
(0.030)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
(0.026)^4 = 4.5698 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
D^4 - d^4 = 3.5302 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
I = \frac{3.14159}{64} \times 3.5302 \times 10^{-7} = 1.733 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Euler Critical Load:**

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \frac{9.8696 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 1.733 \times 10^{-8}}{(0.400)^2}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 9.8696 \times 200 \times 1.733 \times 10 = 34{,}213 \text{ N·m}^2
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{34{,}213}{0.1600} = 213{,}831 \text{ N} \approx 213.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Factor of Safety:**

$$
P_{allow} = \frac{213.8}{3} = 71.27 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to Applied Load:**

- User mass: $m = 100 \text{ kg}$
- Static load on the brace: roughly one-third of the body weight (the cross-brace carries a portion of the load via the seat-rail to side-frame path) ≈ $\frac{100 \times 9.81}{3} = 327 \text{ N}$.
- Dynamic peak (2.5×): $2.5 \times 327 = 817.5 \text{ N} \approx 0.82 \text{ kN}$.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

Since $P_{allow} = 71.27 \text{ kN} \gg 0.82 \text{ kN}$, the brace has a **safety margin of $\approx 87$**, which is extremely conservative. The design is **more than safe**; engineers could potentially downsize the tube to reduce weight, but buckling is **not** the limiting failure mode. The actual limiting mode is **weld fatigue at the cross-brace pivot**.

---

> **Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — December 2023 / Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Compare **mild steel** and **aluminum 6061-T6** as wheelchair frame materials using the specific strength index. Show that aluminum gives a lower frame mass for an equal-strength design, and compute the mass reduction percentage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A standard manual wheelchair has its user-chair center of gravity at a horizontal distance of $0.50 \text{ m}$ behind the rear axle and a height of $0.90 \text{ m}$. The wheelbase is $1.10 \text{ m}$ and the total mass is $90 \text{ kg}$. Determine if the chair will tip backward. If yes, design the position of an anti-tip wheel to provide a static safety factor of 1.5 against rearward tip-over. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**

- Steel: $\sigma_{y,steel} = 250 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Aluminum: $\sigma_{y,Al} = 240 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho_{Al} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Specific Strength:**

$$
\phi_{steel} = \frac{250 \times 10^6}{7850} = 31{,}847 \text{ N·m/kg} = 31.85 \text{ kNm/kg}
$$

$$
\phi_{Al} = \frac{240 \times 10^6}{2700} = 88{,}888 \text{ N·m/kg} = 88.89 \text{ kNm/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Ratio:**

$$
\frac{\phi_{Al}}{\phi_{steel}} = \frac{88.89}{31.85} = 2.79
$$

**Step 3 — Mass Reduction:**

For equal strength, $m_{Al} = m_{steel} / 2.79$.

If a steel frame weighs 10 kg, the equivalent aluminum frame weighs $10 / 2.79 = 3.58 \text{ kg}$.

**Percentage reduction:**

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{10 - 3.58}{10} \times 100\% = 64.2\%
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Aluminum achieves a **64.2% weight reduction** for the same load-bearing capacity, justifying its use in lightweight active-user wheelchairs.

### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**

- $d = 0.50 \text{ m}$ (CG behind rear axle)
- $h = 0.90 \text{ m}$ (CG height)
- $L = 1.10 \text{ m}$ (rear-axle to front-caster)
- $m = 90 \text{ kg}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Required static safety factor: $SSF_{req} = 1.5$

**Step 1 — Compute Tipping Moment:**

$$
M_{tip} = m \cdot g \cdot h = 90 \times 9.81 \times 0.90 = 794.6 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Restoring Moment:**

$$
M_{resist} = m \cdot g \cdot (L - d) = 90 \times 9.81 \times (1.10 - 0.50) = 90 \times 9.81 \times 0.60 = 529.7 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Check for Tipping:**

Since $M_{tip} (794.6) > M_{resist} (529.7)$, the chair **will tip backward**. Anti-tip wheels are required.

**Step 4 — Design Anti-Tip Position:**

The anti-tip wheel becomes the new tip-axis, located at a distance $x$ behind the rear axle. For static stability with $SSF = 1.5$:

$$
M_{resist,new} \geq SSF \cdot M_{tip,new}
$$

In the new configuration, the moment arm of gravity is now $(d + x)$ and the restoring moment arm is $(L + x - d)$:

$$
m g (L + x - d) \geq 1.5 \cdot m g (h) / \text{(effective height consideration)}
$$

A simpler and more KTU-typical formulation uses the **rear-axle as the original pivot** and ensures that when the anti-tip wheel is in contact, the **normal force on it** generates a counter-moment.

For static equilibrium just before tip, taking moments about the anti-tip wheel contact (located $x$ behind the rear axle):

- The new effective wheelbase is $L + x$.
- For $SSF \geq 1.5$:

$$
\frac{L + x - d}{h} \geq \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.667
$$

Wait — applying the standard ISO 7176 tip-formula:

$$
x \geq h / SSF - (L - d) = \frac{0.90}{1.5} - 0.60 = 0.60 - 0.60 = 0
$$

This borderline result means the existing geometry is **just barely stable** with $SSF = 1.5$ at the rear axle. To improve margin, place the anti-tip wheel at:

$$
x = \frac{h}{SSF} - (L - d) + \text{ safety margin}
$$

A practical engineering choice: place the anti-tip wheel **50–75 mm behind the rear axle**, giving an effective $L_{new} = 1.15\text{–}1.175 \text{ m}$. Recheck:

$$
SSF_{new} = \frac{L_{new} - d}{h} = \frac{1.15 - 0.50}{0.90} = 0.722
$$

A value of $0.72 < 1$ still indicates instability for purely vertical load, but with **dynamic considerations** (forward acceleration, push angle, user weight shift), the anti-tip wheel provides a hard mechanical stop that prevents complete rearward overturn.

> **Conclusion:** Anti-tip wheels must be fitted, and a position of **at least 75 mm behind the rear axle** is recommended for clinical safety, regardless of the SSF result, because the SSF analysis is a **static** approximation and dynamic loads can be significantly higher.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Frame-Type Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating specific strength with absolute strength.** Aluminum 6061 has *lower* yield strength than mild steel, but it is *preferred* for wheelchairs because specific strength is the relevant index. Saying "aluminum is stronger than steel" is a **direct factual error** that costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm to m in $E I / L^2$ calculations.** The unit mismatch ($10^9 \cdot 10^{-8} \cdot 10^{-3}$) is the single most common numerical error in buckling problems. **Always convert to SI units (Pa, m⁴, m) before substituting.**
>
> 3. **Not stating the end-condition factor $K$ explicitly.** A buckling problem without a clearly specified $K$ is incomplete. The default for a folding cross-brace pinned at both ends is $K = 1.0$.
>
> 4. **Drawing a "rigid frame" but labeling it as "folding."** Sketch accuracy is examined. Use clear pivot pins and an X-brace for folding; use a continuous perimeter with no pivots for rigid.
>
> 5. **Ignoring dynamic loads.** A static analysis with only body weight underestimates real-world loads. Always include the 2–3× dynamic amplification factor for any wheelchair frame problem.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **frame** is the **load-bearing chassis** of an assistive device, analogous to the human skeleton.
- Frame classification rests on **three axes: geometry, material, manufacturing**.
- The **six geometric types** are: **Rigid, Folding, Modular, Articulated, Standing, Walker/Rollator**.
- **Euler buckling** governs the design of slender frame tubes: $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$.
- For a **hollow circular tube**: $I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$.
- **Specific strength** $\phi = \sigma_y / \rho$ is the **true material selection index** for manually-propelled devices.
- Aluminum 6061-T6 gives **~2.79× the specific strength** of mild steel, enabling **~64% mass reduction** in equal-strength designs.
- **Standard safety factor** for assistive device frames: $SF \geq 3$ (static) and $2 \times 10^6$ fatigue cycles minimum.
- **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** is the ISO 7176 metric for tip-over analysis; values below 1.0 require anti-tip wheels.
- **Anti-tip wheels** are placed 50–75 mm behind the rear axle to mechanically arrest rearward tip.
- **Dominant failure mode** in practice: **weld fatigue at the cross-brace pivot**, not Euler buckling.
- The **most expensive single component** of a wheelchair is often the **frame** (30–50% of total cost).
- **ISO 7176** is the governing standard; **KTU examiners expect** students to mention standards compliance in long answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propelling Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propelling Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROPELLING STRUCTURE (Formal Definition)**
> A *propelling structure* in the context of assistive medical devices is the integrated mechanical, electro-mechanical, or bio-mechanical subsystem that converts user input energy (manual muscular effort, electrical energy from batteries, or a hybrid of both) into controlled translational or rotational motion of the assistive device. It encompasses all components responsible for **force generation**, **torque transmission**, **velocity modulation**, and **directional control** of mobility aids such as manual wheelchairs, powered wheelchairs, mobility scooters, and rehabilitation robots.

The propelling structure sits at the heart of the *Mobility Sub-system* in the standard assistive device block diagram and acts as the interface between the *energy source* (human muscles, lead-acid/Li-ion batteries, supercapacitors) and the *wheel-ground contact patch* that produces the actual traction force.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Mapping**
> This topic directly satisfies the Course Outcome: *CO3 — Design and analyze the mechanical propulsion, transmission, and control components of mobility-assistive devices.* It also contributes to *CO5 — Evaluate ergonomic and biomechanical compatibility of assistive hardware with the end user.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Plain English Intuition"

Imagine you are pushing a shopping cart across a parking lot. Your hands are the **energy source**, your arms are the **transmission links**, and the wheels touching the ground are the **propelling structure output**. Now imagine the cart suddenly weighs ten times more — you'd need gears, a bigger handle (a *lever*), or maybe a small electric motor bolted into the wheel hub to help you.

A **propelling structure** is *exactly* that "help-system" in a wheelchair or scooter:

- In a **manual wheelchair**, the propelling structure is the pair of **push-rims** (the smaller outer rings the user grips) coupled to the **large rear wheels** through a *geared mechanical advantage* (usually 1:1 in standard chairs, but up to 1:2 in lever-drive chairs).
- In a **powered wheelchair**, the propelling structure is the **hub motor + controller + battery + joystick** chain, where electrical energy is converted to torque through electromagnetic induction (BLDC motors) or brushed DC commutation.
- In a **hybrid power-assist wheelchair**, both systems coexist — manual push-rims are retained, but a sensor detects the user's push force and the motor adds supplementary torque.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy summary:** Think of the propelling structure as the *engine + gearbox + drivetrain* of a car, but instead of petrol being combusted, the "fuel" is either the user's hand/shoulder muscles or a rechargeable battery.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value (Range) | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|
| Push-rim diameter | 480 mm – 540 mm | ISO 7176-5 (Wheelchair standards) |
| Drive-wheel diameter | 510 mm – 660 mm (24"/25"/26") | ISO 7176-5 |
| Gear ratio (push-rim to wheel) | 1:1 (standard) to 1:1.6 (lever drive) | RESNA standards |
| Hub-motor rated power | 150 W – 600 W (per wheel) | EN 12184 (Electric wheelchairs) |
| Battery voltage | 24 V / 36 V / 48 V DC | Common wheelchair OEM specs |
| User push force (sustainable) | 30 N – 90 N | ISO 7176-11 |
| Maximum slope capability | 6° (manual) to 10°–12° (powered) | ISO 7176-2 |
| Rolling resistance coefficient (μᵣ) on hard floor | 0.008 – 0.015 | Standard tire data |
| Static friction coefficient (rubber on dry asphalt) | 0.6 – 0.85 | Engineering handbook |

> [!NOTE]
> All numerical values above are engineering-referenced ballpark figures commonly cited in ISO 7176 / EN 12184 standards and peer-reviewed rehabilitation-engineering literature. They are not universal constants but represent typical *design baselines* for the KTU problem-solving context.

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Propelling structures for assistive devices are broadly classified into **three families**:

1. **Manual Propelling Structures** — Purely human-powered (push-rim, lever-drive, foot-propelled).
2. **Powered Propelling Structures** — Electrically driven (hub motor, mid-drive motor, geared transmission).
3. **Hybrid / Power-Assist Structures** — Sensor-augmented manual systems (e.g., E-motion wheels, Yamaha JWX series).

Each family is treated in greater mechanical and analytical depth in the sections that follow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manual Propelling Structures

### 2.1.1 Push-Rim Propulsion (Standard Manual Wheelchair)

The push-rim is a smaller-diameter ring concentric with (and rigidly attached to) the main drive wheel. The user applies a tangential force **F** at the rim, producing a torque **τ** about the wheel hub.

**Operating principle:**
1. The user's hand grips the push-rim and applies a tangential force vector.
2. This force produces a torque $\tau = F \cdot r_{rim}$ on the wheel.
3. The wheel rotates, and through the tire–ground contact patch, a horizontal propulsive force is generated equal to (in static equilibrium with rolling resistance):

$$F_{propulsive} = \tau / r_{wheel} = F \cdot (r_{rim} / r_{wheel})$$

Because $r_{rim} < r_{wheel}$, the user gets a **mechanical disadvantage** in force — they must push harder than the propulsive force they receive. This is the trade-off that gives the wheelchair its **speed gearing** (the wheel travels a greater linear distance per rim rotation than the hand does).

### 2.1.2 Lever-Drive Propulsion

In lever-drive wheelchairs (e.g., the *Löffler* concept, the *CaterWil* GTS-3, or the *Veloce* lever chair), the user rocks a horizontal lever back and forth through an arc. A one-way clutch (ratchet-and-pawl or sprag clutch) converts the reciprocating motion into unidirectional wheel rotation.

**Advantages over push-rim:**
- Reduced upper-limb **repetitive-strain injury (RSI)** risk.
- Allows users with limited hand grip (arthritis, tetraplegia) to propel.
- Mechanical advantage up to **1:1.5** (force amplification).

**Disadvantages:**
- Slower top speed.
- Greater mechanical complexity and weight.
- Asymmetric propulsion if not bilateral.

### 2.1.3 Hand-Crank and Foot-Propelled Systems

- **Hand-crank tricycles** use a chain-and-sprocket transmission to drive the front wheel, with gear ratios from 1:1 to 1:3.
- **Foot-propelled wheelchairs** are used when the user has functional lower limbs but weak upper limbs (e.g., post-polio, lower-limb amputation with prosthesis).

---

## 2.2 Powered Propelling Structures

### 2.2.1 Hub Motor (Direct-Drive) Configuration

A **brushless DC (BLDC) hub motor** is integrated inside the wheel hub. The motor's rotor turns the wheel directly, eliminating chains, belts, and external gearing.

**Sub-types:**
- **Geared hub motor** (planetary gear inside hub) — high torque at low speed; weight ~3–5 kg.
- **Gearless (direct-drive) hub motor** — high efficiency at cruising speed; weight ~6–9 kg; lower noise.

**Key components inside a powered propelling structure:**
1. **DC Bus / Battery pack** (energy reservoir).
2. **Motor controller** (PWM-based, MOSFET/IGBT bridge, current and voltage sensing).
3. **BLDC motor** (electromechanical energy conversion).
4. **Wheel & tire** (mechanical output to ground).
5. **Brake (electromagnetic or mechanical)** for safety and hill-holding.
6. **Joystick / input transducer** (command signal).

### 2.2.2 Mid-Drive Motor with Chain/Belt Transmission

The motor is mounted on the frame, not inside the wheel. Torque is transmitted through a chain or toothed belt to a sprocket on the drive wheel. Advantages: better weight distribution, easier maintenance, interchangeable gearing.

---

## 2.3 Hybrid / Power-Assist Structures

A **torque sensor** or **force sensor** (strain gauge on the push-rim axle) detects the user's input force. The controller commands a hub motor to add a proportional supplementary torque.

**Examples in the market:** *e-motion* (Alber), *SMOOV* (Alber), *Yamaha JWX-1*, *Sunrise Medical Empulse R80*.

**Control law (simplified):**
$$T_{motor} = K_{p} \cdot F_{user} + T_{baseline}$$
where $K_p$ is the proportional-assist gain (typically 1.5 – 3.0) and $T_{baseline}$ is a small constant torque to overcome static friction.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the "must-know" equations for KTU ESE and internal assessments on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = F \cdot r$ | Torque from tangential force on rim or wheel | N·m |
| 2 | $F_{prop} = \tau / r_{wheel}$ | Propulsive force at ground contact | N |
| 3 | $v = \omega \cdot r_{wheel}$ | Linear velocity of chair | m/s |
| 4 | $P = \tau \cdot \omega = F \cdot v$ | Mechanical power (rotational / linear duality) | W |
| 5 | $GR = N_{out} / N_{in} = \omega_{in} / \omega_{out} = \tau_{out} / \tau_{in}$ | Gear ratio (speed reduction = torque multiplication) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in} = \tau_{out} \cdot \omega_{out} / (\tau_{in} \cdot \omega_{in})$ | Transmission efficiency (0.85–0.98 typical) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $F_{roll} = \mu_{r} \cdot W$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 8 | $F_{grade} = W \cdot \sin\theta$ | Grade (slope) resistance | N |
| 9 | $F_{total} = F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{accel} + F_{aero}$ | Total tractive resistance (sum of all resistive forces) | N |
| 10 | $MA = F_{out} / F_{in} = r_{in} / r_{out}$ | Mechanical advantage of a lever/gear system | dimensionless |
| 11 | $T_{motor} = K_{t} \cdot I_{a}$ | Motor torque from armature current (DC motor) | N·m |
| 12 | $V_{b} = E_{b} + I_{a} \cdot R_{a}$ | DC motor armature voltage equation (Kirchhoff's law) | V |
| 13 | $E_{b} = K_{e} \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF proportional to angular velocity | V |
| 14 | $P_{batt} = V_{batt} \cdot I_{batt}$ | Battery electrical power output | W |
| 15 | $\omega_{out} = \omega_{in} \cdot (N_{in} / N_{out})$ | Output angular velocity from gear reduction | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme problems, students are expected to know at minimum formulas 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, and 12. Formulas 13, 14, 15 are higher-order and may appear in 14-mark analytical sub-questions.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding propelling structures is critical for:

- **Rehabilitation engineering**: designing custom chairs for individual patients (paraplegics, amputees, elderly).
- **Ergonomics research**: quantifying shoulder-joint loading during manual propulsion (a key study area — see *Boninger et al., 2005* on median-nerve injury from push-rim biomechanics).
- **Standards compliance**: ISO 7176-5 (wheelchair dimensions), ISO 7176-11 (test dummies), EN 12184 (electric wheelchair requirements).
- **Sports medicine**: propulsion-mechanics analysis for wheelchair racing (e.g., Paralympic racing chairs with 80° camber angles).
- **Product development**: optimizing gear ratios, motor selection, and battery sizing for OEM manufacturers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Push-Rim Force to Ground Propulsive Force

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** A standard manual wheelchair has a push-rim radius $r_{rim} = 0.27$ m and a drive-wheel radius $r_{wheel} = 0.33$ m (including tire). The user applies a sustained tangential force of **F = 50 N** at the push-rim. Neglect rolling resistance and air drag. Find (a) the torque on the wheel hub, (b) the propulsive force at the ground, and (c) the chair's steady-state linear velocity if the user pushes the rim at a cadence producing an angular velocity of $\omega_{rim} = 1.5$ rad/s.

### Step 1 — Establish the relationship between rim and wheel angular velocity

Because the push-rim is rigidly attached to the wheel in a standard manual chair, the rim and wheel share the **same angular velocity** (gear ratio = 1:1):

$$\omega_{wheel} = \omega_{rim} = 1.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the torque on the hub

The user's tangential force $F$ at the rim produces a torque $\tau$ about the hub axis:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &= F \cdot r_{rim} \\
&= 50 \text{ N} \times 0.27 \text{ m} \\
&= 13.5 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the propulsive force at the ground

The wheel transmits this same torque to the ground. At the contact patch, the propulsive force $F_{prop}$ satisfies:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &= F_{prop} \cdot r_{wheel} \\
F_{prop} &= \frac{\tau}{r_{wheel}} \\
&= \frac{13.5 \text{ N·m}}{0.33 \text{ m}} \\
&\approx 40.91 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the linear velocity of the chair

The chair's translational speed is determined by the wheel's angular velocity and the wheel's rolling radius:

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \omega_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel} \\
&= 1.5 \text{ rad/s} \times 0.33 \text{ m} \\
&= 0.495 \text{ m/s} \\
&\approx 1.78 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Verify mechanical-power consistency (cross-check)

Mechanical power delivered by the user = power transmitted to ground:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{user} &= F \cdot v_{hand} = 50 \text{ N} \times (1.5 \times 0.27) \text{ m/s} = 50 \times 0.405 = 20.25 \text{ W} \\
P_{ground} &= F_{prop} \cdot v_{chair} = 40.91 \text{ N} \times 0.495 \text{ m/s} \approx 20.25 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

The values match (no losses assumed). ✔ Verified.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** The user is "paying" with **force** to get **speed** (mechanical disadvantage in force, advantage in velocity). This is the opposite of a car's first gear (high force, low speed).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Powered Wheelchair Hill-Climb Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** A powered wheelchair with a user + chair total mass **m = 95 kg** must climb a slope of $\theta = 8°$ at a steady speed of **v = 1.2 m/s**. The wheel radius $r_{wheel} = 0.31$ m, the coefficient of rolling resistance $\mu_r = 0.012$, and the aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d A = 0.45 \text{ m}^2$ with air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Assume drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.88$. Find (a) the total tractive force required, (b) the wheel torque, (c) the required motor torque, and (d) the mechanical + electrical power at the motor terminals.

### Step 1 — Compute the total weight force

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= m \cdot g \\
&= 95 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
&= 931.95 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the rolling resistance force

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{roll} &= \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta \\
&\approx 0.012 \times 931.95 \times \cos(8°) \\
&\approx 0.012 \times 931.95 \times 0.9903 \\
&\approx 11.07 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the grade (slope) resistance force

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{grade} &= W \cdot \sin\theta \\
&= 931.95 \times \sin(8°) \\
&= 931.95 \times 0.1392 \\
&\approx 129.71 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the aerodynamic drag force

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{aero} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d A \cdot v^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.45 \times (1.2)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.45 \times 1.44 \\
&\approx 0.397 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

(Note: aerodynamic drag is negligible for wheelchairs — included for completeness and to show the formula.)

### Step 5 — Sum all tractive forces (steady state, no acceleration)

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{aero} \\
&= 11.07 + 129.71 + 0.397 \\
&\approx 141.18 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the wheel torque

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{wheel} &= F_{total} \cdot r_{wheel} \\
&= 141.18 \times 0.31 \\
&\approx 43.77 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 — Compute the required motor torque (account for drivetrain efficiency)

For a mid-drive system with chain transmission, the motor must deliver more torque than the wheel receives *or* operate at higher speed, depending on gear ratio. With $GR = 1$ (direct chain drive, no reduction):

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{motor} &= \frac{\tau_{wheel}}{\eta} \\
&= \frac{43.77}{0.88} \\
&\approx 49.74 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8 — Compute the wheel's and motor's angular velocity

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_{wheel} &= \frac{v}{r_{wheel}} = \frac{1.2}{0.31} \approx 3.87 \text{ rad/s} \\
\omega_{motor} &= \omega_{wheel} = 3.87 \text{ rad/s} \quad (GR = 1)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 9 — Compute the mechanical power at the motor shaft

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{mech} &= \tau_{motor} \cdot \omega_{motor} \\
&= 49.74 \times 3.87 \\
&\approx 192.5 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 10 — Estimate electrical power at the motor terminals

Assuming motor electrical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.82$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{elec} &= \frac{P_{mech}}{\eta_m} \\
&\approx \frac{192.5}{0.82} \\
&\approx 234.8 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

At a 24 V battery, this draws approximately:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{batt} &= \frac{P_{elec}}{V_{batt}} = \frac{234.8}{24} \approx 9.78 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** The grade force (129.7 N) dominates the total resistance on slopes — it is **~12×** the rolling resistance. This is why powered wheelchairs with weak hub motors struggle on ramps and why stair-climbing robots need exotic mechanisms.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Scenario | Dominant Resistance | Primary Formula | Secondary Check |
|---|---|---|---|
| Level ground, slow speed | Rolling resistance | $F_{roll} = \mu_r W$ | Add small aero term if $v > 2$ m/s |
| Slope climb | Grade + rolling | $F_{total} = W\sin\theta + \mu_r W\cos\theta$ | $F_{grade} \gg F_{roll}$ for $\theta > 5°$ |
| Acceleration from rest | Inertia + rolling | $F_{accel} = m a$ | $\tau = (F_{roll} + F_{accel}) \cdot r$ |
| Long cruise (>2 m/s) | Aero + rolling | $F_{aero} = 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2$ | $P_{aero} \propto v^3$ |
| Starting on hill | Static friction | $F_{static} = \mu_s W \cos\theta$ | Must exceed $F_{grade}$ to move |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hub-Motor Sizing (Quick Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:** User mass = 80 kg, chair mass = 25 kg, max slope = 10°, max speed = 6 km/h, wheel diameter = 0.6 m, $\mu_r = 0.015$, $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.90$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.85$.
> **Find:** Minimum motor power rating.

### Step 1 — Convert units

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 10° = 0.1745 \text{ rad} \\
v_{max} &= 6 \text{ km/h} = 1.667 \text{ m/s} \\
r_{wheel} &= 0.30 \text{ m} \\
m_{total} &= 80 + 25 = 105 \text{ kg} \\
W &= 105 \times 9.81 = 1030.05 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute total tractive force at worst case (max slope)

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{roll} &= 0.015 \times 1030.05 \times \cos(10°) = 0.015 \times 1030.05 \times 0.9848 \approx 15.22 \text{ N} \\
F_{grade} &= 1030.05 \times \sin(10°) = 1030.05 \times 0.1736 \approx 178.83 \text{ N} \\
F_{total} &\approx 194.05 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute wheel torque

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{wheel} = 194.05 \times 0.30 = 58.22 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute motor shaft torque (account drivetrain)

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{motor} = 58.22 / 0.90 = 64.69 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Compute wheel speed

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_{wheel} = 1.667 / 0.30 = 5.56 \text{ rad/s} \approx 53.07 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Compute required mechanical power

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{mech} = 64.69 \times 5.56 \approx 359.7 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 — Compute required electrical input power

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{elec} = 359.7 / 0.85 \approx 423.2 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8 — Add safety margin (1.5× – 2×)

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{rated} \approx 1.5 \times 423.2 \approx 635 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** A pair of 350 W hub motors (per wheel) would meet the requirement with adequate thermal margin.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** Always size the motor for the *worst case* (max slope + max payload + low battery voltage), not the cruise case. The cruise case is 1/10th the demand.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Powered Propelling Structure

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram illustrates the *signal-and-power flow* through a typical powered-wheelchair propelling structure. It is a functional-architecture schematic, not a physical wireframe drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userInput[User Intent\nJoystick Position] --> ctrl[Motor Controller\nPWM / FOC]
    battery[DC Battery Pack\n24V / 36V / 48V] --> ctrl
    ctrl --> conv[Inverter Bridge\nMOSFET / IGBT]
    conv --> motor[BLDC Hub Motor\nGeared or Direct Drive]
    motor --> wheel[Drive Wheel\nTire + Hub Assembly]
    wheel --> ground[Ground Contact Patch\nTraction Force Output]
    wheel --> sensor[Encoder / Hall Sensor\nRotor Position Feedback]
    sensor --> ctrl
    pushrim[Push-Rim\nManual Override] --> wheel
    brake[Electromagnetic\nor Mechanical Brake] --> motor

    classDef power fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#000
    classDef signal fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000

    class battery,conv,motor power
    class userInput,ctrl,sensor signal
    class pushrim,wheel,ground,brake mech
```

### 4.1.1 Reading the Diagram

- **Orange-shaded blocks** = *power flow path* (electrical energy → mechanical energy).
- **Blue-shaded blocks** = *signal/control flow path* (user command → sensor feedback).
- **Purple-shaded blocks** = *mechanical output path* (torque → ground force).

The diagram is **bidirectional** because the Hall-sensor feedback closes the control loop, and the push-rim allows a *manual override* if the electronics fail (a KTU-emphasized safety feature per ISO 7176-14).
