# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]
**Define the term "propelling structure" as applied to assistive medical devices. List any four components of a powered-wheelchair propelling system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *propelling structure* is the integrated mechanical and electro-mechanical subsystem of an assistive device (such as a manual or powered wheelchair) that converts an input form of energy — typically human muscular effort or electrical energy from a battery — into controlled translational motion of the device, enabling the user to move from one location to another.

Four components of a powered-wheelchair propelling system (any four, ½ mark each):

1. **Battery pack** (energy reservoir — typically 24 V or 36 V Li-ion / Sealed Lead-Acid).
2. **Motor controller** (PWM/FOC-based electronic speed controller).
3. **BLDC hub motor** (electromechanical energy converter).
4. **Drive wheel and tire** (mechanical output interface with the ground).
5. *(Optional credit)* Electromagnetic brake, joystick transducer, encoder/Hall sensors.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]
**Distinguish between a "geared" and a "gearless" hub motor used in powered wheelchairs. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Geared Hub Motor | Gearless (Direct-Drive) Hub Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Internal mechanism | Planetary gear train inside hub | No gears; rotor drives wheel directly |
| Torque at low speed | **High** (gear reduction multiplies torque) | Lower (limited by motor's electromagnetic torque) |
| Efficiency at cruise speed | Lower (gear friction losses ~10–15%) | **Higher** (no gear losses; ~95%) |
| Weight | Lighter for given torque (~3–5 kg) | Heavier (~6–9 kg) |
| Noise | Higher (gear meshing audible) | **Quieter** |
| Maintenance | Periodic gear lubrication | Almost maintenance-free |

- **Advantage of geared:** Higher starting torque — better for hill climbing from rest.
- **Advantage of gearless:** Higher efficiency and silent operation — preferred for long-range indoor use.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Analytical Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulation:** Each Module-end question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (typically a design question and a numerical/derivation question). The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B (Module internal choice).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**A.(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a powered-wheelchair propelling structure. Clearly label the power-flow path, signal/control path, and mechanical-output path. Mention the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**A.(b)** A manual wheelchair is propelled by a user who applies a sustained tangential force of **60 N** on a push-rim of radius **0.28 m**. The drive wheel has an effective rolling radius of **0.34 m**. The chair + user weight is **780 N** and the rolling-resistance coefficient on the floor is **0.015**. The slope angle is **5°**. Assuming steady speed and neglecting aerodynamic drag, compute:
  (i) the torque applied to the wheel hub, **(2 marks)**
  (ii) the propulsive force at the ground, **(2 marks)**
  (iii) the total resistance force opposing motion, and **(1.5 marks)**
  (iv) whether the applied force is sufficient for steady motion on the slope. **(1.5 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — A.(a)** *(7 marks)*

**Block-diagram explanation:**

1. **User Input Transducer (Joystick)** — converts the user's hand displacement into a proportional DC voltage signal (0 – 5 V) representing desired speed and direction. *(0.5 mark)*

2. **Motor Controller (ESC)** — receives the joystick signal, compares it with feedback from the encoder/Hall sensors, and generates Pulse-Width-Modulation (PWM) or Field-Oriented-Control (FOC) commands for the inverter bridge. *(1 mark)*

3. **DC Battery Pack** — supplies electrical energy (typically 24 V, 36 V, or 48 V) to both the controller and the motor through a fused power bus. *(0.5 mark)*

4. **Inverter Bridge (MOSFET/IGBT)** — switches the DC bus into 3-phase AC waveforms at variable frequency, driving the BLDC motor. *(1 mark)*

5. **BLDC Hub Motor** — converts electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy through electromagnetic torque between the stator windings and the permanent-magnet rotor. *(1 mark)*

6. **Drive Wheel and Tire** — translates the rotor's torque into a horizontal propulsive force at the tire–ground contact patch. *(0.5 mark)*

7. **Encoder / Hall Sensor** — measures the rotor position (every 60° electrical) and feeds it back to the controller for commutation timing. Closes the control loop. *(1 mark)*

8. **Electromagnetic / Mechanical Brake** — activated by the controller or by a manual lever; provides a fail-safe stop. *(0.5 mark)*

9. **Push-Rim (manual override)** — allows the user to manually push the chair if the electronics fail (a safety requirement per ISO 7176-14). *(0.5 mark)*

10. **Tire–Ground Contact Patch** — produces the actual traction force $F_{prop} = \tau / r_{wheel}$ balanced against the total resistance. *(0.5 mark)*

*(A neat block diagram with labeled arrows — awarded 1 mark for the diagram itself, integrated into the above points.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — A.(b)** *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $F_{rim} = 60$ N (tangential force on push-rim)
- $r_{rim} = 0.28$ m
- $r_{wheel} = 0.34$ m
- $W = 780$ N (total weight)
- $\mu_r = 0.015$
- $\theta = 5°$

**(i) Torque on wheel hub — (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{hub} &= F_{rim} \cdot r_{rim} \\
&= 60 \times 0.28 \\
&= 16.8 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Propulsive force at ground — (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{prop} &= \frac{\tau_{hub}}{r_{wheel}} \\
&= \frac{16.8}{0.34} \\
&\approx 49.41 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Total resistance force — (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{roll} &= \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta = 0.015 \times 780 \times \cos(5°) \\
&\approx 0.015 \times 780 \times 0.9962 \\
&\approx 11.66 \text{ N} \\
F_{grade} &= W \cdot \sin\theta = 780 \times \sin(5°) = 780 \times 0.0872 \\
&\approx 68.02 \text{ N} \\
F_{total} &= 11.66 + 68.02 \approx 79.68 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Sufficiency check — (1.5 marks):**

Available propulsive force = **49.41 N**

Required propulsive force = **79.68 N**

Since 49.41 N < 79.68 N, the applied force of 60 N is **insufficient** to maintain steady motion on the 5° slope. The chair will decelerate and eventually stop. **Conclusion: The user needs to apply at least 96.8 N on the rim to climb this slope at steady speed.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{required} &= F_{total} \times \frac{r_{wheel}}{r_{rim}} \\
&= 79.68 \times \frac{0.34}{0.28} \\
&\approx 96.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative — KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**

**B.(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **lever-drive wheelchair** with the help of a labeled diagram. Compare its mechanical advantage and ergonomic impact with a standard push-rim wheelchair. **(7 marks)**

**B.(b)** A power-assist wheelchair uses a geared BLDC hub motor with the following specifications:

- Gear reduction ratio $N_{in} : N_{out} = 5 : 1$ (motor turns 5 times per wheel revolution)
- Motor torque constant $K_t = 0.085$ N·m/A
- Armature resistance $R_a = 0.42$ Ω
- Back-EMF constant $K_e = 0.085$ V·s/rad
- Battery voltage $V_b = 36$ V
- Wheel radius $r_{wheel} = 0.32$ m
- User + chair mass $m = 100$ kg, level ground, $\mu_r = 0.012$

Compute:
  (i) the maximum **stall torque** the motor can deliver, **(2 marks)**
  (ii) the motor's **no-load speed** in RPM, **(2 marks)**
  (iii) the **wheel torque** and the **maximum propulsive force** at ground, **(1.5 marks)**
  (iv) whether the chair can overcome the rolling resistance on level ground. **(1.5 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — B.(a)** *(7 marks)*

**Principle of Operation:**

A lever-drive wheelchair replaces the rotating push-rim with a **horizontal lever (handle)** that the user rocks back and forth. The lever is connected through a **chain or shaft** to a **one-way (overrunning) clutch** — typically a sprag or ratchet-and-pawl type. This clutch permits torque transmission in **only one rotational direction** of the wheel, so each forward stroke of the lever produces wheel rotation, and the return stroke is "free-wheeled." *(2 marks)*

**Labeled diagram description (text-based since Mermaid is unsuited to detailed physical drawings):**

```
    [User Hand]
        |
        v
   <-- LEVER (rocks in arc) -->
        |
   [Drive Shaft]
        |
   [One-Way Clutch] ---> [Sprocket]
                              |
                         [Chain / Belt]
                              |
                         [Drive-Wheel Sprocket]
                              |
                         [Wheel Hub]
                              |
                         [Tire / Ground]
```

**Mechanical advantage comparison:** *(2 marks)*

| Parameter | Push-Rim | Lever-Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical advantage | 1:1 (force × rim radius = force × wheel radius) | Up to 1:1.5 (lever length / crank radius) |
| User's hand motion | Continuous circular | Reciprocating arc |
| Force per stroke | Lower (distributed) | Higher (concentrated push) |
| Risk of RSI | **High** (continuous gripping) | **Lower** (neutral wrist posture) |
| Speed | Higher top speed | Slightly lower |

**Ergonomic impact:** *(3 marks)*

1. **Shoulder joint loading** — Lever drive eliminates the repetitive wrist-pronation movement, reducing risk of carpal tunnel syndrome and shoulder impingement (Boninger et al., 2005).
2. **Trunk stability** — Bilateral lever use encourages symmetric trunk engagement, useful for users with scoliosis or post-stroke hemiparesis.
3. **Functional reach** — Users with limited shoulder flexion (e.g., post-burn contracture) can still reach a lever easier than a low-mounted push-rim.
4. **Cognitive load** — Slightly higher (need to time strokes) — may not suit users with cognitive impairment.

---

#### **Model Solution — B.(b)** *(7 marks)*

**Given constants:**
- $GR = N_{in}/N_{out} = 5/1$ (motor speed : wheel speed)
- $K_t = 0.085$ N·m/A
- $R_a = 0.42$ Ω
- $K_e = 0.085$ V·s/rad
- $V_b = 36$ V
- $r_{wheel} = 0.32$ m
- $m = 100$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\mu_r = 0.012$

**(i) Maximum stall torque — (2 marks):**

At stall, $\omega = 0$, so back-EMF $E_b = 0$. The current is limited only by $R_a$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{stall} &= \frac{V_b}{R_a} = \frac{36}{0.42} \approx 85.71 \text{ A} \\
\tau_{stall,motor} &= K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.085 \times 85.71 \approx 7.29 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) No-load speed (motor) — (2 marks):**

At no load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $V_b \approx E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_{0,motor} &= \frac{V_b}{K_e} = \frac{36}{0.085} \approx 423.53 \text{ rad/s} \\
n_{0,motor} &= \frac{\omega_{0,motor} \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{423.53 \times 60}{6.2832} \approx 4043 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Wheel torque and maximum propulsive force — (1.5 marks):**

The gear ratio of 5:1 multiplies torque at the wheel:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{wheel} &= \tau_{stall,motor} \times GR = 7.29 \times 5 = 36.43 \text{ N·m} \\
F_{prop,max} &= \frac{\tau_{wheel}}{r_{wheel}} = \frac{36.43}{0.32} \approx 113.85 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Sufficiency check on level ground — (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 100 \times 9.81 = 981 \text{ N} \\
F_{roll} &= \mu_r \cdot W = 0.012 \times 981 \approx 11.77 \text{ N} \\
\text{Since } F_{prop,max} = 113.85 \text{ N} \gg F_{roll} = 11.77 \text{ N}, \\
\text{the chair can easily overcome the rolling resistance.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Margin of safety:** $113.85 / 11.77 \approx 9.67$ — i.e., the propelling structure has nearly 10× the force needed on level ground, providing ample reserve for slopes and accelerations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Loss-of-Marks Warning)**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s (factor of $2\pi/60$) is the **#1 mark-loser** in motor problems. Always state: $\omega \text{ (rad/s)} = n \text{ (RPM)} \times 2\pi / 60$.
> 2. **Confusing motor torque and wheel torque** — students often forget to multiply by the gear ratio, leading to 50% errors in propulsive force.
> 3. **Back-EMF direction sign** — in KVL, $V_b = E_b + I_a R_a$ (not $V_b = E_b - I_a R_a$). Using the wrong sign will give a negative current.
> 4. **Neglecting cos(θ) in rolling resistance on slopes** — full credit only if $F_{roll} = \mu_r W \cos\theta$ is used; using $F_{roll} = \mu_r W$ on a slope loses 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the block diagram** — Section A.(a) explicitly awards marks for the diagram. A text-only answer without a diagram loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **⭐ Memorize these 12 points the night before your KTU exam:**

- [x] **Definition:** Propelling structure = *energy source + transmission + drive interface* that produces motion.
- [x] **Three families:** Manual (push-rim, lever), Powered (hub motor, mid-drive), Hybrid (power-assist).
- [x] **Key torque equation:** $\tau = F \cdot r$ — apply at the rim to get hub torque, divide by wheel radius to get ground force.
- [x] **Mechanical disadvantage in manual chairs:** The user pushes harder than the propulsive force but travels farther per stroke (velocity advantage).
- [x] **Lever-drive MA:** Up to 1:1.5, but with a reciprocating motion and one-way clutch.
- [x] **Total resistance:** $F_{total} = F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{accel} + F_{aero}$ — on slopes, $F_{grade}$ dominates.
- [x] **DC motor equations:** $\tau = K_t I_a$, $V_b = K_e \omega + I_a R_a$, with $K_t$ and $K_e$ numerically equal in SI units.
- [x] **Gear ratio:** Torque multiplies by $GR$; speed divides by $GR$ (energy conservation).
- [x] **Hub motor types:** Geared = high torque, gearless = high efficiency. Pick by application.
- [x] **Safety:** Push-rim manual override and electromagnetic brake are ISO 7176-14 requirements.
- [x] **Power-assist control:** $T_{motor} = K_p \cdot F_{user} + T_{baseline}$ — proportional-assist with bias torque.
- [x] **Sizing rule of thumb:** For max slope θ on level surface, motor power ≈ 1.5 × (W sinθ) × v to account for losses and acceleration reserve.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 — Propelling Structure Notes**
> These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEBRT745 — Assistive Medical Devices** and follow the expected KTU board-exam valuation pattern. For cross-reference on related topics (push-rim biomechanics, BLDC motor commutation, ISO 7176 series), consult the prescribed textbook and the standard's official text on the BIS / ISO portal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specialized Bases
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Specialized Bases

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specialized Bases** in the context of assistive medical devices refer to the engineered structural foundations of prosthetic and orthotic systems that interface directly with the human body (residual limb, anatomical segment) and the external environment (ground, support surface). They are custom-fabricated or modular load-distribution platforms designed using biomechanical principles to transmit forces, provide stability, and replicate natural human movement.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, specialized bases are categorized into three primary functional families:
- **Terminal Bases (Prosthetic Feet & Hands)**: The distal end of a prosthesis contacting the environment.
- **Socket Bases (Interface Systems)**: The proximal cup-shaped housing enclosing the residual limb.
- **Structural Bases (Shank/Pylon Connectors)**: Load-bearing columns connecting the socket to the terminal base.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Building Analogy** 🏗️
>
> Think of a prosthetic limb like a **multi-storey building**:
> - The **Socket Base** is the *foundation* — it must perfectly fit the unique "soil" (your residual limb) and transfer the entire building's weight safely.
> - The **Pylon/Shank** is the *vertical pillars* — they carry compressive loads.
> - The **Prosthetic Foot** is the *ground floor with shock absorbers* — it absorbs impact, rolls naturally, and pushes back during walking.
>
> Just as a building needs the *right* foundation for the *right* soil, a prosthetic user needs a *specialized base* matching their **activity level (K-level)**, **body weight**, and **amputation level**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Engineering Parameters to Memorize:**
> - **K-Levels (Medicare Functional Classification):** **K0** (no ability) → **K4** (high activity, athlete-level)
> - **Standard Body Weight Limit:** **100 kg** (220 lbs) for conventional SACH feet; up to **150 kg** for heavy-duty modular feet
> - **Heel Height Adjustability:** ±2 cm in modular feet
> - **Toe Lever Arm:** Approximately **1/3 of foot length** for SACH design
> - **Stiffness Categories:** Class I (soft, <60 kg) → Class V (extra stiff, >120 kg)
> - **Dorsi-plantar ROM in Single-Axis Foot:** 15°–30° total range

## 1.4 Classification of Specialized Bases

| Family | Sub-Type | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Terminal Bases** | SACH, Single-Axis, Multi-Axis, Dynamic Response | Ground contact, energy storage, balance |
| **Socket Bases** | PTB, TSB, Suction, Vacuum-assisted | Load transfer, suspension, comfort |
| **Modular Bases** | Endoskeletal pylons, exoskeletal shells | Connection, alignment, cosmetic cover |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Specialized Foot Base Design

### 2.1.1 SACH Foot (Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel)

- **Architecture**: Solid wooden or rigid plastic core + compressed rubber heel wedge + cosmetic rubber toe.
- **Mechanism**: The heel wedge compresses under load → simulates ankle plantarflexion. The solid keel provides **rollover** but no true articulation.
- **Why & How**: A wooden wedge (typically 5°–15° angle) is shaped such that compression mimics ~10° of ankle motion without any moving parts — the *cheapest, most durable, lowest-maintenance* foot base.
- **KTU Key Insight**: Indicated for **K1–K2** level patients; contraindicated for high-activity users.

### 2.1.2 Single-Axis Foot

- **Architecture**: Rigid keel + mechanical ankle joint (single axis) + rubber bumpers dorsally and plantarly.
- **Mechanism**: Mechanical hinge allows true plantarflexion/dorsiflexion. Anterior/posterior rubber bumpers **limit range** and absorb shock.
- **Why & How**: Provides better **knee stability** for transfemoral amputees during heel-strike by allowing rapid plantarflexion (controlled knee flexion). This is critical for above-knee amputees at risk of knee buckling.

### 2.1.3 Multi-Axis Foot

- **Architecture**: Universal joint or split keel allowing motion in **inversion/eversion** and **dorsi/plantarflexion**.
- **Mechanism**: Internal compliance in multiple planes accommodates uneven terrain (grass, gravel, slopes).
- **Why & How**: Reduces shear forces on the socket-limb interface → better comfort for **K2–K3** patients walking on irregular surfaces.

### 2.1.4 Dynamic Response (Energy-Storing) Foot

- **Architecture**: Carbon fiber or fiberglass composite spring-like keel; no mechanical joints.
- **Mechanism**: Material **stores elastic energy** at heel-strike and midstance, then **releases** at toe-off (propulsion).
- **KTU High-Yield Concept**: This is the **gold standard** for K3–K4 patients, athletes, and active individuals. The carbon fiber flexes millions of cycles without fatigue.

## 2.2 Specialized Socket Bases (Interface Systems)

### 2.2.1 PTB Socket (Patellar Tendon Bearing)

- **Concept**: Designed to bear weight primarily on the **patellar tendon**, **medial tibial flare**, and **fibular shaft** — not on the distal end.
- **Why**: Distal end-bearing in transtibial amputees causes pain, skin breakdown, and reduced circulation. PTB redistributes load to tolerant anatomical structures.
- **Key Features**: Medial wedge (PTB bar), posterior brim, anterior trim line below patella.

### 2.2.2 TSB Socket (Total Surface Bearing)

- **Concept**: Distributes load over the **entire residual limb surface** uniformly.
- **How Achieved**: Achieved using silicone or urethane liners that create a uniform pressure profile.
- **Indication**: Used with vacuum suspension and modern high-activity prostheses.

### 2.2.3 Suction Socket

- **Mechanism**: Air-tight seal created between socket and liner → negative pressure during swing phase holds the prosthesis.
- **Advantage**: Eliminates straps, pistoning, and improves proprioception (feels more like a part of the body).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mandatory** for KTU ESE questions from this module.

| # | Formula / Principle | Meaning / Application | Units / Constants |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Stress on socket-limb interface (F = body weight load, A = contact area) | $\sigma$ in N/m² (Pa) |
| 2 | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | Elastic energy stored in carbon keel (k = spring stiffness, x = deflection) | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $\eta_{return} = \dfrac{E_{released}}{E_{stored}} \times 100\%$ | Energy return efficiency of dynamic response foot (typical 80–95%) | Dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | $F_{ground} = m \cdot g$ | Vertical ground reaction force at heel-strike (g = 9.81 m/s²) | Newtons (N) |
| 5 | $\tau = F \cdot d$ | Torque on ankle joint (single-axis foot bumper) | N·m |
| 6 | $P_{socket} = P_{atm} - \rho g h$ | Suction socket pressure differential (Bernoulli-style) | Pa |
| 7 | $\theta_{pf} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h_{wedge}}{L_{heel}}\right)$ | Effective plantarflexion angle in SACH foot | Degrees (°) |
| 8 | $K\text{-level} \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}$ | Medicare functional classification (assigns foot type) | Categorical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Table cells use `\vert` or `\mid` style — never the bare pipe `\|` character** to prevent markdown breaking.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation Engineering**: Specialized bases form the clinical backbone of gait restoration. Each design choice (keel material, bumper density, socket geometry) directly maps to measurable outcomes (walking speed, symmetry, oxygen cost).
- **Sports Performance**: Paralympic sprinters use **vertical shock pylons** + **J-shaped carbon blades** → energy return > 90%.
- **Pediatric Prosthetics**: Modular bases with growth-adjustable sockets reduce replacement frequency by **40%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Return Efficiency of a Carbon Fiber Foot

**Problem Context (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A K3-level transtibial amputee weighing **75 kg** uses a carbon fiber dynamic response foot. The keel deflects by **x = 1.2 cm** under load with an effective spring stiffness of **k = 350,000 N/m**. Calculate the **elastic energy stored**, the **kinetic energy returned at toe-off** (assume 85% efficiency), and the **effective propulsion impulse**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Vertical Ground Reaction Force

The body weight acting on the foot at heel-strike is the mass times gravity acceleration. This represents the maximum compressive force the keel must absorb.

$$
F_{ground} = m \cdot g
$$

Substituting the numerical values (m = 75 kg, g = 9.81 m/s²):

$$
F_{ground} = 75 \times 9.81 = 735.75 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Stored Elastic Energy

The carbon keel acts as a linear spring. The elastic potential energy stored is half the stiffness constant times the square of the deflection. This is the *total mechanical energy* available for return.

$$
E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, k \, x^2
$$

Substituting k = 350,000 N/m and x = 1.2 cm = 0.012 m:

$$
E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 350{,}000 \times (0.012)^2
$$

$$
E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 350{,}000 \times 0.000144
$$

$$
E_{stored} = 25.2 \text{ J}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Efficiency Factor to Get Returned Energy

The foot does not return 100% of the stored energy because some is dissipated as internal damping (hysteresis) and heat. The clinical efficiency of a carbon keel is typically 80% to 95%. Here we use 85%.

$$
E_{returned} = \eta \cdot E_{stored}
$$

$$
E_{returned} = 0.85 \times 25.2 = 21.42 \text{ J}
$$

### Step 4 — Estimate the Equivalent Propulsion Velocity

To translate the returned energy into a kinematic proxy, equate the returned energy to the kinetic energy of a virtual mass equal to the user's leg-foot segment (assume effective mass m_eff = 4 kg for the foot-shank system).

$$
E_{returned} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, m_{eff} \, v^2
$$

Solving for v:

$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, E_{returned}}{m_{eff}}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 21.42}{4}} = \sqrt{10.71} \approx 3.27 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Propulsion Impulse

Impulse is the product of effective propulsion force and contact time. For a typical toe-off duration of **t = 0.10 s** at this foot, the average propulsion force and resulting impulse are:

$$
F_{prop} = \dfrac{E_{returned}}{\bar{x}}
$$

where $\bar{x}$ is the average effective lever arm over which the energy is released, taken as **0.08 m** (approx. 1/3 of a 24 cm foot length).

$$
F_{prop} = \dfrac{21.42}{0.08} = 267.75 \text{ N}
$$

$$
J = F_{prop} \cdot t = 267.75 \times 0.10 = 26.78 \text{ N·s}
$$

### Final Result Summary

> **Stored Energy:** 25.2 J
> **Returned Energy:** 21.42 J (85% efficiency)
> **Effective Propulsion Velocity:** ~3.27 m/s
> **Propulsion Impulse:** 26.78 N·s

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A question combining *energy storage + efficiency + propulsion* is a **guaranteed 14-mark problem** in ESE. Practice writing each equation on a **new line** with a one-sentence justification — that is exactly how KTU examiners award step marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Specialized Base

| Patient Profile | Amputation Level | K-Level | Recommended Base | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Elderly, household walker | Transtibial | K1 | SACH foot + PTB socket | Low cost, durable, sufficient for limited ambulation |
| Above-knee, uneven terrain | Transfemoral | K2 | Single-Axis foot + Suction socket | Knee stability + air-tight suspension |
| Active adult, office worker | Transtibial | K3 | Multi-Axis foot + TSB liner | Terrain adaptation + comfort |
| Athlete / sprinter | Transtibial | K4 | Carbon J-shaped blade + Vacuum socket | Maximum energy return + secure suspension |
| Diabetic, sensitive limb | Transtibial | K2 | Multi-Axis foot + Silicone liner TSB | Pressure redistribution + shock absorption |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Modular Transtibial Prosthesis

The diagram below maps the **interaction pathways** between the specialized base components — from the residual limb down to ground contact. Each node represents a *functional module* and the arrows indicate **force/energy flow direction**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Residual Limb] -->|Load Transfer| B[Socket Base - PTB or TSB]
    B -->|Suspension Force| C[Suspension System - Suction or Pin Lock]
    B -->|Axial Compression| D[Pylon Shank - Endoskeletal]
    D -->|Alignment Coupling| E[Adapter Pyramid]
    E -->|Bending Moment| F[Terminal Base - Prosthetic Foot]
    F -->|Heel Strike Impact| G[Heel Wedge - Rubber or Foam]
    F -->|Rollover Lever| H[Keel - Carbon or Wood]
    F -->|Toe Off Energy| I[Forefoot Spring]
    G -->|Stored Energy| H
    H -->|Propulsion Impulse| A

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#e8f5e9
    style E fill:#e8f5e9
    style F fill:#fce4ec
    style G fill:#f3e5f5
    style H fill:#f3e5f5
    style I fill:#f3e5f5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **top-down arrows** (A → B → D → F) represent the *static load path* (body weight transmission). The **bottom-up arrows** (G → H → A) represent the *dynamic energy return path* during push-off — this is the unique feature of energy-storing feet.

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Selecting a Specialized Base

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Patient Assessment]) --> Q1{K-Level?}
    Q1 -->|K0| R0[No Prosthesis]
    Q1 -->|K1| R1[SACH Foot + PTB Socket]
    Q1 -->|K2| Q2{Terrain Type?}
    Q1 -->|K3 to K4| R3[Dynamic Response Carbon Foot + TSB Vacuum Socket]
    Q2 -->|Flat indoor| R2A[Single Axis Foot]
    Q2 -->|Uneven outdoor| R2B[Multi Axis Foot]
    R1 --> End([Prescription])
    R2A --> End
    R2B --> End
    R3 --> End

    style Start fill:#bbdefb
    style R0 fill:#ffcdd2
    style R1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style R2A fill:#c8e6c9
    style R2B fill:#c8e6c9
    style R3 fill:#fff9c4
    style End fill:#b39ddb
```

## 4.3 Component Cross-Section: SACH vs Dynamic Response Foot

| Feature | SACH Foot | Dynamic Response Foot |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Core Material** | Wood or rigid plastic | Carbon fiber composite |
| **Heel** | Compressed rubber wedge | Internal spring / foam |
| **Toe Lever** | Passive, fixed length | Active flex keel |
| **Ankle Joint** | None | None (material flexion) |
| **Energy Return** | 0–10% | 80–95% |
| **Weight** | 250–400 g | 200–500 g |
| **Cost (approx)** | ₹3,000–₹8,000 | ₹50,000–₹2,50,000 |
| **Lifespan** | 2–5 years | 2–3 years (high activity) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** A common KTU question asks students to *label* the heel wedge, keel, and toe lever on a SACH foot. Always remember: **heel wedge is at the back (posterior)**, **keel is the rigid central core**, and **toe lever is the front 1/3 of the foot**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a SACH foot. State any two of its advantages over a single-axis foot.**

**Model Answer:**
A SACH (Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel) foot is a specialized terminal base consisting of a solid internal keel (wood or plastic), a compressible rubber heel wedge, and a cosmetic rubber toe. The heel wedge compression simulates ankle plantarflexion without any mechanical joint.

**Advantages over Single-Axis Foot:**
1. **No moving parts** → minimal maintenance, no lubrication, no wear of mechanical hinges.
2. **Lower cost** → approximately 5–10× cheaper, making it ideal for low-resource settings and K1-level patients.
3. **Lighter weight** in basic designs and **waterproof** for shower use.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a Patellar Tendon Bearing (PTB) socket? Why is it preferred over an end-bearing socket in transtibial prostheses?**

**Model Answer:**
A PTB socket is a specialized socket base designed for transtibial (below-knee) amputees that bears the majority of body weight on the **patellar tendon**, **medial tibial flare**, and **fibular shaft**, while distributing the remaining load over the total residual limb surface.

**Why preferred over end-bearing socket:**
- The distal end of the residual limb has **poor tolerance** to direct pressure (scar tissue, nerve endings, poor vascularization).
- End-bearing sockets cause **skin breakdown, pain, and reduced circulation** in transtibial amputees.
- PTB design **redistributes load** to pressure-tolerant anatomical structures, improving comfort, mobility, and long-term limb health.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) forgetting to mention the **K-level** when justifying a foot choice, (2) confusing **PTB vs TSB socket** load-bearing areas, and (3) omitting the **energy efficiency equation** $\eta = E_{released}/E_{stored}$ in numerical problems. Always write the **K-level, indication, and contraindication** explicitly.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of **specialized prosthetic foot bases** used in lower limb prosthetics. Explain the design and biomechanical function of the **Dynamic Response (Energy-Storing) Foot** with a neat sketch of the energy return mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 70 kg transtibial amputee uses a carbon fiber foot with effective spring stiffness **k = 300,000 N/m** and keel deflection of **1.5 cm** under load. If the foot's energy return efficiency is **90%**, calculate: (i) the stored elastic energy, (ii) the returned energy, and (iii) the equivalent propulsion impulse if the toe-off contact time is 0.12 s and effective lever arm is 0.09 m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Classification of Specialized Prosthetic Foot Bases:**

1. **Non-articulated (Solid Ankle) Feet**: SACH foot, STEN foot.
2. **Single-Axis (Articulated) Foot**: Mechanical ankle joint with bumpers.
3. **Multi-Axis Foot**: Universal joint allowing inversion/eversion + dorsi/plantarflexion.
4. **Dynamic Response / Energy-Storing Foot**: Carbon fiber or fiberglass composite keel.
5. **Specialty Feet**: Microprocessor-controlled, hydraulic, swimming, running blades.

**Design and Biomechanical Function of the Dynamic Response Foot:**

- **Materials**: High-modulus carbon fiber composite, sometimes with titanium core.
- **Structure**: A long, slender J-shaped or C-shaped keel extends from the heel to the toe. The keel is bonded within a foam cosmetic shell.
- **Biomechanical Function**:
  - At **heel-strike**, the posterior keel compresses → stores elastic strain energy.
  - During **mid-stance**, the keel bends and rolls over the ground reaction force vector.
  - At **toe-off**, the keel recoils → releases 80–95% of stored energy as forward propulsion.
- **Indication**: K3–K4 level patients; athletes; active adults; long-distance walkers.
- **Contraindication**: K0–K1 patients (over-engineered and expensive for low activity).

*(Sketch description for answer booklet: Draw the carbon keel as a curved J-shape from posterior heel to anterior toe, with arrows showing compression at heel and recoil at toe.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- m = 70 kg
- k = 300,000 N/m
- x = 1.5 cm = 0.015 m
- η = 90% = 0.90
- t = 0.12 s
- Lever arm $\bar{x}$ = 0.09 m

**(i) Stored Elastic Energy:**

$$
E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} k x^2
$$

$$
E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 300{,}000 \times (0.015)^2
$$

$$
E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 300{,}000 \times 0.000225
$$

$$
E_{stored} = 33.75 \text{ J}
$$

**(ii) Returned Energy:**

$$
E_{returned} = \eta \times E_{stored}
$$

$$
E_{returned} = 0.90 \times 33.75
$$

$$
E_{returned} = 30.375 \text{ J}
$$

**(iii) Propulsion Impulse:**

The average propulsion force is the returned energy divided by the effective lever arm.

$$
F_{prop} = \dfrac{E_{returned}}{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{30.375}{0.09} = 337.5 \text{ N}
$$

The impulse is the product of the average propulsion force and the contact time.

$$
J = F_{prop} \times t
$$

$$
J = 337.5 \times 0.12
$$

$$
J = 40.5 \text{ N·s}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $E_{stored} = 33.75 \text{ J}$
- $E_{returned} = 30.375 \text{ J}$
- $J = 40.5 \text{ N·s}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **PTB socket** and **TSB socket** for transtibial amputees in terms of load-bearing areas, indications, and clinical advantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of **suction suspension** in prosthetic sockets. Derive the relationship between **socket pressure differential** and the **height of air column evacuated**, and state the clinical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| Parameter | PTB Socket | TSB Socket |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Load-Bearing Areas** | Patellar tendon, medial tibial flare, fibular shaft | Entire residual limb surface (uniform) |
| **Indication** | K1–K3 transtibial amputees with mature, conical residual limb | K3–K4 active amputees, used with silicone liners and vacuum systems |
| **Suspension** | Supracondylar cuff, strap, or pin lock | Suction or vacuum-assisted (air-tight seal) |
| **Pressure Distribution** | Localized to tolerant areas | Uniform across the limb surface |
| **Advantage** | Simpler fabrication, lower cost, easier donning | Better proprioception, reduced pistoning, improved comfort for high activity |

**Clinical Significance:** PTB sockets remain the most prescribed transtibial socket worldwide due to ease of fabrication and tolerance of limb shape variations. TSB sockets have gained popularity with the advent of silicone liners and vacuum systems, especially for active users.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Principle of Suction Suspension:**
Suction suspension uses an **air-tight seal** between the socket wall and a silicone/urethane liner worn on the residual limb. During the swing phase of gait, the prosthetic socket rises slightly relative to the limb, creating a **negative pressure (vacuum)** inside the socket. This pressure differential generates an **adhesive force** that holds the prosthesis to the limb.

**Derivation of Pressure Differential:**

Consider a vertical column of air inside the socket. The atmospheric pressure pushes the limb into the socket, while the pressure inside the socket is reduced. The pressure differential is governed by the hydrostatic principle applied to the air column of effective height *h*:

$$
P_{socket} = P_{atm} - \rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

where:
- $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure ≈ 101,325 Pa
- $\rho_{air}$ = density of air ≈ 1.225 kg/m³
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
- $h$ = effective height of evacuated air column (typically 5–15 cm for a transtibial socket)

**The Holding Force is:**

$$
F_{hold} = \Delta P \cdot A = (P_{atm} - P_{socket}) \cdot A_{contact}
$$

where $A_{contact}$ is the effective contact area between the liner and the socket wall.

**Clinical Significance:**
- Eliminates the need for straps, belts, or pin locks.
- Reduces **pistoning** (vertical movement of limb within socket) → fewer skin abrasions.
- Improves **proprioception** (sensory feedback) → patient feels the prosthesis as a part of the body.
- Better **gait symmetry** and reduced energy expenditure.
- Contraindicated in patients with **unhealed wounds, severe scarring, or excessive perspiration** (compromised seal).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Specialized Bases":**

- ✅ **SACH Foot** = Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel → no joint, wooden keel + rubber heel wedge → **K1**.
- ✅ **Single-Axis Foot** = mechanical ankle joint → provides knee stability → **K2 transfemoral**.
- ✅ **Multi-Axis Foot** = inversion/eversion + dorsi/plantarflexion → uneven terrain → **K2–K3**.
- ✅ **Dynamic Response Foot** = carbon fiber, energy-storing → **80–95% energy return** → **K3–K4**.
- ✅ **PTB Socket** = load on patellar tendon + medial tibial flare → **NOT on distal end**.
- ✅ **TSB Socket** = uniform load on whole limb surface → used with vacuum.
- ✅ **Suction Suspension** = $F_{hold} = \Delta P \cdot A$ → air-tight seal → better proprioception.
- ✅ **K-Levels**: K0 (none) → K4 (athlete) — always quote this in exam answers.
- ✅ **Energy Formula**: $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$ and $\eta = E_{released} / E_{stored} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Propulsion Impulse**: $J = F_{prop} \times t$ where $F_{prop} = E_{returned} / \bar{x}$.
- ✅ **SACH Toe Lever** = anterior 1/3 of foot; **Heel Wedge** = posterior; **Keel** = central rigid core.
- ✅ **Indications** must always be paired with **Contraindications** in long-answer questions.

> **End of Module 3 — Specialized Bases | KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes**

topics.title: Smart Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: SMART WHEELCHAIRS — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Smart Wheelchair (SW)** is a motorized mobility assistive device that augments a conventional powered wheelchair with an intelligent embedded system comprising **sensors, micro-controllers, actuators, and human-machine interfaces (HMI)**, enabling semi-autonomous or fully autonomous navigation, obstacle avoidance, environment mapping, and health monitoring for users with physical, cognitive, or sensory impairments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim phrasing style):**
> A Smart Wheelchair is defined as an *Assistive Medical Device* that integrates a conventional powered wheelchair platform with **perception, cognition, and action** subsystems to provide independent mobility to individuals who cannot operate a standard powered wheelchair using conventional input devices (e.g., joystick, sip-and-puff).

## 1.2 Anatomical Block Taxonomy

A Smart Wheelchair is conventionally decomposed into **three functional layers** matching the classic sense–plan–act paradigm of mobile robotics:

| Layer | Function | Typical Hardware |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Data acquisition from environment & user | Ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR, RGB-D camera, IMU, BCI/EMG electrodes |
| **Cognition Layer** | Decision making, SLAM, path planning | ARM/DSP microcontroller, onboard SoC, ROS node |
| **Action Layer** | Motor drive, braking, HMI feedback | BLDC motors, motor drivers, haptic/tactile display |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Wheelchair as a Self-Driving Car for the Body"**
> Think of a smart wheelchair as a **miniature autonomous vehicle, but the passenger is the "driver"** with limited limb control. A regular wheelchair is like a manual bicycle — the rider must steer every turn. A smart wheelchair is like a **modern self-driving car**: sensors (cameras, ultrasonic) act as the "eyes," the onboard computer is the "brain" that processes surroundings, and the motors are the "legs" that execute decisions. Critically, the **user remains in command** through intent-recognition interfaces (voice, head tilt, BCI), making it a *collaborative* autonomy system, not a replacement autonomy system.

## 1.4 Why Smart Wheelchairs? — Clinical & Engineering Motivation

- **Epidemiological Need:** WHO estimates that **~80 million people** globally require a wheelchair for mobility, of which a significant percentage have **upper-limb dexterity loss** (ALS, spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy, MS).
- **Standard joystick fails** for users with: (i) severe tremors, (ii) limited hand/arm range, (iii) cognitive deficits, (iv) locked-in syndrome.
- **Smart wheelchairs bridge this gap** by offering *alternative input modalities* (voice, EEG, eye-gaze) and *safety augmentation* (collision avoidance).

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (KTU-defined)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **bold** metrics are standard examination values per the KTU 2024 Assistive Devices course:

- **Maximum Speed:** Typically **1.5 m/s to 2.5 m/s** (≈ 5.4 to 9 km/h) for indoor use.
- **Operating Range per Charge:** **15 to 30 km** (depends on battery capacity).
- **Obstacle Detection Range:** **0.1 m to 5 m** (ultrasonic), **up to 30 m** (LiDAR).
- **Payload Capacity:** **100 kg to 150 kg** (standard clinical rating).
- **Battery Type:** **24 V / 36 V / 48 V DC**, Sealed Lead Acid (SLA) or Li-ion.
- **Response Latency:** **< 100 ms** for safety-critical obstacle response.
- **Degrees of Autonomy:** **Level 1 (assisted) to Level 5 (full autonomy)** as per smart wheelchair autonomy scale.

## 1.6 Classification of Smart Wheelchairs (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

1. **Sensor-Augmented Wheelchairs** — Add sensors to a standard powered chair (lowest cost).
2. **Robotic Wheelchairs** — Full autonomy with mapping and SLAM.
3. **Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) Wheelchairs** — Controlled via EEG signals.
4. **Voice-Controlled Wheelchairs** — NLP-driven command interface.
5. **Eye-Gaze / Head-Tracking Wheelchairs** — Camera-based intent detection.
6. **Shared-Control Wheelchairs** — Hybrid: user gives high-level intent, SW handles low-level motion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3: SMART WHEELCHAIRS — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Sub-Systems (Deep Dive)

### 2.1.1 Perception Subsystem — "The Eyes"

| Sensor | Operating Principle | Range | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ultrasonic (HC-SR04)** | Time-of-Flight of 40 kHz acoustic pulse | 0.02 – 4 m | Cheap, weather-immune | Specular reflection, low angular resolution |
| **Infrared (Sharp GP2Y0A21)** | Triangulation of reflected IR | 0.1 – 0.8 m | Compact, fast | Susceptible to ambient light |
| **LiDAR (RPLIDAR A1)** | Rotating ToF laser (905 nm) | 0.15 – 12 m, 360° | High resolution (1°) | Cost, eye-safety |
| **RGB-D Camera (Kinect / Intel RealSense)** | Structured IR pattern + stereo | 0.5 – 3.5 m | Rich semantic data | Sunlight interference |
| **IMU (MPU6050 / MPU9250)** | MEMS accelerometer + gyroscope | ±2000°/s, ±16 g | Inertial frame tracking | Drift over time |
| **Wheel Encoders (Optical)** | Counts pulses per wheel revolution | Infinite (cumulative) | Excellent odometry | Slip on wet floors |

### 2.1.2 Cognition Subsystem — "The Brain"

The cognition layer typically runs one of the following algorithms:

- **Bug Algorithm** (simplest): Move toward goal; on collision, follow obstacle boundary.
- **A\* / D\* Lite** (graph search): Optimal path in known or partially known maps.
- **VFH (Vector Field Histogram):** Real-time obstacle avoidance in dense environments.
- **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping):** Online map building with unknown environment.
- **Deep Learning CNN-based perception:** Object detection (YOLO) for semantic scene understanding.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 emphasis:** The most commonly examined algorithms are **Bug-2** and **VFH** due to their analytical tractability in board examinations.

### 2.1.3 Action Subsystem — "The Muscles"

Two BLDC motors (one per drive wheel) coupled through **differential drive kinematics**. The motor torque, RPM, and gear ratio must satisfy the wheelchair's dynamic load.

## 2.2 Differential Drive Kinematics (Core Theory)

For a two-wheeled differential-drive robot with wheel separation $2L$ (i.e., $L$ is the half-axle length) and wheel radius $r$:

**Left wheel angular velocity:** $\omega_L$ (rad/s)
**Right wheel angular velocity:** $\omega_R$ (rad/s)

**Linear velocity of the wheelchair center:**

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

**Angular velocity of the wheelchair (yaw rate):**

$$\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

**Turning radius (instantaneous):**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special case:** If $\omega_R = -\omega_L$, then $v = 0$ and the wheelchair **rotates in place** about its own center. This is a *zero-radius turn*, critical for indoor maneuverability and a frequent KTU board exam question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Linear velocity of wheelchair center | m/s |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Yaw (turn) rate of wheelchair | rad/s |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{L(\omega_R+\omega_L)}{\omega_R-\omega_L}$ | Instantaneous turning radius | m |
| 4 | $T_{motor} = \dfrac{F_{load} \cdot r}{N \cdot \eta_g}$ | Required motor torque (per wheel) | N·m |
| 5 | $F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 6 | $F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Grade (slope) force | N |
| 7 | $F_{total} = F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{acc}$ | Total tractive force | N |
| 8 | $P_{motor} = F_{total} \cdot v$ | Required mechanical power | W |
| 9 | $E_{batt} = V \cdot C \cdot DoD$ | Usable battery energy | Wh |
| 10 | $R_{range} = \dfrac{E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{sys}}{P_{motor}}$ | Theoretical operating range | m |
| 11 | $t_{res} = \dfrac{d_{safe}}{v_{max}}$ | Safety response time budget | s |
| 12 | $f_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{t_{res}}$ | Required minimum sensor scan rate | Hz |
| 13 | $s = r \cdot \theta_{wheel}$ | Linear displacement per wheel rotation | m |
| 14 | $N_{rev} = \dfrac{d_{dist}}{2\pi r}$ | Total wheel revolutions for distance $d_{dist}$ | rev |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation clash warning:** $r$ denotes **wheel radius** in kinematics and **rolling resistance coefficient** in dynamics. Always state which is meant contextually. Also, $\theta$ is the **slope angle** in grade force, NOT a kinematic angle.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Smart wheelchairs serve as a **canonical testbed for embodied AI** because they:
- Constrain computation to embedded (low-power) hardware.
- Demand *safety-critical* real-time response (< 100 ms).
- Require *multimodal sensor fusion* in a small form factor.
- Bridge *rehabilitation engineering*, *human factors*, and *autonomous robotics*.

Industries applying this architecture: Toyota, Whill (Japan), DEKA/LUCI, and academic prototypes like MIT's *Wheelesley* and the University of Pennsylvania's *Smart Wheelchair* (PerMMA).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3: SMART WHEELCHAIRS — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Differential Drive Kinematic Equations

We derive the three foundational equations from rigid-body rolling-without-slipping constraints.

**Given:**
- Two coaxial drive wheels separated by distance $2L$ (so $L$ is half the axle length).
- Wheel radius $r$ (m).
- Wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ (rad/s).

**Rolling constraint (no slip):** Each wheel's contact point has zero velocity relative to the ground. Hence the linear velocity at each wheel contact equals $r \cdot \omega$ in the wheel's forward direction.

**Step 1 — Linear velocity of each wheel contact point:**

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L \quad \text{(left wheel contact, forward direction)}$$

$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R \quad \text{(right wheel contact, forward direction)}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity of wheelchair geometric center:**
The center lies midway between the two wheels. By the principle of linear superposition of rigid body motion:

$$v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r \cdot \omega_L + r \cdot \omega_R}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify to standard form:**

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

**Step 4 — Yaw rate derivation:**
The wheelchair undergoes an instantaneous rotation about the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. For a differential drive, the ICR lies on the line connecting the two wheel contacts, at a perpendicular distance $R$ from the center.

By the kinematic relation that the velocity at any point on a rigid body equals (yaw rate) $\times$ (perpendicular distance from ICR):

$$v = \omega \cdot R \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{v}{\omega}$$

The ICR distance from each wheel:
- From left wheel: $R + L$
- From right wheel: $R - L$

Equating the wheel velocities to their respective tangential speeds about the ICR:

$$v_L = \omega(R - L) = r \cdot \omega_L$$

$$v_R = \omega(R + L) = r \cdot \omega_R$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the two equations** to eliminate $R$:

$$r(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \omega \cdot [(R+L) - (R-L)] = 2L \cdot \omega$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\omega$:**

$$\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

This is the **yaw rate equation** (Equation 2 in the formula sheet).

**Step 7 — Substituting** $\omega$ and $v$ into $R = v / \omega$:

$$R = \frac{\frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)} = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

This completes the **turning radius equation** (Equation 3 in the formula sheet). ∎

## 3.2 Numerical Example 1 — Turning Radius Computation

**Problem:** A smart wheelchair has wheel radius $r = 0.15$ m and half-axle length $L = 0.25$ m. The right wheel spins at $5$ rad/s and the left wheel at $3$ rad/s. Find the linear velocity, yaw rate, and turning radius.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Linear velocity** (using Equation 1):

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.15}{2}(5 + 3) = 0.075 \times 8 = 0.6 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Yaw rate** (using Equation 2):

$$\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.15}{2 \times 0.25}(5 - 3) = 0.3 \times 2 = 0.6 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Turning radius** (using Equation 3):

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.6}{0.6} = 1.0 \; \text{m}$$

**Result:** The wheelchair moves forward at **0.6 m/s**, rotates at **0.6 rad/s** (≈ 34.4°/s), and follows a circular path of radius **1.0 m**.

## 3.3 Numerical Example 2 — Battery Range Estimation

**Problem:** A smart wheelchair has:
- Total mass (user + chair): $m = 110$ kg
- Rolling resistance coefficient: $\mu_r = 0.015$ (rubber tire on smooth floor)
- Grade: $\theta = 0°$ (flat)
- Speed: $v = 1.5$ m/s
- Battery: 24 V, 35 Ah, Lead-acid, Depth of Discharge (DoD) = 0.7
- System efficiency: $\eta_{sys} = 0.80$ (includes motor, gearing, controller)

**Estimate the operating range.**

**Step 1 — Calculate rolling resistance force** (Equation 5):

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g = 0.015 \times 110 \times 9.81 = 16.19 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Grade force is zero** (flat surface):

$$F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(0°) = 0 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration force** (assume steady-state, $a = 0$):

$$F_{acc} = m \cdot a = 0 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Total tractive force** (Equation 7):

$$F_{total} = 16.19 + 0 + 0 = 16.19 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Required mechanical power** (Equation 8):

$$P_{motor} = F_{total} \cdot v = 16.19 \times 1.5 = 24.29 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Usable battery energy** (Equation 9):

$$E_{batt} = V \cdot C \cdot DoD = 24 \times 35 \times 0.7 = 588 \; \text{Wh}$$

**Step 7 — Effective energy at the wheel** (apply system efficiency):

$$E_{eff} = E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{sys} = 588 \times 0.80 = 470.4 \; \text{Wh}$$

**Step 8 — Operating time:**

$$t = \frac{E_{eff}}{P_{motor}} = \frac{470.4}{24.29} = 19.37 \; \text{hours}$$

**Step 9 — Operating range:**

$$R_{range} = v \cdot t = 1.5 \times 19.37 = 29.05 \; \text{km}$$

**Result:** The wheelchair has a theoretical range of **≈ 29 km** on a single charge under flat, steady-state conditions. (Real-world factors: stop-and-go, slopes, sensor power, and payload variations typically reduce this by 30–50%.)

## 3.4 Numerical Example 3 — Safety Response Time

**Problem:** The smart wheelchair has a maximum speed $v_{max} = 1.5$ m/s. The minimum safe stopping distance (including reaction + braking) is $d_{safe} = 0.45$ m. What is the response time budget, and what is the required sensor scan rate?

**Step 1 — Response time budget** (Equation 11):

$$t_{res} = \frac{d_{safe}}{v_{max}} = \frac{0.45}{1.5} = 0.30 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Required sensor scan rate** (Equation 12):

$$f_{scan} = \frac{1}{t_{res}} = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.33 \; \text{Hz}$$

**Result:** Sensors must scan at **≥ 3.33 Hz** to ensure the controller has time to detect and react. In practice, the **Nyquist criterion** requires **2 ×** the safety rate, so engineering design uses **≥ 10 Hz** for obstacle scanning. This is why ultrasonic sensors (typically 20–40 Hz update rate) are sufficient, but very slow sensors (e.g., 1 Hz GPS indoors) are inadequate.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Suitability

| Sensor | Range | Scan Rate | Cost | Indoor? | Outdoor? | KTU Recommended? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ultrasonic | Short | 20 Hz | Very Low | Yes | Limited | **Yes (entry-level)** |
| IR | Very Short | 30 Hz | Low | Yes | Poor | Yes (close range) |
| LiDAR | Long | 10 Hz | Medium-High | Yes | Yes | **Yes (premium)** |
| RGB-D | Medium | 30 Hz | Medium | Yes | Limited | Yes (perception) |
| IMU | N/A | 100+ Hz | Low | Yes | Yes | **Yes (always)** |
| GPS | Global | 1–10 Hz | Low | No | Yes | No (indoor) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 3: SMART WHEELCHAIRS — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a smart wheelchair, mapping all perception-cognition-action interactions:

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeU[USER] -->|Voice/BCI/EMG/Joystick| nodeA
    nodeA[INPUT INTERFACE MODULE] -->|Intent Signal| nodeC
    nodeB[ULTRASONIC ARRAY 6 SENSORS] -->|Distance m| nodeC
    nodeD[IR PROXIMITY 4 SENSORS] -->|Distance m| nodeC
    nodeE[LIDAR 2D SCANNER] -->|Point Cloud| nodeC
    nodeF[IMU 9 AXIS] -->|Orientation Accel Gyro| nodeC
    nodeG[WHEEL ENCODERS 2X] -->|Pulse Count| nodeC
    nodeC[CENTRAL CONTROLLER ARM CORTEX M4] -->|PWM Command L| nodeH
    nodeC -->|PWM Command R| nodeI
    nodeC -->|Haptic Audio LED| nodeJ
    nodeH[BLDC MOTOR DRIVER LEFT] --> nodeK[LEFT WHEEL BLDC 24V]
    nodeI[BLDC MOTOR DRIVER RIGHT] --> nodeL[RIGHT WHEEL BLDC 24V]
    nodeM[24V LI-ION BATTERY PACK] --> nodeH
    nodeM --> nodeI
    nodeM --> nodeC
    nodeK -->|Encoder Feedback| nodeG
    nodeL -->|Encoder Feedback| nodeG
    nodeK --> nodeN[DIFFERENTIAL DRIVE BASE]
    nodeL --> nodeN
    nodeN -->|Motion Output| nodeU
```

## 4.2 Perception-Cognition-Action Layered View

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP1[PERCEPTION LAYER] -->|Raw Data| nodeP2[SENSOR FUSION KALMAN FILTER]
    nodeP2 -->|Filtered State Estimate| nodeP3[COGNITION LAYER]
    nodeP3 -->|SLAM BUG VFH A STAR| nodeP4[PATH PLANNER]
    nodeP4 -->|Velocity Commands| nodeP5[ACTION LAYER]
    nodeP5 -->|PWM Motor Drive| nodeP6[MOTOR ACTUATORS]
    nodeP6 -->|Wheel Rotation| nodeP7[PHYSICAL WHEELCHAIR MOTION]
    nodeP7 -->|New Sensor Reading| nodeP1
```

## 4.3 Decision-Making Flow — Obstacle Encountered

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeS1[START: User Issues Move Command] --> nodeS2[Read All Sensor Distances]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3{Front Distance Safe?}
    nodeS3 -->|No d less than 0.3m| nodeS4[STOP and Alert User]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[Run VFH Avoidance Algorithm]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[Compute New Heading]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[Generate Differential Wheel Velocities]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[Send PWM to Motor Drivers]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9[Continue Path]
    nodeS3 -->|Yes d greater than 0.3m| nodeS10[Compute Straight Velocity]
    nodeS10 --> nodeS8
    nodeS8 --> nodeS11[END: Motor Actuation]
```

## 4.4 Input Modality Selection Tree

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeI1[USER ASSESSMENT] --> nodeI2{Upper Limb Function}
    nodeI2 -->|Adequate| nodeI3[Standard Joystick Interface]
    nodeI2 -->|Limited Hand| nodeI4{Head Control}
    nodeI2 -->|No Hand No Head| nodeI5{BCI EEG Cap}
    nodeI4 -->|Yes| nodeI6[Head Tracking Camera]
    nodeI4 -->|No| nodeI7[Sip and Puff Switch]
    nodeI5 -->|EEG Decoded| nodeI8[Intent Classifier Neural Net]
    nodeI5 -->|Not Feasible| nodeI9[Eye Gaze Tracker]
    nodeI3 --> nodeI10[Drive Command Output]
    nodeI6 --> nodeI10
    nodeI7 --> nodeI10
    nodeI8 --> nodeI10
    nodeI9 --> nodeI10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeS1`, `nodeP3`) to comply with the compilation safeguard. All node labels use plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 3: SMART WHEELCHAIRS — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Smart Wheelchair. List any four perception sensors used in it.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Smart Wheelchair** is a powered wheelchair integrated with sensors, microcontrollers, and intelligent algorithms to provide semi-autonomous or autonomous mobility to users with physical or cognitive impairments.

Four perception sensors used:

1. **Ultrasonic sensor** — Time-of-flight distance measurement.
2. **Infrared (IR) proximity sensor** — Short-range triangulation.
3. **LiDAR** — 2D/3D laser scanning for mapping.
4. **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** — Orientation and acceleration sensing.

(Any other valid sensor such as RGB-D camera, GPS, wheel encoders, or EEG electrodes are also acceptable.)

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is differential drive in the context of a smart wheelchair? Why is it preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

**Differential drive** is a two-wheeled locomotion mechanism where independent left and right wheel speeds control both linear motion and steering.

**Why preferred:**
- **Zero-radius turning** — The chair can rotate in place when wheels spin in opposite directions, critical for tight indoor spaces.
- **Simple mechanical design** — No steering linkage required.
- **Easy kinematic modeling** — Closed-form equations for $v$, $\omega$, and $R$.
- **Cost-effective** — Only two drive motors required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question B1 **or** Question B2.

---

### **Question B1. (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the three-layer architecture (Perception–Cognition–Action) of a smart wheelchair with a neat block diagram. Discuss any two navigation algorithms used.**

**Model Answer:**

**Three-Layer Architecture:**

1. **Perception Layer:** Acquires environmental and user-state data through sensors:
   - *Exteroceptive*: Ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR, RGB-D camera.
   - *Proprioceptive*: Wheel encoders, IMU.
   - *User-intent*: Joystick, voice, BCI.

2. **Cognition Layer:** Runs on a microcontroller / embedded SoC (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4, Raspberry Pi, Jetson Nano). Performs:
   - Sensor fusion (Kalman / complementary filter).
   - Mapping (occupancy grid).
   - Path planning and obstacle avoidance.
   - Intent decoding (for BCI/voice).

3. **Action Layer:** Executes the cognition layer's commands:
   - Motor driver + BLDC motors.
   - Braking system (electromagnetic or regenerative).
   - Haptic / audio feedback to user.

**Two Navigation Algorithms:**

**1. Bug-2 Algorithm:**
- Move toward the goal along a straight "m-line."
- On obstacle contact, follow the obstacle boundary.
- Leave the boundary at the point closest to the goal and resume m-line motion.
- **Advantage:** Simple, no map required. **Limitation:** Long, non-optimal paths.

**2. Vector Field Histogram (VFH):**
- Builds a 2D occupancy grid of obstacles around the robot.
- Reduces to a 1D polar histogram.
- Selects a steering direction through histogram valleys.
- **Advantage:** Real-time, smooth in dense environments. **Limitation:** Local minima possible.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A smart wheelchair has wheel radius $r = 0.12$ m and half-axle length $L = 0.30$ m. The left wheel rotates at $4$ rad/s and the right wheel at $7$ rad/s. Compute the linear velocity, yaw rate, and turning radius of the wheelchair. Comment on the motion.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Linear velocity:**

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.12}{2}(7 + 4) = 0.06 \times 11 = 0.66 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Yaw rate:**

$$\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.12}{2 \times 0.30}(7 - 4) = 0.2 \times 3 = 0.6 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Turning radius:**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.66}{0.6} = 1.1 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**
Since $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the wheelchair **turns left** (in the conventional coordinate frame, counter-clockwise is positive yaw). The center of the wheelchair follows a circular path of radius **1.1 m** to the left of the ICR, moving forward at **0.66 m/s** and rotating at **0.6 rad/s** (≈ 34.4°/s). The turn completes in $2\pi / 0.6 ≈ 10.47$ s.

---

### **Question B2. (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — *Alternative Choice*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the various input modalities available for operating a smart wheelchair. Compare them on parameters of invasiveness, accuracy, and user fatigue.**

**Model Answer:**

| Modality | Sensor Type | Invasiveness | Accuracy | User Fatigue | Latency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Joystick** | Mechanical | **None** | High | Low | < 50 ms |
| **Voice** | Microphone | None | Moderate (90–95%) | Low | 200–500 ms |
| **Head Tracking** | Camera / IMU | None | High | Medium | 100 ms |
| **Sip-and-Puff** | Pneumatic switch | None | High (binary) | Low | 50 ms |
| **Eye Gaze** | IR camera | None | High | **High** (eye strain) | 100 ms |
| **EMG** | Surface electrodes | **Mild** (skin contact) | Moderate | Low | 50–100 ms |
| **BCI / EEG** | Scalp electrodes | Moderate | Moderate (70–85%) | Medium | 300–1000 ms |
| **EOG** | Surface electrodes | Mild | High (for direction) | Medium | 100 ms |

**Key trade-off:**
- **Low invasiveness (joystick, head tracking)** → Best for users with residual motor function.
- **Voice/BCI** → Used when residual motor function is severely limited.
- **Hybrid shared control** → Combines low-level autonomy (collision avoidance) with high-level user intent → most clinically successful in KTU practical evaluations.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A powered wheelchair system has the following specifications: total mass (user + chair) = 120 kg, rolling resistance coefficient = 0.02, maximum slope = 5°, motor-controller-gear efficiency = 0.75, battery = 48 V, 40 Ah Lithium-ion with DoD = 0.8, and operating speed = 1.2 m/s. Estimate the operating range of the wheelchair under worst-case conditions (including 5° slope).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rolling resistance force:**

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g = 0.02 \times 120 \times 9.81 = 23.54 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Grade force (5° slope):**

$$F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(5°) = 120 \times 9.81 \times 0.0872 = 102.61 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration force (steady-state, $a = 0$):**

$$F_{acc} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Total tractive force (worst case):**

$$F_{total} = 23.54 + 102.61 + 0 = 126.15 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Required mechanical power:**

$$P_{motor} = F_{total} \cdot v = 126.15 \times 1.2 = 151.38 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Usable battery energy:**

$$E_{batt} = V \cdot C \cdot DoD = 48 \times 40 \times 0.8 = 1536 \; \text{Wh}$$

**Step 7 — Effective energy at the wheel:**

$$E_{eff} = E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{sys} = 1536 \times 0.75 = 1152 \; \text{Wh}$$

**Step 8 — Operating time:**

$$t = \frac{E_{eff}}{P_{motor}} = \frac{1152}{151.38} = 7.61 \; \text{hours}$$

**Step 9 — Operating range:**

$$R_{range} = v \cdot t = 1.2 \times 7.61 = 9.13 \; \text{km}$$

**Result:** The wheelchair has a worst-case range of **≈ 9.13 km** on a 5° slope. On flat terrain, the range would be substantially higher (≈ 49 km by recalculation), highlighting the dramatic effect of grade on battery life.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert slope from degrees to radians in `sin(θ)`; using `sin(5°)` as `sin(5)` is a classic error.
> 2. **Confusing $r$ (wheel radius) with $r$ (rolling coefficient):** Always declare units (m for radius, dimensionless for coefficient).
> 3. **Forgetting Depth of Discharge (DoD):** Lead-acid batteries are typically 50% DoD; using 100% DoD overestimates range.
> 4. **Neglecting System Efficiency:** Mechanical + electrical efficiency is typically 0.70–0.85; ignoring it gives 20–30% overestimate.
> 5. **Sign Convention for Yaw:** Clockwise vs. counter-clockwise distinction is critical for turning direction comment.
> 6. **Using $L$ as full axle length vs. half:** The formula uses $L$ as **half-axle length**. Using full axle length halves the calculated $\omega$, a 50% error.
> 7. **Skipping Block Diagrams in Long Answers:** Even if a question asks for derivation, the KTU 2024 scheme awards **1–2 marks** specifically for a labeled block diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Smart Wheelchairs**

- **Definition:** Powered wheelchair + perception + cognition + action layers.
- **Three-Layer Architecture:** Perception (sensors) → Cognition (controller + algorithms) → Action (motors + HMI).
- **Differential Drive:** Two independently driven wheels; enables **zero-radius turn** when wheels counter-rotate.
- **Core Kinematic Equations:**
  - $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$
  - $\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$
  - $R = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$
- **Battery Range Formula:** $R_{range} = \dfrac{V \cdot C \cdot DoD \cdot \eta_{sys}}{F_{total} \cdot v}$ (with units carefully matched).
- **Key Sensors:** Ultrasonic (entry), LiDAR (premium), IMU (always), Encoder (always).
- **Algorithms:** Bug-2 (simple), VFH (real-time dense), A\* (optimal known map), SLAM (unknown map).
- **Input Modalities:** Joystick → Head → Voice → EMG → EOG → EEG (in increasing invasiveness/intent-decoding difficulty).
- **Critical Performance Metrics:** Speed **1.5–2.5 m/s**, Range **15–30 km**, Response time **< 100 ms**, Payload **100–150 kg**, Battery **24/36/48 V DC**.
- **Safety Rule:** Sensor scan rate ≥ 10 Hz for indoor mobility; > 2 × safety rate (Nyquist).
- **Autonomy Levels:** Level 1 (assisted) → Level 5 (full autonomy). Most clinical devices operate at **Level 2–3 (shared control)**.
- **Clinical Indications:** ALS, spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy, MS, locked-in syndrome, severe arthritis.
- **Industrial Examples:** Whill Model Ci, LUCI, Permobility, Toyota's BCI wheelchair prototypes, MIT Wheelesley.
- **KTU Board Exam Hot Topics (high probability):** Differential drive kinematics derivation, Bug-2 vs. VFH comparison, sensor selection rationale, battery range calculation, input modality trade-off table.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low-Technology Aids for Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Low-Technology Aids for Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Low-Technology Aids for Manipulation** are non-powered, mechanically simple assistive devices designed to compensate for impaired upper-limb function (limited reach, weak grasp, restricted joint range of motion, or loss of fine motor control) by extending, stabilizing, or substituting the user's natural hand–arm biomechanical chain. They are typically fabricated from low-cost materials (thermoplastics, aluminum, wood, polymer foams) and require no external power source (battery, mains electricity, or compressed air).

In the KTU 2024 scheme terminology, these aids fall under the **Activities of Daily Living (ADL) Assistive Technology** classification and are considered the **first-line intervention** before powered or robotic alternatives are prescribed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Third Hand Concept":**
> Imagine trying to button your shirt while one arm is in a sling. Your functional arm is overwhelmed, weak, or has limited range. A *low-technology manipulation aid* acts like a **mechanical "third hand" or an "extension of intent"** — it captures the small motion or force you CAN produce and translates (amplifies/redirects) it into a useful grasp, reach, or hold. It does not have a brain; it has a **lever**, a **handle**, and a **grip interface**.

### 1.3 Why "Low-Technology"?

The taxonomy distinguishes devices by:

| Tier | Power Source | Cost Range | Examples |
|------|--------------|------------|----------|
| **Low-Tech** | None (purely mechanical) | ₹200 – ₹5,000 | Reachers, built-up handles, universal cuffs |
| **Mid-Tech** | Battery / simple electronics | ₹5,000 – ₹50,000 | Electric feeders, page turners |
| **High-Tech** | Robotics, AI, sensors | ₹50,000+ | Robotic arms, brain–computer interfaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The clinical and engineering rationale for "low-tech first" is governed by the **Hierarchy of Assistive Technology (HAT) principle** — always try the simplest, cheapest, most reliable solution first. A ₹300 universal cuff can replace a ₹3,00,000 robotic arm for many simple grasp tasks.

### 1.4 Functional Classification of Manipulation Aids

Manipulation aids assist in **four primary functional deficits**:

1. **Reach Deficit** → Solved by *Extenders / Reachers*
2. **Grasp Deficit** → Solved by *Universal Cuffs, Built-up Handles, Orthoses*
3. **Hold/Stabilization Deficit** → Solved by *Mounting systems, Dycem mats, Suction cups*
4. **Fine Motor Deficit** → Solved by *Built-up grips, Weighted utensils, Key holders*

### 1.5 Target User Population

- Individuals with **cerebral palsy, arthritis, stroke (hemiplegia), spinal cord injury (C5–C7), amputations, muscular dystrophy, Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, and geriatric frailty**.
- The **prescription** is typically done by an Occupational Therapist (OT) after a functional assessment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Biomechanical Foundation: Why These Aids Work

A normal human grasp produces a **pinch force** of roughly **20–140 N** (newtons) and a **power grip force** of **200–300 N** in healthy adults. In impaired hands, these values drop to **<20 N**, making it impossible to hold everyday objects (a toothbrush needs ~5 N, a cup ~10 N, a book ~15 N).

**Low-tech manipulation aids exploit three classical mechanical principles:**

> **Principle 1: Class 1 Lever (Force Amplification)**
> Used in reachers. A long effort arm (user's hand) overcomes a shorter load arm (jaw tip), trading distance for force.

> **Principle 2: Circumferential / Enclosure Grasp Substitution**
> Used in universal cuffs. The device holds the object inside a pocket, so the user does not need active finger flexion.

> **Principle 3: Friction and Surface Area Enhancement**
> Used in built-up handles and Dycem mats. Increased cross-sectional area and coefficient of friction reduce the grip force required.

### 2.2 Engineering Design Specifications (KTU 2024 Standard)

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Justification |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------|
| Handle diameter (built-up) | **30 mm – 50 mm** | Larger diameter requires less finger flexion torque |
| Reacher length | **600 mm – 900 mm** (standard); up to 1200 mm | Anthropometric reach for wheelchair users |
| Jaw opening at tip | **0 mm – 100 mm** | To accommodate varied object sizes |
| Trigger force | **< 20 N** | Must be operable by impaired hand |
| Material weight (reacher) | **< 400 g** | To avoid fatigue in extended use |
| Coefficient of friction (Dycem) | **μ ≈ 1.5 – 2.0** (static) | High friction prevents slip |
| Customization time | **< 30 min** | Low-tech aids must be quickly fitted |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable under KTU 2024 ESE and are **verifiable from classical biomechanics and lever theory**.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_{grip} \cdot d_{grip} = F_{load} \cdot d_{load}$ | Lever equation for grasp mechanics | N·m |
| 2 | $\tau = F \times r$ | Torque (rotational effect) | N·m |
| 3 | $MA = \dfrac{L_{effort}}{L_{load}}$ | Mechanical Advantage of a lever | dimensionless |
| 4 | $F_{required} = \dfrac{W_{object}}{MA \cdot \eta}$ | Force required after friction losses | N |
| 5 | $\mu_{s} \geq \dfrac{F_{load}}{F_{normal}}$ | Anti-slip friction criterion (static) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight from mass (g = 9.81 m/s²) | N |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure on palm (comfort) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 8 | $I = \dfrac{1}{12} m L^{2}$ | Moment of inertia of rod (reacher swing) | kg·m² |
| 9 | $T = I \cdot \alpha$ | Torque to angular acceleration (Newton's 2nd for rotation) | N·m |
| 10 | $V_{tip} = V_{hand} \cdot MA_{velocity}$ | Velocity ratio of reacher tip | m/s |

**Where:**
- $F$ = Force, $d$ = distance (lever arm), $MA$ = Mechanical Advantage
- $\eta$ = efficiency of mechanism (typically 0.7–0.9 for low-tech)
- $L$ = length, $m$ = mass, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\mu_s$ = static friction coefficient
- $\tau$ = torque, $r$ = radius, $I$ = moment of inertia, $\alpha$ = angular acceleration

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Clinical Engineering:** Prescription fitting requires calculation of grip strength deficit vs. object weight.
- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Custom 3D-printed and thermoformed aids require biomechanical analysis.
- **Industrial Design:** Standardized anthropometric data (Pheasant & Haslegrave) drives handle dimensions.
- **Public Health:** Low-tech aids are scalable in low-resource settings (Kerala's palliative care and community-based rehabilitation programs).

### 2.5 The Three Pillars of Manipulation Aid Design

1. **Affordability** — Must be reproducible with locally available materials (PVC pipe, foam, Velcro, thermoplastic).
2. **Acceptability** — Cosmetically acceptable; user must not feel stigmatized.
3. **Accessibility** — Easily obtainable from district disability offices, primary health centres, or schemes like **ADIP (Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mechanical Advantage of a Typical Reacher (Trigger-Squeeze Type)

A standard reacher consists of:
- A long shaft (length $L$)
- A handle at the user end
- A trigger (effort point) pulled by the index finger
- A jaw at the tip (load point) gripping an object
- A linkage (often a single pull-rod inside the shaft)

**Setup:** Let the distance from handle pivot to trigger be $d_e$ (effort arm) and the distance from handle pivot to jaw closing point be $d_l$ (load arm).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define the lever system.} \quad & \text{Both effort (trigger) and load (jaw) act on the same rigid shaft,} \\
& \text{but the linkage transmits force along the shaft length.} \\
\text{Step 2: Apply rotational equilibrium about the handle pivot.} \quad & \sum \tau_{pivot} = 0 \\
& F_{trigger} \cdot d_e = F_{jaw} \cdot d_l \\
\text{Step 3: Rearrange to find Mechanical Advantage (MA).} \quad & MA = \frac{F_{jaw}}{F_{trigger}} = \frac{d_e}{d_l} \\
\text{Step 4: Insert realistic values.} \quad & \text{If } d_e = 40 \text{ mm and } d_l = 800 \text{ mm:} \\
& MA = \frac{40}{800} = 0.05 \\
\text{Step 5: Interpretation.} \quad & \text{An MA of 0.05 means the reacher amplifies HAND MOTION, not force.} \\
& \text{For every 80 mm the trigger moves, the jaw moves 5 mm.} \\
& \text{The trade-off: small force at the trigger closes a long-distance jaw.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Grip Force Required for a Built-up Handle
