A user with rheumatoid arthritis can produce only $F_{user}$ = **15 N** of pinch force. They need to lift a cup of tea of mass $m$ = **0.25 kg**. The handle diameter is increased from standard $d_1$ = 20 mm to therapeutic $d_2$ = 40 mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute weight of cup.} \quad & W = m \cdot g = 0.25 \times 9.81 = 2.45 \text{ N} \\
\text{Step 2: Apply friction criterion (no-slip).} \quad & \mu_s \cdot F_{grip} \geq W \\
& F_{grip, min} = \frac{W}{\mu_s} \\
\text{Step 3: Assume skin-on-plastic friction coefficient.} \quad & \mu_s \approx 0.4 \\
& F_{grip, min} = \frac{2.45}{0.4} = 6.13 \text{ N} \\
\text{Step 4: Compare to user's available force.} \quad & F_{user} = 15 \text{ N} > F_{grip, min} = 6.13 \text{ N} \\
\text{Step 5: Verdict — task feasible WITHOUT aid.} \quad & \text{But consider torque: } \tau = F \times d \\
\text{Step 6: Compute finger torque with thin handle.} \quad & \tau_1 = 15 \times 0.010 = 0.15 \text{ N·m} \\
\text{Step 7: Compute finger torque with built-up handle.} \quad & \tau_2 = 15 \times 0.020 = 0.30 \text{ N·m} \\
\text{Step 8: Conclusion.} \quad & \text{Built-up handle DOUBLES the torque the same 15 N force produces,} \\
& \text{reducing the muscular effort required per unit of wrist rotation.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Reacher Trigger Force to Pick Up a 1 kg Object

A reacher with $MA$ = 0.05 picks up a 1 kg object. The user must overcome both object weight and jaw friction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Object weight.} \quad & W = 1.0 \times 9.81 = 9.81 \text{ N} \\
\text{Step 2: Jaw friction loss (assume } \mu_{jaw} = 0.3\text{).} \quad & F_{jaw, required} = W + F_{friction} = 9.81 + (0.3 \times 9.81) = 12.75 \text{ N} \\
\text{Step 3: Trigger force required.} \quad & F_{trigger} = \frac{F_{jaw, required}}{MA} = \frac{12.75}{0.05} = 255 \text{ N} \\
\text{Step 4: Reality check.} \quad & \text{255 N is too high for a weak hand! Design issue.} \\
\text{Step 5: Redesign — increase MA by lengthening trigger arm.} \quad & \text{If } d_e = 80 \text{ mm: } MA = \frac{80}{800} = 0.1 \\
& F_{trigger, new} = \frac{12.75}{0.1} = 127.5 \text{ N} \\
\text{Step 6: Final verdict.} \quad & \text{Still high — the reacher is a DISTANCE multiplier, not a force multiplier.} \\
& \text{Clinically, low-tech reachers are for LIGHT objects (< 0.5 kg).}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Low-Tech Aids

| Device | Deficit Addressed | Mechanical Principle | Typical Material | Cost (INR) |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------|------------------|------------|
| **Universal Cuff** | Grasp (C5–C6 SCI, RA) | Enclosure substitution | Leather / neoprene / 3D-printed PLA | ₹150 – ₹600 |
| **Built-up Handle** | Weak pinch, arthritis | Torque amplification (larger $d$) | Foam tubing, thermoplastic | ₹100 – ₹400 |
| **Reacher (trigger)** | Limited reach (wheelchair) | Class 1 lever (distance MA) | Aluminum shaft + plastic jaw | ₹300 – ₹1,500 |
| **Dressing Stick** | Dressing (hemiplegia) | Hook extension | Wood / plastic | ₹100 – ₹300 |
| **Dycem Mat** | Single-hand stabilization | High-friction surface | Polymer gel sheet ($\mu \approx 1.8$) | ₹200 – ₹500 |
| **Sock Aid** | Lower-body dressing | Form-rigidity + pull straps | Flexible plastic + cords | ₹250 – ₹700 |
| **Key Holder** | Pinch grip loss | Lever + extension | Moulded plastic | ₹80 – ₹250 |
| **Long-Handled Sponge** | Bathing (limited ROM) | Extension | Plastic / foam | ₹150 – ₹400 |
| **Adapted Utensils** | Eating (tremor, weak grip) | Weighting + angle offset | Stainless steel + foam | ₹200 – ₹800 |
| **Pencil Grip** | Writing (fine motor) | Circumferential grip | Foam / silicone | ₹20 – ₹100 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Low-Tech Manipulation Aids] --> B[Grasp Aids]
    A --> C[Reach Aids]
    A --> D[Holding Aids]
    A --> E[Fine Motor Aids]

    B --> B1[Universal Cuff]
    B --> B2[Built-up Handle]
    B --> B3[Palmar Cuff]
    B --> B4[Tenodesis Splint]

    C --> C1[Trigger Reacher]
    C --> C2[Dressing Stick]
    C --> C3[Long-Handled Sponge]
    C --> C4[Sock Aid]

    D --> D1[Dycem Non-Slip Mat]
    D --> D2[Suction Cup Mount]
    D --> D3[Book Stand]
    D --> D4[Wheelchair Tray]

    E --> E1[Pencil Grip]
    E --> E2[Weighted Utensil]
    E --> E3[Key Turner]
    E --> E4[Button Hook]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Trigger Reacher (Block Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    UserHand[User Hand] -->|Grip force F_trigger| Handle[Handle Block]
    Handle -->|Pivot rotation| Trigger[Trigger Lever]
    Trigger -->|Pull-rod force F_rod| Shaft[Inner Shaft L=800mm]
    Shaft -->|Force at jaw F_jaw| JawLink[Jaw Linkage]
    JawLink -->|Closes jaws| Object[Target Object]
    Object -->|Weight W| Ground[Ground Reaction]

    JawLink -.->|Friction loss| FrictionLoss[Friction Loss ~30 percent]
    FrictionLoss -.-> Shaft

    style UserHand fill:#c6e0b4
    style Handle fill:#ffe699
    style Trigger fill:#ffd966
    style Shaft fill:#bdd7ee
    style JawLink fill:#f4b183
    style Object fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style FrictionLoss fill:#bfbfbf
```

### 4.3 Universal Cuff — Functional Design Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input[User opens palm] --> Pocket[Cup-shaped pocket receives object]
    Pocket --> Strap[Velcro strap wraps around dorsum of hand]
    Strap --> Hold[Object held in pocket without active grip]
    Hold --> Activity[User performs ADL task - eating, writing, brushing]

    Object[Object - spoon, toothbrush, pen] -.-> Pocket

    style Input fill:#c6e0b4
    style Pocket fill:#f4b183
    style Strap fill:#ffe699
    style Hold fill:#bdd7ee
    style Activity fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style Object fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow for Prescription (KTU Clinical Engineering Viewpoint)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Patient with upper-limb deficit] --> Assess[OT Functional Assessment]
    Assess --> Q1{Can user grasp object?}
    Q1 -->|NO| GraspAid[Prescribe Grasp Aid - Universal Cuff / Built-up Handle]
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2{Can user reach object?}
    Q2 -->|NO| ReachAid[Prescribe Reach Aid - Reacher / Dressing Stick]
    Q2 -->|YES| Q3{Can user stabilize object single-handed?}
    Q3 -->|NO| HoldAid[Prescribe Holding Aid - Dycem / Tray]
    Q3 -->|YES| FineAid[Prescribe Fine Motor Aid - Pencil Grip / Key Turner]

    GraspAid --> Trial[Trial Fit 2-4 weeks]
    ReachAid --> Trial
    HoldAid --> Trial
    FineAid --> Trial
    Trial --> Outcome{Successful?}
    Outcome -->|YES| Discharge[Discharge with training]
    Outcome -->|NO| Reassess[Reassess or escalate to mid/high-tech]

    style Start fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style Assess fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style GraspAid fill:#f4b183
    style ReachAid fill:#f4b183
    style HoldAid fill:#f4b183
    style FineAid fill:#f4b183
    style Discharge fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style Reassess fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
*Define a "low-technology manipulation aid" and list FOUR examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A low-technology manipulation aid is a non-powered, mechanically simple device that assists a person with upper-limb impairment to perform grasp, reach, hold, or fine-motor tasks of daily living. It requires no electricity, battery, or computer control.
*(Definition – 1 mark; Four examples – 2 marks)*

**Four examples:**
1. Universal cuff (grasp aid)
2. Trigger-type reacher (reach aid)
3. Built-up foam handle (grasp aid)
4. Dycem non-slip mat (holding aid)

*(Any 4 valid examples accepted)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]**
*State the principle of a Class 1 lever. How is it used in a "reacher"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Class 1 lever has the fulcrum positioned between the effort and the load. *(1 mark)*
In a reacher, the fulcrum is at the handle pivot, the effort is applied at the trigger, and the load (jaw) is at the far tip. *(1 mark)*
This configuration gives a high *velocity ratio* (the trigger moves a small distance, the jaw closes a small distance, but the hand is far from the object) — i.e., it is a **distance multiplier**, not a force multiplier. *(1 mark)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the biomechanical rationale for using a **built-up handle** in patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Derive the relationship between handle diameter and the torque required for a cylindrical grasp. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the design, working principle, and clinical indications of a **universal cuff**. List THREE different daily-living activities where it can be used. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Built-up Handle Biomechanics (7 marks):**
- In cylindrical grasp, fingers wrap around a handle. The torque produced by finger flexors is $\tau = F \times r$, where $r$ is the handle radius. *(1 mark)*
- Increasing handle diameter $d$ increases $r$, thus increasing $\tau$ for the same muscle force. *(1 mark)*
- However, very large handles (>50 mm) reduce range of finger flexion; hence the therapeutic range is 30–50 mm. *(1 mark)*
- **Derivation:** For a cylinder of mass $m$ and radius $r_h$, the moment the hand must support to lift it is:
  $$\tau = m g \cdot r_h$$
  With a built-up handle of outer radius $r_h'$, the effective moment arm increases:
  $$\tau' = m g \cdot r_h'$$
  Ratio: $\dfrac{\tau'}{\tau} = \dfrac{r_h'}{r_h}$ *(2 marks)*
- Example: Thin pen, $r_h$ = 5 mm; built-up grip, $r_h'$ = 20 mm → torque increases 4×, drastically reducing required flexor force. *(1 mark)*
- Clinical benefit: relieves pain at MCP joints, redistributes load to palm, reduces joint deviation in RA. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Universal Cuff (7 marks):**
- A universal cuff is a strap (leather/neoprene/3D-printed) that wraps around the dorsum of the hand with a small pocket/palmar pocket that holds the object (spoon, pen, toothbrush). *(1 mark)*
- **Working principle:** *Grasp substitution* — the object is held passively inside the pocket; no active finger flexion is required. The hand acts as a "hook". *(2 marks)*
- **Design features:** Adjustable Velcro strap, rigid or semi-rigid pocket, ambidextrous, washable. *(1 mark)*
- **Clinical indications:** C5–C6 spinal cord injury, advanced rheumatoid arthritis, cerebral palsy, stroke with no functional grasp, limb amputation. *(1 mark)*
- **Three daily-living uses:** *(2 marks)*
  1. Eating (holding spoon/fork)
  2. Oral hygiene (holding toothbrush)
  3. Writing (holding pen/pencil)
  *(Bathing brush, comb, makeup applicator are also acceptable)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**
**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **working principle of a trigger-type reacher**. State its mechanical advantage formula and compute the trigger force required to pick up a 1.5 kg object if the trigger effort arm is 50 mm and the jaw distance is 750 mm. Assume jaw friction loss is 30%. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the role of **Dycem mats** and **non-slip surface technology** in manipulation aids. Discuss TWO engineering parameters (friction coefficient, surface area) that govern their performance. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Trigger-Type Reacher (7 marks):**
- **Description:** A long aluminium shaft (~750 mm) with a handle, trigger, and jaw mechanism at the tip. Pulling the trigger closes the jaw through a pull-rod. *(1 mark)*
- **Principle:** Class 1 lever; trade-off between distance and force. The reacher *amplifies hand motion* so the user does not need to bend or stretch. *(1 mark)*
- **MA formula:** $MA = \dfrac{d_e}{d_l} = \dfrac{50}{750} = 0.0667$ *(1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Object weight.} \quad & W = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \text{ N} \\
\text{Step 2: Add friction loss (30 percent).} \quad & F_{jaw, required} = 1.3 \times 14.715 = 19.13 \text{ N} \\
\text{Step 3: Trigger force from MA.} \quad & F_{trigger} = \frac{F_{jaw}}{MA} = \frac{19.13}{0.0667} \approx 287 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for derivation)*

- **Conclusion:** A 287 N trigger force is impractical for weak hands, confirming that low-tech reachers are limited to **light objects (< 0.5 kg)** unless a multi-pulley or compound lever is used. *(1 mark)*
- **(Sketch is to be drawn on answer paper showing handle, trigger, shaft, jaw.)** *(1 mark)*

**(b) Dycem and Non-Slip Technology (7 marks):**
- Dycem is a polymeric gel sheet with a high static friction coefficient ($\mu_s \approx 1.5$–$2.0$) on its textured side. *(1 mark)*
- It is placed under plates, cups, cutting boards, or jars to **stabilize objects on one side** so they can be operated with a single functional hand. *(1 mark)*
- **Engineering parameter 1 — Coefficient of friction ($\mu_s$):** Higher $\mu_s$ means the user needs less grip force. Anti-slip criterion: $\mu_s \geq \dfrac{F_{load}}{F_{normal}}$. Dycem meets this for typical tableware. *(2 marks)*
- **Engineering parameter 2 — Contact surface area ($A$):** Larger contact area distributes normal force, reduces pressure on the object, and prevents tipping. Frictional force $F_{friction} = \mu_s \cdot F_{normal} = \mu_s \cdot P \cdot A$ where $P$ is the local pressure. *(2 marks)*
- **Material limitation:** Dycem loses friction when wet or dusty; reusable but must be kept clean. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students commonly lose marks in this topic for the following reasons:
> 1. **Confusing "Mechanical Advantage" with "Velocity Ratio":** A reacher's MA is < 1; it multiplies *distance*, not *force*. Examiners deduct marks if you claim it "amplifies grip force."
> 2. **Skipping units in the formula sheet:** Always write units (N, N·m, m/s²). A correct number without units is worth half marks in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Forgetting the friction coefficient in built-up handle analysis:** A built-up handle works because of BOTH torque amplification AND the friction interface. Mention both.
> 4. **Listing devices without biomechanical principle:** Naming a "reacher" alone is worth 1 mark; explaining the lever principle is worth 3.
> 5. **Mixing up "low-tech" with "low-cost" only:** A device is "low-tech" because it is *non-powered* and *mechanically simple*, not just because it is cheap. (An electric wheelchair is mid-tech, even if cheap.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Non-powered, mechanically simple devices that substitute, extend, or stabilize the upper limb.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Assistive Technology (HAT):** Low-tech → Mid-tech → High-tech; always start at the lowest level.
- ✅ **Four Functional Categories:** Grasp, Reach, Hold/Stabilize, Fine Motor.
- ✅ **Biomechanical Principles Used:**
  - Class 1 lever (reachers → distance multiplier)
  - Grasp enclosure (universal cuff → passive hold)
  - Torque amplification (built-up handles → larger $d$)
  - Friction enhancement (Dycem → high $\mu_s$)
- ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $MA = d_e / d_l$
  - $\tau = F \times r$
  - $F_{grip, min} = W / \mu_s$
  - $W = mg$ (g = 9.81 m/s²)
- ✅ **Materials:** Aluminum, plastic, thermoplastics, foam, leather, neoprene, polymer gel (Dycem).
- ✅ **Cost Range:** ₹100 – ₹1,500 typically.
- ✅ **Standard Dimensions:** Handle Ø = 30–50 mm; reacher length = 600–900 mm; trigger force < 20 N (ideal).
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Clinical Tie-ins:** ADIP scheme (Government of India), District Disability Offices, Composite Regional Centres (CRCs), and Kerala State Social Security Mission.
- ✅ **Engineering Trade-off:** Reachers amplify distance, NOT force. Built-up handles amplify torque without changing muscle force. Universal cuffs substitute grasp entirely.
- ✅ **Always prescribe by an Occupational Therapist** after a functional capacity evaluation.
- ✅ **Customization tip:** Thermoplastic (e.g., Ezeform, Orfit) can be moulded directly on the patient's hand for a custom cuff in 15–30 minutes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aids for Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aids for Manipulation # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aids for Manipulation** are engineered mechatronic systems that integrate electrical, mechanical, electronic, and computational subsystems to restore, augment, or substitute the human upper-limb manipulative function in individuals with physical impairments. These devices span the spectrum from externally worn **powered orthoses/exoskeletons** and **end-effector-type rehabilitation robots**, to **wheelchair-mounted robotic arms (WMRA)**, **manipulator-style service robots**, and **prosthetic manipulators** (myoelectric/artificial arms). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEBRT745 / Module 4):** > *Special-purpose electromechanical aids* are *task-specific* devices designed to assist or replicate grasping, reaching, lifting, and dexterous object-handling functions for users with neuromotor disabilities (spinal cord injury, stroke, cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, amputation, or advanced multiple sclerosis). ## 1.2 Taxonomy of Manipulation Aids | Class | Example Device | User Population | |---|---|---| | **Powered Wheelchair-Mounted Robotic Arm (WMRA)** | JACO, iARM, Manus ARM | Tetraplegia, high-level SCI | | **End-Effector Rehabilitation Robot** | InMotion ARM, Armeo Power | Stroke, post-stroke hemiparesis | | **Exoskeleton-Type Manipulator** | MyoPro, FES-based Handmaster | Weak but voluntary muscle activity | | **Prosthetic Manipulator** | DEKA LUKE Arm, bebionic hand | Transradial/transhumeral amputees | | **Service/Social Manipulation Robot** | PR2, Care-O-bot, HSR | Severe disability, elderly | | **Body-Powered / Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) Hybrid** | Handmaster NMS, BionicGlove | Incomplete SCI | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mechanical Butler" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a *mechanical butler* bolted to your wheelchair whose arm is long, jointed, and quiet. You say *"bring me the cup"* (or signal it through a sip-and-puff / chin joystick). The butler *plans the path* through the cluttered table, *reaches* without knocking the lamp, *grasps* the cup firmly but not too tight, and *lifts* it back to your mouth. **A special-purpose electromechanical aid for manipulation is exactly that butler**, except its "intelligence" lives in firmware, its "muscles" are DC servo or BLDC motors, and its "skin" is an array of tactile/force sensors. ## 1.4 The Three Functional Loops Every electromechanical manipulation aid must simultaneously execute **three closed feedback loops**: 1. **Perception Loop** — Sensors (encoders, IMU, tactile/force, vision) sense the world and the joint states. 2. **Cognition/Planning Loop** — A controller (PID, impedance, admittance, ML-based) converts intent → trajectory. 3. **Action Loop** — Actuators (DC motor + harmonic-drive gearhead) move the link/joint, producing force on the environment. > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Mnemonic — PCA:** *Perceive → Compute → Act.* If you forget this in the exam, draw the three-loop block diagram and you instantly recover **3–5 marks**. ## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Frequently Tested) - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Number of independent joint motions. Human arm ≈ **7 DOF**. - **Payload (W):** Mass the manipulator can lift, typically **0.5 kg – 5 kg** for assistive devices. - **Reach (R):** Maximum tip distance, typically **600 mm – 900 mm** for wheelchair-mounted arms. - **Repeatability:** Position accuracy on repeated motion, typically **± 0.5 mm – ± 5 mm**. - **Speed:** End-effector linear velocity, typically **0.1 m/s – 0.5 m/s**. - **Grasp Force:** Pinch force, typically **10 N – 100 N**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of a Manipulation Aid A manipulation aid can be decomposed into **five hierarchical layers**: ### Layer 1 — Mechanical Structure (Skeleton) - **Links** (rigid members), **Joints** (revolute R, prismatic P), **End-Effector** (gripper, hook, suction cup). - Materials: aluminium alloy, carbon-fibre composite, 3D-printed ABS/PLA. - Configuration notations: **RRR** (3-revolute planar), **RPR** (spherical-like), **RRPRR** (typical 6-DOF arm like Jaco). ### Layer 2 — Actuator Subsystem (Muscles) - **DC Servo Motor** + **Harmonic Drive** (zero-backlash gearhead, ratio 50:1 – 160:1). - **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** for high-torque, low-maintenance applications. - **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** for safe human-robot interaction — embeds a spring in the drivetrain. - **Pneumatic Muscle Actuator (PMA / McKibben)** for soft, compliant motion. ### Layer 3 — Sensor Subsystem (Nervous System) | Sensor Type | Physical Quantity | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Optical / Magnetic Encoder** | Joint angle | Position control | | **Potentiometer** | Joint angle | Low-cost angle read-out | | **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** | Orientation, acceleration | Wrist/tool orientation | | **Tactile Sensor Array** (FSR, capacitive) | Pressure distribution | Grasp stability | | **Force/Torque (F/T) Sensor** | Wrist force | Impedance control, safety | | **EMG (Electromyography) Electrode** | Muscle electrical activity | Myoelectric control | | **Vision Camera (RGB-D, e.g., Kinect)** | Object pose | Vision-based grasping | | **EEG (Electroencephalography)** | Brain potential | BCI-driven manipulation | ### Layer 4 — Control Subsystem (Brain) - **Joint-Space Control:** Each motor commanded independently. - **Task-Space / Cartesian Control:** End-effector follows a straight line in 3-D. - **Impedance / Admittance Control:** Controls the *relationship* between force and motion — **mandatory** for safe physical human-robot interaction (pHRI). - **Shared Control:** User gives high-level intent (e.g., "grasp the cup"), robot autonomously plans the low-level motion. - **Myoelectric Pattern Recognition:** Machine learning (LDA, SVM, CNN) classifies EMG signals into discrete grasp modes. ### Layer 5 — Human-Machine Interface (HMI) - **Joystick** (chin, sip-and-puff, head-array). - **Switch / Button Array** (single-switch scanning for very severe impairment). - **Voice Recognition** (e.g., "Jaco, kitchen, cup"). - **Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)** (P300, SSVEP). - **Tongue-Drive Interface (TDI)** (magnetic tracer on tongue). ## 2.2 Why Each Layer Matters — Engineering Trade-offs - **Compliance vs. Stiffness:** A rigid industrial arm is precise but unsafe near a human face. Assistive arms need *intrinsic compliance* → motivates SEAs and impedance control. - **DOF vs. Control Complexity:** Adding DOF increases dexterity (reach the cup from any angle) but explodes the **inverse kinematics** problem. - **Power Autonomy:** Battery capacity limits field use. Typical wheelchair arm runs on **24 V DC, 5–10 Ah**, giving **2 – 4 hours** of intermittent use. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMULA BOX** — Memorise this table for the 14-mark KTU problems. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Degrees of Freedom (Kutzbach / Grübler)** | $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4$ | $n$ = links, $j_i$ = joints with $i$ DOF | | 2 | **Forward Kinematics (Denavit–Hartenberg)** | ${}^{0}T_{n} = {}^{0}A_{1} \cdot {}^{1}A_{2} \cdot \ldots \cdot {}^{n-1}A_{n}$ | $T$ = 4×4 homogeneous transform | | 3 | **DH Link Transform** | ${}^{i-1}A_{i} = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i c\alpha_i & s\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i c\theta_i \\ s\theta_i & c\theta_i c\alpha_i & -c\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i s\theta_i \\ 0 & s\alpha_i & c\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\theta$ = angle, $d$ = offset, $a$ = length, $\alpha$ = twist | | 4 | **Inverse Jacobian** | $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\,\dot{x}$ | $q$ = joint vector, $x$ = task-space vector | | 5 | **Static Force Relation** | $\tau = J^{T}(q)\,F$ | $\tau$ = joint torque, $F$ = end-effector force | | 6 | **Singularity Condition** | $\det(J(q)) = 0$ | Indicates loss of DOF in task space | | 7 | **Workspace Volume (approx. sphere)** | $V \approx \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$ | $R$ = max reach | | 8 | **Grip Force for Stable Grasp** | $F_g \geq \dfrac{W}{2\mu}$ | $W$ = object weight, $\mu$ = friction coefficient | | 9 | **Mechanical Advantage of Gripper** | $\text{MA} = \dfrac{F_{\text{out}}}{F_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{2 L_1}{L_2}$ | $L_1$ = link proximal, $L_2$ = link distal | | 10 | **DC Motor Torque** | $\tau_m = K_t \, I_a$ | $K_t$ = torque constant [Nm/A], $I_a$ = armature current [A] | | 11 | **Back-EMF** | $e = K_e \,\omega$ | $K_e$ = back-EMF constant [V·s/rad] | | 12 | **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) Force** | $F_{\text{SEA}} = k_{\text{spring}}\,(q_m - q_j)$ | $k$ = spring stiffness, $q_m$ = motor pos, $q_j$ = joint pos | | 13 | **Impedance Control Law** | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_d) = F_{\text{ext}}$ | $M$ inertia, $B$ damping, $K$ stiffness, $x_d$ desired | | 14 | **PID Control (Position)** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $e$ = error | | 15 | **Battery Energy / Endurance** | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad ; \quad t_{\text{runtime}} = \dfrac{\text{Capacity [Ah]}}{I_{\text{draw}}[\text{A}]}$ | $V$ = voltage | | 16 | **Myoelectric Classifier Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Confusion-matrix based | | 17 | **Bode Stability Margin** | $\text{GM} = \dfrac{1}{\vert P(j\omega_{pc})\vert} \quad ; \quad \text{PM} = 180^{\circ} + \angle P(j\omega_{gc})$ | Gain/Phase margin | | 18 | **Hooke's Joint Velocity Ratio** | $\dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^{2}\alpha \cos^{2}\phi}$ | $\alpha$ = joint angle, $\phi$ = driving angle | > [!WARNING] > When writing formulas in markdown tables, **never use the bare pipe `|x|`** for absolute value. Use `$\vert x\vert$` or `$\mid x\mid$` to prevent the markdown table from breaking. This is a *frequently lost mark* in the KTU answer script when the formula spills out of the column. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Rehabilitation clinics** use InMotion ARM for stroke patients — 3-D planar reaching tasks to recover motor coordination. - **Assistive living** deployments of JACO arms on powered wheelchairs enable independent drinking/eating. - **Industrial rehabilitation robotics** (e.g., Armeo Power) generates quantitative recovery metrics for insurance reimbursement. - **Disaster response robots** (e.g., JacoKinova) emerged from the same manipulator platforms. - **Space robotics** (Canadarm, Dextre) share kinematic heritage with wheelchair-mounted arms. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Kutzbach / Grübler DOF Formula for a Planar 3R Manipulator > This is a *guaranteed 7-mark derivation* in any KTU Module-4 question on manipulation aids. **Problem:** A planar manipulator has **3 revolute joints** in series. The base is fixed. Compute the system DOF. **Step 1 — Identify the structural parameters.** Number of links (excluding ground): $n = 3$ Number of revolute joints: $j_1 = 3$ (each contributes 1 DOF, hence multiplier 5 in 3-D form). **Step 2 — Apply the planar (2-D) Kutzbach equation.** For a planar mechanism, each rigid link in 2-D has 3 DOF, each revolute joint constrains 2 DOF, hence: $$\text{DOF}_{2D} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{DOF}_{2D} &= 3(3-1) - 2(3) \\ &= 3 \cdot 2 - 6 \\ &= 6 - 6 \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Reconcile with physical intuition.** The result 0 is *counter-intuitive*; this is because the planar Kutzbach counts **independent loops**, not actuators. The corrected formula for an **open kinematic chain** is: $$\text{DOF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$$ where $f_i$ is the DOF of joint $i$. For a 3R planar arm: $$\text{DOF} = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion.** A 3R planar manipulator has 3 DOF (position in $x, y$ + orientation $\phi$). This is the minimum DOF needed to place a gripper anywhere in a 2-D work-plane with a chosen angle — a *workspace-planning benchmark* used in end-effector rehabilitation robots like the InMotion ARM. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Forward Kinematics of a 2R Planar Arm (DH Convention) > High-yield 7-mark derivation. Cover this for "explain forward kinematics with example" type questions. **Setup:** A 2-link planar arm in the $x$–$y$ plane, link lengths $a_1$ and $a_2$, joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ (both measured from the previous link). **DH parameter table:** | $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $a_1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\theta_1$ | | 2 | $a_2$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\theta_2$ | **Step 1 — Write the standard DH matrix for joint 1.** $${}^{0}A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & a_1 c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 & a_1 s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ where $c_1 = \cos\theta_1$ and $s_1 = \sin\theta_1$. **Step 2 — Write the DH matrix for joint 2.** $${}^{1}A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & a_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & a_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the overall transform ${}^{0}T_2 = {}^{0}A_1 \cdot {}^{1}A_2$.** $$\begin{aligned} {}^{0}T_2 &= \begin{bmatrix} c_1 c_2 - s_1 s_2 & -(c_1 s_2 + s_1 c_2) & 0 & a_1 c_1 + a_2(c_1 c_2 - s_1 s_2) \\ s_1 c_2 + c_1 s_2 & c_1 c_2 - s_1 s_2 & 0 & a_1 s_1 + a_2(s_1 c_2 + c_1 s_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify using angle-addition identities.** Using $\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = c_1 c_2 - s_1 s_2$ and $\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = s_1 c_2 + c_1 s_2$: $${}^{0}T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & a_1 c_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & a_1 s_1 + a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5 — Read off the end-effector pose.** $$\boxed{\; x = a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \;}$$ $$\boxed{\; y = a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \;}$$ $$\boxed{\; \phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2 \;}$$ **Step 6 — Verification at $\theta_1 = 0,\, \theta_2 = 0$:** The arm points along the $x$-axis, $x = a_1 + a_2$, $y = 0$ — physically correct (links stretched out in a line). --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Grip Force for a Two-Finger Pinch Grasp **Problem:** A prosthetic/orthotic gripper must lift a cup of mass $m = 0.3$ kg. The friction coefficient between the rubber pad and the cup is $\mu = 0.6$. Find the minimum grip force per finger. **Step 1 — Object weight.** $$W = m g = 0.3 \times 9.81 = 2.943 \;\text{N}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the friction grasp condition.** For a pinch grasp (two fingers on opposite sides), the friction force from each side must balance half the weight: $$\mu F_g \geq \frac{W}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $F_g$.** $$F_g \geq \frac{W}{2\mu} = \frac{2.943}{2 \times 0.6} = 2.4525 \;\text{N}$$ **Step 4 — Apply a safety factor of 2 (typical KTU answer).** $$F_{g,\text{design}} = 2 \times 2.4525 = 4.905 \;\text{N} \;\approx 5 \;\text{N}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to required motor torque via gripper mechanical advantage.** If the finger link ratio is $L_1/L_2 = 4$, then motor force is $F_m = F_g / 4 \approx 1.23$ N, and the required motor torque at a 10 mm finger radius is $\tau = 1.23 \times 0.01 = 0.0123$ Nm — well within the capability of a small DC servo. --- ## 3.4 Derivation — Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) Force Law **Problem:** An SEA has spring stiffness $k = 1000$ N/m. The motor position is $q_m = 0.25$ rad and the joint position is $q_j = 0.20$ rad. Find the interaction force. **Step 1 — Recall the SEA force equation.** $$F_{\text{SEA}} = k \,(q_m - q_j)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$F_{\text{SEA}} = 1000 \times (0.25 - 0.20) = 1000 \times 0.05 = 50 \;\text{N}$$ **Step 3 — Physical interpretation.** The motor has twisted the spring 0.05 rad beyond the joint, storing 0.5 × 1000 × 0.05² = 1.25 J of elastic energy. This stored energy is what the controller uses to *measure* (not just command) the actual force on the human — a critical feature for safe rehabilitation and assistive applications where over-force can cause injury. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Manipulation Aid | User Need | Recommended Aid | Justification | |---|---|---| | C4–C5 SCI, no arm/hand function | **WMRA (JACO)** | Chin/head control + 6-DOF reach + 3-finger grasp | | Stroke survivor with partial recovery | **End-Effector Rehab Robot (InMotion ARM)** | Targets planar reaching for ADL retraining | | Weak but voluntarily innervated muscles | **Myoelectric Orthosis (MyoPro)** | Uses residual EMG to power exoskeleton | | Transradial amputee | **Myoelectric Prosthesis (bebionic, i-Limb)** | Pattern-recognition EMG → multi-grasp hand | | Severe MND / late-stage ALS | **BCI-driven WMRA** | Brain signal as last-resort input | | Mild hand tremor (Parkinson's) | **Active Tremor-Orthosis** | Impedance control damps involuntary motion | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Electromechanical Manipulation Aid ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User Intent Source
Joystick, EMG, Voice, BCI] --> B[Human Machine Interface
HMI Signal Conditioning] B --> C[Controller Unit
PID, Impedance, ML Classifier] C --> D[Motor Driver
H Bridge PWM Amplifier] D --> E[Actuator
DC Servo or BLDC with Harmonic Drive] E --> F[Mechanical Link and Joint
RRR or RRPRR Chain] F --> G[End Effector
Two Finger Gripper or Three Finger Hand] G --> H[Environment
Cup, Book, Phone] F --> I[Encoder and IMU
Joint Position and Orientation] G --> J[Tactile and Force Sensors
FSR Array, F/T Sensor] I --> K[Sensor Fusion Unit
Kalman Filter] J --> K K --> C H --> G ``` > [!NOTE] > The **inner loop** (Encoder → Sensor Fusion → Controller → Motor Driver) is the *position-control* loop operating at 1 kHz. The **outer loop** (Tactile/Force → Sensor Fusion → Controller) is the *impedance / grasp-stability* loop operating at 100 Hz. Both loops are mandatory in any published KTU Module-4 block diagram for **full marks (5 of 5 marks)**. ## 4.2 Kinematic Chain — Wheelchair-Mounted Robotic Arm (JACO-style 6-DOF) ```mermaid flowchart TB Base[Base Mounted on Wheelchair Frame] --> J1[Shoulder Yaw
Revolute Joint 1] J1 --> J2[Shoulder Pitch
Revolute Joint 2] J2 --> J3[Elbow
Revolute Joint 3] J3 --> J4[Forearm Roll
Revolute Joint 4] J4 --> J5[Wrist Pitch
Revolute Joint 5] J5 --> J6[Wrist Yaw
Revolute Joint 6] J6 --> G[Three Finger Underactuated Gripper] G --> O[Target Object
Cup, Spoon, Phone] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **DOF Counting:** J1–J6 = 6 rotational DOF + 1 translational DOF (the wheelchair base can roll forward/back) = **7 effective DOF**, matching the redundancy of the human arm — a key reason JACO is the KTU *canonical* example. ## 4.3 Control Architecture — Three-Loop Impedance Control ```mermaid flowchart TD Xd[Desired Task Space Trajectory
x_d, v_d, a_d] --> Outer[Outer Loop
Impedance Model
M, B, K Parameters] Outer --> TorqueCmd[Computed Joint Torque
tau_cmd] TorqueCmd --> InnerLoop[Inner Loop
Motor Current Controller
PI at 10 kHz] InnerLoop --> Motor[DC or BLDC Motor] Motor --> Spring[SEA Spring
k_spring] Spring --> Joint[Robot Joint
Position q_j] Joint --> Env[Human and Environment] Joint --> FB1[Joint Encoder
q_j] FB1 --> InnerLoop Env --> Fext[External Force
F_ext from Human or Object] Fext --> Spring Spring --> Fmeas[Measured SEA Deflection
F_meas = k x deflection] Fmeas --> Outer ``` > [!TIP] > The **SEA** in the figure is what makes the force measurable. Without it, you only have a *commanded* torque — you cannot know the actual contact force. This is *the* critical reason SEAs dominate rehabilitation/assistive robotics. ## 4.4 Sensor-to-Actuator Data Flow in Myoelectric Prosthesis ```mermaid flowchart LR EMG1[Surface EMG Electrode 1
Biceps Channel] --> Amp1[Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain 1000] EMG2[Surface EMG Electrode 2
Triceps Channel] --> Amp2[Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain 1000] Amp1 --> ADC[16 bit ADC
Sampling 1 kHz] Amp2 --> ADC ADC --> FE[Feature Extraction
MAV, RMS, Waveform Length] FE --> CLF[Classifier
LDA or SVM or CNN] CLF -->|Class Power Grasp| M1[Motor Driver 1
Hand Close] CLF -->|Class Lateral Pinch| M2[Motor Driver 2
Thumb Adduct] CLCNote[Threshold and Decision Delay] -.-> CLF M1 --> Hand[Prosthetic Hand
DC Motor and Tendon Drive] M2 --> Hand Hand --> FS[Tactile Sensor on Fingertip] FS --> Feedback[Vibration or Audio Feedback] Feedback --> User[User] ``` ## 4.5 System-Level Architecture Mapping (Fallback Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Layer1[Layer 1 Mechanical Structure] L1A[Links] L1B[Joints] L1C[End Effector Gripper] end subgraph Layer2[Layer 2 Actuator Subsystem] L2A[DC or BLDC Motor] L2B[Harmonic Drive Gearhead] L2C[Series Elastic Element] end subgraph Layer3[Layer 3 Sensor Subsystem] L3A[Encoders] L3B[IMU] L3C[Force Torque Sensor] L3D[Tactile Array] L3E[EMG or BCI] end subgraph Layer4[Layer 4 Control Subsystem] L4A[Joint PID Loop] L4B[Cartesian Impedance Loop] L4C[Shared Control Planner] end subgraph Layer5[Layer 5 HMI] L5A[Joystick] L5B[Voice Module] L5C[Switch Scanner] end Layer5 --> Layer4 Layer3 --> Layer4 Layer4 --> Layer2 Layer2 --> Layer1 Layer1 --> Layer3 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the term "Degrees of Freedom (DOF)" as applied to a manipulation aid. State the DOF of a standard wheelchair-mounted 6-DOF robotic arm with a 1-DOF wheelchair base.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark) Definition:** Degree of Freedom (DOF) is the number of independent joint motions (rotational or translational) that a manipulator can perform to position and orient its end-effector in 3-D space. - **(1 mark) Calculation:** DOF = 6 (arm) + 1 (wheelchair base translation) = **7 effective DOF**. - **(1 mark) Engineering significance:** This matches the redundancy of the human arm, allowing obstacle avoidance in cluttered home environments — a key functional requirement of an assistive manipulation aid. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between end-effector type and exoskeleton-type rehabilitation robots. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark) End-effector type:** The robot's actuator is attached *only at the hand/wrist*; the patient's arm is moved by the tip. Example: **InMotion ARM**. - **(1 mark) Exoskeleton type:** The robot is worn *alongside* the limb with actuators at each major joint (shoulder, elbow, wrist). Example: **Armeo Power**. - **(1 mark) Comparison:** End-effector robots are mechanically simpler and cheaper but cannot isolate single-joint motion. Exoskeletons provide joint-specific therapy at the cost of higher complexity and donning difficulty. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three functional loops** (Perception, Cognition, Action) of an electromechanical manipulation aid. List the major sensor and actuator types used in each loop. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Derive the **forward kinematics** of a 2R planar manipulator using the **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) convention**. State the end-effector position and orientation as functions of $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks** 1. **(1 mark)** A manipulation aid must execute three concurrent closed loops: Perception (sensors), Cognition (controller), and Action (actuators). [Draw the block diagram from Section 4.1.] 2. **Perception Loop (2 marks):** - **Joint encoders** (optical/magnetic) — sense joint angle $\theta_i$ with resolution typically 12–20 bit. - **IMU** — measures link orientation (roll, pitch, yaw). - **Tactile sensor array** (FSR) — gives distributed pressure on gripper pads; used for grasp stability feedback. - **Force/Torque sensor** at the wrist — measures contact force for impedance control and safety. 3. **Cognition Loop (2 marks):** - **PID controller** for joint position. - **Impedance/admittance controller** for compliant task-space behaviour. - **Shared-control planner** fuses user intent (joystick/EMG) with autonomous obstacle avoidance. 4. **Action Loop (1 mark):** - **DC servo / BLDC motor** + **harmonic drive** + **SEA** for safe, back-drivable, force-controlled motion. 5. **(1 mark)** All three loops must operate at appropriate bandwidths — current loop at 10 kHz, position loop at 1 kHz, impedance/force loop at 100 Hz. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks** 1. **(1 mark)** State the DH parameter table (as in Section 3.2). 2. **(2 marks)** Write the individual link transforms ${}^{0}A_1$ and ${}^{1}A_2$. 3. **(3 marks)** Multiply them, simplify using $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ and $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$. 4. **(1 mark)** State the final results: - $x = a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ - $y = a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ - $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$. --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]** > **(a)** Explain the **architecture and working of a myoelectric prosthetic hand** with a block diagram. List the typical signal-processing and machine-learning techniques used for grasp-mode classification. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A two-finger pinch gripper with rubber pads ($\mu = 0.7$) is to lift a water bottle of mass $m = 0.5$ kg. Calculate the **(i)** minimum grip force per finger, **(ii)** the required motor torque if the finger-lever ratio is $L_1/L_2 = 5$ and the finger acts at a 12 mm radius. Apply a safety factor of 2.5. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks** 1. **(1 mark)** Block diagram from Section 4.4. 2. **(2 marks)** **Working principle:** Surface EMG electrodes (typically Ag/AgCl, placed on flexor and extensor muscle bellies) pick up motor-unit action potentials (~mV amplitude). These are amplified, band-pass filtered (20–500 Hz), notch-filtered for 50 Hz line noise, and digitised at 1 kHz. 3. **(2 marks)** **Feature extraction:** Time-domain features (MAV, RMS, waveform length, zero-crossings) and frequency-domain features (mean/median frequency) form the feature vector. 4. **(1 mark)** **Classification:** Classical ML (LDA, SVM, k-NN) and modern deep learning (CNN, LSTM) classify the feature vector into discrete grasp modes — power grasp, lateral pinch, key pinch, open hand — typically with **> 95 % accuracy** within 100–300 ms. 5. **(1 mark)** **Actuation:** The classified command drives brushless DC motors in the hand, actuating tendon-driven fingers. Tactile feedback (vibration buzzers) closes the loop to the user. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks** **(i) Minimum grip force (3 marks):** - Weight: $W = m g = 0.5 \times 9.81 = 4.905$ N - Friction condition: $F_g \geq \dfrac{W}{2\mu} = \dfrac{4.905}{2 \times 0.7} = 3.504$ N - With safety factor 2.5: $F_{g,\text{design}} = 2.5 \times 3.504 = 8.76$ N **(ii) Required motor torque (4 marks):** - Required finger-tip force = 8.76 N - Mechanical advantage of the lever: $L_1/L_2 = 5$, so motor force $F_m = F_g / 5 = 1.752$ N - Torque at motor shaft: $\tau = F_m \times r = 1.752 \times 0.012 = 0.021$ Nm = **21 mNm** - A small coreless DC motor (e.g., Maxon DCX 16) rated 30 mNm continuous is suitable. **(Conclusion — 1 mark)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Mark Loss #1 — Missing the SEAs:** Many students describe a rehabilitation robot with rigid actuators only. Examiner deducts **1–2 marks** because the safety rationale of compliance is undocumented. Always mention "Series Elastic Actuator" or "intrinsic compliance" for full credit. > 2. **Mark Loss #2 — Wrong DOF Counting:** Counting wheelchair-base translation as 2 DOF (x, y) instead of 1 (forward–backward). Use the corrected formula: $n$ links, sum of joint freedoms for *open chains*. > 3. **Mark Loss #3 — Skipping the Safety Factor in grip-force problems:** A 2× or 2.5× factor is **expected** in any KTU answer; omitting it loses 1 mark. > 4. **Mark Loss #4 — Using `|x|` in formula tables:** The vertical pipe inside a markdown table will break the table layout when you write the answer script in plain-text format. Use `mod(x)` or `abs(x)`. > 5. **Mark Loss #5 — No control-loop bandwidths cited:** When you list the three loops, *always* state the operating frequency: current (10 kHz) → position (1 kHz) → force/visual (100 Hz / 30 Hz). > 6. **Mark Loss #6 — Confusing impedance vs admittance control:** Impedance control = measure position, command force. Admittance control = measure force, command position. This distinction is a 2-mark question by itself. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Definition:** Special-purpose electromechanical manipulation aids are *task-specific* mechatronic systems that restore reach/grasp function. - [ ] **Three Functional Loops:** Perceive → Compute → Act. **(PCA mnemonic)** - [ ] **Five Architectural Layers:** Mechanical → Actuator → Sensor → Control → HMI. - [ ] **DOF Formula (open chain):** $\text{DOF} = \sum f_i$. For JACO arm: 6 + 1 (base) = **7 effective DOF**. - [ ] **DH Convention:** 4 parameters per link — $a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$. The standard 4×4 transformation is in the formula table above. - [ ] **Grip Force Law:** $F_g \geq W / (2\mu)$ — always apply safety factor 2 – 3. - [ ] **SEA Force Law:** $F_{\text{SEA}} = k (q_m - q_j)$ — the spring is *the* force sensor. - [ ] **Impedance Control:** $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_d) = F_{\text{ext}}$ — *the* safe pHRI law. - [ ] **Workspace (approx.):** $V \approx (4/3) \pi R^{3}$ for a spherical reach envelope. - [ ] **Singularity:** $\det J(q) = 0$ — the manipulator loses DOF; never command motion through it. - [ ] **Myoelectric Pipeline:** EMG → Amp → BPF → ADC → Features → Classifier → Motor → Hand → Tactile feedback. - [ ] **Control-Loop Bandwidths:** Current (10 kHz) → Position (1 kHz) → Impedance (100 Hz) → Vision (30 Hz). - [ ] **Canonical Examples:** JACO (WMRA), InMotion ARM (end-effector rehab), MyoPro (exoskeleton), bebionic (prosthesis). - [ ] **Battery Note:** 24 V DC, 5–10 Ah typical, 2–4 hours runtime. - [ ] **Safety Standards:** ISO 13482 (personal care robots), IEC 60601 (medical electrical equipment). - [ ] **Exam tip:** For a 14-mark question, *always* include at least **one block diagram** and **one formula** for ≥ 2 bonus marks in presentation.

topics.title: Electronic Aids to Daily Living
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Electronic Aids to Daily Living # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electronic Aids to Daily Living (EADL)** are assistive devices or integrated systems that enable individuals with physical, sensory, or cognitive disabilities to perform **Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)** — such as operating household appliances, communication, mobility, and environmental control — through **electronic, electrical, or computer-based interfaces**. The system typically replaces manual physical interaction with the environment through an alternative **input modality** matched to the user's residual functional capacity. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, EADLs are classified under **ISO 9999 Class 24** (Assistive products for controlling, carrying, moving, and handling objects) and **Class 15** (Assistive products for domestic life). They are often interchangeably referred to as: - **Environmental Control Systems (ECS)** - **Environmental Control Units (ECU)** - **Assistive Domotics** - **Smart Home Accessibility Layer** > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Board Exams):** > EADL ≠ Assistive Mobility Device (wheelchair). EADLs specifically interface with the **built environment and consumer electronics**, not with personal transport. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a person with **high-level spinal cord injury (tetraplegia)** lying in bed, fully conscious and intelligent, but unable to move arms or legs. Without EADLs, the patient is completely dependent on an attendant for even turning on a fan, switching off the light, answering a phone call, or operating a TV. An **EADL acts like a "Universal Remote Control for the Entire House"** — but instead of pressing physical buttons with fingers, the user triggers commands using a **sip-and-puff switch, eye blink, voice, head movement, or even brain waves (BCI)**. A small processor interprets this biological input and sends the appropriate **infrared (IR), radio-frequency (RF), X-10, or Wi-Fi/Zigbee signal** to the target appliance. > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Summary:** EADL = *Human Will (in any form) + Smart Interface + Home Appliance*. It converts the **user's intent**, expressed in their strongest residual channel, into **machine-executable commands**. ## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics (Memorize for KTU) | Metric | Standard Value / Range | |---|---| | Response Latency | **< 300 ms** (perceived as "real-time") | | Input Power | **5 V DC to 24 V DC** (portable) | | Wireless Range | **10 m to 30 m** (indoor) | | Reliability (MTBF) | **> 10,000 hours** | | Safety Standard | **IEC 60601-1** (medical-grade isolation) | | Wireless Protocols | **IR, RF 433 MHz, X-10, Zigbee, Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz/5 GHz), Bluetooth LE** | | User Access Channels | **1 to 8 (single) / up to 64 (matrix scanner)** | ## 1.4 The "Three-Block" Architecture of Every EADL Regardless of complexity, every EADL is built on three fundamental blocks: 1. **Input Subsystem (Human-Machine Interface / HMI):** Captures the user's intent. 2. **Central Control Unit (CCU / Processor):** Decodes, validates, and routes the command. 3. **Output Subsystem (Actuator / Appliance Interface):** Executes the action on the target device. > [!NOTE] > This three-block architecture is the **most repeated KTU 14-mark question structure** for this topic. Memorize it. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Block ### 2.1.1 Block 1 — Input Subsystem (HMI) The input is the most critical component because **no matter how sophisticated the system, success depends on matching the input device to the user's residual ability**. The hierarchy of clinical preference (from least to most assistive) is: 1. **Direct Mechanical Switch** — Big button, jelly bean, or pillow switch. Used when user retains even **single-site voluntary movement** (e.g., one finger, one toe, head nod). 2. **Pneumatic (Sip-and-Puff)** — User inhales ("sip") or exhales ("puff") through a tube. Pressure transducer converts airflow into electrical signal. Used in **high cervical injury (C1–C3)**. 3. **Voice Recognition (Vocal)** — Speaker-dependent ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition) with **95–99% accuracy** in quiet environments. Limitation: unintelligible speech, dysarthria, noisy room. 4. **Eye-Gaze / EOG** — Electro-oculography measures **corneo-retinal potential (0.4 mV to 1.0 mV)** between electrodes placed on either side of the eye. Horizontal eye movements produce a differential voltage. 5. **EMG (Electromyography)** — Detects **muscle action potentials (50 µV to 5 mV)** from voluntary muscles like frontalis, sternocleidomastoid, or trapezius. 6. **Head/Foot Tracking** — Accelerometer-based tilt sensors or infrared head pointers. 7. **Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)** — EEG-based P300 speller, SSVEP, or motor-imagery. The **most assistive but least reliable** option. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition You Must Quote:** > *"Sip-and-Puff is a pneumatic binary switch where a positive (puff) or negative (sip) pressure of ±5 cm H₂O to ±10 cm H₂O is converted into two distinct logic levels for command selection."* ### 2.1.2 Block 2 — Central Control Unit (CCU) The CCU performs five functions: - **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, filtering (typically **0.1 Hz – 30 Hz bandpass** for EOG, **20 Hz – 500 Hz bandpass** for EMG). - **Feature Extraction:** RMS amplitude, peak detection, threshold comparison. - **Command Mapping:** Maps input event to a specific appliance code (e.g., "Sip → TV ON", "Puff → Channel Up"). - **Safety Interlocks:** Latching, time-outs, confirmation prompts. - **Communication:** IR, RF, X-10 power-line carrier, Zigbee, Wi-Fi. ### 2.1.3 Block 3 — Output Subsystem (Appliance Interface) Two main strategies: - **Direct Wired Switching:** Relay modules (5 V coil, **230 V/10 A contact rating**) wired in parallel with the appliance's manual switch. - **Wireless Emulation:** IR blaster (for TV/AC), RF transmitter (for X-10), or smart-plug (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth). ## 2.2 Classification of EADL Systems | Class | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Class I — Simple** | 1–2 functions, single switch | Fan controller, single-light switch | | **Class II — Standard** | 4–16 functions, scan-mode selection | Possum Compact, DU-IT | | **Class III — Sophisticated** | 32+ functions, computer-based | GEWA Prog, TASH SmartHome, Possum Primo | | **Class IV — BCI-based** | Brain-controlled, no muscle required | g.tec intendiX, P300 speller | ## 2.3 Power-Line Communication — X-10 Protocol (High-Yield) The **X-10 protocol** transmits control signals over the existing 240 V (India) / 110 V (US) AC household wiring, eliminating the need for extra wiring. Each appliance module has a **House Code (A–P)** and a **Unit Code (1–16)**, giving **16 × 16 = 256** unique addressable devices. > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently asks: *"How does X-10 overcome the issue of wiring in old buildings for EADL deployment?"* — Answer: Power-line carrier modulation; no new wiring needed. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | $V_{CR}$ | Corneo-retinal standing potential | $0.4 \le V_{CR} \le 1.0$ | mV | | $V_{EOG}$ | EOG voltage for eye deflection | $V_{EOG} = V_{CR} \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | mV | | $V_{EMG}$ | RMS EMG amplitude | $V_{EMG,RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}$ | mV | | $f_c$ | EMG bandpass center | $20 \le f_c \le 500$ | Hz | | $f_c$ | EOG bandpass center | $0.1 \le f_c \le 30$ | Hz | | $P_{sip}$ | Sip pressure threshold | $P_{sip} \le -5$ | cm H₂O | | $P_{puff}$ | Puff pressure threshold | $P_{puff} \ge +5$ | cm H₂O | | $SNR$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | | $N_{addr}$ | X-10 addressable devices | $N_{addr} = H \times U = 16 \times 16$ | devices | | $R_{relay}$ | Relay contact rating | $R_{relay} = 230\,V,\, 10\,A$ | — | | $\tau$ | System response latency | $\tau \le 300$ | ms | | $A_{batt}$ | Battery autonomy (portable) | $A_{batt} \ge 24$ | hours | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Absolute Value Convention in Tables:** When a row contains an absolute value (e.g., threshold magnitudes), use the LaTeX macro $\vert x \vert$. Never use the bare pipe character `$\vert x \vert$` in markdown table cells, as it breaks the column delimiter. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility EADL engineering sits at the intersection of **rehabilitation engineering, biomedical instrumentation, IoT, and universal design**. Modern applications extend beyond homes to: - **Smart Hospitals:** Bedside EADL for long-term ICU patients. - **Smart Classrooms:** Dyslexia and motor-disability support. - **Aging-in-Place:** Fall detection + voice-controlled environment for elderly. - **Accessible Workspaces:** Section 508 compliance (US), RPwD Act 2016 (India). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — EOG Signal Generation (Eye-Movement → Voltage) The eye behaves like a **rotating dipole**: the cornea is electropositive (+), the retina is electronegative (–). This standing potential $V_{CR}$ (corneo-retinal) is roughly **0.4 mV to 1.0 mV**. When the eye rotates horizontally by an angle $\theta$ from the primary position (looking straight ahead), the **projection of the dipole onto the horizontal axis** changes. The voltage picked up by two skin electrodes placed at the left and right outer canthi is: $$ V_{EOG} = V_{CR} \cdot \cos(\theta) $$ **Derivation Steps:** *Step 1:* Treat the eye as a dipole of magnitude $V_{CR}$ with the positive pole (cornea) at the center of the eye. *Step 2:* When the gaze is at primary position ($\theta = 0^\circ$), the dipole vector is aligned with the inter-electrode axis. The measured differential voltage is maximum: $$ V_{EOG}(\theta=0) = V_{CR} \cdot \cos(0) = V_{CR} $$ *Step 3:* When the gaze rotates to the side by $\theta = 90^\circ$ (extreme lateral), the dipole becomes perpendicular to the electrodes. The differential voltage drops to zero: $$ V_{EOG}(\theta=90^\circ) = V_{CR} \cdot \cos(90^\circ) = 0 $$ *Step 4:* For small-to-moderate gaze angles typically used in EADL (≈ ±30°), the small-angle approximation is valid: $$ \cos(\theta) \approx 1 - \dfrac{\theta^2}{2} $$ *Step 5:* Substituting $\theta = 30^\circ = \pi/6$ rad: $$ \cos(\pi/6) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866 $$ *Step 6:* Hence, with $V_{CR} = 0.7\,mV$ (typical value), a 30° lateral gaze produces: $$ V_{EOG} = 0.7 \times 0.866 = 0.606\,mV $$ > [!NOTE] > This 0.6 mV signal is amplified with a gain of **~1000** using an **instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620, INA128)** to obtain a usable 0.6 V swing for digital logic. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RMS Amplitude of an EMG Signal EMG signals are stochastic (random) in nature. To extract a robust control feature, the **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value over a sliding window of width $T$ is computed: $$ V_{EMG,RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt} $$ **Derivation Steps:** *Step 1:* Let the sampled EMG signal be $v[n]$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$, with sampling period $T_s$ and window width $T = N \cdot T_s$. *Step 2:* The discrete-time approximation replaces the integral with a summation: $$ V_{EMG,RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v^2[n]} $$ *Step 3:* Numerical Example: Suppose $N = 200$ samples and the squared values sum to $\sum v^2[n] = 5{,}000\,\mu V^2$. $$ V_{EMG,RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{5{,}000}{200}} = \sqrt{25} = 5\,\mu V_{RMS} $$ *Step 4:* This RMS value is compared to a **threshold $V_{th}$** (typically 2× the resting noise RMS) using a **Schmitt trigger** to produce a clean binary command. $$ \text{Command} = \begin{cases} \text{ON} & \text{if } V_{EMG,RMS} > V_{th} \\ \text{OFF} & \text{if } V_{EMG,RMS} < V_{th} \end{cases} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — X-10 Address Space Calculation The X-10 protocol uses two independent 4-bit codes transmitted as **binary bit patterns synchronized to the AC zero-crossing**. *Step 1:* House Code alphabet = 16 letters (A to P), so 4 bits per letter. *Step 2:* Unit Code numbers = 16 (1 to 16), so 4 bits per unit. *Step 3:* Total independent addressable appliances in one house: $$ N_{addr} = 2^4 \times 2^4 = 2^8 = 256\, \text{devices} $$ *Step 4:* Real-world usability — only **128 unique codes** are typically used (16 houses × 8 units in the legacy spec, or 16 × 16 in the modern spec). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Input Modalities | Modality | Residual Ability Required | Latency (ms) | Reliability | Cost (INR) | Best Suited For | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Mechanical Switch | 1 voluntary site | **< 50** | ★★★★★ | 500–2,000 | Most patients (1st choice) | | Sip-and-Puff | Mouth breathing | **100–200** | ★★★★ | 5,000–15,000 | High cervical injury | | Voice (ASR) | Intelligible speech | **300–800** | ★★★ | 10,000–25,000 | Speech-capable | | EOG | Horizontal eye movement | **50–150** | ★★★★ | 15,000–30,000 | Locked-in syndrome | | EMG | 1 voluntary muscle | **50–100** | ★★★★ | 12,000–25,000 | Amputee, partial SCI | | Head Tracking | Neck movement | **30–80** | ★★★★ | 8,000–20,000 | Good head control | | BCI (EEG) | None (mental task) | **500–2000** | ★★ | 200,000+ | Total LIS, ALS late-stage | > [!WARNING] > In KTU valuation, students often confuse **EOG** (eye-movement) with **EOG of the eye** vs **EROG (electro-retinogram)**. Always clarify: EADL uses **EOG** (corneal-retinal dipole), not ERG. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level EADL Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[USER INTENT
Brain/Muscle/Voice] --> B[INPUT HMI
Switch or Sip-Puff or EOG or EMG or BCI] B --> C[SIGNAL CONDITIONING
Amplifier plus Filter] C --> D[CONTROL UNIT MCU
Command Mapping and Logic] D --> E[COMMUNICATION LAYER
IR or RF or X10 or WiFi or Zigbee] E --> F[APPLIANCE INTERFACE
Relay or Smart Plug] F --> G[TARGET APPLIANCE
Light or TV or Fan or Bed or Phone] D --> H[FEEDBACK
LCD Buzzer Haptic LED] H --> A ``` ## 4.2 Input Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ASSESS USER] --> B{Can user
speak clearly?} B -- YES --> C[Use VOICE ASR EADL] B -- NO --> D{Can user move
one muscle group?} D -- YES --> E[Use EMG or
MECHANICAL SWITCH] D -- NO --> F{Can user
control eyes?} F -- YES --> G[Use EOG or
EYE GAZE Tracker] F -- NO --> H{Can user
control breath?} H -- YES --> I[Use SIP AND PUFF] H -- NO --> J[Use BCI EEG
P300 or SSVEP] ``` ## 4.3 Detailed Functional Architecture of a Sip-and-Puff EADL ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT A1[Mouthpiece Tube] --> A2[Pressure Transducer
MPX5010 or MPX5700] A2 --> A3[Differential Amplifier
Gain 100x] end subgraph CONTROL A3 --> A4[Bandpass Filter
0.1 Hz to 10 Hz] A4 --> A5[Comparator with Hysteresis
Threshold plus 5 cm H2O] A5 --> A6[Microcontroller
Arduino or PIC or STM32] A6 --> A7[Scan Counter
Scans 8 appliances] end subgraph OUTPUT A7 --> A8[RF Transmitter 433 MHz
or X10 Power Line] A8 --> A9[Receiver Module
at Appliance] A9 --> A10[Relay Driver
MOC3021 plus Triac] A10 --> A11[230 V AC Appliance] end A6 --> A12[Audible Feedback Buzzer
and LCD Display] ``` ## 4.4 System-Level Block Diagram of a Modern BCI-EADL ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Scalp EEG Electrodes
16 or 32 channel] --> S2[EEG Amplifier
g.tec g.USBamp] S2 --> S3[Feature Extraction
Common Spatial Patterns] S3 --> S4[Classifier
LDA or SVM or CNN] S4 --> S5[Command Decoder
P300 Speller or SSVEP] S5 --> S6[Home Automation Hub
WiFi or Zigbee Gateway] S6 --> S7[Smart Bulb] S6 --> S8[Smart Lock] S6 --> S9[Smart AC] S6 --> S10[Smart TV] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Electronic Aids to Daily Living (EADL). List any four daily activities that can be controlled by an EADL system.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** EADL refers to a class of assistive devices or integrated systems that enable individuals with physical disabilities to perform activities of daily living by providing an alternative electronic interface to operate household appliances, communication devices, and environmental controls. **Four activities (1.5 marks — 0.4 each, round to 1.5):** 1. Switching ON/OFF lights and fans. 2. Operating television, set-top box, or music system. 3. Making/receiving phone calls. 4. Opening/closing doors, windows, or curtains. 5. Adjusting bed position or HVAC. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Writing "EADL helps disabled people walk" — **Deduct 1 mark**. EADL does not aid walking; it aids **environmental control**. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Explain the principle of Sip-and-Puff switch. State two applications.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Principle (2 marks):** A sip-and-puff is a pneumatic binary switch. The user inhales (sip, negative pressure) or exhales (puff, positive pressure) through a tube. A pressure transducer converts the pressure change into an electrical signal. A pressure of $\vert P \vert \ge 5\,cm\,H_2O$ is typically used as the switching threshold. Two states (sip and puff) act as binary logic levels, which are interpreted by a microcontroller for command selection. **Two applications (1 mark — 0.5 each):** 1. Environmental control (lights, TV, fan) for high-level spinal cord injury patients. 2. Operation of motorized wheelchair. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Confusing sip-and-puff with **ventilator breath-trigger** — although physically similar, sip-and-puff is **voluntary breath control**, not ventilator-driven. Examiner deducts 1 mark if you mix them. ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Set A]` **(a) [7 marks]** *Draw and explain the block diagram of an Electronic Aid to Daily Living system. Describe the function of each block in detail.* **(b) [7 marks]** *Explain the working of a Sip-and-Puff based EADL system with a neat diagram. Mention the pressure thresholds and clinical population served.* --- **Solution to (a):** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** A standard EADL consists of three main blocks: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INPUT SUBSYSTEM
HMI] --> B[CENTRAL CONTROL UNIT
Processor] B --> C[OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM
Appliance Interface] ``` **Input Subsystem (1.5 marks):** Captures the user's intent through one of the following modalities: mechanical switch, sip-and-puff, voice (ASR), EOG, EMG, or BCI. The selection depends on the user's residual functional ability. The output is a low-amplitude electrical signal (e.g., EOG ≈ 0.6 mV, EMG ≈ 50 µV to 5 mV). **Central Control Unit (1.5 marks):** Performs signal conditioning (amplification, filtering in the band **0.1 Hz to 30 Hz** for EOG, **20 Hz to 500 Hz** for EMG), feature extraction, threshold comparison, and command mapping. Implemented using a microcontroller (e.g., Arduino, STM32) or a PC-based processor. **Output Subsystem (1 mark):** Communicates the command to the target appliance using **IR, RF (433 MHz), X-10 power-line, Zigbee, or Wi-Fi**. Includes relay drivers and safety interlocks. --- **Solution to (b):** **Working Principle (3 marks):** The user places their lips on the mouthpiece and either **sips** (inhales) or **puffs** (exhales). A pressure transducer (e.g., **MPX5010**) converts this pressure differential into an analog voltage. The signal is amplified, filtered, and fed to a microcontroller. The two pressure states — **Sip ($P < -5\,cm\,H_2O$)** and **Puff ($P > +5\,cm\,H_2O$)** — generate two distinct logic levels, enabling binary control. **Scanning Mechanism (2 marks):** In a multi-appliance setup, a scan cycle sequentially highlights each appliance on a display (LED or LCD). A puff selects the highlighted appliance, a sip advances to the next one, and a long-puff confirms the final command. This is the **two-switch scan selection** paradigm. **Clinical Population (1 mark):** Used by individuals with **high cervical spinal cord injury (C1–C3 tetraplegia)**, advanced ALS, or any condition with **loss of voluntary limb control but preserved oral and respiratory function**. **Diagram (1 mark):** Must show mouthpiece → transducer → amplifier → comparator → microcontroller → RF transmitter → receiver → relay → appliance. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Set B]` **(a) [7 marks]** *Compare the various input modalities used in EADL systems. Discuss the suitability of each for different disability populations.* **(b) [7 marks]** *Explain the X-10 protocol used in EADL. Derive the number of addressable devices supported.* --- **Solution to (a):** **Introduction (1 mark):** EADL input modalities are selected based on the user's residual functional ability. The choice is a **clinical matching problem**, not a technical preference. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Modality | Residual Ability | Latency | Reliability | Population | |---|---|---|---|---| | Mechanical Switch | 1 voluntary movement site | < 50 ms | Very High | Stroke, low-level SCI | | Sip-and-Puff | Mouth breathing | 100–200 ms | High | C1–C3 tetraplegia | | Voice (ASR) | Clear speech | 300–800 ms | Medium | MS, low-level SCI | | EOG | Horizontal eye movement | 50–150 ms | High | Locked-in syndrome, ALS | | EMG | 1 voluntary muscle | 50–100 ms | High | Amputees, partial SCI | | Head Tracker | Neck control | 30–80 ms | High | CP, MD | | BCI (EEG) | None (imagination only) | 500–2000 ms | Low | Late-stage ALS, LIS | **Suitability Discussion (2 marks):** The clinical matching follows the **"least assistive sufficient"** principle — start with the simplest modality (mechanical switch) and escalate only when the patient cannot operate it. This maximizes reliability, minimizes cost, and reduces cognitive load. --- **Solution to (b):** **X-10 Protocol Overview (3 marks):** X-10 is a **power-line carrier communication protocol** for home automation. Control signals are superimposed on the 230 V / 50 Hz (India) or 110 V / 60 Hz (US) AC mains. Transmission is synchronized to the **zero-crossing** of the AC waveform: a **1 ms burst at 120 kHz** at the zero-crossing represents a binary '1', and the absence of burst represents a binary '0'. **Addressing Scheme (2 marks):** Each X-10 device has two addresses: - **House Code (A to P)** — 4 bits, 16 possible houses. - **Unit Code (1 to 16)** — 4 bits, 16 possible units per house. **Derivation of Addressable Devices (2 marks):** $$ N_{addr} = 2^{4} \times 2^{4} = 2^{8} = 256\,\text{devices} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students answer **16 × 16 = 256** correctly but lose 1 mark by not specifying the units (devices) or by confusing it with the number of houses. Always write: *"256 unique appliances can be addressed across 16 house codes."* ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on EADL Questions:** > 1. Confusing **EADL** with **assistive mobility devices** (wheelchairs, prosthetics). > 2. Forgetting to specify **units** for physical quantities (e.g., cm H₂O for sip-and-puff, mV for EOG). > 3. Missing the **three-block architecture** in block diagram questions — partial marking depends on labeling all three. > 4. Writing voice-based EADL solutions without mentioning the **ambient noise limitation** (specificity = marks). > 5. For X-10, stating "16 devices" instead of **"256 devices"** because they forget to multiply the 4-bit × 4-bit. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Definition:** EADL = any electronic system that enables a person with disability to control their environment without physical contact. - ✅ **Three-block architecture:** Input HMI → Central Control Unit → Output Appliance Interface. - ✅ **Hierarchy of input modalities:** Mechanical switch → Sip-and-Puff → EMG → EOG → Voice → BCI. - ✅ **Sip-and-Puff threshold:** $\vert P \vert \ge 5\,cm\,H_2O$. - ✅ **EOG voltage:** $V_{EOG} = V_{CR} \cdot \cos(\theta)$ with $V_{CR} \approx 0.4$ to $1.0\,mV$. - ✅ **EMG bandpass:** $20\,Hz$ to $500\,Hz$; typical amplitude $50\,\mu V$ to $5\,mV$. - ✅ **X-10 address space:** $2^4 \times 2^4 = 256$ appliances. - ✅ **System response latency:** $\tau \le 300\,ms$ for real-time feel. - ✅ **Safety standard:** IEC 60601-1 for medical-grade isolation. - ✅ **Indian legislation:** Rights of Persons with Disabilities (RPwD) Act, 2016 mandates accessible environments. - ✅ **Clinical first choice:** Always start with a **mechanical switch** unless contraindicated. - ✅ **Last resort for total paralysis:** BCI (P300 or SSVEP), with the trade-off of longer latency and lower reliability. - ✅ **Wireless protocols to remember:** IR (line-of-sight), RF 433 MHz (long range, no security), Zigbee (mesh, low-power), Wi-Fi (high bandwidth, infrastructure-heavy).

topics.title: Robotic Aids - Principles of Assistive Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Robotic Aids - Principles of Assistive Robots # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Assistive Robot** is a class of robotic system engineered to augment, supplement, or restore functional capabilities of individuals with physical, sensory, or cognitive impairments, thereby enabling them to perform Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) with greater independence, safety, and dignity. The discipline that governs their design is known as **Assistive Robotics**, a sub-domain of **Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)** and **Rehabilitation Engineering**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** > An assistive robot is an electro-mechanical device, controlled by a combination of sensors, actuators, and intelligent control algorithms, which provides physical, sensory, or cognitive assistance to a user. The design philosophy is governed by the **ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** framework, mapping robot functions to user needs, environmental context, and quality-of-life outcomes. ### 1.1.1 Foundational Principles (The Five Pillars) The KTU 2024 Scheme identifies **five cardinal principles** that govern every assistive robot: | # | Principle | KTU Definition | |---|-----------|----------------| | 1 | **Safety (P0 Priority)** | Fail-safe operation; no harm to user under any fault condition. | | 2 | **Adaptability** | Ability to adapt to varying user abilities, body morphology, and environments. | | 3 | **Intuitive HRI** | Low cognitive load; operation via natural intent (voice, gesture, EMG/EEG). | | 4 | **Affordability & Accessibility** | Cost-effective for end-users in low/middle income contexts. | | 5 | **Autonomy Gradient** | Continuum from fully-teleoperated to fully-autonomous, with **shared control** as the core paradigm. | > [!NOTE] > **Autonomy Gradient (Critical for KTU Exams):** > The **Autonomy Level** of an assistive robot ranges from **Level 0 (Full Manual)** → **Level 5 (Full Autonomy)**, analogous to the SAE levels of autonomous driving. Most clinical assistive robots operate at **Level 2–3 (Shared Control)**. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy ### 1.2.1 The "Smart Crutch + Butler" Analogy > [!TIP] > **Imagine a regular wooden crutch.** It is passive — it just holds you up. Now imagine a **crutch that can sense your weight, predict where you want to step, gently push you in that direction, catch you if you stumble, and call for help if you fall.** That is an **assistive robot.** In the same way, an assistive robot is a *prosthesis of capability* — not replacing the human, but **amplifying residual function**, much like: - A **smart wheelchair** is to a manual wheelchair what a **calculator** is to a slide rule. - A **robotic exoskeleton** is to a paralyzed leg what **glasses** are to a near-sighted eye. - A **feeding manipulator arm** is to a human hand what a **fork-extension** is to a stiff shoulder. ### 1.2.2 Engineering Taxonomical Snapshot ``` ASSISTIVE ROBOTS │ ┌──────────┬───────────┼───────────┬──────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ Mobility Manipulator Rehabilitation Sensory Cognitive Aids Arms / Therapy Substitutes Aids │ │ │ │ │ Smart WC Feeding Arm Exoskeleton Nav-Cane Autism Walker Wheelchair Stroke Rehab Guide Dog Therapy Gait-Train Pick&Place Orthotic Braille Companion Exoskel Bed-Mover Prosthetic Reader Reminder ``` > [!NOTE] > **Standard Clinical Metric:** Clinical efficacy of an assistive robot is measured using **FIM (Functional Independence Measure)** scores, ranging from **18 (total dependence)** to **126 (complete independence)**. A clinically meaningful improvement is defined as **≥ 18 FIM points**. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards | Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Significance | |-------------------|--------|------------------------|--------------| | Standard Human Walking Cadence | $f_{step}$ | **1.5 – 2.0 Hz** | Sets bandwidth of gait-assist robots. | | Human Hand Grip Force | $F_{grip}$ | **20 – 300 N** | Defines payload spec for feeding/manipulator arms. | | Body Weight Support Limit (Overground Exoskeleton) | $BWS$ | **0 – 100 %** | Percent of body weight offloaded. | | Safe Human-Robot Contact Force (Head) | $F_{safe}$ | **< 65 N** | Per ISO/TS 15066 (Collaborative Robots). | | Safe Contact Force (Limbs) | $F_{limb}$ | **< 150 N** | Per ISO/TS 15066. | | Maximum Safe Contact Pressure | $P_{max}$ | **< 50 N/cm²** | Quasi-static contact threshold. | | Standard Latency for Tele-Operation | $t_{latency}$ | **< 200 ms** | Beyond this, HRI degrades sharply. | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomical Block Architecture of an Assistive Robot Every assistive robot, irrespective of end-application, can be decomposed into the **Perception–Decision–Action** triad: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Triad Mapping to KTU Syllabus:** > 1. **Perception Layer** = Sensor Suite (proprioceptive + exteroceptive). > 2. **Decision Layer** = Embedded Controller / AI (CNN, RNN, FSM). > 3. **Action Layer** = Actuator + Mechanical Structure (rigid / soft). ### 2.1.1 The Perception Layer (Sensors) | Sensor Type | Physical Quantity Measured | Example in Assistive Robots | |-------------|---------------------------|----------------------------| | **Encoders (Proprioceptive)** | Joint angle / angular velocity | Resolver on exoskeleton knee joint | | **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** | Linear acceleration, orientation | Smart cane fall detection, gait phase | | **Force/Torque (F/T) Sensor** | End-effector force | Wheelchair joystick, feeding arm spoon | | **EMG (Electromyography)** | Muscle electrical activity | Intent decoding for prosthetic hand | | **EEG (Electroencephalography)** | Brain electrical activity | BCI-driven wheelchair | | **Vision (RGB-D / LiDAR)** | Scene geometry | Obstacle avoidance, object recognition | | **Tactile / E-Skin** | Distributed pressure | Safe HRI, grasp stability | | **Ultrasonic / IR** | Proximity | Wheelchair bumper, blind-navigation aid | ### 2.1.2 The Decision Layer (Control Architectures) The control architecture of an assistive robot is hierarchical: ``` HIGH-LEVEL → Task Planner (AI / FSM / Behavior Tree) MID-LEVEL → Motion Planner (Trajectory Generation, RRT*) LOW-LEVEL → Joint Servo (PID / Impedance / Admittance) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Impedance vs. Admittance Control (High-Yield KTU Topic):** > - **Impedance Control:** Robot measures **position error** and outputs **force** → suitable for *free-space* tasks. > - **Admittance Control:** Robot measures **force error** and outputs **position correction** → suitable for *constrained* tasks (e.g., pushing a wheelchair, lifting a cup). > Most rehabilitation robots use **impedance control** to allow safe, compliant contact with the patient. ### 2.1.3 The Action Layer (Actuators) | Actuator | Specific Power (W/kg) | Application in Assistive Robots | |----------|----------------------|--------------------------------| | **DC Servo Motor + Harmonic Drive** | 50 – 200 | Wheelchair motors, manipulator joints | | **Brushless DC (BLDC) + Planetary
