Gear** | 300 – 600 | Exoskeleton hip/knee joints | | **Pneumatic Muscle (PAM / McKibben)** | 1000+ (peak) | Soft exosuits, rehabilitation gloves | | **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** | Tunable stiffness | Safe physical HRI, gait trainers | | **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** | Slow response | Lightweight finger orthoses | | **Hydraulic** | 1000+ | Heavy-payload rehab (Lokomat-class) | --- ## 2.2 Mathematical Framework — KTU Formula Sheet > [!WARNING] > **Mark-Loss Alert:** Students frequently confuse **DOF** with **axes**. **DOF = number of independent parameters** specifying robot configuration. A rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** (3 translation + 3 rotation). ### 2.2.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — Kutzbach / Grübler Criterion $$ DOF = \lambda (n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i $$ Where: - $DOF$ = total degrees of freedom of the kinematic chain - $\lambda$ = degrees of freedom of a single rigid body in space (typically $\lambda = 6$ in 3D, $\lambda = 3$ in 2D) - $n$ = total number of links (including ground) - $j$ = total number of joints - $f_i$ = DOF of the $i^{th}$ joint ### 2.2.2 Workspace Volume of a Spherical Manipulator $$ V_{workspace} = \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(L_{max}^{\,3} - L_{min}^{\,3}\right) $$ For a 2R planar arm: $$ A_{workspace} = \pi \left( L_1 + L_2 \right)^2 - \pi \left( \lvert L_1 - L_2 \rvert \right)^2 $$ ### 2.2.3 Joint Torque Equation (Static Force Balance) For a manipulator with joint variables $q = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$ and external end-effector force $F$: $$ \tau = J(q)^T F + G(q) + B \dot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} $$ Where: - $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ = vector of joint torques - $J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ = Jacobian matrix - $G(q)$ = gravity compensation term - $B \dot{q}$ = viscous friction term - $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ = Coriolis/centrifugal term ### 2.2.4 Impedance Control Law $$ M_d (\ddot{x} - \ddot{x}_d) + B_d (\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) + K_d (x - x_d) = F_{ext} $$ This is the **mass-spring-damper** target dynamics, where the robot behaves as if it has virtual mass $M_d$, damping $B_d$, and stiffness $K_d$. ### 2.2.5 Body Weight Support (BWS) — Overground Exoskeleton $$ BWS \,(\%) = \frac{F_{vertical,robot}}{W_{user}} \times 100 $$ Where $F_{vertical,robot}$ = vertical reaction force provided by the exoskeleton, and $W_{user}$ = body weight of the user. ### 2.2.6 Battery Energy Budget (Autonomous Mobile Assistive Robot) $$ T_{endurance} = \frac{E_{battery} \cdot \eta_{powertrain}}{P_{base} + P_{actuation}} $$ Where $\eta_{powertrain}$ is the drivetrain efficiency (typically **0.75 – 0.92** for BLDC systems). ### 2.2.7 Force-Velocity Trade-off (Cable-Driven Exosuits) $$ F_{assist} \cdot v_{actuator} \leq P_{motor,rated} $$ ### 2.2.8 Summary Table — Quick Reference | Formula | Equation | Used For | |---------|----------|----------| | DOF (Grübler) | $DOF = \lambda (n-1-j) + \sum f_i$ | Kinematic design | | Forward Kinematics | $T_{end} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i(q_i)$ | Pose computation | | Workspace (2R) | $A = \pi [(L_1+L_2)^2 - (L_1-L_2)^2]$ | Reachability check | | Static Joint Torque | $\tau = J^T F + G(q)$ | Holding/manipulation | | Impedance Law | $M_d \ddot{e} + B_d \dot{e} + K_d e = F_{ext}$ | Compliant HRI | | BWS Percentage | $BWS = (F_{robot} / W_{user}) \times 100$ | Gait training | | Battery Endurance | $T = (E_{bat} \cdot \eta) / P_{total}$ | Mobile robot design | | Energy of a Step | $E_{step} = \int F(t) \cdot v(t) \, dt$ | Exoskeleton efficiency | --- ## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility | Domain | Application | Impact | |--------|-------------|--------| | **Stroke Rehabilitation** | MIT-Manus, ArmeoPower | 30–40% faster FIM recovery vs. conventional therapy | | **Spinal Cord Injury** | ReWalk, Ekso GT, HAL | Independent ambulation for T6–T12 paraplegics | | **Cerebral Palsy (Pediatric)** | Lokomat Pro, Pediatric GAITRite | Gait normalization in children | | **Aged-Care / Geriatric** | HOBBIT, Care-O-bot | 24×7 fall detection & activity monitoring | | **Visually Impaired** | Guide dog robot (HyQ), Smart cane with LiDAR | Independent navigation | | **ALS / Locked-In** | BrainGate + JACO arm | Mind-controlled manipulation | | **Elderly Mobility** | Smart wheelchair (Whill, Whill Model C) | Outdoor independence for 60+ users | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Degrees of Freedom of a 2-DOF Planar Feeding Arm > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (KTU-Standard):** > A 2-link planar manipulator (feeding arm) is to be designed for a wheelchair-mounted assistive robot. The first link is $L_1 = 0.30$ m, the second is $L_2 = 0.25$ m, and both joints are revolute. Compute the DOF, the reachable workspace area, and the inverse kinematics for a target cup position $(x_c, y_c)$. ### Step 1 — Identify Parameters for the Grübler Equation We have a planar manipulator (2D), so: - $\lambda = 3$ (a rigid body in 2D has 3 DOF: 2 translations + 1 rotation) - $n = 4$ (ground link + link 1 + link 2 + end-effector) - $j = 3$ (base revolute + elbow revolute + wrist — but for 2R, $j=2$ revolute + 1 ground prismatic equivalent). For 2R arm: $n = 3$ (ground + 2 links), $j = 2$ revolute joints. ### Step 2 — Apply the Grübler Criterion $$ DOF = \lambda (n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i $$ Substituting $\lambda = 3$, $n = 3$, $j = 2$, and $f_1 = f_2 = 1$ (each revolute joint contributes 1 DOF): $$ DOF = 3 (3 - 1 - 2) + (1 + 1) = 3(0) + 2 = 2 $$ > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Check:** A 2R planar arm has 2 independent joint angles ($\theta_1, \theta_2$), so it has 2 DOF. The math agrees. ### Step 3 — Compute the Workspace Area The reachable region is the **annulus** between the inner radius $\lvert L_1 - L_2 \rvert$ and the outer radius $L_1 + L_2$. $$ A_{workspace} = \pi (L_1 + L_2)^2 - \pi (L_1 - L_2)^2 $$ Substituting $L_1 = 0.30$ m, $L_2 = 0.25$ m: $$ A_{workspace} = \pi (0.55)^2 - \pi (0.05)^2 $$ $$ A_{workspace} = \pi (0.3025 - 0.0025) = \pi (0.3000) $$ $$ A_{workspace} \approx 0.9425 \text{ m}^2 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** A workspace of $\approx 0.94 \, m^2$ is sufficient to cover a **typical dining plate (radius $\approx 0.15$ m)** placed in front of a seated user, with comfortable reach margin. ### Step 4 — Inverse Kinematics (Closed-Form for 2R) Let the end-effector target be at Cartesian coordinates $(x, y)$. By the **law of cosines**: $$ \cos \theta_2 = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} $$ Then: $$ \theta_2 = \pm \cos^{-1}\!\left( \frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} \right) $$ The two solutions correspond to **"elbow-up"** and **"elbow-down"** configurations. For $\theta_1$: $$ \theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{L_2 \sin \theta_2}{L_1 + L_2 \cos \theta_2}\right) $$ **Numerical Example:** Let $(x, y) = (0.40, 0.20)$ m. Compute $\cos \theta_2$: $$ \cos \theta_2 = \frac{(0.40)^2 + (0.20)^2 - (0.30)^2 - (0.25)^2}{2 (0.30)(0.25)} $$ $$ \cos \theta_2 = \frac{0.16 + 0.04 - 0.09 - 0.0625}{0.15} = \frac{0.0475}{0.15} $$ $$ \cos \theta_2 \approx 0.3167 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 \approx 71.55^\circ \; (\text{elbow-down}) \; \text{or} \; -71.55^\circ \; (\text{elbow-up}) $$ For $\theta_1$ (using $\theta_2 = 71.55^\circ$): $$ \theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.20}{0.40}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.25 \sin 71.55^\circ}{0.30 + 0.25 \cos 71.55^\circ}\right) $$ $$ \theta_1 = \tan^{-1}(0.50) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.25 \times 0.9487}{0.30 + 0.25 \times 0.3167}\right) $$ $$ \theta_1 = 26.57^\circ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.2372}{0.3792}\right) = 26.57^\circ - 32.05^\circ $$ $$ \boxed{\theta_1 \approx -5.48^\circ, \quad \theta_2 \approx 71.55^\circ} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Singular Configuration Alert:** When $\theta_2 \to 0$ or $\pi$, the manipulator is in a **kinematic singularity**, and $\theta_1$ becomes mathematically ill-defined. Always check that $x^2 + y^2 \in [\lvert L_1 - L_2 \rvert^2, (L_1 + L_2)^2]$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Static Joint Torque for a Feeding Arm Holding a Cup ### Setup The cup (with food) weighs $m = 0.40$ kg. Gravity acts vertically. Compute the joint torques required at the shoulder and elbow to **hold** the cup horizontally at $(x, y) = (0.40, 0.20)$ m. ### Step 1 — Identify the Jacobian For a 2R planar manipulator: $$ J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin \theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ L_1 \cos \theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Force at End-Effector $$ F_{ext} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -m g \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -0.40 \times 9.81 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -3.924 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Jacobian at the Given Configuration Using $\theta_1 = -5.48^\circ \approx -0.0957$ rad, $\theta_2 = 71.55^\circ \approx 1.249$ rad: $\theta_1 + \theta_2 \approx 1.153$ rad $$ \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \approx \sin(1.153) \approx 0.9151 $$ $$ \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \approx \cos(1.153) \approx 0.4034 $$ $$ J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.30 \sin(-0.0957) - 0.25 (0.9151) & -0.25 (0.9151) \\ 0.30 \cos(-0.0957) + 0.25 (0.4034) & 0.25 (0.4034) \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ J \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.0287 - 0.2288 & -0.2288 \\ 0.2986 + 0.1009 & 0.1009 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.2001 & -0.2288 \\ 0.3995 & 0.1009 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Static Torque Equation (gravity term only) $$ \tau = J^T \cdot F_{ext} $$ $$ \tau = \begin{bmatrix} -0.2001 & 0.3995 \\ -0.2288 & 0.1009 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -3.924 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \tau_1 = (-0.2001)(0) + (0.3995)(-3.924) \approx -1.567 \text{ N·m} $$ $$ \tau_2 = (-0.2288)(0) + (0.1009)(-3.924) \approx -0.396 \text{ N·m} $$ The **negative sign** indicates the torque direction is **clockwise** (the motor must counteract the gravitational sag). > [!NOTE] > **Design Check:** The required shoulder torque of $\approx 1.57$ N·m is well within the rating of a standard **BLDC + harmonic drive** actuator (typically 3–10 N·m continuous). The design is feasible. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Assistive Robots | Feature | **Wheelchair-Mounted Arm (JACO/KINOVA)** | **Overground Exoskeleton (ReWalk/Ekso)** | **Surgical / Rehabilitation Robot (ArmeoPower)** | **Smart Cane (LiDAR-based)** | |---------|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|----------------------------| | **DOF** | 6 – 7 | 4 – 8 (actuated joints) | 6 + gripper | 0 (passive base) | | **Mass (kg)** | 5 – 7 | 20 – 30 | 8 – 12 | 0.5 – 1.5 | | **Power Source** | Wheelchair battery | Li-ion 36–48 V | Mains / battery | Battery (USB-C) | | **Control** | Joystick / EMG | Crutch buttons + tilt sensors | Force-feedback therapy games | Haptic + audio | | **End User** | ALS, high-level SCI | Paraplegia (T6–T12) | Stroke, MS, CP | Visually impaired | | **Cost (USD)** | 15,000 – 50,000 | 70,000 – 150,000 | 50,000 – 100,000 | 200 – 2,000 | | **Autonomy Level** | Level 2 (Shared) | Level 3 (Conditional) | Level 2 (Therapist-supervised) | Level 4 (High) | | **Primary Sensor** | Encoders, F/T | IMU, encoders, FSR insole | Encoders, position tracker | LiDAR, IMU, ultrasonic | | **Clinical Outcome** | ↑ FIM by 12–18 | ↑ Walking Index by 8–10 | ↑ Fugl-Meyer by 15+ | ↑ Mobility Score by 30% | | **Key Risk** | Pinch / collision | Fall (operational) | Over-loading soft tissue | Slip / wet-floor | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Figure 1: Hierarchical Control Architecture of an Assistive Robot ```mermaid flowchart TB userInput[/User Intent Layer
Voice, EMG, EEG, Joystick/] taskPlanner[Task Planner
FSM / Behavior Tree] trajectoryGen[Trajectory Generator
RRT* / Polynomial Spline] impedanceCtrl[Impedance / Admittance
Controller] jointServo[Joint Servo Loops
PID with Gravity Comp] actuators[/Actuator Bank
BLDC + Harmonic Drive/] robotBody[/Robot Mechanical
Structure/] feedback1[Force/Torque
Sensor] feedback2[Encoder / IMU
Sensor] feedback3[Vision / Depth
Sensor] safety[ISO 15066 Safety
Supervisor] eStop[/Hardware E-Stop/] userInput --> taskPlanner taskPlanner --> trajectoryGen trajectoryGen --> impedanceCtrl impedanceCtrl --> jointServo jointServo --> actuators actuators --> robotBody robotBody -.-> feedback1 robotBody -.-> feedback2 robotBody -.-> feedback3 feedback1 --> impedanceCtrl feedback2 --> jointServo feedback3 --> taskPlanner safety -.-> jointServo safety -.-> actuators eStop -.-> actuators ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Forward arrows (→) carry **command signals**; dotted arrows (·-·-→) carry **sensor feedback**. The **Safety Supervisor** has the highest authority — it can override any command from any layer to enforce ISO 15066 force limits. --- ## 4.2 Figure 2: Functional Block Architecture of a Wheelchair-Mounted Feeding Robot ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PERCEPTION[PERCEPTION LAYER] joy[Joystick /
Switch Input] emg[EMG Electrodes
on Biceps] camera[RGB-D Camera
on Tray] force[Force/Torque
Sensor at Wrist] end subgraph DECISION[DECISION LAYER] intent[Intent Decoder
CNN / SVM] planner[Task Planner
Pick and Place FSM] ctrl[Low-Level Servo
PID + Gravity Comp] end subgraph ACTION[ACTION LAYER] motor1[Base Motor M1] motor2[Shoulder M2] motor3[Elbow M3] motor4[Wrist M4] spoon[Spoon
End-Effector] end joy --> intent emg --> intent intent --> planner camera --> planner planner --> ctrl ctrl --> motor1 ctrl --> motor2 ctrl --> motor3 ctrl --> motor4 motor1 --> spoon motor2 --> spoon motor3 --> spoon motor4 --> spoon force --> ctrl ``` --- ## 4.3 Figure 3: Classification Tree of Assistive Robots (Functional) ```mermaid flowchart TB AR[Assistive Robots] AR --> MOB[Mobility Aids] AR --> MAN[Manipulator Arms] AR --> REHAB[Rehabilitation Robots] AR --> SENS[Sensory Substitutes] AR --> COG[Cognitive Aids] MOB --> SW[Smart Wheelchair] MOB --> GAIT[Gait Trainer] MOB --> EXO[Exoskeleton] MOB --> WALK[Smart Walker] MAN --> FEED[Feeding Arm
JACO, KINOVA] MAN --> PNP[Pick-and-Place] MAN --> BED[Bed-Mover / Hoist] REHAB --> ARM[Armeo Power] REHAB --> LOKO[Lokomat] REHAB --> TYMO[Tyromotion] SENS --> CANE[Smart Cane] SENS --> GDOG[Guide Dog Robot] SENS --> BRAI[Braille Reader] SENS --> SIGN[Sign-Language
Avatar] COG --> COMP[Companion Robot
PARO, NAO] COG --> REM[Reminder / Scheduler] COG --> AUT[Autism Therapy] ``` --- ## 4.4 Figure 4: Impedance Control Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR xd[Desired Position x_d] x[Actual Position x] e[/Error e = x_d - x/] md[M_d] bd[B_d] kd[K_d] fext[/External Force F_ext/] robot[/Robot + Environment/] xout[/x_dot_dot/] xd --> e x --> e e --> md md --> bd bd --> kd kd --> robot fext --> robot robot --> xout xout --> x ``` > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** The robot is modeled as a virtual **mass-spring-damper** between the user's intended position and the actual position. Adjusting $K_d$ changes "stiffness" — high $K_d$ = stiff robot (precise), low $K_d$ = soft robot (compliant, safe). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define an assistive robot. List any two principles governing its design with a one-line justification for each.** **Model Answer:** > An **assistive robot** is a mechatronic system that physically, sensorially, or cognitively aids a person with disability in performing activities of daily living, while operating in close physical proximity to the human. **Two Principles:** 1. **Safety (Priority 0):** Under any single-point failure, the robot must not cause harm — enforced via redundant force sensors, mechanical compliance (SEA), and ISO/TS 15066 force limits. 2. **Intuitive Human-Robot Interaction (HRI):** The control interface must be learned within minutes; therefore, intent signals (EMG, voice, gesture) are mapped to actions with sub-200 ms latency. > [!NOTE] > **Word Count Target (KTU Valuation):** For 3 marks, write **3–4 sentences** + **two crisp principle statements**. Avoid paragraphs. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between impedance control and admittance control as used in rehabilitation robots.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Impedance Control | Admittance Control | |---------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Input measured** | Position error $e = x_d - x$ | Force error $F_d - F_{ext}$ | | **Output commanded** | Force $F$ to actuators | Position $x$ to actuators | | **Behavior** | Robot behaves as a virtual mass-spring-damper driven by displacement. | Robot behaves as a virtual mass-spring-damper driven by force. | | **Best suited for** | Free-space motion (reaching, walking in air). | Constrained motion (pushing a wheelchair door, holding a wall). | | **Example clinical use** | MIT-Manus reaching therapy. | Lokomat treadmill gait training. | | **Stability** | Stable in free space. | Stable in contact tasks. | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]` **a)** A 2-DOF planar manipulator is to be mounted on a powered wheelchair as a feeding aid. The links are $L_1 = 0.35$ m and $L_2 = 0.30$ m. Using the Grübler–Kutzbach criterion, determine the DOF of the manipulator and calculate the area of its reachable workspace. **(7 marks)** **b)** Explain the functional block diagram of an assistive robot. Identify and justify the role of the three layers: Perception, Decision, and Action. **(7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Step 1 — Grübler–Kutzbach Criterion** For a planar mechanism in 2D, $\lambda = 3$. The 2R planar arm has: - $n = 3$ links (ground, link 1, link 2) - $j = 2$ revolute joints, each $f_i = 1$ $$ DOF = \lambda (n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i = 3 (3 - 1 - 2) + (1 + 1) = 0 + 2 = 2 $$ **DOF = 2** ✓ **Step 2 — Workspace Area** The workspace is the annulus between $\lvert L_1 - L_2 \rvert$ and $(L_1 + L_2)$: $$ A = \pi (L_1 + L_2)^2 - \pi (L_1 - L_2)^2 $$ $$ A = \pi (0.65)^2 - \pi (0.05)^2 = \pi (0.4225 - 0.0025) $$ $$ \boxed{A \approx 1.3195 \text{ m}^2} $$ **Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation** A $1.32$ m² workspace easily covers a **dining plate of radius 0.15 m** and the user's lateral reach envelope. The arm is **kinematically suitable** for feeding tasks. > [!NOTE] > **Alternative Method (if asked):** If Grübler is replaced by simply counting independent joint variables, $DOF = 2$ (since there are 2 revolute joints, each contributing 1 DOF). This is acceptable for 2R planar arms. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution The functional block diagram of an assistive robot has three layers: **1. Perception Layer (Sensors)** - Encoders, IMU, F/T sensors, EMG/EEG, vision. - Converts physical phenomena (motion, force, intent) into electrical signals. - *Justification:* Without accurate sensing, the robot cannot know either the **user's intent** (e.g., EMG-triggered grasp) or its **own state** (encoder feedback). **2. Decision Layer (Controller + AI)** - Hosts the task planner (FSM, behavior tree), motion planner (RRT*), and low-level impedance/admittance controller. - *Justification:* Translates noisy sensor data into **coherent actions** — decides *what to do* (pick up spoon) and *how to do it* (trajectory with compliant force). **3. Action Layer (Actuators + Mechanics)** - BLDC motors, harmonic drives, Series Elastic Actuators (SEA), and the mechanical structure. - *Justification:* Converts electrical commands into **physical motion** with appropriate torque, speed, and compliance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Closed-Loop Justification:** Feedback flows backward from Action → Perception. The **ISO 15066 Safety Supervisor** runs in parallel with **override authority** to all layers. --- ### Question B (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]` **a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **impedance control** scheme used in rehabilitation robots. Derive the impedance equation and explain the role of virtual stiffness $K_d$, damping $B_d$, and inertia $M_d$ in achieving safe human-robot interaction. **(7 marks)** **b)** A 2-DOF planar arm (links $L_1 = 0.30$ m, $L_2 = 0.25$ m) holds a cup of mass 0.50 kg at position $(x, y) = (0.45, 0.15)$ m. Compute the inverse kinematics solution and the joint torques required to hold the cup statically. Use $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Step 1 — Definition** Impedance control models the robot as a **virtual mass-spring-damper** system that regulates the dynamic relationship between the end-effector position error $e = x_d - x$ and the contact force $F_{ext}$. **Step 2 — Impedance Equation Derivation** Starting from Newton's second law for a virtual mass acted on by spring-damper forces and external force: $$ M_d \ddot{e} + B_d \dot{e} + K_d e = F_{ext} $$ Where: - $M_d$ = virtual mass (kg) - $B_d$ = virtual damping (N·s/m) - $K_d$ = virtual stiffness (N/m) - $F_{ext}$ = external force from human/environment (N) - $e = x_d - x$ = position error (m) **Step 3 — Role of Each Parameter** | Parameter | Physical Meaning | Tuning Consequence | |-----------|------------------|---------------------| | $K_d$ (stiffness) | How strongly the robot resists deviation. | High $K_d$ → stiff, precise. Low $K_d$ → compliant, safe. | | $B_d$ (damping) | How quickly oscillations decay. | Critical for stability; under-damped → unsafe vibration. | | $M_d$ (inertia) | How quickly the robot accelerates in response to error. | Low $M_d$ → snappy response. High $M_d$ → sluggish but smooth. | **Step 4 — Block Diagram (Textual)** ``` x_d → (+) → e → [M_d, B_d, K_d virtual admittance] → F_command → Robot → x ↑ ↓ └──── F_ext (from environment) ←┘ ``` **Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation** - **High $K_d$** is used in **precision tasks** (e.g., spoon-to-mouth alignment). - **Low $K_d$ + high $B_d$** is used in **therapy mode** (e.g., stroke arm rehabilitation, where the patient must lead and the robot must "give way"). - The ISO 15066 force limit ($\leq 65$ N head, $\leq 150$ N limbs) is enforced by **bounding $K_d \cdot e$** in software. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Step 1 — Inverse Kinematics** Given $(x, y) = (0.45, 0.15)$ m, $L_1 = 0.30$ m, $L_2 = 0.25$ m. $$ \cos \theta_2 = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} = \frac{0.2025 + 0.0225 - 0.09 - 0.0625}{0.15} $$ $$ \cos \theta_2 = \frac{0.0725}{0.15} \approx 0.4833 $$ $$ \theta_2 \approx \pm 61.10^\circ $$ For $\theta_1$ (using $\theta_2 = 61.10^\circ$): $$ \theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.15}{0.45}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.25 \sin 61.10^\circ}{0.30 + 0.25 \cos 61.10^\circ}\right) $$ $$ \theta_1 \approx 18.43^\circ - 25.12^\circ \approx -6.69^\circ $$ $$ \boxed{\theta_1 \approx -6.69^\circ, \quad \theta_2 \approx 61.10^\circ} $$ **Step 2 — Static Torque Calculation** End-effector force (gravity only): $$ F = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -m g \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -0.50 \times 9.81 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -4.905 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N} $$ Jacobian at this configuration ($\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 54.41^\circ \approx 0.9497$ rad): $$ \sin(54.41^\circ) \approx 0.8131, \quad \cos(54.41^\circ) \approx 0.5821 $$ $$ J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.30 \sin(-6.69^\circ) - 0.25 (0.8131) & -0.25 (0.8131) \\ 0.30 \cos(-6.69^\circ) + 0.25 (0.5821) & 0.25 (0.5821) \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ J \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.0349 - 0.2033 & -0.2033 \\ 0.2979 + 0.1455 & 0.1455 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1683 & -0.2033 \\ 0.4434 & 0.1455 \end{bmatrix} $$ Apply $\tau = J^T F$: $$ \tau_1 = (-0.1683)(0) + (0.4434)(-4.905) \approx -2.175 \text{ N·m} $$ $$ \tau_2 = (-0.2033)(0) + (0.1455)(-4.905) \approx -0.714 \text{ N·m} $$ $$ \boxed{\tau_1 \approx -2.18 \text{ N·m}, \quad \tau_2 \approx -0.71 \text{ N·m}} $$ The **negative signs** indicate the motors must apply **clockwise torques** to counteract gravity. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes (KTU Examiner's Pitfalls):** > 1. **Sign of cos θ₂:** Forgetting the $\pm$ (elbow-up vs. elbow-down) loses 1 mark. > 2. **Order of matrix multiplication:** Students often write $\tau = F \cdot J^T$ instead of $\tau = J^T F$ — this is dimensionally wrong. **Lose 2 marks.** > 3. **Forgetting the Jacobian transpose:** Static equilibrium requires $\tau = J^T F$, not $\tau = J F$. **Lose 2 marks.** > 4. **Units:** Always write N·m for torque. Bare numbers without units → **0.5 mark deduction** per KTU 2024 scheme. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks in Module 4 (Robotic Aids):** > > 1. **DOF vs. Axes Confusion (–2 marks):** DOF ≠ number of motors. DOF is the number of *independent configuration parameters*. A 2R arm with 2 motors has 2 DOF in 2D. > 2. **Impedance vs. Admittance Mix-up (–2 marks):** Impedance → input is **position**, output is **force**. Admittance → input is **force**, output is **position**. > 3. **Skipping the safety layer (–1.5 marks):** Any control block diagram without a **Safety Supervisor** is considered incomplete under KTU 2024 NEP-aligned evaluation. > 4. **No clinical justification (–1.5 marks):** When asked "design a feeding arm," students often stop at the math. Always close with **clinical outcome metrics** (FIM, Fugl-Meyer, etc.). > 5. **Units and sign conventions (–0.5 to 1 mark):** Always include units and explain physical meaning of negative signs. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Robotic Aids:** ### Definitions - ☐ Assistive robot = mechatronic device aiding persons with disability in ADLs. - ☐ Rehabilitation robot = clinical-grade robot for therapy (e.g., Lokomat, ArmeoPower). - ☐ Exoskeleton = wearable robot that augments residual limb function. - ☐ Prosthetic robot = replaces a missing limb (typically myoelectric). - ☐ HRI = Human-Robot Interaction; BCI = Brain-Computer Interface. ### Five Cardinal Principles - ☐ Safety (ISO 15066 limits: 65 N head, 150 N limb). - ☐ Adaptability. - ☐ Intuitive HRI (latency < 200 ms). - ☐ Affordability. - ☐ Autonomy gradient (Levels 0–5). ### Key Equations (Must Memorize) - ☐ Grübler's criterion: $DOF = \lambda (n-1-j) + \sum f_i$. - ☐ Workspace area (2R): $A = \pi [(L_1 + L_2)^2 - (L_1 - L_2)^2]$. - ☐ Static torque: $\tau = J^T F + G(q)$. - ☐ Impedance law: $M_d \ddot{e} + B_d \dot{e} + K_d e = F_{ext}$. - ☐ BWS percentage: $(F_{robot}/W_{user}) \times 100$. ### Sensors & Actuators - ☐ Encoders + IMU (proprioception). - ☐ F/T sensors + EMG/EEG (exteroception + intent). - ☐ BLDC + Harmonic Drive = standard actuation. - ☐ SEA = Series Elastic Actuator (safe HRI). - ☐ PAM = Pneumatic Artificial Muscle (soft exosuits). ### Control Paradigms - ☐ Impedance control = free-space, position-in/force-out. - ☐ Admittance control = constrained, force-in/position-out. - ☐ Shared control = human intent + robot autonomy blended. ### Clinical Metrics - ☐ FIM (Functional Independence Measure): 18–126. - ☐ Fugl-Meyer Assessment: 0–66 for upper limb. - ☐ Walking Index for SCI (WISCI): 0–20. - ☐ 10-Meter Walk Test (10MWT): gait speed in m/s. ### Safety Standards - ☐ ISO/TS 15066 — Collaborative robot force limits. - ☐ IEC 60601 — Medical electrical equipment safety. - ☐ FDA 21 CFR 820 — Quality system regulation for medical devices. - ☐ MDR EU 2017/745 — European Medical Device Regulation. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "I-CARE":** > **I**ntent sensing → **C**ontrol (impedance/admittance) → **A**ctuator (BLDC/SEA) → **R**ehabilitation protocol → **E**nd-point safety.

topics.title: Robots as Personal Assistants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robots as Personal Assistants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Personal Assistant Robot (PAR)** is a class of **service robot** engineered to perform assistive, supervisory, and interactive tasks for an individual user within a domestic, clinical, or institutional environment. According to the **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)**, service robots are classified as robots that "perform useful tasks for humans or equipment excluding industrial automation applications." Personal Assistant Robots fall under the sub-category of **Domestic Service Robots**, specifically tailored to enhance the **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** and **Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADL)** of users with reduced functional capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEBRT745, Module 4):**
> *"Robots as Personal Assistants are intelligent, human-interacting mechatronic systems that supplement, enhance, or substitute human physical, sensory, or cognitive capabilities to support independent living, rehabilitation, and social engagement of users including the elderly, disabled, and convalescing."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highly attentive, multilingual butler who never sleeps, never gets tired, and never judges you.** That is essentially what a Personal Assistant Robot is — a tireless, programmable, sensor-rich mechanical companion designed to *lend its body and brain* to a person who needs help.

- For an **elderly person**, it is the **"remembering brain"** — fetching glasses, reminding about medication, detecting falls.
- For a **paraplegic user**, it is the **"borrowed arms"** — picking up dropped items, opening doors, operating switches.
- For a **visually impaired user**, it is the **"borrowed eyes"** — reading labels, navigating corridors, describing surroundings.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a PAR as a *smartphone in physical form*. Just as your phone became a single device that replaced a camera, map, music player, and notebook — the PAR becomes a single device that replaces a caretaker, a walker, a reminder system, and a social companion.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Terminology

The following standardized metrics govern the design and evaluation of Personal Assistant Robots:

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — The number of independent movements a robot can perform. Human-like manipulation typically requires **≥ 6 DOF** per arm.
- **Payload Capacity** — The maximum mass the robot can safely carry, typically **0.5 kg to 5 kg** for domestic PARs.
- **Operational Autonomy** — Battery endurance, typically **2 to 8 hours** for mobile PARs.
- **Safe Human-Robot Interaction Distance** — Minimum proximity threshold: **0.5 m** for social robots, **0.2 m** for physical contact.
- **Cognitive Latency** — Response time between sensor input and actuator response: **≤ 200 ms** for conversational, **≤ 50 ms** for safety reflexes.
- **Repeatability** — Positional accuracy of repeated actions: **± 0.1 mm to ± 5 mm** depending on application.
- **ISO 13482** — The international safety standard specifically for **"Personal Care Robots"** (the governing benchmark for PARs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 curriculum specifically mandates that students understand the categorization, sensory architecture, and end-effector design of PARs in the context of assistive healthcare, NOT industrial automation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Personal Assistant Robots

Personal Assistant Robots are organized into a **three-tier functional hierarchy**:

### Tier I — Physical Assistance Robots
These directly perform or augment physical tasks. Examples include:
- **Manipulator Arms** (e.g., JACO, iARM by Kinova Robotics)
- **Mobile Fetching Robots** (e.g., HSR — Toyota Human Support Robot)
- **Powered Wheelchair Add-ons** (e.g., intelligent navigation modules)

### Tier II — Sensory & Cognitive Assistance Robots
These augment perception and memory functions.
- **Navigation Assistants for the Visually Impaired** (e.g., GuideCane, NavCog)
- **Medication Reminder Robots** (e.g., Pillo, Catalia Health's Mabu)
- **Cognitive Stimulation Companions** (e.g., NAO, Pepper)

### Tier III — Social & Emotional Companion Robots
These primarily address **affective needs** and psychological well-being.
- **Zoomorphic Companions** (e.g., PARO seal, AIBO dog)
- **Humanoid Social Robots** (e.g., Pepper, Romeo)
- **Pet Therapy Replacements** for hospitals and elder care facilities

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this hierarchy matters:** A KTU exam answer that confuses these tiers will lose marks for lack of clarity. Always identify *which tier* the question is referring to in your answer.

## 2.2 Core Subsystem Architecture

A Personal Assistant Robot is built from **five interconnected subsystems**:

1. **Sensing Subsystem**
   - RGB-D cameras (e.g., Microsoft Kinect, Intel RealSense)
   - LiDAR and ultrasonic proximity sensors
   - Force/torque sensors at end-effectors
   - Microphone arrays for voice input
   - Tactile sensors (capacitive, piezoelectric, E-skin)

2. **Perception & Cognition Subsystem**
   - SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) algorithms
   - Speech recognition (ASR — Automatic Speech Recognition)
   - Natural Language Processing (NLP)
   - Face and emotion recognition (CNN-based)
   - Intent inference via Reinforcement Learning

3. **Actuation Subsystem**
   - Servo motors and brushless DC motors
   - Soft pneumatic actuators (for safe physical contact)
   - Series Elastic Actuators (SEA) for compliant interaction

4. **Communication Subsystem**
   - Wi-Fi / Bluetooth for cloud connectivity
   - Emergency alert systems (GSM/cellular fallback)
   - Telepresence interfaces for remote caregivers

5. **Power & Safety Subsystem**
   - Li-ion battery packs with BMS (Battery Management System)
   - Emergency stop circuits (hardware-level, NOT software-dependent)
   - Collision avoidance via redundant sensor layers

## 2.3 Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) Modalities

Personal Assistant Robots must interact with **non-technical users**, often with cognitive or physical impairments. HRI occurs across **four modalities**:

- **Verbal / Speech-Based** — Voice commands and natural dialogue
- **Gestural** — Hand gestures, body posture, gaze tracking
- **Haptic / Tactile** — Physical touch feedback, guided motion
- **Proxemic** — Respecting social distances (Hall's proxemics applied to robots)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Relationship** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom (Planar Manipulator) | $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j$ | $n$ = number of links, $j$ = number of joints |
| Grübler's Criterion (Spatial) | $DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | $j_i$ = joints with $i$ DOF |
| Forward Kinematics (2-link arm) | $x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | $L$ = link length, $\theta$ = joint angle |
| Forward Kinematics (y) | $y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | Cartesian position of end-effector |
| Inverse Kinematics (2-link) | $\theta_2 = \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$ | Reachability constraint applies |
| Workspace Reach (Maximum) | $r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2$ | Fully extended configuration |
| Workspace Reach (Minimum) | $r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ | Folded-back configuration |
| Battery Operating Time | $T_{op} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{nom}}{P_{total}}$ | Hours; $C$ in Ah, $V$ in V, $P$ in W |
| Battery Operating Time (units) | $T_{op} = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \cdot V_{V}}{P_{W}} \text{ hours}$ | Ensure unit consistency |
| Required Power for Mobility | $P_{mob} = F_{drag} \cdot v + m \cdot g \cdot \mu_r \cdot v$ | $F_{drag}$ = drag, $v$ = velocity, $\mu_r$ = rolling resistance |
| Safe Stopping Distance (collision) | $d_{stop} = \dfrac{v^2}{2a_{max}} + v \cdot t_{react}$ | $a_{max}$ = max deceleration, $t_{react}$ = sensor latency |
| Sensor Fusion Weighting (Kalman) | $\hat{x} = w_1 \cdot x_1 + w_2 \cdot x_2, \quad w_1 + w_2 = 1$ | Weighted sensor combination |
| Speech Recognition Confidence | $C_{rec} = P(\text{word} \mid \text{audio}) = \dfrac{P(\text{audio} \mid \text{word}) \cdot P(\text{word})}{P(\text{audio})}$ | Bayesian posterior |
| ISO 13482 Power Limit (Contact) | $P_{contact} \le 80 \text{ W}$ per contact zone | Safety threshold for physical HRI |
| ISO 13482 Max Contact Force | $F_{max} \le 50 \text{ N}$ (quasi-static) | Below this, contact is "safe" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Grübler-Kutzbach Criterion matters:** KTU frequently asks students to compute DOF of a manipulator arm shown in a diagram. Memorize the planar ($3(n-1) - 2j$) and spatial variants. Subtract 1 from $n$ because the **base link is fixed**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Personal Assistant Robots have transitioned from research curiosities to commercial products. The global market for PARs is projected to exceed **USD 20 billion by 2030**, driven by aging populations in Japan, Germany, South Korea, and increasingly in India. In Kerala specifically, with a rapidly growing elderly demographic in Kottayam, Thrissur, and Ernakulam districts, PARs are being piloted in community elder-care programs and post-stroke rehabilitation centers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Workspace Envelope of a 2-Link Manipulator Arm

The most common analytical question KTU asks is the **workspace calculation** of a planar manipulator arm, as used in a fetching PAR.

### Step 1 — Define Link Parameters
Let the manipulator have two rigid links of length $L_1$ and $L_2$ connected by revolute joints at angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

The position of the end-effector in the base frame is given by homogeneous transformation:

$$
T = T_1(\theta_1) \cdot T_2(\theta_2)
$$

where each joint transformation in 2D is:
$$
T_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i & L_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i & L_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Forward Kinematics
For a 2-link planar arm, the end-effector position $(x, y)$ is:

$$
x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Each link contributes a vector from its joint to its tip. We add these vectors using the **law of cosines** and trigonometric identity $\cos(\alpha + \beta) = \cos\alpha\cos\beta - \sin\alpha\sin\beta$.

### Step 3 — Derive the Workspace Radius
The distance from the base to the end-effector is:

$$
r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}
$$

Squaring and using the identities:
$$
\cos^2\alpha + \sin^2\alpha = 1
$$

we get:
$$
r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

### Step 4 — Find Maximum Reach
Maximum reach occurs when $\cos\theta_2 = +1$ (arm fully extended, $\theta_2 = 0$):

$$
r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2
$$

### Step 5 — Find Minimum Reach
Minimum reach occurs when $\cos\theta_2 = -1$ (arm fully folded, $\theta_2 = \pi$):

$$
r_{\min} = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 - 2 L_1 L_2} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Workspace Area
The total reachable area is the **annulus** (ring) between $r_{\min}$ and $r_{\max}$:

$$
A_{work} = \pi \left( r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2 \right) = \pi \left( (L_1 + L_2)^2 - (L_1 - L_2)^2 \right)
$$

Expanding:
$$
A_{work} = \pi \left( L_1^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 + L_2^2 - L_1^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 - L_2^2 \right)
$$

$$
A_{work} = 4 \pi L_1 L_2
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* This elegant result shows the workspace depends **only on the product of link lengths**, not on their individual magnitudes. Designers should pick $L_1 \approx L_2$ for a near-circular workspace.

## 3.2 Derivation: Safe Stopping Distance for Mobile PAR

A mobile personal assistant robot must stop before colliding with a human. The minimum safe distance is critical for ISO 13482 compliance.

### Step 1 — Define Variables
- $v$ = current velocity of robot (m/s)
- $a_{max}$ = maximum deceleration achievable (m/s²)
- $t_{react}$ = total sensor + processing latency (s)

### Step 2 — Braking Distance (Kinematics)
Using the kinematic relation $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ with final velocity $v = 0$:

$$
0 = v^2 - 2 a_{max} d_{brake}
$$

$$
d_{brake} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}
$$

### Step 3 — Reaction Distance
During sensor latency, the robot continues at velocity $v$:

$$
d_{react} = v \cdot t_{react}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Safe Stopping Distance
$$
d_{stop} = d_{brake} + d_{react} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 a_{max}} + v \cdot t_{react}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* This is the **2nd-order kinematic stopping equation**. The $v^2$ term dominates at high speeds, while the linear $v$ term dominates at low speeds. Mobile PARs in homes typically operate at $v = 0.5$ m/s, with $a_{max} = 1.0$ m/s², yielding $d_{stop} \approx 0.225$ m — well within the **0.5 m ISO proxemic boundary**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A 2-link manipulator on a personal assistant robot has link lengths $L_1 = 30$ cm and $L_2 = 25$ cm. Compute (a) maximum reach, (b) minimum reach, and (c) workspace area.

### Solution:

**(a) Maximum Reach:**
$$
r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2 = 30 + 25 = 55 \text{ cm}
$$

**(b) Minimum Reach:**
$$
r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 30 - 25 \vert = 5 \text{ cm}
$$

**(c) Workspace Area:**
$$
A_{work} = 4 \pi L_1 L_2 = 4 \pi (30)(25) = 3000\pi \approx 9424.78 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Converting to square meters: $A_{work} \approx 0.9425$ m².

> *Insight:* This is the area the robot can sweep on a tabletop — a useful metric for verifying it can reach all objects on a standard bedside table.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Personal Assistant Robot

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the complete functional architecture of a generic Personal Assistant Robot, showing the data flow from sensors through cognition to actuators.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Input Layer] --> A1[Voice Microphone Array]
    A --> A2[Touch Screen and Buttons]
    A --> A3[Gesture Camera RGB]
    A1 --> B1[Speech Recognition ASR]
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Central AI Processor]
    B1 --> C2[Natural Language Understanding]
    C1 --> C3[Task Planner and Scheduler]
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1[Motion Controller]
    C3 --> D2[Speech Synthesizer TTS]
    D1 --> E1[Manipulator Arm Actuators]
    D1 --> E2[Mobile Base Drive Motors]
    D1 --> E3[Gripper and End Effector]
    F1[RGB Depth Camera] --> G1[Sensor Fusion Hub]
    F2[LiDAR and Ultrasonic] --> G1
    F3[IMU and Wheel Encoders] --> G1
    F4[Force Torque Sensors] --> G1
    G1 --> C1
    H1[Battery Management BMS] --> I1[Safety Supervisor]
    I1 --> J1[Emergency Stop Hardware]
    I1 --> J2[Collision Avoidance Override]
    C3 --> I1
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Object Fetching by a Mobile PAR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: User Voice Command] --> S2[ASR Converts Speech to Text]
    S2 --> S3{Intent Recognized?}
    S3 -- No --> S4[Ask for Clarification]
    S4 --> S2
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Identify Target Object via Vision]
    S5 --> S6{Object Found?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Search Adjacent Rooms]
    S7 --> S6
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Plan Path Using SLAM]
    S8 --> S9[Navigate to Object Location]
    S9 --> S10{Obstacle Detected?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Replan Path]
    S11 --> S9
    S10 -- No --> S12[Extend Manipulator Arm]
    S12 --> S13[Close Gripper with Force Feedback]
    S13 --> S14[Retract Arm Secure Object]
    S14 --> S15[Navigate to User Location]
    S15 --> S16[Hand Over Object with Haptic Cue]
    S16 --> S17[End: Log Task and Await Next Command]
```

## 4.3 Tier-Based Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PAR[Personal Assistant Robot] --> T1[Tier 1: Physical Assistance]
    PAR --> T2[Tier 2: Sensory and Cognitive Assistance]
    PAR --> T3[Tier 3: Social and Emotional Companions]
    T1 --> T1a[Manipulator Arms]
    T1 --> T1b[Mobile Fetching Robots]
    T1 --> T1c[Powered Mobility Aids]
    T2 --> T2a[Navigation Aids for Blind]
    T2 --> T2b[Medication Reminders]
    T2 --> T2c[Cognitive Stimulators]
    T3 --> T3a[Zoomorphic Robots PARO AIBO]
    T3 --> T3b[Humanoid Robots Pepper NAO]
    T3 --> T3c[Hospital Therapy Companions]
```
