<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define visual acuity. Explain the Snellen chart and state its limitations.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Visual Acuity (VA)** is the quantitative measure of the eye's ability to discern fine spatial detail, defined as the **reciprocal of the minimum angle of resolution (MAR)** in minutes of arc.

**Snellen Chart** — A standardized chart with letters/symbols constructed such that each letter subtends **5 arc-minutes** of visual angle at the distance from which a normal eye should read it (e.g., the 6/6 line is read at 6 m). It is read at 6 m (or 20 ft) and VA is expressed as $D_{d} / D_{n}$.

**Limitations (must mention for full marks):**
1. Non-uniform progression of letter sizes (not geometric).
2. Different number of letters per row — crowding effect.
3. Projector charts vary in contrast and luminance.
4. Cannot measure VA better than 6/6 reliably.
5. Cultural / literacy bias for letter optotypes.

*(Marking split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for chart principle, 1 mark for limitations.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is Orientation and Mobility (O&M)? List any four cane techniques used in mobility training.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**O&M Definition:** Orientation and Mobility is a specialized area of rehabilitation that teaches persons with visual impairment to *orient* themselves in their environment (establishing position and destination) and to *mobilize* safely and independently (travelling from one place to another).

**Four Cane Techniques (1/2 mark each, 2 marks total):**
1. **Touch Technique** — Tip taps ground at each step.
2. **Constant-Contact Technique** — Tip slides continuously on the ground.
3. **Two-Point Touch Technique** — Pendulum arc touching at each end.
4. **Diagonal Technique** — Used in tight indoor spaces for low-clearance areas.

*(Marking split: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing four techniques.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** You are given an **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each is for **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Choose the one you can answer best.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain in detail the various methods of visual acuity testing for a low-vision patient. Mention the advantages of the LogMAR chart over the Snellen chart. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Methods of VA Testing (4 marks):**

1. **Snellen Chart (Standard)** — Letter chart read at 6 m. Gives Snellen fraction. Best for normal-vision screening; **not precise** for low vision.
2. **LogMAR / ETDRS Chart** — Letters arranged in geometric progression, **5 letters per row**, equal spacing. Read at 4 m or 1 m. **Gold standard** for clinical trials and low vision.
3. **Tumbling-E or Landolt-C Chart** — Direction-of-opening optotype; **literacy-independent**; ideal for children and illiterate adults.
4. **Lea Symbols / Cardiff Cards** — Pictorial optotypes (apple, house, square, circle) for **pre-school children**.
5. **Teller Acuity Cards** — Preferential looking test for **infants and pre-verbal children**.
6. **Near VA — Reduced Snellen / M-cards** — Read at 40 cm; used to prescribe magnifiers.
7. **Counting Fingers (CF), Hand Motion (HM), Light Perception (LP)** — Used when VA is below 1/60.

**Advantages of LogMAR over Snellen (3 marks):**
1. **Geometric progression** — each row is 0.1 logMAR smaller than the previous; allows **parametric statistics**.
2. **Equal number of letters per row (5)** — avoids crowding bias and enables **letter-by-letter scoring** (each letter = 0.02 logMAR).
3. **Wider measurement range** with consistent resolution from 6/60 (1.0) to 6/3 (-0.3).
4. **Better repeatability** — reduces inter-test variability by ~50%.
5. **Standardized luminance and contrast** — ETDRS cabinet provides uniform illumination.

---

**Q.A (b)** Describe the components, procedure, and clinical significance of **Functional Vision Assessment (FVA)** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Definition (1 mark):** FVA is a *task-based, real-world* evaluation of how a person uses residual vision in activities of daily living (ADL), complementing the controlled clinical measures of VA, CS, and VF.

**Components / Domains Assessed (3 marks):**
1. **Reading** — newspaper, book, medicine labels, bills.
2. **Writing** — signature, cheque filling, notes.
3. **Face Recognition** — known vs. unknown persons at 1 m / 3 m / 6 m.
4. **Mobility-Related Vision** — detecting steps, kerbs, obstacles, traffic signals.
5. **Self-Care** — grooming, applying makeup, shaving, dressing in matching colors.
6. **Meal Preparation** — pouring liquids (hot/cold identification), cutting, cooking.
7. **Money & Finance** — identifying currency denominations.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
- Conducted in a *simulated real-life environment* (clinic ADL suite or home visit).
- Standardized checklists scored on a 4-point scale: **0 = independent, 1 = needs verbal cue, 2 = needs physical assistance, 3 = unable**.
- Repeated with prescribed low-vision devices to evaluate **functional gain**.
- Compared against age-matched normative data.

**Clinical Significance (1 mark):** FVA is the **bridge** between clinical numbers and patient goals. It validates device prescription, justifies training hours, and documents rehabilitation outcomes for funding/certification.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**Q.B (a)** Discuss in detail the components of **Orientation and Mobility (O&M) assessment** for a person with severe visual impairment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Orientation Assessment (3.5 marks):**

1. **Cognitive Mapping Ability** — Can the person construct and recall a mental map of a familiar route (home → shop → home)?
2. **Landmark Identification** — Uses auditory (church bell, traffic), tactile (texture of pavements), olfactory (bakery smell), and kinesthetic (steps, slopes) landmarks.
3. **Cardinal-Direction Sense** — Understanding and use of N/S/E/W; ability to maintain straight-line travel using sun, wind, sound gradients.
4. **Spatial Updating** — Recalibrating position after turns; using counting-of-steps and squaring-off techniques.
5. **Compass & Tactile Map Skills** — Reading raised-line maps; using a talking compass.
6. **Use of Public Information** — Bus route numbers (announced), Braille/audible signboards.

**Mobility Assessment (3.5 marks):**

1. **Body Posture & Protective Techniques** — Upper-body (forearm), lower-body (trailing/shuffling), squaring-off.
2. **Cane Skills** — Grip, arc width (must be > body width), rhythm, touch vs. constant contact technique.
3. **Sighted-Guide Technique** — Grip on guide's elbow, half-step-behind walk, narrow-space maneuvers, door-handling.
4. **Street Crossing** — Parallel-parallax technique, traffic-sound analysis, identifying surge and lull.
5. **Indoor Navigation** — Door-finding (knock texture, room number Braille), elevator use, stair negotiation (hand-rail trailing).
6. **Dog-Guide Handling** — Harness control, straight-line command, obstacle avoidance (if applicable).

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the **three principal white-cane techniques** used in O&M training with diagrams and their relative advantages. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**1. Touch Technique (2.5 marks):**
- **Description:** The cane tip *taps* the ground at each step, sweeping an arc slightly wider than the body.
- **Arc width:** ~ **1.5 inches (4 cm) on each side beyond shoulder width**.
- **Rhythm:** Step-step-tap (touch on the second step).
- **Advantages:** Conserves energy on smooth surfaces, gives clear discrete tactile feedback per step, faster on familiar routes.
- **Limitations:** Can miss low-lying obstacles between taps on uneven ground.

**2. Constant-Contact (Slide) Technique (2.5 marks):**
- **Description:** Cane tip *slides continuously* on the ground, never losing contact.
- **Rhythm:** Continuous sinusoidal motion; both feet move while cane slides.
- **Advantages:** Continuous information stream, detects **small drop-offs** (curb edges, platform edges) that the touch technique would miss.
- **Limitations:** More tiring due to continuous motion; cane-tip wear is faster; noisy on rough surfaces.

**3. Two-Point Touch Technique (2 marks):**
- **Description:** Pendulum-like motion, cane tip *touches the ground at each end* of the arc (left and right) — like a metronome.
- **Rhythm:** Step-tap, step-tap, evenly paced.
- **Advantages:** Symmetrical, easy to learn, very clean arc coverage; good for transitioning between indoor and outdoor.
- **Limitations:** Slightly slower than touch technique; not as informative as constant contact on rough ground.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 (–2 marks):** Students often *omit the arc width* specification. **Always state** "wider than body shoulder width" — examiners specifically look for this.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 (–1 mark):** Do not confuse "two-point touch" with "diagonal technique." Two-point is a *pendulum*; diagonal is for *low-headroom* indoor use.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 (–1 mark):** When asked for advantages, give at least *one disadvantage* as well — this is what separates a 5-mark answer from a 7-mark answer.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 (–2 marks):** In FVA/O&M questions, students list items but **do not score them** or **do not link to a real-life task**. The examiner awards marks for the *clinical reasoning*, not the list.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize This Before the Exam**

### Visual Function Assessment
- ☐ **VA = reciprocal of MAR**; normal = **6/6, LogMAR 0, decimal 1.0**.
- ☐ **Snellen → Decimal:** divide test distance by numerator; e.g., 6/12 → **0.5**.
- ☐ **LogMAR formula:** $\text{LogMAR} = -\log_{10}(V_{dec}) = \log_{10}(D_{n} \div D_{d})$.
- ☐ **Reverse:** $V_{dec} = 10^{-\text{LogMAR}}$.
- ☐ **Visual Efficiency (VA only):** $VE = 100 \cdot V_{dec}^{\,2}$.
- ☐ **VE (VA + VF):** $VE = 100 \cdot (V_{dec}^{\,2} \cdot VF_{\text{deg}}) \div 180$.
- ☐ **ETDRS / LogMAR chart** = gold standard (5 letters/row, geometric progression).
- ☐ **CS** measured with **Pelli-Robson** (normal ≈ **1.8–2.0 log**).
- ☐ **Ishihara** = color vision (≥ 14/21 normal).
- ☐ **Amsler grid** = central 10° distortion test (macula).

### Orientation and Mobility
- ☐ **O&M = Orientation (where?) + Mobility (how?)**.
- ☐ **White cane** is the **primary mobility aid**; long-cane, ID cane, support cane are variants.
- ☐ **Three principal techniques:** Touch, Constant-Contact, Two-Point Touch.
- ☐ **Cane arc** must be **wider than body shoulder width**.
- ☐ **Cane length formula:** $L \approx$ sternum-to-ground (about **150 cm** average adult).
- ☐ **Sighted-guide** is the *most basic* travel technique; cane use comes later in training.
- ☐ **Protective techniques** = upper body (forearm), lower body (trailing), squaring-off.
- ☐ **Street-crossing** = parallel-parallax technique.
- ☐ **Dog guide** is for **completely blind or near-totally blind** persons with good physical/mental health.
- ☐ **ETAs** (Ultracane, Miniguide, Ray) extend sensing horizon to **3–6 m**.

### Disability Certification Facts
- ☐ **Legal blindness** in India = VA < 6/60 in better eye **OR** VF < 20° in better eye.
- ☐ **Visual disability %** uses combined VA + VF formula (per Govt. of India guidelines).

### Common Formula-Sheet Numbers
- ☐ $\log_{10}(2) = 0.301$, $\log_{10}(5) = 0.699$, $\log_{10}(10) = 1.000$ — *memorize these for fast LogMAR conversions.*
- ☐ $\tan(15^{\circ}) \approx 0.268$ — *for tunnel-vision FOV problems.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE Exam!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assistive Technologies for Hearing: Approaches to Auditory Sensory Aids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assistive Technologies for Hearing: Approaches to Auditory Sensory Aids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Auditory Sensory Aids** are biomedical electronic systems designed to compensate for hearing impairment by capturing, processing, and delivering acoustic information through either acoustic, electroacoustic, or direct electrical stimulation of the auditory pathway. According to the WHO classification, hearing loss is categorized by the **pure-tone average (PTA)** measured at 500, 1000, 2000, and 4000 Hz, with thresholds classified as:

- **Normal hearing**: PTA $\leq 25$ dB HL
- **Mild hearing loss**: $26$–$40$ dB HL
- **Moderate hearing loss**: $41$–$55$ dB HL
- **Moderately severe**: $56$–$70$ dB HL
- **Severe hearing loss**: $71$–$90$ dB HL
- **Profound hearing loss**: $> 90$ dB HL

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: An "approach" in auditory sensory aids refers to the *pathway* by which the device restores audibility — namely (a) **Acoustic Amplification** (hearing aids), (b) **Bilateral Acoustic-Mechanical Stimulation** (bone conduction / middle-ear implants), and (c) **Direct Electrical Neural Stimulation** (cochlear & brainstem implants). Each approach is selected based on the **type**, **degree**, and **configuration** of hearing loss.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Personal Microphone + Sound Studio" Concept

Think of the human ear as a high-fidelity home theater system:

- The **outer/middle ear** acts like the *speakers and wiring* of the theater.
- The **inner ear (cochlea)** is the *Dolby decoder* that converts electrical signals into nerve impulses.
- The **auditory nerve and brain** are the *audience* that interprets the movie.

When the **speakers break** (outer/middle ear problem → *conductive loss*), we can simply **turn up the volume** — that's a **hearing aid**.

When the **Dolby decoder is broken** (inner ear hair cells damaged → *sensorineural loss*), turning up the volume only creates distortion. In that case, we either:
1. **Bypass the broken decoder** by sending electrical signals directly to the nerve (cochlear implant), or
2. **Use bone conduction** to vibrate the skull and stimulate the inner ear differently (BAHA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight**: The choice of auditory aid is NOT based on audiogram severity alone, but on the **speech recognition score (SRS)** and **etiology of loss**. A patient with severe loss but good speech discrimination may benefit from a hearing aid, while one with poor discrimination despite mild loss may need a cochlear implant.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Mandatory)

| Metric | Definition | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|---|
| **dB HL** | Decibels Hearing Level (referenced to ISO 8253-1 audiometric zero) | 0 dB HL = threshold of normal young adults |
| **SRS** | Speech Recognition Score (percentage of correctly identified words) | $\geq 50\%$ for hearing aid candidacy |
| **Aided SRT** | Speech Reception Threshold with device | Should approach **30 dB HL** for social adequacy |
| **Compression Ratio (CR)** | Ratio of input dB change to output dB change | WDRC: 1.5:1 to 3:1 |
| **Frequency Range** | Bandwidth of amplification | 200 Hz – 8 kHz (preferred) |
| **OSPL90** | Output Sound Pressure Level at 90 dB input | Capped at **118–124 dB SPL** for safety |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Auditory Sensory Aids — The Three Engineering Approaches

### Approach A: Acoustic Amplification (Conventional Hearing Aids)

A **hearing aid** is a wearable electroacoustic system that amplifies sound to compensate for reduced audibility. The general signal flow is:

$$\text{Microphone} \rightarrow \text{Pre-amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Filter Bank} \rightarrow \text{AGC/WDRC} \rightarrow \text{Output Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Receiver (Speaker)}$$

**Why each stage exists:**
- **Microphone**: Electret/dual-MEMS transducer converts acoustic pressure $p(t)$ to voltage $v(t)$ with sensitivity typically $-46$ dBV/Pa.
- **Pre-amplifier**: Boosts weak signal to line level (typical gain 20–30 dB).
- **Filter Bank**: Splits signal into **2 to 48 channels** for frequency-specific gain (mimics the cochlea's tonotopic analysis).
- **AGC / WDRC**: Automatic Gain Control / Wide Dynamic Range Compression — squeezes the 100+ dB range of real-world sound into the patient's reduced dynamic range.
- **Receiver**: Balanced armature or dynamic speaker delivers amplified sound to the ear canal.

**Compression Equation (Knee-Point Compressor)**:

$$G_{out} = \begin{cases} G_{linear} & \text{if } P_{in} < P_{knee} \\ G_{linear} - (CR - 1)(P_{in} - P_{knee}) & \text{if } P_{in} \geq P_{knee} \end{cases}$$

where $P_{knee}$ is the compression knee-point (typically 50 dB SPL) and **CR** is the compression ratio.

**Why this matters**: A patient with recruitment (loudness distortion in sensorineural loss) cannot tolerate loud sounds. WDRC prevents over-amplification while making soft sounds audible — this is the *engineering heart* of digital hearing aids.

### Approach B: Bone-Anchored & Middle-Ear Implants (Bilateral Acoustic-Mechanical)

For **conductive or mixed hearing loss** where the outer/middle ear is damaged but the cochlea is intact, sound is delivered via *vibration of the skull* or *direct mechanical drive of the ossicles*.

- **BAHA (Bone-Anchored Hearing Aid)**: A titanium abutment is osseointegrated into the mastoid bone. The external processor vibrates the skull; the cochlea receives the vibration directly.
- **Vibrant Soundbridge (VSB)**: A floating mass transducer (FMT) is surgically attached to the incus or round window. The FMT converts electrical signals to mechanical vibration, bypassing the eardrum.
- **Bone Bridge**: A semi-implantable device with an external sound processor and an internal magnet-coupled mass vibrating the bone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinguishing Point**: BAHA works because the **cochlear fluid is incompressible** — when the skull vibrates, both oval and round windows move simultaneously, creating a pressure differential that stimulates the basilar membrane. This is known as the **"third window" principle** in audiology.

### Approach C: Direct Electrical Neural Stimulation (Cochlear & ABI)

When hair cells are destroyed but the **auditory nerve fibers remain viable**, electrical current is delivered directly to the nerve via electrodes inserted into the cochlea.

**Cochlear Implant (CI) Subsystems**:
1. **External**: Microphone array → Sound processor → Transmitter coil (via RF link)
2. **Internal**: Receiver-stimulator (implanted under skin) → Electrode array (inserted into scala tympani)
3. **Stimulation strategy**: CIS (Continuous Interleaved Sampling), ACE, or HiRes

**Why CIS works**: The auditory nerve fibers fire in a *phase-locked* manner only up to ~1000 Hz. Above that, the *place principle* takes over — different electrode positions stimulate different characteristic frequency regions. CIS delivers interleaved pulses on different electrodes to prevent channel interaction.

### Approach D: Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs)

These are **non-wearable** or **supplementary** systems that improve the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** in specific environments (classrooms, theaters):

- **FM Systems**: Radio transmission (72–76 MHz) from a microphone worn by the speaker to a receiver worn by the listener.
- **Induction Loop Systems**: Generate a magnetic field that is picked up by the hearing aid's T-coil (telecoil). Standard IEC 60118-4 specifies the magnetic field strength at **100 mA/m** for a -10 dB input.
- **Infrared Systems**: Used in theaters; line-of-sight required.
- **Bluetooth/LE Audio**: Modern wireless streaming directly to hearing aids.

**FM Advantage Formula (dB)**:

$$\text{FM Advantage} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d_{listener-speaker}}{d_{speaker-microphone}}\right)$$

For a typical classroom (listener 3 m away, FM mic at teacher's mouth 0.3 m away), FM advantage = $20 \log_{10}(10) = 20$ dB — a massive SNR improvement.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula/Concept | Symbols | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hearing Loss Degree (PTA) | $L = \frac{H_{500}+H_{1k}+H_{2k}+H_{4k}}{4}$ | dB HL | Classification |
| 2 | Acoustic Gain | $G = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | dB | Amplifier design |
| 3 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \frac{\Delta P_{in}}{\Delta P_{out}}$ | Ratio (1:1 to $\infty$:1) | WDRC |
| 4 | Insertion Gain | $IG = SPL_{aided} - SPL_{unaided}$ | dB | Verification |
| 5 | Real-Ear Measurement | $REUG, REIG, REAG, REOR$ | dB SPL | Fitting |
| 6 | Loudness Summation (Binaural) | $L_{binaural} \approx L_{mono} + 3$ | dB | Bilateral fitting |
| 7 | FM Advantage | $20 \log_{10}\left(d_1/d_2\right)$ | dB | Classroom SNR |
| 8 | Cochlear Stimulation Rate | $f_{stim}$ | 500–5000 pps/ch | CIS strategy |
| 9 | T-coil Sensitivity | H-field | $\geq 25$ mV/m @ 1 mA/m | Loop system |
| 10 | OSPL90 Safety Cap | $OSPL_{90} \leq 118$ dB SPL | dB SPL | Prevent over-amplification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: The compression ratio equation is the single most-tested concept in KTU Part A questions on hearing aids. The **OSPL90** concept appears regularly in fitting questions because exceeding it causes acoustic trauma in patients with recruitment.

## 2.3 Selection Algorithm — Choosing the Right Approach

The clinical decision flow (frequently asked in 14-mark questions) is:

1. **Conductive loss with intact cochlea** → BAHA / Bone Bridge / Conventional hearing aid (if ear canal patent).
2. **Mild-to-moderate sensorineural loss with SRS $\geq 50\%$** → Conventional digital hearing aid with WDRC and directional microphones.
3. **Severe-to-profound sensorineural loss with SRS $< 50\%$** → Cochlear implant candidacy.
4. **Absent auditory nerve (Neurofibromatosis Type 2)** → Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI) — electrodes placed on the cochlear nucleus.
5. **Single-sided deafness (SSD)** → CROS hearing aid, BAHA, or cochlear implant on the deaf side.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Compression Ratio from Input–Output Curve

**Problem**: A patient has a residual dynamic range from 60 dB SPL to 100 dB SPL, but normal hearing requires audibility from 20 dB SPL. The hearing aid must compress the 80 dB input range (20–100 dB SPL) into the patient's 40 dB dynamic range. Calculate the required compression ratio and knee-point.

**Step 1 — Identify the input and output ranges**.

The input acoustic range is:

$$P_{in,max} - P_{in,min} = 100 - 20 = 80 \text{ dB SPL}$$

The patient's tolerable output range is:

$$P_{out,max} - P_{out,min} = 100 - 60 = 40 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the compression ratio**.

The compression ratio is defined as the slope reciprocal in dB terms:

$$CR = \frac{\Delta P_{in}}{\Delta P_{out}} = \frac{80 \text{ dB}}{40 \text{ dB}} = 2:1$$

**Step 3 — Determine the knee-point**.

The knee-point is the input level at which compression begins. To make 20 dB SPL just audible, with linear gain of 40 dB (i.e., output = input + 40 dB), the 60 dB SPL input produces 100 dB SPL output — at the upper limit. So below 60 dB SPL input, linear gain is used; at and above 60 dB SPL, compression with CR = 2:1 begins.

$$P_{knee} = 60 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with the output equation**.

For an input of 80 dB SPL (i.e., 20 dB above knee-point):

$$G_{out} = 40 - (2 - 1)(80 - 60) = 40 - 20 = 20 \text{ dB}$$

So output $= P_{in} + G_{out} = 80 + 20 = 100$ dB SPL ✓ (at the upper comfort limit).

For an input of 100 dB SPL:

$$G_{out} = 40 - (2 - 1)(100 - 60) = 0 \text{ dB}$$

Output = 100 dB SPL ✓.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result**: CR = **2:1** with $P_{knee}$ = **60 dB SPL** is the correct prescription. KTU examiners expect you to show the working — the ratio alone is NOT sufficient for full marks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: FM System SNR Advantage

**Problem**: A classroom measures ambient noise of 60 dB SPL at the listener's position. The teacher's voice is 65 dB SPL at 3 m. An FM microphone is clipped 0.15 m from the teacher's mouth, capturing 78 dB SPL. Calculate the SNR advantage.

**Step 1 — SNR without FM at the listener's ear**.

The listener receives 65 dB SPL (teacher) and 60 dB SPL (noise) — but only when the listener is 3 m away. The unaided SNR is:

$$SNR_{unaided} = 65 - 60 = 5 \text{ dB}$$

This is below the +15 dB SNR required for speech understanding in noise (the famous "Plomp" criterion).

**Step 2 — SNR with FM**.

The FM transmitter captures the teacher's voice at 78 dB SPL (close mic) and routes it directly to the listener's receiver. The teacher's signal at the ear via FM is:

$$S_{FM} = 78 \text{ dB SPL} - \text{transmission losses} \approx 70 \text{ dB SPL}$$

The ambient noise at the listener's ear remains 60 dB SPL (unaffected by FM, as the listener's mic is muted/disabled in FM-only mode).

$$SNR_{FM} = 70 - 60 = 10 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — FM Advantage**.

$$\text{FM Advantage} = SNR_{FM} - SNR_{unaided} = 10 - 5 = 5 \text{ dB}$$

Wait — this is the *total* SNR gain. The geometric distance advantage formula is:

$$\text{FM Advantage (geometric)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d_{listener}}{d_{FM-mic}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{0.15}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(20) \approx 26 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Reconcile**.

The 26 dB geometric advantage represents the *theoretical maximum* if the FM link were perfect. The actual achieved SNR gain (5 dB) is less because the close-mic signal is only 78 dB SPL (not 65 dB) and the receiver has finite output limits. In a typical classroom, the **effective** FM advantage is 15–20 dB — sufficient to overcome the Plomp criterion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip**: When the question asks for FM advantage, you can quote either the **geometric formula (26 dB)** or the **measured SNR difference (5–20 dB)**. Be explicit about which one you are using.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Approaches

| Parameter | Hearing Aid | Bone-Anchored (BAHA) | Cochlear Implant (CI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Indication** | Mild–Severe SNHL or conductive | Conductive / Mixed / SSD | Severe–Profound SNHL |
| **SRS Required** | $\geq 50\%$ | Any (intact cochlea) | $< 50\%$ (poor discrimination) |
| **Pathway** | Acoustic (air conduction) | Bone vibration (mechanical) | Electrical (neural) |
| **Power Source** | Zinc-air / Li-ion | Li-ion | Li-ion + wireless recharge |
| **Surgery Required** | No | Yes (osseointegration) | Yes (mastoidectomy) |
| **Frequency Resolution** | High (analog filter) | Moderate | Limited (8–22 electrodes) |
| **Cost (INR approx)** | 20k – 5L | 4L – 8L | 6L – 25L |
| **Key Risk** | Acoustic feedback, occlusion | Skin infection at abutment | Facial nerve damage, meningitis |
| **Signal Processing** | WDRC, directional mic | Same as HA | CIS / ACE strategy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Wide Dynamic Range Compression" (WDRC) as used in digital hearing aids. Why is it preferred over linear amplification in sensorineural hearing loss?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **Definition (2 marks)**: WDRC is a non-linear signal processing technique in hearing aids that applies different gain values to different input intensity regions, using a low compression threshold (~50 dB SPL) and compression ratio between 1.5:1 to 3:1, thereby compressing the wide dynamic range (~100 dB) of real-world sound into the patient's reduced dynamic range (~40–60 dB).
- **Why preferred (1 mark)**: Sensorineural loss causes *recruitment* — an abnormal growth of loudness. Linear amplification would over-amplify loud sounds, causing discomfort. WDRC ensures soft sounds are audible and loud sounds remain comfortable, matching the patient's reduced dynamic range.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is a Bone-Anchored Hearing Aid (BAHA)? Mention one indication and one contraindication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **Definition (1 mark)**: BAHA is a bone-conduction hearing device consisting of an external sound processor connected to a titanium implant osseointegrated into the mastoid bone, which transmits sound vibrations directly to the cochlea via bone conduction, bypassing the outer and middle ear.
- **Indication (1 mark)**: Chronic suppurative otitis media (CSOM), congenital aural atresia, or single-sided deafness.
- **Contraindication (1 mark)**: Insufficient bone quality/thickness at the mastoid (typically $<3$ mm in children), active skin infection, or age $<5$ years (incomplete skull development).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the three approaches to auditory sensory aids with suitable block diagrams. Discuss the clinical indications for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the working of a cochlear implant. Explain the CIS stimulation strategy and how it differs from simultaneous stimulation. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**The three approaches are:**

**1. Acoustic Amplification (Conventional Hearing Aid)** — Used for **mild to severe sensorineural or conductive hearing loss** with intact ear canal. Working principle: Microphone picks up sound → pre-amplifier → filter bank splits signal into frequency bands → WDRC compressor applies frequency-specific gain matching the audiogram → output amplifier drives receiver. **Indication**: Sensorineural loss with SRS $\geq 50\%$, mild-to-severe conductive loss with patent canal.

**2. Bone-Anchored / Middle-Ear Implant** — Used for **conductive or mixed hearing loss** when the outer/middle ear is damaged but the cochlea is functional. Working principle: External processor converts sound to vibration → titanium abutment transmits vibration to skull bone → cochlear fluid is set into motion via the "third window" effect → basilar membrane is stimulated directly. **Indication**: Chronic otitis media with drainage preventing earmold use, aural atresia, single-sided deafness.

**3. Direct Electrical Stimulation (Cochlear/Brainstem Implant)** — Used for **severe-to-profound sensorineural loss** with poor speech discrimination. Working principle: Microphone captures sound → sound processor extracts envelope information → transmitter coil sends RF signal across skin → implanted receiver-stimulator decodes the signal → current pulses are delivered to electrodes in the cochlea (or cochlear nucleus for ABI) → auditory nerve fibers fire → brain perceives sound. **Indication**: Bilateral severe-to-profound SNHL with SRS $< 50\%$, auditory neuropathy spectrum disorder, NF2 with vestibular schwannoma (for ABI).

**Mark allocation**: 1 mark for naming each approach + 1 mark for working + 1 mark for indications = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for diagram = 7 marks.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Cochlear Implant Working** (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2):
- The **external unit** contains microphones, a sound processor (DSP), and a transmitter coil held magnetically over the implanted receiver.
- The **internal unit** has a receiver-stimulator (hermetically sealed titanium case), a decoding chip, current generators, and an electrode array with 12–22 platinum-iridium contacts inserted into the **scala tympani** of the cochlea, typically inserted 1.5 to 2 turns (≈ 25–30 mm depth).
- The sound processor performs **bandpass filtering** on the audio signal into N frequency bands (matching electrode count), extracts the **envelope** in each band using Hilbert transform or half-wave rectification + low-pass filtering, and modulates pulse amplitude/width.

**CIS (Continuous Interleaved Sampling) Strategy**:
- Pulses are presented on electrodes in a **rapid, non-overlapping, sequential sequence**.
- Each electrode is stimulated for a brief duration ($\sim$25–50 µs) followed by a blanking period.
- The full set of electrodes is cycled at a high rate (e.g., 720 Hz to 1800 Hz per channel for a 12-channel system).
- This **prevents channel interaction** — the simultaneous delivery of current to two electrodes would cause summation of electrical fields and distortion of the place-pitch perception.

**Difference from Simultaneous Stimulation**:
- Simultaneous (analog) strategies deliver current to all electrodes at once, mimicking the analog waveform — higher fidelity but suffers from channel interaction and lower stimulation rates.
- CIS sacrifices temporal fine structure (above ~1 kHz) but provides **clearer place-pitch cues** and is the clinical standard for speech perception in quiet and noise.

**Mark allocation**: 3 marks for CI block diagram and working, 2 marks for CIS strategy, 2 marks for difference from simultaneous.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the architecture of a digital hearing aid. With a block diagram, explain the function of each block. Discuss the role of WDRC in detail with relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle and types of Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs). Derive the SNR advantage of an FM system in a typical classroom. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture of a Digital Hearing Aid** — see block diagram in Section 4.1.

- **Microphone (Electret/MEMS)**: Converts sound pressure variations into electrical voltage, sensitivity typically $-46$ dBV/Pa. Modern devices use **dual microphones** for adaptive directional processing.
- **Pre-amplifier**: Low-noise amplifier (LNA) with gain of 20–30 dB to bring the weak microphone signal to line level.
- **A/D Converter**: Samples at 16–48 kHz with 16–24 bit resolution to capture the full dynamic range of speech.
- **DSP Block**: Performs all programmable functions — filtering, compression, feedback cancellation, noise reduction, directional processing.
- **Filter Bank**: Splits the signal into 2 to 48 channels using a polyphaser filterbank or FFT-based approach. Each channel corresponds to a cochlear critical band.
- **WDRC Compressor**: Applies frequency-specific gain and compression to match the patient's audiogram and residual dynamic range.
- **Output Amplifier and Receiver**: Drives a balanced armature receiver with maximum OSPL90 typically limited to 118–124 dB SPL.

**WDRC Equation** (knee-point at $P_{knee}$, compression ratio CR):

$$G_{out} = \begin{cases} G_{linear} & P_{in} < P_{knee} \\ G_{linear} - (CR-1)(P_{in} - P_{knee}) & P_{in} \geq P_{knee} \end{cases}$$

WDRC uses **multiple knee-points** (e.g., 3:1 above 65 dB SPL, 2:1 between 50–65 dB SPL, expansion below 30 dB SPL) to provide soft sounds with high gain and loud sounds with low gain. Expansion below 30 dB SPL reduces microphone noise and prevents amplification of low-level environmental sounds.

**Mark allocation**: 3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for block functions + 2 marks for WDRC equation and explanation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**ALDs (Assistive Listening Devices)** are supplementary systems that improve SNR in challenging environments where hearing aids alone are insufficient (e.g., classrooms, theaters, places of worship, group conversations).

**Types of ALDs**:
1. **FM Systems**: Radio transmission (72–76 MHz band in India, 200 MHz in some countries) from a clip-on or handheld microphone (transmitter) to a personal receiver coupled to the hearing aid via direct audio input, neckloop, or earhooks.
2. **Induction Loop Systems**: A wire loop around a room generates a magnetic field per IEC 60118-4. The hearing aid's T-coil (telecoil) picks up the field and converts it to audio. Loop strength should be **100 mA/m peak** for a -10 dB reference input.
3. **Infrared Systems**: Line-of-sight IR light modulated by audio; used in theaters and courtrooms; confined to single rooms (no spillover).
4. **Bluetooth / LE Audio**: Modern hearing aids stream audio directly from phones, TVs, and remote microphones using Bluetooth Low Energy Audio (LC3 codec) and Auracast broadcast audio.

**FM SNR Advantage Derivation**:
Consider a classroom of dimensions 8 m × 6 m, with ambient noise $N = 60$ dB SPL. The teacher's voice at 1 m is 70 dB SPL; at the listener's seat (3 m away) the direct voice level follows the inverse square law, dropping by:

$$\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{1}\right) \approx 9.5 \text{ dB}$$

So voice at listener = $70 - 9.5 = 60.5$ dB SPL, giving:

$$SNR_{unaided} = 60.5 - 60 = 0.5 \text{ dB (unacceptable)}$$

With FM mic clipped 0.15 m from the teacher's mouth, the close-mic signal captures 70 dB SPL + $20\log_{10}(1/0.15)$ $\approx 76.5$ dB SPL. The FM link delivers this directly to the receiver:

$$SNR_{FM} = 76.5 - 60 \approx 16.5 \text{ dB}$$

**FM Advantage** = $SNR_{FM} - SNR_{unaided} = 16.5 - 0.5 = 16$ dB, which exceeds the Plomp criterion of +15 dB SNR required for $95\%$ speech intelligibility.

**Mark allocation**: 2 marks for ALD types, 3 marks for FM derivation, 2 marks for SNR criterion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls** (Where students lose marks):
> 1. **Forgetting to include both approaches for ALDs** — students often only mention FM; you must discuss induction loops, IR, and Bluetooth also.
> 2. **Not labelling the audiometric frequencies (500, 1k, 2k, 4k Hz)** in PTA questions — examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Confusing the compression ratio direction** — CR = $\Delta P_{in}/\Delta P_{out}$, NOT the reverse. Writing it backwards loses 1 mark.
> 4. **In CI diagrams, students often draw electrodes outside the cochlea** — they must be in the scala tympani.
> 5. **For Part A, students give 2-mark answers in 3-mark questions** — always include *one extra application/example* to secure the third mark.
> 6. **OSPL90 is sometimes stated as 140 dB SPL** — this is unsafe; the standard cap is 118–124 dB SPL for safety.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Auditory Sensory Aids)**

- ☐ Hearing loss is classified by **PTA** at 500, 1k, 2k, 4k Hz: mild 26–40, moderate 41–55, severe 71–90, profound $>90$ dB HL.
- ☐ The **three engineering approaches** are: (i) Acoustic amplification, (ii) Bone-conduction/mechanical, (iii) Direct electrical neural stimulation.
- ☐ **Digital hearing aid** block: Mic → Pre-amp → A/D → DSP (Filter bank + WDRC) → D/A → Output amp → Receiver.
- ☐ **WDRC** uses multiple knee-points and compression ratios 1.5:1 to 3:1 to fit sound into a patient's reduced dynamic range.
- ☐ **OSPL90** $\leq$ **118–124 dB SPL** is the safety cap; exceeding it causes acoustic trauma.
- ☐ **Compression Ratio** $CR = \Delta P_{in}/\Delta P_{out}$; a 2:1 CR compresses 80 dB input range to 40 dB output.
- ☐ **BAHA** uses the "**third window**" principle — skull vibration creates a cochlear fluid pressure differential.
- ☐ **Indications for BAHA**: chronic otitis media, aural atresia, single-sided deafness.
- ☐ **Cochlear implant** is for severe-to-profound SNHL with SRS $< 50\%$; electrodes are placed in the **scala tympani**.
- ☐ **CIS strategy** delivers **non-overlapping sequential pulses** to prevent channel interaction; standard rate 720–1800 pulses/sec/channel.
- ☐ **ABI (Auditory Brainstem Implant)** is for NF2 patients with damaged auditory nerve; electrodes go on the **cochlear nucleus**.
- ☐ **FM advantage** typically 15–20 dB in classrooms; **geometric maximum** = $20\log_{10}(d_{listener}/d_{mic})$.
- ☐ **T-coil** sensitivity: ≥ 25 mV/m at 1 mA/m loop field; standard loop field = 100 mA/m.
- ☐ **FM band** in India: 72–76 MHz; Bluetooth LE Audio / Auracast is the modern wireless ALD.
- ☐ **Audiometric zero** is defined by **ISO 8253-1**; the audiogram plots threshold in dB HL vs. frequency.
- ☐ **Real-ear measurements** (REUG, REIG, REAG, REOR) verify hearing aid fitting — the **insertion gain** is the key parameter.
- ☐ KTU loves asking: "Compare hearing aid vs. CI", "Why WDRC for SNHL?", "FM advantage derivation", "BAHA working principle".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aids for Auditory Impairments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Auditory Impairment?

**Auditory Impairment** (clinically termed *hearing loss* or *hypoacusis*) is a partial or total inability to hear sounds in one or both ears. It is medically classified by the **World Health Organization (WHO)** based on the degree of hearing loss measured in decibels relative to a normal hearing threshold.

### Formal KTU Definition
> **Auditory impairment** is defined as the average hearing threshold level in the better ear exceeding **25 dB HL** across the standard audiometric speech frequencies (500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, and 4 kHz), impairing the individual's capacity to detect, discriminate, and interpret acoustic signals.

### Classification of Hearing Loss (WHO Standard)

| Grade | Hearing Loss Range (dB HL) | Functional Impact |
|-------|----------------------------|-------------------|
| Normal | 0 – 25 | No impairment |
| Mild | 26 – 40 | Difficulty in soft speech and noisy environments |
| Moderate | 41 – 55 | Difficulty in normal conversational speech |
| Moderately Severe | 56 – 70 | Difficulty even in loud speech |
| Severe | 71 – 90 | Cannot hear conversational speech |
| Profound | ≥ 91 | Cannot hear even amplified speech |

### Types of Hearing Loss (Anatomical Classification)

1. **Conductive Hearing Loss (CHL)** — Caused by obstruction or damage in the *outer* or *middle ear* (e.g., cerumen impaction, otitis media, ossicular chain damage). Sound cannot reach the cochlea efficiently. Usually **medically/surgically treatable** or corrected with conventional hearing aids.

2. **Sensorineural Hearing Loss (SNHL)** — Caused by damage to the *inner ear* (cochlear hair cells) or the *auditory nerve* (CN VIII). Permanent in nature. Often requires **hearing aids** or, in severe cases, **cochlear implants**.

3. **Mixed Hearing Loss** — A combination of both conductive and sensorineural components.

4. **Central Auditory Processing Disorder (CAPD)** — The ear is healthy, but the *brain* fails to process sound signals correctly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Focus:** Module 4 emphasizes the **engineering devices** that compensate for auditory impairment — not the medical/surgical treatment. Expect questions on signal flow, components, specifications, and assistive technology.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Broken Mailbox System:**
> Think of your ear as a *mail delivery system* for sound messages:
> - **Conductive loss** = The *mailbox door* is rusted shut (sound can't get in to be read). Repair the door (surgery) or install a *megaphone* outside (hearing aid).
> - **Sensorineural loss** = The *mailbox door works fine*, but the *letter inside is smudged and unreadable* (damaged hair cells). You need a *digital translator* that re-writes the letter into a new format the brain can read — this is essentially what a **cochlear implant** does.

## 1.3 Categories of Assistive Devices for the Auditory System

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies these into **three main tiers** based on the severity of impairment:

| Tier | Device Category | Best Suited For |
|------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Tier 1** | Conventional Hearing Aids (HA) | Mild to Moderate SNHL/CHL |
| **Tier 2** | Bone-Anchored & Middle Ear Implants | Conductive/Mixed loss, Single-sided deafness |
| **Tier 3** | Cochlear Implants (CI) | Severe to Profound SNHL |
| **Auxiliary** | Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs) | All categories, environmental assistance |

## 1.4 Key Physical & Acoustic Constants (Memorize for KTU Exam)

- **Reference Sound Pressure:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}$ (threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz)
- **Reference Sound Intensity:** $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$
- **Normal conversational speech level:** ~ **60 dB SPL**
- **Threshold of pain:** ~ **120–130 dB SPL**
- **Frequency range of human hearing:** **20 Hz to 20 kHz**
- **Speech frequency range (most important):** **250 Hz to 8 kHz**
- **Audiometric test frequencies:** 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000 Hz

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conventional Hearing Aids — Architecture & Working

A **hearing aid** is a battery-powered electroacoustic device worn on/in the ear that amplifies and modifies sound to compensate for impaired hearing. Modern devices use **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** for selective amplification across frequency bands.

### 2.1.1 Block Diagram of a Modern Digital Hearing Aid

**Stage 1 — Microphone (Transducer):**
Converts acoustic pressure waves into a small electrical analog voltage signal. Modern aids use **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) microphones** with frequency response tailored to speech bands.

**Stage 2 — Pre-amplifier & Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):**
The weak microphone signal (typically in the millivolt range) is amplified and digitized. Sampling rate is usually **16 kHz to 48 kHz**.

**Stage 3 — Digital Signal Processor (DSP):**
This is the *intelligence* of the device. It executes:
- **Frequency-band analysis** (multi-band filterbank)
- **Compression** (Wide Dynamic Range Compression — WDRC)
- **Noise reduction** algorithms
- **Feedback cancellation**
- **Directional microphone processing**

**Stage 4 — Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) & Power Amplifier:**
The processed digital signal is converted back to analog and amplified to a level sufficient to drive the receiver.

**Stage 5 — Receiver (Loudspeaker):**
A miniature balanced armature or dynamic receiver converts the electrical signal back into acoustic energy and delivers it into the ear canal.

**Stage 6 — Battery:**
Modern aids use **zinc-air** (dominant) or rechargeable **lithium-ion** cells. Zinc-air cells operate at **1.35 V nominal**; lithium-ion at **3.7 V**.

### 2.1.2 Types of Hearing Aids (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Abbreviation | Location | Power Level | Indications |
|------|--------------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Behind-The-Ear | **BTE** | Behind the ear, sound delivered via tube + earmold | High power available | Mild to Profound |
| Receiver-In-Canal | **RIC / RITE** | Speaker in canal, body behind ear | Medium-High | Mild to Severe |
| In-The-Ear | **ITE** | Entire shell in concha | Medium | Mild to Severe |
| In-The-Canal | **ITC** | Partially in ear canal | Low-Medium | Mild to Moderate |
| Completely-In-Canal | **CIC** | Deep inside canal | Low | Mild to Moderate |
| Invisible-In-Canal | **IIC** | Near the tympanic membrane | Very Low | Mild to Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Memory Hook:** As you move from BTE → IIC, the **size decreases** but the **battery capacity, power output, and feature-set also decrease**. BTE remains the most powerful form factor.

### 2.1.3 Critical Specifications of a Hearing Aid

- **Peak Gain:** Typically **30 dB to 70 dB** (difference between input and output SPL).
- **Maximum Power Output (MPO):** Limits output to **120–135 dB SPL** to prevent further hearing damage.
- **Frequency Range:** **200 Hz to 8 kHz** (covers speech band).
- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** Should be **< 5%**, ideally **< 3%**.
- **Equivalent Input Noise (EIN):** Should be **< 25 dB SPL** for quiet operation.
- **Battery Life:** Typically **5 to 14 days** (zinc-air 312/13 cells).

---

## 2.2 The Decibel (dB) System — Foundation for All Hearing Calculations

The decibel is a **logarithmic unit** because human hearing perceives sound *intensity* and *pressure* on a logarithmic scale. This is the single most important math concept for KTU auditory module problems.

### 2.2.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL)

The SPL expresses the magnitude of acoustic pressure relative to the threshold of hearing:

$$\text{SPL (dB)} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)$$

where:
- $p$ = measured RMS sound pressure (Pa)
- $p_0$ = reference pressure $= 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}$

### 2.2.2 Sound Intensity Level (SIL)

$$\text{SIL (dB)} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

where:
- $I$ = sound intensity (W/m²)
- $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor 20 for pressure but 10 for intensity?** Because intensity is proportional to the *square* of pressure ($I \propto p^2$), the $20\log$ formula derives from $10\log(p^2/p_0^2) = 20\log(p/p_0)$.

### 2.2.3 Hearing Level (HL) vs. SPL

The **HL scale** is referenced to **RETSPL** (Reference Equivalent Threshold Sound Pressure Level) — a frequency-dependent average normal-hearing threshold. This is the scale used on audiograms.

$$\text{HL (dB HL)} = \text{SPL (dB SPL)} - \text{RETSPL (dB SPL)}$$

| Frequency (Hz) | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 | 8000 |
|----------------|-----|-----|------|------|------|------|
| RETSPL (dB SPL) | 25.5 | 13.5 | 7.5 | 9.0 | 12.0 | 15.5 |

### 2.2.4 Power Gain / Amplifier Gain

The gain ($G$) of a hearing aid amplifier:

$$G_{\text{dB}} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$$

### 2.2.5 Frequency Response & Bandwidth

The bandwidth of an amplifier is defined between the **-3 dB points** (half-power frequencies):

$$f_{\text{BW}} = f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print/Memorize this table — every formula here is fair game in the KTU ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Reference | Application |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $\text{SPL} = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | Convert pressure to dB |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $\text{SIL} = 10\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ | Intensity conversion |
| 3 | Hearing Level | $\text{HL} = \text{SPL} - \text{RETSPL}$ | dB HL (audiogram) | Audiometry interpretation |
| 4 | Voltage Gain | $G_v = 20\log_{10}(V_{\text{out}}/V_{\text{in}})$ | dB | Amplifier specification |
| 5 | Power Gain | $G_p = 10\log_{10}(P_{\text{out}}/P_{\text{in}})$ | dB | Amplifier specification |
| 6 | Bandwidth | $\text{BW} = f_H - f_L$ | Hz | Frequency response |
| 7 | Quality Factor (filter) | $Q = f_0/\Delta f$ | dimensionless | Filter selectivity |
| 8 | Wavelength | $\lambda = v/f$ | $v = 343\ \text{m/s}$ in air | Acoustic design |
| 9 | Sound Pressure Doubling | $\Delta L = 6\ \text{dB}$ per doubling of $p$ | dB | Loudness comparison |
| 10 | Power Doubling | $\Delta L = 3\ \text{dB}$ per doubling of $P$ | dB | Amplifier design |

---

## 2.4 Cochlear Implant (CI) — The Bionic Ear

The **cochlear implant** is the most sophisticated auditory prosthetic, bypassing damaged hair cells and directly stimulating the **auditory nerve (CN VIII)** with electrical pulses.

### 2.4.1 Architecture — External and Internal Components

**External Components (Worn externally):**
1. **Microphone(s):** Capture environmental sound.
2. **Sound Processor:** A behind-the-ear or body-worn unit that digitizes sound and applies bandpass filtering (typically 12–24 channel filterbank).
3. **Transmitter Coil:** Sends encoded signals + power via **RF (Radio Frequency) inductive link** (typically at **5 MHz**) across the skin to the internal receiver.

**Internal Components (Surgically implanted):**
4. **Receiver-Stimulator:** Decodes the RF signal and produces biphasic current pulses.
5. **Electrode Array:** A thin, flexible silicone carrier with **12 to 22 electrode contacts** inserted into the **scala tympani** of the cochlea. Each electrode stimulates a different tonotopic region (apex = low frequency, base = high frequency).

### 2.4.2 Signal Processing Strategy — The "How It Works"

Modern CIs use strategies like **CIS (Continuous Interleaved Sampling)** or **ACE (Advanced Combination Encoder)**:

1. Sound is split into $N$ frequency bands using a **bandpass filterbank**.
2. The envelope of each band is extracted via **half-wave rectification + low-pass filtering**.
3. Envelopes are compressed to fit the patient's electrical dynamic range (~10–20 dB, much smaller than acoustic range).
4. Pulses are delivered to electrodes in a non-overlapping (interleaved) sequence to prevent channel interaction.

### 2.4.3 CI vs. Hearing Aid — KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | Hearing Aid | Cochlear Implant |
|---------|-------------|------------------|
| Mechanism | Acoustic amplification | Direct electrical stimulation of auditory nerve |
| Indication | Mild to Severe loss | Severe to Profound SNHL |
| Surgery required? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Bypasses hair cells? | No | **Yes** |
| Frequency resolution | Analog, broadband | Multi-channel electrode array |
| Power source | Replaceable battery | External battery + implanted electronics |
| Cost | Lower | Significantly higher |

---

## 2.5 Bone-Anchored Hearing Aids (BAHA) and Middle Ear Implants

**BAHA:** Uses a **titanium osseointegrated abutment** screwed into the **mastoid bone** behind the ear. Sound processor vibrations are transmitted *directly through bone* to the cochlea, bypassing the outer/middle ear entirely. Best for chronic otitis media, atresia, and single-sided deafness.

**Middle Ear Implants (MEI):** A transducer attached to the ossicles or round window mechanically vibrates the middle ear structures, providing better fidelity than conventional aids in some cases.

## 2.6 Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs)

These supplement hearing aids in challenging situations:
- **Induction Loop Systems (Hearing Loops):** Use electromagnetic induction (per IEC 60118-4) to transmit audio directly to the hearing aid's T-coil (telecoil).
- **FM Systems:** Radio-frequency transmission (typically 72–76 MHz in many countries) from a microphone worn by the speaker to a receiver on the listener.
- **Infrared Systems:** Used in theaters/courts.
- **Bluetooth/Wi-Fi Streaming:** Modern digital aids support direct audio streaming.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Decibel as a Logarithmic Ratio

**Problem Statement:** Show mathematically why the human ear uses a logarithmic scale and derive the SPL formula from first principles.

### Step 1 — Define the Threshold of Hearing

The faintest sound a healthy young adult can detect has a sound pressure of $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}$. This is our reference.

### Step 2 — Define the Pain Threshold

The threshold of pain is approximately $p_{\text{pain}} = 20\ \text{Pa}$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Linear Ratio

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{p_{\text{pain}}}{p_0} = \frac{20}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 1{,}000{,}000 = 10^6$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Range Problem

A linear scale would require representing values across 7 orders of magnitude. Plotting $10^6$ on a linear graph is impractical. The ear, however, responds *logarithmically* (Weber–Fechner law: perceived intensity $\propto \log$ of stimulus).

### Step 5 — Apply Logarithm to Compress the Range

Using the common logarithm:

$$\log_{10}(10^6) = 6$$

This compresses the million-fold range into a manageable 6-unit number.

### Step 6 — Multiply by 20 (for pressure) and define as dB SPL

We define the Sound Pressure Level as:

$$\boxed{\text{SPL (dB)} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)}$$

**Verification:**
- At $p = p_0$: SPL $= 20\log_{10}(1) = 20 \times 0 = 0$ dB ✓ (threshold of hearing)
- At $p = p_{\text{pain}} = 20$ Pa: SPL $= 20\log_{10}(20 / 2 \times 10^{-5}) = 20\log_{10}(10^6) = 20 \times 6 = 120$ dB ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Doubling Produces 6 dB Increase

**Problem:** Prove that doubling the sound pressure yields a +6 dB change.

### Step 1 — Setup

Let $p_2 = 2p_1$. Compute $\Delta L = L_2 - L_1$.

### Step 2 — Write Both Levels

$$L_1 = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_1}{p_0}\right), \qquad L_2 = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2p_1}{p_0}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Subtract

$$\Delta L = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2p_1}{p_0}\right) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_1}{p_0}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Use Logarithm Subtraction Rule

Using $\log A - \log B = \log(A/B)$:

$$\Delta L = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2p_1/p_0}{p_1/p_0}\right) = 20\log_{10}(2)$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate

$$\Delta L = 20 \times 0.30103 = 6.0206 \approx 6\ \text{dB}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta L = 6\ \text{dB} \text{ for pressure doubling}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 6 dB increase corresponds to a *doubling of perceived loudness only in a very loose sense*. In strict psychoacoustics, a **10 dB increase** is generally required for a perceived "doubling of loudness." However, in most KTU problems, **+6 dB per pressure doubling** is the expected answer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Doubling Produces 3 dB Increase

**Problem:** Show that doubling the acoustic *power* (or electrical power) yields +3 dB.

### Step 1 — Setup

Let $P_2 = 2P_1$.

### Step 2 — Apply Power Formula

$$G_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = 10\log_{10}(2)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$G_{\text{dB}} = 10 \times 0.30103 = 3.0103 \approx 3\ \text{dB}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta L = 3\ \text{dB} \text{ for power doubling}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3 dB for power but 6 dB for pressure?** Because $I \propto p^2$, doubling $p$ quadruples $I$, and $10\log_{10}(4) \approx 6$ dB. So pressure doubling → power quadrupling → 6 dB.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Converting HL to SPL for a Given Frequency

**Problem:** A patient has a hearing threshold of **40 dB HL at 1 kHz**. What sound pressure level (in dB SPL) must be presented for this patient to just detect the tone?

### Step 1 — Recall the RETSPL at 1 kHz

From the table in Section 2.2.3: RETSPL at 1000 Hz $= 7.5$ dB SPL.

### Step 2 — Apply the HL-to-SPL Formula

$$\text{SPL} = \text{HL} + \text{RETSPL}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$\text{SPL} = 40 + 7.5 = 47.5\ \text{dB SPL}$$

### Step 4 — Verification by Definition

By definition, 0 dB HL = 7.5 dB SPL at 1 kHz. So 40 dB HL above this normal threshold = $7.5 + 40 = 47.5$ dB SPL. ✓

$$\boxed{\text{Required SPL} = 47.5\ \text{dB SPL at 1 kHz}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Hearing Aid Gain Selection

**Problem:** A patient has a 50 dB HL hearing loss at 2 kHz. The audiologist wants the amplified speech to reach the patient's **MCL (Most Comfortable Level)** of **65 dB SPL**. The hearing aid microphone picks up speech at 50 dB SPL. Calculate the **required acoustic gain**.

### Step 1 — Identify Known Values

- Patient threshold: $50\ \text{dB HL at 2 kHz}$
- Target output: $65\ \text{dB SPL at 2 kHz}$
- Input speech level: $50\ \text{dB SPL}$

### Step 2 — Compute Target SPL in HL

RETSPL at 2000 Hz $= 9.0$ dB SPL.

$$\text{Target HL} = 65 - 9.0 = 56\ \text{dB HL}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Hearing Loss in dB

The patient needs the sound to be amplified so that what would normally be 0 dB HL (7.5 dB SPL at 1 kHz equivalent) is instead 50 dB HL stronger. More directly, compute the gain needed to go from input SPL to target output SPL:

$$G = \text{Output SPL} - \text{Input SPL} = 65 - 50 = 15\ \text{dB}$$

### Step 4 — Account for Earmold/Ear Canal Resonance

Real earmolds provide a natural **RESR (Real-Ear-to-Coupler Difference)** of about +5 dB at 2 kHz. So the **functional gain** provided by the ear itself is ~5 dB, reducing the required electronic gain.

$$G_{\text{electronic}} = 15 - 5 = 10\ \text{dB}$$

$$\boxed{G_{\text{electronic}} \approx 10\ \text{dB at 2 kHz}}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** The acoustic pressure of a busy street is measured as $p = 0.2\ \text{Pa}$. A hearing aid microphone has a sensitivity of $-46\ \text{dBV/Pa}$ and an amplifier with a gain of 40 dB. Determine:
1. The SPL of the street noise in dB.
2. The microphone output voltage.
3. The amplifier output voltage (in dBV and V).

### Solution

**Part (a) — SPL Calculation:**

$$\text{SPL} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20\log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \times 4 = 80\ \text{dB SPL}$$

**Part (b) — Microphone Output:**

Microphone sensitivity in linear form:
$$S = 10^{(-46/20)} = 10^{-2.3} = 0.00501\ \text{V/Pa} \approx 5\ \text{mV/Pa}$$

$$V_{\text{mic}} = S \times p = 0.00501 \times 0.2 = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 1.002\ \text{mV}$$

In dBV:
$$V_{\text{mic,dBV}} = 20\log_{10}(1.002 \times 10^{-3}) = -59.99 \approx -60\ \text{dBV}$$

**Part (c) — Amplifier Output:**

$$V_{\text{out,dBV}} = V_{\text{mic,dBV}} + G = -60 + 40 = -20\ \text{dBV}$$

Converting back to volts:
$$V_{\text{out}} = 10^{(-20/20)} = 10^{-1} = 0.1\ \text{V} = 100\ \text{mV}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SPL} = 80\ \text{dB},\quad V_{\text{mic}} \approx 1\ \text{mV},\quad V_{\text{out}} = 0.1\ \text{V (or 100 mV)}}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 4]**
**Differentiate between conductive and sensorineural hearing loss. Mention one device suitable for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Conductive Hearing Loss | Sensorineural Hearing Loss |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Site of Lesion** | Outer or middle ear | Inner ear (cochlea) or auditory nerve |
| **Common Causes** | Wax impaction, otitis media, ossicular damage | Aging (presbycusis), noise exposure, ototoxic drugs, genetic |
| **Pathology** | Sound cannot reach cochlea efficiently | Hair cells or nerve fibers damaged |
| **Reversibility** | Often reversible (medical/surgical) | Usually permanent |
| **Bone Conduction Audiogram** | Normal | Abnormal |
| **Air Conduction Audiogram** | Abnormal (elevated) | Abnormal (elevated) |
| **Suitable Device** | Conventional **Hearing Aid** (BTE with high gain) or **BAHA** for chronic cases | **Hearing Aid** (mild-mod) or **Cochlear Implant** (severe-profound) |

**1 mark** for the definition/difference, **1 mark** for causes/site, **1 mark** for the appropriate device.

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 4]**
**List any three specifications of a modern digital hearing aid and state their typical values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Peak Gain:** 30 to 70 dB — defines the maximum amplification the device can provide. *(1 mark)*
2. **Maximum Power Output (MPO):** 120 to 135 dB SPL — sets an upper limit to prevent further auditory damage from over-amplification. *(1 mark)*
3. **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** Less than 5% (ideally < 3%) — ensures clean sound reproduction. *(1 mark)*

*(Optional additional specifications that may earn a bonus point: Frequency range 200 Hz – 8 kHz, EIN < 25 dB SPL, Battery life 5–14 days.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a digital hearing aid. List its key specifications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram & Signal Flow (4 marks):**

The major functional blocks in a digital hearing aid are:

1. **Microphone (MEMS type)** — Converts acoustic waves into a small electrical signal (typically a few mV). Has flat frequency response from ~100 Hz to 10 kHz. *(0.5 mark)*

2. **Pre-amplifier** — A low-noise operational amplifier (e.g., AD8xxx series) that boosts the microphone signal to ADC-compatible levels (~0.5–1 V peak-to-peak). *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Samples the signal at 16–48 kHz with 16–24-bit resolution. The Nyquist theorem ensures speech band (< 8 kHz) is captured faithfully. *(0.5 mark)*

4. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — The "brain" of the hearing aid. Performs:
   - Bandpass filterbank analysis (typically 4–16 channels).
   - **Wide Dynamic Range Compression (WDRC):** Compresses the 60+ dB range of natural sound into the patient's smaller dynamic range.
   - **Adaptive noise reduction.**
   - **Feedback cancellation** (important in BTE aids where microphone and receiver are close).
   - **Directional processing** for noisy environments. *(1 mark)*

5. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) & Power Amplifier** — Class-D amplifiers are preferred for their > 90% efficiency. *(0.5 mark)*

6. **Receiver (Speaker)** — A balanced armature receiver drives the sound through the earmold into the ear canal. *(0.5 mark)*

7. **Battery** — Typically zinc-air (1.35 V) cell; size 312, 13, or 675 depending on form factor. *(0.5 mark)*

**Key Specifications (3 marks):**

| Specification | Typical Value | Significance |
|---------------|---------------|--------------|
| Peak Gain | 30 – 70 dB | Maximum amplification across frequency |
| Maximum Power Output | 120 – 135 dB SPL | Prevents over-amplification damage |
| Frequency Range | 200 Hz – 8 kHz | Covers speech band |
| THD | < 5% (ideally < 3%) | Sound quality |
| EIN (Equivalent Input Noise) | < 25 dB SPL | Self-noise floor |
| Battery Life | 5 – 14 days | Operational endurance |
| Channels (DSP) | 4 – 48 bands | Frequency resolution for fitting |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the principle of operation of a cochlear implant. Compare it with a conventional hearing aid.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Operation (4 marks):**

A **cochlear implant (CI)** is a neuroprosthetic device that bypasses damaged cochlear hair cells and directly stimulates the auditory nerve fibers with patterned electrical pulses.

**External Part:**
- Microphone captures sound.
- **Sound Processor** (worn BTE or off-the-ear) uses a bandpass filterbank (12–24 channels) to split the audio spectrum.
- The envelope of each band is extracted using half-wave rectification and low-pass filtering (typically 200–400 Hz cutoff).
- The envelopes are mapped (logarithmically compressed) to fit the patient's narrow electrical dynamic range.
- The modulated data is transmitted across the skin via an **RF inductive link** at ~5 MHz.

**Internal Part:**
- The **receiver-stimulator** (hermetically sealed in titanium) decodes the RF signal and generates biphasic current pulses.
- These pulses are delivered to the **electrode array** (12–22 platinum-iridium contacts) inserted into the **scala tympani** of the cochlea.
- Each electrode stimulates a different **tonotopic location**: apical electrodes → low frequency perception, basal electrodes → high frequency perception.
- The auditory nerve fibers (CN VIII) fire, sending signals to the brain that are interpreted as sound.

**Cochlear Implant vs. Hearing Aid (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hearing Aid | Cochlear Implant |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Acoustic amplification | Direct electrical stimulation of CN VIII |
| **Indication** | Mild–Severe hearing loss | Severe–Profound SNHL |
| **Bypasses damaged hair cells?** | No | Yes |
| **Surgery required?** | No | Yes (mastoidectomy + cochleostomy) |
| **Spectral resolution** | Broadband, analog | Multi-channel discrete (12–22) |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Very high |
| **Recovery / Rehabilitation** | Minimal fitting | Months of AVT (Auditory-Verbal Therapy) |
| **Power source** | External replaceable battery | External battery + implanted electronics |

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a cochlear implant. List the different sound coding strategies used.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

*(Draw the diagram from Section 4.2 of these notes — Microphone → Sound Processor → Transmitter Coil ⟶ [Skin RF Link] ⟶ Receiver Coil → Stimulator ASIC → Electrode Array → Auditory Nerve)*

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

1. Sound is captured by the microphone and analyzed by the external sound processor.
2. The processor uses a bank of bandpass filters to split sound into multiple frequency bands.
3. Envelope information of each band is extracted.
4. The envelope amplitude is mapped to the patient's electrical dynamic range via logarithmic compression.
5. Pulses are generated and sent via RF coupling to the implanted receiver.
6. The receiver-stimulator delivers biphasic current pulses to corresponding electrodes in the cochlea.
7. Auditory nerve fibers are activated according to the tonotopic arrangement, producing the sensation of sound.

**Sound Coding Strategies (2 marks):**

| Strategy | Full Form | Key Feature |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **CIS** | Continuous Interleaved Sampling | Fixed-rate pulsatile stimulation; non-overlapping channels reduce interaction |
| **ACE** | Advanced Combination Encoder | Selects only the channels with highest amplitude (typically 8 of 22 max); more efficient |
| **SPEAK** | Spectral Peak | Similar to ACE, developed by Cochlear Ltd. |
| **HiRes** | High Resolution | Used by Advanced Bionics; high pulse rates up to ~5000 pps/channel |
| **FSP / FS4** | Fine Structure Processing | Preserves fine structure (phase info) for better music/pitch perception |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the Bone-Anchored Hearing Aid (BAHA) system. What is the role of the telecoil and induction loop in modern hearing aids?**

**Model Solution:**

**BAHA System (3.5 marks):**

The **Bone-Anchored Hearing Aid (BAHA)** is a specialized auditory prosthesis designed for patients with **chronic conductive hearing loss, mixed loss, or single-sided deafness (SSD)** who cannot use conventional air-conduction hearing aids.

**Components:**

1. **Titanium Implant (Fixture):** A small screw (typically 3–4 mm long) surgically placed into the **mastoid bone** behind the ear. Over 3–6 months, it undergoes **osseointegration** — direct bone-to-metal bonding.
2. **Abutment:** Protrudes through the skin and connects the implant to the external sound processor.
3. **External Sound Processor:** A small BTE-style device that snaps onto the abutment via a magnetic or mechanical coupling.
4. **Microphone, DSP, and Vibrator:** Inside the processor; the vibrator converts the processed sound into **mechanical vibrations**.

**Working Principle:**
- The sound processor picks up environmental sound.
- It processes (amplifies, frequency-shapes) the signal via DSP.
- The processed signal drives a **bone vibrator** that transmits vibrations through the abutment into the skull.
- These vibrations travel via **bone conduction** to **both cochleae** directly, completely **bypassing the outer and middle ear**.
- This is particularly beneficial when the outer/middle ear is damaged or chronically infected (e.g., chronic suppurative otitis media).

**Advantages over Conventional HA:**
- No occlusion of the ear canal (comfortable for chronic ear infections).
- Excellent for SSD — sound from the deaf side is routed to the functioning cochlea via bone conduction.
- Cosmetic options available.

**Telecoil (T-Coil) and Induction Loop (3.5 marks):**

A **telecoil (T-coil)** is a small inductive coil of wire inside the hearing aid that acts as a wireless receiver of magnetic fields. When activated, it picks up the audio signal transmitted as a fluctuating magnetic field rather than as acoustic sound.

**Induction Loop System (Hearing Loop):**
1. A wire is installed around the perimeter of a room (auditorium, classroom, theater, place of worship, bank counter).
2. This wire is driven by a **loop amplifier** connected to the venue's sound system (microphone, TV, PA).
3. The amplifier drives an **audio-modulated alternating current** through the loop, generating a corresponding **magnetic field** within the looped area.
4. A hearing aid user simply switches to the **"T" (telecoil) program** to receive the audio directly into their hearing aid, **bypassing environmental noise and reverberation**.

**Standards:** The system follows the **IEC 60118-4** international standard for the magnetic field strength of hearing loop systems (typically 100 mA/m RMS for the long-time average signal).

**Benefits:**
- Eliminates background noise.
- Eliminates the distance problem (sound doesn't decay with distance from speaker).
- Compatible with virtually all modern hearing aids (T-coil is mandated in many countries).
- Low cost to install and use.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE (Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Confusing dB SPL and dB HL.** Students write "patient has 50 dB SPL hearing loss" — this is **incorrect**. The audiogram is in **dB HL**. Mark loss: 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting RETSPL values.** When converting HL to SPL, students forget to add the RETSPL. Always state: **SPL = HL + RETSPL**.
>
> 3. **Using 10 log for pressure and 20 log for power.** This is reversed. Remember: **20 log for pressure and voltage**; **10 log for power and intensity**.
>
> 4. **Confusing cochlear implant placement.** A common error is writing that the electrode array is in the "middle ear." It is inserted into the **scala tympani** of the **cochlea** in the inner ear.
>
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows showing signal flow.** Arrows are **mandatory** in KTU board exam answers. A diagram without flow direction loses 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 6. **Missing the difference between microphone sensitivity units.** `dBV/Pa` is a sensitivity unit (e.g., **-46 dBV/Pa**), not a voltage. Remember: $V = p \times 10^{S/20}$.
>
> 7. **Writing "BAHA is for sensorineural loss."** BAHA is primarily for **conductive/mixed loss** and **single-sided deafness** — not the first choice for pure SNHL.
>
> 8. **Skipping the comparison table in cochlear implant vs HA questions.** The examiner *always* expects a comparison — failing to provide one loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Aids for Auditory Impairments**

### 🎯 Core Concepts
- [ ] **Hearing loss classification:** Conductive (outer/middle), Sensorineural (inner/nerve), Mixed, Central
- [ ] **WHO grading:** Normal (≤25), Mild (26–40), Moderate (41–55), Moderately Severe (56–70), Severe (71–90), Profound (≥91)
- [ ] **Reference pressure:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$; Reference intensity: $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$
- [ ] **SPL formula:** $\text{SPL} = 20\log(p/p_0)$; **SIL formula:** $\text{SIL} = 10\log(I/I_0)$
- [ ] **HL = SPL − RETSPL**; RETSPL at 1 kHz = 7.5 dB SPL
- [ ] **Pressure doubling → +6 dB; Power doubling → +3 dB**

### 🎧 Hearing Aids
- [ ] **Form factors:** BTE > RIC > ITE > ITC > CIC > IIC (descending power, ascending cosmetics)
- [ ] **Key specs:** Peak gain 30–70 dB; MPO 120–135 dB SPL; THD < 5%; EIN < 25 dB SPL
- [ ] **Block chain:** Mic → Pre-amp → ADC → DSP → DAC → Power Amp → Receiver → Earmold → Cochlea
- [ ] **WDRC** = Wide Dynamic Range Compression (key DSP function)
- [ ] **Battery:** Zinc-air (1.35 V) or Li-ion (3.7 V)

### 🦴 Bone-Anchored Hearing Aid (BAHA)
- [ ] **Osseointegrated titanium fixture** in mastoid bone
- [ ] **Bypasses outer/middle ear** — direct bone conduction to cochlea
- [ ] **Best for:** Chronic conductive loss, mixed loss, **single-sided deafness (SSD)**

### 🦾 Cochlear Implant
- [ ] **External:** Mic + Sound Processor + Transmitter Coil
- [ ] **Internal (implanted):** Receiver Coil + Stimulator ASIC + Electrode Array
- [ ] **Electrode array:** 12–22 contacts in **scala tympani**
- [ ] **Tonotopic mapping:** Apex = low freq, Base = high freq
- [ ] **Coding strategies:** CIS, ACE, SPEAK, HiRes
- [ ] **Bypasses damaged hair cells**, stimulates **CN VIII directly**

### 📡 Assistive Listening Devices
- [ ] **Induction loop / Telecoil (T-coil):** IEC 60118-4 standard
- [ ] **FM systems:** 72–76 MHz radio
- [ ] **Bluetooth / Wi-Fi streaming:** Modern digital aids

### 🔢 KTU Numerical Problem Templates
- [ ] dB ↔ linear conversions (pressure, voltage, power)
- [ ] SPL ↔ HL conversions (remember RETSPL)
- [ ] Microphone output voltage: $V = p \times 10^{S/20}$
- [ ] Hearing aid gain: $G = V_{\text{out,dBV}} - V_{\text{in,dBV}}$

### 🎓 Mnemonics to Remember
- **"BITE-CIC"** (form factors from large to small): **B**TE, **I**TE, **T**C, **C**IC
- **"20 for Pressure, 10 for Power"** (dB formulas)
- **"ACE beats CIS for speed; HiRes wins for music"** (CI strategy tradeoffs)

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Aids for Auditory Impairments**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | Course: PEBRT745 — Assistive Medical Devices*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Face-to-Face Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Face-to-Face Communication # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Face-to-Face (F2F) Communication**, in the context of Assistive Medical Devices, refers to a subset of *Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)* systems and technologies that facilitate **direct, co-present, synchronous interpersonal communication** between a person with a communication impairment and an interlocutor who is physically present in the same environment. The term specifically excludes remote, asynchronous, or text-mediated digital messaging and instead emphasizes **real-time, in-person, multimodal message exchange** through aided (device-based) or unaided (body-based) strategies. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):** > Face-to-Face Communication assistive devices are engineered clinical systems that restore, supplement, or replace verbal–vocal communication during direct human interaction. They are categorized under the **Communication Domain of Assistive Technology (AT)**, as classified in the **ISO 9999:2022** standard (Class 22 — *Communication, information, and signaling aids*). Mathematically, the communicative competence of an F2F system is bounded by Shannon's classical channel-capacity formulation: $$ C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits/second]} $$ where $C$ is the channel capacity, $B$ is the bandwidth of the human–machine interface (Hz), $S$ is the signal power generated by the user's intent channel, and $N$ is the ambient noise plus physiological noise. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Remote Translator" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — "Two Friends and a Translator"** > Imagine two friends sitting at a café. One is fluent in English, the other can only move one finger. A *Face-to-Face Communication device* acts like a **personal translator** sitting between them.
