The translator watches every tiny signal from Friend 1 (a finger tap, an eye blink, a muscle twitch) and instantly converts it into a voice, a word on a screen, or a symbol that Friend 2 can understand — **all while both are sitting across the same table, in real time, looking at each other.** The device does not "speak on behalf of the user" in the abstract; it does so *during* a live social encounter. This is the essence of F2F AAC. ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 The KTU 2024 scheme frames Face-to-Face Communication as a *high-weightage* clinical-engineering topic under Module 4 (Communication and Mobility Aids). It directly tests the following Course Outcomes (COs): - **CO4 (Module 4):** *Design and evaluate assistive communication devices for individuals with speech, language, and motor impairments.* - **CO5 (Module 5 cross-link):** *Apply ergonomic, ethical, and clinical-validation principles in AT design.* > [!TIP] > **Clinical Prevalence Statistic (for exam context):** Approximately **1.3% of the global population** (≈97 million people) has a significant communication disability (WHO, 2023). In India, cerebral palsy, ALS, autism spectrum disorder, and post-stroke aphasia account for the largest demand pools for F2F AAC systems. ## 1.4 Classification Snapshot The KTU 2024 module groups F2F communication aids into **two principal families**: | Family | Sub-Type | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Unaided F2F** | Body-based, no device | Gestures, manual sign language, finger-spelling, eye-pointing | | **Aided F2F** | Low-tech | Picture boards, alphabet charts, PECS (Picture Exchange Communication System) | | **Aided F2F** | High-tech | Speech-generating devices (SGDs), eye-gaze AAC, brain–computer interfaces | The rest of this module focuses predominantly on **Aided High-Tech F2F systems** because they constitute the engineering-rich portion of the syllabus. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Block Model of a F2F Communication System A modern F2F assistive communicator can be modeled as a **five-stage signal-processing chain**, conceptually identical to any classical communication system: 1. **Intent Acquisition Stage** — A sensor (EMG electrode, eye-tracker camera, EEG cap, switch, or microphone) captures a physiological or behavioral correlate of the user's communicative intent. 2. **Encoding / Symbol-Selection Stage** — The raw sensor signal is mapped, through an *encoding scheme*, to a discrete symbol from a finite alphabet $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_N\}$. 3. **Decoding / Prediction Stage** — Statistical language models (bigram, trigram, or modern transformer-based word predictors) estimate the most probable next symbol. 4. **Output Synthesis Stage** — A text-to-speech (TTS) engine, an LCD/LED display, or a refreshable Braille cell produces a human-perceivable signal. 5. **Feedback Stage** — The user sees/hears the output, allowing closed-loop correction (latency-dependent). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Trade-off Insight:** > Every additional stage in the chain adds **latency** but improves **symbol accuracy**. The golden metric is the **Effective Communication Rate (ECR)**, defined later in §3. High-end clinical devices target an ECR between **15–30 words per minute (WPM)**, while conversational natural speech averages **130–200 WPM**. ## 2.2 Encoding Schemes — The Heart of F2F AAC The encoding scheme determines *how* a user selects symbols. KTU 2024 emphasizes four schemes: ### 2.2.1 Single-Switch Scanning A single switch (e.g., a sip-and-puff tube, a blink sensor) is used. A cursor highlights rows/columns of a symbol grid in a pre-defined order; the user activates the switch when the desired row/column is highlighted. Selection time is governed by **Fitts's Law** (see §3). ### 2.2.2 Row-Column (Matrix) Scanning A $M \times N$ grid is scanned row-by-row; the user activates the switch once to select a row, then the device scans within that row to select a column. ### 2.2.3 Directed Selection A direct-pointing input (finger, stylus, eye-gaze, head-tracker) selects a cell. Latency is lowest, but motor demand is highest. ### 2.2.4 Predictive Encoding The system dynamically rearranges the symbol grid so that the most probable next symbol occupies the *easiest-to-reach* (Goldilocks) cell. This is the core of **Dasher**, **Predictive AAC**, and modern AI-augmented AAC apps. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the following table for ESE & internal assessments.** | # | Formula / Law | Expression | Variables & Units | Used For | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | $B$ [Hz], $S/N$ [dimensionless] | Upper bound on AAC information transfer | | 2 | Fitts's Law (selection time) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ | $T$ [s], $D$ [m], $W$ [m], $a,b$ [s] | Predicting selection latency in grid-based AAC | | 3 | Index of Difficulty (ID) | $ID = \log_2(D/W + 1)$ | bits | Quantifies motor task complexity | | 4 | Effective Communication Rate | $ECR = \frac{60 \cdot N_{sym}}{T_{tot} \cdot \bar{n}_{sym}}$ | WPM | Clinical benchmark of AAC performance | | 5 | Hit / Selection Accuracy | $A = \frac{N_{corr}}{N_{tot}} \times 100\%$ | percent | Device usability metric | | 6 | Throughput (TP) | $TP = \frac{ID}{MT}$ | bits/s | Fitts throughput | | 7 | Mean Selection Time (scanning) | $T_{scan} = \frac{(R-1)\,t_{r} + (C-1)\,t_{c}}{2}$ | seconds | Row-Column scanning latency | | 8 | Word-Prediction Saving | $WPS = 1 - \frac{K_{predicted}}{K_{uncoded}}$ | dimensionless | Efficiency of language-model prediction | | 9 | Keystroke Saving (KSR) | $KSR = \frac{N_{keys} - N_{actual}}{N_{keys}}$ | dimensionless | Predictive text performance | | 10 | Eye-Tracker Accuracy | $\varepsilon_{eye} = \sqrt{(x_{gaze}-x_{tgt})^2 + (y_{gaze}-y_{tgt})^2}$ | degrees of visual angle | Eye-gaze AAC precision | > [!WARNING] > **Table-Syntax Rule:** Whenever a vertical bar `|` would normally appear (e.g., absolute value $\vert x \vert$, determinant), use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking KTU's answer-script markdown renderers. ## 2.4 Why F2F Communication Devices Are Critically Important in Engineering - **Social-Emotional Restoration:** F2F AAC restores eye-contact, turn-taking, and prosody — components of communication that screen-mediated AAC cannot replicate. - **Clinical Rehabilitation:** Used in stroke, cerebral palsy, ALS, locked-in syndrome, autism, and traumatic brain injury rehab. - **Accessibility Engineering:** Direct driver of *Universal Design* principles, influencing smartphone OS accessibility (iOS Switch Control, Android Look to Speak). - **AI Convergence:** Modern F2F devices integrate LLMs to predict multi-word phrases, pushing ECR from <10 WPM toward natural conversational rates. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fitts's Law Applied to AAC Symbol Selection Fitts's Law is a **psychomotor-modeling** law that quantifies the time required to move a pointer (finger, eye-gaze cursor, or a switch-controlled highlight) from a starting position to a target region of width $W$ at a distance $D$. It is the single most-cited law in human–computer interaction (HCI) and is **must-know for KTU ESE**. ### 3.1.1 Setup and Definitions Let: - $D$ = distance (m) from current cursor position to the target symbol's centroid. - $W$ = effective width (m) of the target cell, measured along the axis of motion. - $a$ = empirically determined intercept constant (s) — accounts for start/stop motor time. - $b$ = empirically determined slope constant (s/bit) — captures information-processing capacity. - $MT$ = mean movement time (s) per selection. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation The **Index of Difficulty (ID)** measures the *information-theoretic* difficulty of a motor task. It is derived by treating the target as a Shannon channel of bandwidth $W$ over a distance $D$: $$ ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad [\text{bits}] $$ > **Reasoning:** The `+1` is added because the target's effective width is *symmetric* around the centerline; the cursor may overshoot in either direction by $W/2$, giving a total reach of $W$ on a span of $D + W/2 + W/2 = D + W$. Normalizing by $W$ and applying $\log_2$ yields Shannon's information per selection. Empirically, movement time is a **linear function of the ID**, with intercept $a$ and slope $b$: $$ MT = a + b \cdot ID $$ Substituting the ID expression: $$ MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) $$ This is **Fitts's Law** as applied to AAC symbol selection. ### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) > A user must select a target cell of width $W = 0.025$ m located at $D = 0.075$ m. Empirically, $a = 0.05$ s and $b = 0.10$ s/bit. Find (a) the Index of Difficulty, (b) the movement time, and (c) the effective communication rate if 5 symbols per message are needed and the average utterance is 4 symbols (with no prediction). **Step 1 — Compute ID:** $$ ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{0.075}{0.025} + 1\right) = \log_2(3 + 1) = \log_2(4) = 2 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2 — Compute MT:** $$ MT = 0.05 + 0.10 \times 2 = 0.05 + 0.20 = 0.25 \text{ s per symbol} $$ **Step 3 — Compute ECR:** $$ ECR = \frac{60}{MT \times \bar{n}_{sym}} = \frac{60}{0.25 \times 4} = \frac{60}{1.0} = 60 \text{ WPM} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A 60 WPM rate is **approaching natural conversational rate** (130–200 WPM). The designer's job is to *minimize* $D$ and *maximize* $W$ for the most probable symbols — which is exactly what predictive encoding (§2.2.4) achieves. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Communication Rate (ECR) and Predictive Saving ### 3.2.1 Definition ECR measures **how many intelligible words a user can communicate per minute** using the F2F device, end-to-end (sensor → output → feedback). $$ ECR = \frac{60 \cdot N_{sym}^{correct}}{T_{tot} \cdot \bar{n}_{sym}} \quad \text{[WPM]} $$ where: - $N_{sym}^{correct}$ = number of correctly selected symbols. - $T_{tot}$ = total time elapsed (seconds). - $\bar{n}_{sym}$ = average number of symbols per word (English ≈ 5.1). ### 3.2.2 Incorporating Word Prediction Let the language model reduce effective keystrokes by a fraction $\eta$ (Keystroke Saving Rate, KSR): $$ \eta = KSR = 1 - \frac{N_{actual}}{N_{keys}} $$ The new effective rate becomes: $$ ECR_{predicted} = \frac{ECR_{base}}{1 - \eta} $$ **Step-by-step numerical verification:** Suppose baseline $ECR_{base} = 12$ WPM and the language model achieves a KSR of $\eta = 0.5$ (saves 50% of keystrokes). $$ ECR_{predicted} = \frac{12}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{12}{0.5} = 24 \text{ WPM} $$ > **Conversion logic:** The denominator $(1 - \eta)$ represents the *fraction of physical keystrokes* the user must still make. Fewer keystrokes → faster message → higher WPM. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Capacity Bound for an F2F AAC Channel To bound the *theoretical maximum* WPM achievable on an F2F channel, we convert channel capacity $C$ (bits/s) to words per minute. $$ C_{WPM} = \frac{60 \cdot C}{H_{word}} $$ where $H_{word}$ is the **per-word entropy** in bits/word (English ≈ 11.3 bits/word after conditioning). **Worked example:** Suppose the sensor band $B = 30$ Hz and the effective SNR is 100 (20 dB). $$ C = 30 \cdot \log_2(1 + 100) = 30 \cdot \log_2(101) \approx 30 \times 6.658 = 199.7 \text{ bits/s} $$ $$ C_{WPM} = \frac{60 \times 199.7}{11.3} \approx 1060 \text{ WPM} $$ > **Insight:** The Shannon bound is **orders of magnitude above** real-world ECR, indicating that *current F2F AAC devices are far from channel-limited*; they are limited by **motor and cognitive bottlenecks** of the user, not by information theory. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a F2F Encoding Scheme | User Profile | Motor Ability | Cognitive Ability | Recommended Encoding | Expected ECR (WPM) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Locked-in (ALS) | Eye-blink only | Intact | Single-switch row-column scan | 1–5 | | Severe CP | Head-control, no fine hand | Intact | Head-tracker directed selection | 5–15 | | Moderate CP | Single-finger touch | Intact | Touch-grid + prediction | 15–30 | | Autism (high-functioning) | Normal motor | Reduced social-pragmatic | Picture-based PECS / tablet SGD | 20–40 | | Aphasia (post-stroke) | Impaired speech, intact motor | Intact | Text-to-speech on tablet | 30–60 | | Late-stage ALS | Eye-gaze only | Intact | Eye-gaze AAC (Tobii) | 10–25 | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Checklist All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (e.g., `n1Input`, `n2Sensor`, `n5Out`). No reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node IDs. All node labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text (no bold/italic/HTML tags). ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Generic F2F AAC System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[n1UserIntent
USER INTENT SOURCE] --> B[n2SensorArray
SENSOR ARRAY EMG EYE EEG SWITCH] B --> C[n3Digitizer
SIGNAL CONDITIONING AND DIGITIZATION] C --> D[n4Encoder
ENCODING SCHEME SELECTION] D --> E[n5LanguageModel
LANGUAGE MODEL PREDICTION MODULE] E --> F[n6OutputStage
OUTPUT STAGE TTS DISPLAY BRAILLE] F --> G[n7Interlocutor
INTERLOCUTOR HUMAN PARTNER] G --> H[n8FeedbackLoop
FEEDBACK OBSERVATION LOOP] H --> A D -.-> I[n9Override
OVERRIDE PREDICTION KEY] I --> D style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 style C fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 style D fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43 style E fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#055160 style F fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b style G fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#b45f06 style H fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d style I fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The closed loop between the user and interlocutor emphasizes the *real-time, synchronous* nature of F2F communication. - The dashed override path represents a *user correction* channel (e.g., backspace, cancel-prediction). - Color coding maps to the five conceptual stages in §2.1. ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Sub-Architecture of an Eye-Gaze F2F AAC ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[n10EyeCamera
INFRARED EYE CAMERA] --> P2[n11PupilDetect
PUPIL CENTER DETECTION] P2 --> P3[n12GazeVec
GAZE VECTOR COMPUTATION] P3 --> P4[n13Calibrate
CALIBRATION MATRIX APPLY] P4 --> P5[n14Overlay
SCREEN OVERLAY CURSOR POSITION] P5 --> P6[n15DwellTimer
DWELL TIME SELECTION GATE] P6 --> P7[n16SymbolCommit
SYMBOL COMMIT TO LANGUAGE MODEL] P7 --> P8[n17TTS
TEXT TO SPEECH SYNTHESIS] P7 --> P9[n18Display
SCREEN TEXT DISPLAY] P8 --> P10[n19Loudspeaker
LOUDSPEAKER OUTPUT] P9 --> P11[n20UserVisual
USER VISUAL FEEDBACK LOOP] P11 --> P1 style P1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 style P2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 style P3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 style P4 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43 style P5 fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#055160 style P6 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b style P7 fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#b45f06 style P8 fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47 style P9 fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47 style P10 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d style P11 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Functional Comparison of F2F Encoding Schemes ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[n21SingleSwitch
SINGLE SWITCH SCANNING] --> R1[n22RowColScan
ROW COLUMN SCANNING] R1 --> S1[n23DirectedSel
DIRECTED SELECTION] S1 --> T1[n24Predictive
PREDICTIVE ENCODING] T1 --> U1[n25HybridAI
HYBRID AI AUGMENTED] Q1 -.->|low motor| V1[n26LowMotorEnd
LOW MOTOR DEMAND END] U1 -.->|high motor| W1[n27HighMotorEnd
HIGH MOTOR DEMAND END] style Q1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 style R1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 style S1 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43 style T1 fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#055160 style U1 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b style V1 fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47 style W1 fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT745]** Define Face-to-Face Communication in the context of assistive medical devices. List its two main categories with one example each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Face-to-Face Communication in assistive medical devices refers to *Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) systems* that enable **real-time, co-present, synchronous interpersonal communication** between a person with a communication impairment and a physically present interlocutor. It is classified under ISO 9999:2022 Class 22 (Communication and information aids). **Categories (1 mark):** 1. **Unaided F2F** — body-based, no device (e.g., manual sign language, finger-spelling). 2. **Aided F2F** — device-based, further split into low-tech (picture boards) and high-tech (speech-generating devices, eye-gaze AAC). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT745]** What is the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** in Fitts's Law? State the formula and explain its significance in F2F AAC design. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Formula (1 mark):** $$ ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad [\text{bits}] $$ **Variables (1 mark):** $D$ = distance to target, $W$ = effective width of target, both in metres. **Significance (1 mark):** The ID quantifies the *information-theoretic* difficulty of a motor selection task. In F2F AAC, designers use Fitts's Law to **size cells in a symbol grid** — wider cells and shorter distances reduce ID, lower movement time, and increase the Effective Communication Rate (ECR). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two alternatives are provided; students answer **either** A or B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** #### **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a modern Face-to-Face Speech-Generating Device (SGD)**. Explain each stage in detail. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** A Speech-Generating Device (SGD) is a high-tech aided F2F AAC system that translates user input into synthetic or digitized speech output during a live conversation. The functional architecture is as follows: **Stage 1 — Input/Selection Subsystem (2 marks):** The user interacts with the device through one of several input modalities: a touch-screen grid, a single-switch scanner, an eye-gaze tracker, a head-pointer, or a brain–computer interface. For a touch-screen SGD, the user taps a symbol on a graphical grid (e.g., 6 × 6 cell matrix). For eye-gaze SGDs, dwell-time or blink selection is used. **Stage 2 — Encoding and Prediction Subsystem (2 marks):** The raw selection event is encoded into a discrete symbol. A language-prediction engine (bigram, trigram, or modern LSTM/transformer) ranks the next likely symbols. Symbols with high probability are dynamically relocated to *easy-to-reach* positions (Goldilocks principle). This reduces effective keystrokes by 40–60%. **Stage 3 — Output Synthesis Subsystem (2 marks):** The composed text string is sent to a **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** synthesizer, which uses either concatenative synthesis (pre-recorded diphones) or parametric/neural synthesis (Tacotron, WaveNet) to produce audible speech. Simultaneously, the text is shown on a display to support lip-reading and feedback. **Stage 4 — Feedback and Calibration (1 mark):** Visual and auditory feedback closes the loop. The user can correct errors, adjust speaking rate, pitch, and voice (e.g., adult/child male/female). Modern SGDs allow personalization of voice to retain the user's vocal identity. > **Block diagram (refer to §4.2):** Mermaid flowchart showing the User → Sensor → Encoder → Language Model → TTS/Display → Interlocutor → Feedback loop. --- #### **(b)** Derive **Fitts's Law** as applied to symbol selection in F2F AAC. A user selects symbols on a 4 × 5 grid; each cell is **0.03 m wide** and the **average distance to a target** is **0.05 m**. Given $a = 0.04$ s and $b = 0.12$ s/bit, compute the **Effective Communication Rate (ECR)** in WPM, assuming an average of **5 symbols per word** and **zero prediction saving**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — State Fitts's Law (1 mark):** $$ MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the given values (2 marks):** $$ ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{0.05}{0.03} + 1\right) = \log_2(1.667 + 1) = \log_2(2.667) $$ $$ ID \approx 1.415 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 3 — Compute movement time per symbol (1 mark):** $$ MT = 0.04 + 0.12 \times 1.415 = 0.04 + 0.170 = 0.210 \text{ s/symbol} $$ **Step 4 — Compute ECR (2 marks):** $$ ECR = \frac{60 \cdot N_{sym}^{correct}}{T_{tot} \cdot \bar{n}_{sym}} = \frac{60}{MT \cdot \bar{n}_{sym}} $$ $$ ECR = \frac{60}{0.210 \times 5} = \frac{60}{1.05} \approx 57.14 \text{ WPM} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):** An ECR of ≈57 WPM is *approaching conversational natural speech rate* (130–200 WPM), confirming that grid-based selection with moderate cell size is a viable F2F AAC strategy for users with intact fine motor control. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Alternative]** #### **(a)** Explain the **Eye-Gaze F2F Communication System** in detail. Discuss its working principle, key components, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Working Principle (2 marks):** An eye-gaze F2F system uses an **infrared (IR) camera** and **IR illuminator** to track the user's pupil and corneal reflection (PCCR). The relative position of the pupil center with respect to the corneal glint is mapped via a **calibration matrix** to a screen coordinate. A virtual cursor follows the user's gaze, and **dwell-time selection** (e.g., 500–800 ms fixation) triggers a symbol commit. **Key Components (2 marks):** 1. IR eye-tracker camera (60–300 Hz sampling). 2. Display with overlaid AAC symbol grid. 3. On-device DSP / microcontroller running the gaze-estimation algorithm. 4. Text-to-speech engine and amplifier. 5. Adjustable mount (table-top, wheelchair-mounted, or glasses-mounted). **Advantages (1.5 marks):** - Truly *hands-free* F2F communication. - High ECR (10–25 WPM) for users with locked-in syndrome. - Maintains natural eye contact, supporting social engagement. **Limitations (1.5 marks):** - Sensitive to head movement, sunlight, and glasses reflections. - Requires accurate initial calibration (9-point grid). - Expensive (high-end systems cost ₹2–6 lakh in India). - Fatigue from sustained fixation; not suitable for users with oculomotor impairment. --- #### **(b)** A user employs a **row-column scanning SGD** with a 5 × 7 grid. Each row takes **0.4 s** to highlight and each column takes **0.5 s**. The average word contains **5 symbols** and the user makes **1 error every 12 symbols** (requiring 2 s to correct). Calculate the **Effective Communication Rate in WPM** considering both selection and error-correction time. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Average time to select one symbol (2 marks):** For row-column scanning, the *expected* number of steps before selection is the average of $(R-1)/2$ row-scans and $(C-1)/2$ column-scans: $$ T_{row} = \frac{(5-1)}{2} \times 0.4 = 2 \times 0.4 = 0.8 \text{ s} $$ $$ T_{col} = \frac{(7-1)}{2} \times 0.5 = 3 \times 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ s} $$ $$ T_{sym}^{sel} = T_{row} + T_{col} = 0.8 + 1.5 = 2.3 \text{ s/symbol} $$ **Step 2 — Error-correction time (2 marks):** Out of every 12 symbols, 1 is wrong and consumes 2 s to correct. The extra time per symbol is: $$ T_{err} = \frac{2}{12} = 0.1667 \text{ s/symbol} $$ **Step 3 — Total time per symbol (1 mark):** $$ T_{sym}^{tot} = 2.3 + 0.1667 = 2.4667 \text{ s/symbol} $$ **Step 4 — Total time per word (1 mark):** $$ T_{word} = 5 \times 2.4667 = 12.333 \text{ s/word} $$ **Step 5 — Effective Communication Rate (1 mark):** $$ ECR = \frac{60}{T_{word}} = \frac{60}{12.333} \approx 4.86 \text{ WPM} $$ > **Interpretation:** Row-column scanning yields a very low ECR (≈5 WPM). This is why modern systems *always augment* scanning with **word prediction** — a 50% KSR would raise ECR to ≈9.7 WPM. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mistakes:** Forgetting to convert seconds-per-symbol to WPM by multiplying by 60. Marks docked: **1 mark**. > 2. **Forgetting the `+1` in Fitts's ID:** This single omission gives a wrong sign and ID. Marks docked: **1 mark**. > 3. **Ignoring error-correction time:** In scanning problems, the examiner **expects** students to factor in error-recovery time. Marks docked: **1–2 marks**. > 4. **Conflating "communication rate" with "selection rate":** The examiner specifically tests whether you know that 1 *word* ≈ 5 *symbols* (English average). Marks docked: **1 mark**. > 5. **Mixing up ECR and Shannon capacity:** These are different metrics; ECR is empirical, $C$ is theoretical. Marks docked: **1 mark**. > 6. **Skipping the interpretation step:** Always end a calculation with a one-line *practical* interpretation. Marks docked: **0.5–1 mark**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Face-to-Face Communication** - ⭐ **F2F AAC = real-time, co-present, synchronous** communication, classified under ISO 9999:2022 Class 22. - ⭐ **Two families:** Unaided (gestures, sign) vs. Aided (low-tech boards, high-tech SGDs). - ⭐ **Five-stage signal chain:** Intent → Sensor → Encoder → Language Model → Output → Feedback. - ⭐ **Fitts's Law is the #1 HCI law** for AAC: $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$. - ⭐ **Index of Difficulty (ID)** is in **bits**; **ECR** is in **WPM**; **Throughput** is in **bits/s**. - ⭐ **Effective Communication Rate** is the gold clinical metric — target **15–30 WPM** for high-functioning users. - ⭐ **Single-switch scanning** is the lowest-motor option; **directed selection** is the fastest. - ⭐ **Word prediction** (KSR 40–60%) can **double** the ECR. - ⭐ **Eye-gaze AAC** is the standard of care for late-stage ALS and locked-in syndrome. - ⭐ **Shannon bound** far exceeds real-world ECR — the bottleneck is **motor**, not *informational*. - ⭐ **Always convert** seconds-per-symbol to WPM using the factor 60 and divide by symbols-per-word. - ⭐ **Always include the `+1`** inside the Fitts's Law logarithm. - ⭐ **Always state units** explicitly — `bits`, `s`, `m`, `WPM`, `° of visual angle`. - ⭐ **Modern SGDs** use neural TTS (Tacotron 2, WaveNet) and transformer-based language models. - ⭐ **Clinical evaluation** of F2F devices uses the WHO ICF framework (Body Function → Activity → Participation). - ⭐ **Ethical note:** Voice-banking is recommended for progressive disorders (ALS) to preserve vocal identity. ---

topics.title: Alerting Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Alerting Devices # 🔔 Alerting Devices — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** > *Alerting Devices* are a specialized class of **Assistive Medical Devices (AMDs)** engineered to detect, transduce, and communicate the occurrence of critical environmental or physiological events to individuals who cannot perceive such events through conventional sensory channels. They form a vital subdomain of **sensory substitution and augmentation technology**, converting one sensory modality (e.g., auditory) into another (e.g., tactile, visual, or amplified auditory stimulus). In the framework of the **KTU PEBRT745 (Assistive Medical Devices)** syllabus, alerting devices are categorized under the broader umbrella of *devices for individuals with sensory impairments*, and are evaluated based on their: - **Detection Sensitivity** (typically rated in **dB SPL** for audio inputs and **Lux** for visual inputs) - **Latency** (response time, generally **< 500 ms** for safety-critical alerts) - **Modality Conversion Fidelity** - **Power Autonomy** (often **> 72 hours** on battery backup) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Translator" of the Senses > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Coffee Mug ☕** > Imagine you are blindfolded in a kitchen. When the coffee mug slips off the counter, you *hear* it crash, *feel* the vibration, and *sense* the change in air. Now imagine your ears are plugged too. An **Alerting Device** is essentially a *personal sensor-to-sense translator*. It is the "third hand and ear" of a person with sensory loss — a small, intelligent sentinel that watches the world 24/7 and *pokes, flashes, or vibrates* the user the moment something important happens. Think of it like a **smoke detector for life itself**: the device continuously monitors a stimulus (sound, motion, temperature, vital sign), and when that stimulus crosses a programmable **threshold**, it fires a **secondary stimulus** that the user *can* perceive. It is, fundamentally, an **event-to-percept converter**. ## 1.3 Classification of Alerting Devices Alerting devices are broadly classified along **three orthogonal axes**: | Classification Axis | Categories | Typical Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **By User Deficit** | Deaf / Hard-of-Hearing, Blind / Low-Vision, Deafblind, Cognitive | Matched to the missing modality | | **By Source Signal** | Auditory (doorbell, alarm, cry), Visual (motion, light), Tactile (vibration), Physiological (BP, SpO₂) | Determines sensor front-end | | **By Output Modality** | Visual (strobe, LCD), Tactile (vibrator, pager), Auditory (loud tone, spoken word) | Determines actuator back-end | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > The principle of **Modality Substitution** is the cornerstone of this topic. Every alerting device can be modeled as a **three-stage pipeline**: `Sensor → Processor → Actuator`. The sensor captures the unperceivable stimulus; the processor interprets and decides; the actuator delivers the perceivable substitute stimulus. # ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Three-Stage Pipeline Every alerting device, regardless of complexity, follows a deterministic signal flow. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to be able to **draw, label, and explain** this pipeline in examinations. ### Stage 1: Sensor (Input Transduction) The sensor converts a physical phenomenon into an electrical signal. The choice of sensor dictates what the device can "see." - **Microphone (Electret / MEMS):** Converts sound pressure waves into voltage. Sensitivity is rated in **dB SPL** (decibels Sound Pressure Level, where 0 dB SPL = 20 µPa). - **Photodiode / LDR:** Converts light intensity into current. Sensitivity rated in **Lux (lx)**. - **Piezoelectric Disc / Accelerometer:** Converts mechanical vibration into voltage. Sensitivity in **mV/g**. - **Thermistor / RTD:** Converts temperature into resistance change. Sensitivity in **Ω/°C**. - **Capacitive Touch / Proximity Sensor:** Detects body capacitance changes. ### Stage 2: Processor (Decision Logic) The processor takes the raw analog signal, filters it, compares it against a threshold, and decides whether to trigger an alert. In modern KTU-context devices, this is often a low-power microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, ATmega328P class) or a dedicated ASIC. - **Amplification:** Operational amplifier stage (e.g., LM358) raises the µV-level sensor output to a usable V-level. - **Filtering:** Band-pass filter rejects noise (e.g., 1 kHz–4 kHz band for voice/cry detection). - **Thresholding:** Comparator (e.g., LM393) produces a binary decision: *event = TRUE / FALSE*. - **Pattern Recognition (Advanced):** For cry detectors or fall detectors, simple **Energy Threshold + Zero-Crossing Rate** algorithms are employed. A cry signature has a fundamental frequency near **300–600 Hz** with strong harmonics. ### Stage 3: Actuator (Output Transduction) The actuator delivers the perceivable stimulus. This is the **modality substitution** point. - **Visual:** High-intensity LED strobe (typically **75–177 candela**, as per UL 1971 standard for signaling appliances). - **Tactile:** Eccentric Rotating Mass (ERM) motor or Linear Resonant Actuator (LRA). Vibration amplitude is rated in **g (acceleration due to gravity, 9.81 m/s²)**; typical alerting pagers operate at **0.5–2 g** at **150–250 Hz**. - **Auditory:** Piezo buzzer emitting **75–100 dB SPL at 1 meter**, often with frequency modulation (e.g., **2.7 kHz** square wave) to be distinguishable from ambient noise. ## 2.2 The Core "Why" — Threshold Theory The fundamental physics of any alerting device is rooted in the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**. An alert must be **perceivable** above the ambient noise floor. The classical decibel conversion of an acoustic stimulus is given by: $$ L_p \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) \quad \text{[dB SPL]} $$ where the **reference pressure** is: $$ p_{\text{ref}} \;=\; 20 \, \mu \text{Pa} $$ and $p$ is the RMS sound pressure in pascals. For the device to trigger reliably, the **trigger threshold** $L_{\text{thresh}}$ must be set above the ambient noise floor $L_{\text{ambient}}$ by a safety margin of typically **+10 dB**: $$ L_{\text{thresh}} \;\geq\; L_{\text{ambient}} + 10 \text{ dB} $$ This 10 dB margin corresponds to a **10× increase in perceived loudness** on a logarithmic scale and is the *de facto* engineering rule-of-thumb in assistive alerting design. > [!NOTE] > **Why 10 dB and not 5 dB?** > Psychoacoustic studies (Fletcher-Munson curves) show that the human ear requires approximately a **10 dB increase** above the masked threshold for reliable detection in typical residential environments. This is why the 10 dB safety margin is universally adopted in **UL 1971**, **IEC 60118-4**, and **ADA** standards referenced in KTU course outcomes. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for ESE** | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application in Alerting Devices | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_{\text{ref}})$ | dB SPL | Microphone trigger threshold | | 2 | Reference Pressure | $p_{\text{ref}} = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ | Pa | Acoustic calibration | | 3 | Trigger Threshold Rule | $L_{\text{thresh}} \geq L_{\text{ambient}} + 10$ | dB | Reliability margin | | 4 | Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_{\text{ref}})$ | dB | Strobe / visual light intensity | | 5 | Effective Intensity (Strobe) | $I_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T I(t)\,dt$ | cd | UL 1971 visual alarm rating | | 6 | Vibration Acceleration | $a = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot A$ | m/s² | ERM motor tactile output | | 7 | Resonant Frequency (LRA) | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz | Tuned vibrotactile alert | | 8 | Battery Autonomy | $t_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{Ah}}}{I_{\text{load}}}$ | hours | Backup power for safety devices | | 9 | Alarm Latency Budget | $t_{\text{latency}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{actuate}}$ | ms | Real-time response requirement (< 500 ms) | | 10 | Photoelectric Lux | $E_v = \frac{\Phi_v}{A}$ | lux (lx) | Light detector calibration | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the markdown table above, the absolute-value-like notation has been replaced with descriptive phrasing. When writing formulas by hand in the exam, never confuse the **sound pressure level** (uses factor **20** in the log) with the **intensity level** (uses factor **10** in the log). The factor difference is **2×** and arises because $I \propto p^2$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Alerting devices are not academic curiosities — they are deployed in: - **Hospital nurse-call systems** (e.g., Rauland-Borg, Ascom) where the alerting pipeline is replicated hundreds of times across wards. - **Home safety ecosystems** for the elderly, where falls, gas leaks, and falls generate multi-modal alerts (phone call + strobe + bed-shaker). - **Public Address (PA) and Mass Notification Systems** that comply with **NFPA 72** for audible/visible notification. - **Wearable medical alerts** (e.g., Life Alert, Apple Watch fall detection) that use a **9-axis IMU** (accelerometer + gyroscope + magnetometer) and run an on-device threshold algorithm. # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Acoustic Trigger Threshold for a Doorbell Alerting Device > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context (KTU-Style):** > A doorbell alerting device is to be designed for a deaf user. The residential ambient noise level is measured at **35 dB SPL**. The doorbell chime produces a sound pressure of **0.20 Pa** at the microphone. The design engineer wants to set a trigger threshold that provides a **10 dB safety margin** above ambient. Determine: > 1. The **dB SPL level** of the doorbell chime at the microphone. > 2. The **trigger threshold** in dB SPL. > 3. The **minimum sound pressure** (in Pa) the chime must produce to activate the device. > 4. The **SNR** in dB. --- ### Step 1 — Compute the dB SPL of the doorbell chime The fundamental decibel equation for sound pressure level is: $$ L_p \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) $$ Substituting $p = 0.20$ Pa and $p_{\text{ref}} = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa: $$ L_p \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.20}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) $$ Evaluate the argument of the logarithm: $$ \frac{0.20}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; \frac{0.20}{0.0000200} \;=\; 10{,}000 $$ Substitute back into the equation: $$ L_p \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) $$ Apply the standard identity $\log_{10}(10^n) = n$: $$ L_p \;=\; 20 \times 4 \;=\; 80 \text{ dB SPL} $$ **Result:** The doorbell chime registers at **80 dB SPL** at the microphone. --- ### Step 2 — Compute the trigger threshold Using the safety-margin rule $L_{\text{thresh}} \geq L_{\text{ambient}} + 10$ dB: $$ L_{\text{thresh}} \;=\; 35 + 10 \;=\; 45 \text{ dB SPL} $$ **Result:** The trigger threshold must be set at **45 dB SPL**. --- ### Step 3 — Compute the minimum sound pressure to trigger the device We invert the dB equation. Starting from: $$ L_{\text{thresh}} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\min}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) $$ Divide both sides by 20: $$ \frac{L_{\text{thresh}}}{20} \;=\; \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\min}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) $$ Exponentiate (base 10) both sides: $$ 10^{L_{\text{thresh}}/20} \;=\; \frac{p_{\min}}{p_{\text{ref}}} $$ Multiply both sides by $p_{\text{ref}}$: $$ p_{\min} \;=\; p_{\text{ref}} \times 10^{L_{\text{thresh}}/20} $$ Substitute the numerical values: $$ p_{\min} \;=\; (20 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10^{45/20} $$ Evaluate the exponent: $$ \frac{45}{20} \;=\; 2.25 $$ So: $$ p_{\min} \;=\; 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{2.25} $$ Compute $10^{2.25}$ using $10^{2.25} = 10^2 \times 10^{0.25}$: $$ 10^{0.25} \;\approx\; 1.778 $$ Therefore: $$ 10^{2.25} \;\approx\; 100 \times 1.778 \;=\; 177.8 $$ Finally: $$ p_{\min} \;=\; 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 177.8 \;\approx\; 3.556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa} $$ $$ p_{\min} \;\approx\; 3.56 \text{ mPa} $$ **Result:** The chime must produce a sound pressure of at least **3.56 mPa** at the microphone to trigger the alert. --- ### Step 4 — Compute the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) The SNR in decibels is the difference between the signal level and the noise level: $$ \text{SNR} \;=\; L_{\text{signal}} - L_{\text{ambient}} $$ $$ \text{SNR} \;=\; 80 - 35 \;=\; 45 \text{ dB} $$ **Result:** The chime provides a **45 dB SNR** at the microphone — a very robust detection margin, indicating the device will trigger reliably even if the chime source weakens by 35 dB. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix | Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value | Engineering Interpretation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Doorbell SPL at mic | $L_p$ | **80 dB SPL** | Comfortably above threshold | | Trigger threshold | $L_{\text{thresh}}$ | **45 dB SPL** | 10 dB above ambient | | Minimum trigger pressure | $p_{\min}$ | **3.56 mPa** | Hardware sensitivity floor | | Safety margin (SNR) | $\text{SNR}$ | **45 dB** | Highly reliable | | Compliance | UL 1971 | ✓ Pass | ADA-compliant alerting | > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** KTU board examiners frequently award a *partial mark* of 1–2 points for **unit consistency** in Step 3. Students who write "Pa" or "mPa" or "µPa" correctly retain full credit; writing bare numbers without units typically costs 0.5–1 mark per sub-part. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of a Generic Alerting Device The following Mermaid block represents the universal three-stage pipeline of an alerting device, with annotations of the KTU syllabus-aligned component categories. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ENVIRONMENTAL
EVENT SOURCE] --> B[SENSOR STAGE
Transducer] B --> C[AMPLIFIER
Op-Amp LM358] C --> D[FILTER
Band-Pass 1-4 kHz] D --> E[COMPARATOR
Threshold Detector] E --> F[PROCESSOR
MCU Decision Logic] F --> G{VENT MODALITY
SELECTION} G -->|Visual| H[STROBE LED
75 cd Output] G -->|Tactile| I[VIBRATION MOTOR
ERM 150 Hz] G -->|Auditory| J[PIEZO BUZZER
90 dB SPL] H --> K[USER
Perceives Alert] I --> K J --> K ``` **Block-Level Interpretation (KTU Examiner's Reading Guide):** - **Node A → B:** The event (doorbell, cry, alarm, smoke) is the physical phenomenon to be detected. - **Node B → F:** The sensor-to-processor chain is the **input subsystem**, performing transduction, amplification, filtering, and thresholding. - **Node F → G:** The processor implements the *decision algorithm* and routes the alert to the appropriate output modality. - **Node G → K:** The *modality substitution* occurs here — the user perceives a stimulus in a channel they can still use. ## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Multi-Modal Notification System For a hospital-grade or smart-home-grade alerting device, the architecture extends to a multi-modal, networked system. The Mermaid block below maps the interaction flow between the alerting device, the user, and the caregiver. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT_LAYER[INPUT SENSING LAYER] S1[MICROPHONE] S2[PIR MOTION] S3[SMOKE SENSOR] S4[ECG / SPO2 PAD] end subgraph PROCESSING_LAYER[PROCESSING LAYER - MCU] P1[SIGNAL CONDITIONING] P2[FEATURE EXTRACTION] P3[THRESHOLD COMPARATOR] P4[ALERT ROUTER] end subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER[OUTPUT ALERT LAYER] O1[VISUAL STROBE] O2[BED SHAKER] O3[LOUD TONE] O4[GSM / WIFI MODULE] end subgraph USER_LAYER[END USER] U1[DEAF USER] U2[BLIND USER] U3[CAREGIVER] end S1 --> P1 S2 --> P1 S3 --> P1 S4 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> O1 P4 --> O2 P4 --> O3 P4 --> O4 O1 --> U1 O2 --> U1 O3 --> U2 O4 --> U3 ``` **Reading the Architecture:** - The **INPUT_LAYER** sub-graph groups all physical-world sensors. - The **PROCESSING_LAYER** sub-graph implements the KTU-referenced signal chain. - The **OUTPUT_LAYER** sub-graph explicitly maps to the **three substitution modalities** (visual, tactile, auditory) plus a **networked** option (GSM/Wi-Fi to caregiver). - The **USER_LAYER** sub-graph closes the loop, showing which user benefits from which output modality — a **direct mapping** between disability and substitution. ## 4.3 Device Family Tree — Common Alerting Devices ```mermaid flowchart LR R[ALERTING DEVICES
PEBRT745] --> A1[FOR HEARING IMPAIRMENT] R --> A2[FOR VISUAL IMPAIRMENT] R --> A3[FOR DEAFBLIND] R --> A4[FOR COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT] A1 --> D1[Doorbell Signaler] A1 --> D2[Smoke Alarm with Strobe] A1 --> D3[Bed Shaker Alarm Clock] A1 --> D4[Baby Cry Signaler] A2 --> D5[Talking Clock] A2 --> D6[Liquid Level Indicator] A2 --> D7[Money Identifier] A2 --> D8[Color Detector] A3 --> D9[Tactile Pager] A3 --> D10[Refreshable Braille Display] A4 --> D11[Medication Reminder] A4 --> D12[Wandering Alarm] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Note:** In the examination, when asked to "classify alerting devices," the tree above is the most efficient structural answer. Use the four-bucket classification (Hearing, Visual, Deafblind, Cognitive) and provide one example device per bucket for a full 7-mark answer. # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) > **KTU University Exam Model** --- ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q1.** Define an *alerting device* in the context of assistive medical devices. Give **two** examples with the sensory deficit each addresses. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **alerting device** is an assistive medical device that detects a critical environmental or physiological event and converts it into a perceivable stimulus via **modality substitution** — replacing a missing sensory channel (auditory, visual, or tactile) with one the user can still sense. | # | Example | Sensory Deficit Addressed | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Bed-shaker alarm clock** | Deaf / Hard-of-Hearing — converts an auditory alarm into a strong tactile vibration transmitted through the mattress | | 2 | **Talking color detector** | Blind / Low-Vision — converts a visual property (color) into a spoken-word auditory output | *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark per example with correct deficit pairing = 3 marks)* --- ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q2.** State and justify the **trigger threshold rule** used in designing acoustic alerting devices. What is the typical safety margin in dB, and why that specific value? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **trigger threshold rule** states that the device's microphone activation level must be set at least **10 dB above the ambient noise floor**: $$ L_{\text{thresh}} \geq L_{\text{ambient}} + 10 \text{ dB} $$ **Justification (2 marks):** - A 10 dB increase corresponds to a **10× increase in perceived sound intensity** on the logarithmic dB scale, ensuring the alert is unambiguously distinguishable from background noise. - Psychoacoustic experiments (Fletcher-Munson) and standards such as **UL 1971** and **IEC 60118-4** adopt this 10 dB value because it represents the minimum differential at which the human auditory system reliably discriminates a target signal from masking noise in typical residential and clinical environments. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (1 × 14 Marks) > **Instructions:** *Answer any ONE of the following.* > *(As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, Part B Module questions carry 14 marks with internal choice between sub-questions.)* --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q.A.** *(a)* Explain the **three-stage pipeline** (Sensor → Processor → Actuator) of an alerting device. For each stage, name **two real components** used in commercial devices and describe the transduction principle. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A **smoke alarm with strobe light** is to be designed for a deaf user in a bedroom with ambient noise of **30 dB SPL**. The smoke sensor's audible alarm produces a sound pressure of **0.10 Pa** at the alerting device's microphone. Determine: (i) the **dB SPL of the smoke alarm** at the device, (ii) the **trigger threshold in dB SPL** using the 10 dB safety margin, and (iii) the **minimum sound pressure** in Pa needed to trigger the device. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Stage 1 — Sensor (Input Transduction) [2 marks]** The sensor stage captures a physical phenomenon and converts it into an electrical signal. Two real components are: - **Electret Condenser Microphone** — converts sound pressure waves into a varying voltage via diaphragm capacitance change. Used in doorbell and baby-cry signalers. - **Passive Infrared (PIR) Sensor** — detects changes in infrared radiation emitted by a human body via a pyroelectric crystal. Used in wander-alert and room-occupancy alerters. **Stage 2 — Processor (Decision Logic) [2.5 marks]** The processor amplifies, filters, and decides. Two real components: - **LM358 Operational Amplifier** — raises the µV-level microphone output to a V-level signal. - **ATmega328P Microcontroller** — runs the threshold-comparison algorithm and routes the alert to the chosen output modality. (Used in Arduino-based alerting prototypes cited in KTU reference material.) **Stage 3 — Actuator (Output Transduction) [2.5 marks]** The actuator delivers the perceivable substitute stimulus. Two real components: - **High-Intensity LED Strobe (75–177 candela)** — provides the visual alert; complies with **UL 1971** for signaling to deaf users. - **Eccentric Rotating Mass (ERM) Motor** — generates a **150–250 Hz vibration** at **0.5–2 g** acceleration; used in bed-shaker alarm clocks and wearable pagers. **Transduction Principle Summary Table:** | Stage | Component 1 | Component 2 | Energy Conversion | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Sensor | Electret Mic | PIR | Acoustic/IR → Electrical | | Processor | LM358 Op-Amp | ATmega328P | Electrical → Decision | | Actuator | LED Strobe | ERM Motor | Electrical → Light/Vibration | --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **(i) dB SPL of the smoke alarm at the device [2 marks]** Apply: $$ L_p \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) $$ Substitute $p = 0.10$ Pa and $p_{\text{ref}} = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa: $$ L_p \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.10}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(5000) $$ Evaluate: $\log_{10}(5000) = \log_{10}(5) + 3 \approx 0.699 + 3 = 3.699$ $$ L_p \;\approx\; 20 \times 3.699 \;\approx\; 73.98 \text{ dB SPL} \;\approx\; 74 \text{ dB SPL} $$ **(ii) Trigger threshold in dB SPL [2 marks]** Using the 10 dB safety margin: $$ L_{\text{thresh}} \;=\; L_{\text{ambient}} + 10 \;=\; 30 + 10 \;=\; 40 \text{ dB SPL} $$ **(iii) Minimum sound pressure in Pa [3 marks]** Invert the dB equation: $$ p_{\min} \;=\; p_{\text{ref}} \times 10^{L_{\text{thresh}}/20} $$ Substitute $L_{\text{thresh}} = 40$ dB SPL: $$ p_{\min} \;=\; (20 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10^{40/20} \;=\; (20 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10^{2} $$ $$ p_{\min} \;=\; 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa} \;=\; 2.0 \text{ mPa} $$ **Final Answer Summary:** | Sub-Part | Quantity | Value | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | (i) | Smoke alarm SPL | **74 dB SPL** | | (ii) | Trigger threshold | **40 dB SPL** | | (iii) | Minimum pressure | **2.0 mPa** | --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)* **Q.B.** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **multi-modal alerting system for a deafblind user**. List the input sensors, processing units, and output actuators used. Why is multi-modal output essential in such a system? *(7 marks)* *(b)* A **bed-shaker alarm clock** is used by a deaf user. The clock's vibration motor operates at **200 Hz** with a peak displacement amplitude of **0.5 mm**. Calculate: (i) the **peak acceleration** in m/s², and (ii) the **acceleration in units of g** (where $g = 9.81$ m/s²). Comment on whether this acceleration is sufficient to wake a deeply sleeping adult. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Working of a Multi-Modal Alerting System for Deafblind Users [5 marks]** A deafblind user has **neither functional hearing nor functional vision**, so the alerting device must use **tactile (and occasionally olfactory or gustatory)** channels exclusively. The block diagram is: ``` [Sensors: Microphone / Doorbell / Smoke / PIR / Physiological] ↓ [Signal Conditioning: Amplifier + Filter] ↓ [Processor: Threshold / Pattern Match] ↓ [Alert Router / Modality Selector MCU] ↓ [Tactile Actuators: Bed Shaker + Wristband Vibrator + Braille Refreshable Display] ↓ [Optional GSM/Wi-Fi to Caregiver] ``` **Input Sensors Used:** - Electret microphone (for doorbell, cry, smoke alarm acoustic signatures) - PIR motion sensor (for room-entry intrusion alerts) - Smoke/gas sensor (MQ-2 type) (for fire safety) - ECG/SpO₂ chest strap (for medical emergency alerting) **Processing Units:** - LM358 op-amp for signal amplification - ATmega328P / MSP430 microcontroller for threshold logic - Optional GSM (SIM800) or Wi-Fi (ESP8266) module for caregiver notification **Output Actuators:** - **Bed shaker** (high-torque ERM motor) — placed under the pillow or mattress - **Wristband pager** (LRA actuator at 175 Hz) — for daytime alerts - **Refreshable Braille display** — for textual information such as medication names **Why Multi-Modal Output is Essential [2 marks]** Multi-modality provides **redundancy and contextual differentiation**: a strong bed-shaker pulse signals an *emergency*; a gentle wrist vibration signals an *informational* event like a visitor. The user's skin sensitivity may also vary across body sites, so distributing the alert across multiple actuators ensures **perceptual reliability** in all situations (sleeping, bathing, working). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given:** $f = 200$ Hz, $A = 0.5$ mm $= 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m. **(i) Peak acceleration [4 marks]** The general equation for the peak acceleration of a simple harmonic oscillator (which an ERM motor approximates at its resonant frequency) is: $$ a_{\text{peak}} \;=\; (2\pi f)^2 \cdot A $$ Step 1: Compute the angular frequency: $$ \omega \;=\; 2\pi f \;=\; 2 \times \pi \times 200 \;=\; 400\pi \text{ rad/s} $$ Step 2: Compute $\omega^2$: $$ \omega^2 \;=\; (400\pi)^2 \;=\; 160{,}000 \pi^2 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2 $$ Step 3: Use $\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$: $$ \omega^2 \;\approx\; 160{,}000 \times 9.8696 \;\approx\; 1.579 \times 10^{6} \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2 $$ Step 4: Multiply by amplitude $A = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m: $$ a_{\text{peak}} \;\approx\; 1.579 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 789.5 \text{ m/s}^2 $$ **Result:** $a_{\text{peak}} \approx 789.5$ m/s² (approximately **790 m/s²**). **(ii) Acceleration in g [2 marks]** $$ a_{\text{peak}} \text{ (in g)} \;=\; \frac{789.5}{9.81} \;\approx\; 80.5 \text{ g} $$ **Result:** The motor delivers approximately **80.5 g** of peak acceleration. **Commentary [1 mark]:** A peak acceleration of **~80 g** at **200 Hz** is well above the **2 g threshold** typically cited for waking a deeply sleeping adult (Bonnet et al., 1978, *Waking effect of vibrotactile stimuli*). Therefore, this bed-shaker is **more than sufficient** to reliably wake the user, even from slow-wave (deep) sleep, and is compliant with the assistive alerting performance criteria referenced in PEBRT745. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!)** > > 1. **Factor-of-2 confusion:** In the dB equation, students frequently write $L_p = 10 \log_{10}(p/p_{\text{ref}})$ — the **correct factor is 20** for sound pressure level. Using 10 instead of 20 gives a result off by **2×** (≈6 dB error) and costs **full marks** for that sub-part. > 2. **Unit omission:** Writing "$\log_{10}(10000) = 4$ dB" instead of "$20 \times 4 = 80$ dB SPL" loses the unit mark. > 3. **Threshold margin reversal:** Some students compute $L_{\text{ambient}} - 10$ instead of $L_{\text{ambient}} + 10$. The margin is **added**, not subtracted — the trigger must be **louder** than ambient, not quieter. > 4. **Mixing pressure and intensity:** A common slip is using $I \propto p^2$ logic in an SPL problem. Remember: the **factor 20** in the SPL formula already accounts for the $p^2$ relationship; do not square the pressure again. > 5. **Skipping the diagram:** Part (a) of Question B explicitly asks for a *"neat block diagram."* A text-only description without a diagram loses **at least 2 marks** in the KTU valuation scheme. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-Second Revision Bullet List — Print This!** - ✅ **Definition:** Alerting devices perform **modality substitution** — converting an unperceivable event into a perceivable stimulus. - ✅ **Three-Stage Pipeline:** `Sensor → Processor (Amplifier + Filter + Comparator + MCU) → Actuator`. - ✅ **Classification:** Four user buckets — Hearing-Impaired, Visually-Impaired, Deafblind, Cognitive-Impairment. - ✅ **Sound Pressure Level Formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_{\text{ref}})$, with $p_{\text{ref}} = 20 \mu\text{Pa}$. - ✅ **Intensity Level Formula:** $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_{\text{ref}})$ — note the factor of **10**, not 20. - ✅ **Threshold Rule:** $L_{\text{thresh}} \geq L_{\text{ambient}} + 10$ dB (UL 1971 / IEC 60118-4 standard). - ✅ **Vibration Acceleration:** $a = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot A$ — the fundamental equation for ERM/LRA motor output. - ✅ **Visual Strobe Rating:** **75–177 candela** effective intensity per UL 1971. - ✅ **Audible Alarm Output:** **75–100 dB SPL at 1 m** for safety-critical alerts. - ✅ **Latency Budget:** $t_{\text{latency}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{actuate}} < 500$ ms. - ✅ **Battery Autonomy:** $t_{\text{life}} = C_{\text{Ah}} / I_{\text{load}} \geq 72$ h for safety devices. - ✅ **Standards to Know:** **UL 1971** (visual signals), **NFPA 72** (notification), **IEC 60118-4** (hearing-loop magnetic field), **ADA** (US accessibility law). - ✅ **Common Devices:** Bed-shaker alarm clock, smoke alarm with strobe, doorbell signaler, baby-cry signaler, talking clock, liquid level indicator, refreshable Braille display, GSM-based wandering alarm. - ✅ **Multi-modal Output = Redundancy:** Always provide ≥ 2 output modalities for safety-critical alerts (e.g., strobe + bed-shaker for fire). - ✅ **For a 14-mark answer:** Always include a **neatly labeled block diagram** with the three stages explicitly named; partial-credit is heavily skewed toward diagrams in KTU 2024 valuation. > **End of Module 4 Notes — Alerting Devices** 🎓 > *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 specifications (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme).*

topics.title: Assistive Listening Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assistive Listening Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs)** are a broad category of specialized electroacoustic instruments, accessories, and peripheral systems designed to augment the listening capabilities of individuals with auditory impairments, particularly in challenging acoustic environments where standard hearing aids alone prove insufficient. 

As defined in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEBRT745 Module 4), ALDs encompass **personal hearing instruments**, **group amplification systems**, and **signal-alerting devices** that work in conjunction with — or independently of — the user's residual hearing pathway.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Hearing Aids:**
> - **Hearing Aid (HA)**: Worn on the body; primarily amplifies speech and ambient sound directly into the ear canal.
> - **Assistive Listening Device (ALD)**: Augments specific signals (TV, telephone, doorbell, public address) by transmitting them directly to the listener's ear, **overcoming distance, background noise, and reverberation** that degrade normal sound propagation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to read a book (the **signal**) in a dimly lit room with a flickering candle (the **background noise**). You could either strain your eyes (analogy to a damaged cochlea struggling) or you could bring a focused flashlight (a **personal amplifier**), or move closer to a window (a **telecoil loop**), or use a magnifying glass on a digital display (a **captioning device**).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "3-D Problem" in Hearing Loss Rehabilitation:**
> Assistive listening devices specifically target the three classical degraders of speech intelligibility:
> 1. **Distance** — Sound intensity drops with the square of distance (Inverse Square Law)
> 2. **Noise** — Competing background sounds mask the target speech
> 3. **Distortion/Reverberation** — Reflections smear the temporal cues in speech

## 1.3 Core Engineering Metrics & Standards

The performance of all ALDs is benchmarked against three primary metrics:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio Improvement | $\Delta$SNR | **+15 to +25** | dB |
| Audio Frequency Bandwidth | $f_{bw}$ | **100 Hz – 8 kHz** (speech) | Hz |
| Maximum Acoustic Gain | $G_{max}$ | **30 to 70** | dB SPL |
| Total Harmonic Distortion | THD | **< 3%** | % |
| Equivalent Input Noise | EIN | **< 25** | dB SPL |
| Transmission Frequency (FM/IR) | $f_t$ | **72–216 (FM) / 95 kHz–3 MHz (IR)** | MHz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Body:** The American Speech-Language-Hearing Association (ASHA) and the Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology Society of North America (RESNA) define ALD performance criteria. The **SNR improvement** of +15 dB is the **clinically accepted minimum** to restore speech intelligibility in noisy environments.

---

## 1.4 Physiological Basis — Why ALDs Are Needed

A normal human ear can detect sounds from **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz** with intensity thresholds as low as **0 dB SPL**. Hearing impairment shifts this threshold upward (a condition called **hearing loss**, measured in dB HL). The degree of loss is classified as:

- **Mild**: 26–40 dB HL
- **Moderate**: 41–55 dB HL
- **Moderately Severe**: 56–70 dB HL
- **Severe**: 71–90 dB HL
- **Profound**: > 90 dB HL

Even with perfectly fitted digital hearing aids, listeners in **noisy environments (SNR < +5 dB)** experience severe speech comprehension loss. ALDs solve this by **improving the local SNR** at the listener's ear.

---

## 1.5 Types of Hearing Loss — Relevance to ALD Selection

1. **Conductive Hearing Loss** — Outer/middle ear pathology (e.g., otitis media, otosclerosis). Sound cannot reach the cochlea. ALDs that use **bone conduction** (e.g., **Bone-Anchored Hearing Aids — BAHA**) bypass the conductive block.

2. **Sensorineural Hearing Loss (SNHL)** — Cochlea or auditory nerve damage (e.g., presbycusis, noise-induced loss). Amplification (conventional HAs) is the primary intervention; ALDs supplement the HA through direct audio input or wireless coupling.

3. **Mixed Hearing Loss** — Both conductive and sensorineural components. Combination strategies (e.g., amplification + FM system) are deployed.

4. **Central Auditory Processing Disorder (CAPD)** — Normal audiogram but poor speech-in-noise performance. **FM systems and remote microphone ALDs** are first-line interventions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The selection of an ALD is **dictated by the audiometric profile**, the **type and degree of hearing loss**, the **communication environment** (classroom, home, workplace), and the **user's manual dexterity and cognitive ability**.

---

## 1.6 Master Classification of ALDs

ALDs are broadly classified into three functional families:

### A. Personal Amplifiers & Hearing Instruments
- Conventional **Hearing Aids** (BTE, ITE, ITC, CIC, RIC)
- **Cochlear Implants (CI)** — for profound SNHL
- **Bone-Anchored Hearing Aids (BAHA)**
- **Middle Ear Implants (MEI)**
- **Auditory Brainstem Implants (ABI)**

### B. Group / Wireless Communication Systems
- **FM (Frequency Modulation) Systems** — radio transmission at 72–216 MHz
- **Infrared (IR) Systems** — used in theaters, courts
- **Induction Loop (Audio-Frequency) Systems** — electromagnetic coupling via telecoil
- **Digital Wireless Systems (2.4 GHz / Bluetooth)** — modern smart-device coupling
- **Hearing Assistive Television Systems (HATS)**

### C. Alerting / Signaling Devices
- **Visual Alert Systems** — flashing lights for doorbells, alarms, baby cries
- **Tactile Alert Systems** — vibrating pagers, bed shakers
- **Telephone Coupling Devices** — T-coil amplifiers, text telephones (TTY/TDD)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Acoustic "3-D Problem" — Mathematical Formulation

In a typical listening environment, the **effective SNR at the listener's ear** is degraded by three independent physical phenomena. The ALD system must address at least one to improve intelligibility.

### 2.1.1 Distance Attenuation (Inverse Square Law)

Sound intensity from a point source falls off as the square of the distance:

$$I(r) = \frac{P_{source}}{4\pi r^2}$$

In decibel form (relative to source intensity $I_0$):

$$L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r}{r_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$

> **Logic:** A speaker's voice measured at **1 meter** is 6 dB louder than at **2 meters**, and 12 dB louder than at **4 meters**. This is why a teacher speaking from 6 m appears inaudible to a hearing-impaired child in the back row.

### 2.1.2 Noise Masking

The **Speech Reception Threshold (SRT)** — the SNR at which a listener correctly identifies 50% of speech — is given by:

$$\text{SRT}_{normal} \approx -4 \text{ dB}$$

$$\text{SRT}_{HI} \approx -4 + (0.014 \times \text{PTA}) \quad \text{[dB]}$$

where **PTA** is the Pure Tone Average hearing loss in dB HL (averaged over 0.5, 1, 2 kHz).

> **Logic:** A listener with 60 dB PTA needs a +0.84 dB SNR just to *detect* speech; comfortable intelligibility requires SNR ≥ **+15 dB** (the KTU benchmark).

### 2.1.3 Reverberation

Reverberation time $T_{60}$ is defined as the time required for a sound to decay by 60 dB after the source stops:

$$T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \times V}{A_{total}} \quad \text{[seconds (Sabine)]}$$

where $V$ = room volume in m³ and $A_{total}$ = total absorption in sabins.

> **Logic:** Long $T_{60}$ smears phonemes; ALDs mitigate this by **delivering direct signal** to the ear, bypassing the reverberant field.

---

## 2.2 Hearing Aid Signal Processing Architecture

A modern digital hearing aid executes five sequential processing stages:

1. **Microphone (Transducer)** — Converts acoustic pressure to electrical voltage; typical sensitivity **−46 dB V/Pa**.
2. **Pre-Amplifier** — Boosts low-level signal; gain **20–40 dB**.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Samples at **16–32 kHz** with 16–24-bit resolution.
4. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — Executes: **compression, noise reduction, feedback cancellation, directional processing, frequency shaping**.
5. **Receiver (Output Transducer)** — Re-converts to acoustic pressure; max output **110–130 dB SPL**.

### Compression Amplification — The WDRC Concept

**Wide Dynamic Range Compression (WDRC)** maps the wide input dynamic range (~100 dB) into the limited residual dynamic range of a hearing-impaired ear (~30–50 dB):

$$G_{applied} = G_{target} \times \left[1 - \frac{CR - 1}{CR}\right] \quad \text{for } P_{in} > P_{knee}$$

where:
- $G_{applied}$ = gain applied to input
- $G_{target}$ = target linear gain (dB)
- $CR$ = compression ratio (typically 2:1 to 4:1)
- $P_{knee}$ = compression activation threshold (typically 40–65 dB SPL)

> **Logic:** Soft sounds are amplified more; loud sounds are amplified less — restoring audibility without exceeding the listener's loudness discomfort level (LDL).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a comprehensive reference for all numerical problems on Assistive Listening Devices in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Inverse Square Law (Sound) | $L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 20 \log_{10}(r/r_0)$ | $L_p$ in dB SPL, $r$ in m |
| 2 | Reverberation Time (Sabine) | $T_{60} = 0.161 V / A$ | $V$ in m³, $A$ in sabins |
| 3 | SRT for Hearing Impaired | $\text{SRT}_{HI} = -4 + 0.014 \times \text{PTA}$ | dB, dB HL |
| 4 | Required SNR for Intelligibility | $\text{SNR}_{req} \geq +15$ | dB |
| 5 | Compression Gain | $G_{out} = P_{in} - \frac{P_{in} - P_{knee}}{CR}$ | dB SPL |
| 6 | Acoustic Gain (HA) | $G_{ac} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{out}/V_{in})$ | dB |
| 7 | FM Carrier Frequency | $f_{c} = 72 - 216$ | MHz (TV band) |
| 8 | Infrared Wavelength | $\lambda = 870 - 950$ | nm |
| 9 | Induction Loop Field Strength | $H = N \cdot I / 2\pi r$ | A/m |
| 10 | SNR Improvement (ALD) | $\Delta \text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(D_s/D_l) + (G_s - G_n)$ | dB |
| 11 | Battery Life | $t = Q_{bat} / I_{draw}$ | hours, mAh, mA |
| 12 | Power Gain (Receiver) | $G_p = 10 \log_{10}(P_{out}/P_{in})$ | dB |
| 13 | Wavelength from Frequency | $\lambda = c / f$ | m, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s |
| 14 | Audiometric Frequencies | 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8 | kHz |
| 15 | Hearing Loss Categories | Mild 26–40 / Mod 41–55 / Severe 71–90 | dB HL |

---

## 2.4 Wireless ALD Transmission Physics

### 2.4.1 FM Systems

Operating in the **72–216 MHz TV band**, an FM system consists of:
- **Transmitter microphone** worn by the speaker
- **Receiver** coupled to the listener's HA via direct audio input, neckloop, or boot
- **Bandwidth**: typically 50 Hz – 15 kHz audio
- **SNR advantage**: **+20 to +25 dB** over acoustic listening at 6 m

The FM capture effect suppresses interference from other transmissions on the same frequency within a 200 kHz channel.

### 2.4.2 Infrared (IR) Systems

Used in **cinemas, theaters, and courtrooms**. The audio signal modulates an IR LED emitting at **870–950 nm**; a photodetector at the receiver demodulates. IR is **line-of-sight** and **confined to one room** (no wall penetration), ensuring privacy.

### 2.4.3 Induction Loop Systems

A wire loop surrounding the listening area is driven by the audio amplifier output. Inside the loop, a **magnetic field H** is generated:

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{2\pi r}$$

The listener's **telecoil (T-coil)** — a small inductive pickup in the HA — converts this fluctuating magnetic field back to audio voltage. Per IEC 60118-4, the field strength should be **400 mA/m peak** at the listening position.

### 2.4.4 Digital Wireless (Bluetooth LE Audio)

Modern hearing aids use **2.4 GHz Bluetooth Low Energy Audio** with **LC3 codec** at 16/24 kHz, providing **+15 to +20 dB SNR advantage** and direct smartphone streaming.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Trade-Offs in ALD Design

| Parameter | Trade-off |
|:---|:---|
| Battery size vs. gain | Higher gain → higher current draw |
| Bandwidth vs. feedback | More gain at high freq → more feedback |
| Latency vs. compression | WDRC introduces < 5 ms delay |
| Number of channels vs. power | 16+ channels drain battery faster |
| Wireless range vs. power | Doubling range quadruples transmit power |

---

## 2.6 Cochlear Implant — The Bionic ALD

For **profound SNHL (>90 dB HL)** where conventional amplification provides no benefit, the **Cochlear Implant (CI)** electrically stimulates the auditory nerve via an electrode array inserted into the cochlea.

**Components:**
1. External: microphone, speech processor, transmitter coil
2. Internal: receiver-stimulator, electrode array (12–22 electrodes)

**Signal chain:** Acoustic → Microphone → Speech Processor (bandpass filters 0.1–8 kHz into N bands) → CIS/ACE strategy → RF link across skin → Stimulator → Current pulses → Auditory nerve

**Critical engineering parameters:**
- Stimulation rate: **900–1800 pulses/sec/electrode**
- Pulse width: **25–100 μs**
- Dynamic range: **6–20 dB** per electrode (mapped from 50 dB acoustic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The CI is classified as a **neural prosthetic**, not a hearing aid, because it bypasses the damaged cochlea and directly stimulates neural tissue.

---

## 2.7 Bone-Anchored Hearing Aid (BAHA)

For **conductive or mixed hearing loss** and **single-sided deafness (SSD)**, the BAHA uses a **titanium abutment** osseointegrated into the mastoid bone. Sound vibrations are transmitted via bone conduction directly to the cochlea, bypassing the outer/middle ear.

**Components:**
1. External sound processor
2. Coupling snap (magnetic or percutaneous abutment)
3. Osseointegrated titanium implant
4. Bone-conducted vibration to skull → contralateral cochlea (via skull vibration in SSD)

**Indication criteria:** BC threshold ≤ 45 dB HL (Ponto), ≤ 65 dB HL (BAHA 5).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — SNR Improvement of a Personal FM System

**Problem:** A teacher speaks from a distance of 6 m. A hearing-impaired student (PTA = 55 dB HL) is seated at the back of the classroom. The classroom background noise is 55 dB SPL at the student's ear. The teacher's voice at 1 m is 70 dB SPL.

(a) Compute the SNR at the student's ear without the FM system.
(b) Compute the SNR with an FM system where the teacher's microphone is 0.3 m from the mouth.
(c) Show that the FM system meets the +15 dB KTU benchmark for intelligibility.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Voice level at the student's ear (6 m distance)**

Using the Inverse Square Law:

$$L_p(6) = L_p(1) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{6}{1}\right)$$

$$L_p(6) = 70 - 20 \log_{10}(6)$$

$$L_p(6) = 70 - 20 \times 0.7782$$

$$L_p(6) = 70 - 15.56$$

$$L_p(6) = 54.44 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Step 2: SNR without FM**

$$\text{SNR}_{no\_FM} = L_{voice}(6) - L_{noise} = 54.44 - 55 = -0.56 \text{ dB}$$

> **Logic:** Negative SNR means the **noise is louder than the voice** at the listener's ear. Speech is essentially unintelligible.

**Step 3: Voice level at the FM microphone (0.3 m from mouth)**

$$L_{FM} = 70 - 20 \log_{10}(0.3) = 70 - 20 \times (-0.5229) = 70 + 10.46 = 80.46 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Step 4: SNR with FM (assuming wireless transmission is noise-free)**

The FM receiver reproduces 80.46 dB SPL voice directly at the listener's ear, while the ambient noise at the ear remains ~55 dB SPL.

$$\text{SNR}_{FM} = 80.46 - 55 = 25.46 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5: SNR Improvement**

$$\Delta \text{SNR} = \text{SNR}_{FM} - \text{SNR}_{no\_FM} = 25.46 - (-0.56) = 26.02 \text{ dB}$$

> **Logic:** This **+26 dB SNR improvement** vastly exceeds the **+15 dB KTU benchmark**, confirming the FM system provides intelligible speech in this scenario.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SRT Calculation for a Hearing-Impaired Listener

**Problem:** A 70-year-old retiree has a Pure Tone Average of 60 dB HL. Determine the Speech Reception Threshold (SRT) and recommend an appropriate ALD strategy.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the SRT formula**

$$\text{SRT}_{HI} = -4 + (0.014 \times \text{PTA})$$

$$\text{SRT}_{HI} = -4 + (0.014 \times 60)$$

$$\text{SRT}_{HI} = -4 + 0.84$$

$$\text{SRT}_{HI} = -3.16 \text{ dB}$$

> **Logic:** The patient requires the speech to be **3.16 dB above the noise** to achieve 50% word recognition. This is a near-normal requirement.

**Step 2: Required SNR for 95% intelligibility (Articulation Index)**

For comfortable, conversational understanding, the **+15 dB** rule applies:

$$\text{SNR}_{req,95\%} = -3.16 + 18.16 = +15 \text{ dB (achieved)}$$

**Step 3: Recommendation**

| PTA | SRT | Recommended ALD |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 60 dB HL | −3.16 dB | **BTE digital HA + FM system** for noisy environments |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reverberation Time of a Classroom

**Problem:** A classroom has dimensions 10 m × 8 m × 3 m (length × width × height). The total absorption (furniture, students, ceiling tiles) is 28 sabins. Calculate $T_{60}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Volume**

$$V = 10 \times 8 \times 3 = 240 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Apply Sabine formula**

$$T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \times V}{A_{total}}$$

$$T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \times 240}{28}$$

$$T_{60} = \frac{38.64}{28}$$

$$T_{60} = 1.38 \text{ seconds}$$

> **Logic:** This $T_{60}$ is **far above the recommended 0.4–0.6 s** for speech intelligibility in classrooms. An FM/IR system is essential.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — WDRC Output Calculation

**Problem:** A hearing aid has target gain $G_{target} = 40$ dB, compression ratio $CR = 3:1$, and compression knee at $P_{knee} = 50$ dB SPL. An input of 70 dB SPL is presented. Compute the output.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Input above knee**

$$P_{in} - P_{knee} = 70 - 50 = 20 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Compressed range**

$$P_{compressed} = \frac{P_{in} - P_{knee}}{CR} = \frac{20}{3} = 6.67 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3: Output SPL**

$$P_{out} = P_{knee} + P_{compressed} = 50 + 6.67 = 56.67 \text{ dB SPL}$$

> **Logic:** A linear amplifier would have given $50 + 40 = 90$ dB SPL — uncomfortably loud. The WDRC keeps it within the patient's **loudness discomfort level (LDL)**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Battery Life of a Digital Hearing Aid

**Problem:** A hearing aid draws 1.4 mA from a 13 mAh zinc-air battery (size 312). Compute the operational life.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Direct calculation**

$$t = \frac{Q_{bat}}{I_{draw}} = \frac{13 \text{ mAh}}{1.4 \text{ mA}}$$

$$t = 9.29 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Account for voltage droop (de-rated to 85%)**

$$t_{real} = 9.29 \times 0.85 = 7.89 \text{ hours} \approx 8 \text{ hours}$$

> **Logic:** Typical daily wear time of 12–16 hours; patients carry spare batteries.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Induction Loop Magnetic Field Strength

**Problem:** A rectangular induction loop has N = 50 turns, current I = 1.5 A. Compute the magnetic field strength at the center, r = 3 m.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{2\pi r}$$

$$H = \frac{50 \times 1.5}{2\pi \times 3}$$

$$H = \frac{75}{18.85}$$

$$H = 3.98 \text{ A/m}$$

> **Logic:** This exceeds the **IEC 60118-4 standard of 400 mA/m** by a factor of ~10, ensuring strong telecoil pickup across the looped area.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — ALD Selection by Clinical Profile

| Patient Profile | PTA (dB HL) | Type of Loss | First-Line ALD | Supplemental ALD |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| Child with otitis media | 35 | Conductive | BTE HA | FM system in classroom |
| Elderly with presbycusis | 50 | SNHL | BTE/RIC digital HA | TV streamer (Bluetooth) |
| Industrial worker with NIHL | 65 | SNHL (high freq) | ITC with notch filter | FM at workplace |
| Single-sided deafness | SSD | Unilateral | CROS / BAHA | None typically |
| Profound bilateral | 95+ | SNHL | Cochlear Implant | FM + alerting devices |
| Auditory neuropathy | Variable | Neural | FM system (off-label) | Captioning (CART) |
| CAPD with normal audiogram | < 25 | Central | Remote microphone FM | Preferential seating |
