# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Digital Hearing Aid Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    micA[Microphone<br>Input Transducer] --> preampA[Pre Amplifier<br>Gain 20 to 40 dB]
    preampA --> adcA[Analog to Digital Converter<br>16 to 24 bit 32 kHz]
    adcA --> dspA[Digital Signal Processor<br>DSP Core]
    dspA --> wsrcA[WDRC Compressor<br>Channel Bank 16 to 48]
    wsrcA --> nredA[Noise Reduction Module<br>Spectral Subtraction]
    nredA --> dirprocA[Directional Processing<br>Adaptive Beamformer]
    dirprocA --> fbcancA[Feedback Canceller<br>NLP Algorithm]
    fbcancA --> dacA[Digital to Analog Converter<br>Reconstruction Filter]
    dacA --> recvA[Receiver Output<br>110 to 130 dB SPL]
    recvA --> earA[Listener Ear Canal]
    batA[Battery 1.4V Zinc Air] -. powers .-> preampA
    batA -. powers .-> dspA
    batA -. powers .-> recvA
```

> **Description:** The microphone converts acoustic pressure into an analog electrical signal. The pre-amplifier boosts the weak voltage. The ADC digitizes the signal. The DSP core runs the hearing aid algorithm, which includes wide dynamic range compression (WDRC), noise reduction, directional processing, and feedback cancellation. The DAC converts the processed digital signal back to analog, and the receiver (miniature loudspeaker) delivers the amplified sound to the ear canal. The 1.4 V zinc-air battery powers the entire system.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cochlear Implant External + Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph externalSystem["External Components"]
        micB[1. Microphone<br>Behind Ear] --> spB[2. Sound Processor<br>DSP Unit]
        spB --> filterBankB[3. Bandpass Filter Bank<br>0.1 to 8 kHz]
        filterBankB --> strategyB[4. Stimulation Strategy<br>CIS or ACE]
        strategyB --> coilTxB[5. Transmitter Coil<br>RF Link 5 MHz]
    end
    coilTxB -. inductive coupling .-> coilRxB
    subgraph internalSystem["Internal Components"]
        coilRxB[6. Receiver Coil<br>Subcutaneous] --> stimB[7. Stimulator IC<br>Current Generator]
        stimB --> electrodeB[8. Electrode Array<br>12 to 22 channels]
    end
    electrodeB --> nerveB[9. Auditory Nerve<br>Spiral Ganglion]
    nerveB --> brainB[10. Auditory Cortex<br>Sound Perception]
```

> **Description:** The microphone captures the acoustic signal, which the sound processor filters into N frequency bands. The stimulation strategy (Continuous Interleaved Sampling or Advanced Combination Encoder) selects which electrodes to activate. The transmitter coil sends the encoded data via a 5 MHz inductive radiofrequency link across the skin. The internal receiver-stimulator decodes the signal and delivers biphasic current pulses to the electrode array inside the cochlea. These pulses stimulate the spiral ganglion cells of the auditory nerve, and the brain interprets them as sound.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — FM Wireless ALD System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    speakerB[Speaker Voice<br>Source 70 dB SPL] --> txMicB[FM Transmitter<br>Microphone]
    txMicB --> txRadioB[FM Modulator<br>72 to 216 MHz]
    txRadioB -. radio wave .-> rxRadioB
    subgraph receiverUnit["FM Receiver Unit"]
        rxRadioB[FM Demodulator] --> ampB[Audio Amplifier]
        ampB --> outputB[Output Coupling]
    end
    outputB --> couplingChoice{Output Type}
    couplingChoice --> neckloopB[1. Neckloop<br>Electromagnetic]
    couplingChoice --> dAIbootB[2. Direct Audio Input<br>Dedicated Boot]
    couplingChoice --> earphonesB[3. Earphones<br>Headphones]
    neckloopB --> telecoilB[HA Telecoil<br>T Mode]
    dAIbootB --> haMicB[HA Microphone<br>MT Mode]
    earphonesB --> earCanalB[Ear Canal<br>Direct Delivery]
```

> **Description:** The speaker's voice is picked up by a lapel or boom microphone connected to the FM transmitter. The audio signal frequency-modulates a carrier in the 72–216 MHz TV band and is broadcast via radio waves. The FM receiver, worn by the listener, demodulates the signal and routes it through one of three output modes: neckloop (inductive coupling to the HA's telecoil), direct audio input boot (wired to the HA's mic port), or direct earphones. Each mode delivers the cleaned, noise-free signal directly to the listener's ear.

---

## 4.4 Master Classification Tree of Assistive Listening Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    aldRoot[Assistive Listening Devices ALDs] --> personalBranch[Personal Amplifiers]
    aldRoot --> groupBranch[Group Communication Systems]
    aldRoot --> alertBranch[Alerting and Signaling Devices]
    personalBranch --> haTypes[Hearing Aids]
    personalBranch --> ciType[Cochlear Implant]
    personalBranch --> bahaType[Bone Anchored HA BAHA]
    personalBranch --> meiType[Middle Ear Implant]
    haTypes --> bteHa[Behind The Ear BTE]
    haTypes --> ricHa[Receiver In Canal RIC]
    haTypes --> iteHa[In The Ear ITE]
    haTypes --> itcHa[In The Canal ITC]
    haTypes --> cicHa[Completely In Canal CIC]
    haTypes --> iicHa[Invisible In Canal IIC]
    groupBranch --> fmSys[FM System 72 to 216 MHz]
    groupBranch --> irSys[Infrared System 870 nm]
    groupBranch --> loopSys[Induction Loop System]
    groupBranch --> btSys[Bluetooth LE Audio 2.4 GHz]
    alertBranch --> visualAlert[Visual Alerts Flashing Lights]
    alertBranch --> tactileAlert[Tactile Alerts Vibrating Pagers]
    alertBranch --> ttyAlert[Text Telephone TTY TDD]
    alertBranch --> captionAlert[Real Time Captioning CART]
```

> **Description:** ALDs are divided into three primary branches. **Personal Amplifiers** include all hearing aids (BTE, RIC, ITE, ITC, CIC, IIC) and surgical implants (CI, BAHA, MEI). **Group Communication Systems** include FM, infrared, induction loop, and modern Bluetooth LE Audio systems. **Alerting Devices** include visual flashing alerts, tactile vibrating pagers, text telephones (TTY/TDD), and real-time captioning (CART) services for users with severe-profound loss.

---

## 4.5 Comparison — Telecoil, FM, IR, Bluetooth Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceSig[Audio Source Signal] --> splitChoice{Transmission Medium}
    splitChoice --> tcoilPath[Telecoil Path]
    splitChoice --> fmPath[FM Path]
    splitChoice --> irPath[IR Path]
    splitChoice --> btPath[Bluetooth Path]
    tcoilPath --> tcoilChar[Inductive Magnetic Loop<br>Range 3 m to 5 m<br>No Encryption<br>IEC 60118-4 Standard]
    fmPath --> fmChar[Radio 72 to 216 MHz<br>Range up to 30 m<br>Channel Selectable<br>ASHA Standard]
    irPath --> irChar[Light 870 nm<br>Range 10 m to 15 m<br>Line of Sight Only<br>Privacy Guaranteed]
    btPath --> btChar[Digital 2.4 GHz<br>Range 10 m<br>LC3 Codec 24 kHz<br>Multi Device Pairing]
    tcoilChar --> haPickup[HA Telecoil Pickup]
    fmChar --> haPickup
    irChar --> haPickup
    btChar --> haPickup
    haPickup --> earDelivery[Delivered to Ear]
```

> **Description:** The same source signal can be delivered to the HA via four different transmission media. Telecoil uses inductive magnetic coupling (3–5 m range, simple, no encryption). FM uses radio transmission in the TV band (up to 30 m, channelized). IR uses modulated infrared light (line-of-sight, room-confined, fully private). Bluetooth LE Audio uses digital 2.4 GHz transmission (10 m, modern smartphones, multi-device streaming). All four ultimately deliver the signal to the HA's receiver and into the ear canal.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs). Differentiate them from conventional hearing aids with two key distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Assistive Listening Devices are specialized electroacoustic instruments designed to improve the signal-to-noise ratio and overcome the 3-D problem (Distance, Noise, Distortion) for hearing-impaired listeners, particularly in adverse listening environments.
- **Difference 1 (1 mark):** Conventional HAs are body-worn, amplify **all ambient sound**; ALDs selectively transmit a **specific signal** (e.g., TV, speaker) directly to the listener's ear, bypassing the degraded acoustic path.
- **Difference 2 (1 mark):** HAs work in the personal acoustic space (≤ 1 m); ALDs work over **extended distances** (3–30 m) and use wireless transmission (FM, IR, induction loop, Bluetooth).

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain the three categories of Assistive Listening Devices recognized by ASHA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Personal Amplifiers / Hearing Instruments (1 mark):** Body-worn devices including conventional hearing aids, cochlear implants, and bone-anchored hearing aids. Function by amplifying or directly stimulating the auditory pathway.
2. **Group / Wireless Communication Systems (1 mark):** FM, IR, induction loop, and Bluetooth systems that transmit audio from a remote microphone/source directly to the listener's ear.
3. **Alerting / Signaling Devices (1 mark):** Visual (flashing lights), tactile (vibrating pagers), and textual (TTY, CART) devices that substitute auditory signals with another sensory modality for alarms, doorbells, telephones, and speech.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — 14 Marks

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of a **digital hearing aid**. Explain each stage in detail, including the role of WDRC compression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle, components, and clinical applications of a **Cochlear Implant**. Compare it with a conventional hearing aid in terms of indication criteria and signal processing strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Digital Hearing Aid

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
*Refer to Section 4.1 — Diagram of Digital Hearing Aid Signal Chain.*

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

A digital hearing aid operates on a five-stage signal chain:

1. **Microphone:** An electret or MEMS microphone converts the incoming acoustic pressure waves into a low-level analog electrical signal. Typical sensitivity is **−46 dB V/Pa**, with a flat frequency response from 100 Hz to 8 kHz.

2. **Pre-amplifier:** A low-noise operational amplifier boosts the microphone signal by **20–40 dB** to bring it within the ADC's input range.

3. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Samples the amplified signal at **16–32 kHz** with **16–24 bit resolution** to preserve speech dynamics.

4. **DSP (Digital Signal Processor):** The brain of the hearing aid. It executes the following algorithms in real time:
   - **WDRC compression:** Maps the wide input dynamic range (~100 dB) into the patient's residual dynamic range (~30–50 dB) using the formula $G_{out} = P_{knee} + (P_{in} - P_{knee})/CR$.
   - **Noise reduction:** Spectral subtraction, modulation-based detection.
   - **Directional processing:** Adaptive beamformer to attenuate rear hemisphere noise.
   - **Feedback cancellation:** Adaptive notch filter to suppress howling.

5. **Receiver:** A balanced armature or dynamic receiver converts the processed analog signal back to acoustic pressure, delivering **110–130 dB SPL** at the ear canal.

**Battery:** A 1.4 V zinc-air cell powers the entire system with a typical life of **5–14 days**.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Cochlear Implant

**Principle (3 marks):**

The Cochlear Implant (CI) is a **neural prosthetic** that bypasses the damaged hair cells of the cochlea and directly stimulates the **spiral ganglion cells of the auditory nerve** with biphasic current pulses. It is indicated for **profound bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (> 90 dB HL)** where conventional amplification provides no benefit.

**Components (2 marks):**

*External:* Microphone, sound processor (DSP unit), transmitter coil with magnet.
*Internal (surgically implanted):* Receiver-stimulator package, electrode array (12–22 electrodes) inserted into the scala tympani of the cochlea via the round window.

**Signal Processing (1 mark):**

The sound processor decomposes the audio into N frequency bands (typically 22 for Cochlear™, 16 for MED-EL). The dominant band amplitudes are converted to biphasic current pulses. Two main strategies:
- **CIS (Continuous Interleaved Sampling):** Stimulates electrodes sequentially at 900–1800 pps per channel.
- **ACE (Advanced Combination Encoder):** Selects M-maxima (e.g., 8 of 22) channels per cycle to reduce power.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Hearing Aid | Cochlear Implant |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Indication | Mild–severe SNHL | Profound SNHL (> 90 dB) |
| Mechanism | Acoustic amplification | Electrical neural stimulation |
| Power | Acoustic at eardrum | Current at auditory nerve |
| Surgery | None | Required (mastoidectomy) |
| Speech in noise | +5 to +10 dB SNR | +10 to +15 dB SNR |

---

### **Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the principle and operation of an **FM wireless Assistive Listening System**. Derive the SNR improvement obtained by an FM system in a typical classroom of dimensions 10 m × 8 m × 3 m, given a teacher speaking at 70 dB SPL at 1 m, classroom noise 55 dB SPL, and a student at 6 m. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Induction Loop Audio-Frequency System** with a neat diagram. State the IEC 60118-4 standard for field strength and derive the magnetic field intensity for a loop with 50 turns carrying 1.5 A at 3 m radius. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — FM System SNR Derivation

**Principle (2 marks):**
An FM system transmits the speaker's voice from a **wireless microphone** worn on the speaker's body to a **receiver** worn by the listener. The receiver reproduces the voice directly into the listener's ear, **bypassing the acoustic path** (and its associated distance/noise/reverberation losses).

**Components (1 mark):** Transmitter mic, FM modulator (72–216 MHz carrier), radio propagation, FM receiver, output coupling (neckloop, DAI, earphones).

**Numerical Derivation (4 marks):**

*Given:* $L_p(1) = 70$ dB SPL (voice at 1 m), $L_{noise} = 55$ dB SPL, student at $d = 6$ m, FM mic at $d_{FM} = 0.3$ m.

**Without FM** — Voice level at 6 m:
$$L_p(6) = 70 - 20 \log_{10}(6) = 70 - 15.56 = 54.44 \text{ dB SPL}$$
$$\text{SNR}_{noFM} = 54.44 - 55 = -0.56 \text{ dB (unintelligible)}$$

**With FM** — Voice level at FM mic (0.3 m):
$$L_{FM} = 70 - 20 \log_{10}(0.3) = 70 + 10.46 = 80.46 \text{ dB SPL}$$
$$\text{SNR}_{FM} = 80.46 - 55 = +25.46 \text{ dB}$$

**SNR Improvement:**
$$\Delta \text{SNR} = 25.46 - (-0.56) = \mathbf{+26.02 \text{ dB}}$$

> **Logic:** The +26 dB SNR improvement vastly exceeds the KTU benchmark of +15 dB required for comfortable speech intelligibility.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Induction Loop System

**Principle (2 marks):**
The induction loop system uses **electromagnetic induction** to transmit audio. An amplifier drives a current through a wire loop installed around the perimeter of the listening area (e.g., a church, theater, classroom). This creates a fluctuating magnetic field inside the loop, which is picked up by the **T-coil (telecoil)** in the listener's hearing aid.

**Diagram (1 mark):**
*Refer to Section 4.5 — Coupling comparison diagram.*

**Components (1 mark):** Audio source, loop amplifier, perimeter wire loop, telecoil pickup in hearing aid, listener.

**IEC 60118-4 Standard (1 mark):**
The standard specifies a **peak magnetic field strength of 400 mA/m** at the listening position, with a frequency response of 100 Hz – 5 kHz (±3 dB) and background field strength ≤ 0.5 mA/m.

**Magnetic Field Derivation (2 marks):**

For a circular loop with $N$ turns, current $I$, at distance $r$ from the loop center:

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{2\pi r}$$

Substituting $N = 50$, $I = 1.5$ A, $r = 3$ m:

$$H = \frac{50 \times 1.5}{2\pi \times 3} = \frac{75}{18.85} = 3.98 \text{ A/m} = 3980 \text{ mA/m}$$

> **Logic:** $H = 3980$ mA/m is **~10× the IEC 60118-4 minimum of 400 mA/m**, providing a strong telecoil signal across the looped area.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Hearing Aids with ALDs:** HAs are body-worn amplifiers; ALDs are signal-specific transmission systems. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this conflation.
> 2. **Forgetting the +15 dB KTU Benchmark:** Any SNR improvement answer must explicitly compare against the +15 dB clinical standard.
> 3. **Wrong Inverse Square Law Sign:** Many students write $+ 20 \log_{10}(d)$ instead of $- 20 \log_{10}(d)$ for distance attenuation. **Always subtract** because intensity falls with distance.
> 4. **CI is NOT a Hearing Aid:** It is a **neural prosthetic**. Use the term "auditory nerve stimulation" not "amplification" in CI answers.
> 5. **Forgetting Units in $T_{60}$:** Sabine formula $T_{60} = 0.161 V / A$ requires $V$ in **m³** and $A$ in **sabins** — check unit conversions.
> 6. **Audiometric PTA:** Use **0.5, 1, 2 kHz** only (3-frequency average). Including 4 kHz is incorrect for PTA in the KTU SRT formula.
> 7. **Hearing Aid Power:** A 1.4 V (not 3 V or 9 V) zinc-air cell powers modern HAs. Mention the specific chemistry (zinc-air, not alkaline).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Assistive Listening Devices**

- ✅ **ALDs** overcome the **3-D problem** (Distance, Noise, Distortion) where HAs alone fail.
- ✅ **+15 dB SNR** is the KTU/ASHA benchmark for comfortable speech intelligibility.
- ✅ **Inverse Square Law:** $L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 20 \log_{10}(r/r_0)$ — every doubling of distance loses 6 dB.
- ✅ **Three ALD categories:** Personal Amplifiers, Group Wireless Systems, Alerting Devices.
- ✅ **Hearing Aid styles (decreasing size):** BTE → RIC → ITE → ITC → CIC → IIC.
- ✅ **WDRC Compression:** $P_{out} = P_{knee} + (P_{in} - P_{knee})/CR$ — restores audibility without exceeding LDL.
- ✅ **FM Systems:** 72–216 MHz, range up to 30 m, **+20 to +25 dB SNR advantage**.
- ✅ **IR Systems:** 870–950 nm, line-of-sight, room-confined (privacy), used in theaters.
- ✅ **Induction Loop:** Magnetic coupling, **IEC 60118-4 = 400 mA/m peak**, $H = NI/(2\pi r)$.
- ✅ **Bluetooth LE Audio:** 2.4 GHz, LC3 codec, +15 to +20 dB SNR advantage, smartphone streaming.
- ✅ **Cochlear Implant:** Indicated for PTA > 90 dB, **electrical neural stimulation**, CIS/ACE strategies, 12–22 electrodes.
- ✅ **BAHA:** For conductive loss and SSD; uses **bone conduction** via titanium osseointegration.
- ✅ **Sabine Formula:** $T_{60} = 0.161 V/A$; classroom optimum 0.4–0.6 s.
- ✅ **SRT formula:** $\text{SRT}_{HI} = -4 + 0.014 \times \text{PTA}$ — used in dB units.
- ✅ **PTA (Pure Tone Average):** Average of audiometric thresholds at **0.5, 1, 2 kHz** in dB HL.
- ✅ **Hearing Loss Categories:** Mild 26–40 / Moderate 41–55 / Mod-Severe 56–70 / Severe 71–90 / Profound > 90 dB HL.
- ✅ **Battery:** 1.4 V zinc-air, life ~ 5–14 days, $t = Q/I$ formula.
- ✅ **CROS / BiCROS** systems for single-sided deafness (SSD) — non-surgical alternative to BAHA.
- ✅ **Tactile ALDs** for deaf-blind users; **CART** (Communication Access Realtime Translation) for severe-profound users.
- ✅ **Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI):** Stimulates the cochlear nucleus — for NF2 patients with severed auditory nerves.

> **End of Module 4 — Assistive Listening Devices | KTU 2024 Scheme PEBRT745**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aids for Persons with Both Visual and Auditory Impairments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT745 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Aids for Persons with Both Visual and Auditory Impairments # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Deafblindness (Combined Visual and Auditory Impairment)** is defined as a *combined visual and hearing impairment of such severity that it is unlikely to be resolved through corrective lenses, hearing aids, or medical/surgical intervention, thereby requiring the use of specialized assistive devices, tactile communication methods, and environmental adaptation systems*. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBRT745)**, **Assistive Devices for Persons with Both Visual and Auditory Impairments** are classified as a distinct sub-category of sensory substitution/augmentation devices. These are engineered systems that simultaneously address the loss of *two sensory channels* (vision and hearing) and rely primarily on the **tactile** and **proprioceptive** sensory channels as the primary information input pathways, with **olfactory** and **vestibular** cues as secondary channels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module specifically emphasizes devices that enable *bidirectional communication* (both input and output) for deafblind users, distinguishing them from monodisability aids (e.g., simple Braille readers or basic hearing aids). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Builder:** Imagine you are sitting in a completely dark, completely silent room. The lights are off, and there is no sound. How would you know someone is calling your name? How would you ask for a glass of water? You would have to rely on **touch, vibration, smell, and movement**. This is the lived experience of a deafblind person. A real-world analogy is the **GPS system in a car**: - **Sighted/hearing people** get information through *visual* displays (map) and *auditory* prompts (turn-by-turn voice). - A deafblind person gets the *same information* through a **vibrating watch on the wrist** (tactile) — different sense, same functional outcome. The engineering goal is therefore **sensory substitution and sensory augmentation**: *translating visual/audio information into touch and vibration signals* the brain can interpret. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Clinical Constants (Bolded for Emphasis) - **Legal Blindness Threshold (WHO 2019 updated):** Visual Acuity (VA) **< 3/60 (0.05)** in the better eye with best correction. - **Profound Hearing Loss Threshold:** Hearing threshold **> 91 dB HL** (decibels Hearing Level) across speech frequencies (500 Hz – 4 kHz). - **Tactile Spatial Acuity:** The human fingertip can discriminate **2-point separations of ~1–2 mm** — this is the *physical upper limit* of any Braille-based or tactile display system. - **Tactile Frequency Response:** Skin receptors optimally respond in the range **40–250 Hz** for vibration-based alerts. - **Refresh Rate of Braille Displays:** Standard refreshable Braille operates at **20–100 Hz**; below 20 Hz causes perceptible flicker. - **Lorm Alphabet Resolution:** Communication at a typical rate of **30–40 letters per minute** when spelled on the palm. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Aids for Combined Visual and Auditory Impairment The KTU 2024 module classifies these aids into **six functional categories**: ### A. Tactile Communication Aids - **Braille System (Louis Braille, 1824)** — raised-dot code read by fingertips. - **Refreshable Braille Displays** — electromechanical pin arrays driven by piezoelectric actuators. - **Tactile Sign Language (Tactile ASL / BSL)** — sign language felt through hand-over-hand contact. - **Lorm Alphabet (Hieronymus Lorm, 19th century)** — letters traced on the palm. - **Print-on-Palm / Deafblind Manual Alphabet** — letters drawn on the palm. - **Tadoma Method** — placing a hand on the speaker's face/throat to feel speech vibrations and lip/jaw movements. ### B. Vibration-Based Alerting Devices - **Vibrating Alarm Clocks** — strong-bed-shaker type (~**70 dB equivalent vibration**). - **Vibrating Pagers and Wristbands** — frequency-coded alerts (e.g., 200 Hz = doorbell, 300 Hz = phone). - **Mobile phone vibration + visual flash** hybrid alerts. ### C. Hearing Aids Augmented with Visual/Tactile Cues - **Cochlear Implants with Visual Speech Cues (Audio-Visual Integration Systems).** - **Hearing Aids with Bluetooth Streaming to Visual Displays** (captioning glasses). ### D. Computer-Based Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) - **Screen Readers (JAWS, NVDA) + Refreshable Braille Display** combination. - **Deafblind-specific AAC Apps** (e.g., Be My Eyes, Aira, ProTactile). ### E. Service Animals - **Hearing Dogs** (signal for sounds) + **Guide Dogs** (mobility) — *dual-trained* dogs exist for deafblind users. ### F. Environmental Control Systems (ECS) - **Smart Home Integration** — voice commands replaced by *switch scanning* or *braille tablet* control. ## 2.2 Operational Principle: Sensory Substitution Framework The **sensory substitution equation** governing any such device can be stated as: $$I_{output} = f(S_{input}, C_{user}, E_{env})$$ Where: - $I_{output}$ = Information delivered via tactile/proprioceptive channel. - $S_{input}$ = Original sensory stimulus (audio, visual, or text). - $C_{user}$ = User's residual sensory/cognitive capacity. - $E_{env}$ = Environmental and contextual parameters. - $f(\cdot)$ = Mapping/transformation function implemented by the device. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Note on LaTeX formatting:** In the tables below, vertical bars are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. | **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Relation** | **Standard Unit** | **KTU Relevance** | |---|---|---|---| | Braille cell resolution | $6 \text{ dots} \rightarrow 2^6 - 1 = 63$ printable patterns | dimensionless | Refreshable Braille design | | Visual acuity threshold (legal blindness) | $VA < 3/60$ | dimensionless ratio | Eligibility criteria | | Hearing loss threshold (profound) | $HT \geq 91$ | dB HL | CI candidacy | | Tactile 2-point discrimination | $\Delta d_{min} \approx 1$ | mm | Braille dot spacing (2.5 mm) | | Vibration frequency band (skin) | $f_{opt} \in [40, 250]$ | Hz | Alert device design | | Refreshable Braille refresh rate | $f_{refresh} \geq 20$ | Hz | Display design constraint | | Tadoma speech rate | $R_{tadoma} \approx 70$–$80$ | words/min | Communication speed | | Lorm spelling rate | $R_{lorm} \approx 30$–$40$ | letters/min | Palm-spelling throughput | | Haptic actuator force output | $F_a \geq 0.1$ | N | Wearable vibrator spec | | Signal-to-noise for tactile alert | $SNR_t \geq 10 \log_{10}(P_{signal}/P_{noise})$ | dB | Alarm distinguishability | | Sensory bandwidth (skin) | $B_{tactile} \approx 1$ kbit/s | bit/s | Theoretical upper data rate | | Battery life (portable aid) | $T_{bat} \geq 24$ | hours | Daily usability requirement | | Working distance (face-to-face tactile sign) | $d_{ws} \in [0, 0.1]$ | m | Close-contact protocol | | Refreshable Braille cell count | $N_{cell} \in [14, 80]$ | cells per line | Display line length | | Pin actuation stroke (piezo) | $s_{pin} \approx 0.7$ | mm | Piezo design spec | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These devices sit at the intersection of **biomedical engineering, human factors, haptics, and signal processing**. They are directly relevant to: - Design of **wearable medical devices** (vibrating insulin pumps, alerting systems). - Development of **inclusive UX/UI** in consumer electronics (Apple's Taptic Engine, Android Live Transcribe). - **Smart city accessibility** design (tactile paving, vibration-crossing signals). - **Tele-rehabilitation and remote-care platforms** for sensory-impaired patients. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Information Capacity of the Tactile Channel The **sensory substitution device** is fundamentally limited by the information rate the skin can process. We derive the theoretical upper bound. ### Step 1 — Define the sensory channel capacity By Shannon's channel capacity theorem applied to a single tactile channel: $$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$ Where: - $C$ = channel capacity in bits per second. - $B$ = bandwidth of the tactile receptor (Hz). - $S/N$ = signal-to-noise power ratio of the skin's mechanoreceptor system. ### Step 2 — Substitute empirical values for human skin Empirical studies (Kaczmarek, Webster, 1989; ISO 13091 standards) show: - Effective tactile bandwidth: $B \approx 250$ Hz (above this, mechanoreceptors cannot resolve). - Typical S/N for trained users: $S/N \approx 100$ (20 dB). ### Step 3 — Compute the capacity $$C = 250 \cdot \log_2(1 + 100)$$ $$\log_2(101) = \frac{\ln(101)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.615}{0.693} \approx 6.66 \text{ bits}$$ $$C \approx 250 \times 6.66$$ $$C \approx 1665 \text{ bits/s}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert to readable text throughput Assuming 8 bits per ASCII character, and accounting for ~40% protocol overhead: $$R_{text} = \frac{C \times (1 - 0.40)}{8}$$ $$R_{text} = \frac{1665 \times 0.60}{8}$$ $$R_{text} \approx 125 \text{ characters/s} \approx 750 \text{ words/min (WPM)}$$ ### Step 5 — Interpretation > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** The skin can theoretically support ~750 WPM — but actual refreshable Braille displays operate at only ~100 WPM because of mechanical pin-response time, not biological limits. The bottleneck is the *device*, not the user. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Refreshable Braille Display Pin Actuation Force A piezoelectric Braille pin must rise **0.7 mm** against the user's fingertip. We derive the actuation force. ### Step 1 — Hertzian contact mechanics The fingertip is treated as an elastic half-space with Young's modulus $E_{skin} \approx 100$ kPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.5$. For a spherical pin tip of radius $r = 0.5$ mm pressed into the skin with displacement $\delta = 0.7$ mm: $$F = \frac{4}{3} \cdot E^* \cdot r^{1/2} \cdot \delta^{3/2}$$ Where the effective modulus is: $$E^* = \frac{E_{skin}}{2(1 - \nu^2)} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{2(1 - 0.25)} = \frac{100000}{1.5} \approx 66666 \text{ Pa}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute values $$F = \frac{4}{3} \times 66666 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{0.5} \times (0.7 \times 10^{-3})^{1.5}$$ Compute each subterm: - $(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{0.5} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.02236 \text{ m}^{0.5}$ - $(0.7 \times 10^{-3})^{1.5} = (7 \times 10^{-4})^{1.5} \approx 1.853 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{1.5}$ $$F = \frac{4}{3} \times 66666 \times 0.02236 \times 1.853 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$F = 1.333 \times 66666 \times 0.02236 \times 1.853 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$F \approx 3.69 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N} \approx 3.7 \text{ mN}$$ ### Step 3 — Add safety factor Applying a factor of 5 for reliable actuation: $$F_{design} = 5 \times F \approx 18.5 \text{ mN}$$ This is the **minimum piezo force spec** for a refreshable Braille pin — a critical design parameter tested in KTU 2024 lab exams. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vibration Alert Detectability (Signal-to-Noise) A deafblind person must distinguish, say, a *doorbell alert* (250 Hz) from background vibration noise (e.g., footsteps, ~50 Hz). ### Step 1 — Define the detectability criterion A vibration is detectable when its amplitude exceeds the threshold of vibration perception, given by: $$a_{detect} = a_{min} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_{c}}\right)^2}$$ Where: - $a_{min} \approx 0.001$ m/s² is the minimum perceivable acceleration at 1 Hz. - $f_c \approx 100$ Hz is the corner frequency of the Pacinian corpuscle. ### Step 2 — Compute detectability at doorbell frequency $f = 250$ Hz $$a_{detect} = 0.001 \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{250}{100}\right)^2}$$ $$a_{detect} = 0.001 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 6.25}$$ $$a_{detect} = 0.001 \cdot \sqrt{7.25} \approx 0.001 \times 2.69 \approx 2.69 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}^2$$ ### Step 3 — Interpretation A vibrator producing acceleration **> 2.7 × 10⁻³ m/s²** at 250 Hz is reliably perceived. This guides the **eccentric rotating mass (ERM) motor** or **linear resonant actuator (LRA)** selection in wearable alert devices. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Tactile Communication Methods) | **Method** | **Speed (WPM)** | **Range** | **Visual Required?** | **Auditory Required?** | **Setup Cost** | **Best Use Case** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Braille (paper) | 100–200 | Independent | No | No | Low | Reading, writing | | Refreshable Braille | 80–120 | Independent | No | No | High (₹₹₹) | Digital access | | Tactile Sign Language | 60–120 | Close contact | No | No | Low | Conversation | | Lorm Alphabet | 5–10 | Close contact | No | No | Low | One-on-one chat | | Print-on-Palm | 5–15 | Close contact | No | No | Low | Quick phrases | | Tadoma | 70–80 | Face contact | No (uses touch) | No (uses touch) | Low | Receiving speech | | Haptic/Vibration alert | N/A (coded signals) | Body-worn | No | No | Medium | Alerting, paging | | Deafblind Service Dog | N/A | Mobility range | No | No | High | Mobility + alerting | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — A Universal Deafblind Communication Aid ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Source: Microphone and Camera] --> B[Signal Preprocessing Unit] B --> C{Adaptive Routing Logic} C --> D[Speech to Text Engine] C --> E[Image and Object Recognition] C --> F[Sound Classification Network] D --> G[Refreshable Braille Display Module] E --> G E --> H[Tactile Pin Array on Hand] F --> I[Vibration Pattern Generator] G --> J[Deafblind User Tactile Readout] H --> J I --> J J --> K[User Response via Touchpad or Switch] K --> L[Text to Braille and Speech Synthesizer] L --> M[Output: Braille Pin Feedback and Speaker] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow — Tactile Sign Language (TSL) Conversation ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Speaker Forms Sign] --> S2[Receiver Places Hand Over Speakers Hand] S2 --> S3[Receiver Feels Shape, Motion, Location] S3 --> S4[Receiver Interprets Meaning] S4 --> S5{Receiver Responds} S5 --> S6[Receiver Forms Sign Back] S6 --> S1 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Refreshable Braille Display Hardware ```mermaid flowchart TD P[PC or Smartphone via USB or Bluetooth] --> MCU[Microcontroller Unit] MCU --> DRV[Piezo Driver Array] DRV --> PIEZO1[Piezo Actuator 1] DRV --> PIEZO2[Piezo Actuator 2] DRV --> PIEZON[Piezo Actuator N] PIEZO1 --> PIN1[Raised Pin 1] PIEZO2 --> PIN2[Raised Pin 2] PIEZON --> PINN[Raised Pin N] PIN1 --> F[Deafblind User Fingertip] PIN2 --> F PINN --> F F -.-> FB[Haptic Feedback Loop] FB --> MCU ``` ## 4.4 Functional Block — Cochlear Implant with Visual Cue Augmentation ```mermaid flowchart LR MIC[Microphone Array] --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor] DSP --> CI[Cochlear Implant Electrode Array] CI --> NERVE[Auditory Nerve] DSP2[DSP Branch] --> CAM[Forward Facing Camera] CAM --> LCD[Transparent HUD on Glasses] LCD --> EYE[Users Eye - Visual Cue] NERVE --> BRAIN[Auditory Cortex] EYE --> BRAIN BRAIN --> PERCEIVE[Integrated Audio Visual Perception] ``` ## 4.5 Conceptual Map — Modalities of Deafblind Communication ```mermaid mindmap root((Deafblind
Communication)) Tactile Channel Braille Paper Refreshable Braille Tactile Sign Language Lorm and Print on Palm Tadoma Vibration Channel Wrist Vibrators Phone Buzz Patterns Floor Vibration Pads Electronic AAC Screen Reader plus Braille AAC Tablets Smartphone Apps Service Based Guide Hearing Dogs Support Service Providers Interveners ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define deafblindness. List any four assistive devices used for persons with combined visual and auditory impairment.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Deafblindness is a condition of *combined visual and hearing impairment* of such severity that the person cannot be fully rehabilitated by corrective lenses, hearing aids, or surgery, and must rely on tactile, proprioceptive, and assistive technological channels for communication and navigation **(1 mark)**. Four assistive devices **(2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. Refreshable Braille display 2. Tactile sign language system 3. Lorm alphabet / Print-on-Palm 4. Vibrating alert devices (wristband / pager) --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Explain the Tadoma method of communication. Mention two of its limitations.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Tadoma is a tactile speech-reading method in which a deafblind person places their hand on the *speaker's face* — typically with the thumb on the lips, fingers on the jaw, and palm on the throat — to feel the **vibrations, lip movements, and airflow** that constitute speech **(1.5 marks)**. Limitations **(1.5 marks — 0.75 each):** 1. Requires *very close physical contact*, socially invasive and not usable in groups. 2. Highly *speaker-dependent*; accuracy drops sharply with unfamiliar speakers, fast speech, or speech impairments. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Option 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]** **(a)** Describe the architecture and working principle of a *refreshable Braille display* system. Explain how a piezoelectric actuator raises a Braille pin, with reference to the design force equation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast *Tactile Sign Language*, *Lorm Alphabet*, and *Print-on-Palm* methods of communication. Tabulate the differences and discuss their typical use-cases. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Working Principle (3 marks):** A refreshable Braille display consists of a linear array of **piezoelectric Braille cells** (commonly 14, 18, 32, 40, or 80 cells per line). Each cell contains **6 or 8 pins** that can be individually raised or lowered to form Braille characters. The pins are driven by **piezoelectric bimorph actuators** controlled by a microcontroller that receives digital text via USB or Bluetooth from a computer or smartphone. The system block diagram is: 1. Host device (PC/phone) → digital text input. 2. Braille translator software → converts text to Braille dot pattern. 3. Microcontroller → drives piezo driver array. 4. Piezo actuators → raise/lower pins. 5. User's fingertip → reads the tactile pattern. **Pin Actuation Mechanism (2 marks):** A piezo bimorph bends when voltage is applied. The bending is mechanically coupled to a **lever/amplifier** that pushes the pin upward by ~**0.7 mm**. When voltage is removed, the pin retracts. **Design Force Derivation (2 marks):** From Hertzian contact mechanics: $$F = \frac{4}{3} \cdot E^* \cdot r^{1/2} \cdot \delta^{3/2}$$ Substituting $E^* = 66.7$ kPa, $r = 0.5$ mm, $\delta = 0.7$ mm: $$F \approx 3.7 \text{ mN per pin}$$ With a safety factor of 5, the **design actuation force** is approximately **18.5 mN**. This value governs the choice of piezo material (typically PZT-5H ceramic) and the amplifier mechanism. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Tabular Comparison (4 marks):** | Aspect | Tactile Sign Language | Lorm Alphabet | Print-on-Palm | |---|---|---|---| | Method | Sign language felt by hand-over-hand | Letters drawn on palm by strokes | Letters written/printed on palm | | Origin | Adaptation of ASL/BSL | 19th c. (Hieronymus Lorm) | 20th c. (Deafblind manual) | | Speed | 60–120 WPM | 5–10 WPM | 5–15 WPM | | Visual required? | No | No | No | | Auditory required? | No | No | No | | Distance | Skin contact | Skin contact | Skin contact | | Two-way? | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Learning curve | High | Medium | Low | **Use-Cases (3 marks):** - **Tactile Sign Language:** Preferred for *fluent, real-time conversation* between two deafblind users or with a sighted partner who knows the sign language. - **Lorm Alphabet:** Used in *European contexts* (Germany, Austria, Netherlands) for slow, structured one-on-one conversation; particularly useful for users with limited hand mobility. - **Print-on-Palm:** Best for *quick tactical phrases* (e.g., "stop", "go", "danger") and in *low-resource settings*; nearly universal since it requires no formal training. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Compulsory Option 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the *sensory substitution* framework. Derive the theoretical *channel capacity* of the human tactile channel and comment on its implications for assistive device design. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a *Cochlear Implant augmented with visual speech cues*. Discuss how audio-visual integration improves speech perception in noisy environments. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Sensory Substitution (2 marks):** Sensory substitution is the process of *conveying information normally received by one sense* (e.g., hearing) *through a different sensory channel* (e.g., touch or vision). For deafblind individuals, the goal is to translate audio and visual inputs into *tactile or proprioceptive* signals the user can perceive. **Derivation (3 marks):** By Shannon's capacity theorem: $$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$ For the human skin: $B \approx 250$ Hz, $S/N \approx 100$ (20 dB). $$C = 250 \times \log_2(101) \approx 250 \times 6.66 \approx 1665 \text{ bits/s}$$ After overhead: $R_{text} \approx 750$ WPM theoretical maximum. **Implications (2 marks):** - Modern Braille displays (offering ~100 WPM) are limited by *mechanical pin response*, not biology. - Future devices using *multi-finger* or *electrotactile* stimulation can approach biological limits. - Implies great headroom exists for next-generation deafblind aids. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Microphone → DSP → (Branch 1) Cochlear Implant Electrode Array → Auditory Nerve. (Branch 2) Camera → Visual Processor → Transparent HUD on Glasses → User's Eye. **Working (2 marks):** The DSP extracts speech features (formants, envelope) and delivers them *electrically* to the cochlear electrodes. Simultaneously, a camera captures the speaker's lip movements, and a transparent head-up display (HUD) on the user's glasses projects *real-time lip-reading cues* (lip contour, jaw motion, gestures) into the user's residual vision. **Audio-Visual Integration (2 marks):** In noisy environments (e.g., SNR < 0 dB), cochlear-implant-alone users suffer severe speech perception loss. By adding *visual cues* (lip-reading, gesture, facial expression), the brain integrates the two streams — a phenomenon known as the **McGurk effect** and **audio-visual gain**. Studies show AV integration provides **2–4 dB SNR improvement**, equivalent to roughly 40–60% better word recognition. For deafblind users who still have residual vision, this hybrid approach is a critical design principle. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not confuse** *deafblind aids* with *hearing aids* or *Braille readers alone* — examiners deduct up to 3 marks if the answer only addresses one disability. > 2. **Tabular answers** in Part B (b) carry at least 4 of the 7 marks — always use a *clean, ruled table* with all required columns. > 3. **Avoid generic answers** like "Braille is dots felt by fingers" without the *technical specs* (2.5 mm spacing, 6/8 dot cells, refresh rate, piezo actuation force). Generic answers cap your score at 60% of the marks allotted for that sub-question. > 4. **For derivation questions**, show *every algebraic step* — partial marks are awarded per step, not just the final value. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:** - **Deafblindness** = combined vision + hearing loss requiring tactile-based rehabilitation. - **Six functional categories** of aids: Tactile communication, Vibration alerts, Augmented hearing aids, AAC, Service animals, ECS. - **Key tactile methods** and their speeds: Braille 100–200 WPM, Tactile Sign 60–120 WPM, Lorm 5–10 WPM, Print-on-Palm 5–15 WPM, Tadoma 70–80 WPM. - **Braille fundamentals:** 6 (or 8) dot cell, 2.5 mm inter-dot spacing, 6 mm inter-cell spacing, 0.7 mm dot height. - **Refreshable Braille** uses **piezoelectric bimorph actuators**; design force ~**18.5 mN** per pin (with safety factor). - **Tactile channel capacity** ≈ **1665 bits/s** ≈ 750 WPM theoretical, 100 WPM actual. - **Optimal skin vibration frequency** = 40–250 Hz. - **Tadoma** uses *face contact* — thumb on lips, palm on throat, fingers on jaw. - **Cochlear implant + visual cue** hybrid uses **audio-visual integration** to gain ~2–4 dB SNR. - **Legal blindness** = VA < 3/60; **profound hearing loss** ≥ 91 dB HL. - **Sensory substitution equation:** $I_{output} = f(S_{input}, C_{user}, E_{env})$. - **Service dogs** for deafblind are *dual-trained* (guide + hearing response). - **Piezo material of choice** for Braille: **PZT-5H** lead zirconate titanate ceramic. ---
